clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 24-year-old athlete. 4 weeks ago had knee surgery for a ligament injury. Reports pain and swelling around the surgical site. Struggling to regain normal movement and function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform sport-specific movements. Favours right leg, walks with limp. Struggles particularly with loaded knee bending (stairs, squats, kneeling).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Knee ligament tear from skiing injury. Reconstruction with autograft hamstring. Early post-operative phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Knee flexion 80 degrees, extension lag 5-10 degrees. Quadriceps strength 3+/5. Hamstring strength weak 3/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays show graft positioning adequate. Tunnel placement appropriate. MRI shows intact graft.

puzzles.explanation: Post-ACL reconstruction represents the early phase following surgical repair of an anterior cruciate ligament tear, typically using an autograft (hamstring, patellar tendon, or quadriceps) to restore knee stability. At four weeks post-operatively, patients naturally present with significant quadriceps inhibition, flexion contractures, and weakness — the surgical trauma to soft tissues, graft integration demands, and immobility during early healing all contribute to this neuromuscular deficit. The extension lag and quadriceps strength of 3+/5 seen here are typical findings at this stage and reflect ongoing motor inhibition rather than graft failure; likewise, hamstring weakness is expected given the harvest site and healing demands. Restoration of knee extension strength and achievement of full passive extension are the primary rehabilitation goals, as deficits at this stage are largely reversible with guided progressional loading and neuromuscular retraining.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old presents with sudden onset of unilateral facial drooping and weakness over 24-48 hours. Notices asymmetry in mirror, difficulty closing eye on affected side, and mild discomfort behind the ear.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot whistle or puff cheeks evenly. Drooling from corner of mouth on affected side. Difficulty with facial expressions—smiling appears lopsided. Eye waters excessively or feels dry depending on position.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previously healthy. No trauma, surgery, or recent dental work. Symptoms appeared over one night without preceding illness or systemic complaints. No hearing loss, dizziness, or other neurological symptoms. Lives in temperate climate with seasonal outdoor exposure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Unilateral facial weakness affecting forehead, eye, and mouth—unable to elevate eyebrow or close eye completely on affected side. Intact sensation across face. No vesicles in ear canal. Mild hyperacusis on affected side. Taste intact. Other cranial nerves and motor/sensory exam normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows acute denervation pattern and reduced motor unit recruitment in affected facial muscles. Brain MRI unremarkable—no mass, stroke, or inflammation. Cerebrospinal fluid normal. No evidence of systemic infection on laboratory screening.

puzzles.explanation: Bell's palsy is an acute, idiopathic paralysis of the facial nerve (CN VII) affecting one side of the face, typically appearing over hours to days in previously healthy individuals. The sudden unilateral weakness across all branches—forehead, eye, and mouth—combined with intact sensation, normal imaging, and preserved taste reflects selective motor involvement of the facial nerve, distinguishing it from central stroke or mass lesions. The characteristic inability to close the eye and elevation of the eyebrow, along with hyperacusis from stapedius paralysis, are hallmark findings. Although the etiology remains debated, viral reactivation and inflammation of the nerve within the temporal bone are leading theories; most patients recover spontaneously within weeks to months, though early corticosteroids and eye care are critical to prevent complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged woman with intermittent hip pain after returning to recreational dancing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain during hip adduction and internal rotation; worse with repetitive flexion movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong hip tightness; no prior hip injury. Recently increased dance frequency and intensity over past 3 months before symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion passive range of motion 100 degrees with pain; positive impingement sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows labral fraying without full-thickness tear; acetabular version abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: Femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) occurs when abnormal anatomy of the femoral head or acetabulum causes mechanical conflict during hip motion, most commonly during flexion and adduction. This patient's lifelong hip tightness and acetabular version abnormality represent the underlying structural predisposition that remained asymptomatic until increased repetitive loading from intensified dancing triggered pain and labral irritation. The restricted hip flexion range and positive impingement sign reflect both the anatomical constraint and secondary capsular tightness that develops as a protective response. FAI is distinct from other hip pathologies in its mechanical nature—symptoms arise predictably with movements that narrow the femoroacetabular space, and early intervention with activity modification and targeted hip mobility work can prevent progression to labral tears and osteoarthritis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports ear pain, jaw stiffness, and headaches; symptoms worse in morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain provoked by talking and chewing; morning symptoms suggest nocturnal grinding.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of psychological stress and parafunctional habits; associated neck muscle tension and frequent tension-type headaches; no prior jaw surgery or significant dental work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Jaw deviation on opening, severe masticatory muscle tenderness, tooth wear visible, limited mouth opening.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows normal disc position with early degenerative joint surface changes; EMG demonstrates sustained nocturnal masseter and temporalis hyperactivity consistent with parafunctional grinding activity.

puzzles.explanation: Temporomandibular joint disorder (TMD) arises from dysfunction of the jaw joint and its surrounding musculature, often triggered or perpetuated by stress-related parafunctional habits such as clenching and grinding. The nocturnal grinding pattern evident on EMG, combined with morning jaw stiffness and tooth wear, reflects sustained muscular hyperactivity during sleep—a hallmark of TMD in psychologically stressed individuals. The jaw deviation on opening and masticatory muscle tenderness indicate compensatory movement patterns and myofascial involvement, which frequently extend to the cervical musculature, explaining the associated neck tension and tension-type headaches. Early degenerative changes on imaging suggest that chronic mechanical overloading from parafunctional activity is beginning to stress the joint surfaces, making stress management and habit modification central to preventing progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young female athlete reports persistent fatigue, mood changes, and recurrent lower limb injuries over past 6 months despite continued training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain usual training intensity; reports disproportionate fatigue on exertion and slow recovery between sessions despite high motivation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Training volume increased 40% over 3 months; coincided with deliberate caloric restriction for competition. Multiple stress fractures in past 4 months. No previous injury history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: HR and BP lower than expected for age; slow resting heart rate. Decreased muscle bulk in legs. Generalized weakness on manual muscle testing (4/5). Menarche at age 14, normal cycle until 8 months ago.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan: T-score −2.5 spine, −2.1 femoral neck (osteopenia range). Serum 25-OH vitamin D 18 ng/mL, iron studies low-normal, TSH elevated at 5.2 mIU/L.

puzzles.explanation: Overtraining syndrome (OTS) represents a maladaptive physiological response to excessive training stress without adequate recovery, characterized by persistent underperformance, fatigue disproportionate to training load, and systemic metabolic dysfunction. This condition arises from a combination of excessive volume, insufficient nutrition, and psychological stress — in this case, the 40% training increase coupled with caloric restriction created a severe energy deficit that triggered suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, evidenced by amenorrhea and the constellation of low bone mineral density, hypothyroidism, and low-normal iron stores collectively termed Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S). The bilateral stress fractures, generalized weakness, bradycardia, and mood disturbance reflect the systemic nature of the condition, where the body has shifted into a catabolic state prioritizing survival over athletic performance. Recovery requires a holistic approach: training reduction, energy availability restoration, micronutrient repletion, and psychological support to restore hormonal homeostasis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old reports progressive shortness of breath over 4 months. Initially noticed only with vigorous exercise; now occurs with normal daily activities. Also reports unusual fatigue and occasional ankle swelling by evening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot climb stairs without stopping to catch breath. Struggles to walk briskly on level ground. Swelling in ankles improves somewhat with leg elevation and overnight rest. Symptoms worse in evenings and when lying flat.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Treated for breast cancer 18 months ago with doxorubicin-based chemotherapy (4 cycles completed). No prior cardiac symptoms or hypertension. Recent weight gain of 3 kg over 2 months despite stable diet. No chest pain or palpitations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elevated jugular venous pressure (6 cm H₂O). Bilateral lower limb pitting edema to mid-shin. Displaced apex beat (6th intercostal space). Quiet S3 gallop at apex. Mild bilateral crackles at lung bases. Hepatojugular reflux present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiography shows reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF 38%). Dilated left ventricle (LVEDV 145 mL/m²). Grades of diastolic dysfunction. Chest X-ray demonstrates bilateral pulmonary edema with increased interstitial markings and mild cardiomegaly.

puzzles.explanation: Heart failure results from the heart's inability to pump sufficient blood to meet the body's metabolic demands, leading to systemic and pulmonary congestion. This patient's reduced ejection fraction (38%) and dilated left ventricle reflect dilated cardiomyopathy — likely chemotherapy-induced, as doxorubicin is a known cardiotoxic agent that damages myocardial contractility months to years after exposure. The clinical picture of progressive dyspnoea, orthopnoea, peripheral oedema, and elevated jugular venous pressure, combined with signs of pulmonary congestion (crackles, bilateral edema on imaging) and forward flow impairment (fatigue, exercise intolerance), is pathognomonic for left-sided heart failure with systemic venous backup. The S3 gallop and displaced apex beat further confirm the dilated, poorly contracting ventricle struggling to maintain adequate cardiac output.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Distance runner reports groin or anterior hip pain that worsens during long runs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to run without severe limp; pain worse with impact activities. Antalgic gait restricts hip extension and loading.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Significant training volume increase, female athlete with energy deficiency. Menarche delayed or amenorrhea.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Groin pain with passive hip flexion, adduction, and internal rotation (FADI). Anterior hip pain with hip extension and axial loading. Hip abductor weakness noted. Positive hop test reproduction of groin pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays may appear normal initially, CT or MRI shows stress fracture on femoral neck. Compression-side fractures seen.

puzzles.explanation: Femoral stress fractures are incomplete fractures of the femoral neck or intertrochanteric region resulting from repetitive submaximal loading that overwhelms bone remodeling capacity, particularly in female athletes with relative energy deficiency and hormonal disruption. The characteristic anterior hip and groin pain that worsens with running reflects the tension and compression forces across the femoral neck during weight-bearing, while the positive hop test and pain with hip extension and axial loading directly provoke these mechanical stresses. Delayed menarche or amenorrhea, as seen in this distance runner, represents a critical risk factor—the hypoestrogenic state impairs bone mineral density and slows remodeling, transforming normal training stress into a fracture risk. Compression-side fractures on the medial femoral neck are particularly concerning due to their poor blood supply; early detection on MRI or CT is essential, as these can progress to displacement without appropriate load management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult with insidious progressive back pain and progressive height loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain upright posture for prolonged periods; stooping and kyphotic positioning for comfort; difficulty walking distances due to postural fatigue.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple previous compression fractures, chronic condition, and associated with corticosteroid use.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Increased thoracic kyphosis with fixed deformity; acute midline tenderness over multiple thoracic vertebrae; no neurological deficit; pain reproduced on axial loading.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows multiple level compression fractures, significant kyphotic deformity, and dEXA scan confirms osteoporosis.

puzzles.explanation: Vertebral compression fractures result from collapse of the vertebral body due to insufficient bone mineral density, most commonly in the thoracic spine of older adults with osteoporosis. The insidious onset and progressive height loss reflect cumulative microfracturing and anterior wedging of vertebrae, while the development of fixed kyphotic deformity — apparent in the increased thoracic curve and stooped posture — is characteristic of advanced disease with multiple level involvement. Chronic corticosteroid use accelerates bone loss through suppression of osteoblast activity and increased osteoclast resorption, making this population particularly vulnerable to recurrent fractures. The absence of neurological deficit and reproduction of pain on axial loading help distinguish uncomplicated compression fractures from those with retropulsion causing cord compromise.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 72-year-old with sudden onset weakness and loss of sensation following a fall from standing height. Acute onset of limb heaviness and numbness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to move arms or legs voluntarily. Cannot grip objects, lift head off pillow, or roll in bed. Difficulty breathing deeply; respiratory effort appears shallow.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall occurred 2 hours ago without protective mechanism. Known osteoporosis. No prior spinal surgery, trauma, or neurological disease. Immediate paralysis from moment of impact.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Flaccid paralysis all four limbs. Complete loss of motor power below C5 level. Sensory level: pinprick and light touch loss below shoulders; preserved sensation at face and neck. Upper trapezius weak but functional. Lower extremity reflexes absent; biceps reflexes present and brisk. Respiratory: shallow breath sounds, paradoxical abdominal breathing pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine: fracture-dislocation at C4-C5 with posterior ligamentous disruption. Spinal cord shows T2 hyperintensity from C4 through C6 indicating acute cord edema and contusion. No hemorrhage. Alignment severely disrupted with cord compression.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical spinal cord injury at the C4-C5 level represents acute disruption of neural transmission in the cervical enlargement, typically from traumatic mechanism such as fracture-dislocation with severe compression. The sudden onset flaccid paralysis below the shoulders with preserved upper trapezius function reflects a motor level at C5, where the uppermost cervical roots still retain innervation to proximal shoulder stabilizers. The characteristic sensory level at the shoulder, combined with the paradoxical abdominal breathing pattern—indicating loss of lower intercostal and abdominal muscle control while diaphragmatic innervation (C3-5) remains intact—are hallmark signs of mid-cervical cord damage. The T2 hyperintensity on imaging reflects acute cord edema and contusion; prognosis depends critically on the degree of structural sparing and timing of decompression, making immediate immobilization and specialist evaluation essential.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 24-year-old athlete with acute knee pain and swelling. Onset 4 weeks ago after injury during skiing. Unable to tolerate walking and light activities without discomfort.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with dynamic control: cutting and pivoting movements cause sense of instability or 'giving way'. Single-leg stance poor.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury mechanism: hyperextension and sudden deceleration during skiing. Treated surgically with graft reconstruction 4 weeks ago. No prior knee surgery or ligament injury history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Knee flexion 125 degrees with soft end-feel, full extension achieved. Quadriceps 4+/5, hamstring 4/5. Anterior drawer test 1+ with firm endpoint. Lachman test mildly positive with firm endpoint. No effusion on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows intact patellar tendon graft with good incorporation. Knee shows normal alignment. No meniscal involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Post-ACL reconstruction represents the immediate post-operative phase following surgical graft placement to restore anterior knee stability, typically using autograft (patellar tendon, hamstring) or allograft material. At 4 weeks post-surgery, the graft is in early incorporation but remains biomechanically vulnerable; functional recovery requires progressive neuromuscular re-education, particularly proprioception and dynamic stabiliser control. The persistent difficulty with cutting and pivoting, poor single-leg stance, and residual strength deficits (quadriceps 4+/5) reflect the normal post-operative timeline, though the mild positive Lachman test with firm endpoint suggests the graft is beginning to function appropriately. Rehabilitation success depends on systematically restoring range of motion, muscular strength, and sport-specific control before return to high-demand activities — premature loading risks graft failure and re-injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged person with widespread musculoskeletal pain that comes and goes across the body.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with stress, cold weather, and poor sleep; improves with gentle movement and warmth.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over weeks following major life stressor; concurrent non-musculoskeletal symptoms including unexplained fatigue, poor concentration, and sleep disturbance. No prior significant medical history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tender points at characteristic sites (bilateral occiput, trapezius, supraspinatus, lateral epicondyle, gluteus, greater trochanter, knee). Normal power 5/5 throughout. Full active and passive ROM all joints. No focal neurological signs. Widespread allodynia to light palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: All standard imaging normal; diagnosis based on clinical criteria including widespread pain index.

puzzles.explanation: Fibromyalgia is a central sensitization syndrome characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain, amplified pain processing, and associated systemic symptoms including fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, and sleep disruption. Unlike structural pathology, fibromyalgia arises from altered nociceptive signaling in the nervous system, often triggered or exacerbated by psychosocial stressors — as evidenced by this patient's gradual onset following major life stress. The hallmark presentation of migratory pain across multiple body regions, severe allodynia on examination despite normal strength and range of motion, and clustering of tender points at characteristic sites (occiput, trapezius, supraspinatus, greater trochanter) are diagnostic benchmarks. Management pivots on patient education, graded aerobic exercise, and sleep optimization rather than imaging-driven approaches, making clinical recognition essential for appropriate physiotherapy intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old manual worker. Gradual onset low back pain over 6 months, now constant. Describes aching quality, no radiation. No trauma or acute injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with repetitive bending and lifting. Unable to sustain loads >15 kg. Prolonged sitting (>2 hours) increases symptoms significantly. Symptoms ease with rest and recumbency.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior back injury. No leg symptoms, numbness, or bowel/bladder changes. Symptoms developed gradually during same job role. Tried NSAIDs with minimal relief. No morning stiffness pattern.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar flexion reduced to 40° with pain onset at 20°. Marked bilateral hip flexor tightness. Core strength 3/5. Left-sided paraspinal muscle tenderness and guarding. Straight leg raise negative bilaterally. Lower limb reflexes and sensation normal. No neural tension signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI lumbar spine shows disc bulging L4-L5 and L5-S1 without neural compression. No foraminal stenosis. Mild facet hypertrophy. No significant degenerative changes. Findings correlate with activity-triggered mechanical pain pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Work-related lower back pain typically arises from cumulative microtrauma and mechanical overload in occupational settings, particularly in manual workers performing repetitive bending and lifting without adequate core stability or postural control. The gradual onset over months, aching quality without radiation, and clear mechanical triggers — worsening with loading and sustained flexion, improving with rest — are hallmarks of non-specific mechanical low back pain driven by occupational demands rather than neural compromise. The imaging findings of disc bulging without neural involvement, combined with examination signs of reduced flexion range, marked hip flexor tightness, and poor core endurance (3/5 strength), point to a movement dysfunction pattern where inadequate proximal stabilization loads the lumbar spine excessively. Management centres on progressive core rehabilitation, postural retraining, activity modification, and addressing workplace ergonomics — the NSAID poor response reflects the mechanical rather than inflammatory nature of this presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old man with progressive difficulty walking and cognitive slowing over 5 years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walks very slowly with walker, fatigue significantly limits activity, problems with attention.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset without clear relapses. History of single demyelinating event 6 years ago. Progressive worsening since.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Spastic paraparesis (MRC 3/5), hyperreflexia and extensor plantars bilaterally, positive Romberg test, marked slowing of thought processing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spinal MRI shows cervical cord atrophy; brain MRI shows extensive white matter changes and gadolinium-enhancing lesions.

puzzles.explanation: Multiple sclerosis is a chronic demyelinating disease of the central nervous system characterized by immune-mediated destruction of myelin sheaths, resulting in patchy lesions (plaques) throughout the brain and spinal cord. This patient exemplifies secondary progressive MS, where an initial demyelinating event is followed by gradual neurological decline without distinct relapses — the progressive accumulation of disability rather than episodic flare-ups distinguishes this from relapsing-remitting disease. The combination of spastic paraparesis, cognitive slowing, and cerebellar signs (positive Romberg) reflects multifocal white matter pathology, while cervical cord atrophy demonstrates axonal loss from chronic demyelination. Brain and spinal MRI showing extensive white matter lesions with gadolinium enhancement confirms active demyelination, the radiological hallmark of MS diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old male is referred to cardiac rehabilitation with breathlessness, exercise intolerance, and bilateral ankle swelling that worsens throughout the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Becomes breathless walking from the bedroom to the kitchen (NYHA Class III). Reports needing 3 pillows to sleep (orthopnoea) and waking at night gasping for air (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea).

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of myocardial infarction 5 years ago. Hypertension and type 2 diabetes. On multiple cardiac medications including ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, and diuretic.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elevated JVP. Bilateral pitting oedema to mid-shins. Bibasal fine crackles on auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows significantly reduced ejection fraction (severely depressed LV systolic function). BNP markedly elevated. Chest X-ray shows enlarged heart silhouette, upper lobe pulmonary venous redistribution, and bilateral pleural effusions.

puzzles.explanation: Congestive heart failure represents a state of inadequate cardiac output and elevated ventricular filling pressures, most commonly due to systolic dysfunction from prior myocardial infarction as in this case. The severely reduced ejection fraction impairs the heart's ability to eject blood forward, causing blood to back up into the pulmonary and systemic circulations — explaining the orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, and bibasal crackles from pulmonary congestion, alongside the peripheral oedema and elevated jugular venous pressure from systemic venous engorgement. The profound exercise intolerance and dyspnoea on minimal exertion reflect the mismatch between cardiac output and metabolic demand, particularly evident during activity. Elevated BNP and the characteristic imaging findings of cardiomegaly with pulmonary redistribution confirm the compensatory mechanisms and structural changes that accompany decompensated heart failure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old boy with insidious hip pain and antalgic gait; pain often referred to thigh and knee region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Antalgic gait, limitation of hip abduction and internal rotation. Weight-bearing difficult.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset, no trauma. Otherwise healthy, similar condition in maternal uncle.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Trendelenburg sign, hip flexion contracture. Limited internal rotation, pain on log roll test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows femoral head deformity with widened physis and early sclerosis; MRI reveals signal loss in femoral epiphysis consistent with bone ischemia.

puzzles.explanation: Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease is a self-limiting childhood hip disorder caused by transient ischemia of the femoral head epiphysis, leading to avascular necrosis and subsequent remodeling — typically affecting boys aged 4–8 with an insidious onset and no antecedent trauma. The condition classically presents with hip and referred thigh or knee pain, antalgic gait, and loss of hip internal rotation and abduction due to synovitis and early deformity, as seen in this young patient. The positive Trendelenburg sign reflects hip abductor weakness from pain inhibition and proximal femur dysfunction, while imaging reveals the pathognomonic femoral head deformity and epiphyseal signal loss indicating bone ischemia. Prognosis depends on age at onset, extent of epiphyseal involvement, and femoral head containment during the remodeling phase; family history (maternal uncle) suggests possible genetic predisposition to the vascular disruption.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Cancer survivor with gradually progressive numbness and burning pain in the feet, starting during treatment and continuing for 2 years after.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk barefoot on uneven ground, frequent falls, difficulty with fine motor tasks like fastening buttons, sleep disrupted by foot pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Single-agent platinum exposure for gynecological malignancy completed 2 years ago. Neuropathy onset coincided with treatment cycles, has not improved despite stopping chemotherapy. No prior peripheral neuropathy or diabetes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked sensory loss distally, weakness of foot intrinsics, absent reflexes, protective gait, hyperesthesia to touch.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG/NCS shows distal lower-limb axonal sensorimotor neuropathy with reduced amplitudes and normal conduction velocities, consistent with length-dependent pattern. No active denervation.

puzzles.explanation: Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) develops when neurotoxic agents—particularly platinum compounds—cause direct mitochondrial injury and axonal degeneration in sensory and motor neurons. The distal, length-dependent pattern seen here is characteristic: longer axons are affected first, explaining why symptoms begin in the feet and spare proximal muscles. Notably, CIPN often persists long after treatment cessation, as the structural axonal damage does not spontaneously reverse; the marked sensory loss, absent reflexes, and distal weakness reflect this cumulative neurotoxic insult. Management focuses on symptom control and fall prevention, as functional recovery is typically limited—making early rehabilitation intervention critical to maintaining mobility and quality of life.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Sudden severe pain in the back of the lower leg during a pushing or jumping movement. Patient heard or felt a pop.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk normally. Refuses to put weight on the leg and drags foot when attempting to move. Cannot plantarflex the ankle actively.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient was pushing off to jump or sprint when injury occurred. No prior ankle or calf problems. Age 45.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Thompson test, palpable gap in tendon, complete loss of plantarflexion strength and severe swelling with ecchymosis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI confirms tendon discontinuity and imaging shows acute rupture with gap between tendon ends. Hemorrhage visible.

puzzles.explanation: Achilles tendon rupture is a complete or partial tear of the largest tendon in the body, typically occurring during eccentric loading of a plantarflexed ankle—such as the explosive push-off during jumping or sprinting. The classic presentation is a sudden sharp pain with an audible or palpable pop, followed by immediate loss of plantarflexion strength and inability to rise onto the toes, as the muscle can no longer transmit force through the damaged tendon. In middle-aged patients without prior tendon pathology, rupture often reflects degenerative changes and reduced vascularity in the tendon substance that accumulate over time. The Thompson test—absence of plantarflexion when the calf is squeezed—remains the most specific bedside finding, while ultrasound or MRI reveals the discontinuity and hemorrhage characteristic of acute rupture.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old with gradual onset of ankle discomfort and stiffness over 3-4 months. No recent trauma recalled. Pain described as deep, achy, worse at end of range in multiple directions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressive loss of ankle mobility affecting gait. Difficulty going up stairs and walking on uneven ground. Symptoms worse with attempts to stretch or move ankle to end-range; little relief from rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset without clear injury. Minimal improvement despite 2 months of standard ankle strengthening and stretching. No significant swelling, redness, or systemic symptoms. No prior ankle surgery or prolonged immobilization.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked restriction in plantarflexion, dorsiflexion, inversion, and eversion. End-feel capsular (hard, non-elastic). Ankle mortise and subtalar joints both restricted. Pain at extreme ranges. No effusion, no instability, neurovascular intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs normal. MRI shows synovial proliferation and fibrosis within talocrural joint capsule with thickened joint lining. No fracture, no cartilage loss, no ligament rupture.

puzzles.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis of the ankle is a primary inflammatory fibrosis of the talocrural joint capsule that leads to progressive stiffness and loss of motion in multiple planes—a condition distinct from post-traumatic stiffness because it occurs without antecedent injury or immobilization. The pathophysiology involves synovial proliferation and collagen deposition within the joint capsule, creating the characteristic hard end-feel and global restriction in plantarflexion, dorsiflexion, inversion, and eversion seen here. This condition typically affects middle-aged individuals and progresses insidiously over weeks to months, with pain that worsens at end-range and minimal response to standard strengthening and stretching protocols—reflecting the structural nature of capsular fibrosis rather than muscular dysfunction. MRI findings of synovial thickening and capsular fibrosis, combined with the pattern of multi-planar restriction and capsular end-feel on examination, confirm the diagnosis and distinguish it from ligamentous injuries, effusions, or osteoarthritis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older patient with chronic knee pain and recurrent episodes of giving way during daily activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Stairs, uneven terrain provoke instability, patient has adapted with activity avoidance, and no recent acute injury recalled.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Remote injury from years prior, progressive compensatory movement patterns, and concurrent symptoms of mechanical catching.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive anterior drawer and Lachman tests with firm endpoints. Moderate quadriceps atrophy. Reports brief episodes of knee 'locking' or catching sensation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows degenerative appearance of ACL remnant with bone marrow edema and secondary osteoarthritis changes.

puzzles.explanation: Anterior cruciate ligament tears result from disruption of the primary restraint to anterior tibial translation, and while often from acute pivoting injuries, chronic ACL insufficiency develops insidiously when the initial injury goes unrecognized or untreated. The knee loses dynamic stability during weight-bearing directional changes, leading to recurrent episodes of giving way on stairs and uneven terrain — exactly the provocative activities that demand dynamic control. Over years, persistent anterolateral translation drives secondary osteoarthritis and creates mechanical symptoms like catching from developing osteophytes and meniscal changes, while quadriceps atrophy reflects both disuse and arthrogenic muscle inhibition from chronic effusion. The positive anterior drawer and Lachman tests with firm endpoints, alongside MRI evidence of a degenerative ACL remnant with bone marrow edema, confirm the chronic nature and secondary joint damage characteristic of long-standing untreated ACL insufficiency.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 9-year-old girl presents with recurring joint pain affecting multiple joints and fatigue. Her parents report she bruises easily and has always seemed more flexible than her peers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Ability to perform unusual contortions, frequent joint dislocations that worsen with movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong excessive flexibility noted in childhood; multiple family members with similar presentation and skin fragility. Prior treatments have been mainly supportive.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Beighton score 7/9; marked passive range of motion in fingers, thumbs, and trunk flexion. Skin appears thin and translucent over joints with visible venous pattern. Multiple small scars and residual bruising on forearms and shins.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows no structural joint damage or ligamentous injury despite recurrent dislocations. Genetic testing unavailable in routine practice; diagnosis based on clinical criteria and family history consistent with heritable connective tissue disorder.

puzzles.explanation: Hypermobility syndrome, or Ehlers-Danlos syndrome hypermobility type (hEDS), is a heritable connective tissue disorder caused by defects in collagen synthesis or structure, resulting in abnormal joint laxity and fragile skin and blood vessels. The condition presents with generalised hypermobility—quantified by the Beighton score—combined with a triad of joint pain, easy bruising, and skin hyperextensibility that reflects the systemic nature of the collagen defect. The early onset in childhood, family clustering, recurrent dislocations without structural damage, and the characteristic thin, translucent skin with visible vasculature seen here are hallmarks of the condition. Management is primarily preventive and supportive, focusing on activity modification, proprioceptive training, and avoiding high-impact loading that precipitates symptomatic dislocations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent muscle pain in multiple body areas; symptoms don't follow a consistent nerve or dermatomal pattern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain sustained posture or repetitive activity; symptoms worsen with prolonged muscle engagement but improve with rest and movement variation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms developed insidiously over months to years; patient reports significant psychological stress and poor sleep quality; multiple specialists consulted with no structural diagnosis identified.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Widespread taut bands, multiple trigger points in different muscles, variable referred pain patterns.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Normal nerve conduction studies and imaging; symptoms don't follow dermatome distribution.

puzzles.explanation: Myofascial pain syndrome is a chronic musculoskeletal condition characterized by hypersensitive trigger points within taut bands of skeletal muscle, causing localized and referred pain that does not follow dermatomal or peripheral nerve distributions. Unlike neuropathic conditions, it develops insidiously through a combination of muscle overload, postural dysfunction, and psychological stressors — the poor sleep and chronic stress seen here are common perpetuating factors — resulting in pain that improves with activity variation and manual release rather than absolute rest. The presence of widespread trigger points with variable referral patterns across multiple muscle groups, combined with normal imaging and nerve conduction studies, is pathognomonic; this distinguishes MPS from structural pathology or neural compression. Central sensitization and altered motor control patterns maintain the condition, making a multimodal approach addressing both the physical trigger points and the underlying perpetuating factors essential to recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 78-year-old with limited mobility. Reports tender, painful area on lower back that has worsened over the past 3 weeks. Denies trauma. Area feels warm and looks red.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Spends 20+ hours daily in bed or wheelchair due to stroke. Cannot reposition independently. Pain worsens with pressure and improves only when weight is fully relieved from affected area.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Immobile for 4 months post-stroke. Poor oral intake, albumin low. Incontinent of urine. History of diabetes. Skin care and repositioning attempted but inconsistently maintained due to staffing limitations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Well-demarcated erythematous area 6 cm diameter over sacrum. Skin surface intact in centre but area feels boggy; shallow fluid-filled blister or very thin skin layer visible. Surrounding skin blanches with pressure; central area does not. Non-purulent serous drainage on dressing. No exposed subcutaneous tissue or bone. Temperature normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows fluid collection in dermis/subcutaneous tissue with intact skin barrier at some points and partial-thickness loss at others. No fascial involvement, no gas in tissues. Consistent with partial-thickness skin loss with shallow undermining.

puzzles.explanation: Pressure injuries develop from prolonged mechanical loading that compromises microcirculation, typically over bony prominences in immobilized patients; Stage 2-3 injuries represent partial-thickness skin loss with dermal involvement but without full-thickness necrosis or exposed fascia. This patient's sacral injury is characteristic of the high-risk post-stroke population—prolonged immobility, poor nutritional status (low albumin), incontinence, and diabetes all impair tissue resilience and healing capacity. The clinical hallmark here is the mixed presentation: central areas of partial-thickness loss with serous drainage alongside surrounding erythema that blanches, reflecting the transition zone between compressed and viable tissue. Early recognition and aggressive pressure redistribution remain critical at this stage, as continued loading will inevitably progress to full-thickness involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 16-year-old boy with progressive difficulty climbing stairs and rising from chairs over the past 2 years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Recently started using wheelchair for community mobility; reports shortness of breath on minimal exertion and occasional chest discomfort with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset age 12–13 with insidious progression. No trauma or viral illness preceding symptoms. Progressive decline despite physiotherapy and bracing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe proximal and generalized weakness, minimal movement in lower extremities, cardiac murmur, respiratory muscle weakness. Parents note similar pattern in maternal uncle (deceased at age 20).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows dilated cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction and EMG shows myopathic pattern. Elevated CK >40,000.

puzzles.explanation: Muscular dystrophy is a group of inherited genetic disorders characterized by progressive degeneration and weakness of skeletal, cardiac, and smooth muscle due to mutations affecting proteins critical for muscle fiber integrity—most commonly dystrophin in Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD). The classic presentation in adolescent males includes proximal weakness (difficulty rising from chairs and climbing stairs), relentless progression despite conservative management, and early involvement of cardiac and respiratory muscles, as reflected here by the dilated cardiomyopathy and exertional dyspnea. The markedly elevated creatine kinase (>40,000 U/L), myopathic EMG pattern, and family history of early male mortality are pathognomonic features that distinguish this from other myopathies. Early cardiac and pulmonary involvement significantly impacts prognosis and reinforces why multidisciplinary management—including cardiac monitoring and respiratory support—is essential to optimize outcomes in these patients.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged patient with ankle pain and swelling after a twisting injury during walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate uneven ground or lateral ankle weight-bearing; ankle becomes stiff and swollen with activity despite normal ROM in plantarflexion and dorsiflexion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 3 days ago. No prior ankle injuries or instability. No systemic disease or inflammatory history. No current medications affecting healing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lateral ankle tenderness,, anterior drawer test positive, limited range of motion with inversion, and plantarflexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays rule out fracture and ultrasound shows ligament. MRI demonstrates partial ligament tear with surrounding inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Lateral ankle sprains result from inversion injuries that stretch or tear the anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL) and other lateral ligament structures, most commonly occurring during weight-bearing activities on uneven surfaces. The classic presentation includes immediate lateral swelling and tenderness with pain on inversion and plantarflexion — the movements that load the injured ligaments — while dorsiflexion and plantarflexion ROM in neutral ankle position remain relatively preserved. The positive anterior drawer test indicates ligamentous laxity from the partial tear, explaining the functional instability on uneven ground and the patient's reluctance to bear weight laterally despite only three days post-injury. This acute presentation with clear mechanism, ligamentous inflammation on imaging, and absence of prior instability distinguishes a straightforward acute sprain from chronic ankle instability or other structural pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient presents with posterior knee pain and vague 'heaviness' in the lower leg during prolonged sitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms exacerbated by activities requiring sustained knee flexion and minimal acute instability but functional impairment noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Remote or poorly recalled mechanism; patient notes gradual onset of calf swelling and tightness over weeks to months. Minimal initial trauma recall.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Posterior tibial sag at 90° knee flexion; posterior drawer test positive but less pronounced than expected for acute injury. Quadriceps atrophy and weakness with positive lag sign. Hip external rotators weak.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows PCL fibre disruption or avulsion from tibial insertion; secondary posterolateral knee OA and posterior capsular thickening. No ACL or meniscal involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) tears result from disruption of the primary restraint to posterior tibial translation, often from a mechanism so subtle or remote that patients may not recall a specific injury — distinguishing them from acute ACL tears with clear traumatic recall. The chronic presentation here, with vague posterior knee heaviness during flexion and gradual calf swelling, reflects the posterolateral restraint insufficiency and compensatory muscular dysfunction rather than acute instability; patients often adapt functionally because the PCL is inherently more forgiving than the ACL when intact secondary restraints remain. The positive posterior tibial sag and modest posterior drawer sign, combined with quadriceps weakness and hip external rotator insufficiency, reveal the functional cascade: PCL incompetence reduces dynamic knee stability, forcing reliance on muscle activation that fatigues with sustained flexion. Secondary posterolateral osteoarthritis and capsular thickening develop over months to years, indicating chronic micromotion and altered load distribution through the tibiofemoral joint.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Progressive lower back pain with bilateral leg symptoms and functional decline over recent weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worsening despite rest, night prominent, and progressive functional decline.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent spinal pathology with constitutional symptoms (weight loss, fever). Acute worsening over past 5 days despite analgesia.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lower limb neurological exam: grade 4/5 bilateral hip flexors and ankle dorsiflexors. Saddle anaesthesia present. Diminished anal tone. Lower limb reflexes variable. Straight leg raise bilaterally limited by pain at 45 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows metastatic disease with epidural spinal cord compression at lumbar spine.

puzzles.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome results from compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots within the spinal canal, most commonly from disc herniation, epidural abscess, tumour, or haematoma — in this case, metastatic disease causing epidural compression. It represents a neurosurgical emergency characterised by bilateral lower limb neurological deficits (weakness, sensory loss), saddle anaesthesia, and bowel/bladder dysfunction reflecting involvement of multiple nerve roots. The progressive bilateral symptoms with saddle anaesthesia and diminished anal tone are pathognomonic; these findings indicate involvement of sacral roots (S3-S5) responsible for perianal sensation and sphincter control. Urgent MRI and decompressive surgery within 48 hours are critical to prevent permanent neurological damage, as functional recovery depends heavily on timing of intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Adolescent athlete with anterior knee pain and localized swelling below the kneecap, worse with jumping and sprinting during a period of rapid growth.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to play sport comfortably; pain worsens with running, jumping, and ascending stairs. Symptoms plateau during periods of slower growth but flare with accelerated growth spurts.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Involved in basketball and volleyball for 2+ years; recently increased training intensity. Reports bilateral quadriceps and hamstring tightness. No prior knee injury or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Prominent tibial tuberosity with localized tenderness, knee extension against resistance reproduces, and palpable apophyseal prominence.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays reveal irregular fragmentation and calcification at the tibial tuberosity apophysis. MRI shows bone marrow edema and soft tissue inflammation localized to the apophyseal insertion without intra-articular pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is a traction apophysitis of the proximal tibia caused by repetitive tensile loading at the patellar tendon insertion during periods of rapid skeletal growth, most common in adolescent athletes aged 12-16. The condition arises from a mismatch between bone growth velocity and soft tissue lengthening; as the femur and tibia lengthen rapidly, the quadriceps and patellar tendon become relatively tight, creating excessive traction stress on the still-ossifying tibial tuberosity apophysis. Symptoms like pain with jumping and running that plateau during slower growth but flare with growth spurts are pathognomonic, reflecting the biomechanical vulnerability during these periods. The localized swelling, irregular apophyseal fragmentation on imaging, and reproduction of pain with knee extension against resistance—as seen in this athlete—confirm the diagnosis, while the absence of intra-articular involvement helps exclude meniscal or ligamentous pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports pain at the base of the great toe, worse with prolonged walking and footwear pressure.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens significantly with walking on hard surfaces and prolonged standing; patient avoids certain footwear due to discomfort at medial foot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual development of deformity and family history common. Often begins in adolescence or early adulthood may worsen with pregnancy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible medial deviation of great toe and bony prominence at first metatarsal head. Bursa inflammation may be present possible second toe crowding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays show increased intermetatarsal angle, hallux valgus angle, joint degenerative changes possible, and assess first metatarsal position.

puzzles.explanation: Hallux valgus is a progressive first ray deformity characterized by lateral deviation of the great toe and medial prominence of the first metatarsal head, resulting from a combination of biomechanical dysfunction, ligamentous laxity, and genetic predisposition. The condition typically emerges in adolescence or early adulthood and is accelerated by systemic factors like pregnancy and hormonal changes that increase ligamentous laxity. Pain arises primarily from the medial eminence being compressed against rigid footwear and from altered first metatarsophalangeal joint mechanics, which explains why symptoms worsen with prolonged walking on hard surfaces and specific shoe types. Radiographic assessment of the intermetatarsal and hallux valgus angles guides severity classification and surgical candidacy, while examination findings of visible deformity, possible bursal inflammation, and secondary toe crowding help distinguish it from other forefoot pathologies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old with acute onset knee pain and swelling after a fall from height landing on one leg. Pain is sharp and persistent; unable to bear weight comfortably on the affected side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk without significant pain and antalgic gait. Knee swells rapidly after activity. Pain worsens with weight-bearing and any attempt to straighten the leg fully. Sitting with knee flexed provides some relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism: fell 8 feet onto flexed knee. Injury occurred 3 weeks ago. History of osteoporosis (on calcium supplementation only, no bisphosphonates). Immediate swelling and ecchymosis. No prior knee surgery or instability episodes before this event.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant joint effusion present. Pain at joint line on palpation. McMurray's test negative. Lachman and anterior drawer tests negative. Valgus and varus stress tests stable. ROM limited by pain; flexion contracture of 15 degrees. Crepitus felt on passive extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT knee shows depressed fracture of lateral tibial plateau with 8mm step-off of articular surface. Comminution present in posterolateral corner. No soft tissue entrapment. Bone density reduced consistent with osteoporosis; minimal healing callus at 3 weeks post-injury.

puzzles.explanation: A tibial plateau fracture is an intra-articular fracture of the proximal tibia that disrupts the articular surface and typically results from high-energy axial loading—such as a fall from height onto a flexed knee. The lateral plateau is most commonly involved, and the depressed articular surface with comminution seen here creates mechanical incongruity that drives joint effusion, pain with weight-bearing, and the persistent flexion contracture characteristic of this injury. Osteoporosis significantly increases fracture risk and impairs healing, explaining the minimal callus formation at three weeks despite the substantial initial trauma. The absence of ligamentous laxity on stress testing helps distinguish this from complex knee injuries involving soft-tissue damage, making tibial plateau fracture a primarily structural problem requiring careful reduction to restore articular alignment and prevent early-onset osteoarthritis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old man presents with radial-sided wrist pain and functional loss following a fall six months ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports pain and weakness with gripping and twisting activities; avoids weight-bearing through the wrist.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall from height was the inciting event. Patient reports persistent wrist stiffness with no history of inflammatory arthritis or prior hand surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked tenderness in the radial wrist anatomical space, restricted radial wrist deviation, and significantly diminished grip strength. Scaphoid compression test positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT imaging demonstrates scaphoid non-union with sclerotic fracture margins. MRI reveals avascular necrosis of the proximal pole.

puzzles.explanation: Scaphoid fractures result from axial loading through a dorsally extended wrist, most commonly during falls on an outstretched hand, and carry a high risk of complications due to the bone's retrograde blood supply from distal to proximal. The proximal pole is particularly vulnerable to avascular necrosis when fracture healing is delayed or non-union develops, as occurred in this patient six months post-injury. The radial-sided tenderness, positive scaphoid compression test, and loss of grip strength with radial deviation reflect both the fracture site and secondary arthritic changes from non-union and AVN. Early diagnosis and immobilization are critical; missed or untreated scaphoid fractures frequently progress to non-union and degenerative wrist arthropathy, making prevention of functional decline a key rehabilitation goal in chronic presentations like this.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Office worker reports eye strain, dry eyes, and headache after prolonged screen time.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen with prolonged computer use; improve with reduced screen time and frequent breaks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Unable to maintain near vision comfort for extended periods; symptoms persist despite distance breaks, but resolve with overnight rest.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced tear break-up time, conjunctival injection, accommodation lag, dry eye findings.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Normal ophthalmologic imaging; dry eye testing confirms reduced tear production or quality.

puzzles.explanation: Computer vision syndrome (also called digital eye strain) arises from the sustained demand of near-focus work on screens, which reduces blink rate and increases tear film evaporation while simultaneously fatiguing the ciliary muscles responsible for accommodation. The hallmark presentation—eye strain, dryness, and headache triggered by prolonged screen use but resolving with rest—reflects the mismatch between visual demand and the eye's compensatory capacity; reduced tear break-up time and accommodation lag are the telltale signs of this overload. Unlike refractive or pathologic eye disease, imaging remains normal, making the clinical history and tear findings diagnostic. Management focuses on ergonomic modification, frequent blinking cues, and the 20-20-20 rule (every 20 minutes, look 20 feet away for 20 seconds) to allow ciliary and lacrimal recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 67-year-old with hypertension reports sudden onset of heart pounding and shortness of breath. Symptoms began at rest during evening. No chest pain. Denies recent illness, trauma, or medication changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Episodes of palpitations occur unpredictably—both at rest and with minimal exertion. Patient reports feeling lightheaded. Symptoms do not consistently worsen or improve with body position, breathing pattern, or activity level.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset 2 days ago, sudden and persistent. Past medical history: hypertension (10 years), chronic kidney disease stage 3. No prior cardiac events, syncope, or arrhythmia. Currently on antihypertensive medication. No recent infections or fever.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Heart rate 110 bpm, irregularly irregular rhythm palpated at radial pulse. Blood pressure elevated at 156/94. Apex beat difficult to locate. Lung bases clear bilaterally. No peripheral edema. Capillary refill normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: 12-lead ECG: absent P waves, irregular RR intervals with irregularly irregular QRS complexes, fibrillatory baseline at 300-600 bpm. Troponin negative. Chest X-ray normal. Transthoracic echo shows normal ejection fraction but left atrial enlargement.

puzzles.explanation: Atrial fibrillation is a supraventricular arrhythmia caused by disorganized electrical activity in the atria, resulting in loss of coordinated atrial contraction and an irregularly irregular ventricular response. It commonly develops in patients with chronic hypertension and renal disease, both risk factors for atrial remodeling and enlargement. The hallmark presentation—sudden palpitations, an irregularly irregular pulse, absent P waves on ECG with a fibrillatory baseline, and left atrial enlargement on imaging—reflects the chaotic atrial activity and loss of atrioventricular synchrony. While hemodynamically well-tolerated in this case (preserved ejection fraction, normal troponin), AF carries significant risk for thromboembolism and requires rate control and anticoagulation to prevent stroke.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Child presenting with heel pain that worsens with sports activity, particularly running and jumping. Pain localised to posterior heel.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate sustained running or jumping. Symptoms improve with activity reduction but return within days of resuming sport. No night pain or resting symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occurs during growth spurts, typically 8-14 years old. High activity level, tight gastrocnemius-soleus complex.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness at calcaneal insertion of Achilles, pain with passive dorsiflexion. Tight calf muscles, pain with squeezing heel.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays may show fragmented apophysis, MRI shows apophyseal inflammation. Self-limited condition resolving with growth plate closure.

puzzles.explanation: Sever's disease is a traction apophysitis of the calcaneal growth plate, occurring when repetitive pull from the Achilles tendon exceeds the biomechanical tolerance of the developing apophysis during rapid skeletal growth. It's the most common cause of heel pain in active children aged 8–14, particularly those with tight gastrocnemius-soleus complexes and high training loads. The hallmark presentation—posterior heel pain with activity that resolves with rest but recurs upon return to sport, combined with tenderness at the Achilles insertion and pain with passive dorsiflexion—reflects the inflammatory response of the incompletely ossified apophysis to tensile stress. Importantly, this is a self-limited condition that resolves as the apophysis fuses with the body of the calcaneus, typically by age 15, making activity modification and flexibility work the cornerstone of management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent dizziness and imbalance 6 weeks after an acute viral illness. Initial severe spinning sensation has largely resolved, but positional symptoms and difficulty with spatial orientation remain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports feeling unsteady in crowded or visually complex environments. Gait on uneven surfaces is slow and cautious. Recovers well with rest and visual fixation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute viral illness 6 weeks ago with initial severe vertigo and nausea. Hearing decline noted; audiometry confirms high-frequency sensorineural loss. No recent head trauma or recurrent episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Dix-Hallpike now negative, Spontaneous nystagmus absent, Romberg test positive but improved from acute phase, Gait shows cautious pattern but not severely restricted, Dynamic gait testing shows deficits with head turns.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows mild residual labyrinthine enhancement with overall resolving inflammation. No central lesions or structural abnormality. Audiometry confirms high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss consistent with inner ear injury.

puzzles.explanation: Labyrinthitis is inflammation of the inner ear (labyrinth) typically triggered by viral infection, causing damage to both the vestibular system and cochlea. The acute phase produces severe vertigo and nausea as the vestibular nuclei and semicircular canals become irritated, but in the post-acute phase—as seen here at 6 weeks—patients experience persistent imbalance, positional symptoms, and high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss reflecting permanent inner ear tissue injury. The characteristic recovery with visual fixation and slower resolution on dynamic tasks reflects compensation through visual and proprioceptive systems as the acute inflammation resolves, though vestibular damage remains. The combination of post-viral onset, audiovestibular loss on testing, and resolving but persistent labyrinthine enhancement on imaging confirms the diagnosis and explains why patients require vestibular rehabilitation to optimize remaining sensory integration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old reports recurrent shoulder dislocation episodes with minimal provocation, recently increasing in frequency.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to throw or lift arm overhead without fear of dislocation. Certain arm positions (particularly abduction and external rotation) trigger immediate apprehension and guarding.

clues.label: History
clues.text: First dislocation occurred 5 years ago, initially requiring manual reduction at ED; now dislocates several times monthly with trivial movements. No significant trauma history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive apprehension test and relocation test. Positive sulcus sign. Generalized ligamentous laxity noted on examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows labral damage with Hill-Sachs lesion, glenoid bone loss. Chronic instability changes.

puzzles.explanation: Glenohumeral instability results from loss of capsular and labral restraint around the shoulder joint, allowing the humeral head to subluxate or dislocate from the glenoid fossa—most commonly anteriorly. The recurrent, increasingly frequent dislocations with minimal provocation reflect chronic capsular stretching and labral damage; once the static and dynamic stabilizers are compromised, even routine movements in abduction and external rotation can trigger instability. The positive apprehension and relocation tests are clinical hallmarks, while imaging findings of labral tears, Hill-Sachs lesions (compression fractures on the humeral head), and glenoid bone loss reveal the cumulative mechanical damage from repeated episodes. Generalized ligamentous laxity supports an underlying connective tissue predisposition, making this patient particularly vulnerable to recurrent instability despite conservative measures.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Road cyclist reports hand numbness affecting ring and little fingers after long rides.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms reproducible with sustained grip on drop bars; relief occurs with wrist extension or lateral deviation, and with padded gloves. Cannot sustain long rides without symptom escalation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive over 6 months with increased training volume and hours on bike. No trauma or prior hand/wrist injury. No systemic symptoms or neck pain. Symptoms worse with more aggressive riding positions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's test over ulnar nerve at wrist, weakness in intrinsic hand muscles, sensory loss in ulnar distribution.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show ulnar nerve slowing at wrist; ultrasound shows compression site.

puzzles.explanation: Cyclist's palsy is a compression neuropathy of the ulnar nerve at the wrist, typically affecting long-distance cyclists who maintain sustained grip on drop bars with the heel of the palm bearing pressure on the handlebars. The ulnar nerve becomes compressed against the hamate bone and pisiform as it passes through the canal of Guyon, particularly when the wrist is held in the extended, slightly deviated position characteristic of aggressive cycling postures. The classic presentation of ring and little finger paraesthesia with intrinsic hand muscle weakness reflects the mixed motor and sensory involvement distal to the compression site. Unlike carpal tunnel syndrome, cyclist's palsy responds specifically to postural modification, padded gloves, and reduced compression loading—making prevention through proper bike setup and equipment the cornerstone of management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 30-year-old female presents with unilateral left-sided buttock pain that she points to with one finger over the posterior superior iliac spine.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: is worse with prolonged standing, walking uphill, and rolling over in bed. Relieved by sitting. Reports when stepping up onto a curb with the left leg.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 4 months postpartum. Vaginal delivery with prolonged second stage of labour. Reports her pelvis felt 'unstable' since delivery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Three or more SIJ provocation tests positive: compression test, thigh thrust (posterior shear), Gaenslen's test, and sacral thrust. Positive active straight leg raise (ASLR) test bilaterally. Lumbar spine examination unremarkable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays are normal. MRI shows mild bone marrow oedema at the left SIJ without erosions or sclerosis, consistent with inflammatory changes rather than structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Sacroiliac joint dysfunction results from aberrant movement or excessive laxity at the SIJ, often triggered by the dramatic hormonal and mechanical changes of pregnancy and delivery. The postpartum presentation here is classic — prolonged labour and vaginal delivery stretch the pelvic ligaments, and the lingering effects of relaxin combined with altered neuromuscular control of the deep stabilizers (particularly the multifidus and transverse abdominis) leave the joint hypermobile and prone to pain. The localized buttock pain at the PSIS, exacerbation with unilateral weight-bearing activities like step-ups, and the cluster of positive provocation tests reflect the biomechanical overload and segmental instability. Importantly, the normal imaging with only mild marrow oedema excludes structural damage — this is a functional disorder of motor control and stability, not arthropathy, and responds well to targeted core stabilization and SIJ bracing rather than imaging-driven interventions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old male tradesperson presents with anterior knee pain and swelling of two weeks duration. Symptoms began without a specific injury incident.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with kneeling on knees; cannot kneel for extended periods without significant discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms triggered by work activities involving sustained kneeling. No acute injury recalled; symptoms developed insidiously over several weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable swelling anterior to proximal tibia; tenderness localized deep to patellar tendon. No effusion in knee joint; passive knee flexion reproduces anterior pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates bursal enlargement with fluid collection.

puzzles.explanation: Infrapatellar bursitis is inflammation of the bursa situated between the patellar tendon and the proximal tibia, typically triggered by repetitive direct pressure or friction rather than acute trauma. This condition is occupational in nature, particularly common in workers who spend prolonged periods kneeling — the sustained compression irritates the bursal lining and promotes fluid accumulation. The anterior swelling localized deep to the patellar tendon, combined with pain on kneeling but absence of joint effusion, distinguishes infrapatellar bursitis from intra-articular pathology; ultrasound confirmation of bursal enlargement is reliable and helps rule out other sources of anterior knee pain. Management typically involves activity modification, pressure relief (cushioned kneeling pads), and anti-inflammatory strategies, with most cases resolving within 4-6 weeks once the irritant stimulus is removed.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes × 12 years reports a non-healing wound on the sole of their foot that began 8 weeks ago. Patient notes drainage from the site and difficulty walking. No clear memory of initial injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight on affected foot. Significant functional limitation with ambulation and balance. Drainage soaks through dressings daily. Pain is minimal despite wound size and appearance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Wound onset unclear—patient discovered lesion rather than recalling trauma. HbA1c 9.2% at last visit. Known peripheral neuropathy for 3 years. Prior foot ulcer on opposite foot 5 years ago, healed with conservative care. No recent vascular interventions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Large ulcer (≥2 cm) on plantar surface with slough and exposed tissue. Surrounding erythema and induration present. Monofilament test: unable to sense 5.07g in forefoot and toes bilaterally. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses palpable but diminished. Ankle-brachial index 0.68.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Foot X-ray shows bone demineralization and loss of cortical definition at metatarsal heads. MRI reveals T2 signal hyperintensity in subcutaneous tissues and marrow edema. Probe-to-bone test positive. Bone culture grows methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus.

puzzles.explanation: Diabetic foot ulcers result from the convergence of peripheral neuropathy, microvascular insufficiency, and biomechanical stress in patients with long-standing hyperglycemia—a triad that fundamentally undermines tissue integrity and healing capacity. The loss of protective sensation (monofilament testing positive at 5.07g) means repetitive pressure and minor trauma go unnoticed, while impaired perfusion (ABI 0.68, diminished pulses) and glycemic control (HbA1c 9.2%) limit the inflammatory and reparative response. This case exemplifies the classic presentation: a large plantar ulcer with minimal pain despite depth, positive probe-to-bone indicating osteomyelitis, and imaging changes of osteopenia—hallmarks of neuropathic rather than purely ischaemic ulceration. Recurrence and chronicity, as seen with this patient's prior contralateral ulcer, underscore why multidisciplinary management addressing glucose control, offloading, infection, and vascular status is essential to break the ulcer cycle.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old woman presents with widespread joint pain and fatigue. Reports dizziness when standing up quickly and has noticed her skin is unusually stretchy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with sustained standing and aerobic activities. Fatigue out of proportion to exertion level. Symptoms worsen significantly with physical activity despite attempting graded exercise.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong history of joint sprains and dislocations. Multiple hospitalizations in childhood for unexplained bleeding. Family history of hypermobility and connective tissue problems. No recent trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Beighton score 7/9. Skin velvety and translucent with visible veining. Tachycardia (104 bpm) at rest, blood pressure drops 18 mmHg on standing. Joint palpation reveals generalized laxity without inflammation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Genetic testing confirms EDS; imaging shows degenerative changes despite young age.

puzzles.explanation: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is a heterogeneous group of heritable connective tissue disorders caused by defects in collagen synthesis, structure, or processing, resulting in abnormal elasticity and fragility across multiple tissues. The syndrome presents with characteristic hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, and tissue fragility — the triad evident in this patient's high Beighton score, velvety translucent skin, and lifelong history of sprains and bleeding — alongside autonomic dysfunction such as orthostatic intolerance and disproportionate fatigue. Early-onset degenerative changes and postural orthostatic tachycardia are common secondary features reflecting the underlying collagen defect and resulting joint instability. Management requires a conservative, cautious approach to exercise with emphasis on proprioceptive training and avoidance of high-load or hypermobility-promoting activities, as traditional graded aerobic exercise can paradoxically worsen symptoms in this population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old with severe initial COVID requiring ICU admission reports ongoing dyspnea and reduced exercise tolerance 6 months later.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot return to work due to dyspnea with stairs. Exercise intolerance marked. Persistent cough.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe COVID with pneumonia and 10 days mechanical ventilation. Post-discharge: progressive fatigue worsened by minimal exertion, night sweats, and brain fog. Gradual decline over 6 months despite apparent medical recovery. No prior cardiopulmonary disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Decreased respiratory muscle strength. Able to walk 200 meters before dyspnea onset. Oxygen saturation drops to 92% with exertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest CT shows resolved pulmonary edema but persistent ground-glass opacities suggesting ongoing inflammation. PFTs show reduced DLCO and exercise-induced desaturation.

puzzles.explanation: Long COVID is a post-acute sequela of SARS-CoV-2 infection characterized by persistent multisystem symptoms—dyspnea, fatigue, cognitive impairment, and exercise intolerance—that persist for weeks to months after the acute phase, even in those who appeared to recover. The pathophysiology remains incompletely understood but involves persistent viral RNA, microvascular thrombosis, autonomic dysregulation, and prolonged inflammatory states affecting pulmonary, cardiovascular, and neurological systems. This patient's presentation is classic: severe initial infection with mechanical ventilation, followed by disproportionate dyspnea on minimal exertion, exercise-induced desaturation, reduced diffusion capacity, and persistent ground-glass opacities indicating ongoing alveolar-capillary inflammation.
