Post-exertional malaise and exertional intolerance, rather than simple deconditioning, are hallmarks that distinguish Long COVID and often worsen with inappropriate high-intensity rehabilitation, making graded, symptom-limited activity crucial in physiotherapy management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Cyclist with chronic pain at ischium; activity-dependent discomfort.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worse during intervals and climbs; improved with cadence reduction and seat height adjustment.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She has a background of recurrent sacroiliac joint pain and recently increased her cycling training volume by 40% over eight weeks. No prior hamstring injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized ischial tenderness, positive Puranen-Orava test. Pain with resisted hip flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic swelling and vascularity within proximal hamstring tendon at ischial insertion; dynamic imaging reproduces pain with hip flexion against resistance.

puzzles.explanation: Proximal hamstring tendinopathy is a degenerative condition affecting the musculotendinous junction of the biceps femoris, semitendinosus, and semimembranosus as they insert on the ischial tuberosity, typically triggered by rapid increases in load or repetitive hip flexion–extension cycles. Cyclists are particularly vulnerable because seated pedalling creates sustained hip flexion combined with forceful hamstring engagement during power phases, especially in climbing and high-intensity intervals where load demands spike. The 40% training increase over eight weeks in this case created a classic overload scenario; combined with her history of sacroiliac dysfunction—which may predispose to altered hip mechanics and proximal hamstring compensation—the ischial insertion became a failure point. Puranen-Orava positivity and ultrasonographic evidence of tendon thickening with neovascularization confirm the inflammatory–degenerative process, distinguishing this from acute muscle strain or referred sacroiliac pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old male with progressive pain in hands and feet, associated with skin changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Morning stiffness lasting over 1 hour. Pain primarily in DIP joints of hands and forefoot. Symptoms improve with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Skin lesions preceded joint symptoms by approximately 1 year. No prior joint disease. Exacerbations correlate with high stress periods. Father has inflammatory arthritis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Psoriatic skin plaques on extensor surfaces. Nail pitting and onycholysis visible. Ray-like swelling of fingers (sausage digits).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows pencil-in-cup deformities and osteolysis. Asymmetric joint involvement. MRI shows inflammation and bone erosions.

puzzles.explanation: Psoriatic arthritis is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy that develops in 5–10% of patients with psoriasis, typically affecting the DIP joints and showing asymmetric distribution — a pattern distinct from rheumatoid arthritis's symmetric MCP involvement. The condition reflects a dysregulated immune response to shared skin and joint antigens, explaining why cutaneous manifestations often precede joint symptoms by months to years, as occurred in this case. The characteristic features here — morning stiffness with activity-dependent improvement, sausage digit swelling from diffuse joint and tendon inflammation, and aggressive erosive changes like pencil-in-cup deformities on imaging — make PsA one of the most destructive arthritides if left untreated. Family history of inflammatory arthritis and stress-triggered exacerbations underscore the genetic predisposition and environmental sensitivity underlying this systemic condition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with severe heel pain following a fall from standing height.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain with heel weight-bearing, massive swelling. Unable to ambulate.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism (3-metre fall onto hard surface). Immediate swelling and inability to weight-bear from time of injury. No significant comorbidities or prior heel pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe diffuse heel swelling with ecchymosis. Loss of normal heel contour. Positive bulge sign. Severe pain on passive ankle dorsiflexion and palpation of heel. Calf tenderness present; compartment syndrome suspected clinically.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan shows intra-articular comminution, loss of Bohler's angle. Subtalar involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Calcaneal fractures result from high-energy axial loading of the heel, typically following falls from height or crush injuries, causing the talus to drive downward through the calcaneus and creating intra-articular comminution. The severity of this injury explains the profound weight-bearing pain, massive soft-tissue swelling, and loss of the normal heel contour—the posterior tuberosity is crushed and depressed, reducing Böhler's angle and disrupting the subtalar joint surface. The presence of calf tenderness and clinical concern for compartment syndrome reflects the substantial trauma to the surrounding tissues; calcaneal fractures frequently cause compartment syndrome in the plantar foot compartments due to interstitial edema from the crush mechanism. Early recognition of this injury pattern is critical, as complications including post-traumatic arthritis, chronic heel pain, and limb-threatening compartment syndrome determine long-term functional outcomes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with hand, numbness in ring, and little fingers progressing over months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform pinch grip or manipulate small objects. Difficulty with finger spreading and coordinated finger movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset over several months. Occupational: prolonged computer work with elbow in flexion. No acute trauma or significant injury history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Interosseous atrophy, weakness of finger abduction and adduction, sensory loss in ulnar distribution, Froment sign positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows slowing across elbow, ultrasound shows nerve thickening at cubital tunnel, and mRI shows nerve compression.

puzzles.explanation: Ulnar nerve palsy results from compression of the ulnar nerve, most commonly at the cubital tunnel behind the medial elbow, where prolonged flexion loading and direct pressure can progressively impair nerve conduction. The insidious onset over months reflects a chronic compression neuropathy rather than acute trauma; occupational positioning with the elbow flexed, as in prolonged computer work, is a classic risk factor. Clinically, ulnar palsy presents with weakness of the intrinsic hand muscles — manifesting as impaired finger abduction/adduction and pinch grip — combined with sensory loss in the ring and little fingers. The interosseous atrophy, positive Froment sign (flexion of the IP joint of the thumb during pinch due to loss of adductor pollicis function), and EMG evidence of slowing across the elbow confirm compression at the cubital tunnel as the site of pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young patient develops foot pain and limited ankle motion during adolescence.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During walking and sports, patient cannot invert or evert the foot; ankle feels stiff and rigid rather than mobile.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset age 10-14, family history of foot problems, and progressive symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Special tests reveal severely restricted subtalar motion in isolation; ankle dorsi/plantarflexion preserved. Foot appears persistently flat with rigid midfoot. Heel varus present on weight-bearing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan shows talocalcaneal or talonavicular coalition and osseous or fibrocartilaginous bridge.

puzzles.explanation: Tarsal coalition is an anatomical anomaly where two or more tarsal bones are abnormally united by an osseous, fibrocartilaginous, or cartilaginous bridge, most commonly between the talus and calcaneus or talus and navicular. The condition typically becomes symptomatic during adolescence as the coalition ossifies and growth plates close, progressively restricting subtalar and midfoot motion and causing the foot to function as a rigid unit rather than a segmented mechanism. The characteristic presentation of insidious onset pain with loss of inversion and eversion in the setting of preserved ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion—along with a rigid flat foot and heel varus—reflects the biomechanical consequence of this fixed midfoot deformity. CT imaging remains the gold standard for diagnosis, revealing the bridge and its composition, while family history is common given the genetic predisposition to this structural abnormality.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Teenager reports mild back discomfort and notices uneven clothing fit.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient continues to participate fully in sports and physical education without restrictions or symptom exacerbation during activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Curve detected at age 10, slowly progressive over 3 years, and still growing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left lumbar scoliosis with rib hump, mild pain with spinal palpation, and flexible curve.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serial X-rays show Cobb angle progression from 22 to 35 degrees and immature skeletal age.

puzzles.explanation: Idiopathic adolescent scoliosis is a lateral spinal curvature of unknown aetiology affecting roughly 2–3% of adolescents, typically identified during growth spurts and most common in females. The condition is characterised by progressive deviation in the coronal plane—here demonstrated by the Cobb angle increase from 22 to 35 degrees—with risk of further progression directly tied to skeletal maturity and curve magnitude. Notably, the flexible nature of the curve and the patient's ability to remain fully active without symptom exacerbation distinguish this from structural or pain-driven presentations; adolescents often report only mild discomfort or cosmetic concerns like uneven clothing fit. Serial imaging monitoring remains essential at this stage, as curves between 25 and 50 degrees in skeletally immature individuals carry substantial progression risk and may warrant bracing to slow advancement during the remaining growth period.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with severe back pain unrelieved by position changes and nocturnal pain present.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain upright posture; symptoms persist despite rest and positional modifications.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fever and systemic malaise present. Risk factors include immunosuppression, recent infection, IV drug use, or recent spinal procedure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Midline vertebral tenderness over affected segment. Possible lower extremity neurological deficit or myelopathic signs if cord compression present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows disc space narrowing, vertebral body involvement, and inflammatory signal changes with endplate involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Spondylodiscitis is an infection of the intervertebral disc and adjacent vertebral bodies, most commonly caused by haematogenous spread of bacteria (often Staphylococcus aureus) in immunocompromised patients, those with recent instrumentation, or IV drug users. The condition presents with severe, relentless back pain that persists regardless of position or rest — distinguishing it from mechanical low back pain — coupled with systemic signs of infection such as fever and malaise. MRI findings of disc space narrowing, vertebral endplate involvement, and inflammatory signal changes are pathognomonic, reflecting the infectious process destroying disc architecture and bone. The presence of neurological deficits, as may occur here, indicates cord compression from abscess formation or severe inflammation, warranting urgent imaging and often antibiotic therapy ± surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 38-year-old right-handed office worker. Wrist pain with intermittent swelling. Onset gradual over past 2 years. No systemic symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with gripping and sustained typing. Clicking or clunking sensation during wrist movement. Symptoms worse with radial/ulnar deviation. Can tolerate light activities but heavy lifting limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall on outstretched hand 2 years ago. Initially treated conservatively with rest and splinting. Symptoms improved initially then plateaued; never fully resolved. No prior wrist injury. No inflammatory arthropathy history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive midcarpal clunk test with radial deviation. Grip strength reduced 20-30% compared to opposite side. Palpable clunking during passive wrist motion in midcarpal region. Pain on axial thumb loading. Wrist ROM mildly restricted in extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Wrist X-rays (AP, lateral, scaphoid views): normal carpal alignment, no fracture lines, no scaphoid waist gap, no SLAC pattern. MRI: partial tear of dorsal intercarpal and radiocarpal ligaments; preserved scaphoid integrity; mild synovitis without cartilage loss.

puzzles.explanation: Wrist sprains result from ligamentous injury to the intrinsic carpal ligaments or extrinsic radiocarpal structures, commonly following falls on an outstretched hand (FOOSH) mechanism. This patient's chronic presentation reflects a partial ligamentous tear affecting dorsal intercarpal and radiocarpal ligaments—sufficient to cause persistent instability and synovitis, but without the progressive degenerative pattern seen in scapholunate dissociation or midcarpal instability. The characteristic midcarpal clunk during radial deviation and palpable clunking on passive motion reflect dorsal ligament laxity allowing abnormal carpal translation, which mechanically loads the scaphoid—explaining pain with axial thumb loading and grip weakness. The initial improvement followed by plateau is typical of incomplete ligament healing: adequate scar formation to reduce acute inflammation, but insufficient to restore normal intercarpal stability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Healthcare or animal facility worker reports progressive respiratory symptoms at work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms resolve on days off and weekends, recur upon return to workplace.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive sensitization over months to years and initial symptoms mild but worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Baseline forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1) reduced; significant reversibility or fluctuation noted across shift timings; wheezing audible during work-shift samples.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Peak flow diary shows ≥20% variability between work days and non-work days; specific IgE elevated to workplace-relevant allergen; baseline spirometry shows obstructive pattern consistent with chronic exposure effect.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational asthma is workplace-induced airway hyperresponsiveness triggered by repeated inhalation of a specific sensitizing agent—in this case, an allergen encountered in healthcare or animal facility settings. The condition develops through a progressive sensitization process over months to years, initially presenting with mild symptoms that worsen with continued exposure. The classic clinical hallmark is temporal relationship: symptoms resolve completely during time away from work (weekends, holidays) and recur predictably upon return, as reflected in the peak flow variability and shift-dependent wheezing. The reduced baseline FEV1 with significant reversibility indicates established airway obstruction with an allergic inflammatory component, confirmed by elevated workplace-relevant IgE, distinguishing this from non-occupational asthma and making workplace exposure identification and removal critical to preventing permanent airway remodelling.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient woke unable to lift hand at wrist. Arm was in unusual compressed position during sleep. No preceding trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot extend wrist or fingers. Wrist held in flexion (droops downward). Has numbness over back of hand and thumb.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset after sleeping on arm in compressed position across armchair. No trauma, no prior episodes. No sensory or motor symptoms before sleep.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist drop present. Finger extension severely weak (grades 1-2). Elbow extension normal strength. Sensory loss confined to first web space and dorsal thumb. No other neurological deficits distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows conduction block at mid-humerus and ultrasound shows nerve compression. X-ray normal.

puzzles.explanation: Radial nerve palsy results from compression of the radial nerve as it winds around the mid-humerus, most commonly from prolonged external pressure such as sleeping with the arm draped over a chair or crutch use — a presentation known as 'Saturday night palsy.' The nerve's superficial course at the spiral groove makes it vulnerable to compression injuries that typically spare more proximal motor branches, explaining why elbow extension remains intact while wrist and finger extension are paralyzed. The characteristic wrist drop, weakness of finger extension in the absence of intrinsic hand involvement, and sensory loss limited to the dorsal first web space (radial nerve territory) make this pattern clinically distinctive. Conduction block on EMG at the compression site and ultrasound confirmation of nerve narrowing establish the diagnosis and prognosis; most cases recover spontaneously within 6-12 weeks as the acute compression resolves.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports chest wall pain, described as sharp or aching. Chronic onset over weeks to months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Movements involving trunk rotation and deep breathing exacerbate pain; difficulty with overhead reaching and lifting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset following repeated heavy lifting 3 months ago. No acute trauma. Previous similar episodes resolved with rest. No systemic illness or referred symptoms from viscera.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Focal tenderness over lateral chest wall, between ribs 4–6. Pain reproduces with palpation and resisted trunk rotation. Normal neurological examination. Costochondral junctions non-tender.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates muscle oedema and minor scarring in external intercostal muscles at symptomatic level with no nerve root compression; confirms musculoskeletal origin without fracture or pneumothorax.

puzzles.explanation: Intercostal muscle strain involves microtears or inflammation of the intercostal muscles between the ribs, typically caused by repetitive loading, heavy lifting, or forceful trunk rotation. The muscles are vulnerable in this region because they work eccentrically during trunk control and must stabilize during rotational movements, making them susceptible to cumulative microtrauma. The characteristic sharp, activity-dependent pain with exacerbation on rotation and deep breathing reflects the mechanical irritation of these muscles, which are active during both movements; the focal tenderness over the lateral chest wall and positive resisted rotation test confirm muscular involvement rather than visceral or neural pathology. Most cases, as demonstrated here, resolve within weeks to months with load management and progressive mobilization, though scarring may predispose to recurrent episodes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old presenting with gradual-onset pain in the ball of the foot. Describes aching and burning sensation that worsens as the day progresses.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain aggravated by prolonged standing and walking, particularly in tight or narrow footwear. Symptoms ease with rest and wider-fitting shoes. No specific injury recalled.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 3 months ago without trauma. Patient reports a sedentary job but increased activity recently. No prior foot surgery or fracture. Pain has been gradually worsening despite activity modification and rest.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized tenderness over the 2nd and 3rd metatarsal heads. Reduced plantar intrinsic muscle strength bilaterally. Pain reproduced with compression across metatarsal heads (metatarsal squeeze test). ROM of great toe normal but plantarflexion-loaded positions uncomfortable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing anteroposterior foot radiographs show subtle narrowing of the 2nd-3rd metatarsophalangeal joint spaces and early plantar callus formation under those heads. MRI reveals chronic stress changes in the plantar plate with intact ligamentous structures. No fracture or arthropathy.

puzzles.explanation: Metatarsalgia is chronic pain at the metatarsal heads — the weight-bearing joints of the forefoot — typically arising from mechanical overload, intrinsic muscle weakness, or ligamentous strain rather than structural pathology. The gradual onset and bilateral reduction in plantar intrinsic strength here reflect the common mechanism: prolonged standing and walking cause cumulative microtrauma to the plantar plate and surrounding soft tissues, particularly at the 2nd and 3rd metatarsal heads which bear disproportionate load. Footwear constraint and plantarflexion-loaded activities are classic aggravating factors because they increase pressure across the metatarsal heads and reduce the shock-absorbing capacity of weak intrinsics. The characteristic finding of stress-related changes on MRI without frank fracture or joint degeneration distinguishes this from early arthropathy, making conservative management — rest, offloading footwear, and intrinsic strengthening — the foundation of treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 20-month-old child. Parents report child walks on outer border of foot. Foot appears twisted inward and downward. No history of trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child ambulates with weight-bearing on lateral foot surface. Difficulty achieving heel strike during gait. Appears to walk on toes and outer edge of sole.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Deformity present since birth. Parents received initial treatment recommendation in neonatal period but discontinued intervention at 6 months. No orthotic device used since treatment stopped.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Rigid forefoot adduction and inversion. Ankle plantarflexion present but limited dorsiflexion. Heel in varus position. Medial foot crease visible. Calf musculature atrophic. No associated pain or swelling.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing foot radiographs show talus deviated laterally, calcaneus in varus with increased pitch angle, navicular displaced medially. Metatarsals adducted relative to midfoot axis.

puzzles.explanation: Clubfoot (talipes equinovarus) is a congenital structural deformity involving four primary components: forefoot adduction, hindfoot varus, ankle plantarflexion, and midfoot cavus — resulting from abnormal development of the tarsal bones and their supporting soft tissues. The condition presents at birth as a visibly twisted foot with inward and downward positioning, and without early intervention (typically the Ponseti method or serial casting), the deformity becomes fixed and progressively rigid, as evidenced by this child's lateral weight-bearing pattern and limited dorsiflexion despite walking ability. The radiographic findings of lateral talus deviation, varus heel with increased calcaneal pitch, and medial navicular displacement reflect the underlying skeletal malalignment that perpetuates abnormal gait mechanics. Discontinuation of treatment at 6 months allowed the deformity to become entrenched; ongoing physiotherapy and orthotic support remain essential to prevent secondary changes and optimize functional ambulation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young person with acute chest wall pain, worse with movement and breathing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by deep breathing, coughing, and direct pressure to the chest wall. She avoids lying on her left side and finds relief only with complete rest and analgesia.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset over days without preceding trauma. Multiple investigations performed to rule out cardiac and pulmonary causes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible swelling and erythema at the costochondral junctions. Reproducible tenderness on direct palpation of affected cartilage. No cardiac or respiratory findings.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows no rib fractures, malignancy, or cardiac pathology. Costochondral changes consistent with benign inflammatory swelling.

puzzles.explanation: Costochondritis is inflammation of the costochondral junctions—the cartilaginous articulations between the ribs and sternum—typically presenting with acute onset chest wall pain that worsens with movement, breathing, and direct palpation. It is predominantly a clinical diagnosis, with reproducible tenderness at the costochondral cartilage being the hallmark finding, as seen in this case with visible swelling and erythema. The condition is benign and self-limiting, though its presentation—bilateral chest pain worse with deep breathing and coughing—often prompts cardiac and pulmonary investigations to exclude more serious pathology. Management is conservative, relying on activity modification, rest, analgesia, and occasionally NSAIDs or local corticosteroid injection, with most cases resolving within weeks to months.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient presents with persistent lateral ankle pain and feeling of ankle instability following an inversion sprain three months ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform lateral ankle proprioceptive tasks; difficulty with stairs and single-leg stance; gives way on direction changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Follows ankle inversion injury and persistent despite ankle rehabilitation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lateral hindfoot tenderness with deep palpation between talus and calcaneus; positive talar tilt and anterior drawer; pain with inversion stress; subtalar joint mobility reduced.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates oedema and inflammatory changes localized to subtalar coalition region with associated synovitis; weightbearing CT reveals subtle talocalcaneal rotation and reduced posterior facet alignment.

puzzles.explanation: Sinus tarsi syndrome is a chronic functional instability of the subtalar joint resulting from inversion injury to the ankle, characterized by inflammation within the sinus tarsi—the cone-shaped space between the talus and calcaneus. While the initial ankle sprain resolves, ongoing micromotion and synovitis at the subtalar joint perpetuate lateral hindfoot pain and proprioceptive deficits, explaining why traditional ankle rehabilitation alone fails to restore stability. The deep lateral tenderness, positive inversion stress tests, and reduced subtalar mobility reflect both local inflammation and mechanical dysfunction at this critical shock-absorbing articulation. Imaging typically reveals synovitis and subtle talocalcaneal malalignment that accounts for the persistent proprioceptive loss and instability on dynamic tasks like stairs and directional changes—a clinical presentation distinct from residual ankle ligamentous instability alone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 32-year-old female with insidious onset polyarticular pain and swelling in hands, wrists, and knees over 6 months. Associated fatigue, low-grade fevers, and unintentional weight loss (3–4 kg). No known trauma or recent infection.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant morning stiffness lasting 1–2 hours. Difficulty gripping objects and performing fine motor tasks. Symptoms worse with sustained activity; some joints improve with rest, others remain persistently swollen. Reports worsening with sun exposure.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms fluctuate over weeks and months. Family history notable for autoimmune disease. No prior joint trauma, infection trigger, or occupational exposure. Currently taking no disease-modifying therapies. Reports recurrent mouth ulcers and occasional facial redness in sun-exposed areas.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symmetric swelling and warmth at MCP and PIP joints bilaterally. Reduced grip strength (15 mmHg). No joint deformity or subluxation. Oral ulceration on hard palate noted. No nodules, effusions, or systemic lymphadenopathy palpated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain film radiographs of hands and wrists show no erosions, joint-space narrowing, or bone deformity. Positive ANA with anti-dsDNA antibodies and low complement levels (C3, C4). Negative rheumatoid factor. Urinalysis shows mild proteinuria and dysmorphic RBC casts.

puzzles.explanation: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a multisystem autoimmune disease driven by immune complex deposition and anti-dsDNA antibody production, predominantly affecting women of childbearing age. The polyarticular arthritis is typically non-erosive and symmetric in distribution—as seen in the MCPs and wrists here—and is often accompanied by constitutional symptoms, photosensitivity, and mucocutaneous features like oral ulcers and malar erythema. The presence of positive anti-dsDNA antibodies, low complement levels, and evidence of glomerulonephritis (dysmorphic RBCs and proteinuria) confirm active systemic disease; the fluctuating course and absence of rheumatoid factor further distinguish SLE from seropositive rheumatoid arthritis. Morning stiffness and functional grip loss reflect active synovitis, but the non-erosive radiographs are characteristic of lupus arthritis and carry a more favorable long-term prognosis for joint preservation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged female with sharp lateral hip pain after increasing walking distance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain reproducible with side-lying; aggravated by crossing legs while standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in running or gym activity; gluteal weakness noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Exquisite tenderness at trochanter, positive lateral hip pain test. Weak hip abduction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals gluteal medius tendinopathy with associated bursal inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome (GTPS) is a clinical syndrome of lateral hip pain arising from pathology of the gluteal tendons and/or their associated bursa, most commonly affecting middle-aged women with sudden increases in loading or those with underlying gluteal weakness. The characteristic sharp lateral pain, exquisite tenderness at the trochanter, and reproduction with side-lying and adduction-biased positions reflect the anatomical irritation of these structures, particularly the gluteus medius which is critical for pelvic stability during single-leg activities like walking and running. The gluteal weakness and tendinopathic changes seen on imaging distinguish GTPS from hip joint pathology, and its strong association with activity modification and muscle imbalance makes it highly responsive to targeted abductor strengthening and movement retraining. Unlike femoroacetabular impingement or labral pathology, GTPS pain is localized to the lateral hip and reproduced with load-bearing in the frontal plane rather than with hip flexion or internal rotation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old female office worker presents with pain in her left buttock that radiates down the back of her thigh. She has been treated for sciatica but her lumbar MRI was normal.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting, crossing legs, and climbing stairs. Driving is uncomfortable. Pain eases with standing and walking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms present for 4 months. No low back pain. No trauma but increased running recently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness on deep palpation reproducing buttock and leg symptoms. Positive FAIR test (flexion, adduction, internal rotation). Positive Pace sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI pelvis shows mild hypertrophy and oedema of the piriformis muscle with close relationship to the sciatic nerve. MR neurography suggests nerve irritation at that level.

puzzles.explanation: Piriformis syndrome results from compression or irritation of the sciatic nerve as it passes through or beneath the piriformis muscle in the gluteal region, often triggered by muscle hypertrophy, inflammation, or overuse—particularly from running. Unlike true lumbar radiculopathy, it presents with buttock pain and posterior thigh radiation despite a normal lumbar spine, making a normal MRI of the lumbar region a key diagnostic clue. The characteristic worsening with sitting, leg crossing, and stair climbing reflects the muscle's active role in these movements, while positions that relax the piriformis tend to provide relief. Specialized orthopedic tests (FAIR and Pace sign) combined with imaging evidence of piriformis hypertrophy and MR neurography showing nerve irritation at that level confirm the diagnosis and distinguish it from other causes of sciatica.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older individual with persistent lateral hip pain, sharp or burning in character, limiting activity and function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged standing, walking on inclines, and lying on affected side. No significant morning stiffness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic presentation; recent increase in activity or prolonged walking. No acute trauma or hip surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Trendelenburg sign; palpable tenderness over greater trochanter; pain with resisted hip abduction and external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickened bursa with fluid; gluteus medius tendon appears hypoechoic with areas of tendinopathy but no full-thickness tear.

puzzles.explanation: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome (GTPS) results from irritation and inflammation of the gluteus medius and minimus tendons and their associated bursa at the lateral hip, typically arising from chronic overload, muscle weakness, or repetitive activity rather than acute injury. The condition classically presents as sharp lateral hip pain worsening with sustained weight-bearing activities like prolonged standing and hill walking, particularly when lying on the affected side — positions that compress the bursa or increase abductor demand. The positive Trendelenburg sign and weakness on resisted abduction reflect underlying gluteus medius insufficiency, while imaging findings of bursal thickening and tendinopathy confirm the inflammatory process without full-thickness tear, distinguishing GTPS from acute tendon rupture. This condition is especially common in older adults with activity-related exacerbations and responds well to progressive loading and motor control rehabilitation of the hip abductors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Athlete with acute posterior thigh pain during sprinting or kicking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight through affected leg; limping or favoring limb immediately after injury and unable to resume activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No significant prior symptoms and high-speed activity or overstretching mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible bruising in posterior thigh and palpable gap or indentation in muscle belly. Positive straight leg raise and reproduction with hamstring stretch.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI confirms muscle tear with retraction and fluid collection.

puzzles.explanation: Hamstring strain is an acute muscle tear within the biceps femoris, semitendinosus, or semimembranosus, typically occurring when rapid knee extension overstretches an eccentrically loaded muscle during sprinting or kicking. The injury is most common in athletes during high-speed activities, particularly those requiring explosive acceleration or deep hip flexion with knee extension. The visible bruising and palpable gap in the muscle belly reflect the severity of the tear and associated hematoma formation — classic signs of acute muscle rupture rather than simple strain. Positive straight leg raise and hamstring stretch tests reproduce the pain by placing tension across the damaged tissue, while imaging reveals the characteristic muscle retraction and fluid collection within the torn fascicles, confirming the diagnosis and grading severity for rehabilitation planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with incidental finding of posterior knee mass and minimal or no symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Mass may feel tense or fuller with knee extension, may fluctuate with activity level, or patient notes transient swelling that resolves with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cyst present for extended period without change and no acute episodes. Underlying knee pathology may be minimal; often found incidentally on imaging ordered for other reasons.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable cyst without significant tenderness, primary knee examination largely normal, and size stable neurovascular intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI confirms simple cyst with no complex features. Primary knee joint may be normal or show minimal degenerative changes.

puzzles.explanation: A Baker's cyst is a benign synovial outpouching from the posterior knee capsule, typically originating from the gastrocnemius-semimembranosus bursa, that communicates with the joint space and fills with synovial fluid. Most commonly found in patients with underlying knee pathology — whether osteoarthritis, meniscal tears, or chronic inflammation — the cyst can persist asymptomatically for years, enlarging or tightening with knee extension and activity before resolving with rest. The key clinical feature is its often incidental discovery; many patients have no symptoms until the cyst ruptures or compresses surrounding structures, which explains why imaging frequently reveals a simple, uncomplicated fluid collection in an otherwise stable knee. Management is conservative in most cases, as the cyst often regresses once the underlying joint pathology is addressed.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old with leg pain and occasional numbness. Symptoms started gradually 8 months ago and have been worsening over the past 2 months despite initial improvement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain radiates down the leg with certain movements. Bending forward sometimes helps, sometimes doesn't. Pain worsens with repetitive activity. Difficulty with stairs and prolonged sitting or standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Had lumbar surgery (disc herniation repair) 18 months ago. First 6 months were good with steady improvement. Then gradual return of leg symptoms without clear trigger. No new trauma or falls.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Spinal movement is stiff and limited, worse in some directions. Leg strength variable and inconsistent on repeat testing. Reflexes normal. Sensory changes don't follow a single clear nerve pattern. Nerve stretch tests produce leg pain but pattern is unpredictable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows post-surgical scar tissue at the operative level with the nerve root appearing tethered or bound to surrounding structures. No new disc herniation. No significant canal stenosis to account for current symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Nerve root adhesion occurs when scar tissue forms around a nerve root following spinal surgery, tethering it to adjacent structures and restricting its normal mobility during movement. Unlike recurrent disc herniation or stenosis, adhesions produce variable, inconsistent neurological findings because nerve irritation depends on the degree of traction during specific movements rather than static compression — explaining why strength testing and nerve stretch responses fluctuate and don't follow a predictable dermatomal pattern. The biphasic history is characteristic: initial post-operative improvement as acute inflammation resolves, followed by gradual symptom recurrence as fibrosis matures and the tethered nerve becomes increasingly irritable with repetitive loading. MRI confirmation of tethered nerve root surrounded by scar tissue, absent new pathology, establishes adhesion as the mechanical culprit and guides conservative management toward mobility restoration and activity modification rather than re-operation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports heel pain that worsens after periods of rest, particularly noticeable with initial weight-bearing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate prolonged standing or running; symptoms improve transiently with calf stretching but recur with activity resumption.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset with increased running volume, associated with tight calves, and high-impact activities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Maximal tenderness along medial plantar fascia at heel insertion; pain reproduced with passive ankle plantarflexion and toe extension. Ankle dorsiflexion ROM limited to 5 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates plantar fascia thickness >4 mm at heel insertion with hypoechoic appearance consistent with inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Plantar fasciitis is inflammation of the plantar fascia, the thick fascia spanning the plantar surface from the calcaneal tuberosity to the metatarsal heads, typically triggered by repetitive tensile loading and inadequate tissue recovery. The condition characteristically presents with heel pain that worsens after rest periods—the classic 'first-step pain'—because overnight non-weight-bearing allows the fascia to contract and become more irritable; weight-bearing initially exacerbates this before temporary stretching provides relief. In this case, the restricted dorsiflexion and tight calves created excessive plantarflexion dominance, increasing chronic tension through the fascia at its insertion. Ultrasound findings of increased thickness and hypoechoic signal confirm the inflammatory response, distinguishing this from other heel pain mimics and guiding prognosis—most cases resolve with load management, calf mobility work, and gradual return to activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old female university student reports diffuse anterior knee pain that has gradually worsened over 2 months since starting running.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by going up and down stairs, squatting, and prolonged sitting with knees bent. Running downhill is worse than uphill.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No locking, giving way, or significant swelling. Increased her running volume by 40% over 3 weeks. Wears old running shoes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness on patellar facets with compression. Positive Clarke's test (patella grind). Increased dynamic valgus on single-leg squat.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray unremarkable. MRI shows no structural pathology — no cartilage defects, intact menisci and ligaments. Mild patellar lateral tilt on axial views.

puzzles.explanation: Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS) is a mechanical knee disorder arising from altered patellar tracking and load distribution across the patellofemoral joint, typically in young, active individuals. The condition is driven by a combination of biomechanical factors — weak hip abductors and external rotators leading to dynamic valgus, quadriceps imbalance, and anatomical predispositions like lateral patellar tilt — rather than structural joint damage. The characteristic presentation here of anterior knee pain aggravated by high-load flexion activities (stairs, squats, downhill running) and the clinical finding of increased dynamic valgus reflects the mechanism: aberrant patellar alignment increases pressure on lateral facets and alters patellofemoral contact stress. The normal imaging and absence of meniscal or ligamentous pathology are reassuring; PFPS management focuses on hip and quadriceps strengthening, load management, and footwear optimization rather than structural repair.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old presents with pain and swelling at the shoulder tip after direct blow.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with adduction across body (cross-body adduction); pain at top of shoulder.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mechanism was sudden collision; patient reports immediate onset. No prior shoulder injury or surgery. Unremarkable general health.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness at AC joint, positive AC joint compression test. Crossover test positive, assess grade of injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows AC joint displacement, assess for grade 1-3 injury. Comparison views helpful.

puzzles.explanation: The acromioclavicular (AC) joint is a planar synovial articulation between the distal clavicle and acromion, stabilized by the AC ligaments superiorly and the coracoclavicular ligaments inferiorly. Acute AC joint sprains result from direct trauma or fall onto the shoulder, causing ligamentous injury that ranges from simple capsular strain (Grade I) to complete superior and coracoclavicular ligament disruption with clavicular displacement (Grade III). The mechanism here — a direct blow causing immediate-onset pain at the shoulder tip — is classic, as is the positive cross-body adduction test, which compresses the joint by internally rotating the humerus. Radiographic assessment with comparison views is essential to grade the injury and determine the degree of clavicular displacement, which guides conservative versus surgical management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old with sudden severe elbow pain and visible deformity after a fall onto an outstretched arm during sport.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot flex or extend elbow; extreme difficulty locating elbow joint contour due to gross swelling and visible deformity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Immediate swelling within minutes. No prior elbow injury or instability. High-energy impact; patient did not attempt to relocate the elbow.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elbow held in 45° flexion, resistant to passive movement. Posterolateral bony prominence palpable. Radial and ulnar pulses present; sensory intact in radial, ulnar, and median distributions. No crepitus.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anteroposterior and lateral X-rays show posterior displacement of ulna and radius relative to humerus. Coronoid process and radial head intact. No associated fractures.

puzzles.explanation: Elbow dislocation occurs when high-energy trauma forces the ulna and radius posteriorly relative to the humerus, disrupting the articulations of the ulnohumeral and radiohumeral joints. This injury is most common in young adults after fall on an outstretched arm or direct posterior force, and presents with immediate gross swelling, severe pain, and loss of active and passive range of motion — the elbow classically held in partial flexion as muscles splint against further displacement. The posterolateral prominence palpable on examination reflects the displaced radial head and olecranon, while preservation of pulses and sensory function indicates intact neurovascular structures despite the severe deformity. Posterior dislocations without associated coronoid or radial head fractures, as in this high-energy mechanism, generally have a more favorable prognosis once reduced and stabilized.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Athlete reports sudden-onset pain in the groin region during a match, aggravated by explosive movements and change of direction.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with resisted leg crossing (adduction) and rapid acceleration; symptoms ease with rest but recur with return to sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute non-contact injury during acceleration or lateral movement; pain radiates along medial thigh.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Adductor tenderness, positive adductor squeeze test, pain with hip adduction against resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows muscle strain with hypoechoic area; MRI shows muscle fiber disruption and edema.

puzzles.explanation: Groin strain involves acute injury to the adductor muscle group—most commonly the adductor longus—typically occurring during explosive movements requiring rapid hip adduction or acceleration. The injury mechanism reflects the high eccentric loads placed on these muscles during sports like soccer, ice hockey, and rugby, where athletes must rapidly change direction or accelerate laterally. The characteristic presentation of sudden-onset medial thigh pain with difficulty in resisted leg crossing and positive adductor squeeze testing distinguishes this from other groin pathologies; imaging showing muscle fiber disruption and localized edema confirms the strain rather than referred pain from the hip joint or spine. Recovery depends on injury grade and rehabilitation compliance, with early identification and modified activity crucial to preventing chronic groin pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old reports progressive finger deformity over several years. Affected fingers feel stiff and are adopting an unusual resting posture. No acute injury or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with precise hand tasks like buttoning or picking up small objects. Cannot fully extend affected fingers at the middle joint, though some flexion remains possible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Longstanding inflammatory arthritis diagnosed 8 years ago. Fingers affected one at a time over past 5 years; now involves thumb and two fingers. Gradual progression despite medical management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: PIP joint held in fixed flexion (~90°), DIP joint in compensatory hyperextension. Active extension at PIP severely limited; passive extension also restricted. No focal trauma or skin breakdown noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-ray shows central slip tendon atrophy with PIP joint subluxation. MRI demonstrates central slip rupture at PIP level with intact lateral bands laterally displaced. Joint destruction consistent with chronic inflammatory disease.

puzzles.explanation: Boutonnière deformity results from rupture or attenuation of the central slip of the extensor digitorum tendon at the proximal interphalangeal joint, causing the PIP to flex while the intact lateral bands migrate volarly and lose mechanical advantage, driving compensatory DIP hyperextension. In inflammatory arthritis, chronic synovitis erodes and weakens the central slip over years, leading to the characteristic posture — a PIP joint 'buttonholed' into flexion with the lateral bands now acting as flexors of that joint rather than extensors. The progressive, multi-digit involvement and severe loss of active PIP extension seen here are hallmark features distinguishing this from acute traumatic rupture; early aggressive therapy to restore central slip tension may prevent fixed deformity, but established cases with joint subluxation become increasingly difficult to reverse. This condition exemplifies how inflammatory arthritis doesn't just attack joints — it systematically destroys the soft tissue architecture that controls hand function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old woman presents 10 weeks after surgery for breast cancer. Reports progressive shoulder stiffness and reduced arm mobility. No acute trauma or fall. Pain is dull and restrictive rather than sharp.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to raise arm overhead or reach across body. Difficulty with fastening bra, combing hair, or lifting light objects. Symptoms worse with repetitive overhead movements. No neurological symptoms (numbness, tingling).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Underwent modified radical mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection. Surgical wound healed well. Did not receive adjuvant radiation. No significant shoulder pathology prior to surgery. Minimal physiotherapy since discharge.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder internal rotation severely restricted (45° compared to 75° contralateral). External rotation mildly limited. Forward flexion reduced to 110°. No palpable mass or cord-like bands. Scapular dyskinesis noted. Strength 5/5 throughout. No sensory deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder ultrasound shows no rotator cuff tear, subacromial effusion, or biceps pathology. Axillary web syndrome not present on physical examination or imaging. MRI chest/axilla shows normal post-surgical changes only, no recurrence. Imaging findings support adhesive capsulitis secondary to surgical immobility.

puzzles.explanation: Post-mastectomy rehabilitation addresses the predictable loss of shoulder mobility and function that follows breast cancer surgery, particularly when axillary dissection disrupts neurovascular structures and surrounding soft tissues. The constellation of restricted internal rotation, scapular dyskinesis, and functional deficits in overhead and cross-body movements reflects adhesive capsulitis developing from surgical immobility and tissue trauma — a condition fundamentally different from rotator cuff pathology or neurological injury, which imaging and strength testing effectively exclude. Early intensive physiotherapy, incorporating gentle glenohumeral mobilization, scapular stabilization, and progressive loading, is critical during this window; delayed intervention significantly prolongs recovery and functional limitations. Understanding that this represents a mechanical restriction rather than neural or structural damage guides treatment toward active-assisted and active-resisted exercise, not protection or decompression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old female violinist presents with diffuse arm pain, numbness in two of her fingers, and a sensation of heaviness in her right arm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are provoked by prolonged overhead activities and playing violin for more than 20 minutes. Reports occasional colour changes in her hand during cold weather.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lean build with postural forward head and rounded shoulders. History of whiplash 2 years ago. Symptoms worsening with increased rehearsal schedule over 12 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Roos test (elevated arm stress test) at 90 seconds — reproduces symptoms and hand pallor. Positive Adson's test with diminished radial pulse. Tender anterior and middle scalenes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows a rudimentary cervical rib on the right. Doppler ultrasound demonstrates subclavian artery compression with arm abduction. Nerve conduction studies are normal.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic outlet syndrome (TOS) results from neurovascular compression as the brachial plexus and subclavian vessels pass through the thoracic inlet, between the anterior and middle scalenes, behind the clavicle, and across any anatomical variants like cervical ribs. This young violinist's presentation is classically TOS: overhead repetitive activity combined with postural dysfunction (forward head, rounded shoulders) compresses the neurovascular bundle, producing diffuse arm pain, selective paresthesia, and the hallmark vascular sign of hand pallor with positional maneuvers—all reproduced by the Roos and Adson tests. The presence of a rudimentary cervical rib provides an anatomical predisposition, while the history of whiplash suggests prior scalene irritation that lowered the compression threshold. Normal nerve conduction studies are typical in TOS, as the compression is often functional and positional rather than causing frank demyelination, making clinical examination and imaging findings the diagnostic cornerstones.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 36-year-old reports persistent fatigue, dyspnea, and cognitive difficulties. Symptoms began 4 months ago after a mild acute COVID-19 infection.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Profound fatigue worsening with minimal exertion and lingering for days (post-exertional malaise). Dyspnea on walking. Difficulty concentrating at work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Initially recovered from acute COVID-19 but failed to return to baseline function; decline occurred during apparent convalescence. Previously very active; now significantly limited. No other acute precipitant identified.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vital signs and resting SpO2 normal. Formal cognitive testing shows measurable impairment (not subjective complaint). Heart rate response to minimal exertion assessed—check for abnormal chronotropic response.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows no acute consolidation or effusion. High-resolution CT of the chest demonstrates mild ground-glass opacities in the lower lobes bilaterally. Pulmonary function tests show a mild restrictive and diffusion-capacity defect.

puzzles.explanation: Long COVID is a post-viral syndrome affecting 10–30% of COVID-19 survivors, characterized by persistent multisystem symptoms—fatigue, dyspnea, cognitive dysfunction, and autonomic instability—that emerge during apparent recovery and persist for weeks to months. The hallmark feature is post-exertional malaise: disproportionate symptom exacerbation following minimal physical or cognitive effort, often lasting days, reflecting potential mitochondrial dysfunction or impaired cardiopulmonary reserve. The constellation of measurable deficits here—objective cognitive impairment, abnormal heart rate response to exertion, and subtle pulmonary changes (ground-glass opacities with restrictive mechanics and reduced diffusion capacity)—distinguishes Long COVID from purely psychogenic fatigue and suggests underlying microvasculopathy and persistent inflammation. Rehabilitation must be carefully titrated to avoid triggering post-exertional crashes, making graduated, symptom-limited exercise prescription central to physiotherapy management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old presents with progressive chest discomfort and shortness of breath over the past 8 weeks. Denies chest trauma or recent illness. States symptoms began gradually and are worsening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dyspnea now limits walking >50 metres on flat ground. Unable to climb stairs without stopping. Reports occasional palpitations and irregular heartbeats. Symptoms persist at rest and with minimal exertion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recently completed 6 months of dual immunotherapy (checkpoint inhibitors) for metastatic melanoma, finishing 2 months ago. No prior cardiac disease, no prior anthracycline exposure. Developed inflammatory symptoms during treatment but was managed conservatively.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vitals: HR 104, BP 118/76, RR 22, SaO2 94% on room air. Lung auscultation clear bilaterally. Cardiac: irregular rate, no murmur. Peripheral edema absent. JVP normal. No signs of acute infection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ECG: diffuse ST-segment elevation with PR depression. Troponin I elevated at 4.2 ng/mL (ref <0.04). Echocardiogram: global left ventricular hypokinesis, ejection fraction 25%, no regional wall motion abnormality, normal valve function.

puzzles.explanation: Cardiotoxicity post-cancer treatment results from direct myocardial injury caused by immunotherapy agents, particularly checkpoint inhibitors, which can trigger fulminant myocarditis through immune activation. The global left ventricular dysfunction with reduced ejection fraction, diffuse ST changes with PR depression, and elevated troponin reflect acute myocardial inflammation rather than coronary ischaemia—a pattern distinct from anthracycline-induced cardiomyopathy, which typically emerges months to years later. The rapid progression of dyspnoea within 8 weeks of completing dual immunotherapy, combined with the constellation of inflammatory markers and systemic signs, is characteristic of immune checkpoint inhibitor–associated myocarditis, a life-threatening but potentially reversible complication that demands urgent cardiology involvement and consideration of immunosuppressive therapy. Early recognition in cancer survivors with new cardiac symptoms is critical, as aggressive management during the acute phase can substantially improve outcomes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old athlete. Sudden onset of ankle pain and swelling after a misstep during sport. Pain on the outer side of ankle. Onset within minutes of injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear full weight immediately after injury. Pain with walking and weight-shifting. Swelling prevents normal shoe fit. Pain worsens with inversion movements of the foot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Single inversion injury during athletic activity 48 hours ago. No prior ankle injuries. No significant medical history. No radiation of pain down leg or into foot. No reports of numbness or tingling.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Swelling and bruising over lateral ankle. Pain with palpation anterior and inferior to lateral malleolus. Anterior drawer test positive with pain. Inversion stress test reproduces pain and demonstrates mild translation. Internal rotation of tibia on fixed foot increases ankle pain. No neurological deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ankle X-rays (AP, lateral, mortise views) show no fracture or syndesmotic injury. MRI demonstrates high-signal edema in anterior talofibular ligament consistent with acute partial tear. No deltoid or syndesmotic ligament involvement. No osteochondral injury.

puzzles.explanation: Lateral ankle sprains result from inversion injury that disrupts the lateral ligamentous complex, most commonly the anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL), which is the primary restraint to inversion and anterior tibial translation. The acute presentation with immediate swelling, bruising over the anterolateral ankle, and positive anterior drawer and inversion stress tests reflects the structural disruption of these key stabilizers. The MRI confirmation of ATFL edema with partial tearing is characteristic of Grade II sprains, distinguishing this from milder Grade I ligamentous stretching or more severe complete ruptures affecting secondary stabilizers like the calcaneofibular ligament. Athletic populations sustain these injuries frequently due to high-demand dynamic loading, and early functional rehabilitation — rather than prolonged immobilization — is now the standard approach to restore proprioception and prevent chronic ankle instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Pregnant patient at 35 weeks gestation reports severe, debilitating pelvic pain affecting all mobility. Pain radiates to lower extremities. Significant impact on function and quality of life.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Ambulation severely limited; walking with severe antalgic gait. Unable to perform any household activities. Sleeping nearly impossible despite multiple positional changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain worsens significantly with weight-bearing and asymmetric leg movements (stairs, rolling in bed). Single-leg stance nearly impossible. Pain localises to pubic symphysis and sacroiliac joints.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant pain with any pelvic motion. Marked diastasis recti with functional weakness. Severe pelvic floor dysfunction with spasm.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Obstetric imaging confirms normal pregnancy progression. No fetal compromise. Pelvic imaging shows pubic diastasis of 3cm.

puzzles.explanation: Antenatal pelvic girdle pain (PGP) is a musculoskeletal condition arising from altered biomechanics, hormonal changes (relaxin-mediated ligament laxity), and increased load demands during pregnancy, typically emerging in the second and third trimesters. The condition is characterized by pain at the pubic symphysis and sacroiliac joints, exacerbated by weight-bearing and asymmetric movements like stair climbing or rolling in bed — the pelvis loses its rigid force-coupling, forcing compensation through weakened core musculature and pelvic floor. The severe functional impact seen here, with marked diastasis recti, pelvic floor dysfunction, and pubic diastasis exceeding 2 cm, reflects the progressive structural separation and neuromuscular inhibition that distinguish PGP from simple pregnancy-related back pain. Management focuses on load management, pelvic stability retraining, and targeted support rather than imaging-driven intervention, as structural findings alone do not predict symptom severity or recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with progressive pain and stiffness in the great toe, affecting walking. Pain worsens with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform push-off phase of gait or sustain propulsive activities. Pain with weight-bearing through medial forefoot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset insidious over years. Patient reports gradual progression despite activity modification. No recent trauma or acute event.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe dorsiflexion limitation, pain and stiffness predominate. First MTP joint swelling present, gait adapted for pain avoidance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays show advanced joint degenerative changes, significant osteophytes. Marked joint space loss, bone-on-bone appearance possible.

puzzles.explanation: Hallux rigidus is progressive osteoarthritis of the first metatarsophalangeal joint, typically developing insidiously over years due to repetitive microtrauma, biomechanical overload, or underlying joint incongruity rather than acute injury. The condition is clinically distinctive for its profound loss of dorsiflexion—the motion essential for normal push-off during gait—which forces patients to adopt compensatory walking patterns and severely limits propulsive activities. The degenerative changes visible on imaging, from early osteophyte formation to advanced bone-on-bone articulation, directly correlate with symptom progression and the mechanical stiffness that characterizes this condition. Unlike other forefoot pathologies, hallux rigidus specifically impairs the terminal stance and push-off phases of gait, making functional limitation during walking a hallmark presentation.

puzzles.answer: Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged patient with insidious-onset groin pain and progressive hip discomfort; no recent trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens progressively with weight-bearing and standing; night pain prominent and sleep-disrupting; functional decline over weeks to months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of steroid use, alcohol abuse, previous femoral neck fracture, or radiation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Antalgic gait, hip internal rotation severely limited and painful; positive FABER and FADIR tests; hip flexion contracture present; pain reproduced by combined hip flexion-adduction-internal rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Early MRI shows bone marrow edema and later stages show segmental collapse on X-ray.

puzzles.explanation: Avascular necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head occurs when the blood supply to the femoral head is interrupted, leading to bone death and eventual mechanical collapse—a devastating hip condition most commonly triggered by corticosteroid use, alcohol abuse, previous femoral neck fracture, or radiation therapy. The insidious groin pain and progressive functional decline reflect the natural history of the disease: early stages show marrow edema on MRI as ischaemic bone begins to necrose, while later stages progress to segmental collapse visible on radiographs. The severe limitation of hip internal rotation, positive hip flexion tests, and night pain are hallmark features reflecting the mechanical instability and synovial inflammation that develops as the femoral head structure deteriorates. Early diagnosis through MRI is critical since surgical intervention (core decompression, vascularized grafting) can halt progression if caught before collapse, whereas late-stage disease often requires hip arthroplasty.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with shoulder pain and progressive arm weakness 6 weeks after shoulder surgery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot initiate shoulder abduction; arm hangs limply at side. Significant functional loss despite minimal pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Iatrogenic nerve injury during shoulder surgery 6 weeks prior. No prior shoulder pathology. No improvement with conservative management over 6 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe deltoid weakness (grade 1/5), notable deltoid atrophy, sensory loss over lateral shoulder, normal rotator cuff strength.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows complete denervation of deltoid and ultrasound shows deltoid atrophy. MRI shows nerve compression at quadrangular space from post-surgical changes.

puzzles.explanation: Axillary nerve palsy results from compression or injury to the axillary nerve, most commonly as an iatrogenic complication during shoulder surgery when the nerve is stretched or compressed during retraction or positioning. The nerve's primary motor function is innervation of the deltoid muscle, making loss of shoulder abduction initiation the pathognomonic feature — patients cannot lift the arm away from the body and assume a characteristic hanging posture. The severe deltoid atrophy and complete denervation pattern on EMG following surgery reflect the nerve's vulnerability as it passes through the quadrangular space, where post-surgical haematoma, scarring, or direct compression can cause prolonged denervation. The preserved rotator cuff strength helps distinguish this from other post-surgical shoulder complications, as the axillary nerve does not innervate the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, or subscapularis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 78-year-old woman is referred after multiple falls over the past three months. Falls occur both during the day and at night, often without a clear trip or slip.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk without support, requires assistance with stairs, and reports difficulty adapting to changes in floor level or lighting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple comorbidities including diabetes and hypertension. Wears bifocal glasses but admits to not wearing them consistently indoors. Recent medication changes within the past two months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Romberg test positive with eyes closed. Tandem stance unable to hold >3 seconds. Gait shows reduced arm swing and shortened stride. Ankle dorsiflexion strength 4/5 bilaterally. Cognitive screen (MoCA) 24/30 with slowed processing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain shows age-appropriate mild cortical atrophy and small vessel ischemic changes. No acute stroke, no subdural collection. Cervical spine imaging normal.

puzzles.explanation: Falls in the elderly represent a multifactorial geriatric syndrome resulting from the intersection of age-related physiological decline, sensorimotor impairment, cognitive changes, and environmental hazards. This patient's presentation illustrates the classic vulnerability profile: reduced postural stability (positive Romberg, impaired tandem stance), gait disturbance with shortened stride and diminished arm swing, mild peripheral neuropathy from diabetes (ankle dorsiflexion weakness 4/5), and cognitive slowing that impairs adaptive balance responses during transitions—particularly lighting changes and floor level variations. The small vessel ischemic changes and inconsistent use of corrective lenses compound these intrinsic deficits by compromising proprioceptive and visual feedback. Risk assessment and intervention require a comprehensive approach addressing medication review, home modification, balance retraining, vision optimization, and management of underlying vascular and metabolic disease rather than focusing on any single cause.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient Child age 3 has swollen knee joints and morning stiffness lasting over an hour.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty walking after waking, refuses to run, play, and refuses to bear weight on legs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset 2 months ago, no fever or trauma, and otherwise healthy development.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral warm knee effusions with restricted flexion/extension, positive antinuclear antibodies, and no systemic signs of infection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows symmetric synovial hypertrophy with inflammatory enhancement in both knees; no bone erosion or cartilage damage. Findings consistent with early inflammatory arthropathy.

puzzles.explanation: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) is a chronic inflammatory arthropathy of unknown etiology affecting children under 16, characterized by persistent joint inflammation and synovial proliferation in the absence of infection or trauma. The oligoarticular form, suggested here by bilateral knee involvement in a young child, typically presents insidiously with morning stiffness exceeding one hour and joint swelling that limits weight-bearing and functional mobility during the stiffest periods. The positive antinuclear antibodies and symmetric synovial hypertrophy with inflammatory enhancement on MRI are hallmark features distinguishing JIA from infectious arthritis—notably, the absence of fever, systemic toxicity, and early bone changes. Early diagnosis and aggressive immunosuppressive management are critical to preventing permanent joint damage and preserving long-term function in these young patients.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient Child age 10 has pain around the kneecap during activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform jumping and running without knee pain; stairs and deep squatting particularly provocative.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No acute trauma, insidious onset, and active in sports rapid growth.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive j-sign with knee flexion, weak hip abductors bilaterally, tight rectus femoris, mild effusion on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays normal, MRI shows no cartilage damage or meniscal pathology, slight lateral patellar tracking on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Patellofemoral pain syndrome in children is a common overuse condition characterised by anterior or retropatellar knee pain during dynamic activities, arising from altered patellar tracking and local stress rather than structural damage. During periods of rapid growth, imbalances between strong hip extensors and weak hip abductors create lateral tracking of the patella, increasing patellofemoral joint contact stress — the positive J-sign and tight rectus femoris seen here exemplify these biomechanical drivers. Activity-dependent symptoms during jumping, running, and stair climbing reflect the high loads through this joint, while normal imaging and intact cartilage distinguish it from structural pathology. Management focuses on addressing proximal hip weakness and muscle flexibility to restore normal patellar alignment, making it highly responsive to targeted physiotherapy in the paediatric population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 20-year-old college athlete presents with persistent headache, dizziness, and difficulty concentrating that developed gradually over several hours. Reports sensitivity to bright light and loud sounds. No recent significant medical history.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to sustain focus during lectures and studying. Headache worsens with screen time and mental exertion. Balance problems noticed when turning head quickly or changing positions. Fatigue disproportionate to activity level.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 6–8 hours after a collision during sports competition 2 weeks ago. Had a minor head impact 4 weeks prior without lasting effects. Did not lose consciousness but felt dazed immediately after the recent collision. Gradual progressive worsening rather than acute onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Alert and oriented, GCS 15. Pupils equal and reactive. Single-leg stance asymmetrical (left side more impaired). Delayed response on rapid visual tracking task. Elevated resting heart rate (95 bpm). No focal neurological deficits, motor weakness, or speech changes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain unremarkable—no contusion, hemorrhage, or structural abnormality. Neuropsychological testing reveals slowed processing speed and executive dysfunction compared to pre-season baseline. Objective deficits persist despite 2 weeks of rest. Findings consistent with post-concussive syndrome.

puzzles.explanation: Traumatic brain injury encompasses a spectrum of neurological insult from mechanical impact, ranging from mild concussion to severe diffuse axonal injury. This case illustrates post-concussive syndrome — the persistent constellation of cognitive, vestibular, and autonomic symptoms that can follow even mild TBI without structural damage on conventional imaging. The gradual worsening over hours to days, combined with objective deficits in balance (single-leg stance asymmetry), processing speed, and executive function despite a normal MRI, reflects diffuse microscopic axonal disruption and neuroinflammatory changes that neuroimaging cannot yet visualize. Young athletes are particularly vulnerable to recurrent injury and protracted recovery; return-to-play protocols emphasize gradual symptom-limited progression and neurocognitive clearance to prevent compounding injury and prolonged disability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Elderly gentleman with hip discomfort in both groin and lateral regions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with stairs and getting up from chair; better with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive worsening over several years. Remote hip injury in his 20s with persistent symptoms since then.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Antalgic gait, significant hip flexion contracture. Pain at end-range movements.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows cartilage loss, bone marrow lesions, and joint space reduction.

puzzles.explanation: Hip osteoarthritis is progressive cartilage degeneration of the hip joint, typically secondary to joint trauma or primary to age-related wear; it commonly follows earlier injury, as in this patient's remote hip trauma decades ago that seeded degenerative changes.
