The condition characteristically causes groin and lateral hip pain that worsens with weight-bearing activities like stair climbing and rising from a chair, while improving with rest—reflecting mechanical joint dysfunction. Patients develop compensatory movement patterns including antalgic gait and hip flexion contractures as the joint loses motion and proprioception. Imaging findings of cartilage loss, bone marrow lesions, and joint space narrowing confirm the structural damage and are consistent with the Kellgren-Lawrence progression typical of symptomatic hip OA.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old with progressive, insidious shoulder pain over several months. Pain is diffuse around the posterior shoulder.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform overhead reaching or throwing. Activities aggravate pain, particularly cross-body adduction. Progressive functional decline over months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior trauma or injury. No history of shoulder instability. Pain developed insidiously without clear precipitant. Patient denies neck pain or radicular symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked infraspinatus weakness (4/5) on manual testing. Supraspinatus strength intact. Positive infraspinatus atrophy noted on inspection. EMG shows denervation pattern in infraspinatus; supraspinatus EMG normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows suprascapular nerve compression and associated paralabral ganglion cyst visible.

puzzles.explanation: Suprascapular neuropathy results from compression of the suprascapular nerve as it passes through the spinoglandular notch, most commonly by a paralabral ganglion cyst arising from the superior labrum. The nerve's anatomical course means it innervates the supraspinatus proximally and infraspinatus distally, but compression typically affects the infraspinatus preferentially, sparing supraspinatus function—a distinctive pattern that helps differentiate this from rotator cuff disease. The insidious presentation, progressive infraspinatus atrophy with preserved strength in supraspinatus, and the neurogenic denervation pattern on EMG are hallmarks of nerve compression rather than primary muscle pathology. The functional loss in overhead and throwing activities reflects both infraspinatus weakness and the chronic compensatory changes that develop when this key external rotator is denervated.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 75-year-old reports progressive fatigue and shortness of breath on exertion over the past 8 weeks. Describes feeling deconditioned and anxious about returning to normal activity levels. Denies chest pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking tolerance severely limited—becomes breathless after 50-100 metres. Unable to climb stairs without stopping. Swelling in left lower leg restricts comfortable walking. Reports fear-avoidance with activity despite medical clearance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cardiac surgery performed 3 months ago. Lives alone, minimal home support. Prior sedentary lifestyle. Reports not yet engaged in structured rehabilitation program. No recent hospitalizations or complications reported since discharge.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sternal scar well-healed. Left leg shows pitting edema and skin texture changes consistent with saphenous vein harvest site. Reduced lower limb strength (4/5 hip and knee extensors bilaterally). Deconditioning evident on 6-minute walk test. Heart sounds regular with normal rate at rest.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram: normal valve function, ejection fraction 50%, no structural abnormalities. Chest X-ray: clear lung fields, normal cardiac silhouette. Doppler ultrasound of lower limb: no deep vein thrombosis; findings consistent with post-operative venous insufficiency and lymphedema.

puzzles.explanation: Post-cardiac surgery rehabilitation addresses the physiological and psychological sequelae that follow coronary artery bypass grafting or valve replacement, where patients experience deconditioning, lower limb complications from saphenous vein harvesting, and often significant anxiety despite successful surgical outcome. The combination of reduced exercise tolerance, progressive fatigue, and fear-avoidance behaviour reflects both genuine cardiorespiratory deconditioning and the psychological impact of major surgery—factors that structured cardiac rehabilitation specifically targets through graded aerobic training, strength work, and psychoeducation. In this case, the left lower leg swelling and skin changes represent saphenous vein harvest sequelae, common post-CABG complications that cause venous insufficiency and lymphedema; these require early mobilization and lymphatic management to prevent chronic morbidity. The absence of a formal rehabilitation program is notable, as early engagement (typically 4-8 weeks post-surgery) with supervised exercise, education, and graduated return-to-function is the standard approach to restoring functional capacity and confidence in this population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 32-year-old with recurrent episodes of painful oral ulceration over past 8 years. Patient reports episodes resolve and recur unpredictably. Recently experiencing lower limb pain and some visual disturbances.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty eating and speaking during flares due to oral pain. Lower limb pain worsened by prolonged sitting and friction from clothing. Visual symptoms include blurred vision and photophobia, worse with screen time.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Oral ulcers appeared first, followed years later by recurrent genital ulceration and skin lesions on lower extremities. Pattern of flares and remissions unrelated to obvious triggers. No trauma or infection preceding symptoms. Symptoms more prominent in warmer months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Multiple shallow oral ulcers with erythematous borders. Scarring visible on genital tissues. Palpable nodules and pustular lesions over anterior shins and lower legs. Reduced visual acuity bilaterally. No obvious joint swelling or systemic signs of infection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ophthalmologic exam: bilateral anterior and posterior uveitis with retinal vasculitis and inflammatory exudates. Inflammatory markers elevated (CRP, ESR). No evidence of infection or malignancy on laboratory workup. HLA-B51 testing positive.

puzzles.explanation: Behçet's disease is a chronic, relapsing systemic vasculitis of unknown etiology, strongly associated with HLA-B51 positivity, that classically presents with recurrent oral ulceration followed by genital ulcers and characteristic skin lesions like erythema nodosum on the shins. Ocular involvement—particularly bilateral uveitis with retinal vasculitis—occurs in up to 70% of patients and is a major cause of morbidity, manifesting here as visual disturbance and photophobia. The unpredictable flare-remission pattern, absence of infectious or malignant triggers, and elevated inflammatory markers distinguish it from other vasculitides; seasonal exacerbation and involvement of multiple mucocutaneous and vascular sites are hallmark features. Management focuses on immunosuppression and anti-inflammatory therapy to prevent ocular and neurological complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult with severe burning pain in a band-like region of the trunk or limb that began weeks after a shingles rash resolved.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient avoids wearing clothing or touching affected skin due to severe pain; activities requiring contact with the affected area are impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain persists months after acute varicella zoster infection; severe in elderly or immunocompromised.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Decreased sensation to pinprick and light touch in dermatomal distribution; hyperalgesia to pinprick; residual erythema or post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation in the affected dermatome.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the affected dermatome shows no evidence of spinal cord compression, malignancy, or other structural lesion explaining pain. Findings support post-inflammatory neuropathic pain without structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN) is a persistent neuropathic pain syndrome that develops after acute varicella zoster virus infection (shingles), resulting from inflammation and scarring of the dorsal root ganglion and peripheral nerve fibres within the affected dermatome. The characteristic band-like burning pain, dysaesthesia to light touch, and hyperalgesia reflect sensitization of nociceptors and abnormal neural signalling in the damaged sensory pathway — pain that can persist for months or years even after the rash has completely resolved. Older adults and immunocompromised individuals are at highest risk, likely due to impaired viral clearance and prolonged inflammatory changes. The absence of structural pathology on imaging is diagnostically important, confirming that this is a pure neuropathic pain problem requiring neuromodulatory and adjunctive pain management strategies rather than surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young patient with widespread joint pain and chronic pain complaints.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Repeated joint dislocations with minimal trauma; difficulty with high-demand activities due to instability and fatigue.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong hypermobility since childhood; family history of joint laxity or connective tissue disorder; multiple joint dislocations across different sites over years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, and tissue fragility poor wound healing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging normal; structural degenerative changes absent despite clinical hypermobility; genetic testing available to confirm specific EDS subtype.

puzzles.explanation: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (EDS) is a heterogeneous group of heritable connective tissue disorders caused by defects in collagen synthesis, cross-linking, or structure, resulting in widespread tissue fragility and joint hypermobility. The recurrent dislocations with minimal trauma and generalized hypermobility seen in this young patient reflect fundamental instability of the connective tissue supporting joints — a hallmark distinguishing EDS from primary ligamentous laxity alone. The triad of hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, and poor wound healing, combined with a lifelong history and family involvement, points to systemic collagen pathology rather than localized dysfunction. Normal imaging despite significant clinical findings is characteristic; diagnosis relies on clinical assessment, assessment tools like the Beighton score, and increasingly on genetic confirmation to identify the specific subtype and guide prognosis and management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 26-year-old female, 8 weeks gestation. Reports onset of bilateral pain in lower abdomen, groin, and lateral hip region over the past 2 weeks. Pain described as aching and intermittent. No trauma or recent falls.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged standing, climbing stairs, and rolling over in bed. Some relief with rest and lying down. Difficulty with activities requiring leg separation or weight-shifting between feet.

clues.label: History
clues.text: First pregnancy. No previous pelvic or spinal injury. However, reports a lifelong history of joint hypermobility—has always been 'very flexible' and notes easy bruising and frequent sprains as a teenager. No significant musculoskeletal treatment prior to pregnancy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness over pubic symphysis and bilateral sacroiliac joints. Positive Patrick's test and FABER test bilaterally. Asymmetry noted on palpation of anterior superior iliac spines. Generalised ligamentous laxity evident (thumb-to-forearm contact, knee hyperextension). Hip and lumbar ROM preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound shows widening of the pubic symphysis (>9 mm) compared to non-pregnant baseline. MRI pelvis demonstrates ligamentous laxity and mild separation at sacroiliac joints bilaterally, consistent with hormonally-mediated joint and ligament changes of early pregnancy.

puzzles.explanation: Antenatal pelvic girdle pain arises from hormonally-mediated laxity of the sacroiliac joints and pubic symphysis during pregnancy, compounded by pre-existing joint hypermobility that reduces the pelvic ring's load-bearing capacity. The condition classically presents with bilateral groin, lower abdominal, and lateral hip pain triggered by activities requiring leg separation or asymmetrical weight-shifting—climbing stairs, rolling over in bed, and prolonged standing all demand pelvic stability the ligamentously lax joints cannot provide. Patients with underlying connective tissue laxity, as evident here from lifelong hypermobility and easy bruising, are at significantly higher risk because their baseline ligamentous support is already compromised before pregnancy hormones further increase joint compliance. Tenderness over the pubic symphysis and sacroiliac joints, combined with positive hip provocation tests and imaging confirmation of symphyseal widening and sacroiliac separation, distinguishes this from simple mechanical low back pain and confirms the pelvic girdle as the primary pain generator.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Female patient reports chronic vulvar pain described as burning, rawness, and irritation without visible pathology. Pain constant but variable in intensity. Dyspareunia severe; sexual activity avoided.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sitting intolerance, particularly with pressure on perineum. Symptom triggered by tight clothing. Standing and walking preferred over prolonged sitting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive vulvar pain over 3 years with no identified cause despite extensive gynecologic evaluation. Multiple treatments attempted without sustained relief. Pain affecting relationship and psychological well-being.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Normal vulvar skin appearance; no obvious dermatologic pathology. Pain reproduction with light touch to vulva (allodynia). No visible erythema, ulceration, or lesion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Vulvoscopy unremarkable; biopsy if performed shows only scattered perivascular inflammation with no infection, malignancy, or lichenoid changes. No neuromas or abnormal innervation on histology.

puzzles.explanation: Vulvodynia is a chronic pain condition of the vulva characterized by burning, rawness, or irritation in the absence of identifiable pathology — a diagnosis of exclusion made after ruling out infection, dermatologic disease, and malignancy. The hallmark features are pain with minimal provocation (allodynia to light touch), absence of visible vulvar changes despite severe symptoms, and significant functional impact on sitting tolerance and sexual activity. The pathophysiology remains incompletely understood but involves altered neuropathic processing, possible nerve sensitization, and central pain amplification; the progressive three-year course without clear etiology or sustained response to typical gynecologic interventions is typical. Management focuses on neuropathic pain strategies, pelvic floor assessment and rehabilitation, and psychological support rather than pursuit of a structural diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Parent notices child walks with stiffness and one-sided weakness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Right hand function limited; child prefers left hand despite being right-handed family member.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Child cannot climb stairs or run smoothly; fatigues easily during play compared to peers.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right-sided hemiparesis with increased tone; right foot in plantarflexion with tiptoe gait. Hyperreflexia in right lower limb; positive Babinski sign bilaterally. Minimal right hand dexterity with fisted posture at rest.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain shows focal injury to left motor cortex and corticospinal tract consistent with perinatal insult. No progressive intracranial pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Cerebral palsy is a non-progressive motor disorder resulting from a static lesion to the developing brain, typically sustained during the perinatal period—in this case, focal injury to the left motor cortex and corticospinal tract. The right-sided hemiparesis, increased tone, hyperreflexia, and positive Babinski sign reflect upper motor neuron damage, while the characteristic equinus posture (plantarflexion toe-walking) and fisted hand posture at rest exemplify the spasticity that defines the spastic hemiplegia subtype. Hand preference deficits and functional limitations in bilateral activities like stair climbing are typical as children with unilateral motor impairment fatigue more easily and compensate with their unaffected side. Importantly, the non-progressive imaging confirms the static nature of the insult—distinguishing cerebral palsy from acquired or degenerative conditions—making early intervention and neuroplasticity-based rehabilitation the cornerstones of management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Office worker reports low back or mid-back pain that develops during work hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting and poor posture; improves with movement breaks and stretching.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 6-12 months ago; patient reports no trauma or prior back history. Does not improve with rest days or weekends off.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Limited thoracic rotation and extension; pain reproduces with sustained slouching posture held for 5 minutes. Abdominal strength graded 4/5; lumbopelvic stability impaired.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI lumbar spine normal; no disc herniation, spondylosis, or instability. Postural analysis via photography shows loss of normal lumbar lordosis and anterior pelvic tilt pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Postural syndrome represents a mechanical low back or mid-back pain pattern driven by sustained postural deviation rather than structural pathology—in this case, the loss of lumbar lordosis and anterior pelvic tilt typical of prolonged slouching at a desk. Unlike discogenic or instability patterns, postural syndrome responds predictably to movement and positional correction but worsens with continued poor positioning, which is why this patient finds no relief during rest days when habitual desk posture resumes. The mechanism reflects cumulative microtrauma to ligaments, discs, and supporting musculature from sustained end-range positions, compounded here by core weakness (abdominal 4/5) that fails to maintain neutral alignment throughout the workday. Recognition of normal imaging alongside reproducible pain with slouching and improvement with movement breaks is the clinical hallmark—distinguishing postural syndrome from underlying pathology and making ergonomic and motor control retraining the primary intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old with recurrent pneumonia and chronic cough dating back to infancy presents for evaluation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot keep up with peers during play. Morning cough productive. Recurrent ear infections and sinusitis.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Neonatal intensive care admission for prematurity and respiratory distress. Three episodes of pneumonia by age 5. Immotile cilia suspect.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Clubbing noted in fingers. Bilateral fine end-inspiratory crackles at lung bases. Hyperinflation evident on auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: HRCT chest shows dilated bronchi with bronchus-to-artery ratio exceeding 1 in bilateral lower lobes, bronchial wall thickening, and bronchiolectasis. Bronchiectasis pattern is cylindrical.

puzzles.explanation: Bronchiectasis is permanent, pathological dilation of the bronchi resulting from destruction of the elastic and muscular components of the bronchial wall, typically following severe or recurrent lower respiratory tract infections. Primary causes include impaired airway clearance (such as immotile cilia syndrome or cystic fibrosis) and post-infectious damage, with childhood-onset disease often tracing back to severe early infections or developmental compromise—as in this case with prematurity and suspected ciliary dysfunction. The clinical picture of recurrent pneumonia, productive cough, and systemic manifestations like clubbing and sinusitis reflects chronic suppurative airway disease and impaired mucociliary clearance. High-resolution CT imaging showing bronchus-to-artery ratios >1 and bronchial wall thickening with cylindrical bronchiectasis pattern in the lower lobes is pathognomonic, confirming the diagnosis and guiding aggressive airway clearance management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young athletic male with anterior hip pain during high-demand activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with deep hip flexion and internal rotation; mechanical catching sensation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recurrent episodes over several years with periods of relative relief. Training modification sometimes helps briefly, but symptoms return with intensity. No prior hip surgery or significant trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive FADIR test, limited internal rotation in flexion. Posterior hip tightness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging confirms cam morphology with alpha angle >60 degrees; acetabular coverage normal. Labral pathology evident on high-resolution sequences.

puzzles.explanation: Femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) occurs when abnormal hip joint morphology—typically a cam deformity (as seen here with an alpha angle >60°) or pincer morphology—causes abnormal contact between the femoral head-neck junction and the acetabular rim during motion. This mechanical conflict is especially problematic during deep flexion and internal rotation, movements that compress the already reduced space, leading to labral pinching and degeneration over time. Young, athletic individuals are at highest risk because their high-demand activities repeatedly stress the impingement zone, explaining the recurrent nature of symptoms and temporary relief with activity modification. The positive FADIR test and imaging confirmation of cam morphology with labral pathology make FAI distinctive—it's a structural problem driving pain, not simply muscular weakness or general capsular tightness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old male retired painter presents with difficulty lifting his arm and a deep ache on the outside of his upper arm that worsens at night.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot reach overhead to change a light bulb. Reports a 'sudden worsening' event 3 months ago when lifting a heavy bucket, though he's had on-and-off problems for years.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decades of overhead work as a painter. Progressive weakness in the affected arm. Difficulty with hair washing and dressing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive drop arm test. Weakness in external rotation and abduction. Positive empty can test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows a full-thickness tear of the supraspinatus tendon (2cm retraction) with early fatty infiltration of the muscle belly (Goutallier grade 2). Associated superior labral fraying.

puzzles.explanation: A rotator cuff tear represents a full-thickness or partial disruption of one or more of the four rotator cuff tendons, most commonly the supraspinatus due to its exposed position and high demand during overhead activities. Decades of overhead loading — as in this painter's occupational history — creates cumulative microtrauma and degenerative changes that predispose to sudden failure, even from seemingly minor trauma like lifting a bucket. The clinical presentation is distinctive: positive drop arm and empty can tests indicate supraspinatus involvement, while the inability to maintain abduction and external rotation reflects both mechanical loss of the tendon and pain inhibition. Full-thickness tears with retraction and fatty infiltration (Goutallier grade 2) signal chronicity and reduced healing potential, explaining why conservative management alone often provides insufficient functional recovery in this population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Chronic construction worker reports progressive finger pain, numbness, and color changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot grip or manipulate objects when hands are cold; symptoms worsen significantly in winter months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Persistent symptoms despite ergonomic modifications and tool changes; progressive despite reducing vibration exposure in past 3 years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Blanching follows typical asymmetric pattern (index/middle > ring/little) with biphasic color changes on rewarming. Sensory loss in affected digits; thenar/hypothenar muscle wasting present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Doppler ultrasound shows intermittent digital artery occlusion on cold challenge; EMG/NCS demonstrates demyelinating pattern consistent with vibration-induced neuropathy.

puzzles.explanation: Vibration white finger (VWF) is a form of hand-arm vibration syndrome resulting from prolonged exposure to vibrating tools, causing vasospastic episodes in digital arteries combined with progressive peripheral nerve damage. The condition manifests as characteristic blanching of fingers — particularly the index and middle fingers due to asymmetric exposure — triggered or exacerbated by cold, followed by reactive hyperemia on rewarming as perfusion returns. The progressive nature despite reduced vibration exposure reflects that VWF involves both acute vasomotor dysfunction and chronic structural changes in blood vessels and nerves that may not fully reverse once established. Thenar wasting and demyelinating neuropathy findings indicate concurrent nerve involvement beyond simple vascular phenomena, distinguishing VWF from primary Raynaud's phenomenon and explaining why ergonomic modifications alone often fail to halt progression in workers with significant prior exposure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old male presents with progressive inability to fully straighten his right ring and little fingers. He cannot lay his hand flat on a table.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty putting his hand in his pocket, pulling on gloves, and shaking hands. The contracture has gradually worsened over 2 years. No pain associated with the condition.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Northern European descent. History of heavy alcohol use (now sober 5 years). Type 2 diabetes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable firm nodule and cord in the palmar fascia extending to the ring finger. Fixed flexion contracture at the MCP joint (40 degrees) and PIP joint (25 degrees) of the ring finger. Positive Hueston tabletop test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Clinical diagnosis — imaging not typically required. Ultrasound shows hypoechoic nodule in the palmar aponeurosis with fibrous cord extension. No imaging features of tenosynovitis.

puzzles.explanation: Dupuytren's contracture is a progressive fibroproliferative disorder of the palmar fascia that causes nodule formation and subsequent cord contraction, leading to irreversible flexion deformities of the fingers. The condition has a strong genetic predisposition—particularly in Northern European populations—and is associated with several systemic factors including diabetes, alcohol use, and advancing age, though the exact mechanisms remain unclear. The classic presentation involves insidious onset of palmar nodules that evolve into cords tracking toward the fingers, typically affecting the ring and little fingers and progressing over months to years without pain. The inability to lay the hand flat and the measured flexion contractures at both MCP and PIP joints seen here are pathognomonic, and the positive tabletop test elegantly captures the functional deficit that distinguishes this condition from other causes of finger stiffness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old presents with acute knee pain, swelling, and stiffness over the past week. Reports difficulty straightening leg fully and pain with bending. Guarding evident during movement. No recent trauma reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk without limp. Stairs and squatting impossible. Cannot achieve full active knee extension or flexion. Pain worsens with repetitive bending. Swelling increases with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began immediately following a minimally invasive knee procedure performed 10 days ago for investigation of chronic anterior knee discomfort. Pre-procedure imaging was inconclusive. No acute injury or fall reported during recovery period.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Active knee flexion limited to 80°, extension lag of 12°. Soft, boggy end-feel to flexion. Moderate suprapatellar effusion palpable. Quadriceps strength 4/5. Patellofemoral pain with compression. No instability signs. Mild warmth over joint.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Arthroscopic findings: intact menisci and ligaments, patellofemoral articular surface with mild chondral softening, no loose bodies or significant synovitis. Post-procedure ultrasound confirms moderate joint effusion. No evidence of infection or haemarthrosis.

puzzles.explanation: Knee arthroscopy rehabilitation addresses the post-operative inflammatory response and mechanical dysfunction that follows diagnostic or therapeutic knee arthroscopy, particularly when pre-operative pathology is subtle or non-acute. The immediate onset of swelling, extension lag, and restricted range following the procedure reflects synovial irritation and reactive effusion — a predictable consequence of intra-articular instrumentation — rather than structural damage, as confirmed by intact menisci and ligaments on arthroscopic inspection. Early management focuses on controlling effusion through compression and elevation, restoring quadriceps activation to address the extension lag, and graded range-of-work restoration, as aggressive early mobilisation can perpetuate inflammation. This presentation is particularly characteristic when pre-operative imaging yields inconclusive findings, as patients and clinicians may not anticipate the significant post-operative stiffness and functional limitation that can occur despite minimal pathological findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with persistent unexplained fatigue disproportionate to activity level; generalised malaise and reduced energy affecting daily function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to sustain work shifts or household activities; becomes exhausted after minimal exertion; struggles with cognitive tasks and concentration throughout day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent history of cancer diagnosis and completion of chemotherapy/radiation approximately 3–6 months ago. Fatigue onset coincided with treatment and has persisted through recovery phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Six-minute walk test shows reduced distance covered compared to pre-morbid baseline. Grip strength reduced bilaterally. Heart rate rises excessively with minimal exertion. Mood screening indicates depression/anxiety symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Inflammatory markers (CRP, IL-6, TNF-α) elevated despite normal cardiac imaging and thyroid function. Metabolic panels show iron deficiency and low vitamin D. Pattern of systemic inflammation consistent with post-treatment cancer-related fatigue.

puzzles.explanation: Cancer-related fatigue is a persistent, disproportionate exhaustion that extends beyond normal recovery from cancer treatment, driven by residual systemic inflammation, chemotherapy-induced metabolic changes, and deconditioning rather than simple disease activity. Unlike fatigue from anaemia or thyroid dysfunction alone, it presents as a multifactorial syndrome combining reduced exercise tolerance, cognitive impairment, and mood disturbance—reflecting both persistent cytokine elevation and the neurobiological effects of treatment. The elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, IL-6, TNF-α) despite normalised imaging, combined with the exaggerated heart rate response during minimal exertion, underscore the autonomic and metabolic dysregulation characteristic of this condition. Rehabilitation focuses on graduated aerobic and resistance training, nutritional optimisation for deficiencies like iron and vitamin D, and concurrent management of mood symptoms, as these interventions address the multidimensional nature of post-treatment recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 65-year-old man reports tremor in right hand that is worse at rest and improves with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Slow movements when picking up objects, difficulty with fine motor tasks, stooped posture when standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tremor began 2 years ago and is slowly progressive. Family member noticed loss of facial expression. Writing becoming smaller.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting tremor (pill-rolling), cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia, masked facies, shuffling gait with reduced arm swing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DaTscan shows significantly reduced dopamine uptake in bilateral striatum, confirming dopaminergic denervation. MRI brain normal, ruling out structural or secondary causes.

puzzles.explanation: Parkinson's disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra, leading to a dopamine deficit in the striatum and downstream motor circuit dysfunction. The classic tetrad—resting tremor (the characteristic pill-rolling appearance), rigidity (often cogwheel in nature), bradykinesia, and postural instability—reflects this basal ganglia pathology; the tremor typically worsens at rest and resolves with intentional movement because the affected circuits lose their ability to inhibit unwanted motor activity in the absence of goal-directed action. Beyond motor signs, the hypokinetic features seen here—the progressive micrographia, masked facies, and reduced arm swing during gait—underscore the pervasive slowness and loss of automatic motor planning that defines the condition. DaTscan imaging showing reduced striatal dopamine uptake provides definitive confirmation of the neurochemical basis, distinguishing true Parkinson's disease from secondary parkinsonian syndromes or structural mimics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old breast cancer survivor presents with progressive dyspnea and fatigue 18 months after completing anthracycline-based chemotherapy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Orthopnea and lower limb edema noted. Symptoms worse when lying flat; legs swell by evening after minimal activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cumulative anthracycline dose 450 mg/m². No prior cardiac history, hypertension, or diabetes. No recent infection or fever. Symptoms began insidiously 12 months post-treatment completion.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild tachycardia, normal blood pressure. Baseline lung exam clear. Displaced apex beat.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows EF 38% (baseline 60% at end of treatment). Global hypokinesis without regional wall motion abnormality. Diastolic dysfunction present.

puzzles.explanation: Cardiotoxicity post-cancer treatment results from direct myocardial injury caused by anthracycline chemotherapy, which generates reactive oxygen species and damages cardiac myocytes — this injury can progress insidiously months or years after treatment completion. The condition presents as dilated cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction and global hypokinesis, distinguishing it from regional ischaemic patterns or acute infectious causes; cumulative anthracycline dose above 400 mg/m² carries substantially elevated risk. The orthopnea, evening lower limb edema, and displaced apex beat in this patient reflect the classic presentation of anthracycline-induced heart failure — a reduced ejection fraction state with biventricular dilatation developing well after treatment cessation. Early detection through serial echocardiography and careful cardio-oncology follow-up are essential, as ACE inhibitors and beta-blockers may slow progression or partially reverse dysfunction if caught early.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young athletic patient reports diffuse body pain triggered by minor injuries that don't resolve.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to return to baseline training; minimal exertion produces pain lasting days, leading to progressive deconditioning and fear-avoidance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset following concussion or viral illness; accompanied by cognitive difficulties and persistent fatigue.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tender points elicited across all body quadrants with minimal pressure; normal strength and reflexes; no focal neurological deficits or signs of structural damage.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Normal structural imaging; functional neuroimaging shows abnormal pain processing in central nervous system.

puzzles.explanation: Fibromyalgia is a central sensitization disorder characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain and altered pain processing in the brain and spinal cord, often triggered by infections, trauma, or stress in genetically predisposed individuals. Unlike structural pathologies, fibromyalgia presents with diffuse tender points across all body regions yet normal strength, reflexes, and structural imaging—the pain amplification occurs at the neurobiological level, not the tissue level. The post-viral or post-traumatic onset with cognitive dysfunction and fatigue reflects the systemic nature of central sensitization, and the severe deconditioning cycle seen here is characteristic, as patients' fear-avoidance responses to disproportionate pain perpetuate disability. Functional neuroimaging revealing abnormal pain processing, rather than structural findings, confirms the diagnosis and underscores why tissue-based interventions alone are insufficient; multimodal rehabilitation addressing pain neuroscience education, graded activity, and psychological factors is essential.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 25-year-old recreational athlete. Anterior knee pain, difficult to pinpoint precisely. Pain quality variable—sometimes sharp, sometimes dull ache. Intermittent mechanical symptoms: occasional catching or popping sensation, unpredictable in timing and frequency.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered by knee flexion-based activities: stairs (especially descent), squatting, prolonged sitting with knee bent. Symptoms worse with repetitive bending. Swelling minimal or absent. Pain may resolve spontaneously mid-activity or persist, making pattern unpredictable.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic intermittent symptoms over 18 months. No clear traumatic onset—gradual insidious progression. Prior physiotherapy (quadriceps/hip strengthening) and NSAIDs provided temporary relief only; symptoms recur. Imaging workup to date unremarkable: no meniscal tears, ligamentous laxity, or osteoarthritis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness along medial aspect of joint line and suprapatellar pouch. Knee ROM full and pain-free in passive extension. All ligamentous tests negative (Lachman, anterior drawer, pivot shift). No joint effusion. Pain reproduction unreliable and inconsistent on repeat testing; some special maneuvers occasionally provoke symptoms but cannot be reliably replicated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI: thickened, fibrotic tissue noted in suprapatellar region with abnormal signal characteristics. Tissue appears to impinge on medial patellofemoral space during flexion sequences. Subtle cartilage signal change adjacent to impingement zone. Diagnostic arthroscopy confirms abnormal synovial fold with inflammatory changes and localized chondral reaction. Resection of offending tissue results in symptom resolution.

puzzles.explanation: Plica syndrome results from inflammation and fibrosis of a synovial fold (plica) that normally exists as a remnant of embryologic knee development; the medial suprapatellar plica is most commonly symptomatic. During knee flexion, this thickened, inelastic tissue can impinge on the patellofemoral joint and medial femoral condyle, causing the characteristic anterior knee pain and mechanical symptoms—catching, popping, and unpredictable locking—that are often difficult to reproduce reliably on examination. The insidious, chronic intermittent presentation without clear trauma, combined with minimal imaging findings on standard studies yet positive arthroscopic findings, is classic; conservative strengthening provides only temporary relief because it does not address the underlying structural irritant. Definitive diagnosis typically requires arthroscopy, which reveals the inflammatory synovial fold and localized chondral changes, and arthroscopic resection (plica plication or excision) is the gold standard treatment for persistent cases.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 32-year-old woman presenting with radial-sided wrist pain of 6 weeks duration. Onset was gradual and painless initially, now causing functional limitation. Reports swelling and stiffness in the thumb region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain significantly worse when lifting objects with thumb abducted and wrist extended. Wringing wet cloths and fastening buttons exacerbate symptoms. Gripping and twisting motions also provoke discomfort. Symptoms ease with rest and immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 6 weeks post-partum. No antecedent trauma or wrist injury. Increased repetitive hand activities due to infant care (bottle preparation, diaper changes, lifting). No prior wrist problems. No systemic inflammatory disease or rheumatological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized tenderness and mild soft tissue swelling over the radial styloid process. Positive Finkelstein test with reproduction of pain. Crepitus palpable over the dorsal thumb base with active thumb abduction and wrist extension. Pain-free grip strength. No thenar wasting.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickening and hypoechoic edema of tendons in the first dorsal compartment at the radial styloid. No bone erosion or fracture. Increased fluid within the sheath. MRI confirms peritendinous inflammation without tendon rupture.

puzzles.explanation: De Quervain's tenosynovitis is an inflammatory condition affecting the extensor pollicis brevis and abductor pollicis longus tendons in the first dorsal compartment at the radial styloid, causing pain with thumb abduction and wrist extension. The postpartum onset combined with increased repetitive hand activities—particularly thumb-intensive tasks like bottle preparation and lifting—is classic; hormonal changes and fluid retention in the peripartum period lower the inflammatory threshold, while the mechanical loading from infant care overwhelms the irritated sheath. The localized tenderness over the radial styloid, positive Finkelstein test, and palpable crepitus on active thumb motion are hallmark clinical findings, distinguishing this from carpal tunnel or other wrist pathology. Imaging reveals the characteristic tenosynovial thickening and peritendinous edema without structural damage, confirming inflammation rather than structural failure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old female. Recurrent episodes of severe spinning sensation lasting 20 minutes to 3 hours, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. Episodes unpredictable. Between attacks: sensation of fullness in one ear, ringing sound, and difficulty hearing conversation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During acute attacks: completely unable to work or safely move; severe nausea and vomiting. Between episodes: fully functional and symptom-free. Progressive difficulty hearing low-pitched sounds over months, worsening on one side only.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-month history of recurrent episodes. Progressive unilateral hearing loss particularly affecting low frequencies. Tinnitus with roaring or rushing quality, fluctuates in intensity. No prior head trauma, no recent infection, no ototoxic medication use. No family history of hearing loss.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: During acute attack: spontaneous nystagmus present (horizontal and rotatory), positive Dix-Hallpike test, unable to walk straight (positive Romberg). Hearing assessment reveals reduced air conduction on affected side at low frequencies. Between episodes: neurological exam normal, nystagmus resolves, gait steady.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Audiometry: sensorineural hearing loss at 250-1000 Hz on affected ear, low frequencies worse than high frequencies. Caloric testing (bithermal): reduced warm and cold responses on affected side. MRI internal auditory canal: normal; rules out retrocochlear pathology. Electrocochleography shows elevated summating potential-to-action potential ratio on affected side.

puzzles.explanation: Ménière's disease is a primary disorder of endolymphatic regulation in the inner ear, causing episodic vertigo, fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness—typically unilateral and idiopathic in origin. The condition results from endolymphatic hydrops, an accumulation of fluid within the cochlea and semicircular canals that distorts hair cell mechanics and disrupts both vestibular and auditory function. The characteristic pattern here—acute spinning attacks with nystagmus and nausea interspersed with symptom-free intervals, alongside progressive low-frequency hearing loss and roaring tinnitus on one side—reflects the hallmark fluctuating nature of endolymphatic pressure. Electrocochleography findings of elevated summating potential, combined with normal imaging and caloric asymmetry, anchor the diagnosis and distinguish Ménière's from retrocochlear pathology or other vestibular syndromes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with ankle pain following inversion injury; symptoms persist beyond typical resolution timeframe.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform cutting/pivoting movements; pain and instability with activities requiring ankle rotation. Difficulty with weight-bearing on uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Initial injury diagnosis may have been missed, slow recovery. Previous ankle sprain complications, recurrent episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Syndesmotic tenderness present; positive squeeze test and cross-leg test. Pain reproduced with dorsiflexion-external rotation. Weight-bearing limited by anterior ankle discomfort.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI confirms chronic syndesmotic inflammation and ligamentous thickening. Stress radiographs demonstrate persistent tibiofibular widening compared to contralateral side.

puzzles.explanation: A high ankle sprain involves injury to the syndesmotic ligaments that bind the distal tibia and fibula, occurring proximal to the ankle mortise and often missed on initial presentation. Unlike typical inversion ankle sprains, syndesmotic injuries produce pain with dorsiflexion-external rotation and are characterized by prolonged recovery due to the poor vascularity and high mechanical demand of the distal tibiofibular joint. The difficulty with cutting and pivoting movements reflects the syndesmosis's critical role in controlling tibial-fibular separation during rotational loading. Imaging findings of persistent tibiofibular widening on stress radiographs and chronic ligamentous thickening underscore why early recognition and appropriate immobilization are essential—misdiagnosis or inadequate initial treatment frequently leads to chronic instability and functional limitations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young adult (25 years old) with progressive lower limb weakness causing frequent falls and difficulty with stairs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Marked sensory loss in hands and feet affecting proprioception and fine motor tasks. Difficulty with balance in darkness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Noticed foot deformities as teenager. Father has similar condition. Slowly progressive.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Stocking-glove sensory loss (vibration and proprioception worse distally). Widespread areflexia. Peroneal nerve thickening palpable in legs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows demyelinating neuropathy with slowed conduction velocities and genetic testing shows PMP22 duplication.

puzzles.explanation: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease is a hereditary peripheral neuropathy caused by mutations affecting myelin formation or axonal integrity — in this case, PMP22 duplication leading to demyelination. The condition typically manifests in adolescence or early adulthood with distal lower limb weakness and atrophy, progressing proximally over years, combined with sensory loss in a stocking-glove distribution affecting proprioception and vibration sense first. The characteristic foot deformities (pes cavus, hammer toes) and palpable peroneal nerve thickening reflect chronic denervation and myelin pathology, while areflexia and slowed motor conduction velocities on EMG confirm the demyelinating phenotype. Autosomal dominant inheritance with incomplete penetrance explains the family history and variable presentation across generations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Athlete with anterior knee pain localised below the kneecap, persistent despite training load reduction, affecting jumping and sprinting performance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Explosive movements like jumping, bounding, and rapid deceleration are highly provocative; symptoms worsen with repeated plyometric activity and running on hard surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms have persisted for over 18 months despite physiotherapy, corticosteroid injections, and multiple rest periods. No significant improvement with conservative management. Patient reports occasional swelling and stiffness on mornings after high-load training.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Focal nodularity palpable in tendon, significant with resisted knee extension, and eccentric quadriceps loading highly provocative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows chronic tendinopathy with significant structural disorganization and possible tears or partial rupture. Considerable degeneration.

puzzles.explanation: Patellar tendinopathy is a chronic overuse condition characterised by microtearing, collagen disorganisation, and failed healing within the patellar tendon, typically affecting explosive athletes through repetitive high-load plyometric activity. The condition is distinctly resistant to conservative management—rest, physiotherapy, and injections often fail because the underlying structural pathology (tendon thickening, nodularity, partial tears) persists even when symptoms temporarily improve. The 18-month chronicity with morning stiffness and the exquisite provocation on eccentric loading and explosive movements reflect the tendon's compromised capacity to tolerate force transmission during rapid deceleration and jumping. Advanced imaging findings of significant disorganisation and possible partial rupture signal a tendon in a degenerative state, making load management and potentially surgical intervention more relevant than further conservative trials.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Gradual blurred vision

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty reading small print and recognizing faces at distance

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive onset over months, no eye trauma or disease, patient >65 years

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced visual acuity, presbyopia noted, anterior chamber clear, no cataracts

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Optical coherence tomography shows normal macula, age-appropriate retinal changes only

puzzles.explanation: Age-related vision impairment results from cumulative physiological changes in the eye's optical and neural structures — lens hardening (presbyopia), reduced pupil responsiveness, and age-related retinal changes — rather than discrete pathology like cataracts or macular degeneration. The hallmark presentation of gradual blurred vision with particular difficulty reading near print and recognizing distant faces reflects the combined effect of decreased lens elasticity and reduced contrast sensitivity that characterizes normal aging. This differs from pathological conditions in that imaging reveals only age-appropriate changes without structural disease, and the anterior segment remains clear. Rehabilitation for age-related vision impairment focuses on optical correction, environmental lighting optimization, and activity modification rather than medical or surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports medial-sided knee pain that began insidiously 2-3 weeks ago, no history of trauma or acute injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain provoked by stairs (especially descending) and running. Symptoms worse with hip adduction and internal rotation movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent history of repetitive running or gym activity, prior knee or hip surgery, or persistent hip/hamstring stiffness despite stretching.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness maximal over proximal medial tibia (pes anserine insertion). Pain with resisted hip adduction and internal rotation. Hip flexibility significantly limited (adductor and hamstring tightness). Knee effusion absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI shows bursal fluid collection, tendon changes, bursa thickening, and associated muscle strain of sartorius/gracilis/semitendinosus.

puzzles.explanation: Pes anserine bursitis is inflammation of the bursa between the pes anserine tendons (sartorius, gracilis, semitendinosus) and the medial tibial plateau, typically triggered by repetitive knee flexion-extension combined with hip adduction and internal rotation forces. The insidious onset and pain pattern with stair descent and running reflect the cumulative microtrauma from these combined movements, while the absence of effusion helps distinguish it from intra-articular pathology. Hip stiffness — particularly adductor and hamstring tightness — is a key driver, as it alters tibiofemoral mechanics and increases shear stress at the pes anserine insertion. Tenderness precisely at the proximal medial tibia with pain on resisted hip adduction clinches the diagnosis and differentiates this from collateral ligament or meniscal involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old male presents with acute onset of localised chest wall swelling and pain at the second costochondral junction on the left side. The swelling appeared suddenly several days ago and has been progressively enlarging.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 28-year-old male presents with acute onset of localised chest wall pain and visible swelling. Symptoms began several days ago and have been progressively worsening.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He had a mild upper respiratory tract infection with a productive cough two weeks prior. He denies any chest trauma or heavy manual work. Of note, there is no fever, weight loss, or systemic symptoms that would suggest a sinister aetiology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: A visible, firm, fusiform swelling is palpable at the second costochondral junction measuring approximately 3cm. The swelling is warm and tender to direct palpation. Respiratory examination is otherwise clear, and cardiovascular examination is unremarkable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray is normal with no cardiopulmonary pathology. CT of the chest wall confirms focal non-suppurative inflammatory swelling of the costal cartilage at the second costochondral junction, with no features of malignancy or infection. These findings, in combination with the characteristic clinical presentation, confirm the diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Tietze syndrome is a self-limiting inflammatory condition of the costochondral junctions, characterized by focal swelling and tenderness of the costal cartilage itself—distinct from the more common costochondritis, which involves pain without visible swelling. The condition typically affects young adults and often follows an upper respiratory tract infection with associated cough, as the repetitive mechanical stress on the chest wall triggers localized cartilage inflammation. The second and third costochondral junctions are most frequently involved, presenting with the acute onset of a firm, warm, fusiform swelling that can be quite alarming to patients but is entirely benign and non-suppurative. Diagnosis is clinical, supported by imaging that rules out serious pathology; the condition is self-resolving over weeks to months, managed conservatively with NSAIDs and activity modification.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged athlete noticing a clicking sensation in the hip during running and jumping; pain gradually worsening over weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform running drills or high-intensity hip movements without triggering the clicking sensation; limiting participation in sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Started 6 weeks ago after ramping up training volume; initially just a painless click, now sharp pain with certain hip movements.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable snapping sensation directly over greater trochanter on passive hip flexion/extension with external rotation; no hip ROM restriction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows tensor fasciae latae snapping over trochanter; may see underlying bursal inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Snapping hip syndrome occurs when soft tissue structures—most commonly the iliotibial band or tensor fasciae latae—subluxate over bony prominences like the greater trochanter during hip motion, producing an audible or palpable click. The condition develops from repetitive hip flexion-extension, particularly under load, making athletes who rapidly increase training volume vulnerable to this mechanical irritation. The progression from painless clicking to sharp pain reflects evolving inflammation of the underlying bursa, which explains why high-intensity movements trigger symptoms while passive range remains preserved. Dynamic ultrasound imaging of the tensor fasciae latae snapping over the trochanter, combined with the characteristic click on passive flexion-extension with external rotation, confirms the diagnosis—distinguishing this from true intra-articular hip pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports progressive unilateral arm swelling and heaviness over several years. Sensation of heaviness and tightness in the limb. Recurrent skin infections increasingly problematic.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to perform overhead activities, carrying, or prolonged use of affected arm. Significant functional loss in activities of daily living despite conservative treatment attempts.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Left-sided mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection 2 years ago. Patient completed adjuvant radiation therapy to the chest wall. Attempted early compression therapy and exercise without sustained improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pitting edema in proximal arm, non-pitting in forearm and hand. Skin thickened and fibrotic. Stemmer sign positive. Circumferential measurements show 4-5 cm difference compared to contralateral limb. Evidence of old cellulitis scars.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates delayed tracer clearance and asymmetric drainage pattern. Absence of axillary lymph node activity on affected side. Ultrasound confirms diffuse subcutaneous fibrosis consistent with chronic lymphostasis.

puzzles.explanation: Breast cancer–related lymphedema develops when surgical removal of axillary lymph nodes and subsequent radiation damage impair the lymphatic drainage capacity of the upper limb, causing progressive fluid accumulation in the interstitial space. The classic presentation—unilateral swelling emerging months to years post-treatment, with pitting edema proximally and fibrotic skin changes distally—reflects the chronic inflammatory remodeling that occurs as lymphostasis persists; recurrent cellulitis further perpetuates this cycle by triggering additional tissue fibrosis. The positive Stemmer sign, significant circumferential asymmetry, and imaging confirmation of delayed tracer clearance with absent nodal activity are diagnostic hallmarks. Unlike acute post-surgical edema, this chronic secondary lymphedema shows poor response to compression and exercise alone once tissue fibrosis dominates, requiring more intensive decongestive therapy and long-term garment management to prevent further functional decline.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Newborn with persistent head tilt to one side; parents report asymmetric head position since birth.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty turning head past midline in one direction; resists passive rotation away from affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Difficult breech extraction at delivery; birth weight 3.8 kg with large frame.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Firm, non-tender mass palpable along sternocleidomastoid; passive rotation to opposite side blocked; no apparent neurological deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows sternocleidomastoid muscle echogenicity increased with muscle thickening; fibrosis present.

puzzles.explanation: Congenital torticollis is a postural deformity caused by shortening and fibrosis of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, typically following difficult delivery—particularly breech extraction with larger infants—that damages muscle fibres and triggers inflammatory changes. The characteristic presentation includes a palpable firm mass (sternocleidomastoid hematoma evolving to fibrosis) within the muscle, persistent head tilt toward the affected side, and restricted passive rotation away from that side. Early recognition and aggressive stretching therapy are critical, as the fibrotic changes visible on ultrasound can become irreversible; without intervention, secondary skeletal deformities including facial asymmetry and plagiocephaly develop. Prompt referral for intensive physiotherapy in the first weeks of life offers the best chance of preventing long-term contracture and restoring normal cervical mobility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old manual worker with gradual onset of bilateral arm discomfort over the past year. Reports aching and fatigue in forearms and hands, worse by end of workday. No acute injury or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty sustaining repetitive gripping and forceful pinching tasks. Symptoms increase with prolonged assembly work and fine motor activities. Pain eases with rest and activity modification but returns with resumption of work demands.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 15 years in repetitive manual assembly work with awkward postures and static positioning. Symptoms began insidiously 12 months ago, initially in dominant limb. Tried ergonomic adjustments and periodic breaks with modest relief. No prior injury, no systemic disease, no pregnancy or metabolic risk factors.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild tenderness over forearm musculature and wrist flexors bilaterally. Grip strength reduced symmetrically. Full range of motion preserved but end-range wrist and elbow positions reproduce symptoms. No focal neurological deficits, sensory intact, reflexes normal. No muscle wasting.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of bilateral forearms shows mild muscular edema and thickening of flexor-pronator tendons. No focal nerve swelling, no compression points, no structural lesions. Findings consistent with chronic myofascial strain without neuropathic involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational overuse syndrome results from cumulative microtrauma to muscles, tendons, and fascia caused by repetitive activities, forceful gripping, and sustained awkward postures — in this case, 15 years of assembly work. Unlike nerve compression syndromes, it produces diffuse myofascial pain and fatigue without neurological deficits or focal nerve pathology, reflecting chronic inflammation and muscular adaptation to demand exceeding tissue tolerance. The bilateral distribution beginning in the dominant limb and progression over months reflects the loading asymmetry common in manual work; the preserved neurological examination and absence of nerve swelling on imaging distinguish it from compressive neuropathies. Management focuses on activity modification, ergonomic intervention, and graduated return to work rather than surgical decompression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old woman with pain in multiple joints, particularly hands and feet. Symptoms worse in the morning, with stiffness that takes 2 hours to improve. Symmetric distribution.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to grip or perform fine motor tasks. Symptoms are worst in the mornings before activity, then gradually improve throughout the day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset 6 months ago. Progressive functional decline. Smoking history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable warmth and soft tissue swelling at MCPs and PIPs bilaterally. Morning stiffness reproduced on examination. No nodules or deformity palpated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays demonstrate uniform joint space narrowing across multiple MCPs and PIPs with periarticular osteopenia. Serology positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies.

puzzles.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis is a systemic autoimmune condition where anti-CCP antibodies and rheumatoid factor drive chronic inflammation of the synovial lining, leading to progressive joint destruction if untreated. The hallmark presentation—symmetric polyarticular involvement of small joints with morning stiffness lasting hours—reflects the inflammatory nature of the process, which is typically worst after periods of inactivity and improves with movement. The early radiographic findings here of periarticular osteopenia and uniform joint space narrowing, combined with positive serology, confirm the diagnosis at a stage before irreversible erosions or hand deformities develop. Smoking is a significant modifiable risk factor that accelerates disease progression, making early recognition and immunosuppressive intervention critical to preserve function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult with insidious-onset hip or groin pain, worsening with activity and improving with rest. Morning stiffness lasting 15-30 minutes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with loaded hip flexion (stairs, car transfers, bending) and sustained walking. Pain particularly felt in groin, worse later in day with fatigue. Can walk short distances without difficulty.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual progressive onset over months to years; possible previous hip injury or surgery. BMI elevated; pain steadily worsening despite activity modification.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Limited hip internal rotation and flexion, pain at end-range hip movements. Trendelenburg gait present, pain with log-roll test. Hip adductor tightness noted on stretch.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays show joint space narrowing, osteophytes, and sclerosis characteristic of osteoarthritis.

puzzles.explanation: Hip osteoarthritis is degenerative joint disease characterized by progressive loss of articular cartilage, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes—typically developing over years in response to cumulative loading, previous trauma, or structural predisposition. The groin pain, morning stiffness, and functional difficulty with loaded hip flexion (stairs, transfers) are hallmark presentations, as the anterior joint capsule and iliopsoas are irritated by osteophytes and loss of joint space. The insidious worsening despite activity modification and presence of a Trendelenburg gait reflect underlying capsular tightness and hip abductor weakness secondary to pain inhibition and disuse. Imaging confirmation of joint space narrowing and osteophytes distinguishes OA from inflammatory arthropathies, and early recognition allows timely intervention to optimize function and delay progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old with gradual onset of shortness of breath over the past week. Reports feeling unusually fatigued and experiencing mild chest discomfort. No fever or cough. Smoker with history of hypertension.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Breathlessness now triggered by walking up stairs or brisk walking on flat ground. Can manage slow walking but feels limited. Denies orthopnea but notices fatigue increases with any exertion. No relief with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Immobilized for 3 weeks following recent cancer treatment requiring bed rest and limited mobility. Patient noticed right calf swelling and mild calf tenderness 5 days ago, attributed to inactivity. No recent surgery, fracture, or known clotting disorder. Lives sedentary lifestyle.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate elevated at 105 bpm. Oxygen saturation 92% on room air at rest, dropping to 88% with minimal exertion. Respiratory rate 22. Right calf circumference 2 cm larger than left. Bilateral ankle edema noted. Mild elevation of jugular venous pressure. No focal lung crackles or consolidation on auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: D-dimer markedly elevated at 2.8 μg/mL (reference <0.5). CT pulmonary angiography shows filling defects within segmental branches of right pulmonary artery and subsegmental left pulmonary arteries. Echocardiogram demonstrates acute right ventricular dilation with tricuspid regurgitation and elevated pulmonary artery pressures.

puzzles.explanation: Pulmonary embolism occurs when a thrombus, typically originating from a deep vein thrombosis in the lower extremities, lodges in the pulmonary arterial tree and obstructs blood flow to the lungs. This patient's recent immobilization following cancer treatment created the classic Virchow triad risk factors — stasis from bed rest, endothelial injury from malignancy, and hypercoagulability from cancer itself — culminating in the right calf swelling that preceded respiratory symptoms. The characteristic exertional dyspnea without orthopnea, combined with hypoxaemia disproportionate to examination findings (clear lungs), reflects ventilation-perfusion mismatch from perfused but non-ventilated lung segments. Acute right ventricular strain manifesting as elevated pulmonary pressures, RV dilation, and tricuspid regurgitation on echo, along with markedly elevated D-dimer and CT confirmation of pulmonary artery filling defects, establishes PE as a medical emergency requiring immediate anticoagulation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Athlete reports bilateral shin pain limiting training capacity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate impact loading; pain worsens with increases in training volume or intensity, improving with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic symptoms from high-volume training; poor recovery between sessions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral tenderness along medial tibial border; pain reproducible on palpation of posteromedial tibia. No focal bony step-off or point tenderness. Tibialis posterior and soleus demonstrate mild weakness; inversion strength mildly reduced bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bilateral periosteal changes on imaging, no fracture line; significant training-related stress evident.

puzzles.explanation: Medial tibial stress syndrome (MTSS) results from repetitive microtrauma and periosteal inflammation along the posteromedial tibia, typically from excessive impact loading in athletes without adequate recovery. The condition reflects cumulative stress on the tibialis posterior and soleus attachments rather than a discrete fracture; periosteal changes on imaging without a fracture line are pathognomonic. Bilateral presentation with pain reproducible along the medial tibial border and associated weakness in ankle inversion is classic, and the pain pattern—exacerbated by volume increases and relieved by rest—mirrors the underlying load-dependent inflammation. MTSS remains fundamentally a training error diagnosis; managing it requires load reduction, gradual progression, and addressing biomechanical factors like foot pronation or calf inflexibility that increase tibial stress.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 70-year-old female with acute onset of severe knee pain, swelling, and marked loss of knee bending. Mobility severely restricted. Recent surgical intervention to knee.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bend knee actively beyond 70 degrees. Cannot perform sit-to-stand or bed transfers without significant assistance. Quadriceps not firing; patient unable to straighten knee against gravity alone.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multi-year progressive knee arthritis affecting all three compartments, unresponsive to conservative management. Surgical intervention performed 1–3 days ago. BMI 33. No major comorbidities. Pre-operative knee range was severely limited.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Surgical wound clean, staples intact, no drainage. Moderate diffuse swelling around knee. Hard end-feel at flexion limit; pain-limited range. Significant quadriceps inhibition with extensor lag of 15–20 degrees. No signs of infection or instability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative AP and lateral knee X-rays: cemented prosthetic components well-aligned, no periprosthetic fractures, no loose or malpositioned implants. Alignment within normal parameters.

puzzles.explanation: Total knee replacement (TKR) is a surgical procedure in which severely arthritic knee joint surfaces are replaced with prosthetic components, typically performed for end-stage osteoarthritis unresponsive to conservative care. In the immediate post-operative period (1–3 days post-op), significant quadriceps inhibition is expected due to surgical trauma, pain, and swelling, manifesting as extensor lag and severe loss of active knee flexion — as seen in this patient's inability to achieve flexion beyond 70 degrees or perform functional tasks like sit-to-stand. The hard end-feel and pain-limited range reflect post-operative stiffness and inflammation rather than implant failure; well-aligned prosthetic components on imaging confirm successful surgical placement. Early physiotherapy focusing on pain management, quadriceps re-education, and progressive range-of-motion work is critical at this stage to prevent stiffness and restore functional mobility, which typically improves significantly over the first 6 weeks.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old runner reports medial foot pain that has developed gradually over four months, worse with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to run; pain limits prolonged standing. Experiences foot instability during walking, particularly on uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No recalled specific injury; pain developed without trauma. Patient reports family history of flat feet and hypermobility.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Single-leg stance reveals marked arch collapse. Navicular drop test ≥8 mm. Dorsiflexion and eversion weakness noted. Pain on spring ligament palpation between medial malleolus and navicular.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows ligament disruption; ultrasound demonstrates gap in ligament.

puzzles.explanation: The spring ligament (plantar calcaneonavicular ligament) is a critical dynamic stabilizer of the medial longitudinal arch, particularly during single-leg stance and push-off. When injured, it permits excessive navicular descent and arch collapse, leading to progressive functional limitations like the marked instability and reduced weight-bearing tolerance seen in this runner. Spring ligament injury classically develops insidiously in individuals with inherent ligamentous laxity or hypermobility—as reflected in this patient's family history—rather than from acute trauma, and is confirmed by direct tenderness at the medial midfoot combined with imaging evidence of ligament disruption. The key distinguishing feature is the dramatic navicular drop on single-leg testing, which reflects loss of the arch's dynamic support and is virtually pathognomonic for this injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old female. Gradual onset of pressure sensation in lower abdomen and perineum over past 2 years. Symptoms progressively worse as day goes on. Reports feeling 'something there' that improves when lying down.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged standing or walking triggers or worsens discomfort. Sitting for long periods also problematic. Symptoms improve significantly with rest in supine. Avoids exercise and social outings due to progressive nature of symptoms throughout the day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Three vaginal deliveries with instrumental assistance required. Long history of chronic straining with bowel movements over 40+ years. Post-menopausal for 20 years. No recent trauma or surgery. Progressive symptom onset without acute event.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation of perineal body reveals reduced tissue tone and support. With Valsalva maneuver, downward descent of tissues noted. Anterior and posterior vaginal wall bulging evident. Pelvic floor strength markedly reduced on manual assessment. Visible tissue protrusion beyond introitus at rest and with straining.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic MRI with dynamic imaging during straining shows marked descent of pelvic viscera beyond normal anatomical boundaries. Defecography confirms significant posterior wall bulging and impaired evacuation. Ultrasound demonstrates excessive pelvic floor motion and loss of normal support structures.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic organ prolapse results from weakening and laxity of the pelvic floor support structures — the fascia, ligaments, and muscles that suspend the bladder, uterus, and rectum — allowing these organs to descend and herniate through the vaginal introitus. The condition develops cumulatively from childbirth trauma (particularly instrumental delivery), chronic straining, estrogen deficiency in menopause, and aging, all of which compromise connective tissue integrity and neuromuscular function. The classic presentation of progressive perineal heaviness worsening throughout the day and relieved by recumbency reflects the gravitational load on compromised supports during upright activity. Dynamic imaging revealing excessive visceral descent beyond normal anatomical boundaries, combined with markedly reduced pelvic floor strength on examination, confirms the mechanical failure of support that defines this condition.
