clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports posterior knee swelling with associated calf pain and tightness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to fully extend knee; symptoms worse with prolonged standing and walking. Occasional acute exacerbations with sudden increase in calf swelling and pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of gradual onset swelling over months to years. Patient reports episodic knee pain exacerbated by activity. Recent increase in symptoms following increased activity level.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable mass in popliteal fossa, tender on deep palpation. Knee flexion limited to 90 degrees. Primary knee joint crepitus and mild effusion present. Calf soft tissue swelling present but neurovascular exam normal, capillary refill brisk.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows large cyst with possible rupture, fluid extravasation into calf. Underlying knee joint changes associated with OA or meniscal.

puzzles.explanation: A Baker's cyst is a popliteal fossa synovial outpouching that develops secondary to intra-articular knee pathology—typically osteoarthritis or meniscal tears—where elevated joint pressure forces synovial fluid into the posterior knee space. The cyst can remain asymptomatic or cause posterior knee fullness and restriction of knee extension, but becomes clinically significant when it ruptures, allowing fluid to dissect into the calf and mimic deep vein thrombosis with acute swelling, pain, and tightness. The chronic intermittent nature of this patient's symptoms, combined with the palpable mass in the popliteal fossa and imaging evidence of rupture with calf fluid extravasation, is pathognomonic. Management focuses on addressing the underlying intra-articular lesion rather than the cyst itself, as resolution of the primary pathology typically leads to cyst resorption.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young athlete experiences sudden arm pain and weakness after contact injury. Burning quality to pain radiating into hand.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot lift arm overhead or abduct shoulder; grip strength markedly reduced on affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute impact during contact sport with neck distraction or shoulder compression mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Deltoid and rotator cuff weakness; sensory loss in C5-C6 distribution; intact motor branches distally; no Horner's sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI may show nerve edema, often normal in neurapraxia, and assess for avulsion in severe cases.

puzzles.explanation: Brachial plexus injury occurs when the network of nerves exiting the cervical spine (C5-T1) is traumatized by traction, compression, or impact — commonly from contact sports involving neck distraction or shoulder depression mechanisms. The upper trunk (C5-C6) is most frequently affected, explaining the characteristic weakness in shoulder abduction and overhead lifting alongside sensory loss in the radial forearm and thumb distribution. Neurapraxia, the mildest form involving myelin damage without axonal disruption, is most common in athletes and often recovers spontaneously within weeks to months, though more severe injuries involving axonal damage or root avulsion carry poorer prognoses. The preservation of distal motor function and absence of Horner's sign here indicate the lesion is proximal without involvement of sympathetic fibers, helping classify severity and guide prognosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with midfoot pain following an ankle injury months ago. Pain worsening despite initial treatment.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate single-leg stance or push off during walking. Pain significantly limits running and jumping activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute inversion ankle injury 12 months ago. Initially treated conservatively for ankle sprain, then discharged. Midfoot pain began 2 months post-injury and has not resolved despite rest and physio.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive midfoot abduction stress test (forefoot abducted passively with hindfoot fixed). Increased midfoot hypermobility compared to contralateral side. No tenderness over syndesmosis or anterior ankle. Subtle increase in height of medial midfoot arch bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing X-rays show increased gap between first and second cuneiforms (>5mm). CT demonstrates articular surface irregularity at the tarsometatarsal joints with early post-traumatic OA. No acute fracture; findings consistent with chronic ligamentous disruption.

puzzles.explanation: Lisfranc injury involves disruption of the ligamentous complex—particularly the plantar ligament and interosseous ligament—that stabilizes the tarsometatarsal (TMT) joints where the midfoot articulates with the metatarsal bases. These injuries are frequently missed on initial presentation when radiographs appear normal, only to declare themselves months later as chronic midfoot pain and progressive instability due to unrepaired ligamentous laxity. The characteristic findings here—increased TMT joint gaps on weight-bearing X-ray, forefoot abduction laxity on examination, and early post-traumatic arthrosis of the TMT joints—reflect chronic ligamentous incompetence that cannot generate the stiffness required for push-off and single-leg stance. This delayed presentation after an apparently routine inversion ankle sprain underscores why high-index-of-suspicion imaging and stress testing of the midfoot remain essential in ankle injury assessment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 78-year-old presenting 2–3 weeks post-discharge from acute hospital stay. Reports feeling 'foggy,' easily fatigued, and moving slower than before. Family notes patient seems confused about timing of events and forgets what was discussed minutes earlier.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk safely without supervision; requires assistance with stairs, bathing, dressing. Difficulty concentrating on simple tasks (reading, TV). Easily distracted by minor noises or activity in the room. Frequently disoriented to time and place during the day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Admitted 3 weeks ago with severe infection (sepsis). Spent 10 days in ICU on antibiotics and supportive care. During hospitalization, was acutely confused, agitated, required sedation. No prior cognitive decline. No head injury, stroke, or neuro-psychiatric history. Infection now resolved clinically.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Proximal leg weakness (hip flexors 3/5, knee extensors 3+/5); distal strength intact. Gait slow, unsteady, reduced stride length. Attention testing: cannot sustain focus >90 seconds; fails to complete 3-step commands. Alert but inconsistently oriented (knows name, not date). No focal neurological deficits, cranial nerves intact, reflexes normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT head: no acute stroke, bleed, or mass. Metabolic panel normal (electrolytes, glucose, renal function, liver function). Cognitive screening (Montreal Cognitive Assessment) score 18/30, consistent with mild-to-moderate impairment. Electroencephalogram: slow background activity, no seizures. Cultures negative at discharge.

puzzles.explanation: Delirium in the rehabilitation phase represents the ongoing cognitive and functional recovery after an acute delirious episode, typically following critical illness such as sepsis. The acute infection triggered diffuse cerebral dysfunction—manifested during hospitalization as agitation and confusion—and while the infection has resolved, the brain's recovery lags behind physiological markers. Post-ICU delirium rehabilitation is characterized by persistent inattention, disorientation to time and place, proximal weakness from critical illness myopathy, and fatigue lasting weeks to months even after infection clearance and normal metabolic workup. This patient's preserved alertness, lack of focal neurological signs, slow EEG background activity, and difficulty sustaining attention over 90 seconds are hallmark features of the rehabilitation phase—distinct from the acute hyperactive or hypoactive delirium seen in the ICU, and requiring graded mobility, cognitive retraining, and realistic expectation-setting as core rehabilitation goals.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old athlete. Insidious onset mid-back pain over 8 weeks. No trauma. Dull ache, occasionally sharp with certain movements. No radiation, no night pain, no systemic symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered by overhead reaching and trunk rotation during sport. Stiffness worst in morning, improves with gentle movement. Unable to perform throwing motions or overhead press at baseline intensity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden increase in training volume 10 weeks ago. Previous episodes of similar mid-back tightness, resolved with rest. No prior trauma, fracture, or inflammatory history. Pain does not wake patient at night. Unilateral rib cage feels restricted compared to opposite side.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Thoracic extension and ipsilateral rotation significantly limited in mid-thoracic region (T5-T8 levels). Palpation reveals unilateral rib cage restriction and segmental stiffness. Rib spring test reduced on one side. Full neurological exam normal. Strength 5/5 throughout bilateral upper extremities. No scapular dyskinesis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows normal vertebral alignment, disc heights, and rib positioning. No fracture, no scoliosis, no degenerative changes. Clinical diagnosis supported by restriction pattern on movement screening and positive response to manual therapy and mobility work directed at thoracic segments.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic spine dysfunction describes a mechanical restriction of segmental mobility in the mid-thoracic region, typically arising from muscular stiffness, facet joint irritation, or rib cage asymmetry rather than structural pathology. The insidious onset following a training load increase and the selective limitation of extension and ipsilateral rotation at T5–T8 are hallmark features, as this region naturally resists rotational demand and adapts poorly to sudden volume changes. The unilateral rib cage restriction and positive rib spring test suggest associated costotransverse joint involvement, which commonly co-occurs with thoracic segmental stiffness and explains the overhead and throwing-related pain in this athlete. Unlike disc herniation or inflammatory conditions, thoracic dysfunction presents with normal imaging and preserved neurological findings, making movement pattern analysis and manual mobility assessment the clinical cornerstone of both diagnosis and management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 8-year-old child presenting with acute onset of neck pain and head tilt to one side. Parents report child holding head in unusual position since yesterday. No trauma reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child reluctant to turn head toward affected side. Neck movements guarded and limited. Prefers to rotate trunk instead of turning neck. Complains of muscle tightness and discomfort with attempted correction.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms developed acutely over 12-24 hours. Child had upper respiratory infection 1-2 weeks prior with fever and sore throat, now resolved. No history of neck trauma, falls, or unusual activity. No prior episodes of similar symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Head tilted away from midline with chin rotated toward opposite shoulder. Unilateral neck muscle firm and tender to palpation. Cervical rotation limited toward affected side; extension mildly restricted. No neurological deficits. Full upper extremity strength and sensation intact. Pupil size and reactivity normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of neck shows unilateral sternocleidomastoid or upper trapezius muscle echogenicity changes consistent with acute myositis. MRI cervical spine normal; no subluxation, bony abnormality, or cord pathology. Inflammatory markers mildly elevated. Diagnosis supported by post-viral myositis presentation with imaging correlation.

puzzles.explanation: Acquired torticollis in children is an acute onset neck deformity typically triggered by viral illness or upper respiratory infection, where inflammatory changes develop in the neck musculature—most commonly the sternocleidomastoid or upper trapezius—causing muscle spasm and contracture. The characteristic head tilt away from the affected side with chin rotation toward the opposite shoulder reflects protective muscle guarding and the mechanical constraint imposed by the inflamed, shortened muscle. Unlike congenital torticollis, which develops in infancy and often involves fibrosis of the sternocleidomastoid, the acquired form emerges acutely over hours to days following systemic viral illness, with imaging showing acute myositis rather than structural abnormality. The dramatic improvement typically occurs over weeks with conservative management—stretching, gentle mobilisation, and NSAIDs—making the post-viral myositic origin clinically and radiologically evident.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old male athlete presents with intermittent knee pain that has been worsening over the past three months. He plays competitive basketball and notices the knee sometimes catches or locks during games.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are aggravated by running, jumping, and pivoting movements. He reports occasional episodes where the knee feels like it locks momentarily before releasing. There is mild swelling after intense activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No specific traumatic injury recalled. The symptoms began insidiously and have gradually progressed. He has increased his training load significantly over the past six months in preparation for the competitive season.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: There is tenderness on palpation of the medial femoral condyle. Wilson's test is positive, with pain on internal rotation of the tibia during extension that resolves with external rotation. A small effusion is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals a well-defined osteochondral lesion of the medial femoral condyle with evidence of partial fragment separation. The overlying cartilage shows signs of instability with fluid signal tracking beneath the fragment.

puzzles.explanation: Osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) is a condition where a segment of subchondral bone gradually separates from its blood supply, leading to necrosis and potential detachment of the overlying articular cartilage and bone fragment. It most commonly affects adolescent athletes during periods of rapid growth and increased training intensity, particularly at the medial femoral condyle of the knee — the weightbearing surface most susceptible to repetitive compressive and shear forces. The insidious onset following a training load increase, combined with mechanical symptoms like catching and locking, reflects the progressive destabilization of the osteochondral fragment as it partially separates. Wilson's test — eliciting pain with tibial internal rotation in extension — is the classic provocative maneuver for OCD of the medial femoral condyle, and MRI findings of fragment instability with fluid tracking beneath it determine whether the lesion remains in situ or is at imminent risk of complete displacement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent knee swelling and pain following recent knee surgery. Quadriceps weakness noted with difficulty walking. Minimal sensation of instability.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking on flat surfaces improving but stairs remain challenging. Difficulty with pivoting and cutting movements. Unable to bear weight on affected leg for dynamic activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: ACL tear sustained 6 weeks ago during soccer with pivot-shift mechanism. Concomitant meniscal tear. Treated surgically. No prior knee surgery or significant past medical history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Effusion present, Lachman test shows improved stability compared to acute injury. Anterior drawer test moderately positive. Quadriceps lag evident strength approximately 4-/5 and range of motion limited in extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows appropriate graft positioning and early integration into bone tunnels. No graft rupture or retear. Associated meniscal repair healing appropriately.

puzzles.explanation: Post-ACL reconstruction represents the early rehabilitation phase following surgical graft placement, typically the first 8-12 weeks when the graft is establishing bony integration at the tunnel entrances—a critical window for functional recovery. The persistent quadriceps weakness and extensor lag are hallmark features of this phase, reflecting both the trauma of surgery and reflex inhibition from joint effusion and pain, which must be addressed aggressively to prevent long-term strength deficits. The residual difficulty with dynamic pivoting and weight-bearing on stairs reflects incomplete neuromuscular control and proprioceptive retraining, even as the Lachman test demonstrates significantly improved passive stability compared to the acute tear. Early post-operative management focuses on restoring quadriceps strength through isolated and functional strengthening, regaining full extension range of motion, and progressively loading the graft while protecting it from excessive rotational stress.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman with known osteoporosis presents with sudden severe pain in the right hip and groin after a fall from standing at home. She cannot stand or walk.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to internally rotate or adduct the hip. Pain is significantly worse with any attempt to move the leg into these ranges compared to other movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Osteoporosis, postmenopausal. Fall from standing, multiple prior fractures.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexed, abducted and externally rotated at rest. Severe pain on internal rotation and adduction. Log roll test produces severe groin pain. No crepitus. Neurological exam intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray demonstrates a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture with varus angulation and shortening.

puzzles.explanation: Hip fractures occur when the femoral neck—the narrow region between the femoral head and shaft—breaks, typically from a fall or direct trauma in older adults with compromised bone quality. Intracapsular fractures within the joint capsule carry significant risk of femoral head necrosis due to disruption of the lateral femoral circumflex artery, the main blood supply to the femoral head. The characteristic resting posture of hip flexion, abduction, and external rotation reflects the pull of unopposed hip musculature and is a protective mechanism to minimize pain; patients resist internal rotation and adduction because these movements compress the fracture site and stretch the joint capsule. The displaced nature with varus angulation seen on imaging indicates the proximal fragment has shifted into a precarious position, explaining the severe pain with any attempt to mobilize the hip and the inability to bear weight—a hallmark presentation requiring urgent surgical intervention to restore vascularity and joint mechanics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adolescent with chronic thoracic pain and postural concerns.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty maintaining upright posture; pain worsens with prolonged sitting or activities requiring trunk extension; forward-curved posture develops over time.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive kyphosis noticed by parents, symptomatic since teen years, and adult residual symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Rigid thoracic kyphosis with loss of extension ROM; vertebral segments appear stiff rather than mobile; posterior chain tightness; pain reproducible at apical kyphotic level on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show anterior vertebral body wedging (≥5° across multiple consecutive thoracic vertebrae), irregular endplates, and loss of disc space height—consistent with resolved Schmorl's nodes and mature structural change.

puzzles.explanation: Scheuermann's disease is a structural kyphotic deformity of the thoracic spine resulting from repetitive microtrauma to the vertebral endplates during adolescent growth, causing anterior vertebral wedging and disc space collapse. The condition typically emerges in early-to-mid teens and is characterized by progressive, rigid kyphosis that resists correction—distinguishing it from postural kyphosis, which remains mobile and reducible. The anterior wedging of at least 5° across consecutive vertebrae, combined with irregular endplates and Schmorl's node sequelae, creates a structurally fixed deformity that persists into adulthood. Pain arises from the stiffened apical segments and posterior ligamentous tension as the spine attempts to accommodate the anterior collapse, making extension maneuvers and sustained upright posture consistently provocative.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old recreational golfer presents with gradual onset inner arm pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with grasping, throwing, or flexing the wrist and relief with rest and immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Repetitive gripping activities at work over the past 3 years, with pain onset following a weekend golf tournament. Previous left shoulder impingement treated conservatively 2 years ago, now resolved.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness on palpation of inner bony prominence and pain with resisted wrist flexion and mild weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates tendon inflammation, thickening, and X-rays show no fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Medial epicondylitis, or golfer's elbow, results from overuse and microtrauma to the common flexor-pronator tendon origin at the medial epicondyle, typically from repetitive wrist flexion and gripping. The condition classically presents with medial elbow pain that worsens with resisted wrist flexion and gripping activities—exactly the movements demanded in golf and prolonged occupational gripping—while rest relieves symptoms. The 3-year accumulation of repetitive stress combined with the acute golf tournament load is a typical injury pattern, and the ultrasound findings of tendon inflammation and thickening confirm the inflammatory overuse pathology. Unlike fracture or nerve compression, medial epicondylitis responds well to activity modification, eccentric loading, and graduated return to activity, making early recognition and conservative management essential to prevent chronicity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old female presenting with pain and swelling at the knee, with difficulty regaining movement. Symptoms acute in onset over the past 2-3 weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty achieving heel-to-buttock flexion and full knee extension. Struggles with stairs and sit-to-stand transfers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe osteoarthritis resistant to 6+ months of physiotherapy and injections. Open surgical approach used. Patient reports good pre-operative functional level relative to disease severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant effusion present. Knee flexion 60 degrees, extension lag 10 degrees. Quadriceps inhibition evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays show appropriate prosthetic alignment. Component position good. No instability.

puzzles.explanation: Total knee replacement is a surgical intervention for severe osteoarthritis in which the joint surfaces are resurfaced with metal and polyethylene components, restoring load-bearing capacity but requiring a period of protected healing and intensive rehabilitation. The acute presentation of swelling and significant flexion limitation in the immediate post-operative weeks reflects the inflammatory response to surgical trauma and the inherent stiffness that accompanies soft tissue disruption and capsular tightening. The quadriceps inhibition and extension lag are characteristic neuromuscular sequelae following TKR, driven by pain-mediated reflex suppression and require aggressive active and passive mobilization to prevent permanent loss of range. Early mobilization outcomes depend on surgeon technique and post-operative rehabilitation intensity, making the first 4-6 weeks critical to restoring functional movement patterns such as stair negotiation and sit-to-stand transfers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 78-year-old bedbound patient. Reports localized pain and discomfort over bony prominence (sacrum, heels, or hips). Area feels warm to touch. Onset over 7-10 days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to reposition self or transfer independently. Spends 12+ consecutive hours daily in same position without weight relief or repositioning by caregivers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prolonged immobility for 4+ weeks due to acute illness or surgery. Poor nutritional intake. History of diabetes or peripheral vascular disease. Skin integrity previously intact; no prior open wounds at this site.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Skin erythema (blanching or non-blanching) over pressure point. Possible blister formation or shallow break in skin. Dermis may be visible but wound edges remain intact. Minimal purulent drainage if any. Palpable induration surrounding visible lesion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates partial-thickness loss with intact deeper fascial planes. No significant undermining or tracking beyond visible wound margins. MRI (if performed) shows inflammation confined to dermis/shallow subcutaneous tissue without deep involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Pressure injuries develop from sustained mechanical loading over bony prominences, causing ischaemic tissue necrosis that progresses from partial- to full-thickness involvement depending on pressure magnitude and duration. Stage 2-3 injuries represent the critical transition where superficial skin loss (epidermis and dermis) becomes visible as blisters or shallow ulcers, but deeper structures remain intact — distinguishing them from stage 4 lesions that expose muscle and bone. The presentation here is classic: prolonged immobility combined with poor nutrition and vascular compromise creates a perfect storm for pressure ulcer development, with the sacrum and heels bearing the greatest load in bedbound patients. Early recognition and aggressive off-loading are essential, as stage 2-3 lesions remain potentially reversible if pressure is relieved and wound care optimized, whereas deeper stages carry significant morbidity and slower healing trajectories.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old office worker presents with persistent mid-thoracic stiffness and aching that has worsened over six months. Localized pain between shoulder blades with morning stiffness improving throughout the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain posture for extended periods; pain increases with reaching forward or overhead activities; some relief with manual therapy.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No specific traumatic event; symptoms coincided with new desk setup and remote work three months ago; no previous spinal issues; denies trauma or falls.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced thoracic rotation, tenderness over mid-thoracic segments, and no neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT/MRI reveals minor facet joint changes and disc desiccation without neural compromise; no structural pathology explaining functional restriction.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic spine dysfunction is a mechanical disorder arising from postural strain, muscular imbalance, and segmental stiffness without significant structural pathology — a hallmark presentation in sedentary workers transitioning to prolonged seated postures. The mid-thoracic region is particularly vulnerable to dysfunction because it normally has modest mobility and bears significant load through kyphotic positioning at a desk; prolonged flexion loading leads to posterior chain weakness, anterior chest tightness, and reversible joint stiffness rather than degenerative change. The combination of morning stiffness improving with movement, reduced rotation, and relief with manual therapy reflects the neuromuscular and mechanical nature of the problem — tight facet joint capsules and thoracic extensors responding to mobilization rather than fixed structural damage. Unlike true discogenic or facet-mediated pain with neural involvement, thoracic dysfunction is fundamentally a postural-movement disorder that responds well to repositioning, strengthening, and restoring segmental mobility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 78-year-old with acute onset of severe groin and upper thigh pain. Pain began suddenly during mobilization 2 weeks ago. No recent trauma reported. Pain radiates to lateral hip and knee.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to weight-bear or stand without severe sharp pain and sensation of hip giving way. Walking aids provide no relief. Pain worsens with any hip rotation or crossing legs. Previously mobile without assistive devices.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hip surgery performed 3 months ago for orthopedic injury; procedure involved internal fixation with plates and screws. Initial post-operative recovery appeared normal. Patient is on corticosteroids for chronic respiratory condition. Bone density noted to be poor on prior imaging.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked pain with passive hip internal/external rotation and adduction. Positive Trendelenburg sign. Palpable clunking sensation on hip rotation under load. No swelling, warmth, or skin changes. Neurovascular examination intact. Hip flexion and abduction severely limited by pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows loss of reduction at original fixation site with lateral cortical breach and medial comminution. Fracture line visible at proximal femoral shaft with hardware malposition. Severe osteopenia throughout pelvis and femur. CT confirms gap formation at fracture site with screw backing out.

puzzles.explanation: A subtrochanteric fracture is a break in the proximal femur just below the greater trochanter, a biomechanically challenging site where the shaft transitions from the wide metaphysis and experiences high tensile and shear forces. These fractures are particularly prone to failure in older patients with osteopenia or poor bone quality, especially when treated with internal fixation—the corticosteroid use and documented poor bone density in this case created ideal conditions for hardware failure and loss of reduction. The sudden onset during mobilization 2-3 months post-operatively, combined with the palpable clunking and imaging findings of screw backing-out and medial comminution, indicates classic mechanical failure of fixation rather than initial healing. The severe limitations in weight-bearing, hip rotation, and the positive Trendelenburg sign reflect both the underlying fracture displacement and loss of femoral head-neck stability—hallmark signs that the original construct has failed and the fracture has redisplaced.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged patient with progressive leg weakness and difficulty with breathing that worsens toward evening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot climb stairs, requiring mechanical ventilation at night, and weakness improves after rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Started with eye drooping 1 year ago, progressed to generalized weakness over months. Recently hospitalized for acute respiratory decline requiring intubation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ptosis and ophthalmoplegia bilaterally. Weakness of facial and bulbar muscles (nasal speech, difficulty with chewing). Strength deficits worsen after sustained muscle contraction; improve after rest.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Repetitive nerve stimulation demonstrates >10% decremental response in proximal muscles. Anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies positive.

puzzles.explanation: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder of the neuromuscular junction where antibodies attack nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, preventing normal neuromuscular transmission and causing progressive muscle weakness with activity. The hallmark pathophysiology—fatigable weakness that worsens with use and improves with rest—distinguishes it from primary muscle or nerve diseases, as seen in this patient's inability to sustain stair climbing yet recovery after rest. The characteristic ocular onset with ptosis and ophthalmoplegia, progressing to bulbar and respiratory involvement, is typical of generalized myasthenia gravis, particularly in middle-aged patients where anti-receptor antibodies are present. Repetitive nerve stimulation showing decremental response (>10% amplitude drop) reflects the reduced safety margin of neuromuscular transmission, and the positive antibody titre confirms the autoimmune etiology requiring immunosuppressive management alongside cholinesterase inhibitors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 7-year-old child. Parents report clumsiness, frequent tripping, and difficulty with motor tasks peers manage easily. Child also avoids certain clothing textures and reacts strongly to loud noises and bright lights.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot keep up in team sports or group PE classes. Struggles with ball skills and crossing midline. Prefers repetitive, solitary play. Coordination noticeably worse when doing two things at once.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Developmental milestones delayed: walked at 18 months, speech started around age 3. No significant injury or acute change. Sensory sensitivities (textures, sounds, lights) present since infancy. Currently in mainstream school with support. Receives occupational therapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gait asymmetrical and energy-inefficient; poor dynamic balance. Difficulty with tandem stance and crossing midline bilaterally. Reduced proprioceptive awareness—struggles to grade force or judge limb position. Hyper-responsive to light touch; avoidant reactions to textures. Fine motor tasks (pencil grip, fasteners) immature for age.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Functional MRI shows altered connectivity in motor planning, sensory integration, and social-cognitive networks. Standardised motor scales (GMFM, PDMS) confirm delays in gross and fine motor milestones. Sensory Profile questionnaire shows significant sensory modulation dysfunction across multiple channels.

puzzles.explanation: Autism Spectrum Disorder is a neurodevelopmental condition characterized by altered connectivity across motor planning, sensory integration, and social-cognitive networks, resulting in pervasive difficulties with motor coordination, sensory processing, and social communication. Children with ASD commonly present with dyspraxia-like features—poor bilateral coordination, difficulty crossing midline, and inefficient motor planning—alongside sensory modulation dysfunction that manifests as hypersensitivity to tactile, auditory, and visual inputs. The delayed motor milestones (walking at 18 months, speech delay) combined with lifelong sensory sensitivities and the characteristic struggle with dual-task coordination seen in this case reflect the core motor and sensory processing differences underlying ASD. Occupational and physical therapy targeting sensory integration, motor planning, and functional task practice remain essential interventions to improve motor efficiency and reduce avoidant behaviors that limit participation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 64-year-old female with recurrent episodes of room-spinning vertigo lasting seconds to minutes. Triggered by lying down or rolling in bed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo occurs with specific positional changes: head tilted back, rolling in bed, looking up. Can predict and avoid triggering positions. Between episodes, completely normal.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Vertigo onset insidious, 3 weeks ago. No recent head trauma or significant illness. No prior balance problems or vestibular disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Alert and oriented. Gait and balance normal between episodes. No neurological deficits. Positive Dix-Hallpike test elicits vertigo with characteristic nystagmus.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hallmark feature: brief (<60 seconds) rotatory nystagmus with Dix-Hallpike maneuver. Nystagmus direction consistent with posterior canal involvement. No imaging typically needed.

puzzles.explanation: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) occurs when otoliths (calcium carbonate crystals) become dislodged within the semicircular canals of the inner ear, most commonly the posterior canal, causing brief episodes of vertigo with specific head positions. The condition typically presents with sudden-onset, short-lived spinning sensations triggered reliably by positional changes — lying down, rolling in bed, or looking up — with complete symptom-free intervals between episodes, distinguishing it from other vestibular disorders. The positive Dix-Hallpike test producing characteristic brief rotatory nystagmus is the diagnostic hallmark and reflects the pathognomonic cupuloithiasis or canalithiasis mechanism. BPPV is highly amenable to physical therapy; canalith repositioning procedures like the Epley maneuver work by mechanically moving the dislodged crystals back into the utricle, often providing rapid resolution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged woman with groin and anterior hip pain, worse with prolonged sitting and certain movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with hip flexion and adduction; limited external rotation in flexion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset over months; no clear traumatic event; symptoms gradually worsened with running and hip-heavy activities; no prior hip surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: FABER test reproduces anterior hip pain; hip external rotation in flexion is limited to 15°; adductor tightness; pain with log-roll test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows over-coverage of acetabulum; coxa profunda or acetabular retroversion.

puzzles.explanation: Femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) occurs when abnormal hip bone morphology—typically acetabular over-coverage, coxa profunda, or retroversion—causes pathological contact between the femoral neck and acetabular rim during hip flexion and adduction. This chronic mechanical conflict gradually irritates the acetabular labrum and articular cartilage, producing the characteristic insidious groin pain that worsens with hip-loaded activities like running and prolonged sitting. The severely restricted external rotation in flexion and positive FABER test reflect the anatomical pinching; adductor tightness is a common protective response. Early recognition and activity modification are critical, as untreated FAI can progress to labral tears and osteoarthritis, making the distinction from simple flexor tightness clinically important.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 84-year-old woman reports increasing unsteadiness and balance problems over the past year. She moves more slowly than before and feels less confident walking without support. No recent falls or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with stair negotiation and walking on uneven surfaces. Requires handrails and close supervision. Gets lost in familiar environments and sometimes forgets why she started walking. Reduced speed but no pain with movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cognitive changes noticed 18 months ago: forgetfulness, word-finding difficulties, mood changes. Family reports insidious onset, gradual progression. No single event triggered decline. Multiple falls in past 6 months without loss of consciousness. Lives in residential care.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mini-Cog score 12/30. Gait slow and cautious with slightly widened base. Normal muscle strength throughout. Mild rigidity in neck and trunk. Resting tremor absent. Turning takes many small steps. Postural sway present. Cognition clearly impairs safety during mobility tasks. Cranial nerves intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain reveals generalised cortical atrophy with predominant frontal and parietal involvement. Ventricular enlargement disproportionate to sulcal widening. White matter changes in periventricular regions. No focal lesions, infarcts, or subdural collections. Pattern consistent with neurodegenerative process affecting cognition and motor control centres.

puzzles.explanation: Dementia-related mobility decline reflects the convergence of cognitive impairment and motor system dysfunction within a progressive neurodegenerative process, where cortical atrophy affecting frontal and parietal regions compromises both executive function and motor planning. The characteristic presentation—slowed gait with cautious steps, postural instability, and difficulty with complex motor tasks like stair negotiation—stems from frontal lobe involvement that impairs motor sequencing and attention, rather than from primary muscle weakness or pyramidal/extrapyramidal signs. The cognitive deficits themselves become a major fall risk factor, as memory loss and confusion compound the motor challenges; patients forget their destination mid-walk or cannot safely navigate environmental obstacles. This pattern of insidious, progressive decline with imaging evidence of generalized atrophy distinguishes dementia-related mobility decline from stroke, Parkinson's disease, or normal aging, and necessitates a mobility approach that emphasizes environmental modification, supervision, and cognitive cueing rather than standard strengthening or balance retraining alone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old male. Acute onset of bilateral lower limb weakness and numbness following a high-impact work accident. Reports loss of sensation in perineal area and difficulty with urinary control.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk or bear weight on legs. Bilateral leg strength severely reduced. Unable to perform hip and knee flexion/extension. Bowel and bladder function impaired.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Crushed between heavy machinery with direct compression to lower back. Incident occurred 4 hours ago. No prior spinal problems. Immediate onset of symptoms at time of injury. Transported emergently by EMS.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral lower limb weakness grade 1/5 throughout. Saddle anesthesia (S3-S5 distribution). Absent anal wink and bulbocavernosus reflex. Flaccid paralysis with no voluntary movement. Patchy sensory loss below L4.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI lumbar spine demonstrates hemorrhagic infarction within the conus medullaris with acute cord edema and compression from burst fracture at L1-L2 with retropulsion into spinal canal.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spinal cord injury, particularly at the conus medullaris level, results from traumatic compression causing hemorrhagic infarction and disruption of neural tissue — in this case from a burst fracture with retropulsion following high-impact trauma. The conus is especially vulnerable to compression injury because it represents the terminal portion of the spinal cord (around L1-L2), where sacral and lower lumbar nerve roots are densely packed, making it exquisitely sensitive to ischemic damage. The constellation of bilateral lower limb paralysis, saddle anesthesia across the S3-S5 dermatomes, and loss of bowel and bladder control with absent anal wink and bulbocavernosus reflexes is the hallmark presentation of conus medullaris syndrome — reflecting the simultaneous involvement of bilateral motor pathways and sacral parasympathetic fibers. The acute flaccid paralysis with hemorrhagic cord changes on MRI indicates severe tissue injury with poor prognosis for meaningful functional recovery, making emergency decompression and spinal stabilization critical within the first hours post-injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old reports gradual onset of jerky, unpredictable movements in arms and legs over past 18 months. Also notes increasing difficulty concentrating and mood changes. No recent head injury or medication changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Movements worse when trying to hold steady posture or reach for objects; improves slightly at rest. Difficulty maintaining balance while walking. Has dropped items repeatedly. Cannot sustain attention during conversations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Movements began gradually without clear trigger. Father experienced similar progressive movement disorder starting in his 50s; uncle also affected. No recent infections, toxin exposure, or head trauma. On no new medications.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral irregular, flowing involuntary movements of limbs and trunk; worse with attempted purposeful motion. Eye pursuit jerky and interrupted. Gait unsteady. Strength intact. Cognition slow; word-finding difficulty. Reflexes normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Genetic testing shows abnormal CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion (>39 repeats) in HTT gene on chromosome 4. Brain MRI confirms selective atrophy of caudate nucleus and putamen with ventricular enlargement.

puzzles.explanation: Huntington's disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder caused by expansion of CAG trinucleotide repeats in the HTT gene, leading to selective degeneration of the striatum (caudate and putamen). The disease typically manifests in midlife with the triad of involuntary movements (chorea), cognitive decline, and psychiatric symptoms — all evident in this patient's gradual onset of jerky limb movements, concentration difficulties, and mood changes. The bilateral, irregular choreiform movements that worsen with intentional motor tasks (Huntington's sign) combined with impaired saccadic eye pursuit are pathognomonic features reflecting striatal pathology. Strong family history of similar progressive movement disorders in his father and uncle, coupled with genetic confirmation of the CAG repeat expansion and characteristic MRI findings of caudate-putamen atrophy, make this diagnosis definitive.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old male with sudden onset severe upper arm pain and visible lump or swelling in the biceps region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weakness with elbow flexion and supination reduces arm strength.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He felt a sudden pop in the upper arm while lifting a heavy box overhead at work three days ago. No prior shoulder surgery or rotator cuff history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked weakness with elbow flexion and supination strength testing (grade 2/5). Visible bulge or absence of normal biceps contour in upper arm on inspection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows absent tendon at shoulder. MRI confirms rupture with retracted muscle.

puzzles.explanation: Long head of biceps rupture occurs when the tendon avulses from its attachment at the glenoid labrum, typically from a sudden eccentric load in middle-aged or older adults whose tendon has undergone degenerative changes. The mechanism — a sudden pop during overhead lifting — is classic for acute rupture, as the compromised tendon fails under high tensile stress. The visible bulge or lump represents the retracted muscle belly migrating distally in the arm, creating the characteristic "Popeye deformity," while the weakness in elbow flexion and supination reflects loss of the biceps' dual motor function. Though functionally limiting, isolated long head rupture is often managed conservatively in lower-demand patients, with the short head of biceps and brachialis providing residual flexion strength.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports significant pain in the knee with variable symptoms depending on activity level and time of day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking improving with single-point cane support. Knee flexion progressing but extension lag persists; sit-to-stand transfers improving.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of severe knee osteoarthritis refractory to conservative management. Presentation consistent with early post-operative recovery phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Well-healed incision with mild effusion. Active knee flexion approximately 90 degrees with 15-degree extension lag. Quadriceps weakness evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show proper prosthesis positioning with neutral limb alignment. No evidence of loosening.

puzzles.explanation: Post-total knee replacement (TKR) is a major reconstructive procedure requiring months of progressive rehabilitation to restore function and manage the inflammatory response to surgical trauma. Early post-operative recovery typically involves significant pain, quadriceps weakness, and an extension lag — the inability to fully straighten the knee actively — as neuromuscular control gradually returns despite improving passive range of motion. The variable pain pattern and activity-dependent symptoms reflect the balance between tissue healing and the mechanical demands of mobilization; walking with assistive device support and progressive sit-to-stand transfers are hallmark early goals that reduce pain while protecting the healing soft tissues. Well-aligned prosthesis position and absence of loosening are encouraging prognostic signs, but persistence of the extension lag and quadriceps weakness necessitate targeted strengthening and proprioceptive retraining to prevent long-term mobility limitations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent cough and shortness of breath over the past week. Experiencing fatigue and general malaise. No recent trauma or specific injury reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk more than a few steps without becoming breathless. Difficulty sleeping due to cough. Cannot perform usual daily activities. Symptoms worse with exertion, better with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 5-7 days ago during a period of upper respiratory congestion. No recent travel or sick contacts recalled. No significant past medical history. No recent medications. Gradual onset with progressive worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elevated respiratory rate at 28-32 breaths/min. Oxygen saturation 92-94% on room air. Chest wall movement asymmetrical. Crackles heard on auscultation over lower left region. Reduced breath sounds in left lower zone. No focal areas of tenderness on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates opacity with air bronchograms in left lower lobe. No pleural effusion noted. No pneumothorax. Findings consistent with alveolar consolidation.

puzzles.explanation: Pneumonia is an infection causing inflammation and consolidation of lung parenchyma, typically following upper respiratory viral illness that allows bacterial colonization of the lower airways. The classic presentation combines productive cough, dyspnea, and systemic symptoms like fatigue with objective signs of hypoxaemia, tachypnoea, and asymmetrical chest wall movement reflecting unilateral consolidation. The focal findings here—crackles and reduced breath sounds in the left lower lobe with radiographic evidence of alveolar consolidation—are hallmark features of bacterial pneumonia, which commonly affects dependent lung zones due to gravity-dependent pooling of secretions. Early recognition and treatment are critical, as the progressive dyspnoea on exertion and oxygen desaturation indicate significant respiratory compromise.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Increasing drainage and separation at incision site, beginning second post-operative week.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight or perform upper limb loading activities; prone positioning contraindicated due to wound location.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Major surgical procedure 10 days prior with initially unremarkable course. Risk factors include obesity, diabetes, poor nutrition, and immunosuppression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Partial wound separation with serosanguineous drainage. Surrounding tissue induration with minimal erythema; patient systemically stable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows separation of fascial layers with fluid collection. Early abscess formation possible.

puzzles.explanation: Wound dehiscence is partial or complete separation of surgical wound layers, typically occurring in the first 2–3 weeks post-operatively when tensile strength of healing tissue is still low. The condition results from excessive mechanical stress, impaired wound healing, or infection, with major risk factors including obesity, diabetes, malnutrition, and immunosuppression — all present in this case. The characteristic serosanguineous drainage and fascial layer separation visible on ultrasound, appearing in the second post-operative week, reflect disruption of the healing cascade before sufficient collagen cross-linking has occurred. Management requires immediate wound care assessment, possible fluid drainage, strict activity restriction, and careful monitoring for evolution to full dehiscence or abscess formation, as seen with the developing fluid collection.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young soccer player reports 'seeing stars' and brief confusion after heading the ball forcefully.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sat out game, reports feeling 'foggy' and having trouble remembering play sequences.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Second head impact to same region 3 weeks prior; reported worsening of fogginess and concentration issues since first incident despite return to light training.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: GCS 15, normal neurological exam, normal pupil response, mild headache, no focal deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT head normal. ImPACT baseline neurocognitive testing shows decline from pre-season scores in processing speed and reaction time; score consistent with concussive injury.

puzzles.explanation: Concussion is a form of mild traumatic brain injury characterized by transient disruption of cerebral function caused by biomechanical forces, typically from blunt head trauma or rapid acceleration–deceleration. The hallmark features include immediate post-injury symptoms like confusion and visual disturbances (the 'stars'), followed by persistent postconcussive syndrome involving cognitive impairment, memory difficulties, and fogginess—exactly what this athlete experienced after heading impact. Repeat head injury within weeks is particularly concerning, as the brain remains neurologically vulnerable during recovery; pre-season baseline testing and objective neurocognitive decline (processing speed, reaction time) provide critical evidence of injury even when structural imaging and bedside neurological examination appear normal. Management requires strict return-to-play protocols with symptom resolution and gradual exertional progression to prevent second-impact syndrome and persistent deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young professional with persistent groin discomfort interfering with gym workouts.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered during hip-intensive activities and dynamic movements; relieved with rest and activity modification.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset over 6 months; no prior hip injury or surgery. Symptoms began after increasing deadlift volume at the gym.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive anterior hip impingement test; pain on flexion-internal rotation combined movement. Hip flexion ROM limited to 100° and internal rotation to 10° in flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: 3D CT imaging demonstrates combined cam and pincer morphology.

puzzles.explanation: Femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) occurs when abnormal morphology of the femoral head-neck junction (cam lesion) or acetabular rim (pincer lesion) causes mechanical conflict during hip motion, most commonly in young, active adults. The condition is often asymptomatic until a specific activity—such as increased deadlifting—loads the hip into flexion and internal rotation, the precise movement pattern that narrows the joint space and provokes pain. Combined cam and pincer morphology, as revealed on imaging, creates a particularly restrictive mechanical pattern, explaining both the positive impingement testing and the significantly limited internal rotation in flexion. Early recognition and activity modification are critical, as repetitive impingement can progress to labral damage and secondary osteoarthritis if the underlying bony conflict remains unaddressed.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient 34-year-old occupational worker develops chest tightness. A 34-year-old occupational worker develops chest tightness, cough, and wheezing specifically during work shifts and weekends off symptom-free.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient is unable to perform sustained physical exertion; symptoms improve markedly with rest and are worse in the evening after activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has a childhood history of atopy and seasonal allergies. No prior respiratory diagnoses. Symptoms began 6 weeks ago and have progressively worsened. No recent infections or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, diffuse expiratory wheeze is audible on auscultation bilaterally. Peak expiratory flow rate is 52% of predicted. Accessory muscle use is present with mild tachycardia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms and no consolidation. Spirometry demonstrates FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.62 with 18% bronchodilator reversibility. Fractional exhaled nitric oxide is elevated at 42 ppb.

puzzles.explanation: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease characterized by reversible airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and eosinophilic inflammation — the elevated fractional exhaled nitric oxide here is a marker of that underlying inflammation. The work-related pattern of symptoms with symptom-free weekends is classic for occupational asthma, where workplace exposures trigger bronchoconstriction in a predisposed individual with an atopic history. The bilateral expiratory wheeze, hyperinflation on imaging, reduced FEV1/FVC ratio, and bronchodilator responsiveness all confirm the obstructive physiology, while accessory muscle recruitment and tachycardia reflect the increased work of breathing during acute exacerbation. Progressive deterioration over weeks, combined with atopic background and seasonal allergies, points to an underlying inflammatory sensitization rather than acute infection or mechanical obstruction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old warehouse worker. Acute onset of low back pain 3 days ago. Pain is constant, 7/10 severity, no trauma or fall.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with bending forward and prolonged sitting. Eases when lying flat or standing still. Reports difficulty with stairs and getting in/out of vehicles.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain began after lifting heavy boxes. Initially localized to lower back, but now radiates into the right buttock and outer thigh. No prior back surgery. No fever, no bowel/bladder changes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Straight leg raise test limited to 40° on the right with pain reproduction. Right ankle reflex diminished compared to left. Hip flexion and knee extension strength 4/5 on right. Palpable tenderness over low lumbar spine.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI lumbar spine shows right posterolateral disc protrusion at L5-S1 level with contact against the exiting L5 nerve root. No spinal cord compression. No fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar disc herniation occurs when the nucleus pulposus of an intervertebral disc ruptures through the annulus fibrosus, often triggered by flexion loading—as in this warehouse worker's heavy lifting. The posterolateral disc protrusion can directly irritate or compress adjacent nerve roots; in this case, contact with the exiting L5 nerve root explains the radicular pain into the buttock and lateral thigh, along with the diminished reflex and lower limb weakness. The characteristic pattern of worsening with flexion and sitting (increased intradiscal pressure) and relief with lying flat (reduced loading) is typical of discogenic pain, while the nerve root involvement manifests as reproducible straight leg raise pain and myotomal weakness. MRI confirmation of the protrusion site and nerve contact distinguishes this from other causes of acute low back pain and guides the prognosis and management strategy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 38-year-old woman presents with persistent tiredness and low energy lasting over a year. Reports generalized muscle soreness and difficulty concentrating. Symptoms are not explained by a current medical condition or medication.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to return to work. Activities of daily living cause disproportionate exhaustion lasting days. Light tasks like shopping or housework trigger significant fatigue worsening. Sleep quality poor despite increased rest attempts.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Illness onset 14 months ago following flu-like illness with fever and sore throat. Gradual progression with no recovery plateau. No prior autoimmune, thyroid, or psychiatric diagnoses. Multiple treatment attempts (rest, physiotherapy, cognitive behavioural therapy) have had minimal benefit.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vital signs normal at rest. Blood pressure drops significantly on standing (systolic drop 20+ mmHg). No motor weakness, sensory loss, or focal neurological signs. Mild cervical lymph node enlargement. Cognitive testing shows slow processing speed but normal orientation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Comprehensive metabolic panel, thyroid function, and standard immunology normal. Natural killer cell function reduced. Inflammatory markers mildly elevated. Two-day exercise challenge shows failure of cardiac output to increase appropriately and delayed symptom recovery over 48 hours.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS/ME) is a complex systemic condition characterized by profound, persistent fatigue disproportionate to activity level, accompanied by post-exertional malaise — a hallmark feature where minor physical or cognitive effort triggers delayed, severe symptom deterioration lasting days. The pathophysiology remains incompletely understood but involves dysregulation of immune function (evident here in reduced NK cell counts and mild inflammatory elevation), autonomic dysfunction (reflected in the orthostatic blood pressure response), and impaired cellular energy metabolism. Onset typically follows a viral illness, as in this patient's post-influenza presentation 14 months prior, suggesting a potential infectious trigger in genetically susceptible individuals. The failure of appropriate cardiac output response to exertion, combined with delayed recovery on two-day exercise challenge, distinguishes CFS from deconditioning or psychiatric causes and reflects the underlying physiological dysfunction central to this diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old warehouse worker with sudden leg pain following heavy lifting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with sitting, forward bending, and coughing. Partially relieved by lying on side with knees bent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Two previous back pain episodes over 5 years without leg symptoms. Smoker with sedentary lifestyle outside work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive straight leg raise at 35 degrees reproducing leg pain in a specific dermatomal pattern. Reduced ankle jerk with weak great toe extension and sensory loss in L5 distribution.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows posterolateral disc herniation at L4/L5 compressing the left L5 nerve root.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar disc herniation occurs when the nucleus pulposus breaches the annulus fibrosus, typically at the posterolateral aspect of the disc, compressing adjacent nerve roots and causing radicular leg pain in a dermatomal distribution. The acute onset following heavy lifting and the characteristic worsening with lumbar flexion and increased intradiscal pressure (sitting, forward bending, coughing) reflect the mechanical nature of this condition. L5 nerve root compression, as confirmed here by the positive straight leg raise, weak great toe extension, and L5 dermatomal sensory loss, is the most common compressive pattern at L4/L5 due to the posterolateral location of herniation. Recurrent episodes and smoking both impair disc nutrition and accelerate degenerative changes, making recurrence more likely than in the general population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Adult patient with acute knee pain after forced flexion or direct anterior tibial blow; minimal to moderate swelling over initial hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with kneeling and deep squatting, symptoms worsen with stairs. Less instability sensation compared to ACL injury.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury from high-energy mechanism; patient often unaware of complete injury details. May report feeling or hearing a pop at time of injury, with rapid effusion development.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive posterior sag sign at rest, posterior drawer test positive with knee at 90 degrees. Quadriceps strength assessment important.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates PCL fiber disruption with increased T2 signal throughout ligament; proximal or distal avulsion may be present. Associated posterolateral corner injury or medial meniscal tear common.

puzzles.explanation: The posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) is the primary restraint to posterior tibial translation, and disruption typically occurs through high-energy mechanisms such as forced knee flexion or direct anterior tibial blow — injuries that can occur so suddenly that patients are unaware of the full extent of damage. Unlike ACL injuries, PCL tears often produce deceptively minimal swelling initially and less subjective instability, making them frequently underdiagnosed on first presentation; however, the characteristic posterior sag sign at rest and positive posterior drawer test at 90° reveal the underlying loss of restraint. The functional deficit emerges with loading activities like deep squatting, stair descent, and kneeling, as the quadriceps must work harder to compensate for posterior laxity. MRI confirmation shows T2 signal hyperintensity throughout the ligament fibers or avulsion injury, and associated posterolateral corner or meniscal involvement is common with these high-energy mechanisms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old retired teacher reports gradual onset of visual discomfort over the past 3 years. Complains of eye strain, headaches, and fatigue when reading or doing detailed work. No eye pain, redness, or discharge.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient struggles to read small print on medication bottles, restaurant menus, and books despite previously having no reading difficulty. Holds materials at arm's length to see them clearly. Symptoms worse in low light and improve when wearing distance glasses. Near vision tasks cause fatigue within 15 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began around age 65. No history of eye disease, trauma, diabetes, or autoimmune conditions. Distance vision has remained stable; patient only wore distance correction previously. No recent changes in medications. Gradual, progressive course without sudden onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Distance visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally; near visual acuity significantly reduced without correction. Near point of accommodation measured at 40 cm (age-predicted normal ~25 cm). Extraocular movements intact. Pupils reactive and equal. Anterior segment examination unremarkable; no lens opacity, no angle closure signs. Visual fields normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cycloplegic refraction confirms inability of ciliary muscle to increase lens power sufficiently for near tasks. Optical coherence tomography shows normal retinal structure. No cataracts or macular pathology. Intraocular pressure within normal limits.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related vision impairment, or presbyopia, results from progressive hardening and loss of elasticity in the crystalline lens combined with weakening of the ciliary muscle, making accommodation for near vision increasingly difficult with age. This condition typically emerges in the fifth to sixth decade of life and is nearly universal by age 65, as seen in this retired teacher whose distance vision remained sharp while near vision deteriorated significantly. The characteristic presentation—struggling with small print, holding materials at arm's length, and fatigue with sustained near work—reflects the increased accommodative demand that the aging lens can no longer meet. Unlike pathological eye disease, presbyopia shows a gradual, predictable course with normal retinal structure, intraocular pressure, and ocular health, making it a refractive rather than pathological condition best managed with near optical correction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of acute vertigo lasting 2-3 hours, accompanied by unilateral ear fullness and roaring tinnitus. The episodes began 18 months ago and have progressively worsened. Between attacks, she reports fluctuating low-frequency hearing loss and mild persistent dizziness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During acute episodes, she experiences severe nausea with vomiting and is unable to walk safely. She has noticed that exposure to loud noise and crowded environments tends to trigger or worsen symptom clusters. Her symptoms are unpredictable in timing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hypertension is managed on medication. She reports that high salt intake and periods of stress seem to cluster her symptom episodes. She denies recent head injury or upper respiratory infection. No known family history of similar vestibular or hearing disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, during an acute episode, horizontal nystagmus is present with characteristic rotatory component. Romberg test shows significant instability. Dix-Hallpike maneuver does not reproduce vertigo. Caloric testing demonstrates reduced vestibular response on the affected side. Audiometry shows sensorineural hearing loss pattern in low frequencies.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the temporal bones with high-resolution sequences shows asymmetric enlargement of the membranous labyrinth (endolymphatic hydrops) on the affected side. No mass at the cerebellopontine angle. No demyelinating lesions or other structural abnormalities.

puzzles.explanation: Ménière's disease is a chronic inner ear disorder characterized by pathological accumulation of endolymph within the membranous labyrinth (endolymphatic hydrops), leading to the classic triad of episodic vertigo, fluctuating hearing loss, and tinnitus. The acute episodes result from sudden pressure increases distorting the basilar membrane, causing both vestibular (vertigo, nystagmus) and cochlear (hearing loss, roaring tinnitus) dysfunction—the unilateral presentation and asymmetric labyrinthine enlargement on imaging are diagnostically defining. The progressive low-frequency sensorineural loss, unpredictable timing, and exacerbation with salt intake and stress reflect the idiopathic nature of endolymph regulation dysfunction, though the precise etiology remains unclear. Caloric testing demonstrating reduced vestibular response on the affected side provides objective evidence of unilateral vestibular hypofunction, distinguishing this from central causes like multiple sclerosis or posterior circulation disorders.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive low back and hip pain over 2 years, which has significantly impacted his mobility and independence. He reports increasing bone pain in the lumbar spine and proximal femur, particularly with weight-bearing activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: He demonstrates a slightly widened gait and reports pain with prolonged standing. Walking distances have diminished from 2 miles to 200 meters. He experiences pain relief with rest but finds the pain reoccurs with minimal activity, restricting his previously active lifestyle.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He has a family history of Paget's disease (father was affected). No recent trauma, but he notes gradual increase in shoe size and hat size over the past decade. Laboratory work shows markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase at 285 U/L (normal 30-120) and elevated urinary hydroxyproline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, the lumbar spine and proximal femurs demonstrate localized warmth indicating increased bone vascularity. Direct percussion over affected areas elicits pain. No neurological deficits currently present, though mild lower extremity weakness (4+/5) noted in hip flexors. Bone deformity palpable in lumbar region with mild anterior bowing of left femur.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the lumbar spine and pelvis show characteristic mixed lytic and sclerotic bone lesions with coarse trabecular pattern. The femoral necks and lumbar vertebrae demonstrate cortical thickening and increased bone density. Radionuclide bone scan reveals significantly increased uptake in multiple sites including lumbar spine, pelvis, and proximal femurs.

puzzles.explanation: Paget's disease of bone is a chronic disorder of bone remodeling characterized by abnormal osteoclastic activity followed by disorganized osteoblastic response, resulting in weakened, deformed bone with abnormal architecture. The condition typically affects older adults and has a genetic component, as evidenced by this patient's paternal family history. The progressive bone pain, localized warmth from increased vascularity, deformity (anterior femoral bowing, lumbar kyphosis), and markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase with elevated urinary hydroxyproline reflect the accelerated bone turnover characteristic of this disease. The mixed lytic and sclerotic lesions with coarse trabecular pattern on imaging, combined with high radionuclide uptake, are pathognomonic findings that distinguish Paget's disease from other causes of bone pain and deformity in older adults.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old office worker presents with eye discomfort and occasional blurred vision that has developed gradually over 3 months. She reports the symptoms worsen as her workday progresses and describes mild frontal headaches in the afternoons. Symptoms are worse on working days than weekends.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Her symptoms are tightly correlated with continuous near-work activity, improving rapidly with breaks and distance viewing. She also reports significantly fewer symptoms during periods away from work, such as weekends and vacation. This pattern has been consistent and reproducible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Transition to full-time remote work 3 months ago coincided with symptom onset. She has noticed reduced blinking when concentrating on her screens. Recent optometric assessment was normal, and she denies any systemic autoimmune or rheumatological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally at distance but becomes 20/40 at near distance (approximately 40 cm). Tear film break-up time is reduced at 4 seconds (normal >5 seconds), indicating aqueous tear deficiency. Ocular surface staining with lissamine green shows scattered punctate epithelial erosions. Accommodation amplitude measured at 10 diopters (expected 12-14 at age 32).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anterior segment optical coherence tomography demonstrates decreased tear meniscus height and thinning of the tear film layer. Meibography reveals mild gland dropout in the lower lid consistent with meibomian gland dysfunction from reduced blinking. No retinal pathology identified on dilated fundoscopy or OCT of optic disc and macula.

puzzles.explanation: Computer vision syndrome, also called digital eye strain, results from prolonged near-work accommodation and reduced blinking during screen use, leading to a cascade of ocular surface changes and asthenopic symptoms. The pathophysiology involves accommodation fatigue (manifesting as reduced amplitude and near blur), tear film instability from decreased blink rate and meibomian gland dysfunction, and punctate epithelial keratopathy—all evident in this patient's rapid symptom onset after transitioning to full-time remote work. The characteristic temporal pattern of progressive symptoms throughout the workday, dramatic improvement with distance viewing and time away from screens, and normal refractive status with reduced accommodation amplitude distinguish this from refractive error or dry eye disease alone. Management focuses on restoring blink frequency, regular accommodation breaks (20-20-20 rule), and optimizing screen ergonomics to reduce the sustained near-convergence demand.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old office manager presents with chronic lower back pain and neck stiffness that has developed over 8 months of desk work. She describes gradual onset of central low back discomfort and progressive cervical tightness without any acute injury or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain intensifies significantly after prolonged sitting (>2 hours) and improves markedly with movement, stretching, and postural correction. She reports morning stiffness that resolves within 30 minutes of activity. Standing and walking provide symptomatic relief. Pain does not radiate distally and is not aggravated by specific movements or neural tension tests.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient works 8-10 hours daily at a desk with minimal breaks. Recent ergonomic assessment identified workplace risk factors contributing to sustained postural deviation. No history of significant spinal trauma, prior disc pathology, or neurological symptoms. General health unremarkable.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, postural assessment reveals prominent forward head posture (approximately 3 cm anterior to plumb line) with thoracic kyphosis exaggeration and anterior pelvic tilt. Cervical and lumbar range of motion are full but slightly limited by muscle tightness. Lumbar flexion shows reversal of normal curve but no radicular pain. Straight leg raise 85 degrees bilaterally. Slump test negative. Palpation reveals sustained muscle tension in cervical paraspinals and lumbar erector spinae.

clues.label: Imaging
