clues.text: Plain radiographs show preserved spinal alignment and disc heights without degenerative changes. MRI demonstrates normal disc signal, intact facet joints, normal canal diameter, and no neural compression. Imaging is structurally normal.

puzzles.explanation: Postural syndrome results from sustained deviation from neutral spinal alignment, typically through repetitive occupational positioning, leading to cumulative soft tissue stress and motor control dysfunction rather than structural pathology. The condition is characterized by central, non-radicular pain that worsens with prolonged static postures and improves with movement and postural correction — a pattern perfectly exemplified by the office-based aggravation and rapid relief with activity. The forward head posture with thoracic kyphosis and anterior pelvic tilt creates predictable chains of muscle tightness and weakness; in this case, the cervical and lumbar paraspinals bear sustained load as stabilizers. Crucially, imaging remains structurally normal, distinguishing postural syndrome from mechanical derangement or neural compromise, and making ergonomic correction and movement-based rehab the primary management tools.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 27-year-old overhead throwing athlete (baseball pitcher) presents with progressive medial elbow pain over 6 weeks. The pain is localized to the medial aspect of the elbow and has progressively worsened with continued pitching activity despite attempting rest periods.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specifically triggered by throwing, particularly during the late cocking phase and deceleration phase of the pitch. He reports ability to perform other upper extremity activities without pain. Throwing velocity has decreased noticeably, and he cannot maintain control of pitches. Non-throwing activities cause minimal discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He increased his pitching volume significantly 2 months ago without adequate progression in training load. He reports no specific acute traumatic event but rather insidious onset with progressive symptoms. He played through mild pain initially, thinking it would resolve. No previous elbow surgery or significant injury to this extremity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, point tenderness is elicited directly over the medial collateral ligament origin. The moving valgus stress test reproduces pain (also called dynamic valgus stress test). Milking maneuver is positive, reproducing sharp pain over the ligament. Manual valgus stress at 90 degrees elbow flexion demonstrates increased laxity compared to contralateral side with a soft end-feel. Grip strength is reduced on affected side. No nerve involvement signs on upper limb neurological testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the elbow reveals partial thickness tear of the anterior bundle of the ulnar collateral ligament with associated edema at the ligament origin. High signal intensity within the ligament substance indicates ligamentous disruption. Small joint effusion present. Flexor-pronator origin shows mild edema consistent with associated overuse strain. No osteochondral lesion identified.

puzzles.explanation: Ulnar collateral ligament (UCL) injury results from chronic valgus stress to the medial elbow, most common in overhead throwing athletes where repetitive late-cocking and deceleration forces place enormous tensile loads on the anterior bundle. The insidious onset following sudden training load increase is typical; the ligament gradually fatigues and fails, rather than tearing acutely, leading to the progressive medial elbow pain and throwing dysfunction seen here. The constellation of positive valgus stress testing, milking maneuver, and palpable tenderness at the ligament origin, combined with MRI evidence of partial-thickness tearing and ligamentous edema, confirms structural compromise. Early-stage UCL injuries like this partial tear represent a critical window for conservative management with load modification and progressive rehabilitation before progression to complete rupture necessitates surgical reconstruction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 72-year-old woman presents with hip region pain and functional decline over the past 2 months. Reports difficulty with walking, stairs, and sitting. Pain is activity-dependent and accompanied by generalized weakness in the hip area. No acute injury or fall.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to climb stairs reciprocally or rise from a low chair without upper extremity support. Ambulates with a cane but reports instability and fear of falling. Specific difficulty with hip abduction (side-stepping, getting in/out of car). Hip flexion beyond 90 degrees is limited and guarded.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began gradually 8-10 weeks prior. No recent trauma or fall. History of progressive hip joint degenerative disease treated conservatively for 3+ years. Recent surgical intervention via anterolateral approach. Completed prescribed supervised rehabilitation protocol 4 weeks ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip abduction strength 2+/5, hip flexion strength 3/5. Hip flexion ROM limited to 85 degrees. Hip abduction ROM limited to 25 degrees. Hip extension appears relatively preserved. Log-roll test negative. Flexion-adduction test negative. Mild anterior swelling. Lower extremity sensation and reflexes normal. Gait demonstrates Trendelenburg pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show appropriate prosthetic component positioning, no loosening or malalignment. MRI reveals gluteus medius and minimus muscle atrophy and signal change in the tensor fasciae latae. No acute muscle tear. Findings consistent with superior gluteal nerve irritation or stretch injury from surgical approach.

puzzles.explanation: Post-total hip replacement complications can arise from the surgical approach itself, particularly the anterolateral approach used here, which places the superior gluteal nerve at risk of stretch injury or irritation as it passes near the surgical field. The resulting gluteal nerve palsy manifests as selective weakness of hip abduction and flexion with characteristic atrophy of the gluteus medius and minimus, producing a positive Trendelenburg gait and functional deficits in climbing stairs and lateral weight shifting. Though prosthetic positioning appears sound on imaging, the MRI evidence of muscle signal change and atrophy confirms neuromuscular compromise rather than component failure. Recovery can be prolonged, sometimes requiring 6–12 months, and demands careful rehabilitation to prevent hip instability and falls while neural function recovers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man with a history of recurrent shoulder pain presents with acute worsening of his chronic shoulder discomfort following a fall onto his right shoulder 2 weeks ago. He reports constant aching pain in the lateral shoulder and difficulty with overhead activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: He has marked weakness with attempts to raise his arm overhead, which significantly limits reaching activities and self-care. Sleeping on the affected shoulder is impossible. Passive motion causes less pain than active motion. Pain is worse with lifting, pulling, and throwing activities. He avoids these activities due to weakness and sharp pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He has a 2-year history of intermittent right shoulder pain from overuse related to his work as a carpenter. He had not sought formal treatment, managing with over-the-counter analgesics. The acute trauma 2 weeks ago appears to have converted his chronic tendinopathy into a symptomatic tear. He reports no prior shoulder surgery or significant trauma to this shoulder.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, abduction is weak and painful at 3/5. External rotation strength is reduced compared to internal rotation (which is relatively preserved). Drop arm test cannot be completed due to weakness. Supraspinatus tenderness is present on palpation. Apprehension test is negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the right shoulder reveals a full-thickness rotator cuff tear of the supraspinatus measuring 1.2 cm in anteroposterior dimension with retraction. Fatty infiltration is present in the supraspinatus muscle at stage 2. The infraspinatus is intact. Acromion is hypertrophic (curved type) contributing to subacromial space narrowing. Acromioclavicular joint osteoarthritis is mild.

puzzles.explanation: A rotator cuff tear represents a full-thickness or partial-thickness disruption of the tendons that stabilize the glenohumeral joint, most commonly affecting the supraspinatus due to its position in the subacromial space. This patient's 2-year history of overuse-related tendinopathy, combined with a relatively low-energy traumatic event, exemplifies how chronic degeneration predisposes to acute tear — repetitive microtrauma and ischaemic changes weaken the tendon until a fall precipitates failure. The characteristic weakness in active abduction and external rotation, contrasted with preserved passive range and relatively intact internal rotation, reflects the specific loss of supraspinatus and infraspinatus function. Hypertrophic acromial anatomy and fatty infiltration on imaging confirm both the mechanical and biological contributors to this common shoulder pathology, and emphasize why early surgical consultation is typically indicated for full-thickness tears in active individuals.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 64-year-old man with a 12-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with progressive bilateral lower limb pain and sensory loss over 18 months. He describes a burning sensation in his feet and difficulty with fine sensory perception.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking distances are limited due to burning pain in his feet, and he reports difficulty with balance and proprioception, particularly in darkness. He has had episodes of stumbling. Nighttime pain significantly disrupts sleep quality. He avoids walking barefoot due to numbness and fear of injury. Standing tolerance is reduced to 15-20 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Poor medication adherence has resulted in suboptimal glycemic control (HbA1c 9.2%) throughout his diabetes course. Associated comorbidities include hypertension and hyperlipidaemia. Notably, B12 level is normal and there is no history of chemotherapy, malignancy, or significant trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, ankle reflexes are bilaterally diminished (1+/4). Vibratory sense is markedly reduced at both ankles (perceives only 2/8 seconds of tuning fork). Proprioception is impaired in toes (unable to detect direction of passive toe movement). Two-point discrimination is diminished in feet (distance >1 cm). Monofilament testing shows decreased sensation to 5.07 monofilament in distal lower extremities bilaterally. Motor strength is 4+/5 in foot dorsiflexors with distal lower extremity weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies demonstrate reduced conduction velocities and decreased amplitudes in both peroneal and tibial nerves consistent with demyelination. Distal latencies are prolonged. F-waves are abnormal or absent. Electromyography shows denervation potentials (fibrillations and positive sharp waves) in intrinsic foot muscles, confirming axonal degeneration. MRI of lumbar spine is normal without disc herniation or stenosis contributing to symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Peripheral neuropathy represents progressive damage to peripheral nerves, most commonly resulting from prolonged hyperglycemia in diabetes, where chronic glucose elevation damages both myelin and axons through oxidative stress and metabolic dysfunction. This patient's 12-year history of poor glycemic control has led to a distal, symmetrical sensorimotor pattern—the hallmark of diabetic polyneuropathy—with bilateral loss of vibration sense, proprioception, and protective sensation in the feet, accompanied by characteristic burning pain and diminished reflexes. The nerve conduction findings confirm both demyelination (slowed velocities, prolonged latencies) and axonal loss (reduced amplitudes, denervation on EMG), reflecting the mixed pathology typical of diabetes. Management focuses on glycemic optimization, pain control, and fall prevention through balance training and proprioceptive re-education, since the loss of proprioceptive feedback—particularly evident in darkness—places these patients at high risk of injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old woman reports persistent fatigue lasting 4 years that significantly impairs her ability to work and engage in daily activities. Physical exertion, even mild, triggers worsening of symptoms lasting days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She is now largely housebound and unable to sustain any consistent activity level. Even brief periods of light activity (walking to the mailbox, preparing a meal) result in disproportionate exhaustion lasting 24-48 hours, forcing her to rest significantly afterward.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began approximately 2 months after a presumed viral illness. Prior to illness, she was an active professional with full work capacity. Reports new-onset difficulties with concentration and short-term memory. Denies depression, major life stressors, or psychiatric illness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mental status clear and oriented, though demonstrates slowed processing and occasional difficulty with recall. No signs of joint inflammation or objective muscle weakness (5/5 strength throughout). Neurological examination otherwise normal. Cognitive testing reveals mild impairment in attention and processing speed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain normal with no white matter abnormalities or structural lesions. Metabolic panel, CBC, thyroid function, and autoimmune markers (ANA, RF, anti-CCP) all normal. Sleep study demonstrates non-restorative sleep architecture without obstructive apnea.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS) is a complex systemic condition characterized by profound, disabling fatigue lasting at least 6 months, often triggered or worsened by physical or cognitive exertion—a phenomenon termed post-exertional malaise. The hallmark feature is disproportionate exhaustion following minimal activity, as seen with this patient's 24-48 hour crashes after light exertion, distinguishing it from simple deconditioning or depression. The post-viral onset, cognitive dysfunction (particularly slowed processing and attention deficits), and non-restorative sleep architecture are classic features, while normal investigations (imaging, metabolic, immune markers) underscore that ME/CFS remains a clinical diagnosis of exclusion. The severity and duration of functional impairment—from previously active professional to largely housebound in 4 years—reflects the progressive disabling nature of this condition and the importance of early recognition to prevent further deconditioning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old recreational soccer player presents with insidious-onset deep groin pain that began 3 months ago without any specific traumatic event. He describes sharp anterior hip pain with occasional clicking sensation within the hip joint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are aggravated by prolonged sitting, particularly with hip flexion greater than 90 degrees, and by cutting and pivoting movements in soccer. He experiences pain with hip flexion-adduction activities. Walking and straight-line running are tolerable but aggravated by direction changes. Pain improves with rest and anti-inflammatory medication.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He has no prior hip injuries or surgery. Training volume increased markedly 6 months ago with shorter recovery between matches. Denies any family history of joint disease. Generally fit with no other musculoskeletal complaints. No previous groin or hip symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the anterior impingement test (FADIR - flexion, adduction, internal rotation) reproduces his groin pain. Hip flexion adduction test is positive. Log-roll test is negative. McCarthy test demonstrates reproduction of clicking and pain. Hip range of motion shows mild limitation in internal rotation (25 degrees compared to 35 degrees on contralateral side). Pain does not radiate down the leg. Neurological examination is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the hip with intra-articular gadolinium demonstrates an anterior-superior labral tear (ASLAP-type tear) at the 1-2 o'clock position. Labral fraying and signal intensity changes indicate labral fiber disruption. No hip joint effusion. The femoral and acetabular cartilage appears intact. No femoroacetabular impingement morphology. No occult fractures or osteonecrosis identified.

puzzles.explanation: A hip labral tear involves disruption of the fibrocartilaginous labrum that deepens the acetabular socket and stabilizes the hip joint, commonly occurring at the anterior-superior attachment (ASLAP lesion) in athletes with repetitive pivoting and loading. The insidious onset without trauma, combined with deep groin pain worsened by hip flexion-adduction and cutting movements, reflects mechanical instability and pinching of torn labral tissue during impingement positions — precisely why FADIR and McCarthy tests reproduce symptoms. The clicking sensation and positive impingement tests, alongside mild internal rotation loss, are characteristic findings that distinguish labral pathology from simple intra-articular irritation. MRI arthrography definitively confirms the diagnosis by visualizing the labral fiber disruption, and the absence of osteoarthritic changes explains why this athlete remains otherwise functionally intact despite significant mechanical symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old woman reports progressive weakness and clumsiness affecting her left arm and leg over the past 6 months. Onset followed a minor respiratory infection. She describes episodes of tremor and difficulty with fine motor control. She is significantly distressed about her symptoms and reports high levels of anxiety.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She reports inability to use her left hand for fine motor tasks but demonstrates inconsistent functional use of the limb in natural settings. Her gait is slow and cautious, but the pattern is variable and non-organic. She reports improvement with distraction and inconsistency between reported symptoms and observed function during casual observation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She experienced significant life stressors approximately 6 months prior to symptom onset, including relationship dissolution and work-related stress. She reports high emotional distress and anxiety. No family history of neurological disease. She is a high-achiever with perfectionist traits. She has seen multiple specialists without definitive organic diagnosis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, she demonstrates weakness that does not follow anatomical nerve or dermatome distribution, described as 'give-way' weakness that gives impression of effort limitation rather than true paralysis. Hoover sign is positive (contralateral hip extension weakness when attempting to lift affected limb). Gait is inconsistent and varies with attention. Romberg test shows excessive sway that normalizes with distraction. Strength grades vary between examinations.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI including diffusion weighted imaging is completely normal with no white matter lesions, acute infarcts, or structural pathology. No evidence of demyelinating disease. Cervical and thoracic spine MRI show normal cord signal and normal structural anatomy. Comprehensive neurophysiological testing including EMG and NCS is entirely normal. No evidence of organic neurological disease on imaging or electrophysiological studies.

puzzles.explanation: Functional Neurological Disorder (FND) is a condition where neurological symptoms arise from a dysfunction in how the nervous system processes and regulates motor and sensory function, rather than from structural or electrical pathology — hence the completely normal imaging and neurophysiology. It typically emerges following identifiable psychological stressors and is characterized by inconsistency: weakness that doesn't respect anatomical distributions, positive Hoover and Romberg signs, and variable strength testing that improve with distraction, as seen in this case. The normal MRI and EMG effectively exclude demyelinating disease, stroke, and neuromuscular pathology, shifting the diagnostic focus to functional mechanisms. Recognition of FND requires integrating the constellation of inconsistent neurological signs with the psychological context and therapeutic engagement — a diagnosis made on positive clinical findings, not by exclusion alone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old woman with 8 months of sharp, burning pain in the forefoot. Pain radiates into the toes and is often accompanied by tingling or numbness. Symptoms are intermittent and activity-dependent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens significantly with tight or narrow footwear and high heels. Walking more than 30 minutes triggers symptoms. Complete relief occurs with rest, foot elevation, and shoe removal. Pain-free during non-weight-bearing activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 8-month progressive course with no preceding trauma or foot surgery. 25-year occupational history requiring prolonged standing. Symptoms have persisted despite independent footwear modifications and conservative adjustments to daily activities. No diabetes, inflammatory arthropathy, or systemic disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness elicited in the third intermetatarsal space with direct palpation. Positive Mulder's click (clicking sensation with lateral compression of metatarsal heads). Limited passive plantarflexion of the affected toes. Normal foot pulses and skin sensation in other areas. No calluses, bunions, or structural deformities noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound reveals an 8 mm hypoechoic nodular mass in the third intermetatarsal space with plantar location between the metatarsal heads. MRI confirms T2-hyperintense fusiform mass at the same location with perineural fibrosis. No fractures, joint pathology, or other forefoot abnormalities.

puzzles.explanation: Morton's neuroma is a benign perineural fibrosis and thickening of the interdigital nerve, most commonly affecting the third intermetatarsal space where the nerve is naturally more confined. The condition develops from chronic compression and nerve irritation — typically from repetitive metatarsal head loading, narrow footwear, or prolonged standing — causing the characteristic sharp, burning pain that radiates into the toes and worsens with weight-bearing activity. The dramatic relief with shoe removal and non-weight-bearing positions reflects the mechanical nature of the compression; the Mulder's click on examination is pathognomonic, created by the neuroma moving between the metatarsal heads during lateral compression. Imaging confirms the diagnosis with a well-demarcated hypoechoic or T2-hyperintense mass in the intermetatarsal space, often showing the perineural fibrosis that distinguishes it from simple nerve swelling.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old sedentary office worker presents with gradually worsening unilateral hip and buttock pain over 4 months, radiating down the posterolateral thigh. The pain is constant and has significantly impaired their ability to sit for prolonged periods at work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting, hip internal rotation movements, and driving; it eases with standing, walking, and hip external rotation. They must change positions frequently throughout the day and have started standing at their desk.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No recent trauma or falls reported. The patient has a sedentary lifestyle with minimal physical activity and denies regular stretching or conditioning. No bowel/bladder dysfunction or weight loss reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, pain is reproduced with the FAIR test (flexion-adduction-internal rotation) and Freiberg's sign (internal rotation of flexed hip). Hip external rotators are weak at 4/5, and palpation over the greater sciatic notch elicits tenderness. The SLR and Kernig's tests are negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the hip and pelvis shows no structural pathology, disc herniation, or facet arthrosis. However, hypertrophy of the piriformis muscle is noted with signal changes suggesting muscle strain, and mild compression of the sciatic nerve is visualized.

puzzles.explanation: Piriformis syndrome results from compression or irritation of the sciatic nerve as it passes through or beneath the piriformis muscle in the deep gluteal space, often triggered by prolonged sitting and muscular tightness in sedentary individuals. The classic presentation of unilateral buttock pain radiating posterolaterally, reproducible by hip flexion-adduction-internal rotation (FAIR test), reflects the anatomical relationship between the piriformis and sciatic nerve; the muscle hypertrophy and strain evident on MRI here directly compresses the nerve. Pain relief with hip external rotation and standing, combined with weakness of the external rotators and negative neural tension signs, helps distinguish piriformis syndrome from true sciatica or nerve root pathology. This condition responds well to a program addressing the underlying postural dysfunction and muscular tightness that prolonged sitting perpetuates.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old retired tradesperson presents with progressive bilateral lower back and buttock pain over 18 months, with radiation into both lower extremities when walking. Symptoms are reproducible and significantly limit walking distance and tolerance for standing activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking more than 100 meters triggers pain and numbness in both legs, forcing the patient to sit and rest; symptoms resolve quickly with sitting or forward bending. The patient has adopted a flexed posture at the waist and can walk longer distances leaning on a shopping trolley.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Longstanding history of lower back pain since 1990s following manual work. No acute traumatic event precipitated this decline. Patient reports no bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or lower limb weakness. Sleep quality is disrupted due to nighttime pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, bilateral hip and ankle reflexes are diminished (1+). Sensation to light touch is reduced in a bilateral L5 and S1 distribution. Straight leg raise is full and painless. The patient demonstrates exaggerated lumbar lordosis with mild kyphotic correction when bending forward.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine reveals multilevel degenerative changes with significant central stenosis at L4-L5, characterized by ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, disc bulging, and facet joint arthrosis. The spinal canal diameter is severely narrowed, particularly at the level of maximum stenosis.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spinal stenosis results from progressive narrowing of the spinal canal due to degenerative changes—disc bulging, facet arthrosis, and ligamentum flavum hypertrophy—that compress nerve roots and the cauda equina. The classic presentation of neurogenic claudication, where bilateral lower limb pain and numbness develop reliably with walking but resolve rapidly with sitting or forward flexion, reflects the dynamic nature of the stenosis: extension closes the canal further while flexion opens it. The multilevel degenerative changes and severe central stenosis at L4-L5 seen here are typical in older patients with longstanding lumbar pathology, and the preserved strength and reflexes (albeit diminished) distinguish this from acute nerve root compression or cauda equina syndrome. The exaggerated lordosis and adoption of a flexed posture represent the patient's functional adaptation to remain symptomatic-free.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old woman presents with persistent pelvic pain and deep perineal discomfort over 3 years that has significantly impacted her quality of life, sexual function, and work productivity. Pain is constant but fluctuates in intensity throughout the day and menstrual cycle.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens markedly with prolonged sitting and sexual intercourse. Significant functional limitations in work tolerance and positional activities. Improves with position changes, lying down, and heat application.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has significantly modified activities to avoid triggers. Pain fluctuations correlate with menstrual cycle. Has adapted work and social habits around symptom patterns.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation of pelvic floor muscles reveals significant guarding and tenderness, particularly on the left side. Pain is reproduced with pelvic floor pressure and vaginal palpation of obturator internus. Straight leg raise and hip range of motion are unremarkable, but internal hip rotation is limited by discomfort.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound and MRI imaging show no ovarian cysts, fibroids, or endometrial pathology. No structural abnormalities of the bladder, uterus, or bowel are identified. Imaging is essentially normal, ruling out other organic pelvic pathology as the primary driver of symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic pelvic pain syndrome is a persistent pain condition affecting the pelvic region without identifiable structural pathology — a diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out endometriosis, fibroids, cysts, and other organic disease. The syndrome involves central sensitization, pelvic floor muscle dysfunction, and often visceral hyperalgesia, creating a self-perpetuating cycle where guarding and tension amplify pain signals. The constellation of findings here — constant pain with cyclical fluctuations, marked pelvic floor tenderness and guarding on examination, functional limitations with sitting and intercourse, and notably normal imaging — is characteristic of this condition. Management requires a biopsychosocial approach including pelvic floor physical therapy, pain neuroscience education, and often multidisciplinary care, as the pain experience extends beyond any single anatomical lesion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 56-year-old office worker with 6 months of progressive right-sided neck pain and right occipital headaches. No radiation into arm. Pain worsens by end of workday and disrupts sleep when rolling onto right side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain aggravated by sustained neck extension, prolonged sitting, and rightward head turning. Relief with frequent position changes, heat, and gentle stretching. Symptoms fluctuate throughout day but no consistent weakness or numbness in hand.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset without trauma or motor vehicle accident. Poor ergonomic setup at workstation. No prior neck injury, no radiation, no tingling or weakness in fingers. No significant medical comorbidities affecting neck.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right cervical rotation restricted to 60 degrees (left 80+ degrees). Right lateral neck flexion reproduces focal pain at mid-cervical region. Palpation elicits focal tenderness in right paravertebral soft tissues at C4-C5 level. Full strength and reflexes throughout upper extremities; normal sensation in all fingers.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine MRI shows degenerative changes and hypertrophic joint margins at right C4-C5 level with mild facet-mediated foraminal narrowing. No neural compression, no disc herniation. CT confirms osteophytic changes at right-sided zygapophyseal joint consistent with osteoarthritis. Nerve conduction studies normal.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical facet joint syndrome results from osteoarthritic changes and mechanical irritation of the zygapophyseal joints, which are diarthrodial joints between adjacent vertebral facets prone to degenerative remodeling, especially in the mid-cervical spine. The condition characteristically produces unilateral neck pain and referred occipital headaches without arm radiation — a critical distinction from nerve root compression — because pain is mediated by facet joint innervation (the medial branch of the dorsal rami) rather than neural compromise. The mechanical pattern here is classic: extension and ipsilateral rotation compress the already-narrowed joint space, while position changes and lateral flexion away from the affected side provide relief. Focal restriction of cervical rotation, tenderness at the facet level, and imaging showing hypertrophic joint changes without foraminal stenosis severe enough to cause nerve compression confirm the diagnosis and explain why this patient has normal neurology despite significant structural degenerative changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old office worker presents with progressive lower back pain over 2 years. Pain is localized to the lower lumbar and sacroiliac region, worsened by prolonged sitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports pain with sitting for more than 30 minutes and difficulty rising from a chair. Pain is relieved by lying down and walking. Forward bending is limited and uncomfortable.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No history of acute trauma or injury. Patient reports morning stiffness and tightness in hip flexors and lower back. Minimal exercise activity. No radicular symptoms or systemic features.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Thomas test markedly positive. Hip abductor strength 4/5 bilaterally. Core stability poor: cannot maintain neutral spine in prone bridge. Lumbar extension painful and limited. Palpable anterior pelvic tilt with increased lumbar lordosis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine shows no disc herniation, stenosis, or neural compromise. Mild degenerative changes are present but not sufficient to explain symptoms. Imaging does not reveal significant structural pathology; findings are consistent with muscular and postural dysfunction rather than structural disease.

puzzles.explanation: Lower Crossed Syndrome is a postural dysfunction pattern characterized by tightness in the hip flexors and lumbar erector spinae paired with weakness in the deep abdominal and hip abductor muscles, creating an anterior pelvic tilt and excessive lumbar lordosis. This imbalance is particularly prevalent in sedentary individuals whose prolonged sitting shortens hip flexors and inhibits gluteal activation, exactly mirroring this patient's 2-year progressive decline from office work. The marked positive Thomas test, weak hip abductors, poor core stability, and pain with both sitting and forward bending all reflect the characteristic movement impairments and postural derangement of the syndrome. Importantly, the absent structural pathology on imaging confirms that the lower back and sacroiliac pain stems from muscular imbalance and faulty movement patterns rather than disc or neural pathology — making this highly responsive to targeted stretching of hip flexors and progressive core and gluteal strengthening.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old recreational weightlifter presents with acute onset sharp anterior shoulder pain and an audible 'pop' during bench press. He reports immediate arm weakness and functional loss. Pain persists at rest and with movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot lift overhead or carry loads. All shoulder movement, particularly reaching across the body, reproduces anterior pain. Symptoms present even at rest but substantially worse with active use.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long history of heavy weightlifting with progressive training intensity over recent years. No prior shoulder pathology. Injury occurred during a heavy loaded lift. No neck involvement or constitutional symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, anterior shoulder is tender with visible ecchymosis along proximal arm. Shoulder flexion strength is 3+/5. Speed's test and O'Brien's test reproduce anterior shoulder pain. Rotator cuff strength testing is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the shoulder demonstrates complete rupture of the long head of biceps tendon at the intertubercular groove with significant proximal retraction of approximately 5 cm. There is associated hemorrhage and edema in the bicipital groove. The rotator cuff tendons are intact without tear or signal abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: Long head of biceps (LHB) rupture occurs when the proximal tendon, anchored in the intertubercular groove, fails under excessive eccentric loading—particularly common in weightlifters over 50 whose tendons have undergone progressive degenerative changes. The acute 'pop' and immediate proximal arm ecchymosis are hallmark signs of acute rupture, as the tendon retracts and blood spills into the surrounding tissues. While the LHB contributes to shoulder flexion and supination, isolated rupture rarely causes catastrophic functional loss because the short head of biceps and rotator cuff compensate; however, this patient demonstrates 3+/5 flexion weakness reflecting the acute inflammatory phase and neuromuscular inhibition. The intact rotator cuff on imaging is reassuring—LHB ruptures are frequently isolated events in degenerative tendons, distinguishing them from more complex injuries.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of progressive bilateral symmetrical distal lower extremity pain, numbness, and burning sensation starting in her toes. Symptoms began approximately 4 weeks into a chemotherapy regimen and have steadily worsened.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports significant functional limitation with walking and balance, particularly in low-light environments. She experiences increased symptoms with prolonged standing or walking and finds relief with sitting and lower extremity elevation. She reports difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning clothes and writing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports no diabetes, significant alcohol use, or family history of hereditary neuropathy. She denies recent infections or medication changes aside from chemotherapy. No prior chemotherapy exposure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, bilateral lower extremity sensation is diminished distally in a stocking distribution. Vibration sense is reduced to the ankle bilaterally. Achilles reflexes are absent bilaterally. Motor strength in lower extremities remains intact at 5/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies and electromyography demonstrate a sensory-predominant axonal polyneuropathy with reduced sensory nerve action potentials bilaterally in the sural and superficial peroneal nerves. No demyelinating features are present. MRI of the lumbar spine is normal, ruling out structural causes.

puzzles.explanation: Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) is a dose-dependent, often irreversible adverse effect of neurotoxic chemotherapy agents that causes axonal degeneration, typically affecting the longest and largest diameter nerve fibres first. The classic presentation is a bilateral, symmetrical, distal, sensory-predominant polyneuropathy — a 'stocking-glove' distribution — that emerges weeks to months into treatment and may progress despite dose reduction or cessation. The absence of demyelination on nerve conduction studies, preserved motor strength, and the temporal relationship to chemotherapy initiation distinguish CIPN from other common neuropathies like diabetes or hereditary sensorimotor neuropathy. Management focuses on symptom control and functional optimization, as recovery is often incomplete; early recognition and dose modification remain the most effective preventive strategies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 71-year-old retired teacher presents with painless progressive weakness of the right shoulder over 12 months, with inability to actively raise her arm overhead. She initially attributed symptoms to aging but sought evaluation when function significantly deteriorated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She cannot perform overhead tasks such as reaching into cabinets or combing her hair. Attempting to abduct her shoulder against gravity results in immediate collapse. She can perform passive range of motion comfortably but cannot actively lift her arm from her lap. Non-rotator cuff activities are relatively preserved with good hand grip strength.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She denies any acute shoulder injury or trauma. She did have a remote history of shoulder impingement symptoms 8 years ago that improved with conservative treatment. She has not undergone any shoulder surgery. She reports long-standing use of corticosteroid inhalers for COPD (15 years). She attributes her weakness to aging and declining activity level.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, she demonstrates severe weakness of shoulder abduction (1/5) and external rotation (0/5). The drop arm test is dramatically positive with inability to maintain any arm elevation. Infraspinatus atrophy is marked and visible bilaterally with greater atrophy on right (affected) side. Supraspinatus atrophy is also present. Strength testing demonstrates preserved hand grip (5/5) and elbow strength (5/5). Passive shoulder motion is pain-free and full.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the right shoulder reveals a large full-thickness rotator cuff tear involving both supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons measuring 2.8 cm x 1.5 cm with significant retraction. Severe fatty infiltration of both muscles indicating chronic degenerative change (Goutallier grade 3-4). Supraspinatus muscle atrophy is advanced. No significant arthritic changes. Acromion is a hooked morphology.

puzzles.explanation: A rotator cuff tear occurs when one or more of the four muscles stabilizing the glenohumeral joint rupture, most commonly the supraspinatus and infraspinatus due to their vulnerable anatomy and load-bearing demands. This 71-year-old's chronic presentation — painless weakness with marked atrophy, preserved passive motion, and a dramatically positive drop arm test — is classic for a large full-thickness tear, particularly when combined with risk factors like long-standing corticosteroid use (which weakens tendon collagen), prior impingement, and a hooked acromion that predisposes to mechanical wear. The severe fatty infiltration (Goutallier grade 3-4) indicates this is a chronic degenerative process that has evolved over many months, not an acute rupture, which is why she initially mistook her symptoms for age-related decline. The bilateral infraspinatus atrophy despite unilateral tear involvement is a key imaging finding that underscores the systemic degenerative nature of her rotator cuff pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 78-year-old woman with known osteoporosis fell at home and was unable to get up. She has severe pain in the hip and groin region with immediate onset. She was found on the floor 2 hours later.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot tolerate any weight on the left leg. Pain is constant and severe; even passive hip movements (flexion, rotation) are acutely painful. She is bed-bound and requires full assistance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has had osteoporosis for 8 years but reports poor adherence to therapy. She lives independently and ambulated with a walker at baseline. No head injury, confusion, or loss of consciousness reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the left lower extremity is shortened and externally rotated compared to the right. Left hip is markedly tender, and any attempt at movement (flexion, internal/external rotation) reproduces severe pain. Hip flexors and extensors are 0/5 due to pain inhibition. Sensation and pulses are intact distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays of the left hip reveal a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture. CT imaging confirms a subcapital fracture with approximately 90-degree angulation. There is significant osteopenia evident on imaging. Associated soft tissue swelling is noted in the hip region.

puzzles.explanation: Hip fracture, typically resulting from a fall in elderly patients with compromised bone density, occurs when the femoral neck or intertrochanteric region fractures due to the combination of poor bone quality and acute trauma. Intracapsular femoral neck fractures like this one are particularly serious because they disrupt the blood supply to the femoral head, risking avascular necrosis and nonunion—necessitating urgent surgical intervention rather than conservative management. The immediate onset of severe pain, inability to bear weight, and the classic presentation of a shortened, externally rotated limb reflect the acute disruption of bone continuity and associated soft tissue injury, while the complete loss of motor control around the hip reflects pain inhibition rather than true neurological deficit. In osteoporotic patients with poor medication adherence, even low-energy falls can produce displaced fractures; early mobilisation post-operatively becomes critical to prevent secondary complications like pneumonia and deep vein thrombosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 14-year-old girl is referred to physiotherapy following screening detection of spinal curvature at school. She reports no pain and has no functional complaints, but her mother notes she appears to have unequal shoulder heights and breast heights when viewed from behind.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports no activity limitations, pain, or breathing difficulty with normal daily activities, sports, or school. She has no difficulty with physical education and participates in volleyball without symptoms. Posture appears asymmetrical with shoulder drop on one side and truncal asymmetry evident.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient and parents have noticed progressive postural changes over the past 6–12 months. The asymmetry has gradually worsened, prompting school screening referral. No prior spinal injury, trauma, or neuromuscular conditions. No family history of scoliosis or connective tissue disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, Adam's forward bend test reveals asymmetrical rib prominence on the right side with thoracic rib hump measuring approximately 1.5 cm. Shoulder heights are unequal with right shoulder higher than left. Skin examination is normal without hairy patches, cafe-au-lait spots, or dimpling.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spinal X-rays demonstrate a right thoracic spinal curvature measuring approximately 35 degrees Cobb angle. The curve is primarily thoracic with apex at T7-T8. Vertebral heights are normal, and there is no kyphosis, vertebral rotation, or bony abnormalities suggesting secondary causes.

puzzles.explanation: Idiopathic adolescent scoliosis is a lateral spinal curvature greater than 10 degrees that develops during the growth spurt, with no identifiable underlying pathology — accounting for 80% of all scoliosis cases. It presents most commonly in adolescent females and is often detected incidentally during routine screening, as the patient may have no pain or functional limitations despite progressive postural changes. The characteristic physical findings—unequal shoulder heights, rib hump on forward bending, and truncal asymmetry—reflect the three-dimensional nature of the deformity, with vertebral rotation creating the visible rib prominence. Cobb angle measurement on radiographs is the gold standard for diagnosis and monitoring, and curves in the 25–40 degree range during growth typically warrant physiotherapy for core stability and posture education, with imaging follow-up to detect progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents with a 5-year history of involuntary urine leakage with coughing, sneezing, and laughing that has progressively worsened and is now affecting her social and professional life. She reports light-to-moderate urine loss during these exertional events, particularly when activities are unexpected.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Incontinence is reliably triggered by physical exertion including coughing, sneezing, jumping, and exercise activity. Walking long distances may precipitate leakage if the bladder is full. The patient reports relief with pelvic floor muscle contraction before coughing and with regular pelvic floor muscle training exercises.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports she can manage symptoms by contracting pelvic floor muscles proactively (before coughing/sneezing) and has noticed improvement with dedicated pelvic floor muscle training over the past 6 months. She has not sought other treatments (pessary, medications, or injections) to date.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, pelvic floor muscle strength is graded 3/5 using Oxford scale, with reduced endurance and poor voluntary contraction quality. Cough test with full bladder reproduces leakage. Abdominal examination is soft without masses. Neurological examination is normal. Urinalysis is negative for infection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound during Valsalva maneuver demonstrates urethral hypermobility with funneling of the bladder neck and descent of the proximal urethra of approximately 1.5 cm. Post-void residual is normal at 15 mL. No pelvic organ prolapse or other structural abnormalities are identified.

puzzles.explanation: Stress urinary incontinence results from inadequate urethral closure pressure during increases in intra-abdominal pressure, most commonly due to pelvic floor muscle weakness and urethral hypermobility following childbirth, aging, or chronic straining. The condition is characterized by involuntary urine leakage triggered by physical exertion—coughing, sneezing, exercise—rather than urge or detrusor instability, and typically affects women in midlife as estrogen-dependent connective tissue support diminishes. The marked improvement with pelvic floor muscle training and the ability to voluntarily contract muscles before exertional events reflect the primary role of neuromuscular dysfunction; the documented urethral hypermobility on ultrasound confirms loss of supportive tissue stability around the bladder neck and proximal urethra. Conservative management through targeted pelvic floor strengthening remains first-line treatment and can be highly effective in restoring continence when compliance is maintained.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old manual laborer presents with acute onset lower back and leg pain following a lifting injury at work 2 weeks ago. Pain radiates down the right leg with associated numbness and tingling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is significantly worse with forward bending and prolonged sitting. Walking and extension-based movements provide relief. Patient has stopped heavy lifting and adopted a lumbar support belt.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports a 3-week history of progressive worsening symptoms after the acute lifting incident. He has a prior history of lower back pain 5 years ago that resolved with conservative care. He denies bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or progressive neurological decline. He reports sleeping poorly due to leg pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, straight leg raise on the right is limited to 40 degrees due to leg pain. Right ankle dorsiflexion strength is 4/5 with normal hip flexor strength. Sensation to light touch is reduced in the right L5 distribution (lateral leg and dorsum of foot). Right knee reflex is normal but ankle reflex is diminished at 1+.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine demonstrates a right-sided disc herniation at L5-S1 level with extrusion of nuclear material. There is compression of the right L5 nerve root with associated nerve root edema. The herniated material is in the axillary zone, just lateral to the spinal canal. No cauda equina syndrome is evident.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar radiculopathy results from compression of a nerve root as it exits the spinal canal, typically by a herniated intervertebral disc, leading to pain, sensory disturbance, and motor weakness in the distribution of that nerve. The acute lifting mechanism in this case caused disc herniation at L5-S1, compressing the right L5 nerve root and producing the characteristic L5 dermatomal pattern—sensory loss over the lateral leg and foot dorsum with dorsiflexion weakness. The mechanical nature of the injury explains why forward bending and sitting worsen symptoms by increasing intradiscal pressure and nerve root tension, while extension and walking provide relief by reducing mechanical irritation; the limitation in straight leg raise and diminished ankle reflex confirm neural involvement. The absence of cauda equina red flags (bowel/bladder dysfunction, bilateral symptoms, progressive neurological decline) indicates this is appropriate for conservative management with activity modification and time, as most disc herniations gradually reabsorb over weeks to months.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old recreational runner presents with progressive lower leg pain over 6 weeks that has forced her to reduce running frequency and distance. She reports morning stiffness and tightness in the lower leg that improves with activity, and localized swelling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by running, particularly on hard or uneven surfaces, and by impact activities. The patient reports pain during the first 10-15 minutes of running that improves during the run, then worsens again after activity. Walking causes mild discomfort, and the patient has modified her training to non-impact activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient recently increased her running volume by 50% over the past 8 weeks as she trained for a half-marathon. She reports a change to a new running shoe 3 months ago. No acute trauma, no previous lower leg injuries. She denies systemic symptoms, fevers, or weight loss. She runs 4-5 days per week.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, localized tenderness is present along the posteromedial tibia in the middle third of the leg, spanning approximately 5 cm. There is mild swelling and erythema over the tender area. Palpation reproduces pain. Ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion are full and painless. Lower extremity alignment shows mild pronation at the foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals cortical stress reaction at the posteromedial tibia in the middle third with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema. Tibialis posterior and soleus fascia show inflammation. No fracture line is visible, consistent with early-stage stress injury rather than frank fracture or other pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Medial tibial stress syndrome (MTSS) is an overuse injury involving inflammation of the periosteum and surrounding musculotendinous structures along the posteromedial tibia, typically from repetitive traction and microtrauma during impact activities. It occurs when training loads exceed the bone's adaptive capacity, especially with rapid volume increases, biomechanical factors like foot pronation, or changes in running surface or footwear. The characteristic presentation here—localized posteromedial tibial tenderness, pain that improves during activity then worsens afterward, and periosteal inflammation on imaging without a stress fracture—distinguishes MTSS from the more severe continuum of stress fractures. Management centers on load reduction, addressing biomechanical contributors, and graduated return to impact activities once inflammation resolves.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 16-year-old with persistent productive cough since early childhood and shortness of breath with exertion. Cough productive of thick sputum. Recurrent chest infections over many years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to keep up with peers during sports. Fatigue limits daily activities. Cough worsens with exercise and when lying flat. Recently struggling with stairs and long walks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began in infancy. Multiple hospitalizations for respiratory infections throughout childhood. Takes pancreatic enzyme supplements with meals. Growth slower than siblings. Salty sweat noted by parents during childhood.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Barrel chest, finger clubbing present. Decreased air entry bilaterally with scattered crackles. Hyperresonance to percussion. Accessory muscle use during breathing. Forced expiratory time prolonged.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: HRCT chest shows bronchiectasis with bronchial wall thickening, predominantly upper lobes. Hyperinflation with bronchial secretion impaction and air trapping. Spirometry shows FEV1 55% predicted with obstructive pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Cystic fibrosis is a recessive genetic disorder affecting the CFTR protein, leading to abnormally thick, viscous secretions in the lungs, pancreas, and sweat glands. The hallmark pulmonary manifestation is progressive bronchiectasis — the bilateral bronchial wall thickening and airway dilation seen on imaging — driven by recurrent infections and impaired mucociliary clearance of inspissated secretions. The constellation of findings here — early-onset productive cough, salty sweat, pancreatic insufficiency requiring enzyme supplementation, severe airflow obstruction with hyperinflation, and characteristic finger clubbing from chronic hypoxaemia — reflects the multisystem burden and chronicity typical of CF. Physiotherapy management focuses on airway clearance techniques and exercise tolerance, though progression to severe obstruction (FEV1 55%) underscores the importance of early, aggressive intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 8-year-old presenting with progressive lower limb weakness and reduced sensation. Parents report child has never achieved independent walking. Difficulty with trunk stability when sitting unsupported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Uses wheelchair for all community mobility. Cannot stand or walk without assistance. Requires help with toileting and bowel/bladder management. Poor trunk control limits independence in sitting activities and transfers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Condition detected prenatally. Required emergency surgical intervention within 48 hours of birth. History of multiple spinal procedures during childhood for progressive deformity. Shunt placement performed in infancy for management of associated hydrocephalus.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lower extremity strength 2/5 bilaterally throughout. Sensory level detectable at mid-thoracic region with normal sensation above, absent below. Significant scoliosis visible on inspection. Spasticity present in lower limbs. Reflexes brisk and hyperreflexic distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI spine shows segmental spinal cord malformation with partial cord agenesis at thoracic level. X-ray demonstrates fixed scoliotic curve of 45 degrees with vertebral body abnormality. Lateral recess stenosis from abnormal spinal canal ossification noted.

puzzles.explanation: Spina bifida is a neural tube defect resulting from incomplete fusion of the vertebral arches and underlying dura during early embryonic development, allowing the spinal cord and meninges to herniate through the defect. The severity depends on the level and extent of cord involvement — in this case, mid-thoracic pathology explains the profound lower limb paralysis, complete sensory loss below that level, and the characteristic spasticity and hyperreflexia typical of upper motor neuron injury. Associated structural complications like progressive scoliosis, tethering, and hindbrain malformation (necessitating shunt placement) are common sequelae that demand ongoing surgical and rehabilitation management throughout childhood. Early postnatal repair, intensive physiotherapy for trunk control and transfers, and careful monitoring for secondary deformities remain central to optimizing function and participation in this population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 47-year-old woman with progressive swelling of the right upper extremity following cancer treatment. Notes heaviness and tightness in arm and hand with clothing becoming too tight over weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with fine motor tasks including gripping and keyboard work. Unable to wear rings or watch on affected arm. Experiences increased swelling after prolonged use of arm or in warm weather. Sleep disrupted due to uncomfortable arm positioning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Right breast cancer treated with mastectomy and axillary lymph node dissection 18 months ago. Completed adjuvant chemotherapy and radiation to chest wall. No known family history of lymphatic disorders. Recent cellulitis episode of affected arm.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right arm circumference exceeds left by 3.5 cm at forearm; 2.8 cm at wrist. Pitting edema present in dorsum of hand and forearm. Skin thickened with loss of normal contours; palpable nodularity in subcutaneous tissue. Stemmer sign positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates absent lymph uptake in right axilla with delayed transit in upper extremity. Ultrasound confirms honeycomb pattern of subcutaneous edema with echogenic dermal thickening. Absent or hypoplastic deep lymph nodes on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Lymphoedema is chronic swelling caused by impaired lymphatic drainage, most commonly secondary to surgical removal or damage of lymph nodes—in this case, the axillary dissection and radiation therapy following breast cancer treatment. The condition develops when residual lymphatic channels cannot handle the fluid load, leading to progressive accumulation of protein-rich interstitial fluid and subsequent fibrosis of subcutaneous tissues. The constellation of findings here—the positive Stemmer sign, pitting then non-pitting edema progressing to skin thickening, absent axillary uptake on lymphoscintigraphy, and exacerbation with arm use or heat—are pathognomonic of lymphovenous insufficiency. Secondary lymphoedema typically emerges months to years post-treatment, and while cellulitis episodes accelerate progression, early physiotherapy focusing on manual lymphatic drainage, compression garments, and limb elevation can slow fibrosis and preserve limb function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old competitive athlete with 3 weeks of progressive shoulder blade pain. Notices the shoulder blade on one side looks different when training, and overhead activities feel uncomfortable.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform push-ups, bench press, or throwing activities without exacerbation of scapular pain. Shoulder flexion and horizontal adduction movements reproduce symptoms. Fatigue and heaviness in shoulder girdle after minimal activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset during intense strength training involving heavy bench pressing 3 weeks ago. No prior shoulder injuries. Progressive weakness noted with each training session. Denied neurological symptoms or neck pain referral.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked scapular winging with medial border prominence; winging increases with wall push-up. Weakness of scapular protraction on manual testing. Inability to maintain scapular position during forward arm raise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG demonstrates denervation pattern in serratus anterior with positive sharp waves and fibrillations. Serratus anterior muscle shows altered signal intensity on MRI. Nerve conduction studies of long thoracic nerve show evidence of axonal injury.

puzzles.explanation: Serratus anterior injury results from damage to the long thoracic nerve, which exclusively innervates this muscle, causing paralysis or significant weakness of the primary scapular protractor. The condition classically presents with scapular winging—medial border prominence most visible during push-up activities—because loss of serratus function removes the force that normally anchors and pulls the scapula anteriorly against the ribcage. The acute onset during heavy bench pressing, combined with progressive weakness and inability to perform horizontal adduction movements, reflects the vulnerability of the long thoracic nerve to traction injury during forceful overhead and pressing activities. EMG denervation patterns and MRI signal changes confirm axonal injury rather than simple muscle strain, distinguishing this from other causes of scapular dysfunction and requiring a longer rehabilitation timeline focused on nerve recovery and scapular stabilization retraining.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 35-year-old with shoulder pain and stiffness for 6 weeks following a sports-related overhead injury. Pain worsens with movement and at night. Currently attending physiotherapy with minimal improvement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to reach overhead or out to side beyond 45 degrees. Sleeping on affected shoulder not tolerable. Avoids pushing/pulling activities. Pain with dressing and grooming tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during overhead throwing sport. Underwent surgical repair 6 weeks ago. Patient reports good adherence to post-operative restrictions and early physiotherapy protocol. No prior shoulder pathology or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant discrepancy between passive ROM (110° flexion, 80° abduction) and active ROM (60° flexion, 45° abduction). Marked muscle guarding present. Suprasinatus strength testing shows 4/5 due to pain inhibition rather than true weakness. Pain-limited movement throughout range.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative ultrasound demonstrates successful repair integrity with no evidence of re-tear, gap formation, or fluid collection. Findings exclude major structural failure and support mechanistic cause of persistent stiffness as adhesions or inadequate post-operative mobilization rather than surgical complication.

puzzles.explanation: Shoulder arthroscopy is a minimally invasive surgical technique used to visualize, diagnose, and treat intra-articular shoulder pathology, commonly performed for rotator cuff repairs following traumatic overhead injuries. Post-operative stiffness is a frequent complication, particularly in the early phase when protective muscle guarding and capsular adhesions can significantly limit active range of motion despite intact repair integrity—as evidenced by the substantial gap between passive and active ROM in this case. The marked discrepancy between passive mobility (110° flexion) and active movement (60° flexion) indicates pain-inhibited guarding and soft tissue restrictions rather than structural failure, a pattern that typically responds to progressive, carefully dosed physiotherapy focusing on pain modulation and graduated mobilization. Early recognition of this stiffness pattern and aggressive but controlled rehabilitation are critical to prevent chronic capsular contracture and functional loss, particularly in young, active patients who require overhead mobility for return to sport.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old office worker with frequent bilateral headaches described as pressing/squeezing around the head. Headaches occur most days, lasting hours to full day, without complete resolution between episodes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen with sustained computer work and poor posture; improve with brief breaks and neck stretching. Stress and fatigue are clear triggers. No nausea or light sensitivity, but neck stiffness accompanies headache onset.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Headache pattern started approximately 2 years ago coinciding with job change requiring prolonged desk work. Denies head trauma, neurological symptoms, or medication overuse. Previous episodes responded well to rest and over-the-counter analgesics.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation reveals significant tenderness in bilateral upper trapezius and suboccipital muscles. Forward head posture noted with chin jutting during cervical flexion. Cervical range of motion preserved but reproduction of symptoms with sustained flexion. No focal neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays demonstrate loss of normal cervical lordosis consistent with postural dysfunction. MRI excludes structural pathology, mass, or intracranial abnormality. No evidence of cervical spondylosis or foraminal stenosis.

puzzles.explanation: Tension-type headache is the most common headache disorder, arising from sustained contraction of cervical and cranial muscles—typically triggered by postural strain, stress, or fatigue rather than intracranial pathology. The bilateral, pressing quality and association with forward head posture and prolonged computer work reflect the muscular origin: poor posture increases load on the upper trapezius and suboccipital muscles, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of muscle tension and referred pain around the head. Unlike migraine, tension-type headache lacks the autonomic features (nausea, photophobia) and typically responds well to simple analgesia, muscle relaxation, and postural correction—the pattern evident in this office worker's two-year history. The preserved cervical ROM and absence of structural pathology on imaging confirm the functional, muscle-mediated nature of the condition, making ergonomic and postural intervention cornerstone components of management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 24-year-old male presents with pain and swelling over the back of the hand. Onset was acute 2 weeks ago. Patient reports visible knuckle prominence and difficulty making a fist. No numbness or tingling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with gripping or clenching. Patient unable to make a tight fist or grip forcefully. Swelling localized to the dorsal hand. Activities requiring hand closure are significantly limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred from direct impact to the hand during contact activity 2 weeks ago. No prior hand injuries or fractures. Patient initially managed at home with rest, ice, and elevation only. Has not received formal imaging or acute medical evaluation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable bony prominence over dorsal fifth metacarpal. Localized tenderness at metacarpal neck level. Mild dorsal swelling persisting. Full passive range of motion preserved but active flexion limited by pain. No neurovascular compromise. Rotational alignment of digits appears intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray of hand reveals fracture through the neck of the fifth metacarpal with apex-dorsal angulation of approximately 35 degrees. Metacarpal head remains aligned with no intra-articular involvement. No other carpal or metacarpal fractures identified.

puzzles.explanation: A boxer's fracture is a fracture through the neck of the fifth metacarpal, typically caused by direct axial loading during a punch or similar impact. The characteristic apex-dorsal angulation occurs because the intrinsic hand muscles and flexor tendons create a flexion moment at the fracture site, allowing the metacarpal head to rotate dorsally while the base remains stable. The acute presentation with preserved passive range of motion but limited active flexion reflects pain inhibition rather than structural block — the fracture itself remains stable provided the hand avoids forceful gripping during healing. Most boxer's fractures with less than 40 degrees of angulation, as seen here, achieve functional union with conservative management of immobilization and early controlled mobilization, though some patients retain minor cosmetic prominence or slight loss of grip strength if inadequately rehabilitated.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with a 5-year history of chronic pain in the pelvic region and medial thigh. She reports dyspareunia, urinary frequency, and pain with bowel movements. Denies fever, weight loss, or systemic symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She avoids prolonged standing and sitting due to symptom exacerbation. She reports difficulty with normal bowel and bladder function. Symptoms ease with rest in a supine position with hips flexed.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient reports a history of hysterectomy 8 years ago without resolution of pain. She has had multiple consultations with gynecologists and urologists with no clear diagnosis. She denies fever, weight loss, or malignancy risk factors. She reports significant psychological distress related to chronic pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pelvic floor muscles are hypertonic and tender on palpation. Hip abduction and internal rotation produce discomfort at end-range. Lower limb reflexes and motor strength are intact. Sensory testing reveals decreased light touch sensation in a dermatomal pattern corresponding to S2-S4, suggesting possible nerve root irritation or neuropathic component.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the pelvis demonstrates no visible masses, adhesions, or structural abnormalities. The bladder and rectum appear normal. No evidence of neural foraminal stenosis or nerve root compression. Imaging findings are reassuring and do not identify obvious pathology explaining the patient's chronic pain.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic pelvic pain syndrome is a complex, multifactorial condition characterized by persistent pain in the pelvic region lasting more than 6 months without identifiable structural pathology — a hallmark of neuropathic and myofascial involvement. The syndrome often develops following pelvic surgery or gynecological procedures, as in this case post-hysterectomy, where altered tissue mechanics, nerve sensitization, and pelvic floor dysfunction create a self-perpetuating cycle of pain, muscle guarding, and visceral dysfunction. The hypertonic pelvic floor muscles and sensory changes in the S2–S4 distribution reflect both peripheral sensitization and central pain amplification, while the association with dyspareunia, urinary frequency, and bowel dysfunction illustrates how pain affects multiple systems sharing pelvic innervation. Management requires a biopsychosocial approach integrating pelvic floor physical therapy, neuromodulation, and psychological support, as structural imaging normalcy often shifts focus toward functional and neuropathic mechanisms rather than surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 19-year-old student with persistent headaches, dizziness, and significant difficulty concentrating. Reports fatigue that interferes with daily tasks. Symptoms began 4 weeks ago following a motor vehicle collision.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate prolonged visual tasks or bright environments. Vertigo triggered specifically by rapid head movements. Gait unsteady, particularly on uneven surfaces or when turning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Loss of consciousness for approximately 3 minutes at scene. Admitted to hospital and required ICU-level monitoring in acute phase. No mechanical ventilation needed. Previously no significant medical or neurological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Dynamic gait assessment reveals unsteady balance with increased arm swing for compensation. Positive Romberg test with 20-second stance instability. Dix-Hallpike maneuver triggers vertigo; Fukuda test shows significant deviation. Cranial nerves II-XII intact; no focal neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Follow-up MRI demonstrates resolving contusions in frontal lobes with minimal residual subdural fluid. Microhemorrhages visible on susceptibility-weighted imaging in white matter tracts. Cerebrospinal fluid spaces normal; no mass effect or midline shift.

puzzles.explanation: Traumatic brain injury encompasses a spectrum of diffuse and focal pathology resulting from mechanical forces to the head, ranging from mild concussion to severe diffuse axonal injury. This patient's presentation reflects moderate-to-severe TBI with both focal contusional damage and diffuse axonal injury — evidenced by microhemorrhages in white matter tracts on susceptibility imaging — typical mechanisms of injury in high-energy motor vehicle collisions. The constellation of post-concussive symptoms four weeks post-injury (persistent headaches, concentration deficits, and fatigue) combined with vestibular dysfunction and gait instability reflects incomplete recovery of cerebral and brainstem function; the positive Dix-Hallpike and Fukuda deviation are characteristic of central vestibular involvement rather than peripheral pathology. Resolution of acute mass effect on imaging is encouraging prognostically, though the microhemorrhages and ongoing cognitive-vestibular deficits underscore the importance of structured, graduated rehabilitation to restore neural connectivity and functional recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old with acute-onset knee swelling localized to the front of the kneecap. Visible fullness anterior to patellar tendon with noticeable increase in size over several days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 45-year-old with acute-onset knee swelling over the past week. Reports visible fullness in front of the knee that has progressively worsened. Denies significant trauma or mechanical symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: New job requiring frequent kneeling positions on hard flooring for 8-10 hours daily. Symptoms began after three weeks of work. Denies recent fall or direct knee trauma. No previous knee problems or inflammatory arthritis history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable, fluctuant swelling anterior to patella measuring approximately 4 cm in transverse diameter. Skin erythema and slight warmth over bursa; no fever. Pain with deep palpation of bursa. Knee joint motion unrestricted; meniscal and ligamentous tests normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates simple fluid collection anterior to patella without debris or septations. Bursa measures 1.2 cm at greatest depth. Patellar tendon and underlying joint space sonographically normal. No bursal wall enhancement on post-contrast imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Prepatellar bursitis is inflammation of the bursa lying anterior to the patella, most commonly triggered by repetitive kneeling or direct pressure on hard surfaces. The bursa is a synovial fluid-filled sac that normally facilitates smooth gliding of overlying skin and soft tissues; prolonged mechanical irritation causes it to accumulate fluid and become symptomatic.
