Unlike residual limb pain (which stems from local tissue pathology), phantom pain occurs in a non-existent body part and resists peripheral interventions—a key distinguishing feature evident in this case where well-healed tissues and intact local sensation do not correspond to pain severity. The delayed onset weeks to months post-injury and unpredictable burning or cramping quality reflect central sensitization and maladaptive plasticity rather than ongoing nociception. Functional neuroimaging showing abnormal contralateral sensorimotor activation during pain episodes confirms the neural mechanism: the brain's 'map' of the missing limb has become dysregulated, making this a central rather than peripheral pain problem requiring neuroplastic rather than tissue-focused approaches.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old admitted 3 days ago with sudden severe shortness of breath and low blood oxygen levels. Rapid deterioration over 24-48 hours despite treatment. Now on mechanical ventilation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Minimal tolerance for any activity. Even small bed position changes or brief attempts to reduce ventilator support trigger sudden drops in oxygen levels and worsening breathing distress. Requires continuous high oxygen support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 5 days ago following community-acquired pneumonia and subsequent infection in bloodstream. Prior medical history: COPD and diabetes. Blood inflammatory markers severely elevated and worsening despite antibiotics.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Fine crackling sounds in both lungs throughout. Lungs are stiff and difficult to inflate—requires high pressures on ventilator. Oxygen in blood remains critically low (60-70 mmHg) even with very high oxygen delivery and optimized ventilator settings. Heart exam normal, no fluid overload signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray: bilateral hazy infiltrates with ground-glass appearance and areas of consolidation in both lungs. CT shows extensive two-sided opacities. Heart size and structure normal. BNP negative. Pattern consistent with acute widespread lung tissue damage from inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) is a severe inflammatory lung injury characterized by increased alveolar-capillary permeability, resulting in non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema and profound gas exchange impairment. It develops rapidly following a triggering insult—here, community-acquired pneumonia with sepsis—causing diffuse alveolar damage, bilateral infiltrates, and the characteristic stiff, poorly compliant lungs that require high ventilator pressures. The hallmark is refractory hypoxemia despite aggressive oxygen therapy and mechanical ventilation support, as evidenced by the critically low oxygen levels persisting even with optimized settings. The severe systemic inflammatory response (elevated markers, sepsis-driven cascade) overwhelms the lung's protective mechanisms, making even minimal activity intolerable and necessitating intensive ventilatory management to maintain oxygenation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old recreational athlete presents with episodes of lateral knee instability and giving way during activity. Pain is localized below the lateral knee line and occurs unpredictably with certain movements, causing apprehension.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports symptoms with pivoting movements, lateral bounding, and activities requiring rapid direction changes. Pain and instability are worse with running on uneven surfaces. Single-leg standing and jumping activities trigger episodes of giving way or shifting sensation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient sustained a direct blow to the lateral knee during contact sport 2 years ago. Initial swelling resolved, but persistent lateral instability began after return to activity. No prior knee injuries or ligamentous laxity in other joints. Athlete reports lack of confidence when changing direction during play.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the anterior drawer test of the fibula is positive with subluxation noted. Palpation reveals prominence of the fibular head with reproduction of symptoms. The fibular head glides anteriorly with passive knee flexion. Lateral collateral ligament testing is intact; anterolateral rotatory instability is not demonstrated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates disruption of the anterior and posterior tibiofibular ligaments consistent with chronic instability. Ultrasound shows fibular head translation of 8mm compared to the contralateral side.

puzzles.explanation: Proximal tibiofibular joint (PTFJ) instability results from disruption of the anterior and posterior tibiofibular ligaments, which normally restrain the fibular head and maintain its relationship with the tibia during knee motion. This condition typically develops after direct lateral knee trauma—particularly in athletes—and presents with recurrent anterolateral subluxation episodes triggered by pivoting or rapid deceleration rather than the rotatory instability seen in ACL insufficiency. The fibular head glides excessively into extension and external rotation, creating the characteristic sensation of lateral knee giving way and apprehension during dynamic loading, especially on uneven terrain. Diagnosis relies on recognizing excessive fibular head translation on examination (the anterior drawer test of the fibula) and imaging confirmation of ligamentous disruption; conservative management with proprioceptive retraining and activity modification is first-line, though recurrent instability may warrant surgical stabilization.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational badminton player reports sudden sharp pain in the posterior ankle during a push-off movement. Patient immediately fell and was unable to bear weight. Rapid swelling developed over the following hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot walk without assistance. Plantarflexion against gravity is completely absent. Attempts to stand on toes are impossible. Passive dorsiflexion stretch produces sharp pain in posterior ankle.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient had increased training volume over 4 weeks prior to injury. No prior ankle injuries, chronic ankle pain, or Achilles complaints. No medical comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable gap in Achilles tendon approximately 5 cm proximal to insertion. Thompson test positive: manual calf squeeze produces no ankle plantarflexion. Swelling and ecchymosis present posterior ankle.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows complete discontinuity of the Achilles tendon with 2 cm separation between tendon ends. MRI demonstrates full-thickness tear at the musculotendinous junction with edema and hemorrhage in surrounding tissues. Sagittal imaging clearly delineates the gap between ruptured segments.

puzzles.explanation: Achilles tendon rupture is a complete loss of continuity of the largest tendon in the body, typically occurring at the musculotendinous junction where vascularity is poorest and tensile stress concentrates during forceful plantarflexion. It classically presents with sudden sharp pain during a push-off or jumping movement, immediate loss of plantarflexion strength, and inability to stand on toes — as demonstrated by this patient's complete loss of plantarflexion against gravity and positive Thompson test. The dramatic presentation in a recreational athlete with recent training load escalation reflects the biomechanical demands placed on a tendon that may not have adapted adequately to increased activity. Palpable gap, immediate functional loss, and imaging confirmation with discontinuity of the tendon and surrounding edema make this diagnosis straightforward and clinically urgent, as early surgical or conservative management significantly influences long-term functional outcomes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 56-year-old woman presenting 2 months ago with progressive fatigue and generalized weakness. Reports difficulty with daily activities and reduced energy tolerance compared to baseline. No acute trauma or recent illness preceding symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking tolerance declined to 10 minutes; requires frequent rest breaks throughout day. Difficulty with overhead reaching and lifting on one side. Stairs precipitate significant fatigue. Grip strength notably reduced on affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Completed multimodal cancer treatment 8-12 weeks ago: neoadjuvant chemotherapy, surgical intervention with reconstruction, and post-operative radiation therapy. No prior significant medical comorbidities. Functional decline parallels treatment completion timeline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder abduction limited to 85° with pain and tissue restriction. Palpable axillary cording with pain on palpation. Grip strength asymmetry (18 kg affected vs 28 kg unaffected). Ankle dorsiflexion strength 4/5 bilaterally. Glove-like sensory loss in feet bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy confirmed in bilateral lower extremities on nerve conduction studies. Imaging post-radiation shows tissue fibrosis and scar restriction in axillary region. No structural lesions or recurrence on surveillance imaging. Assessment indicates treatment-related toxicity requiring functional restoration.

puzzles.explanation: Cancer rehabilitation addresses the multifaceted functional decline that follows curative oncological treatment, driven by the cumulative toxicity of chemotherapy, surgery, and radiation rather than active disease. This patient exemplifies the constellation of treatment-related sequelae: chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) causing distal sensory loss and ankle weakness, surgical/radiation-induced axillary cording and shoulder restriction limiting overhead mobility, and the pervasive cancer-related fatigue that disproportionately impairs activity tolerance and energy reserve. Cancer rehabilitation differs fundamentally from other post-operative or neurological conditions because it must simultaneously address multiple tissue systems affected by different treatment modalities while managing fatigue as a primary limiting factor rather than a secondary symptom. Restoration of function requires graded activity pacing, targeted mobility work for fibrosed tissues, sensory retraining for neuropathy, and strategic energy management to rebuild exercise capacity and return to meaningful activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 18-year-old soccer player was struck in the head by another player's elbow during a match 5 days ago. The athlete reports headache, dizziness, and sensitivity to light and noise. Memory of the incident and the preceding 10 minutes is absent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient experiences worsening of symptoms with cognitive tasks and physical exertion. Reading, concentrating on schoolwork, and typing increase headache severity. Nausea occurs with rapid head movements. Balance appears impaired when eyes are closed or on uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior head injuries. The incident occurred during competitive play; witness noted 20–30 seconds of apparent confusion and disorientation immediately after impact, but no documented unconsciousness. Symptoms have persisted unchanged over 5 days despite rest; athlete was not permitted to continue playing. No vomiting, seizure, or loss of bowel/bladder control reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the King-Devick test shows slowed reading speed and tracking difficulties. Tandem stance test demonstrates mild balance impairment with 1-2 foot touches required. Rapid alternating movements of hands are slightly uncoordinated bilaterally. Pupil reactivity is normal; cranial nerve examination is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT imaging of the head is normal with no acute intracranial abnormality, hemorrhage, or fracture identified. Baseline neuroimaging prior to injury is not available for comparison. Routine imaging does not show diffuse axonal injury on conventional sequences.

puzzles.explanation: Concussion is a complex pathophysiological process triggered by biomechanical forces that cause functional disturbance of the brain, typically without structural damage visible on standard imaging. The hallmark presentation involves impaired neurocognitive function—evident here in the post-traumatic amnesia, slowed processing on the King-Devick test, and difficulty concentrating—alongside vestibulo-cerebellar signs such as balance impairment and nausea with head movement, reflecting disrupted brainstem and cerebellar integration. The symptom exacerbation with both cognitive and physical exertion is pathognomonic for concussion and reflects heightened cerebral metabolic demand in an injured brain; this exertional intolerance typically resolves over days to weeks as neurochemical and metabolic homeostasis is restored. The absence of structural findings on CT does not exclude concussion—most concussions are metabolic injuries rather than structural ones—and gradual symptom resolution with cognitive and physical rest remains the standard management approach.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old competitive swimmer presents with insidious-onset lower back pain localized to one side of the midline. Pain worsens with backward arching or hyperextension movements. The pain is sharp and increases with high-intensity training or sprinting activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient experiences pain with butterfly stroke and backstroke more than freestyle. Pain intensifies with training progression and return to competitive intensity. Prolonged sitting and forward bending are tolerated without significant symptoms. Pain is worst at end-of-day after training.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has trained competitively for 5 years with high training volume and repetitive spinal extension loading. Pain onset occurred 8 weeks ago coinciding with increased training intensity. No traumatic event or acute injury. Family history is notable for low back pain in both parents.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, unilateral lumbar pain is reproduced with single-leg hyperextension test on the affected side. Lumbar extension combined with rotation toward the painful side increases symptoms. Palpation reveals paraspinal muscle tenderness at the level of pain. Lower extremity neurological examination is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan demonstrates a unilateral pars interarticularis defect at the L5 level with intact vertebral body and disc. SPECT imaging shows increased uptake at the symptomatic level, indicating active bone stress. Plain radiographs may appear normal; advanced imaging is required for definitive visualization.

puzzles.explanation: Spondylolysis is a stress fracture of the pars interarticularis, the narrow bony bridge connecting the superior and inferior facet joints of the lumbar spine. It develops from repetitive hyperextension and rotation loading — particularly common in young athletes engaged in activities like swimming, gymnastics, and weightlifting — where the pars bears excessive shear and compressive forces. The unilateral presentation and reproduction of pain with single-leg hyperextension (stressing the ipsilateral pars) are hallmark clinical findings, and the sharp, activity-related pain that resolves with forward bending reflects the biomechanical nature of the lesion. CT imaging showing the discrete defect at L5 with active bone remodeling on SPECT confirms active stress injury, distinguishing this from asymptomatic spondylolysis found incidentally in up to 6% of the population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old competitive runner. Gradual onset of forefoot pain under the ball of the foot over 6 weeks. Pain described as aching and localized but patient unsure of exact structure involved. No history of trauma or acute injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered specifically by propulsive activities: sprinting, hill running, and push-off maneuvers cause sharp discomfort. Walking and swimming tolerated. Pain worsens with prolonged running but improves with rest. Symptoms worse in minimalist footwear.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms coincided with increase in training volume and switch to lighter racing shoes 6 weeks ago. No prior foot surgery or fracture. No systemic inflammatory conditions. Pain gradually worsening despite activity modification. Right foot dominant and symptomatic side.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Point tenderness elicited directly plantar to the first metatarsal head, in the groove between the two sesamoid bones. Pain reproduced with resisted plantarflexion of the first metatarsophalangeal joint. First MTP joint range of motion preserved but limited by discomfort. No swelling, erythema, or skin changes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows intact sesamoid bones without fracture or fragmentation. MRI demonstrates bone marrow edema within the medial sesamoid with surrounding soft tissue inflammation. No other forefoot pathology identified.

puzzles.explanation: Sesamoiditis is inflammation of the sesamoid bones beneath the first metatarsal head—two small ossicles embedded in the flexor hallucis brevis tendon that bear significant load during push-off. In competitive runners, repetitive high-force plantarflexion loading, particularly with increased training volume or minimalist footwear that reduces cushioning, causes microtrauma and inflammatory response within the sesamoid bone marrow. The condition is characterized by point tenderness in the sesamoid groove and pain specifically during propulsive activities—sprinting and hill running—where plantarflexion stress peaks, while preserved first MTP motion excludes joint arthropathy. MRI showing bone marrow edema with preserved bony integrity differentiates sesamoiditis from sesamoid fracture and guides conservative management focused on activity modification, shock absorption, and gradual return to running.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old construction worker. Acute onset of bilateral arm and leg weakness and numbness following a fall from height 2 weeks ago. Reports loss of sensation below shoulders and inability to move legs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk or stand independently. Unable to grip or manipulate objects with either hand. Breathing difficulty when lying flat; symptoms improve with trunk support. Dependent for all self-care.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred after fall with impact to neck/upper back. No previous neurological disease or spinal problems. Immediate onset at time of trauma. Hospitalized urgently; some voluntary movement returning in shoulders and upper arms over past 2 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder and elbow strength 3-4/5 bilaterally; wrist and hand muscles 0-1/5. Legs completely flaccid. Sensory loss to all modalities below C6 dermatome bilaterally. Lower limb reflexes brisk and hyperactive. No anal tone or bulbocavernosus reflex.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine shows central spinal cord signal change with hemorrhage and edema at C5-C6. CT demonstrates cervical vertebral fracture-dislocation with posterior ligament rupture and spinal canal compromise. Post-surgical imaging confirms vertebral alignment and decompression.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical spinal cord injury results from traumatic disruption of neural tissue in the upper spinal cord, causing immediate loss of motor and sensory function below the lesion level. This patient's acute presentation following neck trauma with central cord hemorrhage and edema at C5-C6 is characteristic — the bilateral arm and leg weakness reflects damage to both corticospinal tracts, while the relatively preserved shoulder and upper arm strength (3-4/5) compared to profound hand weakness (0-1/5) indicates a central cord pattern of injury affecting the more medially-located hand and lower limb motor fibers preferentially. The brisk lower limb reflexes and absent bulbocavernosus reflex are typical of acute spinal shock transitioning to hyperreflexia, and the respiratory compromise reflects involvement of the phrenic nerve supply (C3-C5), making early surgical decompression and stabilization critical to prevent secondary injury and optimize neurological recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 8-year-old child. Parents report child appears clumsy during sports and play, frequently trips, difficulty with ball skills. Also noted to be withdrawn in group settings and becomes upset in noisy or crowded environments.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Struggles with activities requiring rapid coordination changes (e.g., catching, dodging). Prefers solitary or parallel play rather than team activities. Can perform individual tasks well but noticeably slower to process instructions during group play.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Clumsiness noticed since age 3-4 years. No significant trauma or neurological events. Speech and language milestones age-appropriate. Parents report child has rigid routines, dislikes changes to schedule, and has few close peer relationships despite adequate speech.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gross motor strength and tone normal. Gait unremarkable. Dysdiadochokinesia present (impaired rapid alternating movements). Difficulty with motor planning on complex bilateral tasks. Fine motor strength intact. Mild postural instability during tandem stance. Eye contact inconsistent; appears to avoid social engagement during assessment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Standardized neuropsychological testing shows deficits in social communication, restricted interests, and motor planning difficulties. Brain MRI structurally normal. No focal abnormalities on EEG. Clinical presentation meets DSM-5 criteria for autism spectrum disorder with associated motor coordination concerns.

puzzles.explanation: Autism spectrum disorder is a neurodevelopmental condition characterized by persistent difficulties in social communication and interaction, alongside restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior or interests. Motor coordination challenges—including dysdiadochokinesia, impaired motor planning, and apparent clumsiness during dynamic tasks—are now recognized as core features in many autistic individuals, reflecting differences in cerebellar and proprioceptive processing rather than primary motor weakness. The constellation of features here—social withdrawal in group settings, sensory sensitivities to noise and crowding, rigid adherence to routines, and the notable discrepancy between intact individual task performance and slow processing during group instruction—are hallmark presentations. Early identification of motor and sensory profiles alongside social-communicative difficulties enables targeted physiotherapy to address coordination, balance, and motor planning, while supporting the child's natural preference for structured, predictable environments.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old retired teacher reports recent onset of spinning sensation when changing positions in bed or bending to pick up objects from the floor. Episodes last seconds to minutes and are accompanied by mild nausea. No hearing loss or tinnitus is noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient avoids rapid head movements and reports difficulty with balance confidence when turning the head quickly. Morning routines are affected as dizziness occurs when lying down or rolling over. Walking is cautious but functional without assistive device.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent head trauma is denied. The patient reports mild diabetes and hypertension managed with medications. Multiple falls in the past year have increased safety concerns. No prior episodes of vertigo are documented.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Dix-Hallpike maneuver reproduces brief rotatory nystagmus with 2-3 second latency. Head impulse test normal bilaterally. Gait base is narrow with mild weight transfer asymmetry. Romberg test elicits mild sway.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT temporal bones shows normal semicircular canal anatomy without canalith debris or hydrops. MRI excludes retrocochlear mass, demyelination, and central pathology. Findings consistent with age-related otolith dysfunction.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related vertigo results from progressive degeneration of the otolith organs (utricle and saccule) in the inner ear, which normally sense linear acceleration and head position relative to gravity. As otolithic calcium carbonate crystals degenerate or migrate with aging, they become unstable and can trigger brief episodes of intense vertigo with position changes—particularly when bending forward or lying down—accompanied by nystagmus and brief nausea. The normal imaging findings and positive Dix-Hallpike response with characteristic latency suggest benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) secondary to age-related otolith dysfunction rather than true canalolithiasis, explaining why canal anatomy appears preserved. This presentation is common in older adults, especially those with multiple falls and balance confidence deficits, and typically responds well to vestibular rehabilitation and canalith repositioning strategies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old with long-standing lower leg swelling affecting the left lower extremity. Reports progressive discomfort and functional decline with difficulty walking and standing for prolonged periods.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids prolonged standing or walking; symptoms worsen throughout the day. Difficulty with stair climbing and footwear selection due to ankle swelling. Night-time elevation partially relieves symptoms. Recent episode of severe swelling after long airplane flight.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of left lower limb deep vein thrombosis 8 years prior, treated with anticoagulation. Persistent leg swelling despite treatment completion. Multiple episodes of cellulitis requiring antibiotics over the past 3 years. Obesity and sedentary lifestyle. No previous limb trauma or cancer history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left lower leg pitting oedema from midthigh to ankle. Skin appears thickened and dimpled in places; subcutaneous tissues feel firm and non-compressible on deep palpation. No acute erythema or warmth. Mild restriction in ankle dorsiflexion. Calf circumference asymmetry 4 cm compared to right side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates severely delayed radiolabelled tracer clearance from the left lower limb with evidence of collateral lymphatic drainage pathways and dermal backflow. No significant deep venous obstruction on repeat compression ultrasound.

puzzles.explanation: Lymphoedema is chronic swelling resulting from impaired lymphatic drainage and accumulation of tissue fluid, often developing secondary to lymph vessel damage or lymph node dysfunction. In this case, the previous deep vein thrombosis likely caused endothelial injury and chronic inflammatory changes that compromised lymphatic function, a common pathway to secondary lower limb lymphoedema—particularly evident here in the progressive, non-pitting characteristics and thickened, dimpled skin reflecting chronic fibrosis. The severely delayed tracer clearance on lymphoscintigraphy confirms lymphatic insufficiency rather than venous obstruction, while recurrent cellulitis episodes reflect the compromised drainage and increased infection risk typical of established lymphoedema. Management focuses on compression therapy, activity modification, skin care, and manual lymphatic drainage to optimize the remaining lymphatic function and prevent further deterioration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old office worker with a sedentary occupation reports generalized fatigue and difficulty with sustained activity. Breathing becomes labored after climbing a single flight of stairs. No acute illness precedes these symptoms, which developed gradually over several months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Previously independent with household tasks, the patient now requires frequent rest periods. Walking distance has progressively decreased to approximately two blocks. Cardiac symptoms are denied, but dyspnea is proportional to exertion intensity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient experienced prolonged illness requiring hospitalization and bed rest six months ago. Since discharge, activity levels have remained low despite medical clearance to resume normal function. No new cardiac or pulmonary diagnoses have been established.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate is elevated at 92 bpm, with blood pressure 118/76 mmHg. Six-minute walk test demonstrates distance of 280 meters with dyspnea rating of 4/10. Lower extremity strength is 4/5 throughout major muscle groups. Cardiopulmonary auscultation is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Resting energy expenditure by indirect calorimetry is 15% below predicted for age and body composition. Maximal oxygen uptake (VO₂ max) estimated at 18 mL/kg/min, consistent with 'poor' cardiovascular fitness category for age. Skeletal muscle cross-sectional area reduced by 12% compared to age-matched normative data on thigh MRI.

puzzles.explanation: Deconditioning is a syndrome of progressive functional decline resulting from prolonged inactivity, bed rest, or sedentary behaviour, causing simultaneous deterioration in cardiovascular, muscular, and metabolic capacity. The pathophysiology involves loss of skeletal muscle mass and oxidative enzyme activity, reduced stroke volume and cardiac efficiency, and impaired oxygen extraction at the cellular level — explaining why this patient develops dyspnea with minimal exertion and shows reduced VO₂ max despite normal cardiac and pulmonary structures on examination. Following hospitalization and bed rest, many patients fail to reinitiate activity despite medical clearance, creating a self-perpetuating cycle; the elevated resting heart rate, reduced six-minute walk distance, and modest strength loss reflect the typical multiplex physiology of disuse rather than organ-specific disease. Restoration requires graded, progressive aerobic and resistance training tailored to rebuild both cardiovascular and skeletal muscle reserve.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old manual laborer reports progressive shoulder weakness and drooping appearance of the shoulder over four weeks. Shoulder elevation is effortful and shoulder shrugging is noticeably asymmetrical. No acute trauma or neck pain is reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty with overhead work tasks and carrying loads on the affected shoulder. Shrugging against resistance feels weak compared to the contralateral side. Neck lateral flexion and rotation are unaffected functionally.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset was insidious over four weeks without preceding neck trauma, surgery, or focal nerve injury event. No family history of neuromuscular disease. Patient works as manual laborer with repetitive overhead demands.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, shoulder shrug strength is 2/5 bilaterally asymmetrical with evident atrophy of the upper trapezius on the affected side. Sternocleidomastoid strength is 5/5 bilaterally. Neck flexion and lateral flexion strength are 5/5 bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography shows denervation potentials in the trapezius muscle with reduced motor unit recruitment. MRI of the neck demonstrates normal spinal canal and nerve root foramina. No mass lesions are identified along the accessory nerve course.

puzzles.explanation: Accessory nerve palsy results from damage to the cranial nerve XI as it travels through the neck and shoulder, causing selective weakness of the trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles. Unlike brachial plexus injuries or cervical radiculopathies, this condition produces a characteristic pattern of isolated shoulder elevation weakness with preserved neck flexion and rotation — as seen in the normal sternocleidomastoid strength here — making it clinically distinctive. The insidious four-week onset with progressive upper trapezius atrophy and asymmetrical shrug weakness, combined with normal imaging and EMG denervation in the trapezius distribution, confirms the diagnosis. Accessory nerve injury can be iatrogenic (surgery, biopsy) or result from traction during overhead work or blunt trauma to the posterior triangle, though idiopathic cases occur and typically show good recovery with conservative management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old office manager presents with insidious onset of posterior neck and upper back pain that worsens during the workday. Headaches originating from the base of the skull are frequent. Symptoms are not associated with trauma or recent illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports significant limitation with sustained computer work and avoids overhead activities. Forward head posture is evident during daily tasks and contributes to symptom exacerbation. Postural correction temporarily eases discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a ten-year history of desk-based work without ergonomic modifications. Recent job transition to new workspace increased postural demands. No prior neurological symptoms or imaging are documented. Sleep quality has diminished due to nighttime neck stiffness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, cervical extension is limited to 35 degrees (normal 50 degrees) with reproduction of upper back pain. Scapular positioning demonstrates bilateral elevation and protraction relative to the thorax. Manual muscle testing of lower trapezius and serratus anterior shows weakness (4/5). Upper limb neurological testing is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays demonstrate loss of cervical lordosis with anterior translation of the head relative to C7. MRI shows no neural compromise. Soft tissue imaging reveals marked bilateral upper trapezius hypertrophy with corresponding atrophy of lower trapezius and serratus anterior, consistent with scapular dyskinesis.

puzzles.explanation: Upper crossed syndrome results from chronic postural dysfunction where tight, overactive upper trapezius and pectoralis muscles cross with weak, inhibited lower trapezius and serratus anterior, creating a distinctive pattern of muscular imbalance across the cervical and scapular regions. This presentation is particularly common in desk workers: sustained forward head posture and rounded shoulders shift the cervical load anteriorly, perpetuating upper trapezius dominance while the scapular stabilizers fatigue and atrophy. The loss of cervical lordosis and limited extension observed here, combined with the characteristic scapular elevation and protraction during examination, directly reflect this postural compensatory pattern. Management focuses on restoring motor control through lower trapezius and serratus anterior strengthening alongside upper trapezius and pectoral stretching, with ergonomic modification and postural retraining essential to prevent recurrence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old carpenter reports progressive lateral forearm pain that is worse with gripping and sustained pinching activities. Pain is localized to the proximal forearm and does not extend into the hand. Symptom onset was gradual without documented injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty with sustained tool use and reports pain increases with repetitive supination and pronation movements. Gripping strength is significantly diminished compared to the contralateral side. Light activity such as writing causes mild discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occupational repetitive strain history is significant with decades of carpentry work requiring pronation and gripping. Symptoms began insidiously two months ago. No cervical radiculopathy history or neurological dysfunction is reported. No previous forearm injuries have occurred.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resisted supination reproduces lateral forearm pain. Resisted wrist extension is weak at 4/5 with lateral forearm reproduction. Palpation over the radial tunnel elicits point tenderness. Two-point discrimination and sensation are normal in the dorsal first web space.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the forearm demonstrates mild edema in the extensor carpi radialis muscle near the radial tunnel. High-resolution ultrasound shows compression of the posterior interosseous nerve with soft tissue impingement. No fractures or significant mass lesions are identified.

puzzles.explanation: Radial tunnel syndrome results from compression of the posterior interosseous nerve (PIN) as it passes through the radial tunnel at the proximal forearm, typically from soft tissue impingement such as muscular hypertrophy, fascial bands, or edema rather than bony pathology. Unlike radial nerve compression at the spiral groove, which causes wrist drop and dorsal hand sensory loss, PIN compression presents with isolated lateral forearm pain and weakness of wrist and finger extension without sensory deficits — the preserved two-point discrimination here is diagnostically important. The presentation is characteristic of chronic occupational strain: decades of repetitive pronation-supination in carpentry work creates extensor muscle edema and overuse, compressing the nerve as it courses between the extensor carpi radialis and supinator, and resisted supination and wrist extension reproduce symptoms by tensioning the compressed nerve. Ultrasound confirmation of PIN compression with soft tissue impingement distinguishes this from cervical radiculopathy or other lateral elbow pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational swimmer reports acute onset of sharp posterior shoulder pain during a training session. Pain is located in the lower scapular region and radiates laterally toward the mid-arm. No arm numbness or tingling is experienced.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports pain increases significantly with arm pulling movements and rowing-like activities. Overhead activities provoke sharp symptoms, whereas resting the arm in the lap is comfortable. Swimming strokes that emphasize pull-through phase are most symptomatic.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during sprint set in week 6 of periodized training block after progressive volume build. Patient denies prior shoulder injuries. No relevant comorbidities or inflammatory history. Training load increased 40% over preceding 4 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resisted shoulder adduction and internal rotation provoke sharp pain. Palpation over posterolateral shoulder at mid-scapular border is exquisitely tender. Scapular dyskinesis noted during arm elevation. Forward flexion limited to 120° due to muscle guarding; extension restricted to 20°.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates hyperintense signal within muscle belly at the junction of middle and lower thirds, consistent with edema and microtearing. No complete disruption of muscle-tendon continuity. Supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and labral structures are intact. No subacromial impingement.

puzzles.explanation: Latissimus dorsi strain results from acute overload of this large posterior shoulder muscle, which is the primary internal rotator and adductor of the glenohumeral joint and a key driver of pulling and swimming motions. The muscle's broad insertion and its role in controlling arm deceleration during the pull-through phase of swimming makes it vulnerable during high-volume sprint training, especially when load increases rapidly as in this case. The characteristic presentation—sharp posterolateral pain with resisted adduction and internal rotation, exquisite tenderness at the mid-scapular border, and restricted extension—reflects direct muscle fiber injury at the musculotendinous junction. MRI confirmation of edema and microtearing without complete disruption indicates a partial strain; conservative management with load modification, progressive eccentric training, and motor control work typically restores full swimming function within 4–6 weeks.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 22-year-old reports progressive shortness of breath with minimal exertion and persistent cough productive of thick sputum. Symptoms began in early childhood and have worsened steadily. Frequent chest infections requiring antibiotics.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking tolerance severely limited to 100–200 metres before dyspnea forces rest. Unable to participate in any sustained aerobic activity. Requires multiple daily airway clearance sessions with mechanical devices to manage secretions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Condition identified in infancy via screening. Two respiratory hospitalizations in past 2 years. Recurrent sinus infections and history of nasal polyps requiring surgery. Reports loose, fatty stools. Blood glucose elevated on recent screening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral inspiratory crackles throughout lung fields, decreased air entry at bases. Barrel chest and increased thoracic kyphosis noted. Six-minute walk test: 280 metres with oxygen desaturation to 84%. Productive cough yields copious thick greenish secretions.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates hyperinflation, bronchial wall thickening, and areas of bronchiectasis. High-resolution CT shows diffuse bronchial dilation with bronchus-to-artery ratio >1.5 bilaterally and bronchial wall thickening consistent with chronic airway damage.

puzzles.explanation: Cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the CFTR gene, leading to defective chloride channel function and abnormally thick, dehydrated secretions across multiple organ systems. In the lungs, this viscous mucus obstructs airways, trapping bacteria and triggering chronic infection, inflammation, and progressive bronchiectasis — explaining the copious thick sputum, recurrent hospitalizations, and imaging findings of bronchial dilation and thickening seen here. Pancreatic insufficiency (fatty stools, elevated blood glucose) and sinopulmonary disease (nasal polyps, recurrent sinus infections) reflect the multisystem nature of the condition. The severe exercise limitation and barrel chest with increased kyphosis demonstrate how early-onset, relentless airway damage compromises both ventilation and skeletal integrity over decades of disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old child presents with parental concerns about apparent clumsiness and difficulty with age-appropriate motor tasks. Teachers report the child drops objects frequently and moves awkwardly during class activities. Gross motor skills appear noticeably behind peers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child demonstrates difficulty with bilateral coordinated movement tasks such as skipping or riding a bicycle. Handwriting is immature for age with poor pencil grip and inconsistent letter formation. Ball-catching and throwing demonstrate poor timing and coordination.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Developmental milestones were met but with noted awkwardness in acquisition of motor skills. Early childhood gross motor development appeared slower than siblings. No history of neurological disease, cerebral palsy, or intellectual disability is documented. Symptoms have been lifelong and persistent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, rapid alternating movements are slow and irregular bilaterally. Tandem stance reveals significant loss of balance. Gait assessment shows asymmetrical arm swing with trunk instability. Strength and deep tendon reflexes are normal throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Movement ABC-2 testing shows motor proficiency score at the 3rd percentile. Standardized assessment of coordination, fine motor control, and balance all fall below expected range for age. Brain MRI is structurally unremarkable.

puzzles.explanation: Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD) is a neurodevelopmental condition characterized by significant difficulty acquiring and executing coordinated motor skills that cannot be attributed to intellectual disability, neurological disease, or other medical conditions. The disorder reflects a fundamental impairment in motor planning and execution — children struggle with both gross motor tasks like balance and gait control, and fine motor skills like handwriting and pencil grip, despite normal strength and reflexes. The persistent, lifelong nature of motor awkwardness combined with below-age-level performance on standardized coordination testing is pathognomonic; structural brain imaging is typically normal, distinguishing DCD from acquired or degenerative neurological conditions. Early identification and motor-specific physiotherapy intervention focusing on task-specific training and adaptive strategies can significantly improve functional outcomes and reduce secondary developmental complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old cyclist reports acute onset of severe pain in the shoulder girdle region following a fall. Visible deformity is present over the anterior shoulder. Shoulder movement is severely limited and guarded.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot bear weight through the upper extremity or support the arm without severe pain. Horizontal adduction across the chest reproduces sharp pain at the injury site.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred from direct impact to the anterior shoulder striking a curb. Symptom onset was immediate with rapid swelling. No prior shoulder girdle injuries or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation elicits sharp point tenderness over the injury site with a palpable step deformity. The clavicle appears shortened. Neurovascular examination is intact with normal distal pulses and sensation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: AP chest X-ray demonstrates a fracture line through the lateral clavicle with superior displacement and angulation of the fragments. Associated soft tissue swelling is present.

puzzles.explanation: Clavicle fracture results from direct impact to the shoulder girdle, with the distal third being most vulnerable due to its transition from cancellous to cortical bone and limited soft tissue protection. The immediate severe pain, visible deformity, and palpable step deformity with clavicular shortening reflect the acute displacement of bone fragments — particularly the superior angulation seen on imaging, which occurs because the sternocleidomastoid and upper trapezius pull the proximal fragment upward. The inability to bear weight or perform horizontal adduction reflects both the fracture itself and reactive guarding from painful muscle contraction, while the intact neurovascular status is reassuring despite the proximity to the brachial plexus and subclavian vessels. Most lateral third fractures, like this one, are managed conservatively with immobilization and progressive mobilization as pain allows, with surgical intervention reserved for displaced fragments or neurovascular compromise.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old woman with a known history of osteoporosis presents with acute onset of severe midline thoracic back pain. Pain began without clear precipitant and is constant in nature, not relieved by positional changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Forward bending sharply exacerbates pain at the injury site. The patient can tolerate standing and walking for only brief periods before pain escalates significantly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 15-year history of diagnosed osteoporosis with multiple medication-treated risk factors. Recent history includes a minor fall from standing height that may have triggered onset. No direct significant trauma is reported, yet symptoms began acutely.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation over the mid-thoracic vertebrae elicits severe point tenderness. Thoracic flexion is markedly limited to approximately 20 degrees. Neurological examination is intact with normal lower extremity strength and sensation throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Thoracic spine X-rays demonstrate acute wedge-shaped compression deformity of T7 vertebral body with 30 percent height loss. MRI shows acute signal change on STIR imaging consistent with recent compression fracture. Posterior vertebral body line remains intact without retropulsion.

puzzles.explanation: Vertebral compression fractures occur when osteoporotic bone loses sufficient trabecular density to fail under axial loading, causing collapse of the vertebral body—often from minimal trauma or even simple positional stress. This patient's acute thoracic pain with severe point tenderness and marked flexion limitation reflects the acute inflammatory phase and mechanical instability at the fracture site, while preserved neurological function indicates the posterior vertebral wall remains intact without spinal cord compromise. The wedge-shaped deformity on imaging is pathognomonic, and the recent STIR signal change confirms acute fracture rather than chronic collapse. In osteoporotic patients, multiple vertebral compression fractures compound kyphotic deformity and progressive functional decline, making early recognition and aggressive anti-osteoporotic management crucial to prevent cascade fracturing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old distance runner reports progressive deep buttock pain that worsens with running and sitting. Pain is located in the sacral region and does not radiate into the lower extremity. Symptoms developed insidiously over eight weeks of increased training volume.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot tolerate any high-impact activity. Running and jumping are completely prohibited. Interestingly, pain does not ease with typical rest and NSAIDs despite reduced activity, suggesting a slower-healing pathology.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Training volume increased by 40 percent over the past three months to prepare for a marathon. The patient reports a female athlete triad history with irregular menses and low bone density screening concerns. Previous stress fractures in the femoral neck occurred two years ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation over the sacral ala reproduces localized pain. Single-leg stance on the contralateral leg reproduces symptoms on the affected side. Hip strength testing reveals weakness in hip abductors at 4/5. Neurological examination is completely normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Initial X-rays show no obvious fracture line. MRI demonstrates a linear fracture line through the sacral ala with surrounding bone marrow edema extending into the sacral body. CT imaging confirms fracture orientation consistent with stress mechanism. Imaging shows no displacement.

puzzles.explanation: Sacral stress fractures result from repetitive loading exceeding bone capacity to remodel, particularly in endurance athletes with compromised bone quality or biomechanical dysfunction. This runner's presentation — progressive deep buttock pain worsening with high-impact activity, insidious onset during a training volume spike, and history of female athlete triad with prior stress fracture — reflects classic risk factors: rapid loading progression combined with systemic bone fragility. The linear fracture through the sacral ala with marrow edema, reproduced by contralateral single-leg stance (which loads the sacrum ipsilaterally via hip abductor weakness), is pathognomonic and why initial X-rays often miss what MRI readily shows. Unlike simple muscular buttock pain, this injury resists conservative management without addressing both the stress overload and underlying bone health deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by parents who report noting asymmetry in shoulder positioning. The right shoulder appears elevated and displaced medially compared to the left. No complaints of pain are reported by the child.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child participates in sports with minimal limitation but demonstrates restricted overhead shoulder mobility on the affected side. Scapular positioning is visibly asymmetrical during functional tasks. Swimming and throwing activities demonstrate compensatory patterns.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Despite minimal functional limitation, the child reports difficulty reaching overhead on the affected side and avoids butterfly strokes in swimming due to restricted shoulder abduction. Asymmetry has been present since early childhood without progression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the affected scapula is positioned medially and superiorly relative to the contralateral side. The inferior angle is displaced upward and medially. ROM testing demonstrates limited shoulder abduction and external rotation. Strength testing is normal throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiography and CT imaging of the scapula demonstrate abnormal hypoplasia and elevation of the scapula with superior and medial displacement. The scapula is smaller than the contralateral side with shortened vertical axis. MRI confirms intact neuromuscular innervation without nerve compression.

puzzles.explanation: Sprengel's deformity is a congenital condition characterized by underdevelopment and abnormal elevation of the scapula, resulting from failure of normal scapular descent during embryonic development. The affected scapula becomes hypoplastic, positioned superiorly and medially, which restricts shoulder abduction and overhead mobility—as evidenced by the difficulty with swimming strokes and throwing mechanics in this young patient. Despite the obvious structural asymmetry, neurological function remains intact and pain is typically absent, making this primarily a cosmetic and functional limitation rather than a neurogenic problem. Management is typically conservative with physiotherapy focusing on scapular and shoulder mobility, though surgical intervention may be considered if functional impairment is significant.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old female presents with insidious onset of symmetrical hand and wrist pain, worse in the mornings. She reports significant stiffness affecting grip strength and daily activities, with swelling in multiple joints affecting function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning clothes and opening jars due to joint pain and reduced range of motion. Morning stiffness lasts over 60 minutes, improving only after activity and warm showers. Fatigue is a limiting factor in her functional capacity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-month progressive course with no recent trauma or infection preceding symptoms. Family history significant for autoimmune disease. Mild systemic symptoms including malaise and low-grade fevers accompanying the articular complaints.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, symmetrical swelling and warmth noted in metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Grip strength reduced at 15 kg bilaterally (normal >25 kg). Positive squeeze test of metacarpal heads elicits pain. Range of motion limited in wrist extension and flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays reveal periarticular soft tissue swelling, juxta-articular osteopenia, and early marginal erosions at metacarpophalangeal joints. Ultrasound confirms synovial hypertrophy with increased vascularity on power Doppler, consistent with active inflammatory synovitis.

puzzles.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis is a systemic autoimmune disease characterized by chronic inflammation of the synovial lining, leading to progressive joint destruction and extra-articular manifestations. The hallmark presentation—symmetrical polyarticular involvement of small joints, prolonged morning stiffness exceeding 60 minutes, and the insidious progression over months—reflects the underlying Th1-driven immune response that preferentially targets the hands and wrists. The periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions on imaging, coupled with synovial hypertrophy and increased vascularity on ultrasound, indicate active inflammatory synovitis causing both functional loss and irreversible joint damage. Associated constitutional symptoms and family history of autoimmune disease are typical features that support the diagnosis and justify early disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) therapy to prevent further structural damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old with chronic daily cough productive of sputum. Reports recurrent chest infections over the past 3-4 years, each taking weeks to resolve. Increasingly short of breath with normal activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk more than one flight of stairs without stopping. Cough worsens when lying flat; must prop up to sleep. Morning is worst—large amount of thick sputum expectoration. Exercise tolerance declining over months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Childhood history of recurrent pneumonia with incomplete recovery between episodes. Prior tuberculosis exposure (treated) and long-term smoking. Conventional asthma or COPD treatments provide minimal relief. No recent hemoptysis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Persistent fine-to-coarse crackles in lung bases that do not clear with cough. Resting oxygen saturation 91-93%. Prolonged expiratory phase on auscultation. Resonance normal on percussion. No wheeze; minimal response to bronchodilators during examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest shows bronchial luminal diameter larger than accompanying pulmonary artery (bronchus-to-artery ratio >1:1) in basilar segments. Bronchial walls thickened without normal progressive tapering. Traction changes in lower lobes; no emphysematous changes.

puzzles.explanation: Bronchiectasis is permanent, abnormal dilatation of the bronchi due to destruction of the elastic and muscular components of the bronchial wall, typically resulting from severe or recurrent infection, untreated tuberculosis, or congenital abnormalities. The condition creates a self-perpetuating cycle: dilated airways cannot clear secretions effectively, leading to chronic suppurative infection and further airway damage. The characteristic imaging finding—a bronchus-to-artery ratio exceeding 1:1 with loss of normal bronchial tapering—combined with basilar crackles that persist despite coughing and the history of childhood pneumonia with incomplete recovery, makes this diagnosis classic. The morning sputum production, orthopnea, and marked decline in exercise tolerance reflect the accumulated secretion burden and progressive parenchymal fibrosis typical of established bronchiectasis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old woman in her third trimester presents with unilateral pain in the lower back and buttock region. Pain onset occurred approximately 8 weeks ago and has progressively worsened with advancing pregnancy. Patient describes the discomfort as sharp and aching, affecting activities and sleep quality.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports pain with walking, climbing stairs, and rolling over in bed. Certain positions such as standing on one leg or crossing legs exacerbate symptoms significantly. Pain is notably worse at the end of the day and after prolonged standing or walking activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: This is the patient's second pregnancy; she had a complication-free first pregnancy 6 years prior. Current pregnancy is progressing normally without complications. Hormonal factors and weight gain associated with pregnancy are contributing factors. No prior history of low back or pelvic pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the long dorsal sacroiliac ligament test reproduces pain on the affected side. Patrick's test and FABER positioning elicit discomfort in the sacroiliac region. Palpation over the sacroiliac joint produces tenderness; midline sacral tenderness is absent. Hip abductor strength is reduced due to pain inhibition.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates normal fetal development with appropriate amniotic fluid volume. No structural abnormalities are noted in pelvic anatomy. MRI, if indicated, would show no degenerative changes or inflammatory findings in sacroiliac joints or supporting structures.

puzzles.explanation: Antenatal pelvic girdle pain (PGP) results from sacroiliac joint dysfunction triggered by pregnancy-related hormonal changes—particularly relaxin-mediated ligamentous laxity—combined with altered biomechanics and weight redistribution in the third trimester. The condition characteristically presents with unilateral or bilateral sacroiliac pain that worsens with asymmetrical loading activities like stair climbing, single-leg standing, and rolling in bed, as the gravid pelvis becomes increasingly unstable. Sharp sacroiliac tenderness with positive provocation tests (FABER, long dorsal ligament test) and normal imaging distinguish PGP from structural pathology; notably, it typically resolves postpartum once hormonal influences normalize and mechanical demand decreases. The absence of prior pelvic pain, despite a previous uncomplicated pregnancy, underscores how individual hormonal responses and cumulative mechanical stress in successive pregnancies can precipitate this self-limiting but functionally disabling condition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old collegiate soccer player reports sudden-onset anterior thigh pain, swelling, and bruising following a direct impact during play. Immediate functional impairment noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight on the affected limb; pain is reproducible with passive knee flexion and restricts walking distance to a few metres. Limb feels swollen and tense.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Single high-energy impact mechanism from opponent's knee strike. No previous thigh injuries or compartment syndrome history. Injury occurred 5 days ago; progressive swelling and bruising noted despite ice and compression application. No anticoagulation therapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Firm, tender swelling palpable over anteromedial quadriceps extending proximally. Passive knee flexion markedly painful at end-range (60°). Quadriceps strength 3/5 with pain-guarding pattern. Compartments firm but no clinical signs of compartment syndrome.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound reveals well-defined hypoechoic collection within quadriceps musculature consistent with intramuscular haematoma, measuring 5 cm x 3 cm. Muscle fibers partially disrupted with surrounding edema. No evidence of compartment syndrome on clinical correlation, though compartments appear tense.

puzzles.explanation: Quadriceps contusion results from blunt trauma causing muscle fiber disruption and intramuscular haematoma formation, commonly seen in contact sports when a direct blow damages the quadriceps muscle belly. The severity depends on the force, location, and depth of penetration; this injury produces a characteristic well-demarcated haematoma with surrounding edema and myofascial irritation, as evidenced by the 5 cm collection and markedly restricted passive knee flexion due to pain and muscle guarding. The progressive swelling over 5 days reflects ongoing inflammatory response and fluid accumulation within the fascia — a critical distinction from compartment syndrome, where the intact fascial envelope contains bleeding and carries risk of rising pressures. Recovery hinges on early aggressive range-of-motion work within pain tolerance to prevent contracture formation and restore quadriceps function, as quadriceps strength deficits from guarding can persist if rehabilitation is overly protective.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old female reports involuntary urinary leakage triggered by coughing, sneezing, and exertion. Symptoms began 6 months ago and have persisted despite conservative management.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient avoids high-impact activities and reports difficulty with running, jumping, and childcare activities involving movement. Reports pain during intercourse and reduced sexual enjoyment secondary to symptoms and self-consciousness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Uncomplicated vaginal delivery with episiotomy; labour duration 8 hours with instrumental assistance. No significant perineal trauma documented at delivery. Previous pregnancies without pelvic floor symptoms. Reports no pelvic pain but significant functional and psychological impact from symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On pelvic floor assessment, palpable weakness and reduced tone noted in pelvic floor musculature. Voluntary contraction strength graded at 3/5 on manual assessment. Positive cough stress test with demonstrable urine loss. Pelvic organ support appears normal on visual inspection without prolapse.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates normal bladder neck position at rest; however, dynamic ultrasound during cough shows insufficient urethral mobility and descent, with inadequate urethrovesical angle closure. No significant prolapse noted. Pelvic floor muscles show reduced echogenicity consistent with neuromuscular dysfunction.

puzzles.explanation: Postnatal pelvic floor dysfunction represents weakness and neuromuscular impairment of the pelvic floor muscles following vaginal delivery, even without overt traumatic injury. The mechanism involves stretch injury to the pudendal nerve and pelvic floor musculature during labour and delivery, resulting in denervation and loss of contractile function that persists beyond the typical 6–12 week postpartum recovery window. Stress urinary incontinence emerges as the cardinal feature when weakened muscles cannot generate sufficient urethrovesical angle closure and urethral support during sudden intra-abdominal pressure rises—explaining the cough and exertion triggers seen here. The combination of reduced pelvic floor strength (3/5), normal organ position, and dynamic ultrasound evidence of inadequate urethral mobility during strain confirms the diagnosis and distinguishes it from intrinsic sphincter deficiency or prolapse, making graded progressive resistance training the cornerstone of conservative management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old male presenting with progressive shortness of breath and persistent productive cough over the past 18 months. Patient reports cough is worse in the mornings. No fever, chest pain, or recent trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Marked difficulty with sustained exertion—climbing stairs leaves him winded, walking >100 meters on flat ground triggers breathlessness. Cough productive of clear to white sputum, particularly with activity. Symptoms worse in cold air and early mornings. Able to perform light sedentary tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 40-pack-year smoking history; quit 2 years ago but symptoms continued/worsened. No recent infections or hospitalizations. Prior occupational dust exposure (construction worker, 30+ years). No cardiac history, no asthma diagnosis in youth. Denies hemoptysis or orthopnea.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Barrel-shaped chest, pursed-lip breathing observed. Hyperresonant percussion throughout lung fields. Diminished breath sounds bilaterally with prolonged expiratory phase. No wheezes, crackles, or edema. Oxygen saturation 88% at rest, drops to 82% with walking. Heart sounds normal. No cor pulmonale stigmata.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray: bilateral hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms, increased anteroposterior diameter, reduced vascular markings. HRCT: centrilobular emphysema predominantly upper lobes, small airway wall thickening, areas of low attenuation (air-trapping) on expiratory images. No focal masses or fibrosis. Spirometry: FEV₁/FVC ratio 0.58, FEV₁ 42% predicted (GOLD Stage 3).

puzzles.explanation: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease results from progressive, irreversible airflow limitation caused by emphysema and small airway disease, typically from long-term smoking or occupational exposure. The pathophysiology involves destruction of alveolar walls (reducing elastic recoil), inflammation and thickening of small airways, and air-trapping—all evident in this patient's hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms, and prolonged expiration on exam. COPD characteristically presents with exertional dyspnea worsening in cold air and mornings (increased sputum production and airway reactivity), barrel chest from hyperinflation, and a significant gap between rest and exercise oxygen saturation reflecting compromised gas exchange. The centrilobular emphysema pattern on imaging and FEV₁/FVC ratio of 0.58 confirm irreversible airflow obstruction, distinguishing COPD from asthma and establishing the need for pulmonary rehabilitation, bronchodilators, and smoking cessation counseling.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old female reports unilateral headaches that began after starting a new office-based role. Pain worsens with certain neck movements and sustained postures. Analgesic medications provide minimal relief.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen during prolonged computer work and morning stiffness. Patient reports difficulty with work tasks requiring sustained posture or repeated neck turns to the right. Symptoms partially resolve with periodic breaks and postural changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-month history of recurrent episodes following increased screen time and poor ergonomics. No history of head trauma, though she notes chronic forward head posture. No photophobia or phonophobia. Pain began after sustained office work transitions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On cervical examination, restricted right cervical rotation (60 degrees; normal >80 degrees) and extension provokes familiar headache. Positive cervical flexion-rotation test reproducing unilateral frontal pain. Palpation reveals asymmetrical muscle tension in right upper trapezius and cervical paraspinals.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine MRI reveals mild degenerative changes at C3-C4 and C4-C5 levels with facet joint hypertrophy. No significant nerve root compression. Straightening of normal cervical lordosis noted. Multilevel spondylotic changes most prominent at C3-C5 corresponding to referred pain distribution.

puzzles.explanation: Cervicogenic headache originates from dysfunction or pathology in the cervical spine—most commonly involving the upper three cervical segments (C1-C3) and their associated facet joints, nerve roots, and musculature—with pain referred to the head via convergence of cervical and trigeminal pathways. It typically presents as unilateral pain that worsens with neck movement or sustained postures, precisely as seen with this patient's exacerbation during prolonged computer work and restriction in cervical rotation. The combination of facet joint hypertrophy, loss of lordosis, and positive cervical flexion-rotation testing (which specifically loads the C2-C3 facet joint) strongly supports upper cervical origin, distinguishing it from primary headache disorders and typical nerve root compression. Unlike migraine, cervicogenic headache responds poorly to analgesics alone but improves dramatically with postural correction and movement breaks—a characteristic therapeutic signature that guides both diagnosis and management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 41-year-old female. Gradual onset upper chest and shoulder girdle discomfort over past 3 months. Occasional tingling in hand. Symptoms variable throughout day, worse with certain positions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Overhead reaching and sustained arm elevation provoke symptoms. Carrying heavy objects difficult. Relief with arm support or lying down. Sleeping position affects symptom intensity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 3 months after motor vehicle accident with hyperextension-hyperflexion mechanism. Gradual progression despite conservative care. Worsens with work-related repetitive reaching and load-carrying. No prior upper extremity pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable prominence and tenderness at superior thoracic aperture on affected side. Scalene muscles notably tight bilaterally. Elevated arm stress test produces fatigue and colour change in hand within 2 minutes. Cervical and shoulder ROM preserved but asymmetrical muscular tension noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT cervical-thoracic region shows narrowed space between anterior and middle scalene muscles at first rib level, with muscular hypertrophy encroaching on neurovascular bundle. Cervical spine imaging normal; rib fractures excluded.

puzzles.explanation: First rib dysfunction results from mechanical narrowing of the thoracic outlet—the space bounded by the anterior and middle scalenes, first rib, and clavicle—leading to compression of the neurovascular bundle (brachial plexus and subclavian vessels). The condition commonly develops after traumatic hyperextension-hyperflexion mechanisms that disrupt scalene muscle tone and rib mechanics, and is perpetuated by overhead reaching and load-bearing activities that demand sustained scalene tension. Bilateral scalene tightness combined with a positive elevated arm stress test producing rapid distal colour change and fatigue—reflecting vascular compromise rather than isolated neurological involvement—is highly characteristic. The CT findings of muscular hypertrophy narrowing the interscalene space confirm structural compression at the thoracic inlet, distinguishing this from cervical radiculopathy or shoulder pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old female presents with progressive bilateral upper extremity pain, numbness, and a sensation of heaviness in the hands. Symptoms began insidiously over several months without clear trauma or precipitant.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot sustain overhead activities or maintain elevated arm positions for more than a few minutes. Symptoms worsen with tasks requiring arms held away from body, such as carrying or reaching. Sleep is disrupted by symptom flares with certain sleeping positions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-month progressive course without clear precipitant, though patient reports history of thoracic outlet symptoms with exercise. Recent workplace ergonomic changes and increased computer use. No prior surgical intervention. Symptoms initially postural but now present at rest in some positions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Adson test and Roos test reproducibly recreate symptoms bilaterally. Hand intrinsic weakness noted; thenar and hypothenar muscle bulk diminished. Supraclavicular palpation elicits tenderness and Tinel sign bilaterally. Pulses are equal and symmetric at rest.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MR angiography demonstrates reduced arterial caliber through thoracic outlet with compression of subclavian artery between anterior and middle scalene muscles. Color Doppler ultrasound confirms flow abnormality with positional changes. Cervical ribs absent but muscular hypertrophy contributes to narrowed interscalene space.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic outlet syndrome (TOS) results from neurovascular compression as the brachial plexus and subclavian vessels traverse the thoracic inlet, most commonly due to scalene muscle hypertrophy, postural dysfunction, or anatomical variants. The bilateral presentation with progressive insidious onset and exacerbation with overhead positioning reflects the postural and functional nature of muscular TOS, distinguished here from vascular variants by the combination of hand intrinsic weakness (lower trunk compression) and reproducible provocation on Adson and Roos testing. The reduced arterial caliber on imaging confirms the vascular component of compression, though the absence of cervical ribs and prominence of muscular hypertrophy emphasize that most cases—particularly with recent postural changes and computer use—stem from scalene tightness rather than anatomical anomaly. Sleep disruption and positional variability of symptoms are hallmark features that differentiate TOS from single-nerve entrapments, which remain relatively constant regardless of arm position.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old overhead athlete reports insidious onset of deep shoulder pain with clicking and catching sensations over the past 8 months. Pain is greatest with throwing activities and worsens progressively with continued participation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Throwing reproduces sharp deep pain with a catching sensation that forces cessation of activity. Late-cocking phase of throwing causes particular symptom exacerbation. Nighttime symptoms limit sleep comfort in certain positions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Eight-month history following increased training volume in throwing sport. No specific traumatic event preceding onset. Previous right shoulder impingement syndrome. Progressive worsening despite rest periods and anti-inflammatory medication management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive O'Brien's test producing deep shoulder pain consistent with patient's complaint. Anterior slide test and crank test in abduction/external rotation reproduce pain. Apprehension test positive in 90-90 position, suggesting anterior-superior instability pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MR arthrography reveals posterior superior labral tear extending from 10 o'clock to 2 o'clock position with associated irregular articular surface. Fluid tracking into the tear confirms disruption. Subtle associated undersurface rotator cuff fraying noted. Anteroinferior labrum appears intact.

puzzles.explanation: A shoulder labral tear involves disruption of the fibrocartilaginous labrum that deepens the glenoid socket and anchors key stabilizing structures. In overhead athletes, the posterior-superior labrum is particularly vulnerable during the late-cocking phase of throwing, where extreme external rotation and horizontal abduction place eccentric loads on the biceps anchor and adjacent labral tissue — repetitive microtrauma and progressive loading gradually compromise this region. The characteristic deep, catching pain with clicking, positive O'Brien's test, and reproduction during late-cocking throw mechanics reflect the biomechanical instability and labral irritation. MR arthrography, as shown here with the posterior-superior tear and fluid tracking, remains the imaging standard and often reveals associated undersurface cuff changes that develop secondarily to the altered glenohumeral mechanics.

clues.label: Complaint
