clues.text: 4-year-old child. Parents report slower development than siblings and peers. Child is floppy, tires easily, and seems weaker overall. Developmental milestones delayed. No significant illness or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with climbing stairs without upper limb support. Cannot jump or hop. Wide-based gait noted. Fine motor tasks clumsy—pencil grasp immature, self-feeding messy. Fatigues with sustained activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Identified in infancy with global delays across motor and cognitive domains. No acute neurological events. Associated with congenital cardiac anomaly on prior workup. Attends early intervention. Family reports lifelong low muscle tone.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked ligamentous laxity: thumb touches forearm, ankle dorsiflexion >40°, significant knee valgus at rest. Hypotonia diffuse. Sagittal plane control poor in supported standing. Prone head lift test: forehead unable to clear mat. Strength mildly reduced throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Karyotype reveals trisomy 21 (47 chromosomes). Echocardiogram shows endocardial cushion defect. Cervical spine imaging demonstrates atlantoaxial instability. Brain MRI shows simplified gyral pattern and hypoplastic corpus callosum.

puzzles.explanation: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) results from an extra copy of chromosome 21, leading to widespread effects on connective tissue integrity, muscle tone, and neurological development. The hallmark presentation combines generalized hypotonia with ligamentous laxity—the excessive range of motion and tissue compliance reflect abnormal collagen organization and reduced muscle stiffness that characterize the condition. Children with Down syndrome typically show global developmental delay, poor antigravity control (as evidenced by the inability to clear the forehead from the mat), and compensatory wide-based gait patterns due to low tone and reduced postural stability. Associated structural anomalies, particularly atlantoaxial instability and congenital cardiac defects, are common and require careful monitoring during physical activity and rehabilitation planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old runner reports insidious onset of deep anterior shin pain that began during training progression. Pain is activity-related and localized to the lower anterolateral leg, worse with running and relieving with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Onset of pain occurs during running, classically after 20 minutes of sustained activity. Sharp, deep pain in the shin prevents continued running and requires activity modification. Pain does not occur with walking at normal pace. Symptoms worsening with increased training volume despite attempts to rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Twelve-week progressive history correlating with increased distance running and addition of high-impact drills. Recent changes to training surface from track to asphalt. No previous lower extremity injuries. Training load increased 40% over 4 weeks preceding symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, focal tenderness elicited over anterior-medial tibia at the junction of proximal and middle thirds. Mild swelling noted over the painful region. Positive hop test reproducing familiar pain. Pain reproduced with resisted dorsiflexion. Percussion over tibia elicits focal pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Tibial radiographs appear radiographically normal in early stages. MRI reveals characteristic findings including bone marrow edema on T2 sequence localized to the anterior tibia in the symptomatic region. STIR imaging shows hyperintense signal reflecting inflammatory response. No complete fracture line identified.

puzzles.explanation: Tibial stress fractures result from repetitive loading that exceeds the bone's capacity to remodel, causing microdamage accumulation in the tibia — particularly common in distance runners during training progression. The anterior-medial tibia at the junction of proximal and middle thirds is a classic location for stress fractures due to tension-side forces during dorsiflexion and propulsion. The insidious onset correlating with a 40% increase in training load over four weeks, combined with the switch to asphalt, reflects the fundamental biomechanical principle that stress fractures occur when load is applied faster than bone adaptation; early imaging may show only marrow edema and inflammatory changes on MRI before radiographic fracture lines appear. The focal tenderness, positive percussion sign, and pain with resisted dorsiflexion are hallmark findings that distinguish stress fractures from other causes of shin pain such as medial tibial stress syndrome, and management hinges on early load reduction to prevent progression to complete fracture.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old female reports a painless bulge in the posterior knee that became noticeable after knee swelling and pain. The mass does not limit function significantly but causes subjective awareness and occasional discomfort with certain knee positions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Bulge becomes more apparent with knee extension and activity; the mass reduces somewhat with knee flexion and rest. Prolonged walking increases prominence. Certain knee positions occasionally provoke mild aching in the popliteal region. No mechanical symptoms or true locking noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Two-year history of gradual enlargement following prior knee effusion related to osteoarthritis. No acute traumatic event preceding cyst appearance. Bilateral mild knee osteoarthritis evident on imaging. Intermittent knee swelling management with activity modification and NSAIDs.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, a palpable firm mass noted in the popliteal fossa, best appreciated with knee extended. No warmth or erythema overlying the mass. Popliteal mass transilluminates, suggesting cystic fluid-filled nature. Knee effusion palpable. Active flexion and extension pain-free without mechanical restriction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound confirms anechoic fluid collection in popliteal fossa measuring 3.5 cm with no internal echoes. Direct communication demonstrated between cyst and knee joint capsule, confirming synovial origin. MRI shows T2 hyperintense cyst with intact walls, no hemorrhage. Associated knee joint effusion and mild degenerative changes noted.

puzzles.explanation: A Baker's cyst is a benign synovial outpouching arising from the posterior knee joint capsule, typically communicating directly with the joint space through a one-way valve mechanism. It forms secondary to chronic knee pathology — in this case, osteoarthritis with persistent effusion — as synovial fluid accumulates in the popliteal fossa behind the knee. The painless, transilluminable mass that enlarges with knee extension and activity (as the joint effusion pressurizes) is pathognomonic; it characteristically reduces with flexion and rest when intra-articular pressure decreases. The bilateral mild osteoarthritis and two-year gradual progression reflect the chronic mechanical irritation that drives cyst formation, distinguishing it from acute popliteal pathology and making conservative management of the underlying joint disease the primary treatment strategy.

puzzles.answer: Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old with insidious onset groin pain over 3 months. Pain is deep, progressive, and worse with weight-bearing and certain hip movements. No history of trauma. Symptoms gradually limiting walking distance and function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with stairs, prolonged standing, and transitioning from sitting to standing. Pain worsens with hip flexion beyond 90 degrees and internal rotation. Pain eases with rest and hip abduction positioning. Limp noted when walking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 2-year history of corticosteroid use for chronic respiratory disease. Past history of excessive alcohol consumption. Combined risk factors for bone vascular compromise. Symptoms progressive over 3 months without prior hip injury or infection.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion limited to 90 degrees with pain. Internal rotation severely restricted to 10–15 degrees. Hip abductor and flexor strength 4/5, pain-limited. FABER test positive for groin pain. External rotation and hip extension relatively preserved. No acute swelling or warmth.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows irregular hypodense lesion at femoral head with crescent sign and subchondral collapse. Central marrow signal abnormality with surrounding edema. X-ray confirms flattening of femoral head contour and mixed lucent/sclerotic changes consistent with Stage 3 disease.

puzzles.explanation: Avascular necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head occurs when interruption of blood supply causes death of bone marrow and osteocytes, typically in the weight-bearing anterolateral portion of the femoral head. Corticosteroid use and alcohol abuse are two of the most common risk factors, both capable of triggering vascular compromise through different mechanisms — corticosteroids via fat embolism and increased marrow pressure, alcohol through direct endothelial injury. The insidious onset with progressive groin pain worsening with flexion and internal rotation, combined with the characteristic crescent sign and subchondral collapse on imaging, marks Stage 3 disease where mechanical failure of the femoral head is already underway. Early recognition and surgical intervention (core decompression or vascularized grafting) offer the best chance to halt progression before total hip arthroplasty becomes necessary.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old office worker presents with a 3-month history of dull, aching pain localised to the upper trapezius and levator scapulae bilaterally. Pain reproduces with palpation of specific tender points in the muscle belly but not with cervical spine movement testing. No history of trauma or significant injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting at a desk with poor posture, particularly with forward head positioning. Neck rotation and lateral flexion toward the affected side reproduce pain. Symptoms ease with heat application, massage, and neck stretching. The patient notes morning stiffness lasting 15-20 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient attributes symptom onset to recent intensive project work with increased stress. Reports previous episodes of neck pain that resolved with rest and therapy 2-3 years ago. No significant medical history, no rheumatological or autoimmune conditions. Current sleep disrupted; wakes with stiffness that improves with movement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, firm myofascial trigger points are identified in the upper trapezius that reproduce referred pain to the posterior head and temporal region when pressed. Active cervical range of motion is within normal limits but reproduces local muscle tenderness at end-ranges. The Levine test for thoracic outlet syndrome is negative, ruling out neural involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine unremarkable for degenerative changes, disc herniation, or neural compression. Ultrasound of trapezius: normal muscle echotexture, no focal lesion, normal vascularity. Normal findings support clinical diagnosis of primary muscle pain disorder without structural substrate.

puzzles.explanation: Myofascial pain syndrome results from persistent muscle tension and the development of trigger points — hypersensitive nodules within the muscle belly that maintain a self-perpetuating cycle of contraction and ischaemia. Unlike cervical radiculopathy or structural pathology, it presents as localised, reproducible tenderness without neurological signs or imaging abnormalities, as seen in this patient's normal imaging and negative thoracic outlet testing. The bilateral upper trapezius involvement triggered by sustained poor posture and psychological stress is classic; the referred pain to the posterior head and temporal region reflects the convergence of nociceptive input from the muscle trigger point, not dermatomal distribution. Response to manual therapy, heat, and movement — combined with the absence of morning stiffness that improves with activity, distinguishing it from inflammatory arthropathy — confirms the mechanical, muscle-based nature of this condition, making management focused on postural correction, stress reduction, and trigger point deactivation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old carpenter presents with a 2-month history of inner elbow pain that developed insidiously without trauma. Pain worsens with gripping and repetitive hand activities. No prior similar episodes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot sustain grip-based activities or perform forceful wrist flexion. Pain is particularly triggered by combined wrist flexion with pronation movements. Symptoms improve with activity modification and rest, but return with repetitive gripping tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works as a carpenter and reports recent increase in overhead work and repetitive gripping tasks over the past 3 months. There is no history of direct trauma to the elbow. The patient has not sought prior treatment for musculoskeletal conditions. Symptoms began gradually after intensive project work in winter months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, tenderness is maximal directly over the medial epicondyle. The Golfer's elbow test, performed with wrist flexion against resistance, reproduces medial elbow pain. Wrist flexor strength is diminished at 4/5 compared to the contralateral side. Elbow flexion and extension are preserved, but pronation with wrist flexion is limited by pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the medial elbow demonstrates heterogeneous echotexture and thickening of the flexor carpi radialis and palmaris longus tendon origins. There is mild hypoechoic change within the tendons consistent with tendinopathy. Power Doppler imaging shows increased vascularity at the medial epicondyle consistent with chronic inflammatory response.

puzzles.explanation: Medial epicondylitis, or golfer's elbow, results from overuse-related tendinopathy of the wrist flexor and pronator muscle group originating at the medial epicondyle, typically from repetitive gripping, wrist flexion, and pronation activities. The condition develops insidiously through cumulative microtrauma rather than acute injury, making it common in trades requiring sustained grip strength like carpentry. The characteristic presentation—pain localized to the medial epicondyle that reproduces with resisted wrist flexion, combined with sonographic evidence of tendon thickening and increased vascularity—reflects the chronic inflammatory and degenerative process at the musculotendinous junction. Unlike lateral epicondylitis, medial epicondylitis specifically impairs wrist flexor function and is exacerbated by the combination of flexion with pronation, which maximally loads the affected flexor-pronator group.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old woman. Shoulder pain began 4 months ago, gradual onset, no clear injury. Deep ache localized to the joint itself. Pain worse at night, disrupting sleep. Constant quality but varies throughout the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot reach behind back to fasten bra or tuck in shirt. Overhead activities limited—difficulty washing hair or reaching high shelves. Pain with these movements but worse with the stiffness itself. Minimal relief from rest or activity modification.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mild shoulder strain 6 months ago from awkward lifting. Immobilized in sling for 3 weeks on medical advice. After removing sling, stiffness and pain progressively worsened despite no further injury. No diabetes, no inflammatory disease, no previous shoulder problems. No radiation of pain down arm.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive external rotation at side markedly restricted (15°, normally 80°). Passive abduction restricted (60°, normally 180°). Forward flexion passive only to 110° (normally 180°). Active movement even more limited—patient cannot initiate most motions. Pain at end-range passively but significant apprehension. Rotator cuff strength intact where motion allows testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MR arthrography shows marked reduction in glenohumeral joint volume. Thickened, contracted joint capsule visible on axial and coronal views with patchy T2 edema. Rotator cuff muscles show signal change consistent with disuse but no tears or tendon pathology. Labrum intact. Findings consistent with capsular fibrosis and inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) is a self-limiting inflammatory condition characterized by progressive fibrosis and contracture of the glenohumeral joint capsule, typically triggered by immobilization, minor trauma, or systemic factors like diabetes or thyroid disease. The condition progresses through three clinical phases: initial inflammation with pain, intermediate freezing with progressive stiffness and loss of both active and passive motion, and eventual thawing with gradual recovery over 12-24 months. This patient's presentation is classic—the insidious worsening following brief immobilization, the marked restriction in external rotation and abduction disproportionate to true muscle weakness, the nocturnal pain disrupting sleep, and the imaging findings of capsular thickening with preserved rotator cuff integrity all hallmark the condition. Early recognition and aggressive active-assisted range-of-work within pain tolerance, rather than prolonged immobilization, are critical to preventing the severe functional limitations that characterize the freezing phase.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 78-year-old woman presents with generalized muscle weakness and loss of functional strength affecting multiple muscle groups. The patient reports progressive difficulty with ambulation and basic activities of daily living over the past 2 years. The insidious onset and diffuse distribution could suggest sarcopenia, myositis, neurological disease, or systemic metabolic derangement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty rising from a chair without upper extremity support, climbing stairs, and walking long distances. Standing balance is compromised, and the patient has experienced several near-falls. Fine motor tasks such as opening jars and buttoning buttons are increasingly difficult. The patient describes feelings of fatigue that limit daily participation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Weakness is generalized and affects multiple muscle groups symmetrically. No recent significant illness or immobilization. Dietary intake adequate but reports reduced physical activity over past 5 years due to knee osteoarthritis. No weight loss beyond age-appropriate changes. No respiratory, cardiac, or endocrine symptoms reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, grip strength is measured at 18 kilograms bilaterally, which is below the 10th percentile for age and sex. Knee extension strength is 3+/5 bilaterally, and hip flexor strength is 3/5. Gait speed is reduced at 0.8 meters per second. The Short Physical Performance Battery score is 4, indicating high fall risk and poor functional mobility.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan shows low muscle mass with appendicular skeletal muscle index of 5.8 kilograms per meter squared, below the threshold for sarcopenia diagnosis. Computed tomography of the thigh shows marked intramuscular fat infiltration and reduced cross-sectional muscle area. MRI confirms widespread myosteatosis and atrophy of bilateral quadriceps and hip flexor muscles.

puzzles.explanation: Sarcopenia is an age-related syndrome characterized by progressive loss of skeletal muscle mass, strength, and function, typically beginning around age 70 and accelerating with physical inactivity. The condition reflects a combination of mechanisms: reduced protein synthesis, increased protein catabolism, motor neuron loss, and accumulation of intramuscular fat that further impairs contractile function—evident here in the marked myosteatosis and reduced muscle cross-sectional area. The reduced gait speed, poor Short Physical Performance Battery score, and profound weakness in hip and knee extensors despite adequate nutrition illustrate how sarcopenia primarily compromises the antigravity muscles essential for mobility and fall prevention, rather than presenting as selective focal weakness. Diagnosis requires demonstration of low muscle mass (confirmed by the low appendicular skeletal muscle index) combined with documented weakness or poor physical performance, making this presentation a textbook example of primary age-related sarcopenia worsened by the preceding years of reduced physical activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 70-year-old woman with established osteoarthritis presents with chronic bilateral lower limb pain, heaviness, and progressive functional decline over 2 years. She reports walking has become increasingly difficult, and she experiences numbness in both feet that worsens with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Standing still is worse than walking, and walking backwards or with hip flexion (leaning on a walker) improves symptoms. The patient reports increased pain during extension-based movements and relief with flexion-based positioning. She has reduced daily activity significantly and requires assistive devices.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known history of lumbar spondylosis diagnosed 10 years ago. Patient reports no recent trauma, falls, or acute exacerbations. She denies incontinence, retention, or bowel changes. She is on treatment for hypertension but is otherwise systemically well.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral lower extremity strength is 4+/5 in hip flexors and ankle dorsiflexors. Patellar reflexes are diminished bilaterally (1+). Sensory testing reveals decreased sensation in both feet in an L5-S1 distribution. The lumbar spine shows marked stiffness with minimal forward bending capability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT imaging of the lumbar spine demonstrates severe multilevel degenerative stenosis involving L3-L4, L4-L5, and L5-S1 levels. There is marked narrowing of the spinal canal, lateral recess stenosis, and facet-mediated stenosis. Disc bulging and osteophyte formation are prominent contributors.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spinal stenosis is progressive narrowing of the spinal canal due to degenerative changes—disc bulging, osteophyte formation, and facet hypertrophy—that compress nerve roots and compromise blood flow to the cauda equina. The characteristic presentation of neurogenic claudication—bilateral lower limb heaviness and pain worse with standing and lumbar extension, but improved with flexion and walking with hip bent forward—reflects the dynamic nature of canal compromise; flexion widens the canal, extending relief. The L5-S1 distribution of sensory loss and diminished ankle reflexes in this long-standing spondylosis case, combined with multilevel stenosis on imaging, explains both the widespread bilateral symptoms and the functional decline requiring assistive devices. This condition demands careful monitoring for cauda equina syndrome (bowel/bladder changes, bilateral leg weakness) and consideration of surgical intervention if conservative management fails.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 71-year-old male with sudden-onset dizziness, vertigo, and nausea. Reports facial numbness on one side and double vision. Symptoms began this morning without warning. PMHx: hypertension (poorly controlled), diabetes, prior TIA 5 years ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to stand or walk without support. Head movements trigger nausea. Difficulty with fine motor coordination on one side of body. Drooping of mouth corner and facial asymmetry on one side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden onset this morning. No recent head trauma, infection, or illness. Previous TIA event 5 years ago. Currently on aspirin only; no other antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy. Vascular risk factors: longstanding hypertension and diabetes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left facial weakness with mouth droop. Horizontal nystagmus on rightward gaze. Poor rapid alternating movements (dysdiadochokinesia) on right side. Significant imbalance on Romberg test. Left-sided miosis and ptosis. Cranial nerves II-XII otherwise grossly intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain with DWI shows acute restricted diffusion in left pontine tegmentum (dorsomedial region), approximately 8 × 6 mm. FLAIR sequences confirm acute ischemic pattern. MRA intracranial shows patent major vessels without significant stenosis or occlusion.

puzzles.explanation: A brainstem stroke results from sudden interruption of blood supply to the midbrain, pons, or medulla, causing acute neurological deficits that reflect the compact organization of critical motor, sensory, and cranial nerve pathways in this region. This patient's acute pontine infarction in the tegmentum affected multiple crossing tracts and nuclei: the corticospinal tract (causing contralateral facial weakness), the dorsal motor nucleus of CN VII (ipsilateral mouth droop), the MLF and oculomotor pathways (horizontal nystagmus and double vision), and cerebellar connections (dysdiadochokinesia and ataxia). The constellation of ipsilateral cranial nerve signs with contralateral motor findings, combined with the sudden onset in a patient with poorly controlled vascular risk factors and prior TIA, is pathognomonic for brainstem stroke and demands urgent imaging and acute intervention to minimize brainstem tissue loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old recreational runner presents with a 3-month history of gradual-onset medial ankle pain. The dull ache worsens with activity and improves with rest. No recent ankle injury recalled.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking, stair climbing, and running aggravate pain. Patient reports difficulty maintaining arch support during prolonged standing. Arch support and rest provide relief. Jumping provokes sharp medial ankle pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient is a recreational runner who recently increased training volume by 30% over the past 2 months without adequate progressive loading. There is no history of ankle trauma or prior ankle instability. The patient notes that symptoms correlate with increased running mileage. Body mass index is 28, contributing to increased lower extremity loading during activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited along the posterior tibial tendon approximately 4 centimeters proximal to the medial malleolus. The single-leg heel raise test reveals mild difficulty with heel elevation on the affected side, with ability to complete 10 repetitions compared to 15 on the unaffected side. The arch shows subtle flattening with weight-bearing compared to the contralateral foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the posterior tibial tendon demonstrates mild hypoechoic signal within the tendon substance, suggesting early tendinopathy without significant fiber disruption. The tendon measures 6.2 millimeters in anteroposterior diameter, mildly enlarged compared to contralateral side at 5.8 millimeters. Power Doppler shows mild increased vascularity at the tendon surface consistent with early inflammatory response.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior tibial tendon dysfunction (PTTD) results from progressive degeneration and inflammation of the tendon that supports the medial longitudinal arch of the foot, typically driven by overuse, increased loading, or age-related changes. Grade 1 PTTD is characterized by tendinopathy without structural failure—the tendon remains intact but demonstrates edema, hypoechoic signal, and early inflammatory changes on imaging, as reflected in the mild enlargement and increased vascularity seen here. The condition classically manifests with medial ankle pain along the tendon course that worsens with arch-demanding activities like running and stair climbing, while single-leg heel raise strength begins to decline due to pain inhibition rather than complete loss of function. The gradual-onset presentation following training volume increase, combined with subtle arch flattening and difficulty sustaining heel elevation, are hallmark findings that distinguish early PTTD from acute ankle sprains and other medial-sided pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old runner presents with lateral knee pain that develops during running activities. The pain is localized to the lateral knee joint line and describes a sharp, stabbing quality. The 4-week onset during increased training volume could represent iliotibial band syndrome, lateral meniscal tear, lateral ligament sprain, or patellofemoral pain syndrome.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is reproducibly aggravated when running more than 3 kilometers, particularly with downhill running. The patient reports pain with knee flexion between 20-30 degrees during the running gait cycle. Pain is eased with rest, ice, and foam rolling of the lateral thigh. Walking at normal pace does not provoke symptoms, but stairs require modification.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient increased running volume from 15 kilometers per week to 35 kilometers per week over 4 weeks while training for a marathon. A recent change in running surface to concrete from park trails preceded symptom onset. The patient reports running in worn-out shoes with poor cushioning. No history of prior knee injury or lower extremity biomechanical dysfunction.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The Ober test reveals hip adduction limited to 15 degrees on the affected side compared to 25 degrees contralaterally, indicating iliotibial band tightness. Hip abductor strength is 4/5 on the affected side versus 5/5 on the contralateral side. Noble compression test is positive. Knee effusion and ligamentous laxity are absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates thickening of the iliotibial band measuring 4 millimeters compared to normal 2-3 millimeters. T2-weighted imaging shows edema adjacent to the lateral femoral condyle at the region of band friction. No meniscal tears, ligamentous injuries, or cartilage defects are identified. The vastus lateralis muscle shows normal signal intensity without fatty changes.

puzzles.explanation: Iliotibial band syndrome results from repetitive friction of the thickened ITB against the lateral femoral condyle during knee flexion-extension cycles, particularly exacerbated by increased training volume and biomechanical stressors. The condition classically presents with lateral knee pain triggered during running — especially on downhill terrain and at the 20-30° flexion angle where friction peaks — while walking remains pain-free due to reduced loading and less dynamic knee motion. The tight ITB with weak hip abductors seen on examination reflects the underlying dysfunction: insufficient glute medius strength allows dynamic hip adduction during the stance phase, increasing lateral knee stress and ITB friction. The MRI findings of band thickening and lateral femoral condyle edema confirm the chronic inflammatory response to repetitive microtrauma, and the rapid response to foam rolling reflects the mechanical nature of the problem — tissue restriction rather than structural damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 82-year-old woman. Sudden onset severe hip pain after a fall from standing height at home. Unable to bear weight on affected leg. Pain is constant and not relieved by position changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk or stand. Limb held in external rotation and flexion at rest. Any attempt to move the hip—especially internal rotation, adduction, or weight-bearing—causes severe sharp pain. Requires maximum assistance for transfers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known osteoporosis for 15 years with suboptimal treatment adherence. Low physical activity level. Fall was low-energy: simple loss of balance during bed transfer at home with no direct blow to the hip. Became immobilized for 2 hours post-fall.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Affected limb rests in external rotation (~45°) and slight flexion. Visible limb length discrepancy: affected side approximately 2 cm shorter. Palpable crepitus at hip. Severe pain on any passive movement. Lower extremity pulses and sensory function intact. No skin compromise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic X-rays show fracture line extending from greater trochanter to lesser trochanter with comminution. Fracture involves medial cortex—classified as unstable pattern. Radiographs confirm osteoporotic bone quality. CT confirms no hip dislocation; documents fracture displacement and geometry.

puzzles.explanation: An intertrochanteric fracture results from a break through the hip between the greater and lesser trochanters, typically occurring in elderly patients with osteoporosis after low-energy falls—the weakened bone simply cannot withstand the shear and compressive forces across this region. The characteristic presentation of external rotation and flexion at rest, limb shortening, and severe pain with any hip movement reflects both the fracture displacement and the muscle forces (external rotators and hip flexors) acting unopposed across the unstable break. Comminution and involvement of the medial cortex, as seen on imaging, define this as an unstable pattern with high risk of malunion and non-union if not surgically stabilized. The intact neurovascular examination is reassuring, but early operative fixation is critical—prolonged immobility in this age group rapidly leads to deconditioning, pressure injuries, and systemic complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 31-year-old woman. Onset 2 years ago of pain and difficulty with vaginal penetration. Describes involuntary tightening that prevents entry despite wanting intercourse to proceed. No visible trauma, bleeding, or discharge.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate vaginal penetration during intercourse. Pain occurs with penetration attempts. Also reports difficulty during gynecological examination. States tightening is involuntary and beyond her control. No pain with other sexual activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No history of sexual trauma, assault, or abuse. Denies prior gynecological surgeries. No hormonal contraceptive use. Menarche age 13, regular cycles, no endometriosis or pelvic surgery history. Good overall health. Symptoms began gradually without identifiable triggering event or infection.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vulva and introitus visually normal—no lesions, redness, or atrophy. Attempt to insert single digit at introitus elicits marked involuntary circumvaginal muscle contraction, preventing insertion. Pelvic floor muscles palpably hypertonic and guarded. No tenderness on external palpation. Internal exam cannot be completed due to protective muscle guarding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Transperineal ultrasound shows increased thickness and echogenicity of bulbocavernosus and superficial transverse perineal muscles, indicating chronic contraction. Pelvic MRI normal—no endometriosis, fibroids, anatomical variants, or structural pathology explaining the penetration difficulty.

puzzles.explanation: Vaginismus is a persistent involuntary contraction of the pelvic floor muscles, particularly the bulbocavernosus and superficial transverse perineal muscles, triggered by attempted or anticipated vaginal penetration. It occurs without structural pathology, organic disease, or identifiable trauma, distinguishing it from pain conditions like dyspareunia or vulvodynia — the normal pelvic anatomy and imaging findings here are diagnostic hallmarks. The condition reflects a protective neuromuscular reflex, often psychogenic in origin, where anticipatory anxiety creates a guarding response that becomes habitual and self-perpetuating. Conservative management focuses on desensitization through progressive vaginal dilators, pelvic floor relaxation training, and addressing any underlying relationship or psychological factors, with physiotherapy-led pelvic floor rehabilitation forming the cornerstone of treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old football player presents with acute-onset severe pain over the anterolateral hip following direct shoulder-to-hip contact during a tackle. Pain is localized and well-demarcated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient is unable to return to play and has severe limitation with hip flexion and trunk movements. Walking is difficult with a pronounced limp, and the patient favors the unaffected side. Pain is exacerbated by activities requiring hip flexion and lateral trunk flexion away from the injury. Running and kicking are impossible due to pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mechanism: direct shoulder-to-hip contact during tackle. No prior hip injuries. No history of bone fragility or medical conditions affecting bone quality.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, significant tenderness and swelling are present over the iliac crest, particularly at the attachment site of the abdominal musculature. Ecchymosis is developing over the area. Hip flexion produces pain at the iliac attachment and anterior hip region. The anterior superior iliac spine and anterior inferior iliac spine are palpated and found to be intact without step-off or displacement. Hip strength testing is limited by pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the pelvis show no evidence of fracture through the iliac crest or acetabulum. CT imaging of the pelvis reveals soft tissue swelling over the anterolateral hip with blood products within the muscles at the iliac attachment. No occult fracture is identified. Bone integrity is maintained with normal cortical thickness.

puzzles.explanation: A hip pointer is a contusion of the iliac crest and adjacent soft tissues—typically the abdominal musculature and tensor fasciae latae—resulting from blunt direct trauma, commonly seen in contact sports. The mechanism of direct shoulder-to-hip impact causes hemorrhage and inflammation at the muscle attachments to the iliac crest, producing the characteristic severe, well-demarcated pain and functional limitation with hip flexion and trunk movements. Imaging confirms the diagnosis by demonstrating soft tissue swelling and blood products without fracture, distinguishing it from more serious iliac crest fractures or avulsion injuries. Recovery relies on pain management, activity modification, and graduated strengthening, though swelling and ecchymosis can persist for weeks due to the rich vascularization of the region.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational soccer player presents with acute-onset lateral knee pain after a twisting injury to the lower limb. The patient felt the knee 'give way' during the mechanism and experienced immediate, severe pain. Onset is acute, mechanism is non-contact.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient is severely limited with weight-bearing and has a sense of instability with any dynamic movement or single-leg stance. Immediate swelling suggests significant ligamentous or meniscal involvement. Pain is lateral and worsens with any rotational or cutting movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a prior history of an ankle sprain on the contralateral side 6 months ago. There is no prior knee injury. The soccer injury occurred when the patient landed on the outside of the foot with the knee in slight flexion, forcing the knee into inversion and external rotation. The patient continued playing briefly before severe pain forced him to stop.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited along the lateral collateral ligament from the fibular head distally. The varus stress test at 30 degrees of knee flexion shows increased laxity compared to the unaffected side, graded as 1+ opening. The anterior drawer test is negative, and Lachman's test is negative, making anterior cruciate ligament tear less likely. Mild joint effusion is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates a partial tear of the lateral collateral ligament with interruption of approximately 50% of the fiber bundle at the level of the fibular head. T2-weighted imaging shows edema and hemorrhage within and around the ligament. The anterior cruciate ligament and menisci appear intact. Soft tissue swelling is present in the lateral knee compartment.

puzzles.explanation: Lateral collateral ligament (LCL) sprains result from excessive varus stress or inversion-external rotation forces applied to a flexed knee, disrupting the fibrous bands that stabilize the lateral knee compartment. This injury is particularly common in cutting and pivoting sports like soccer, where rapid directional changes create combined rotational and valgus loads. The acute onset with immediate instability and the characteristic mechanism—landing on the outside of the foot with the knee slightly flexed—are hallmark presentations that distinguish LCL injuries from other knee ligamentous injuries. A positive varus stress test at 30° flexion combined with MRI evidence of partial fiber disruption confirms the diagnosis; the intact ACL and negative Lachman's test help rule out more complex multiligamentous injuries that would require different management approaches.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A neonate with bilateral foot deformity noted at birth. Parent reports inability to passively correct foot position to neutral. Family history significant for similar presentation in maternal grandfather.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Feeding and sleeping unaffected. Able to move hips and knees freely. Parents concerned about future weight-bearing and whether foot position will prevent normal gait development.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Full-term vaginal delivery at 39 weeks. No maternal infections, medications, or diabetes during pregnancy. No other congenital anomalies. Foot deformity noted immediately at birth by delivery team.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, bilateral feet demonstrate equinus deformity with inversion and adduction of the forefoot. Pirani score measures 6/6, indicating moderate deformity. Passive dorsiflexion limited to 5 degrees bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anteroposterior and lateral foot radiographs demonstrate talar-navicular uncovering. Talocalcaneal angle measures 15 degrees (normal >20 degrees). Tibiocalcaneal angle shows equinus positioning of 45 degrees.

puzzles.explanation: Clubfoot, or talipes equinovarus, is a congenital deformity resulting from abnormal development of the foot and ankle structures, with a strong genetic component — the paternal family history here is typical of this inheritance pattern. The condition is characterized by a fixed equinus position (plantarflexion), inversion of the heel, and adduction of the forefoot, creating the classic 'club' appearance that cannot be passively corrected to neutral. The bilateral presentation with moderate severity (Pirani score 6/6) and imaging findings of talar-navicular uncovering and reduced talocalcaneal angle are characteristic, reflecting the underlying structural malalignment of the tarsal bones. Early diagnosis at birth, as in this case, allows for prompt initiation of the Ponseti method or similar conservative casting protocols, which offer excellent outcomes for achieving functional, plantigrade feet and preventing the gait disturbances the parents rightfully anticipated.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old recreational runner reports gradual onset of medial foot and ankle pain over 6 weeks. Pain worse with morning stiffness and gradually improves with activity. Denies prior significant ankle injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain increases significantly after running, particularly when going uphill. Walking on uneven surfaces elicits discomfort. Foot appears to pronate excessively during gait, and patient reports difficulty with single-leg stance activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient recently increased running frequency from 3 to 5 days weekly. History of mild pes planus bilaterally. Takes no anti-inflammatory medications. No history of systemic inflammatory conditions or previous ankle surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, point tenderness elicited along the posterior tibial tendon just posterior and inferior to the medial malleolus. Single-leg heel raise test demonstrates weakness and inability to maintain inversion at midfoot. Mild swelling noted in the medial ankle region with positive tenosynovitis sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging reveals hypoechoic thickening of the posterior tibial tendon measuring 8mm in cross-sectional diameter (normal <5mm). Fluid collection evident within the tendon sheath. Power Doppler demonstrates increased neovascularization within and around the tendon.

puzzles.explanation: Tibialis posterior tendinopathy results from chronic overload of the posterior tibial tendon, which is responsible for dynamic arch support and inversion during gait. In this case, the combination of increased running volume, underlying pes planus, and excessive midfoot pronation created cumulative stress on a tendon already working overtime to stabilize the medial arch. The characteristic presentation—morning stiffness that improves with activity, difficulty with single-leg heel raises, and weakness of inversion—reflects the tendon's critical role in arch control; ultrasound findings of tendon thickening, fluid in the sheath, and neovascularization confirm the chronic inflammatory response and failed healing attempt. Early recognition is crucial, as progressive dysfunction can lead to dynamic flatfoot deformity and secondary midfoot osteoarthritis if left untreated.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old accountant reports gradual progressive imbalance and unsteady gait over 2 years. Wife notes increased stumbling and incoordination affecting upper and lower limbs. Patient reports trouble with fine motor tasks such as writing and buttoning clothes. No weakness or sensory symptoms reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports unsteady gait that worsens on uneven terrain or in dim lighting. Requires rail support for stairs. Reaches for objects but overshoots the target consistently.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began insidiously without preceding infection or trauma. No significant past medical history. Father had similar symptoms starting in late 40s. Patient denies alcohol use. No medications with neurotoxic potential. Denies sensory symptoms or weakness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tandem stance maintained for only 2 seconds with eyes open, impossible with eyes closed. Finger-nose testing demonstrates consistent overshooting bilaterally. Rapid alternating hand pronation-supination becomes irregular and slows after 2-3 cycles.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI shows selective atrophy of the cerebellum, particularly the midline vermis. Scattered white matter signal changes in the deep periventricular regions. Genetic testing reveals a pathogenic trinucleotide repeat expansion in the SCA region.

puzzles.explanation: Ataxia is a disorder of motor coordination arising from cerebellar dysfunction or its connections, characterized by dysmetria (inaccurate limb movement), dysdiadochokinesia (irregular rapid alternating movements), and balance impairment—without accompanying weakness or sensory loss. Spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs) are a family of inherited neurodegenerative conditions caused by trinucleotide repeat expansions, typically presenting with progressive gait unsteadiness, incoordination of limbs, and dysarthria that worsen over years. The selective vermian atrophy seen on MRI, combined with the patient's insidious bilateral symptoms, consistent overshooting on finger-nose testing, and positive family history with late-adult onset, are hallmarks of SCA. The absence of weakness or sensory findings helps distinguish ataxia from other causes of imbalance, making the cerebellar origin clinically clear.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman with recurrent episodes of severe vertigo lasting 20 minutes to 3 hours. Associated with left-sided hearing loss, tinnitus described as low-frequency roaring, and left ear fullness sensation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Episodes are completely unpredictable and incapacitating—patient must lie down during attacks. Severe nausea and vomiting accompany most episodes. Avoids driving due to fear of attacks. Between episodes, reports only mild imbalance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: First episode 18 months ago. Frequency has remained stable at 2-3 attacks monthly despite rigorous sodium restriction and diuretic trial over 6 months. No recent head trauma, no ototoxic medications, no prior vertigo before this illness began.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: At rest, no spontaneous nystagmus. Dix-Hallpike and Romberg maneuvers normal. Caloric irrigation shows reduced ice water response on left (40% reduction compared to right). Weber test: sound lateralizes to left ear (bone conduction intact). Audiometry shows low-frequency air-bone gap on left side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Gadolinium-enhanced MRI demonstrates enhancement of the left endolymphatic sac and duct, consistent with endolymphatic hydrops. High-resolution imaging shows increased endolymphatic volume occupying >50% of the cochlear and vestibular spaces. Pure-tone audiometry shows low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Menière's disease is a primary disorder of the inner ear characterized by excessive accumulation of endolymph (endolymphatic hydrops), causing recurrent episodes of severe vertigo, fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness. The unpredictable, incapacitating vertigo attacks lasting 20 minutes to 3 hours—accompanied by severe nausea and vomiting—are the hallmark presentation, resulting from sudden increases in endolymphatic pressure that distort the vestibular and cochlear structures. The low-frequency hearing loss and low-frequency roaring tinnitus are distinctive features reflecting cochlear hydrops in the apical turns, and the reduced caloric response on the affected side confirms vestibular involvement. While sodium restriction and diuretics are first-line medical management, many patients remain refractory to medical therapy; the gadolinium-enhanced MRI evidence of endolymphatic hydrops occupying over 50% of the cochlear and vestibular spaces confirms the diagnosis in this unresponsive case.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 56-year-old recreational athlete reports sudden-onset severe calf pain during activity, accompanied by an audible 'pop'. Rapid onset of swelling and inability to bear weight on the affected leg.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to plantar flex foot or heel walk. Attempted jogging elicits severe calf pain. Swelling progressed over 24 hours with prominent bruising visible on the medial ankle and foot sole. Calf tightness severely restricts ankle dorsiflexion range.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recreational athlete with no prior calf problems. No warning signs before injury. No relevant medical history or current medications. Relatively infrequent training schedule (twice weekly).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpable defect detected in the gastrocnemius-soleus complex at the musculotendinous junction. Positive Thompson test with absent plantar flexion response on calf squeeze. Severe pain on passive ankle dorsiflexion. Swelling and bruising extending to medial ankle with ecchymosis tracking down to sole.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates complete tear of the medial gastrocnemius muscle with muscle fiber retraction. Hemorrhagic collection noted within the muscular compartment measuring 4 x 6 cm. MRI confirms soleus muscle involvement with T2 hyperintense edema throughout the muscle belly and proximal musculotendinous junction.

puzzles.explanation: Tennis leg is an acute rupture of the medial gastrocnemius muscle, typically at the musculotendinous junction, occurring during sudden plantarflexion or dorsiflexion loading in middle-aged recreational athletes. The characteristic audible 'pop' followed by sudden severe pain, rapid swelling, and the palpable defect reflect the acute muscular disruption and hemorrhagic extravasation into the compartment. A positive Thompson test — absent plantarflexion response on calf squeeze — confirms the rupture by demonstrating loss of functional continuity in the triceps surae complex. While often self-limiting with conservative management (early immobilization, ice, compression), complete tears like those shown on ultrasound with significant fiber retraction require careful monitoring for compartment syndrome and prolonged rehabilitation to restore strength and plantarflexion capacity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old soccer player with chronic ankle pain that developed gradually over 8 months. Denies recent injury, though had minor ankle sprains several years ago. Pain is constant but varies with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Kicking activities consistently trigger sharp anterior ankle pain. Pain worsens with plantarflexion combined with inversion. Walking on uneven terrain elicits discomfort. Stiffness occurs after periods of inactivity, gradually improving with gentle movement. Running speed significantly affected.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-level athlete with progressive symptoms and no single injury event. Training volume is substantial (3-4 sessions weekly). Has not undergone ankle surgery previously. No systemic disease or inflammatory indicators reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpable tenderness at the anterior tibiofibular interval. Plantarflexion-inversion combined movement reproduces pain sharply. Anterior ankle drawer test negative. Anterior impingement test positive with pain reproduction. Ankle plantarflexion range 50 degrees with restriction felt anteriorly.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals anterior osteophyte formation at the talonavicular joint margin measuring 4mm. Hypertrophic soft tissue nodule present anterior to the capsule with T2 hyperintense signal. Anterior joint capsule demonstrates thickening and folding consistent with synovitis. Plain radiographs confirm subtle talonavicular joint space osteophytes.

puzzles.explanation: Ankle impingement syndrome develops when repetitive plantarflexion-inversion movements cause anterior ankle structures—bone, cartilage, synovial tissue, and capsule—to become pinched between the talus and tibia, typically at the anterior tibiofibular interval or talonavicular joint. In this athlete, the gradual onset without frank injury, combined with the imaging finding of marginal osteophytes and hypertrophic soft tissue, reflects cumulative microtrauma from high-volume training rather than acute ligamentous injury. The sharp pain on plantarflexion-inversion combined with positive anterior impingement testing and restricted plantarflexion range clinically isolates anterior structures. Long-term repetitive stress in soccer—particularly kicking and changing direction on uneven ground—perpetuates synovitis and osteophyte formation, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of stiffness and activity limitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 14-year-old female reports bilateral anterior knee pain for 4 months. Onset gradual without specific injury. Pain worsens through the day and after sports activities. Denies night pain or morning stiffness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered by running, jumping, and change of direction. Stair climbing (especially descending) significantly aggravates symptoms. Prolonged knee bending while sitting uncomfortable. Symptoms ease with rest and walking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began coinciding with start of competitive soccer season and increased training load 4 months ago. Marked growth spurt over past 18 months. No prior knee trauma, surgery, or morning symptoms. Currently trains 5 days per week.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip abductors weak (4/5 bilaterally). Single-leg squat demonstrates dynamic knee valgus collapse. Q-angle elevated at 16°. Patellar tilt positive. Tenderness over medial patellar facet. Vastus medialis obliquus activation diminished compared to vastus lateralis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI normal: no cartilage damage, meniscal pathology, or ligamentous injury. Patellofemoral joint space preserved. No bone marrow edema. Findings consistent with functional overload and neuromuscular imbalance without structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Patellofemoral pain syndrome in adolescents results from biomechanical overload of the patellofemoral joint due to neuromuscular imbalance, typically arising during periods of rapid growth and increased training demand. The classic presentation here—bilateral anterior knee pain triggered by dynamic activities like running and descending stairs, coupled with weak hip abductors and dynamic knee valgus—reflects inadequate proximal stability failing to control femoral internal rotation and adduction during loaded knee flexion. The rapid onset coinciding with both growth spurt and competitive season, combined with normal imaging and preserved cartilage, is pathognomonic for this functional overload pattern rather than structural pathology. Treatment focuses on hip strengthening (particularly gluteus medius and maximus), VMO activation, and graduated return to sport, as the underlying tissues remain structurally intact.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 71-year-old male. Sudden onset severe dizziness and vertigo 12 hours ago. Patient reports room spinning, unable to stand safely, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. No head trauma reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk without assistance. Unsteady when sitting upright. Fine motor tasks markedly difficult—unable to feed himself or write. Symptoms present at rest and worsened with any movement. No focal weakness in limbs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began acutely without warning while at home. No recent illness, head injury, or falls. Past medical history: hypertension, type 2 diabetes. Takes metformin and amlodipine. No prior episodes of dizziness. No recent medication changes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Alert and oriented. Speech slightly slurred. Finger-nose test: marked inaccuracy bilaterally with overshoot and oscillation. Rapid alternating movements of hands clumsy and dysrhythmic. Gait: wide-based, unsteady, veering to one side. Romberg test: unable to maintain upright position even with eyes open. Horizontal nystagmus on rightward gaze. Normal motor strength and reflexes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain shows acute infarction in right cerebellar hemisphere with mild obstructive hydrocephalus. No brainstem involvement. CT angiography shows no vertebral artery dissection. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis normal.

puzzles.explanation: Cerebellar ataxia results from acute disruption of cerebellar function—in this case, from ischaemic stroke of the right cerebellar hemisphere—impairing the coordination and timing of voluntary movements. The cerebellum is essential for motor planning and proprioceptive feedback, so its damage produces the characteristic triad of dysmetria (inaccuracy with overshoot), dysdiadochokinesia (clumsy rapid alternating movements), and ataxic gait, without true weakness. The sudden onset and severe presentation here, with vertigo, nystagmus, and obstructive hydrocephalus from cerebellar swelling, reflect the acute ischaemic event; notably, preserved strength and reflexes confirm the motor pathways remain intact—the problem is purely coordination. This acute cerebellar syndrome demands urgent recognition because progressive oedema can lead to brainstem compression and requires close monitoring or surgical decompression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old recreational skier. Acute onset of knee pain 2 weeks ago during a fall on the slopes. Pain is localized to the inner side of the knee. Mild to moderate swelling present since injury. No mechanical symptoms (catching, locking). Pain present with walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain significantly worse with side-to-side movements and changes of direction. Difficulty with lateral weight shifts and pivoting activities. Walking downstairs more difficult than upstairs. Swelling increases with activity but improves with rest and elevation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury mechanism: valgus stress to planted foot with internal tibial rotation during fall. No prior knee injuries. No giving way or instability sensation. Mechanism suggests ligamentous rather than meniscal injury. Symptoms stable over 2 weeks—not progressively worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized tenderness along medial joint line and proximal medial ligament. No effusion. Full passive and active knee extension. Minimal pain with flexion. Lachman test and posterior drawer test negative. McMurray's test negative. Able to single-leg stand.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI: Grade II sprain of medial collateral ligament with partial fiber disruption. Minimal fluid signal in ligament substance. No meniscal tear. No anterior cruciate ligament involvement. Femoral condyle and tibial plateau intact. Findings consistent with incomplete ligamentous tear.

puzzles.explanation: Medial collateral ligament (MCL) sprains result from valgus stress with tibial internal rotation, disrupting the ligament's fibers that stabilize the knee against inward collapse. The MCL's extraarticular position and robust blood supply generally support favorable healing, which explains why this patient's Grade II partial tear remained stable over two weeks without progression to instability. The characteristic presentation here—localized medial joint line tenderness, pain with side-to-side movements and weight shifting, absence of mechanical symptoms, and negative tests for ACL and meniscal involvement—distinguishes MCL injury from intraarticular pathology. Most isolated MCL sprains respond well to conservative management with progressive loading, making early activity modification and gradual return to sport the mainstay of treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old post-operative patient (Day 2 following abdominal surgery) reports shallow breathing and reduced exercise tolerance. Complains of mild dyspnea on minimal exertion. Mild dry cough present but non-productive.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to ambulate more than 5 metres without dyspnea and desaturation. Splinting behavior evident during coughing. Baseline oxygen saturation 96% on room air at rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Surgical procedure was open partial colectomy under general anesthesia with 90-minute operative time. Pre-operative history significant for 40 pack-year smoking history, though quit 5 years ago. Mild COPD with baseline FEV1/FVC ratio 65%. Pain adequately controlled with multimodal analgesia including epidural catheter.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced breath sounds throughout right lower lobe with stony dullness to percussion. Respiratory rate elevated at 22 breaths per minute. Oxygen saturation 92% on room air. No clubbing, cyanosis, or peripheral edema. Chest wall pain on deep palpation limits voluntary deep inspiration.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates wedge-shaped radiodensity at right lung base with obscuration of right heart border. No mediastinal shift or pleural effusion. Remainder of lung fields clear. Findings consistent with lobar collapse.

puzzles.explanation: Atelectasis is collapse of alveoli due to loss of surfactant, obstruction of airways, or compression, and represents one of the most common pulmonary complications following general anesthesia and surgery. Post-operative atelectasis typically occurs in dependent lung regions — here the right lower lobe — and results from a combination of factors: prolonged supine positioning, reduced ventilation from pain-splinting, anesthetic-induced surfactant washout, and impaired mucociliary clearance. The wedge-shaped consolidation with lobar collapse on imaging, combined with reduced breath sounds, dullness to percussion, and the characteristic desaturation with minimal exertion, reflects the perfusion-ventilation mismatch created by non-ventilated but still-perfused lung tissue. Early mobilization, incentive spirometry, and adequate analgesia to permit deep inspiration are cornerstones of prevention and recovery in this patient.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 41-year-old woman reports episodic vertigo occurring 2-4 times monthly without clear triggers. Episodes last 30 minutes to 4 hours. Associated with frontal headache and motion sensitivity during attacks. Denies hearing loss or tinnitus.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During episodes, lying still in quiet dark room is essential. Visual motion and head movement exacerbate dizziness severely. Bright lights or screens worsen symptoms. Nausea accompanies most attacks though vomiting occurs in only 30%. Complete symptom resolution between episodes with normal balance and coordination.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Personal history of migraine headaches since age 18, typically responding to triptans. Mother and maternal grandmother both with migraines. Symptoms began 18 months ago, initially attributed to inner ear disease. Audiometric testing normal. Caloric irrigation normal bilaterally. No recent vestibular testing abnormalities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination during asymptomatic period, gait normal and balance unimpaired. Nystagmus not observed. Dix-Hallpike maneuver produces no vertigo or nystagmus. Weber and Rinne testing normal. Cranial nerves intact. Head impulse test normal. No spontaneous nystagmus on straight-ahead gaze.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI with and without gadolinium demonstrates no acute abnormality, demyelinating lesions, or structural pathology. White matter signal intensity normal. Cerebellopontine angle clear bilaterally. No evidence of temporal bone or inner ear abnormality. Findings support primary neurological headache disorder rather than vestibular or structural etiology.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular migraine is a primary headache disorder where transient vestibular dysfunction occurs as part of the migraine cascade, rather than from structural inner ear disease. The condition classically presents with episodic vertigo lasting minutes to hours, accompanied by photophobia, phonophobia, and motion intolerance — as seen in this patient's need for a dark, quiet room during attacks. The strong personal and family history of migraine, normal peripheral vestibular testing (caloric response, head impulse test, Dix-Hallpike), and unremarkable neuroimaging establish the diagnosis by ruling out central or otologic causes. Between episodes, patients show completely normal balance and coordination because the vestibular dysfunction is transient and related to the migraine pathophysiology rather than structural damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old teacher with a 3-month history of progressive medial ankle and arch discomfort. Pain develops gradually throughout the day and is particularly bothersome at end of workday. Denies acute injury or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged standing aggravates symptoms significantly. Stair climbing and walking downhill cause sharp pain along the medial foot. Patient notes arch feels weak and unstable, particularly on walking surfaces that lack support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occupation requires standing 6+ hours daily. History of chronic ankle sprains (5+ episodes over 15 years) with subsequent lateral ligament laxity. Early signs of hypertension managed with lifestyle modification. No diabetes or systemic inflammatory disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visible midfoot collapse and valgus heel positioning evident during standing. Positive too-many-toes sign observed on visual inspection. Posterior tibial strength graded 3+/5 with reproduction of pain during muscle testing. Medial ankle swelling present with tenderness along tendon course.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI imaging demonstrates edema and partial tearing within the mid-portion of the posterior tibial tendon. Tendon diameter measures 7.5mm with signal changes indicating intratendinous mucoid degeneration. Mild lateral ankle joint space narrowing suggesting chronic instability pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Tibialis posterior tendinopathy results from chronic overload and degenerative changes in the tendon that supports the medial arch and inverts the foot — a condition especially common in those with prolonged standing demands and prior lateral ankle instability. The tendon's progressive failure leads to loss of dynamic arch support, causing the characteristic midfoot collapse, valgus heel drift, and too-many-toes sign seen on standing, alongside medial-sided pain that worsens throughout the day. The combination of eccentric loading during stair descent and weight-bearing on unsupported surfaces drives symptom exacerbation, while MRI findings of mucoid degeneration and partial tearing reflect the underlying structural compromise. This presentation—functional weakness despite preserved neurological integrity, chronic ankle ligament laxity predisposing to altered biomechanics, and work-related volume loading—represents the classic pattern of posterior tibial tendon dysfunction progressing from inflammation toward insufficiency.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 8-year-old child presenting with lower limb weakness and reduced sensation in lower extremities. Parents report ongoing challenges with bladder and bowel control despite toilet training efforts.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Limited ambulation; child uses wheelchair for community distances but can walk short distances indoors with bilateral ankle-foot orthoses. Difficulty with stairs and uneven surfaces. Recurrent skin issues on buttocks and lower back.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Condition identified on prenatal ultrasound. Child underwent surgical intervention in infancy for closure of spinal defect. Occasional episodes of lower back pain. No significant head trauma, but parents report the child has required one prior abdominal surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Motor strength in hip flexion 4/5 bilaterally, knee extension 3/5, ankle dorsiflexion 2/5. Sensory testing intact to mid-thigh, diminished below knee. Reduced anal tone on digital rectal exam. Fixed flexion contractures noted at hips and knees. Palpable scar on lower back midline.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spinal MRI shows incomplete spinal cord development in lower thoracic/lumbar region with fatty infiltrate within spinal canal. Vertebral bodies show minor bony segmentation abnormality. No syrinx or tethering. Renal ultrasound demonstrates hydronephrosis bilaterally.

puzzles.explanation: Spina bifida is a neural tube defect occurring in early embryogenesis when the spinal cord and vertebral column fail to close completely, resulting in varying degrees of spinal cord dysplasia and neurological impairment depending on the level and severity of the defect. The lower thoracic and lumbar involvement in this case produces the characteristic pattern of lower limb weakness, sensory loss below the knee, and neurogenic bowel and bladder dysfunction — reflective of cauda equina involvement — alongside the fixed contractures that develop from unopposed spastic muscles and chronic disuse. The bilateral hydronephrosis underscores a common secondary complication: neurogenic bladder dysfunction leading to elevated intravesical pressures and upper urinary tract compromise, making renal protection a critical long-term management goal. Early surgical closure, orthotic support for ambulation, and ongoing surveillance for tethering and syrinx formation represent the multimodal physiotherapy and medical approach essential for optimising functional outcomes and preventing secondary complications in this population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 78-year-old gentleman with sudden onset difficulty seeing to one side. Reports words appear cut off when reading. Wife noticed he seems confused this morning. No eye pain. Onset was acute, while sitting at breakfast.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot safely navigate his home environment. Repeatedly walks into furniture and doorframes on the right side. Wife reports he seemed confused about which room he was in earlier. Denies awareness of any visual problem—attributes difficulty to 'not paying attention.'

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of atrial fibrillation (anticoagulated with warfarin, INR 2.1). Hypertension managed with lisinopril. Prior stroke 3 years ago affecting right motor cortex (minor residual weakness). Denies recent falls or head trauma. Symptom onset this morning with acute awareness of vision loss.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visual field confrontation demonstrates right-sided field loss equally in both eyes. Pupils reactive, eye movements normal. Unable to recall examiner's name or the date. Mild increase in right knee reflexes. No focal motor weakness on standard strength testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Acute MRI with DWI sequences shows restricted diffusion in the left posterior circulation distribution. Left occipital cortex and medial temporal lobe involved. Calcarine fissure region includes acute signal changes. Mild involvement of left thalamic region on T2 imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior cerebral artery (PCA) stroke results from occlusion of the PCA, typically from cardioemboli (as in atrial fibrillation) or atherosclerotic disease, causing infarction in the occipital lobes and medial temporal structures supplied by this vessel. The homonymous right visual field defect—affecting both eyes equally—is the hallmark finding, reflecting damage to the left occipital cortex at the calcarine fissure; patients classically deny awareness of the deficit (anosognosia) despite clear environmental consequences like repeatedly colliding with furniture. The acute confusion and disorientation reflect involvement of medial temporal lobe structures critical for memory and spatial orientation, while preserved eye movements and reactive pupils distinguish this from midbrain or oculomotor involvement. The prior right-sided stroke and current anticoagulation status illustrate how recurrent cardioembolic events pose particular risk in patients with atrial fibrillation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 6-year-old child with global developmental delay. Parents report the child walked later than expected, speaks in short phrases, and has difficulty keeping up with peers in play activities. Generalized muscle tone feels 'floppy' to caregivers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child requires handrail support on stairs and frequent rest breaks during play. Cannot run smoothly or jump. Struggles with fine motor tasks like buttoning and drawing. Balance is noticeably poor; trips easily on uneven ground.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Delayed motor milestones: sat at 8 months, walked at 20 months. History of congenital heart defect (now surgically repaired). No significant perinatal complications. Hearing and vision screening showed mild delays in processing. Currently in special education services.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized hypotonia throughout all extremities. Passive range of motion increased; knees hyperextend easily. Ligamentous laxity noted at ankles and wrists. Core and lower extremity strength graded 3/5. Gross motor milestones significantly delayed for age. Mild facial features: upslanting palpebral fissures, flat nasal bridge, low-set ears.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Karyotype analysis: 47 chromosomes with trisomy of chromosome 21 confirmed. Cervical spine flexion-extension imaging shows atlantoaxial subluxation risk. Cardiac echocardiogram documents history of endocardial cushion defect (repaired). Spine radiographs show mild scoliosis progression.

puzzles.explanation: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) is a chromosomal disorder caused by an extra copy of chromosome 21, resulting in intellectual disability and a characteristic constellation of physical features including upslanting palpebral fissures, flat nasal bridge, and generalized ligamentous laxity. The significant hypotonia and delayed motor milestones seen in this child reflect reduced muscle tone and strength typical of the condition, often compounded by associated cardiac anomalies like endocardial cushion defects that limit exercise tolerance during early development. The increased joint mobility and muscle weakness create a cascade of functional limitations—poor balance, difficulty with fine motor coordination, and reliance on assistive strategies—that define the motor profile across childhood. Children with Down syndrome require close monitoring for atlantoaxial instability and progressive scoliosis, both of which can further compromise mobility and necessitate specialized physiotherapy to optimize functional independence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old university student reports wrist pain and swelling after a fall on outstretched hand while playing basketball 3 days ago. Pain is localized to the thumb-side of the wrist. Despite mild swelling, patient reports being able to use the hand for most daily tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to grip or squeeze without discomfort. Gripping activities and return to basketball are not tolerated. Wrist extension range of motion mildly reduced but does not severely limit daily function. Radial-sided (thumb-side) swelling persists on examination.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism with fall from height of 6 feet. Wrist extended at impact. Initial swelling subsided somewhat but residual discomfort persists. Anxiety regarding healing timeline and return to sport. Denies prior wrist injuries. Right-hand dominant; injury on dominant side.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness elicited at the radial-sided scaphoid tubercle. Positive scaphoid compression test reproducing wrist pain. Radial-sided wrist swelling palpated. Pain with ulnar deviation combined with wrist extension. Range of motion slightly limited in extension (50 degrees) and radial deviation (15 degrees). Neurovascular status normal.

clues.label: Imaging
