clues.text: Initial plain radiographs (AP, lateral, and scaphoid views) appear normal without obvious fracture line. CT imaging (High-resolution thin-section) demonstrates non-displaced fracture of the scaphoid waist with minimal separation (<2mm). MRI confirms T2 hyperintense signal through the fracture line with surrounding bone marrow edema. No ligamentous injury visualized.

puzzles.explanation: Scaphoid fractures result from axial loading through an extended wrist—the mechanism here with a fall on outstretched hand—and are the most common carpal bone fracture, particularly in young, active individuals. The scaphoid's precarious blood supply, which enters distally and can leave the proximal pole tenuous, makes even non-displaced fractures prone to nonunion and avascular necrosis if missed or undertreated. The clinical presentation of radial-sided tenderness at the scaphoid tubercle, positive scaphoid compression test, and pain with combined ulnar deviation and wrist extension are hallmark findings; the persistent discomfort despite mild swelling and functional ability reflects the injury's deceptiveness—initial radiographs may appear normal, as they did here, yet high-resolution CT and MRI reveal the fracture line and bone marrow edema. Early recognition and immobilization are critical because delayed diagnosis and continued loading substantially increase healing complications and time to return to sport.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old competitive swimmer presents with insidious-onset lower back pain localized to one side of the midline. Pain worsens with backward arching or hyperextension movements. The pain is sharp and increases with high-intensity training or sprinting activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient experiences pain with butterfly stroke and backstroke more than freestyle. Pain intensifies with training progression and return to competitive intensity. Prolonged sitting and forward bending are tolerated without significant symptoms. Pain is worst at end-of-day after training.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has trained competitively for 5 years with high training volume and repetitive spinal extension loading. Pain onset occurred 8 weeks ago coinciding with increased training intensity. No traumatic event or acute injury. Family history is notable for low back pain in both parents.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, unilateral lumbar pain is reproduced with single-leg hyperextension test on the affected side. Lumbar extension combined with rotation toward the painful side increases symptoms. Palpation reveals paraspinal muscle tenderness at the level of pain. Lower extremity neurological examination is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan demonstrates a unilateral pars interarticularis defect at the L5 level with intact vertebral body and disc. SPECT imaging shows increased uptake at the symptomatic level, indicating active bone stress. Plain radiographs may appear normal; advanced imaging is required for definitive visualization.

puzzles.explanation: Spondylolysis is a stress fracture of the pars interarticularis, the narrow bony bridge between the facet joints in the posterior lumbar spine, resulting from repetitive hyperextension and rotation loading. Young athletes in extension-dominant sports—particularly swimmers performing butterfly and backstroke—are at highest risk, as these movements concentrate shear stress on the pars. The unilateral presentation with positive single-leg hyperextension testing and reproduced symptoms on extension-rotation reflects the mechanical loading pattern, while SPECT imaging showing increased metabolic activity indicates active stress remodeling at the fracture site. Unlike discogenic pain, forward bending and sitting are well-tolerated because flexion unloads the posterior elements; the sharp midline-lateral pain and paraspinal tenderness are hallmark features of this condition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old male reports acute onset bilateral leg pain and progressive weakness over 48 hours. He has noticed recent loss of bladder control and numbness in the perineal area.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk more than short distances; stairs are impossible. Reports severe difficulty lifting feet off ground when walking. Symptoms worsen significantly within 10 minutes of lying flat.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long history of low back pain with known disc disease. Acute worsening triggered by occupational heavy lifting 2 days ago. Currently unable to voluntarily move feet or toes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Straight leg raise test reproduces radicular pain bilaterally. Lower extremity myotomes show grade 2-3 strength in ankle dorsiflexors and plantarflexors. Anal sphincter tone is diminished on rectal examination. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are absent bilaterally; perianal sensory testing shows loss of sensation in S2-S5 distribution.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows a large central disc herniation at L4-L5 level causing severe compression of multiple nerve roots in the spinal canal. There is abnormal signal change within the cord and loss of fluid around the compressed nerves.

puzzles.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome (CES) results from acute compression of multiple lumbosacral nerve roots in the spinal canal, most commonly by a large central disc herniation, and represents a neurosurgical emergency. The syndrome is characterized by the constellation of bilateral lower limb radiculopathy, saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder dysfunction, and bilateral lower motor neuron weakness — a triad that distinguishes it from unilateral disc herniation and makes it immediately recognizable on examination. The acute presentation here, with rapid-onset bilateral leg pain, perineal sensory loss, and urinary retention following heavy lifting, reflects the catastrophic nature of the compression; the absence of reflexes and severe weakness (grade 2-3) in ankle movers indicate significant nerve root damage from the large L4-L5 herniation compressing the cauda equina itself. Early recognition and urgent surgical decompression within 48 hours are critical to prevent permanent neurological deficit and irreversible bowel-bladder dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old patient presents with progressive difficulty lifting the left foot during walking over the past 2 weeks. The foot feels heavy and unresponsive, with no pain. Gait has become increasingly awkward, and the patient has begun tripping.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports tripping frequently and catching the left toe when walking, particularly on uneven surfaces. Climbing stairs is significantly impaired; the patient cannot dorsiflex the ankle against gravity. Running and sports are impossible. The patient describes walking with a high-stepping gait to compensate for the dorsiflexion weakness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset was acute with no preceding trauma or injury. The patient recalls a pressure point or direct blow to the lateral knee area approximately 10 days before symptom onset. Recent prolonged sitting with leg crossed at the knee and tight band-aid or wrap around the upper fibular region preceded the symptoms by several days.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ankle dorsiflexion strength is 0-1/5 on the left; plantarflexion and inversion are intact at 5/5. Great toe extension is severely weakened. The patient demonstrates sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and first web space between the great toe and second toe. Tapping over the fibular head reproduces symptoms, suggesting nerve entrapment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the left knee and proximal tibia shows external compression of the common peroneal nerve at the fibular head level. T2-weighted imaging reveals increased signal within the nerve distal to the compression point, indicating axonal swelling and denervation changes. No structural lesion or tumor identified.

puzzles.explanation: Peroneal nerve palsy results from compression of the common peroneal nerve as it winds around the fibular head, a superficial anatomical point highly vulnerable to external pressure. The nerve innervates the tibialis anterior and extensor digitorum longus muscles, so compression classically produces foot drop — the inability to dorsiflex the ankle — forcing the characteristic high-stepping gait to avoid tripping. The acute onset following pressure at the lateral knee, combined with preserved plantarflexion and inversion strength (supplied by the tibial nerve), clearly identifies peroneal involvement; the sensory loss over the dorsum and first web space further confirms the distribution. MRI findings of nerve swelling distal to the fibular head compression point indicate early denervation changes, typical of entrapment neuropraxia, which may recover with decompression and activity modification.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old office administrator reports bilateral neck and shoulder tension with occipital headaches occurring 3-4 times weekly. The discomfort is described as a dull ache with occasional sharp shooting sensations, worsening as the workday progresses.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms triggered specifically by sustained computer work and forward head posturing. Patient avoids prolonged desk tasks but tolerates short activity bursts. Morning stiffness present but improves with movement. No neurological symptoms reported.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 15-year history of sedentary desk work with recent job transition increasing daily screen time to 6+ hours. Recent workplace ergonomic changes (monitor repositioning downward). No prior neck injury, no radicular history. Stress levels elevated in past 3 months coinciding with symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical rotation limited bilaterally with headache reproduction at end-range; flexion/extension restricted approximately 20 degrees. Upper trapezius demonstrates marked hypertonia with trigger points; levator scapulae tender on palpation. Scapular positioning: elevated and protracted bilaterally. Neurological testing: normal reflexes and sensation throughout upper limbs; no dermatomal deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical X-rays: reversal of normal lordosis with anterior head position (approximately 3 cm deviation from neutral alignment). MRI: mild degenerative disc bulging at C4-C5 and C5-C6 without neural foraminal stenosis or cord compression. No acute structural pathology. Soft tissue signal shows myofascial thickening in bilateral upper trapezius consistent with chronic muscular tension.

puzzles.explanation: Neck tension from sustained desk work results from chronic muscle overload and postural stress — prolonged forward head posturing disrupts the cervical spine's mechanical alignment and forces the upper trapezius and levator scapulae into sustained isometric contraction to maintain head position. The progressive ergonomic mismatch, combined with elevated stress and increased screen time, perpetuates this cycle: shortened, hypertonic muscles develop trigger points, cervical mobility decreases, and referred occipital headaches emerge from myofascial irritation. Unlike radiculopathy or structural stenosis, the normal neurology and absence of neural compromise here confirm a primarily muscular and postural problem. Management focuses on restoring cervical lordosis through postural retraining, ergonomic optimization, manual release of myofascial restrictions, and targeted stabilization of deep cervical flexors to offload the overworked superficial muscles.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old male with a desk-based job presents with insidious-onset lower back and hip discomfort that has gradually worsened over the past year. He reports difficulty maintaining an upright posture and finds that his symptoms worsen as the day progresses.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered and amplified by prolonged static positions, particularly sitting at desks or in cars. Activities requiring floor-level work or rising from low surfaces provoke significant discomfort. Standing and walking provide relief. Patient notes postural 'corrections' ease symptoms within minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset was insidious without trauma. The patient has maintained a sedentary lifestyle for a decade with minimal structured exercise. No history of previous back injury, no progressive neurological symptoms, and no constitutional red flags. Current medications and comorbidities are unremarkable.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, bilateral hip flexor tightness is evident with positive Thomas test. Hip abductor weakness is noted at 4+/5 bilaterally. Lumbar erector spinae and rectus femoris are tight; abdominal core strength is poor. Pelvic tilt is anterior with excessive lumbar lordosis. Neurological examination is entirely normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine shows minimal degenerative changes without disc herniation, stenosis, or facet arthrosis of clinical significance. The spinal cord and nerve roots are not compressed. Imaging is essentially normal and does not identify structural pathology; findings support a muscular and postural etiology.

puzzles.explanation: Lower crossed syndrome is a postural dysfunction characterized by a specific pattern of muscular imbalance: tightness in the hip flexors and lumbar extensors paired with weakness in the abdominal core and hip abductors. This imbalance drives anterior pelvic tilt and excessive lumbar lordosis, creating a compensatory 'crossed' pattern of muscle tightness and weakness that perpetuates poor spinal mechanics. The insidious onset in a sedentary worker with minimal neurological involvement and normal imaging is classic—prolonged sitting selectively shortens hip flexors and erector spinae while the deep core and gluteal stabilizers atrophy from disuse. Correction of the postural fault and progressive strengthening of the abdominal and hip abductor musculature, combined with stretching of the tight hip flexors, forms the foundation of management and explains why postural corrections and standing provide rapid relief.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old male reports chronic low back pain with intermittent pain radiating into the left lower leg. Symptoms began 5 months ago and have worsened despite initial post-operative improvement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient demonstrates severe limitations with bending forward; even slight flexion beyond 15 degrees triggers back pain. Sit-to-stand transfers are slow and guarded with pain reproduction. Walking tolerance is reduced to 10-15 minutes before requiring rest. The patient reports difficulty with rotation movements and cannot lift objects heavier than 10 pounds.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient underwent fusion surgery for spondylolisthesis grade 2 with spinal stenosis. Prior to surgery, 3 years of conservative management had failed. Post-operatively, the patient had initial improvement for 3 months, followed by recurrent pain. Current medications include opioids; the patient is 4 weeks into physical therapy with minimal improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar flexion limited to 20 degrees; forward bending reproduction test triggers left lower extremity radicular pain at approximately L5 distribution. Straight leg raise positive on left at 60 degrees reproducing radicular symptoms. Hip flexor strength reduced to 4/5 bilaterally. Palpation over surgical scar elicits localized tenderness without warmth or swelling.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative CT imaging shows successful fusion with solid bony bridging across the L4-L5 segment. Hardware positioning is appropriate without subsidence. However, MRI reveals adjacent segment degeneration at L3-L4 with left-sided foraminal stenosis and nerve root contact. T2-weighted imaging shows disc desiccation and early stenotic changes at the level above the fusion.

puzzles.explanation: Post-spinal fusion syndrome encompasses the constellation of persistent or recurrent symptoms following initially successful spinal fusion surgery, often driven by adjacent segment degeneration — accelerated degenerative changes at levels above or below the fusion due to altered biomechanics and increased load-bearing demands. In this case, the solid L4-L5 fusion created a rigid segment, shifting stress proximally to L3-L4 where disc desiccation and foraminal stenosis have developed, re-engaging the L5 nerve root and reproducing the patient's original radicular pattern. The initial post-operative improvement followed by declining tolerance to flexion and activity, combined with MRI evidence of stenotic changes at the adjacent level, distinguishes this from acute post-operative complications or fusion failure. This represents a common long-term sequela affecting 5-15% of fusion patients, requiring careful rehabilitation to restore segmental mobility above and below the fused level while managing activity load.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 4-year-old child with persistent head tilt to one side. Parents first noticed the abnormal posture at around 6 weeks of age. Head position has remained relatively fixed since infancy, though range of motion has gradually decreased over time.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child cannot turn head fully to midline or opposite side; head consistently drifts back to tilted position. Trunk rotation compensates for limited cervical movement. Passive correction attempts meet resistance and child immediately returns to preferred posture.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset at 6 weeks postpartum; no birth trauma, forceps delivery, or intrauterine constraint reported. Not associated with congenital conditions, acute infection, or trauma. Recent normal vision and vestibular screening. Static presentation over several years without progression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right-sided head tilt with reduced cervical lateral flexion and rotation. Palpation reveals firm, thickened muscle bulk on right neck. No neurological deficits, no spasticity, no evidence of CNS involvement. Muscle appears hypertrophic and fibrotic on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of right sternocleidomastoid shows marked thickening with echogenic fibrotic tissue replacing normal muscle architecture. MRI cervical spine demonstrates normal vertebral alignment, no intraspinal lesions, and confirms primary soft-tissue pathology. No structural or neurological abnormality identified.

puzzles.explanation: Acquired torticollis in infancy typically results from fibrosis and contracture of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, often following birth trauma, haematoma, or compartment syndrome in the neonatal period—though in this case the exact precipitant remains unclear. The characteristic presentation of a fixed head tilt with ipsilateral muscle thickening and hypoechoic fibrotic changes on ultrasound reflects the end-stage of muscle remodelling, where normal contractile tissue has been replaced by non-compliant fibrous tissue. The early onset at 6 weeks of age, gradual loss of cervical mobility, and the child's tendency to reflexively return to the preferred posture once corrected are hallmarks of this mechanical shortening. Unlike acute or spastic torticollis from neurological causes, the static course, normal neurological examination, and preserved imaging of the cervical spine confirm this as a structural myofascial contracture requiring early physiotherapy intervention and possible surgical release if conservative management fails to regain functional range.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old distance runner reports posterior knee pain for 6 weeks. Pain is sharp and burning, worsens with running, and has progressively limited training volume. No acute injury event.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running downhill significantly aggravates the pain; the patient reports immediate discomfort when initiating downhill running on trails. High-speed activities and sudden deceleration reproducibly provoke sharp posterior knee pain. Walking is generally tolerated; however, descending stairs with full body weight loading triggers symptoms. Prolonged sitting with knees flexed beyond 90 degrees causes mild discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Training error: 40% mileage increase over recent weeks with shift to hilly terrain and tempo running. Bilateral posterior knee pain noted, right > left. No prior knee pathology relevant to current complaint. Previous patellar tendinitis fully resolved 3 years ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resisted knee flexion with external rotation at 30° reproduces pain. Pain elicited with combined resisted ankle plantarflexion and knee flexion. Posterior knee joint line tender to palpation. Minimal effusion; soft tissue thickening palpable over popliteal tendon insertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the bilateral knees demonstrates thickening and signal hyperintensity within the popliteus tendon at its femoral origin, measuring approximately 7mm in cross-sectional diameter compared to normal 3-4mm. Fluid signal is noted in the popliteal sheath, indicating reactive tenosynovitis. T2-weighted imaging shows increased signal within the muscle belly compatible with myotendinous strain.

puzzles.explanation: Popliteal tendinopathy is an overuse injury of the popliteus muscle-tendon unit, which acts as a dynamic restraint and initiates knee flexion and internal tibial rotation — functions particularly stressed during downhill running and deceleration. The condition typically develops from training errors, particularly sudden increases in volume or intensity on eccentric-demand terrain, leading to microtrauma and reactive inflammation within the tendon and surrounding sheath. The sharp, burning posterior knee pain that worsens with downhill running, stair descent, and resisted knee flexion with external rotation reflects the popliteus's biomechanical role in controlling eccentric knee extension and tibial external rotation. MRI confirmation of tendon thickening, tenosynovitis, and myotendinous strain, combined with the characteristic mechanism and imaging findings, distinguishes this from isolated posterolateral capsular pathology or meniscal injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old soccer player experienced a non-contact pivoting injury during match play 3 weeks ago with immediate onset of acute knee pain. The player reports persistent swelling, functional instability, and apprehension during weight-bearing activities, preventing return to sport.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The knee gives way unpredictably during lateral movements and pivoting maneuvers; the player demonstrates a guarded gait. The patient reports significant pain and instability when weight shifts from the injured leg or during rapid direction changes. Ascending and descending stairs are performed slowly with hand rail support. The player cannot tolerate running or cutting movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred during a sudden deceleration with planted foot and external rotation at the knee; the player felt a pop and immediate pain. The player was unable to continue playing and experienced rapid knee swelling within 2 hours. No prior knee injuries. The player reports difficulty with proprioception and describes feeling unable to trust the knee.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lachman test at 30 degrees of knee flexion demonstrates markedly increased anterior tibial translation with soft end-feel, graded as 2-3/3 for positive anterior cruciate ligament insufficiency. Anterior drawer test is positive at 90 degrees. Pivot shift test reproduces apprehension and dynamic instability with subluxation and reduction. Moderate knee effusion is palpable; pain-free range of motion is limited to 0-80 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates disruption of the anterior cruciate ligament fibers with diffuse increased signal throughout the substance of the ligament, consistent with acute complete tear. T2-weighted imaging shows bone bruising at the lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibial plateau, confirming the mechanism of injury. No meniscal tear is identified.

puzzles.explanation: Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tear results from disruption of the primary restraint to anterior tibial translation, typically occurring during deceleration with a planted foot and sudden rotational loading—the classic non-contact pivoting mechanism seen in this soccer player. The immediate pop, rapid effusion, and positive Lachman and pivot shift tests reflect acute complete ligament disruption, while the bone bruising pattern at the lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibia confirms the high-energy valgus-external rotation mechanism. Loss of proprioceptive feedback and functional instability during weight-bearing and cutting movements are hallmark consequences of ACL insufficiency, as the ligament's mechanoreceptors normally provide crucial neurosensory input for dynamic knee control. MRI confirmation of diffuse signal change throughout the ligament substance distinguishes complete tear from partial injuries and guides surgical versus conservative management decisions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old athlete presents with acute severe pain at the base of the great toe and swelling following a sudden forced backward bending of the toe during sport. Weight-bearing is immediately compromised.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot participate in sport and ambulates with significant pain avoidance. Push-off activities and toe-off during gait are severely limited. Functional activities requiring toe dorsiflexion or plantarflexion force are impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred with a forceful dorsiflexion load through the first ray when the player's planted foot was struck by an opponent, forcing the great toe into maximum extension. Immediate pain and swelling developed within minutes. The player reported a popping sensation at the time of injury. No prior first ray pathology or injury history documented.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Swelling and ecchymosis present over the dorsal and medial first MTP joint. Passive dorsiflexion is restricted and painful. Plantarflexion strength is 3/5. Palpation reveals focal tenderness over the plantar plate insertion and medial ligamentous structures.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show a small dorsal avulsion fracture at the base of the proximal phalanx with minor sesamoid malalignment. MRI demonstrates disruption of the plantar plate with surrounding edema and hemorrhage within the joint capsule.

puzzles.explanation: Turf toe is an acute sprain or strain of the first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint complex, typically caused by forced dorsiflexion of the great toe during push-off activities—hence its prevalence in athletes on artificial surfaces. The plantar plate, a thick ligamentous structure that stabilizes the joint plantarly, is the primary structure at risk, and hyperextension injuries can result in partial or complete tears with associated capsular hemorrhage and occasionally avulsion fractures as seen here. The characteristic presentation of immediate pain with a popping sensation, combined with severe functional limitation during push-off and plantarflexion—the movements that load the plantar plate—reflects the critical role this structure plays in generating propulsive force. Recognition of plantar plate disruption on imaging and demonstration of plantarflexion weakness are essential, as the degree of structural damage determines rehabilitation progression and return-to-sport timelines.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old male of Northern European descent presents with an 8-year history of chronic bilateral lower back pain. The pain is insidious in onset and follows an unusual pattern: worst upon waking and improving as the day progresses. He reports accompanying systemic symptoms including persistent fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Marked morning stiffness lasting 90 minutes that resolves with movement and activity. Pain and stiffness paradoxically worsen with rest and overnight immobility, opposite to typical mechanical back pain patterns. Significant sleep disturbance with frequent nocturnal awakenings and night sweats.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive disease course with gradual postural changes: loss of lumbar spine flexion and increasing thoracic kyphosis. Recent episode of anterior uveitis. Positive family history of rheumatological disease in a first-degree relative. Recent rheumatological workup completed.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar spine flexion is severely restricted with the patient unable to touch toes, demonstrating significant loss of forward bending (Schober test shows only 2cm expansion, normal 5cm). Spinal extension is also limited. Chest expansion is reduced to 2cm (normal greater than 5cm), indicating thoracic involvement. Occiput-to-wall distance is 12cm, indicating loss of cervical lordosis and increased thoracic kyphosis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine MRI demonstrates multiple vertebral bodies bridged by bony ankylosis in a linear pattern, with complete ossification of the anterior longitudinal ligament and intervertebral discs. Sacroiliac joints show bilateral complete fusion. Active inflammatory changes visible in early disease areas on T1-weighted imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy driven by HLA-B27–mediated inflammation of the axial skeleton, characteristically affecting the sacroiliac joints and spine with progressive bony ankylosis. The condition classically presents with inflammatory back pain — insidious onset, worst on waking, and paradoxically improving with activity — accompanied by systemic features like fatigue, fever, and extra-articular manifestations such as uveitis. The bilateral sacroiliac fusion and complete vertebral bridging seen on imaging reflect advanced disease, while the marked restriction in spinal flexion, thoracic kyphosis, and reduced chest expansion demonstrate the characteristic postural and functional consequences of progressive spinal ankylosis. Northern European descent and male predominance align with the epidemiology of this HLA-B27–associated condition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old postmenopausal female presents with progressive basilar thumb pain and functional limitation in pinching and grasping activities. Pain is localized to the base of the thumb at the carpometacarpal joint and worsens with repetitive gripping.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by pinching activities, holding small objects, opening jars, and gripping with the thumb opposed. Hand dominance is affected preferentially. Simple precision tasks such as holding a pen or threading a needle reproduce symptoms within minutes. Symptoms ease with rest and cold application.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Morning stiffness at the base of thumb lasting 10-15 minutes, improving with warmth and gentle activity. Progressive onset over 3 years. History of repetitive hand use from office work and hobbies. No significant trauma. Recent menopause. No inflammatory or rheumatological conditions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The carpometacarpal joint demonstrates swelling and bony enlargement with palpable osteophytes. Active thumb opposition is weak and limited to 3/5 strength. Abduction and adduction strength are reduced. Grinding sensation is palpable during passive thumb motion. A positive grind test reproduces pain with axial loading and rotation of the first metacarpal. No overlying inflammation or swelling of soft tissues noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand radiographs demonstrate significant first carpometacarpal joint osteoarthritis with joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and subluxation of the metacarpal base. Characteristic degenerative changes include marginal osteophytes creating a squared-off appearance. Plain films also show degenerative changes in the scaphotrapezial joint, consistent with grade 3-4 osteoarthritis in Eaton-Littler classification.

puzzles.explanation: Thumb carpometacarpal (CMC) osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease at the base of the thumb, exceptionally common in postmenopausal women due to hormonal influences on cartilage and ligamentous laxity. The CMC joint is particularly vulnerable to early osteoarthritis because of its unique saddle morphology and high mechanical demands during pinching and gripping — the very activities that provoked symptoms here. The characteristic presentation of basilar thumb pain with functional loss in opposition and precision tasks, combined with palpable grinding and a positive grind test, reflects the underlying joint incongruity and osteophytic changes visible on imaging. Eaton-Littler grade 3-4 disease with metacarpal subluxation indicates advanced cartilage loss and ligamentous insufficiency, explaining both the mechanical instability and the morning stiffness typical of osteoarthritis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old female presents with acute-onset sharp shoulder pain that began 1-2 weeks ago without any remembered injury. Pain is severe, poorly responsive to analgesics, and significantly limits arm use. She recalls some mild discomfort in the weeks prior.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Active shoulder abduction and forward flexion are severely limited beyond 40°, with inability to perform overhead tasks. Significant night pain disrupts sleep. Passive range of motion, however, remains relatively preserved.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No significant past medical history or shoulder surgery. Recent cervical imaging was normal, ruling out cervical radiculopathy as source. Patient is otherwise generally healthy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive range of motion of the shoulder is relatively preserved compared to severe limitation in active range. Abduction lag is marked, with inability to maintain arm in abduction against gravity. The 'empty can' test for supraspinatus is positive with pain reproduction. Infraspinatus strength testing reveals 3/5 strength. Palpation over the rotator cuff insertion demonstrates point tenderness. No joint instability appreciated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder imaging reveals a discrete calcific deposit within the supraspinatus tendon with surrounding inflammatory changes and edema. The calcification is intra-tendinous and demonstrates early resorption phase characteristics on advanced imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Calcific tendinitis is an inflammatory condition characterized by deposition of calcium hydroxyapatite crystals within the rotator cuff tendons, most commonly affecting the supraspinatus in middle-aged patients without prior trauma. The acute phase is driven by crystal-induced inflammation and edema within the tendon, explaining the sudden-onset severe pain and marked functional loss despite preserved passive range of motion — the pathognomonic dissociation between severe active limitation and relatively maintained passive mobility. The intra-tendinous calcification visible on imaging, combined with positive impingement signs (empty can test) and the resorption phase characteristics, confirms the inflammatory stage rather than chronic asymptomatic deposits. Night pain and poor analgesic response are characteristic of this inflammatory cascade; most cases resolve spontaneously over weeks to months as the body resorbs the calcific deposit, though physiotherapy focusing on pain-controlled mobility and scapular stability optimizes recovery during this process.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 26-year-old runner. Anterior lower leg pain, gradual onset over 4 weeks. Pain is activity-related and worsens with running on hard surfaces. No history of trauma or acute injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered within 2–3 minutes of running or uphill walking. Stair climbing and dorsiflexion-loaded activities consistently aggravate symptoms. Rest and elevation provide relief. Unable to maintain usual running volume.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training intensity and weekly mileage over the past 6 weeks. Pain localizes to the anterior shin region, not generalized. No compartment swelling, no neurological symptoms, no prior lower leg injury on this side.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness elicited on palpation along the anterior tibial region. Resisted ankle dorsiflexion reproduces pain and demonstrates 4+/5 strength due to pain inhibition. Passive plantarflexion stretch triggers discomfort. Crepitus palpable with active dorsiflexion. No compartment firmness or acute swelling.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic thickening and heterogeneous echotexture along the anterior tibial tendon. Doppler imaging demonstrates increased peritendinous vascularity. No fluid collection or complete tear. MRI confirms tendon signal abnormality and mild peritendinous edema without acute rupture.

puzzles.explanation: Tibialis anterior tendinopathy results from repetitive microtrauma to the tendon, typically triggered by sudden increases in dorsiflexion-loading activities such as running or hill work — a classic overuse injury in runners. The tendon becomes inflamed and degenerative, presenting with pain on dorsiflexion-resisted activities, anterior shin tenderness, and characteristic crepitus on movement, as the damaged tendon surfaces generate friction during contraction. The imaging findings of hypoechoic thickening with increased peritendinous vascularity reflect the chronic inflammatory and neovascular response to load; notably, the absence of a complete tear or fluid collection distinguishes this from acute rupture. Management focuses on activity modification, eccentric loading, and progressive return to running, as the gradual onset over weeks of increased training volume indicates a load-management issue rather than structural failure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old recreational runner presents with forefoot pain that worsens toward the end of the day. The pain is localized to the plantar aspect beneath the metatarsal heads, with no recent trauma or swelling reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is significantly aggravated by prolonged standing, walking on hard surfaces, and activities requiring push-off (running, dancing). Relief occurs with rest, elevation, and wearing supportive footwear with cushioning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient recently increased running volume by 30% two months ago and has worn minimalist shoes for three years. They report high arches and a history of bunions in their family, with no previous lower limb injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation directly over the metatarsal heads (particularly 2nd-4th) reproduces pain. Metatarsophalangeal joint extension is painless. Foot posture shows increased arch height with no callusing or visible deformity in the interdigital spaces.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show no fracture lines or bony pathology. Ultrasound of the forefoot demonstrates thickening of the plantar fascia in the metatarsal region without discrete mass or bursal distension.

puzzles.explanation: Metatarsalgia is a common overuse condition characterized by inflammation and load overconcentration beneath the metatarsal heads in the forefoot, typically resulting from cumulative stress rather than acute trauma. The plantar fascia and surrounding soft tissues in this region bear significant load during propulsive activities like running and dancing, making the condition particularly sensitive to increased training volume and footwear changes — as reflected in this patient's recent shift to minimalist shoes combined with a 30% running increase. High-arched feet predispose to metatarsalgia because they concentrate load on the forefoot and reduce shock absorption capacity, while the reproduction of pain on direct palpation over the metatarsal heads, absence of MTP joint involvement, and imaging showing plantar fascia thickening without discrete pathology are classic hallmarks. Management focuses on load reduction, arch support, and footwear modification to redistribute pressure and restore tolerance.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old office worker presents with unilateral pain in the posterior pelvis and buttock region that radiates occasionally to the lateral thigh. The pain pattern is asymmetric and reproducible with certain movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting (particularly one side more than the other), single-leg stance, and climbing stairs. Relief is obtained by lying down, changing position frequently, and avoiding sustained forward bending.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has been working from home for six months with a suboptimal desk setup and sits with a crossed-leg posture regularly. No history of trauma, pregnancy-related symptoms, or previous low back pain episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Patrick's test on the symptomatic side with pain localized to the posterior pelvis. Gaenslen's test reproduces pain. Single-leg stance on the painful side causes posterior pelvic pain. Palpation over the SIJ reveals tenderness. Hip ROM is preserved and painless.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic X-rays reveal normal joint alignment without signs of osteoarthritis. MRI of the sacroiliac joints shows mild inflammatory changes in the synovial portion with no erosive changes or ankylosis.

puzzles.explanation: Sacroiliac joint dysfunction occurs when asymmetric loading, poor postural habits, or muscle imbalance compromise the joint's stability and proprioceptive control, leading to localized pain and referred symptoms into the buttock and lateral thigh. Unlike referred pain from lumbar radiculopathy, SIJ dysfunction produces pain that is reproducible with loading patterns — particularly unilateral stance and stair climbing — and the preserved hip mobility here rules out primary hip pathology. The mild synovial inflammatory changes on imaging reflect the mechanical irritation pattern typical of dysfunction, and the postural predisposition from prolonged asymmetric sitting with sustained hip flexion is a classic mechanism in office workers. Specific provocation tests like Patrick's and Gaenslen's, combined with pain localized to palpation over the joint rather than radiating distally, confirm that the SIJ itself is the pain generator rather than neural compression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old retired accountant reports progressive hip pain and stiffness over eight months. Pain is located deep in the hip and groin region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty with activities requiring hip flexion and rotation: putting on shoes, climbing stairs, and rising from a seated position are now significantly limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has no history of hip trauma, falls, or previous hip pathology. They are sedentary post-retirement and report gradual onset of stiffness without preceding acute pain or swelling. No fevers or systemic symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion limited to 70 degrees, internal rotation severely restricted (10 degrees), external rotation limited to 20 degrees. FABER test is restricted and uncomfortable. Scour test elicits deep hip pain. Hip strength testing is difficult due to pain-limited ROM.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain hip radiographs demonstrate joint space narrowing at the femoral head and acetabulum with marginal osteophytes bilaterally. No loose bodies or femoral head flattening evident.

puzzles.explanation: Frozen hip, or adhesive capsulitis of the hip, is a condition of progressive capsular fibrosis and contracture that leads to marked restriction of hip motion, particularly internal rotation and flexion. It typically develops insidiously over months in middle-aged to older adults, often without preceding trauma or obvious inciting event, though sedentary lifestyles and periods of immobility can predispose to its onset. The deep groin pain with severe loss of internal rotation and the global pattern of capsular restriction—affecting flexion, rotation, and adduction—are characteristic of primary hip capsulitis rather than osteoarthritis, despite the radiographic changes showing joint space narrowing and marginal osteophytes. This presentation reflects the fibrotic remodeling of the joint capsule itself, which distinguishes it from mechanical hip pathology and makes it particularly responsive to aggressive physiotherapy aimed at restoring capsular extensibility through graduated range-of-motion work.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 65-year-old former manual worker presents with an insidious onset of lower back pain and progressive right lower extremity pain over 8 months. The patient describes radiating pain into the right calf and foot associated with sensory changes, limiting walking capacity to less than a block.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports that prolonged standing and walking exacerbate symptoms, while sitting and lying down provide relief. Forward bending and activities requiring spinal extension are particularly troublesome. Symptoms are reproducible with positional changes and activity level, with a clear pattern of claudication-like presentation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long history of intermittent lower back pain over 20+ years, with progressive worsening in the last 2 years. No acute injury precipitated the current symptoms. Patient denies bladder/bowel dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or sudden leg weakness. Sleep is disrupted by nighttime symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right ankle reflex is absent (0) and patellar reflex diminished (1+). Sensory testing reveals decreased sensation in the right L5-S1 dermatome. Right hip and knee extension strength is mildly reduced at 4/5. Femoral stretch test is negative, but SLR on the right reproduces radicular pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine shows moderate to severe stenosis at L4-L5 with right-sided lateral recess stenosis and intervertebral disc herniation. There is compression of the right L5 nerve root with associated edema. Degenerative changes and facet joint arthrosis are evident at this level.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spinal stenosis results from degenerative narrowing of the spinal canal and lateral recesses, typically from disc herniation, facet hypertrophy, and ligamentous thickening — progressive changes that accumulate over years of spinal loading. The condition classically presents with neurogenic claudication: pain and sensory changes worsening with standing and extension (which further narrows the canal) and resolving with flexion and sitting (which opens the canal). In this case, the 20-year history of intermittent back pain progressing to unilateral radiculopathy, combined with the characteristic claudication pattern limiting walking to a single block, reflects the stenotic canal compromising the L5 nerve root. The absent ankle reflex, L5-S1 dermatomal sensory loss, and moderate right lateral recess stenosis on imaging confirm nerve root compression rather than simple discogenic pain, distinguishing this from acute disc herniation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old female. Gradual onset of skin tightness and stiffness over 2 years, beginning in hands and progressing proximally. Reports difficulty with fine motor tasks and reduced facial expression. No acute trauma or injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform fine tasks requiring finger dexterity (buttoning, writing). Wrist and finger extension limited. Hands become painful and change color (white/blue) with cold exposure. Difficulty opening mouth wide.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms started insidiously in fingers 2 years ago without known trigger. Bilateral and progressive. Recent onset of difficulty swallowing solid foods and persistent heartburn. Fatigue with minimal exertion. No previous autoimmune diagnosis. Family history of autoimmune disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Skin visibly thickened and indurated over dorsal hands, fingers, forearms, and face. Fingers appear tapered with loss of normal flexion creases. Reduced extension ROM in all finger joints bilaterally. Mouth opening limited. Symmetric findings. Skin appears shiny and tight, especially around lips and eyes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest shows patchy ground-glass opacities in bilateral lung bases consistent with interstitial lung disease. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) positive with anticentromere antibodies. Elevated inflammatory markers. Esophageal manometry shows reduced peristalsis.

puzzles.explanation: Scleroderma (systemic sclerosis) is a progressive autoimmune connective tissue disorder characterized by excessive collagen deposition and fibrosis affecting skin and internal organs, with limited cutaneous disease typically progressing from the hands proximally over months to years. The pathognomonic skin changes—tightening, induration, loss of normal creases, and characteristic facial features like microstomia and perioral tightening—reflect dermal fibroblast activation and collagen overproduction. This patient's bilateral, symmetric, progressive involvement beginning distally in the hands, combined with systemic manifestations (Raynaud's phenomenon, dysphagia, reduced esophageal motility, and interstitial lung disease) and positive anticentromere antibodies, are classic for limited cutaneous scleroderma. Early recognition is critical as physiotherapy addressing joint mobility, hand function, and swallowing compensation can significantly impact quality of life and functional independence as the disease progresses.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old male. Gradual onset shortness of breath over 6-8 weeks. Complains of chest discomfort that worsens with deep breathing. No acute injury or fall. Reports feeling more fatigued than usual.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking upstairs and sustained walking on flat ground provoke breathlessness. Difficulty taking deep breaths; patient prefers shallow breathing. Lying flat significantly worsens symptoms; propped-up position provides relief. Cough is dry and non-productive.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms developed insidiously without trauma or infection. Patient reports waking at night short of breath; needs extra pillows to sleep comfortably. Fatigue is out of proportion to activity level. No fever, no recent illness. Long-standing smoking history but no acute exacerbation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced breath sounds at right lung base. Dullness to percussion over right lower chest region. Asymmetric chest expansion—right side moves less than left. Tactile fremitus decreased on right. No crackles, wheezes, or friction rub heard. Heart sounds normal; no peripheral swelling.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows opacification at right lung base with meniscus sign at lateral margin. Mediastinum remains midline. Ultrasound confirms anechoic fluid collection between visceral and parietal pleural layers at right base, measuring 4–5 cm in depth. No evidence of pneumothorax or consolidation.

puzzles.explanation: Pleural effusion is an abnormal accumulation of fluid between the visceral and parietal pleura, occurring secondary to conditions that increase capillary hydrostatic pressure, decrease colloid osmotic pressure, or impair lymphatic drainage. The insidious onset over weeks, orthopnoea relieved by propping up, and exertional dyspnoea reflect the mechanical restriction of lung expansion and diaphragmatic excursion caused by the fluid collection. The characteristic physical findings—diminished breath sounds, dullness to percussion, reduced tactile fremitus, and asymmetric chest expansion—localise the effusion to the right lung base, while the imaging findings of a meniscus sign and anechoic fluid collection on ultrasound confirm the diagnosis and rule out pneumothorax or consolidation. In a 68-year-old with smoking history and gradual symptom progression, malignancy, heart failure, and chronic liver disease remain the most common aetiologies, necessitating further investigation into the effusion's aetiology and characterisation of the fluid itself.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 41-year-old administrative assistant. Gradual onset of medial knee pain over 3 months. Achy quality, activity-dependent, worse with stairs and running. No acute injury or swelling initially reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered by stair climbing, jumping, and leg press exercises. Aggravated by prolonged knee flexion while sitting. Eased by rest and ice. Direct pressure on inner knee is uncomfortable.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms coincided with starting gym membership 3 months ago—squats and leg press twice weekly. Sedentary job (8 hrs/day sitting). No prior knee injuries, surgeries, or inflammatory conditions. No trauma. Symptoms bilateral or unilateral not specified initially.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Focal tenderness and mild swelling on medial tibia, below the joint line. McMurray and Lachman tests negative. Pain reproduced with resisted knee flexion (hamstring/gracilis contraction). No knee effusion, normal ligamentous stability, full painless ROM in extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic fluid collection at pes anserine bursa insertion (medial tibia, below articular surface). No cartilage damage, ligament tears, or meniscal pathology. MRI confirms bursal inflammation without associated joint involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Pes anserine bursitis is inflammation of the bursa where the conjoined tendons of the sartorius, gracilis, and semitendinosus insert on the anteromedial tibia—a common overuse injury in runners and those new to resistance training. The condition develops from repetitive knee flexion and tibial internal rotation, particularly during squatting and leg press movements, which compress and irritate the bursa between the tendons and bone. Characteristic focal tenderness below the medial joint line, reproduction of pain with resisted knee flexion, and absence of intra-articular pathology (normal ligament and meniscal tests) distinguish pes anserine bursitis from knee ligamentous injuries or meniscal tears. The gradual onset following a sudden increase in training volume—classic overuse presentation—combined with imaging confirmation of bursal inflammation makes this a straightforward mechanically-driven diagnosis responsive to activity modification and load management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 29-year-old male marathon runner reports progressive anterior chest wall pain localized to the nipple region, with associated skin irritation. Symptoms began during long training runs and have worsened with increased mileage.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running longer distances (>8 miles) and is significantly worse when wearing cotton shirts. Symptom relief occurs with proper sports bra usage, petroleum jelly application, or running in cold weather. Pain-free walking and cycling occur.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has trained for marathons for five years without previous nipple issues but recently switched to lower-quality technical shirts. Increased mileage over the past four weeks correlates with symptom onset. No history of dermatologic conditions or trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nipple area shows erythema and excoriation with no purulent drainage or systemic infection signs. Superficial skin ulceration visible at the nipple tip. No masses, discharge, or lymphadenopathy appreciated. Vital signs normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray unremarkable (normal cardiac silhouette, clear lung fields) — confirms mechanical/superficial etiology and excludes cardiac or pulmonary involvement. Clinical diagnosis based on physical examination findings.

puzzles.explanation: Runner's nipple is a mechanical friction injury caused by repetitive rubbing of the nipple against clothing during running, particularly during prolonged efforts that exceed typical training duration. The condition results from loss of the protective keratin layer and superficial skin breakdown, leading to erythema, excoriation, and ulceration—exactly as seen in this runner's recent transition to lower-quality technical fabric and increased mileage. Unlike dermatologic or cardiac pathology, runner's nipple is purely mechanical and responds predictably to friction reduction through moisture-wicking fabrics, lubrication, or protective barriers, which explains the rapid symptom relief with proper sports wear and petroleum jelly. Prevention and management centre on identifying the offending shirt material and applying friction-reducing strategies before symptoms develop during training runs.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old college cross-country runner reports localized, sharp pain in the lower leg (anterior tibia or fibula region) that worsens progressively over three weeks. Pain is worse with running and limiting athletic participation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running, jumping, and high-impact activities trigger sharp pain that can be severe enough to cause limping. Walking and non-impact activities (swimming, cycling) are relatively pain-free. Pain increases with morning stiffness and improves minimally with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The athlete increased training intensity and volume by 50% over the past month preparing for conference championships. They run on hard surfaces (track and pavement) exclusively. No previous lower limb injuries or stress fractures. Training increased from 40 to 60 miles weekly.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable point tenderness over the anteromedial tibia or lateral fibula corresponding to pain location. Single-leg hop test elicits sharp pain. Muscular flexibility normal; no calf or anterior compartment swelling. Neurovascular status intact with brisk reflexes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Initial plain radiographs may appear normal early in the injury. CT scan demonstrates focal cortical thickening and periosteal new bone formation at the fracture site without a complete fracture line. MRI shows marrow edema and endosteal thickening.

puzzles.explanation: A stress fracture is a partial break in bone cortex resulting from repetitive microtrauma that accumulates faster than the bone's remodeling capacity can repair it—particularly common in young athletes after rapid increases in training volume or intensity. The anterior tibia and fibula are classic sites in runners due to the high ground reaction forces and limited soft tissue coverage in these regions. The progressive pain with impact activities, normal imaging early on, and eventual periosteal reaction and marrow edema on advanced imaging reflect the pathologic cascade: initial microdamage triggers an inflammatory response and osteoclastic resorption, followed by compensatory new bone formation as the bone attempts repair. The 50% jump in weekly mileage on hard surfaces exemplifies the biomechanical overload that overwhelms the bone's adaptation window, making this a quintessential overuse injury in endurance athletes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 4-year-old boy is referred by his parents who note that his feet appear to have collapsed arches when standing. The child reports occasional mild foot discomfort after prolonged activity but is not significantly symptomatic or limited functionally.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child is fully ambulatory and participates in all playground activities without limitation or observable gait deviation. No pain with normal play or walking for typical childhood durations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Deformity noted since infancy with no antecedent trauma, swelling, or progressive pain. Parents have flexible flatfeet and remain fully asymptomatic and functional into adulthood.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: In standing, the medial longitudinal arch is absent bilaterally with the entire foot contacting the ground. When sitting or suspended (not weight-bearing), the arch reappears normally. No rigidity, tenderness, or toe deformities are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing X-rays of the foot show increased talocalcaneal angle and decreased talonavicular angle consistent with flexible pes planus. No tarsal coalition, abnormal bone development, or tuberosity enlargement. Arch reconstitution evident on non-weight-bearing views.

puzzles.explanation: Flexible flatfoot in children represents a normal variant of foot development, where the medial longitudinal arch collapses under weight-bearing load but reconstitutes when the foot is unloaded — a phenomenon driven by ligamentous laxity rather than structural pathology. The condition is nearly universal in infants and typically resolves naturally by age 5-8 as the plantar fascia and intrinsic foot muscles mature and strengthen. This patient's presentation is entirely characteristic: asymptomatic arch collapse in standing that corrects with non-weight-bearing positioning, a positive family history suggesting inherited ligamentous laxity, and normal functional capacity without gait deviation. The key clinical distinction is the dynamic nature of the deformity and absence of pain or rigidity — rigid or progressive flatfoot warrants investigation for tarsal coalition or neuromuscular pathology, but flexible pes planus in an asymptomatic, fully functional child requires reassurance rather than intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 51-year-old teacher reports intermittent episodes of dizziness, tinnitus, and blurred vision occurring during cervical rotation to one side. Episodes last 10-30 seconds and resolve quickly with position change.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty with overhead tasks at work, particularly sustained looking upward during classroom instruction. Symptoms worse with driving (head checks) and confined spaces where rotation is frequent. Symptom-free when reclining or neck immobilized.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Two-year history of neck pain and progressive bilateral stiffness from desk work. Motor vehicle accident one month ago with significant deceleration-type injury; initially treated conservatively. Symptoms of dizziness began insidiously 3 weeks post-accident and have progressively worsened with head movements. No prior episodes of vertigo or neurologic deficits.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical active ROM limited in rotation and extension bilaterally. Dizziness and blurred vision reliably reproduced with combined cervical extension and ipsilateral rotation (positive vertebral artery provocation test). Nystagmus present during maneuver. Upper extremity strength, reflexes, and sensation intact. No focal neurologic deficits. Postural stability normal in supine.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine MRI demonstrates osteophytic changes and disc bulging at C5-C6 and C6-C7 with foraminal stenosis and lateral recess narrowing on the right side. Vertebral artery appears patent on angiography. However, dynamic imaging (MRA with head rotation) reveals significant compression of the right vertebral artery lumen during ipsilateral rotation, consistent with extrinsic vascular compromise from osteophytes.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical arterial dysfunction occurs when degenerative changes in the cervical spine—osteophytes, disc material, or fibrotic bands—mechanically compress the vertebral or carotid arteries, particularly during neck rotation or extension. The vertebral artery is especially vulnerable as it courses through the transverse foramina, and extrinsic compression during ipsilateral rotation can suddenly reduce blood flow to the brainstem and cerebellum, causing the characteristic brief episodes of vertigo, visual disturbance, and tinnitus seen here. This presentation is distinctive because symptoms are reliably triggered by specific cervical movements and resolve with positional change, distinguishing it from central vertigo or primary vestibular disorders; the combination of osteophytic narrowing on imaging with dynamic vascular compression during provocative testing confirms the mechanical nature. Post-traumatic acceleration-deceleration injuries can accelerate degenerative changes and destabilize previously compensated stenosis, explaining the acute worsening following the motor vehicle accident in this case.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged adult presents with progressive weakness in the hands affecting fine motor tasks. The patient reports difficulty with activities including writing and buttoning clothes over the past several months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports progressive loss of grip strength and fine dexterity. Functionally limited in occupational tasks. Notably, patient remains cognitively intact and independent in self-care, with no sensory symptoms reported.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began focally in one region and gradually spread to involve other body areas. Progressive nature over 6–12 months. No significant trauma, infection history, or toxic exposures. No sensory changes reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Examination reveals asymmetric weakness predominantly in intrinsic hand muscles with visible muscle fasciculations in forearms and shoulders. Muscle bulk is reduced in affected regions. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk. Sensory examination remains intact throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Needle EMG demonstrates acute denervation potentials with fibrillations and positive sharp waves in weak muscles, alongside reduced motor unit recruitment. MRI of cervical spine and brain are structurally normal with no evidence of compression or signal abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: Motor neurone disease (MND) is a progressive neurodegenerative condition characterized by selective loss of upper and lower motor neurons, leading to progressive weakness, atrophy, and eventual paralysis while preserving cognitive function and sensation. The focal onset in the hands with gradual spread to proximal muscles, combined with the hallmark findings of visible fasciculations, brisk reflexes, and reduced muscle bulk, reflects the pattern of motor neurone degeneration seen in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), the most common adult-onset MND subtype. The EMG findings of acute denervation potentials and reduced motor recruitment, paired with normal structural imaging ruling out compression or other mimics, confirm the diagnosis of primary motor pathway dysfunction rather than a mechanical or systemic cause. This progressive distal-to-proximal pattern with preserved sensation and cognition is diagnostically characteristic and distinguishes MND from peripheral nerve or myopathic disorders.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A recreational athlete presents with localised pain in the posterior heel region, particularly where the shoe heel contacts the heel. Pain has developed gradually over recent weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot tolerate firm-heeled shoes and struggles with prolonged walking. Pain worsens significantly with activities requiring repetitive ankle plantarflexion or calf loading.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent change in training volume or shoe type triggered symptoms. No history of acute injury. Athlete reports chronic irritation from heel counter pressure, suggesting gradual mechanical irritation rather than single insult.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, swelling is palpable in the space between the Achilles tendon and calcaneus. Tenderness is elicited with direct palpation of this region. Pain is reproduced with passive plantarflexion and when the bursa is compressed against underlying bone.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging demonstrates anechoic fluid collection posterior to the calcaneus, between the Achilles tendon and bone. Bursitis is confirmed with bursal distension. MRI may show associated calcaneal bone prominence (Haglund exostosis) contributing to compression.

puzzles.explanation: Retrocalcaneal bursitis is inflammation of the bursa situated between the Achilles tendon and the calcaneus, typically triggered by repetitive mechanical irritation from shoe pressure or increased calf loading rather than acute trauma. The bursa normally allows smooth gliding of the tendon, but chronic compression—particularly from firm heel counters or training volume changes—causes fluid accumulation and pain localized to the posterior heel. The characteristic difficulty with plantarflexion-dominant activities and firm-heeled shoes reflects direct bursal compression during these movements, while imaging findings of bursal distension and associated Haglund exostosis demonstrate how bony prominences can perpetuate irritation in susceptible individuals. Management focuses on offloading the bursa through heel lift modification, activity adjustment, and addressing any underlying biomechanical drivers of repeated compression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports posterior lower back and buttock pain that worsens with sitting. Symptom onset was recent. The pain is described as localised and constant.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports inability to sit for more than 15–20 minutes without severe discomfort. Difficulty rising from a chair is pronounced. Patient resorts to standing or lying down frequently throughout the day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports no recent fall or direct blow to the buttock area. Symptom onset coincided with a new job requiring 8+ hours daily sitting. No prior episodes of similar pain. Symptoms have steadily worsened over the past 4 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation of the coccyx elicits point tenderness. Coccygeal mobilisation (gentle anteroposterior movement) reproduces the patient's familiar pain. Hip flexion and rotation are full and painless. Straight leg raise is negative bilaterally. No lower limb neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs reveal coccygeal deviation, angulation, or fracture of the coccygeal segments. Dynamic imaging in sitting and standing positions demonstrates abnormal coccygeal mobility. MRI may show associated soft tissue inflammation around the coccyx.

puzzles.explanation: Coccydynia is pain localized to the coccyx resulting from direct trauma, repetitive microtrauma, or structural abnormalities such as coccygeal deviation, angulation, or fracture. The condition is mechanically driven: sitting compresses the coccyx against the ischial tuberosities, aggravating underlying inflammation or instability, which explains why the patient here developed symptoms after transitioning to prolonged occupational sitting. Diagnosis relies on point tenderness on direct palpation combined with pain reproduction on coccygeal mobilization—neurological examination remains normal since the coccyx is well below the spinal cord termination. Imaging demonstrating coccygeal malalignment and abnormal dynamic mobility in sitting versus standing positions confirms the structural basis and helps exclude other causes of posterior pelvic pain such as piriformis syndrome or referred pain from the lumbar spine.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged to older adult reports marked fatigue and breathlessness with minimal physical exertion, significantly worse than before a recent acute cardiac event.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot walk more than one flight of stairs without severe dyspnea and chest discomfort. Previously could climb two flights without symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: ECG at time of acute event showed ST elevation in anterior leads with peak troponin elevation confirming full-thickness MI. Revascularization was performed via PCI with stent placement. Patient completed basic cardiac rehab phase but remains severely deconditioned.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Six-minute walk test: patient achieves 280 m with dyspnea at 4 minutes (distance well below predicted for age). At rest: heart rate 92 bpm, BP 138/88 mmHg. No peripheral edema. Lung bases clear.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Transthoracic echocardiogram: left ventricular ejection fraction 35% (severely reduced). Anterior wall akinesia with dyskinetic segment at apex consistent with anterior MI territory. Mild mitral regurgitation secondary to LV dilatation.

puzzles.explanation: Cardiac rehabilitation post-myocardial infarction addresses the profound deconditioning and reduced cardiac reserve that follows acute transmural injury, particularly when anterior wall involvement compromises left ventricular function and ejection fraction. The marked dyspnea on minimal exertion — struggling to climb stairs that were previously manageable — reflects both the reduced contractility of the scarred, akinetic anterior wall and the compensatory tachycardia and elevated filling pressures the weakened ventricle generates. With an ejection fraction of 35% and secondary mitral regurgitation from LV dilatation, the heart's ability to increase output during activity is severely limited, necessitating graded aerobic reconditioning, strength work, and cardiac risk factor modification to improve functional capacity and reduce arrhythmia risk. Early mobilization within safe hemodynamic limits remains critical in this phase to reverse deconditioning whilst the myocardium undergoes remodelling.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An adult reports progressive muscle weakness and fatigue that has worsened over the past few years. The patient recalls a severe neurological illness in childhood requiring hospitalization and prolonged recovery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient can no longer perform tasks requiring sustained exertion. For example, they tire rapidly with walking or repetitive arm movements, though rest does not fully resolve fatigue between days. Previously able activities now become impossible within hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient had achieved near-complete functional independence following the childhood illness, remaining stable for several decades. Worsening began insidiously 2-5 years ago without new infection, injury, or clear trigger. New joint pain and muscle cramps have also emerged.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weakness is confined to muscles originally affected by polio and is asymmetrically distributed. Fasciculations are visible in affected muscles. Sensation is intact. Cranial nerves and unaffected limbs are normal. Reflexes are brisk in healthy limbs but diminished or absent in weakened muscles.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of affected limbs demonstrates muscle atrophy in the distribution of previous poliomyelitis. Electromyography reveals abnormal motor unit action potentials consistent with chronic motor neuron loss. EMG may show evidence of denervation in formerly affected muscles.

puzzles.explanation: Post-polio syndrome (PPS) is a late-onset progressive neuromuscular disorder affecting polio survivors decades after the initial infection, resulting from degeneration of motor neurons that had compensated through terminal axonal sprouting during the original illness. The syndrome typically emerges 15–40 years after polio recovery with gradual weakness confined to previously affected muscles, exertional fatigue disproportionate to activity level, and new joint pain or cramps — features reflecting exhaustion of the sprouted motor units that had maintained functional independence for years. The asymmetric weakness, visible fasciculations in affected muscles with preserved sensation and normal cranial nerves, alongside EMG findings of chronic denervation and MRI evidence of selective muscle atrophy in the polio distribution, distinguish PPS from other motor neuron disorders and confirm recurrent motor neuron loss rather than new infection or systemic disease. No cure exists, but activity pacing, assistive devices, and strength training of unaffected muscles remain the physiotherapy cornerstones for managing progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient, often older adult, presents with brief episodes of rotatory vertigo triggered specifically by changes in head position. Nausea accompanies the episodes, and onset may be sudden or gradual.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Episodes are consistently triggered by particular head movements such as looking upward, rolling over in bed, or rapid position changes including bending down. The patient develops avoidance behaviour, limiting functional activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset is often acute, sometimes preceded by head trauma or upper respiratory viral illness, though no clear precipitant is identified in many cases. The condition may recur intermittently. Hearing changes or tinnitus may be absent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The Dix-Hallpike maneuver reproduces vertigo with a characteristic latency of 2-10 seconds. Nystagmus appears with similar delay and typically shows rotatory and vertical components. The positional nystagmus fatigues with repeated maneuvers. Supine roll test may be positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain and internal auditory canals is normal, excluding retrocochlear or central lesions. Caloric testing shows reduced vestibular function on the affected side, consistent with peripheral vestibular origin.

puzzles.explanation: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) results from displacement of otoliths (calcium carbonate crystals) within the semicircular canals of the inner ear, most commonly the posterior canal. This causes brief, intense vertigo triggered by specific head positions as the loose debris shifts within the canal, which is why the characteristic latency and fatiguing nystagmus appear on Dix-Hallpike — the nystagmus diminishes with repetition as the crystals settle. The condition typically affects older adults and may follow head trauma or viral illness, though often occurs without clear precipitant. BPPV is peripheral in origin (normal brain imaging, reduced caloric response on affected side) and remains highly treatable with canalith repositioning maneuvers, distinguishing it from central causes of positional vertigo.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete reports chest tightness and difficulty breathing that develops during or shortly after intense training sessions. Symptoms resolve quickly with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are reliably triggered by sustained high-intensity aerobic exercise, particularly in cold or dry air. The patient notes wheezing during these episodes.
