clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset correlates with competitive training intensity rather than general fitness level. The patient may have a background of allergic rhinitis, atopic dermatitis, or mild asthma. Baseline lung function is typically normal between episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting respiratory examination and lung auscultation are completely normal. Baseline spirometry is normal. However, examination performed immediately post-exercise reveals wheezing and audible breathing difficulty.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-exercise spirometry shows FEV1 decline of >12% from baseline, measured 5-10 minutes after standardised treadmill running. Methacholine bronchial challenge test is positive. Resting spirometry between episodes remains within normal limits.

puzzles.explanation: Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction (EIB) is transient airway narrowing triggered by the hyperventilation and cooling of airways that occurs during intense aerobic activity, particularly in cold or dry environments. The condition reflects a heightened bronchial responsiveness rather than underlying asthma, though it's more common in atopic individuals and those with allergic rhinitis. The hallmark presentation—normal resting lung function with acute wheezing and >12% FEV1 decline within minutes of stopping exercise—distinguishes EIB from chronic obstructive airway disease and makes post-exercise spirometry essential for diagnosis. Rapid symptom resolution with rest and the positive methacholine challenge reflect the underlying hyperresponsiveness of the airways to both osmotic stress and pharmacological stimuli.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old reports sudden onset of severe spinning sensation. Episodes last 20-30 seconds and occur unpredictably. Associated nausea but no hearing loss, tinnitus, or focal neurological symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo triggered specifically when lying back or turning over in bed. Patient avoids head movements and reports fear of bending forward. Symptoms resolve if patient remains still. No dizziness when standing or walking slowly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset 2 weeks ago with no preceding head trauma or infection. No recent medication changes. Episodes have occurred 3-4 times since onset, each identical in character. No prior vestibular history. Patient is otherwise healthy with normal hearing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Dix-Hallpike maneuver reproduces vertigo with 5-second delay. Nystagmus appears with rotatory and upbeating components, fatigues after 30-40 seconds with repeated testing. Supine roll test positive on right. Romberg and tandem stance normal. Cranial nerves intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI normal, excluding stroke or mass. Videonystagmography during Dix-Hallpike shows characteristic torsional-vertical nystagmus with delayed onset and fatigue pattern. Head impulse test normal. Caloric testing normal bilaterally.

puzzles.explanation: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) results from otolithic debris (calcium carbonate crystals) becoming dislodged within the semicircular canals of the inner ear, most commonly the posterior canal. This causes brief episodes of severe vertigo triggered by specific head positions — particularly head extension or rolling in bed — because the displaced crystals stimulate the canal's sensory receptors with movement. The characteristic 5-second latency, brief duration, and fatiguing nystagmus on repeated Dix-Hallpike testing are hallmark diagnostic features, reflecting the time needed for debris to move through endolymph and the system's rapid adaptation. The acute onset without trauma or systemic illness in this otherwise healthy 68-year-old, combined with normal imaging and normal caloric/head impulse responses, confidently excludes central causes and confirms peripheral vestibular pathology isolated to the posterior semicircular canal.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young adult presents with recurrent episodes of acute dyspnoea and chest discomfort that occur without obvious physical exertion. The patient reports sudden onset of breathing difficulty.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Episodes are often triggered by stress or anxiety-provoking situations. The patient demonstrates rapid, shallow breathing pattern. Symptoms include dizziness, perioral paresthesia, and chest tightness that resolve spontaneously.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms recur unpredictably over weeks to months. Patient has been investigated for cardiac causes with normal results. Known history of generalised anxiety disorder diagnosed 2 years ago. No recent life changes or trauma; symptoms worse during work pressure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: During observation of an acute episode: rapid respiratory rate (24-28 breaths/min), shallow breathing pattern. Manual hyperventilation for 2 minutes reproduces exact symptom complex including perioral tingling and lightheadedness. Chest auscultation clear bilaterally. Heart rate elevated but regular; blood pressure normal. No focal neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Capnography during a triggered episode shows ETCO2 of 28 mmHg (normal 35-45 mmHg), with rapid, shallow waveform. 12-lead ECG and transthoracic echocardiogram normal; troponin negative. Chest X-ray shows no acute cardiopulmonary pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Anxiety-related hyperventilation is a respiratory pattern disorder where psychological stress triggers rapid, shallow breathing that exceeds metabolic CO₂ production, leading to acute hypocapnia. The characteristic presentation—sudden dyspnoea, chest tightness, and perioral paraesthesia triggered by stress—reflects the alkalosis-induced reduction in ionised calcium and altered neuromuscular excitability rather than cardiac pathology. The diagnostic hallmark is reproduction of the entire symptom complex during controlled hyperventilation, combined with capnographic evidence of significantly reduced end-tidal CO₂, which rules out organic cardiopulmonary disease. Recognition of this pattern is crucial because reassurance, breathing retraining techniques, and anxiety management are therapeutic, whereas unnecessary cardiac investigations reinforce health anxiety and perpetuate the cycle.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old with acute onset severe lower back pain radiating into both legs. Pain began suddenly without warning. Reports significant lower limb weakness developing over hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty walking and standing. Progressive bilateral leg weakness affecting ability to climb stairs or rise from sitting. Pain worsens with spinal flexion. Able to ambulate but gait severely compromised.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of chronic intermittent lower back pain with prior imaging showing disc bulging. Heavy lifting 12 hours before symptom onset. Rapid deterioration over 6-8 hours with no improvement despite rest. No prior episodes of this severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked bilateral leg weakness: hip flexion 3/5 and knee extension 3/5 bilaterally. Sensory loss in perineal region. Diminished perianal sensation. Reduced anal sphincter tone on digital rectal examination. Patellar reflexes present but ankle reflexes diminished bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI lumbar spine: large central disc herniation at L4-L5 level compressing multiple nerve roots of the cauda equina. Significant canal stenosis with displacement and compression of the dural sac. Findings consistent with acute massive disc extrusion requiring urgent surgical intervention.

puzzles.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome results from acute compression of multiple nerve roots of the lumbar spine, typically by massive central disc herniation, and represents a neurosurgical emergency. The syndrome is clinically defined by a constellation of lower motor neuron signs — bilateral leg weakness, sensory loss in the saddle distribution (perineum and perianal region), and loss of anal sphincter tone — reflecting involvement of sacral nerve roots S2-S5, which govern bowel/bladder control and perianal sensation. The acute onset over hours following heavy lifting, combined with progressive bilateral lower limb weakness and the characteristic perineal sensory loss seen here, is pathognomonic and demands immediate imaging and decompressive surgery to prevent permanent neurological deficit and loss of continence. The large central L4-L5 disc extrusion compressing the cauda equina at the level where multiple roots converge creates this distinctive bilateral presentation that differs sharply from unilateral radiculopathy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports chest wall pain with exertion and difficulty with upper limb tasks. Pain is worse with deep breathing and coughing. Recovery is in the early weeks post-operatively.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot reach overhead or behind back. Cannot perform bed mobility independently. Fatigue limits walking to <100 metres. Activities of daily living such as dressing and hygiene are severely restricted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient underwent major chest surgery for lung pathology within the past 2–4 weeks and is currently in early post-operative rehabilitation phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Chest wall scar is firm and adherent. Scapular dyskinesis present bilaterally. Breathing pattern shallow and predominantly upper-chest; diaphragmatic excursion reduced on palpation. Intercostal muscles tender and restricted. Shoulder external rotation markedly reduced compared to internal rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows stable lung parenchyma with no pneumothorax or pleural effusion. Rib cage alignment normal. Healing of incision line is complete without evidence of dehiscence.

puzzles.explanation: Post-thoracotomy rehabilitation addresses the complex musculoskeletal and respiratory sequelae following major chest surgery, where the surgical incision through the chest wall and rib spreaders cause acute trauma to intercostal muscles, fascia, and neural structures. The characteristic presentation combines chest wall pain exacerbated by deep breathing and coughing with profound functional limitation in upper limb tasks—reflective of both direct surgical trauma and the protective guarding that accompanies chest pain. Scapular dyskinesis, restricted shoulder external rotation, and shallow breathing patterns are hallmark secondary complications: serratus anterior inhibition from long thoracic nerve irritation, pectoral tightness from scar adhesions, and diaphragmatic splinting from pain all converge to create a cycle of restricted thoracic mobility and reduced functional capacity. Early intervention targeting pain-free breathing re-education, gentle scar mobilisation, and progressive scapular and shoulder control is essential to prevent chronic stiffness and facilitate return to overhead activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged adult presents with neck pain that radiates into one arm. The pain has developed gradually or acutely following minor trauma or activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is reproducibly triggered by certain neck movements, particularly extension and ipsilateral rotation. The patient reports functional limitation with arm activities and difficulty maintaining posture.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long hours at desk work. Symptoms worse with repeated neck extension during driving or overhead tasks. No significant prior cervical pathology, but occasional stiffness before onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, neck movement is limited, particularly extension and ipsilateral side-bending. Upper extremity weakness is demonstrated in a dermatomal distribution. Diminished reflexes may be present. Spurling's test reproduces radicular pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of cervical spine demonstrates disc herniation at the affected level with nerve root compression and narrowing of the neural foramen. The herniated material displaces neural structures.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical disc herniation occurs when the nucleus pulposus breaches the annulus fibrosus and displaces into the spinal canal or neural foramen, compressing the nerve root and causing radicular pain down the affected arm. The condition typically develops insidiously with repetitive loading—particularly extension and rotation—or acutely following minor trauma, with prolonged desk work and overhead activities being common culprits in middle-aged adults. The dermatomal pattern of weakness and diminished reflexes, combined with positive Spurling's test reproducing the radicular pain, reflects mechanical compression of the exiting nerve root at the level of herniation. MRI confirmation of disc material displacing neural structures and narrowing the intervertebral foramen distinguishes this from other causes of cervical radiculopathy, making it the most treatable mechanical variant when conservative management is insufficient.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old male presents with acute onset unilateral lower leg weakness and pain. Patient reports difficulty pushing off with the affected foot and burning sensation along the sole. Symptoms developed over 3–5 days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk on tiptoes or push foot downward against resistance. Difficulty climbing stairs and walking on uneven surfaces. Gait appears flat-footed on affected side. Pain and weakness localized to calf and foot sole.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 3 weeks after knee arthroscopy for meniscal repair. No regional anesthesia or nerve blocks administered. No prior diabetes, neuropathy, or trauma. No improvement with rest or conservative measures over past 2 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Plantarflexion strength reduced to 2/5 on affected side; inversion weakness also present. Dorsiflexion and eversion intact at 5/5. Sensory loss in sole and medial foot distribution. Calf muscle bulk diminished compared to contralateral side. Reflexes normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows focal hypoechoic swelling of tibial nerve in proximal lower leg posterior to tibia. Nerve conduction studies demonstrate delayed motor latency to ankle plantarflexors and absent sensory responses in medial plantar nerve. EMG shows acute denervation in soleus and flexor digitorum longus.

puzzles.explanation: Tibial nerve palsy results from compression or injury to the tibial nerve as it courses through the posterior compartment of the leg, most commonly occurring at the level of the popliteal fossa or as it passes behind the medial tibial plateau. The nerve's motor branches supply the plantarflexors and invertors of the foot, so compression produces characteristic weakness in push-off and inversion, along with sensory loss in the sole and medial foot — the clinical picture seen in this post-arthroscopy presentation. Iatrogenic injury during knee surgery, particularly from retraction trauma or haematoma formation in the posterior compartment, is a recognized complication that typically declares itself over days to weeks. Ultrasound evidence of focal nerve swelling, delayed motor latencies on nerve conduction studies, and acute denervation potentials in soleus confirm the diagnosis and localize the lesion to the proximal tibial nerve.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old reports persistent pain and stiffness in left lower leg since motor vehicle accident 4 months ago. Pain intensity varies unpredictably day-to-day despite consistent activity levels. Reports poor sleep quality and feeling 'on edge' most days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids weight-bearing on left leg when possible. Refuses to sit in car or return to driving. Difficulty with stairs and standing balance. Reports anxiety with any activity involving left leg—pain worsens when attempting previously injured movements, even light stretching.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Left tibia fracture from MVC; surgery performed and wound healed 2 months post-injury. Baseline ROM and strength now adequate for function. However, patient reports exaggerated fear of re-injury, avoidance of rehabilitation progression, and intrusive memories triggered by rehabilitation clinic visits or vehicles.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left lower leg shows marked muscle guarding on passive ROM; defensive bracing present. Heart rate elevation noted during left leg handling. ROM, strength, and sensation objectively intact and functional. Disproportionate pain behavior during testing—catastrophizing language, excessive fear-avoidance responses, visible distress unrelated to actual tissue pathology.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT imaging: healed left tibia fracture with minimal residual gap, no active inflammation. Mild post-traumatic osteoarthritis at fracture site—severity does not explain current pain or functional limitation. Psychological assessment (PCL-5) elevated; symptoms consistent with trauma-related hyperarousal, re-experiencing, and avoidance behavior interfering with physical recovery.

puzzles.explanation: Post-traumatic stress disorder in the context of physical rehabilitation occurs when trauma-related psychological symptoms—hyperarousal, re-experiencing, and avoidance—become the primary barrier to recovery from a healed physical injury. In this case, the tibia fracture has consolidated with minimal residual pathology, yet persistent pain and movement dysfunction are driven by fear-avoidance and catastrophic thinking rather than active tissue damage. The constellation of intrusive trauma memories triggered by rehabilitation settings, exaggerated startle responses during examination, and disproportionate fear of re-injury despite intact strength and sensation exemplifies how the nervous system remains sensitized to threat following major trauma. Effective rehabilitation requires integrated trauma-informed physiotherapy that addresses both the hypervigilant autonomic state and graded, confidence-building exposure to previously avoided movements, rather than focusing solely on the now-healed structural injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A school-aged child presents with hip pain or limp that has developed insidiously. The pain may radiate to the knee, and the child demonstrates functional limitation with walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child reports pain with hip abduction and internal rotation movements. Difficulty with climbing stairs, getting in/out of car seats, and sitting cross-legged. Antalgic gait is visible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset over weeks to months. Male child. No preceding trauma, infection, or surgery. Parents report gradual onset of limp without clear cause. No systemic illness or relevant medical history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, hip internal rotation and abduction are limited. The Trendelenburg sign is positive. Hip pain is elicited with internal rotation. Muscle weakness and atrophy may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate femoral head flattening, fragmentation, or widening of the joint space. MRI shows femoral head necrosis and loss of epiphyseal blood supply. Sequential imaging documents progressive changes.

puzzles.explanation: Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease is an idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head epiphysis occurring in children aged 4–8 years, predominantly boys, caused by transient disruption of the blood supply to the growing epiphysis. The condition progresses through predictable stages: initial necrosis, fragmentation with femoral head flattening, and eventual reossification, during which the compromised bone becomes mechanically vulnerable. The insidious limp, pain radiating to the knee, and positive Trendelenburg sign reflect hip abductor weakness and loss of normal femoral head mechanics; internal rotation and abduction are particularly limited because these movements stress the already-weakened epiphysis. Management focuses on containment strategies—keeping the femoral head centred within the acetabulum during the fragmentation phase—to optimize the shape of the reossifying head and reduce the risk of long-term osteoarthritis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged adult presents with pain in the region behind the ankle and on the sole of the foot. The pain has developed gradually and is reproducible with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by prolonged standing or walking, particularly in shoes that compress the medial ankle. The patient experiences pain relief with rest and elevation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No significant trauma history. Symptom onset correlates with increased activity, standing tolerance, or recent ankle swelling from unrelated cause. No prior ankle surgery or chronic ankle instability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited with palpation posterior to the medial malleolus. The Tinel sign is positive at the tarsal tunnel. Pain is reproduced with passive foot eversion. Sensory changes may be present on the sole of the foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows flexor retinaculum thickening and posterior tibial nerve compression at tarsal tunnel level with associated nerve edema and T2 signal hyperintensity. Ultrasound may show reduced nerve mobility with ankle plantarflexion.

puzzles.explanation: Tarsal tunnel syndrome results from compression of the posterior tibial nerve as it passes beneath the flexor retinaculum behind the medial malleolus, typically from tenosynovial thickening, space-occupying lesions, or chronic ankle swelling. The condition presents with pain radiating from the medial ankle to the sole of the foot, often worsening with prolonged standing or activity and improving with rest and elevation — a pattern that reflects the dynamic nature of nerve compression during weight-bearing. The positive Tinel sign at the tarsal tunnel combined with sensory changes in the medial and lateral plantar distributions (sole of foot) makes this distinct from ankle pain arising from ligamentous or articular sources. Like carpal tunnel at the wrist, tarsal tunnel syndrome is primarily a compression neuropathy, and MRI findings of flexor retinaculum thickening and nerve edema confirm the structural basis of symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged adult presents with pain in the anterior shoulder region at the shoulder joint. The pain develops during shoulder movements or activities requiring arm lifting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by overhead activities and activities involving repetitive elbow flexion. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with lifting or reaching activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has no significant trauma history. Pain has developed gradually with overhead activities. Recent increase in activity level may be contributory.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the bicipital groove. Speed's test reproduces anterior shoulder pain. Yergason's test is positive with pain in the bicipital groove. Shoulder range of motion is usually preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes within the tendon, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation. Tendon calibre abnormalities are evident.

puzzles.explanation: Biceps tendinopathy is a degenerative or inflammatory condition of the long head of the biceps tendon, typically developing from repetitive overhead loading or sudden activity increases that exceed the tendon's tolerance. The long head originates at the superior glenoid labrum and travels through the bicipital groove, making it vulnerable to friction and compressive loads during overhead work and elbow flexion activities. Biceps tendinopathy presents with anterior shoulder pain that reproduces on overhead tasks and loading tests like Speed's and Yergason's, with tenderness localized to the bicipital groove itself — a distinguishing feature that separates it from other shoulder pathologies. Ultrasound and MRI reveal tendon thickening, hypoechoic signal changes, and inflammation within the substance of the tendon, reflecting underlying degeneration rather than acute tear, which guides conservative management focused on load management and progressive tendon conditioning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An older adult presents with chronic neck pain and progressive stiffness. The patient reports difficulty with neck movement and occasional radiation into the arm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient struggles with overhead reaching and turning the head while driving. Prolonged reading or computer work triggers exacerbations. Symptoms are inconsistent day-to-day but generally worse with cooler weather or after poor sleep.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Neck pain began in the patient's 50s after years of desk work. Symptoms have slowly worsened over the past decade. The patient has had 2–3 episodes of sudden sharp pain with arm tingling that resolved within weeks. No significant trauma or whiplash history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical extension is severely limited (10–15° loss). Lateral rotation to both sides is restricted. Upper limb strength is 5/5 throughout. Grip strength is mildly reduced and asymmetrical. Walking shows no ataxia. Reflexes are brisk in the lower limbs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates multilevel degenerative changes including osteophytes, disc space narrowing, and spinal canal stenosis at C5–C6 and C6–C7 levels. No cord compression or T2 signal changes.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical spondylosis is a degenerative condition of the cervical spine characterised by osteophyte formation, disc degeneration, and ligamentous thickening that narrows the spinal canal and neural foramina. It develops insidiously over years, particularly in those with prolonged static postures like desk work, and typically becomes symptomatic in the fifth to seventh decades. The restricted extension and rotation seen here, combined with intermittent radicular symptoms and mild grip weakness, reflect mechanical irritation of nerve roots at multiple levels—the multilevel stenosis at C5–C6 and C6–C7 on imaging explains the variable day-to-day presentation as positional changes alter foraminal compromise. Importantly, the absence of myelopathic signs (preserved strength, no ataxia, no cord signal changes) indicates nerve root involvement rather than cord compression, distinguishing this from the more serious myelopathic variant.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old retired businessman reports chronic, progressively worsening pain in multiple joints. Onset was insidious over decades. He experiences stiffness that partially improves with activity and temporary relief with heat and anti-inflammatories.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot walk more than short distances or climb stairs without significant pain and fatigue. Overhead reaching (e.g., getting items from shelves) is severely restricted. Symptoms are worst at end of day after activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 30-year history of knee pain from an old soccer injury and reports that both parents developed arthritis in their 60s. He is sedentary post-retirement with minimal exercise. No systemic inflammatory symptoms, fever, or recent weight loss.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral knee crepitus with palpable osteophytes at joint lines. Knee flexion limited to 100 degrees on one side. Hip internal rotation painful and limited (20 degrees). Glenohumeral abduction limited to 120 degrees bilaterally with scapular compensation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of knees, hips, and shoulders show classic osteoarthritis findings: joint space narrowing, marginal osteophytes, and subchondral sclerosis. No erosions or inflammatory changes. Serum inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) are normal.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease characterized by progressive loss of articular cartilage, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes, typically developing over decades in weight-bearing and high-mobility joints. This condition results from a combination of mechanical wear (particularly in joints with prior injury, as this patient's history of knee trauma demonstrates), genetic predisposition, reduced activity levels, and age-related changes in cartilage resilience. The classic presentation of insidious polyarticular involvement with morning stiffness that improves with activity, end-of-day pain exacerbation, and mechanical symptoms like crepitus and range-of-motion loss reflects the structural joint damage visible on imaging. The absence of systemic inflammatory markers and erosive changes distinguishes osteoarthritis from rheumatoid arthritis, making it a purely mechanical disorder requiring load management and activity modification as primary interventions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with insidious midfoot pain localised to the arch. Pain develops during or worsens with repetitive impact activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports pain is worse with repetitive impact and high mileage. Able to walk slowly on flat ground but cannot jog or run. Pain increases with each subsequent day of training.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training distance or intensity coincides with symptom onset. Female athletes with amenorrhea are at increased risk. No antecedent trauma or fall.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, midfoot is tender to palpation over the arch. Pain reproduced with resisted plantarflexion and inversion. Mild swelling noted over midfoot. Single-leg stance is painful but tolerated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows bone marrow edema and periosteal reaction in the midfoot tarsal navicular region. CT or radiographs reveal stress fracture line with early callus formation.

puzzles.explanation: Navicular stress fractures result from repetitive compressive and shear loading through the midfoot during high-impact activities, most common in distance runners and jumping athletes. The navicular's precarious blood supply — particularly in its central third — makes it especially vulnerable to stress injury when training volume escalates beyond tissue tolerance. Classically, these fractures present with insidious arch pain that worsens progressively with continued activity, and may accompany female athlete triad factors like amenorrhea that compromise bone density. The imaging findings of bone marrow edema with early callus formation confirm the stress fracture in situ, and the characteristic tenderness over the dorsal midfoot combined with pain on plantarflexion-inversion loading help differentiate this from other arch pathologies; early recognition is critical, as continued weightbearing risks displacement and prolonged disability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young to middle-aged adult with shoulder pain and a catching or clunking sensation during arm movement. Symptoms are intermittent and activity-related.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform overhead reaching or throwing without increased symptoms. Pain is reproducible with specific arm positions rather than constant throughout the day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset over months with repetitive overhead loading, or acute onset following a prior shoulder dislocation or subluxation event. No recent direct trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the crank test, anterior slide test, or O'Brien's test reproduces symptoms. Apprehension and relocation signs may be present. Weakness in external rotation may be demonstrated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging with intra-articular contrast demonstrates labral tear with characterisation of tear location and size. MRI confirms association with rotator cuff involvement or instability.

puzzles.explanation: A shoulder labral tear involves disruption of the fibrocartilaginous labrum that deepens the glenoidal socket and anchors the biceps tendon, typically occurring from repetitive overhead loading in throwing athletes or following instability events. The labrum's role in shoulder stability and proprioceptive feedback means that tears produce the characteristic mechanical symptoms—catching, clunking, and pain reproducible in specific arm positions—rather than constant discomfort. Superior labral tears (SLAP lesions) are especially common with overhead activities and may coexist with rotator cuff pathology or anteroinferior labral damage in post-dislocation cases, as demonstrated by the combination of positive O'Brien's and apprehension testing. MRI with intra-articular contrast remains the diagnostic standard, revealing both the tear morphology and any associated capsular or rotator cuff involvement that guides rehabilitation intensity and prognosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old runner reports lateral knee pain that begins during training sessions and persists for several hours afterward.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot complete long-distance running (>5km). Pain worsens with downhill running and does not occur during cycling or swimming.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in running distance or intensity has coincided with symptom onset. The patient may have underlying biomechanical abnormalities including hip weakness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the Ober test demonstrates hip flexor and IT band tightness. Tenderness is palpable over the lateral epicondyle of the femur. The Noble compression test reproduces pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates IT band thickening and bursal fluid collection. MRI shows bursal inflammation and IT band signal changes. Imaging confirms inflammation at the distal IT band.

puzzles.explanation: Iliotibial band syndrome results from inflammation of the IT band and underlying bursa at the distal femur, typically triggered by repetitive knee flexion-extension loading combined with underlying hip weakness or biomechanical dysfunction. The condition is highly prevalent in runners because repetitive running cycles subject the relatively inelastic IT band to chronic friction against the lateral femoral epicondyle, particularly during downhill running where eccentric loading intensifies. The characteristic lateral knee pain that emerges mid-training and persists afterward, combined with activity-specific aggravation (running but not cycling), reflects the mechanical nature of the irritation. Hip weakness and IT band tightness create a biomechanical cascade that perpetuates the problem — the Ober test, Noble compression test, and imaging evidence of bursal inflammation and IT band thickening confirm the diagnosis and highlight why proximal hip strengthening forms the cornerstone of management alongside load modification.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old office worker complains of progressive anterior knee pain over the past 3 months. The pain is dull and aching, triggered by prolonged sitting and stair climbing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activities involving repetitive knee flexion-extension—particularly running on inclined surfaces—trigger sharp catching sensations that force the patient to stop. The pain improves with rest and ice application, but intensifies when resuming the same activity pattern within 24 hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient recalled a hyperextension mechanism during a hiking trip 4 months ago, though initial symptoms were mild. She reports a history of anterior knee pain in her early 20s that resolved with conservative management. Recent increased walking duration in her job has coincided with symptom escalation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, point tenderness is elicited along the medial femoral condyle at the level of the joint line when the knee is flexed 30 degrees. McMurray's test is negative and there is no effusion. Patellar tracking and ligamentous stability are intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates a thickened and fibrotic medial synovial plica measuring 4mm in thickness with adjacent signal changes in the articular cartilage of the medial femoral condyle. No meniscal pathology or intra-articular loose bodies are identified.

puzzles.explanation: Plica syndrome occurs when a embryologic synovial fold (plica) becomes thickened, fibrotic, and irritated, typically the medial plica, causing it to impinge on the adjacent articular cartilage during knee flexion and extension. The condition classically presents in office workers and athletes with repetitive knee motion, producing the characteristic catching sensation and medial joint line tenderness that emerges gradually after minor trauma or activity escalation. The hyperextension mechanism and subsequent increased walking demands in this case likely triggered synovial inflammation and plica thickening, leading to mechanical irritation of the medial femoral condyle articular surface visible on imaging. Unlike meniscal pathology, plica syndrome presents with negative McMurray's testing and normal ligamentous stability, making the combination of medial joint line tenderness at 30° flexion, negative meniscal signs, and MRI confirmation of plica thickening with cartilage changes the diagnostic hallmark.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An adult with type 2 diabetes presents with a foot wound that is not healing well. The patient reports the wound is painless.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty walking on the affected foot and cannot tolerate normal shoes. Gait is antalgic or avoidant.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing diabetes with suboptimal glycemic control. Patient reports numbness in the feet. The ulcer appeared insidiously without recalled trauma or injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, a full-thickness ulcer is present, often plantar surface. Loss of protective sensation is demonstrated with monofilament testing. Surrounding erythema may be absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show no bony erosion or osteomyelitis. Soft tissue depth and extent of ulcer are confirmed on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Diabetic foot ulcers result from the confluence of neuropathy, ischemia, and repetitive mechanical trauma in the setting of chronic hyperglycemia — a triad that overwhelms normal tissue repair. Sensory loss from distal symmetrical polyneuropathy removes the protective warning system for injury, allowing unnoticed pressure or minor trauma to evolve into full-thickness wounds; the painless presentation and insidious onset reflect this neuropathic component. The plantar surface location is characteristic, as peak pressure zones under the metatarsal heads and heel bear weight repetitively throughout the day. Suboptimal glycemic control impairs neutrophil function and collagen synthesis, slowing healing and increasing infection risk; absent surrounding erythema does not exclude deep infection. Early recognition of protective sensation loss via monofilament testing remains the cornerstone of prevention before ulceration occurs.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Overhead athlete with insidious-onset shoulder pain during throwing or pressing movements. Pain worsens with continued activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to sustain loaded overhead positions or throwing without compensatory shoulder hiking or trunk rotation. Cannot perform push-ups without scapular winging.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began after returning to sport following a prior rotator cuff strain 6 months ago. Pain initially resolved but recurred when resuming full training. No significant trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Scapular positioning abnormal with altered kinematics during arm elevation. Weakness noted in lower trapezius and serratus anterior. Rotator cuff strength grossly intact. Impingement signs equivocal or negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows supraspinatus tendinopathy without full-thickness tear. No labral pathology. Plain films normal. Imaging findings support functional scapular origin of symptoms rather than primary rotator cuff tear.

puzzles.explanation: Scapular dyskinesis is a kinetic chain disorder characterized by abnormal scapular positioning and movement patterns during arm elevation, typically resulting from weakness or imbalance in the scapular stabilizers — particularly the lower trapezius and serratus anterior. In overhead athletes, this dyskinesis causes the scapula to fail to adequately upwardly rotate and externally rotate during arm elevation, leading to compensatory shoulder hiking, altered glenohumeral mechanics, and secondary impingement of the rotator cuff tendons. The presentation here — insidious onset after a prior rotator cuff strain with inadequate neuromuscular re-education, combined with clear scapular winging on examination and intact rotator cuff strength — is classic for a functional scapular origin masquerading as primary cuff pathology. Addressing the underlying scapular control deficits through targeted strengthening of the lower trapezius and serratus anterior, rather than focusing on the rotator cuff alone, is essential for return to throwing and overhead activity without recurrence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old presenting with gradual onset of wrist pain over 6 months. Pain is activity-related, worse with repetitive hand use. No history of trauma or acute injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with sustained gripping and fine motor control. Symptoms aggravated by prolonged keyboard use, writing, and repetitive twisting motions. Pain eases with rest and ice.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Works in data entry, 8+ hours daily at keyboard for past 2 years. Symptoms began 6 months ago and have gradually worsened despite activity modification. No prior wrist injury. No systemic inflammatory conditions or rheumatologic history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized swelling and tenderness over dorsal or volar wrist. Pain reproduced with resisted wrist extension/flexion. Full range of motion preserved but end-range movement painful. No neurological deficit or instability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows focal tendon thickening with hypoechoic intrasubstance changes and surrounding fluid. MRI confirms signal abnormality within the tendon consistent with degeneration and reactive inflammation. No tendon rupture.

puzzles.explanation: Wrist tendinopathy represents cumulative microtrauma to the wrist's flexor or extensor tendons, typically developing insidiously from repetitive loading rather than acute injury—in this case, eight hours daily of keyboard use gradually overwhelmed the tendon's adaptive capacity. The hallmark presentation of activity-related pain that worsens with gripping and repetitive motions but improves with rest reflects the inflammatory and degenerative changes within the tendon itself, as confirmed by the ultrasound findings of thickening and hypoechoic changes alongside reactive fluid accumulation. Unlike nerve compression syndromes, tendinopathy preserves neurological function and produces pain purely with resistance to the affected muscle-tendon action across its range, making resisted wrist extension or flexion the key reproducing test. Early recognition and load management—modifying the aggravating activity, targeted eccentric strengthening, and anti-inflammatory strategies—are critical to halting progression, as chronic degenerative changes become increasingly resistant to conservative treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young athlete with hip pain during or after sport. Pain is intermittent and activity-dependent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform high-intensity movements—running, kicking, or cutting activities provoke symptoms. Pain worsens with repetitive hip motion over time.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over weeks of increased training. Patient reports tightness in hip flexors and lateral hip. No prior hip injury or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Snapping elicited on hip flexion–adduction test or during single-leg stance. Pain reproduced consistently. Marked limitation in hip internal rotation; iliopsoas and TFL tightness on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI demonstrates the anatomical cause, including iliotibial band snapping, iliopsoas snapping, or intra-articular loose body. Dynamic ultrasound may demonstrate the snap.

puzzles.explanation: Snapping hip syndrome occurs when soft tissue structures—most commonly the iliotibial band, iliopsoas tendon, or less frequently intra-articular loose bodies—catch and release across bony prominences during hip motion, creating an audible or palpable snap. The condition is prevalent in young, active individuals performing repetitive high-intensity movements, particularly those involving hip flexion-adduction combined with internal rotation, as the biomechanical demands of running and cutting expose the underlying tightness in the iliopsoas and tensor fasciae latae. The intermittent, activity-dependent nature and marked restriction in internal rotation reflect the chronic adaptive shortening of hip flexor musculature exacerbated by training load. Dynamic ultrasound imaging capturing the snap in real-time remains the most confirmatory tool, enabling differentiation between the intra-articular and extra-articular variants and guiding targeted soft tissue release and hip mobility restoration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports progressive shin pain that worsens with activity. Pain began gradually over the past 2–3 weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports sharp, localized pain along the medial tibia that prevents completion of running workouts. Walking and non-impact activities are pain-free.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in running distance or intensity coincides with symptom onset. The patient may have underlying biomechanical abnormalities or training errors.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked focal tenderness over anteromedial tibia at a discrete site. Percussion (tap test) over the area elicits sharp pain. Hip and ankle ROM normal. Single-leg hop test not performed due to pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may be normal initially, or show callus formation at chronic stage. MRI demonstrates bone stress with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema. MRI confirms diagnosis when radiographs are negative.

puzzles.explanation: A tibial stress fracture results from repetitive micro-trauma to bone that exceeds its capacity to remodel, typically occurring in runners who rapidly increase volume or intensity without adequate recovery. The medial tibia is particularly vulnerable due to high ground reaction forces and the stress concentration at the posteromedial border, where the soleus and tibialis posterior originate. The hallmark presentation—sharp, activity-dependent pain at a discrete site with focal bone tenderness and a positive percussion test—reflects the underlying periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema visible on MRI, often before callus appears on plain radiographs. Early recognition and relative rest are critical; continuing high-impact loading risks progression to complete fracture.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 46-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of burning forefoot pain localized to the third web space that have occurred intermittently over 2 years and are now becoming more frequent. She describes sharp, shooting pain radiating into the adjacent toe surfaces.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by prolonged walking and constrictive footwear, particularly high heels. Symptoms resolve with rest and removing shoes. She has significantly curtailed walking distance and modified her shoe choices to manage symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain onset correlates with increased use of narrow, high-heeled footwear. No prior foot surgery or significant trauma. Symptoms have progressively worsened over the past 6 months despite conservative footwear modifications.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation of the third intermetatarsal space elicits sharp pain with reproduction of her typical symptoms. Mulder's click is readily demonstrated when dorsiplantar pressure is applied while squeezing the metatarsals transversely. A palpable click or nodule may be felt in the web space. Two-point discrimination is reduced in the third and fourth toe web space region compared to other forefoot areas.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates a hypoechoic mass in the third intermetatarsal space with characteristic appearance. MRI shows a T2-hyperintense lesion between the third and fourth metatarsal heads, confirming neural tissue enlargement consistent with neuroma. No evidence of metatarsalgia or other mass lesions.

puzzles.explanation: Morton's neuroma is a benign perineural fibrosis of the interdigital nerve, most commonly affecting the third web space where the nerve is anatomically tethered and subjected to maximal compression between metatarsal heads. The condition develops insidiously in response to repetitive compression and traction forces, particularly from prolonged narrow footwear and high heels, leading to nerve edema and fibrous proliferation rather than a true tumor. The characteristic presentation—sharp, burning pain radiating into adjacent toes triggered by walking and relieved by shoe removal—reflects the mechanical irritation of an enlarged, ischemic nerve tissue. Mulder's click (a palpable or audible snap during transverse metatarsal compression) is pathognomonic, resulting from the neuroma subluxating within the intermetatarsal space, and when combined with reduced two-point discrimination and imaging confirmation of the T2-hyperintense lesion, it reliably distinguishes Morton's neuroma from other sources of forefoot pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete, typically involved in endurance sports, reports chest tightness and difficulty breathing that develops during or immediately after high-intensity exercise. Symptoms are exercise-dependent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient can maintain only light activity without symptoms; attempts at sustained running or competitive training consistently trigger respiratory distress within 5-10 minutes. Symptoms resolve completely within 15-30 minutes of rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset correlates with competitive training intensity rather than general fitness level. The patient may have a background of allergic rhinitis, atopic dermatitis, or mild asthma. Baseline lung function is typically normal between episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting physical examination unremarkable: normal auscultation, no baseline wheeze or crackles, normal peak flow at rest. Post-exercise clinical assessment reveals audible wheeze and apparent increased work of breathing within 5 minutes of stopping exercise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Methacholine or exercise challenge testing confirms bronchial hyperresponsiveness. Repeat spirometry 10 minutes after exercise re-challenge shows no FEV1 recovery, ruling out simple exercise-induced dyspnea from deconditioning.

puzzles.explanation: Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction (EIB) is a transient narrowing of the airways triggered by intense physical exertion, particularly in endurance athletes, and stems from airway cooling and drying during high ventilatory demands rather than true asthma. The condition is clinically distinctive because symptoms emerge specifically during or immediately after vigorous exercise—resolving within 15-30 minutes of rest—while baseline lung function and resting examination remain completely normal, distinguishing it from persistent asthma. The presence of atopic features (allergic rhinitis, atopic dermatitis) and positive methacholine challenge testing reflect the underlying airway hyperresponsiveness that characterises this condition, though FEV₁ recovery patterns help confirm the exercise-triggered mechanism. Management typically involves pre-exercise beta-2 agonists or leukotriene inhibitors, allowing athletes to train and compete without airway limitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports knee pain, swelling, and difficulty with mobility. Onset was acute following a surgical procedure several weeks to months ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot climb stairs without rail support and has marked difficulty with walking on uneven surfaces. Unable to achieve full knee extension during gait cycle.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Surgery was performed for end-stage joint disease secondary to osteoarthritis or old trauma. Patient is 4–8 weeks post-operatively and attending outpatient physiotherapy three times per week.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, knee extension deficit is present with measurable lag. Knee flexion is restricted compared to goal. Mild to moderate swelling is evident. Quadriceps strength is reduced.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show prosthetic components in neutral alignment with intact bone-cement interface and no loose bodies. No evidence of component malpositioning or early loosening.

puzzles.explanation: Total knee replacement (TKR) involves resurfacing the damaged articular surfaces of the tibiofemoral joint with prosthetic components, typically performed for end-stage osteoarthritis or post-traumatic arthropathy when conservative management has failed. The immediate post-operative period (4–8 weeks) is characterized by significant tissue trauma, inflammation, and quadriceps inhibition—a reflex suppression of quadriceps activation that contributes to extension lag and functional limitations like stair climbing and uneven terrain navigation. Early mobilization and aggressive physiotherapy are essential during this window to restore knee range of motion, regain quadriceps strength and neuromuscular control, and prevent arthrofibrosis; the swelling and restricted flexion seen here reflect normal post-operative inflammation, but intensive therapy can significantly improve these parameters by 12 weeks. With well-aligned prosthetic components and an intact bone-cement interface, the prognosis for functional recovery is favorable, provided rehabilitation progresses appropriately.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports shoulder pain following trauma with mechanism and severity varying. Pain is present at rest and with movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient guards the shoulder and cannot use the arm for any functional task. Unable to reach across body or elevate arm even passively. Severe guarding suggests significant structural injury rather than simple muscle strain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred from high-impact mechanism: motor vehicle collision, fall from height, or direct blow to posterior/lateral shoulder. No prior shoulder pathology or fracture history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Crepitus palpable over posterior shoulder. Scapular winging absent. Marked swelling and ecchymosis over scapular region. Neurovascular exam intact. Clavicle and acromion non-tender; pain localised to scapular body or borders.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate scapular fracture with characterisation of fragment location and displacement. CT may be performed for better characterisation of complex fractures.

puzzles.explanation: Scapular fractures result from high-energy trauma — such as motor vehicle collisions or falls from height — that imparts significant force directly to the posterior shoulder girdle. These injuries are uncommon but serious, often indicating substantial force delivery to the scapula itself (rather than the more frequently fractured clavicle or proximal humerus). The characteristic presentation here — severe pain with guarding, marked swelling and ecchymosis over the scapular region, and palpable crepitus localized to the scapular body — reflects the traumatic insult to this broad, muscular bone. Most scapular fractures are managed conservatively with early mobilization once acute pain settles, though complex or displaced fractures may require CT imaging and careful neurovascular monitoring given the proximity to vital structures.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with acute onset foot pain following a push-off injury during sport. Pain is worse with weight-bearing through the forefoot.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform plantarflexion activities. Difficulty with walking lunges, jumping, or any activity requiring forceful push-off with the forefoot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during a football match when player planted foot and fell forward, forcing the big toe into hyperextension. Immediate swelling and inability to bear weight.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Plantarflexion of the first MTP joint reproduces sharp pain. Passive dorsiflexion is guarded. Turf sign present — hallux in plantarflexion at rest due to pain inhibition.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs exclude fracture or dislocation of the first MTP joint. MRI demonstrates soft tissue injury including ligament and cartilage involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Turf toe is a hyperextension injury of the first metatarsophalangeal joint, typically occurring when an athlete plants their foot and is forced into sudden dorsiflexion — a mechanism common in football and other push-off sports. The injury involves varying degrees of ligamentous and capsular disruption, with potential cartilage involvement that distinguishes it from simple ligament sprains. The characteristic presentation of acute forefoot pain exacerbated by plantarflexion activities and push-off movements reflects the critical load-bearing role of the first MTP joint in propulsion; the guarded dorsiflexion and plantarflexed resting posture observed here are protective responses to pain. While radiographs may appear normal, advanced imaging reveals the soft tissue damage, and recovery depends heavily on early protection and controlled mobilization to prevent chronic stiffness and functional limitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Overhead athlete with insidious-onset shoulder pain exacerbated by repetitive upper limb activity and overhead movements. Pain develops gradually over several weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to complete full training sessions; must reduce volume and intensity. Pain particularly severe in the late stages of extended training sets.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-volume repetitive overhead sport, particularly swimming with intensive training programme. Symptom onset correlates with increased training volume or intensity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive impingement signs are demonstrated. Rotator cuff weakness, particularly supraspinatus, is evident. Scapular dyskinesis may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI demonstrates rotator cuff tendinopathy or partial tear, often affecting the supraspinatus. Associated subacromial inflammation is evident.

puzzles.explanation: Swimmer's shoulder is a chronic rotator cuff pathology arising from repetitive overhead loading and inadequate scapular control during the high-velocity, high-volume demands of competitive swimming. The supraspinatus bears the brunt of this loading, particularly during the late pull-through phase of the stroke, leading to progressive tendinopathy and inflammation in the subacromial space. The insidious nature and correlation with training volume increases reflect cumulative microtrauma rather than acute injury, while scapular dyskinesis and rotator cuff weakness compound the problem by allowing excessive glenohumeral translation and impingement. Unlike acute impingement, swimmer's shoulder develops over weeks to months of sustained activity modification and often requires a structured reduction in training volume alongside scapular stabilisation work to offload the injured rotator cuff.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman with moderate heel pain history presents with acute forefoot pain beneath the second metatarsal head. The pain began suddenly 2 weeks ago after an awkward step forward during yoga, and is described as sharp and stabbing when weightbearing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Ambulation, particularly on hard surfaces, reproduces severe pain that worsens with each step. The patient notes that the second toe drifts into extension relative to the other toes during standing. Walking barefoot is markedly worse than when wearing supportive footwear with a rocker sole.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 5-year history of inflammatory arthropathy affecting the small joints of her feet and hands. She denies previous foot trauma but recalls 18 months of progressive swelling in the second metatarsophalangeal joint. Predisposing factors include pes planus and high training volume in recreational activities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpable tenderness is localized to the plantar aspect of the second metatarsal head with pain reproduction during the vertical metatarsophalangeal stress test. The drawer test of the second metatarsophalangeal joint demonstrates dorsal translation greater than 4mm compared to the contralateral side, suggesting ligamentous insufficiency. Subtle dorsal swelling at the metatarsophalangeal joint is noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the second metatarsophalangeal joint reveals a full-thickness tear of the plantar plate with hypoechoic edema in the deep structures. MRI confirms disruption of the plantar plate ligament fibers with associated joint effusion and synovitis, and secondary changes of dorsal metatarsal head subluxation.

puzzles.explanation: A plantar plate tear is a disruption of the fibrocartilaginous ligament complex on the plantar surface of the metatarsophalangeal joint, which normally provides dynamic stability and prevents dorsal translation of the proximal phalanx. The condition typically results from acute hyperextension injury (as from an awkward weightbearing step) or chronic attenuation from repetitive stress, inflammatory arthropathy, or structural predisposition such as pes planus. The characteristic dorsal drift of the affected toe and focal plantarmetatarsal head tenderness reflect the loss of restraint to dorsal migration of the phalanx; pain worsens with weightbearing on hard surfaces because the plantar plate bears compressive and translational loads with each step. The positive drawer test demonstrating >4mm dorsal translation is the key clinical correlate of ligamentous insufficiency, distinguishing plantar plate pathology from simple metatarsalgia or joint inflammation alone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old patient with acute onset headache, dizziness, and difficulty concentrating. Reports feeling mentally 'cloudy' and slowed down. Symptoms began yesterday without prior warning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to focus on reading or screen time without worsening headache. Balance feels unsteady, especially with head turns. Noise and bright lights increase symptoms noticeably.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms started immediately after a fall with head contact 24 hours ago. Patient does not recall the exact moment of impact. No loss of consciousness witnessed, but felt momentarily dazed. Has experienced similar symptom clusters twice before—4 years ago and 2 years ago—following separate head injuries, both resolved within weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gait and balance assessment shows mild postural instability. Rapid head movement testing reveals dizziness on rightward rotation. Cognitive screening shows slower processing speed and mild attention deficit. Cranial nerves intact. No focal neurological deficits. Neck range of motion full and painless.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT head obtained in emergency department: no acute intracranial hemorrhage, no skull fracture, no contusions or focal lesions. Standard structural imaging normal. Diagnosis confirmed by clinical presentation, symptom timeline, examination findings, and exclusion of structural brain injury.

puzzles.explanation: Concussion is a type of traumatic brain injury resulting from biomechanical forces that disrupt normal neuronal function, without necessarily causing structural damage visible on standard imaging. It characteristically presents with acute postconcussive symptoms—headache, dizziness, cognitive fog, and sensory sensitivity to light and noise—that emerge immediately after head trauma and reflect underlying neurometabolic dysfunction rather than hemorrhage or contusion. The constellation of vestibular symptoms (dizziness on head rotation), processing slowing, and attention deficits seen here is typical of the acute phase, while the history of recurrent concussions with similar symptom clusters reflects the cumulative vulnerability that develops with repeat head injuries. Most concussions resolve within 2–4 weeks with appropriate rest and graduated return to activity, though careful monitoring is essential given this patient's prior injury history.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young adult athlete reports shoulder pain with overhead activities. Pain is vague, deep, and poorly localized to the shoulder joint. Onset gradual over weeks to months. No specific trauma recalled.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain reproducible with overhead throwing, serving, or pressing movements. Patient reports clicking or catching sensation during loaded shoulder motion. Difficulty with late cocking phase of throwing. Symptoms ease with rest but recur with sport return.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of repetitive overhead sport (throwing, tennis, volleyball, overhead press). Symptoms began after increasing training volume. No prior shoulder surgery. Conservative treatment with NSAIDs and rest provided only temporary relief. Sensation of shoulder instability during provocative movements.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive O'Brien's test (pain with flexed, adducted, internally rotated shoulder). Positive Crank test (apprehension with shoulder abducted, externally rotated). Tenderness at anterosuperior joint line. Rotational ROM preserved. No significant glenohumeral instability on apprehension test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI with intra-articular gadolinium demonstrates tear of superior labrum extending from anterior to posterior biceps anchor. Tear extends above and behind the biceps footprint. No associated rotator cuff tear.

puzzles.explanation: A SLAP lesion (Superior Labrum Anterior-to-Posterior tear) occurs when the biceps anchor at the superior glenoid labrum fails under repetitive overhead loading, particularly during the late cocking phase of throwing when the biceps is eccentrically loaded. The condition is hallmark in young overhead athletes and presents with vague, deep shoulder pain and a clicking or catching sensation during loaded motion — reflecting the mechanical instability created by the labral defect. The relatively preserved rotational range of motion and negative apprehension test help distinguish SLAP from frank glenohumeral instability, while positive O'Brien's test (flexion-adduction-internal rotation) targets the anterosuperior labrum directly. MRI with intra-articular contrast is the gold standard for confirming the characteristic anterior-to-posterior tear at the biceps footprint, and arthroscopic repair remains the definitive treatment when conservative management fails.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A recreational runner reports insidious onset of pain at the back of the ankle that worsens with running. Pain is worst in the morning and improves slightly once warmed up.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered specifically by push-off activities and stairs. The patient reports pain is worst at the start of activity, then eases slightly mid-run, only to worsen again at the end.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. The patient may have prior history of Achilles pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness is palpable 2–6 cm proximal to the calcaneal insertion. Pain is reproduced on single-leg calf raise and resisted plantarflexion. No swelling or warmth noted. Ankle dorsiflexion range is within normal limits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates Achilles tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation confirming tendinopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Achilles tendinopathy is a chronic overuse injury characterised by degenerative changes within the tendon substance, typically affecting the mid-portion 2–6 cm above the calcaneal insertion where blood supply is poorest. The condition arises from repetitive microtrauma exceeding the tendon's capacity to repair—commonly triggered by sudden increases in running volume or intensity—leading to collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and ultimately structural weakening seen on imaging as thickening and signal changes. The characteristic morning pain that eases with warm-up reflects the tendon's initial stiffness after periods of rest, whilst the biphasic response during activity (pain, improvement, recurrence) is classic as the tendon fatigues over time. Diagnosis hinges on localised mid-tendon tenderness combined with pain on plantarflexion loading, particularly single-leg calf raise, differentiating it from insertional tendinopathy or other ankle pathologies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports foot pain and mild swelling after a twisting injury while playing sports. Pain is moderate and located in the middle of the foot.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot bear weight comfortably on the affected foot. Walking is severely limited; patient prefers to keep the midfoot immobilised.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during a high-impact mechanism (fall from height, crush injury, or forceful inversion while planted). Patient reports immediate, severe pain. No prior foot or ankle pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Swelling localised to the medial midfoot. Tenderness elicited along the tarsometatarsal joint line. Abduction stress test of the forefoot demonstrates abnormal motion and reproduces sharp pain. Pain also elicited with passive plantar flexion of the first ray.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may show widening of the Lisfranc joint space. Stress radiographs demonstrate increased widening. MRI characterises soft tissue injury.

puzzles.explanation: A Lisfranc injury involves disruption of the tarsometatarsal (TMT) joint complex, where the base of the metatarsals articulate with the midfoot — a critical region for maintaining the foot's longitudinal arch and rigidity during push-off. These injuries typically result from high-impact mechanisms with the foot planted, causing forceful abduction or axial loading that tears the strong Lisfranc ligament and surrounding capsular structures. The constellation of midfoot tenderness along the TMT joint line, abnormal forefoot abduction, and loss of weight-bearing capacity reflects disruption of this articulation, which is essential for converting the foot from a flexible shock absorber to a rigid lever during gait. While plain radiographs may show subtle widening of the joint space — particularly under stress — early diagnosis often requires MRI or CT imaging, making a high index of suspicion critical: missed Lisfranc injuries lead to chronic midfoot instability and post-traumatic arthritis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Overhead athlete presents with shoulder pain that worsens with throwing or serving motions. Pain is poorly localised but feels deep within the joint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot complete full overhead sequences without pain or fear of shoulder 'slipping'. Reports dead-arm sensation during throwing. Symptoms worse with late cocking phase of overhead activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rapid return to competition after prior shoulder injury, or sudden increase in throwing volume. No prior significant shoulder pathology. Failed conservative treatment after 6-8 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the crank test and compression-rotation test reproduce symptoms. The anterior slide test is positive. Associated apprehension may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging with intra-articular contrast demonstrates superior labral tear with characterisation of tear pattern. MRI confirms involvement of the biceps anchor.

puzzles.explanation: A SLAP (superior labrum anterior-to-posterior) lesion is a tear of the superior labrum at the attachment of the long head of biceps, typically occurring in overhead athletes due to eccentric loading during the late cocking phase of throwing or serving. The condition is mechanistically distinct from instability—it results from repetitive microtrauma or sudden high-load abduction and external rotation, causing the biceps anchor to peel away from the glenoid rim. The characteristic deep, poorly localised shoulder pain, dead-arm sensation during throwing, and reproduction on compression-rotation and crank tests reflect the labral pathology and associated biceps involvement. MRI arthrography is the diagnostic standard, clearly delineating the tear pattern and biceps anchor disruption, which explains why conservative management often fails and why return to overhead sport requires structured rehabilitation or surgical repair.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged adult reports shoulder pain that develops gradually with activity. Onset over weeks to months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform overhead tasks or sustained lifting. Activities like reaching to high shelves, throwing, or carrying cause sharp pain. Pain worsens with repetition throughout the day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of repetitive overhead work or sport. Recent increase in training volume or activity level. No acute trauma or fall. Symptoms have persisted despite rest attempts. Previous episodes of shoulder discomfort.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive impingement signs including Neer's test and Hawkins' test are present. Pain with resisted shoulder abduction is demonstrated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates rotator cuff tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Rotator cuff tendinopathy represents a continuum of degenerative change in the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, or subscapularis tendons, typically resulting from cumulative microtrauma, impingement, or age-related collagen degeneration rather than acute injury. The insidious onset with gradual worsening during repetitive overhead activities reflects the mechanical irritation and inflammatory response characteristic of this condition—tendons become thickened and hypoechoic on imaging as they attempt to remodel under load. Pain with resisted abduction and positive impingement signs (Neer's, Hawkins') occur because subacromial space narrows during these movements, compressing already inflamed tissue. The history of increased training volume and previous episodes underscores the cumulative nature of the pathology and the importance of activity modification and progressive loading in recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old presenting with progressive involuntary jerking movements over 18 months. Movements occur at rest and with activity. Patient also reports mood changes and difficulty concentrating at work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with fine motor control: handwriting deteriorated, dropping objects, struggling with buttons. Gait feels uncoordinated and unpredictable. Symptoms worsen with stress and emotional arousal.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset without trauma or toxin exposure. Progressive decline over 18+ months. Family history significant: parent died in 50s with similar movement disorder and cognitive decline. Not on any medications known to cause movement disorder.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Involuntary choreiform movements increase with voluntary movement and distraction. Reduced smooth pursuit eye movements. Mild cognitive slowing noted on screening. Gait irregular with unpredictable lurching. Emotional lability observed during assessment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Genetic testing reveals pathogenic CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion (>40 repeats). MRI shows bilateral striatal atrophy, particularly putaminal involvement. Findings consistent with neurodegenerative pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Huntington's disease is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder caused by an abnormal CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the HTT gene, leading to progressive damage primarily to the striatum and other brain regions. The classic triad of chorea (involuntary jerking movements), cognitive decline, and psychiatric symptoms typically emerges in mid-adulthood, as seen in this 45-year-old with a positive family history of parental death from a similar movement disorder. The characteristic worsening of choreiform movements during voluntary activity and distraction, combined with the insidious progressive course over 18 months, emotional lability, and oculomotor slowing, are hallmark features that distinguish Huntington's from other movement disorders. Imaging confirmation of striatal atrophy alongside genetic verification of pathogenic CAG repeat expansion (>40 repeats) makes this diagnosis definitive and enables family screening and prognostic counselling.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient recovering from a prolonged critical illness presents with generalized weakness and fatigue. They are having difficulty performing basic tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot stand or walk without assistance and cannot lift head off pillow. Respiratory weakness is also evident with difficulty clearing secretions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Extended stay in ICU for critical illness is documented. The patient may have had prolonged mechanical ventilation or sepsis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: All limb muscles grade 3 or less. Cranial nerves intact. Reflexes are brisk and symmetrical. Sensation is normal. No fasciculations. Mild proximal predominance noted in lower limbs.

clues.label: Imaging
