clues.text: EMG shows brief, small, polyphasic motor unit action potentials with early recruitment pattern. Muscle biopsy demonstrates selective Type 2 fiber atrophy with occasional myocyte necrosis, minimal inflammation, and preserved Type 1 fibers—consistent with critical illness myopathy.

puzzles.explanation: ICU-acquired weakness is a critical illness myopathy that develops during prolonged mechanical ventilation or sepsis, characterized by selective atrophy of Type 2 muscle fibers and myocyte necrosis with minimal inflammation. The condition manifests as global weakness affecting proximal and respiratory muscles while preserving sensation, reflexes, and cranial nerve function—distinguishing it from neuropathic complications like critical illness polyneuropathy. The mechanism involves direct myofibrillar injury from sepsis, corticosteroids, neuromuscular blocking agents, or immobility, leading to the characteristic brief, small polyphasic motor units on EMG with early recruitment. Early mobilization, nutritional support, and careful drug selection remain key to prevention and recovery in the critically ill population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 67-year-old retired postal worker. 8-month progressive history of pain along the inner side of the foot and ankle. Onset gradual, no recalled injury. Pain described as aching and worsens throughout the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty walking on uneven surfaces and stairs. Unable to stand on tip-toes or walk uphill. Pain worse after prolonged standing or walking. Swelling noted on inner ankle and arch area by day's end.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong flat feet. Type 2 diabetes for 12 years (HbA1c 7.8%), controlled on metformin. Mild peripheral neuropathy confirmed. Obesity (BMI 31). No prior foot surgery or acute trauma. Symptoms have not improved with rest and over-the-counter anti-inflammatories over 4 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive single-leg heel raise test—unable to lift heel off ground on affected side. Severe arch flattening in standing with heel valgus deformity. Point tenderness along the course from medial malleolus to navicular. No acute swelling or erythema. Posterior tibial strength 4/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickened, hypoechoic posterior tibial tendon with loss of fibrillar architecture. Fluid collection surrounding the tendon sheath. MRI demonstrates signal intensity change within the tendon substance consistent with degeneration. No complete rupture. Arch height significantly reduced on weight-bearing X-ray.

puzzles.explanation: Tibialis posterior tendinopathy results from chronic degeneration and inflammation of the tendon that supports the medial arch and inverts the foot—a common site of overuse injury in patients with flat feet, obesity, and systemic metabolic disease like diabetes. The progressive, insidious presentation with pain along the medial arch and ankle, worsening throughout the day, reflects the tendon's cumulative loading during weight-bearing activities; the inability to perform a single-leg heel raise is the clinical hallmark, demonstrating loss of dynamic arch support and inversion strength. In this patient, lifelong pes planus combined with diabetic tissue fragility and prolonged standing as a postal worker created the ideal environment for tendon degeneration, evidenced by the hypoechoic, disorganized ultrasound findings and the flattened arch on imaging. Unlike acute inflammatory conditions, the chronicity (8 months) and poor response to rest underscore the degenerative nature—structural tendon damage rather than simple inflammation—requiring load management and targeted strengthening rather than immobilization alone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old runner reports acute posterior lower leg pain that started mid-training session yesterday. Pain is sharp and localised to one side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot achieve full stride length or push off explosively. Walking is possible but slow and cautious. Unable to walk on tiptoes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain began suddenly without warning during sprint intervals. No prior calf injury or significant lower leg history. Patient reports feeling a sudden 'pop' or sharp grab in the muscle at onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the calf muscles. Resisted plantarflexion or knee flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption, fluid collection, or hematoma within the calf. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Calf strain results from partial or complete disruption of muscle fibers in the gastrocnemius or soleus, typically occurring during sudden eccentric loading or explosive plantarflexion — the exact mechanism seen here during sprint intervals. The sudden 'pop' sensation and immediate functional loss are hallmark features: plantarflexion weakness, inability to achieve push-off, and loss of tiptoe walking reflect disruption of the plantarflexors' force-producing capacity. The local tenderness, swelling, and hematoma on imaging distinguish this acute myotendinous injury from other lower leg conditions like compartment syndrome or deep vein thrombosis. Severity ranges from minor fiber disruption (grade I) to complete muscle tear (grade III), with imaging grading critical for prognosis and rehabilitation staging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient presents with acute knee pain following a twisting injury during sports. Pain is significant and onset was immediate.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot bear weight on the affected leg. Walking and stair negotiation are impossible. Feels like the knee is unstable or 'giving way'.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during a sport requiring rapid deceleration and pivoting. No prior knee injuries. Patient reports immediate swelling and inability to continue activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, varus instability is demonstrated with varus stress test. Positive posterolateral drawer sign and external rotation recurvatum test indicate posterolateral corner injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates injury to posterolateral corner structures including fibular collateral ligament, popliteus tendon, and biceps femoris. MRI characterises injury severity.

puzzles.explanation: Posterolateral corner (PLC) injuries involve disruption of the fibular collateral ligament, popliteus tendon, and biceps femoris—the primary restraints to varus and external rotation forces at the knee. These injuries typically occur during high-energy pivoting or deceleration mechanisms that load the knee in varus and external rotation, explaining the acute presentation and immediate functional loss in this athlete. The characteristic varus instability, positive posterolateral drawer sign, and external rotation recurvatum test reflect the biomechanical consequence of losing posterolateral restraint, with the knee unable to resist varus and posterior translation. PLC injuries are often initially overlooked but are clinically significant as isolated posterolateral insufficiency leads to chronic instability and accelerated knee pathology if left untreated.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Sudden onset loss of elbow strength after forceful arm activity. Patient reports acute pain in the anterior elbow.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform resisted elbow flexion or carry objects with the affected arm. Simple overhead reach is preserved but resisted activity is impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during eccentric loading—e.g., resisting arm extension while holding a heavy object. Patient is middle-aged. No prior elbow surgery or chronic tendinopathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable gap in the distal antecubital fossa where tendon normally inserts. Supination strength preserved but flexion strength severely reduced. Positive biceps squeeze test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates absence of the distal biceps tendon insertion and proximal retraction of the muscle-tendon unit. MRI shows complete rupture at the radial tuberosity.

puzzles.explanation: Distal biceps tendon rupture occurs when the tendon insertion at the radial tuberosity fails under eccentric load, typically during forceful arm extension against resistance in middle-aged individuals whose tendon has undergone age-related weakening. The mechanism—resisting extension while loaded—places maximal stress on the already-compromised insertion, causing acute rupture and proximal retraction of the muscle belly into the arm. Clinically, this produces the distinctive loss of elbow flexion and supination strength with a palpable gap in the antecubital fossa, while passive range and overhead reaching remain intact because the long head of biceps and other flexors remain functional. The preserved supination strength despite weak flexion is the key diagnostic feature, distinguishing this from proximal ruptures or brachial artery injuries that would compromise supination; ultrasound or MRI confirms the diagnosis by demonstrating complete tendon absence at the tuberosity with proximal muscle retraction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 3-year-old child presents with persistent leftward head tilt and neck rotation limitation noted by parents over the past 6 months. The head tilt is constant, and the child avoids turning the head to the right.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child has difficulty following objects that require rightward head rotation and shows reluctance to eat food presented on the right side. Attempts to manually straighten the neck cause significant distress. The abnormal posture does not improve with rest and is present throughout sleep and waking hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Parents recall a difficult breach delivery with forceps application; however, the child had normal head positioning at 1 month postpartum. The tilt gradually worsened over 4-6 months without clear precipitant. No history of trauma, fever, or recent upper respiratory infection. Developmental milestones were met appropriately.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation of the left sternocleidomastoid reveals a firm, non-tender palpable abnormality within the muscle belly. Passive cervical rotation to the right is limited to 35 degrees compared to 70 degrees to the left. No cervical lymphadenopathy, facial asymmetry, or neurological deficits are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates an echogenic nodule within the left sternocleidomastoid muscle measuring 1.2 by 0.8cm with minimal vascularity. MRI of the cervical spine and soft tissues confirms a well-defined mass consistent with fibromatosis within the sternocleidomastoid with no intracranial abnormality or spinal cord compression.

puzzles.explanation: Congenital muscular torticollis results from fibrosis and contracture of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, most commonly due to compartment syndrome, ischemia, or fibrous tumor formation during infancy. The classic presentation—persistent head tilt toward the affected side with limited rotation away from it—reflects the shortened muscle's mechanical constraint on cervical mobility. In this case, the gradual onset following a difficult forceps delivery and the imaging findings of a fibromatous mass within the muscle are characteristic; fibromatosis of the sternocleidomastoid is a benign proliferative condition that can develop in early childhood and progressively worsen if untreated. Early physiotherapy focusing on passive and active-assisted stretching, combined with postural re-education, is essential to prevent permanent contracture and facial asymmetry, though some cases require surgical division of the muscle.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old presents 3 weeks post-operatively with persistent pain and swelling in the lower leg and foot. Reports difficulty with mobility and functional activities despite initial post-operative instructions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear full weight on affected limb. Gait severely compromised; using crutches or walker. Pain and swelling increase with any attempt at weight-bearing or ankle motion. Able to elevate to reduce swelling temporarily.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 3 weeks ago from high-energy mechanism (motor vehicle collision). Underwent surgical intervention with hardware placement. No significant medical history. Compliant with post-operative restrictions but slower-than-expected progress in early mobilization phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked periarticular swelling and mild erythema. Dorsiflexion 8 degrees, plantarflexion 15 degrees. Focal tenderness at surgical site. Single-leg stance not tolerated. Balance impaired; proprioceptive deficits noted. Strength testing limited by pain and guarding. No neurovascular compromise distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs show appropriate hardware position and alignment. CT confirms anatomic reduction of articular surfaces. MRI demonstrates expected post-surgical soft-tissue edema around fixation site consistent with early healing phase (3 weeks post-op).

puzzles.explanation: Post-ankle ORIF represents the early recovery phase following surgical fixation of ankle fractures, typically with open reduction and internal fixation using plates, screws, or both to restore articular congruity and mechanical stability. At three weeks post-operatively, patients remain in a vulnerable inflammatory phase characterized by periarticular edema, pain-limited range of motion, and proprioceptive deficits—despite radiographic confirmation of anatomically sound hardware placement. The marked swelling, severe weight-bearing intolerance, and guarding observed here are expected hallmarks of early healing rather than mechanical failure; successful rehabilitation depends on carefully graded mobilization and progressive weight-bearing as soft-tissue inflammation resolves. Proprioceptive training and early dorsiflexor strengthening become critical during this window to prevent long-term ankle instability and functional limitations, even as radiographic alignment appears perfect.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Adolescent with progressive upper back pain and postural changes noticed over months. Pain worsens with activity and at end of day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain upright posture during prolonged sitting or standing. Difficulty with overhead activities and extension-based movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset during growth spurt in early adolescence. Family history of spinal issues. No trauma. Progressive course over 12–24 months without prior intervention.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, fixed thoracic kyphosis is evident that does not correct with forward bending. Back pain is elicited with extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate thoracic vertebral body wedging and Schmorl's nodes characteristic of Scheuermann's disease. Kyphosis is measured.

puzzles.explanation: Scheuermann's disease is a structural kyphotic disorder of the thoracic spine occurring during the adolescent growth spurt, characterized by anterior vertebral body wedging and herniation of disc material into the vertebral endplates (Schmorl's nodes). The pathophysiology involves disrupted endplate ossification, likely multifactorial with genetic predisposition and mechanical loading playing key roles. The hallmark feature is a fixed, non-correctable thoracic kyphosis that persists even with forward bending — distinguishing it from postural kyphosis — combined with pain on extension and difficulty maintaining upright posture during prolonged loading. The progressive course over 12–24 months during peak adolescent growth and imaging findings of vertebral wedging with Schmorl's nodes confirm the diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An older adult reports severe joint pain that has worsened over many years. Pain is constant and limits most daily activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty with prolonged walking and stair negotiation. Unable to rise from a chair without arm support. Gait is slow and antalgic with lateral trunk shift.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms have progressed over 10+ years despite physiotherapy, analgesia, and weight management. Patient previously attended multiple treatment courses without sustained relief.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable bony enlargement at joint margins. Active range of motion severely restricted in flexion and internal rotation. End-feel hard. Quadriceps and hip abductors markedly wasted bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate severe degenerative changes with joint space obliteration, osteophytes, and bone sclerosis indicating advanced arthritis.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced osteoarthritis represents the end-stage of progressive joint degeneration, where articular cartilage is substantially lost and bony remodelling dominates the pathophysiology. The severe restriction in range of motion with a hard end-feel, combined with marked periarticular muscle wasting and radiographic findings of joint space obliteration and extensive osteophyte formation, reflect the chronicity and structural deterioration that has accumulated over the decade-long disease course. The antalgic gait pattern with lateral trunk shift and functional dependence on upper limb support are characteristic compensation strategies for the advanced hip or knee involvement limiting weight-bearing tolerance. At this stage, physiotherapy aims to optimise remaining function and pain management rather than reverse structural change, making surgical intervention often the more definitive option for restoring mobility and quality of life.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports ankle gives way unexpectedly during everyday activities. Onset gradual over months following an ankle injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to walk confidently on uneven ground or grass. Avoids sport participation due to fear of ankle going over. Normal walking on flat surfaces manageable.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Ankle sprain sustained 18 months ago playing football; initial swelling resolved but patient never regained full confidence. No episode of complete ligament tear. No prior physiotherapy or rehabilitation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the anterior drawer test demonstrates anterior translation of the talus. The talar tilt test shows inversion laxity. Proprioceptive deficit is evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Stress radiographs demonstrate increased talar tilt and anterior translation compared to normal ankle. MRI may show ligament signal changes and chronic inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic ankle instability develops when the lateral ligaments (particularly the anterior talofibular ligament) fail to fully recover after an inversion injury, leaving the ankle mechanically lax and proprioceptively impaired. Unlike an acute sprain, the ligaments here have healed but incompletely—the lack of structured rehabilitation after the initial injury 18 months ago meant proprioceptive retraining was never established, perpetuating the instability cycle. The combination of positive anterior drawer and talar tilt tests, demonstrating genuine ligamentous laxity, alongside the patient's fear-avoidance during uneven walking and inability to return to sport, is classic: mechanical instability often interacts with neuromuscular deficits and psychomotor compensation. Early aggressive proprioceptive training and peroneal strengthening could still improve functional stability, even if ligament laxity persists on imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old male with sudden-onset unilateral leg swelling and calf pain over the past 2 days. Reports the leg feels heavy and tight. No recent trauma or fall.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Swelling does not improve with leg elevation. Pain is constant rather than activity-dependent. Patient reports difficulty with normal walking due to discomfort and heaviness in the affected limb.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 8 days after a surgical procedure requiring general anesthesia and postoperative bed rest. Takes lisinopril for hypertension. No prior history of similar episodes. Did not receive blood thinner injections or compression stockings after surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Affected calf is visibly swollen, warm, and slightly reddened compared to the opposite side. Calf circumference differs by 3 cm between legs. Palpable firmness noted along the posterior calf. Distal foot shows mild puffiness. Passive ankle dorsiflexion reproduces sharp pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Compression ultrasound of lower limb veins shows echogenic material within the popliteal vein that does not compress with probe pressure. Doppler imaging demonstrates absent blood flow in the affected vein. Contralateral vein is fully compressible with normal flow.

puzzles.explanation: Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is the formation of a blood clot within a deep vein, typically triggered by venous stasis, endothelial injury, or hypercoagulability—the classic Virchow's triad. This patient's risk was substantially elevated by prolonged immobility following surgery, particularly without thromboprophylaxis, combined with the endothelial trauma of anesthesia itself. The sudden unilateral swelling, calf pain, and characteristic warmth reflect venous obstruction and inflammatory response; notably, elevation fails to improve symptoms because the underlying problem is venous outflow obstruction rather than simple fluid pooling. Compression ultrasound showing non-compressible echogenic material with absent Doppler flow in the popliteal vein—the most common site of proximal DVT—is the diagnostic standard, and distinguishes this from musculoskeletal or lymphatic causes that would not produce absent venous flow.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old recreational soccer player reports sudden onset medial knee pain with a popping sensation during a game 1 week ago. The pain is localized to the medial joint line and worsens with weightbearing and twisting movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot return to sport due to intermittent episodes of knee locking where the knee becomes stuck in slight flexion for several seconds before suddenly extending with a pop. Squatting and cutting movements reliably trigger both locking episodes and sharp joint line pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred during a rapid deceleration while pivoting on the planted foot. He has no prior knee injuries, previous meniscal pathology, or osteoarthritis. His current pain is out of proportion to prior minor knee sprains and has not improved with standard rest and ice protocols over 7 days.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, point tenderness is elicited directly over the medial joint line. The McMurray test—performed with the knee flexed 90 degrees, internally rotating the tibia while extending—reproduces the patient's locking sensation and sharp pain. The Thessaly test at 20 degrees flexion reproduces medial joint line discomfort. No effusion is appreciated, and Lachman and anterior drawer tests are negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee reveals a vertical longitudinal tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus extending to the free edge. The tear pattern is consistent with degenerative changes at the tear interface. No signal abnormality in the anterior cruciate ligament, posterior cruciate ligament, or medial collateral ligament is identified.

puzzles.explanation: A meniscal tear results from disruption of the fibrocartilage meniscus, typically from a combination of compression and rotational forces—in this case, deceleration with pivoting on a planted foot. The medial meniscus, particularly its posterior horn, is vulnerable to tears that extend to the free edge, where they can displace and mechanically block knee extension, producing the characteristic locking and popping sensations. The vertical longitudinal tear pattern here, with its degenerative interface, is classic for traumatic tears in middle-aged patients; such tears often fail to resolve with conservative management because the loose fragment continues to impinge during dynamic movements like squatting and cutting. Positive McMurray and Thessaly tests—reproducing the mechanical symptoms at the medial joint line—combined with MRI confirmation make this diagnosis definitive and typically warrant arthroscopic intervention to restore smooth, pain-free motion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult reports increased caution with movement and reluctance to walk or change position. No acute injury reported, but patient seems tense and hesitant.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient is now declining stairs, uneven surfaces, and crowded environments. Spends most time seated or holding onto furniture. Previously independent with community ambulation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall occurred 3 months ago after a trip; patient struck hip but imaging was normal. Since then, has become progressively withdrawn. No other medical or psychological history of note.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gait shows shortened stride, reduced arm swing, and frequent pauses with guarding. Patient clings to rails or therapist's arm for reassurance despite intact strength and ROM. Berg Balance Scale 18/56 indicates high fall risk. Timed Up and Go demonstrates hesitant, slow, overly cautious movement with freezing episodes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI and CT head are unremarkable. Cognitive screening normal. Geriatric Depression Scale and Falls Efficacy Scale confirm high anxiety and low fall confidence markedly disproportionate to objective balance deficits.

puzzles.explanation: Post-fall anxiety syndrome is a psychologically-mediated movement disorder where fear of falling following a traumatic fall event leads to progressive functional decline, despite intact neuromuscular capacity. The mechanism involves catastrophic thinking and avoidance behaviours that paradoxically increase fall risk: shortened stride, reduced arm swing, and guarding patterns actually destabilize posture and gait mechanics. This patient demonstrates the classic presentation—a minor fall with negative imaging followed by disproportionate anxiety, progressive withdrawal from activities, and objective gait deterioration (shortened stride, freezing) that far exceeds any balance impairment on testing. The discrepancy between high Falls Efficacy Scale scores and preserved strength, ROM, and normal cognitive screening is pathognomonic; psychologically-driven fear, not physical deficit, is the primary driver of functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An older adult reports episodes of dizziness triggered by head movements or changes in position. Symptoms began gradually over weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot walk in dim lighting or on uneven surfaces. Turning in bed or looking up causes brief spinning sensations. Gait is cautious and slow.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No recent head trauma, infection, or ototoxic medications. Hypertension controlled on current medication. Mild hearing loss noted. No previous episodes of vertigo.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tandem stance not achieved. Romberg test positive with eyes closed. Dix-Hallpike and head impulse test negative. Nystagmus absent at rest. Proprioceptive deficits evident. Gait ataxia present without focal neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI head shows no acute lesion or posterior fossa mass. Videonystagmography reveals reduced caloric response bilaterally consistent with vestibular involution. Saccular function impaired on dynamic posturography testing.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related vertigo results from gradual degeneration of vestibular hair cells and central vestibular pathways, causing progressive loss of balance and spatial orientation — particularly in older adults without acute structural disease. The condition manifests as position-dependent dizziness triggered by head movements and environmental challenges like dim lighting or uneven surfaces, reflecting reduced ability to integrate visual, proprioceptive, and vestibular inputs. The bilateral reduction in caloric response and impaired saccular function indicate genuine vestibular involution rather than peripheral canal dysfunction, while negative head impulse testing rules out acute BPPV or central lesions. Compensatory gait changes and positive Romberg testing are characteristic adaptations to chronic vestibular insufficiency, and rehabilitation targeting static and dynamic balance becomes the mainstay of treatment for this progressive but non-progressive-disease condition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old presents with chest wall pain and stiffness. Reports difficulty with arm movement on affected side and reduced exercise tolerance. Symptoms began several weeks ago and have been gradually improving.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot reach overhead or behind back on affected side. Dyspnea with exertion beyond walking on flat ground. Reports difficulty sleeping on affected side and clothing irritation over anterior chest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain onset correlates with surgical intervention to chest approximately 4–6 weeks prior. No trauma or infection reported. Standard post-operative care provided. Comorbidities include mild COPD.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness and palpable scar along anterior chest wall midline. Shoulder abduction limited to 80°, external rotation 60°. Reduced inspiratory capacity with accessory muscle recruitment. Scapular winging noted during arm elevation. No neurovascular compromise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows appropriate bony healing at sternal/rib level with intact hardware and no evidence of non-union. Lung fields clear, cardiac silhouette normal. No pleural effusion or atelectasis.

puzzles.explanation: Post-thoracotomy rehabilitation addresses the predictable musculoskeletal sequelae following cardiac or pulmonary surgery, where incision through the sternum or ribs and retraction of chest wall structures causes inflammation, scar tissue formation, and reflex inhibition of surrounding muscles. The constellation of findings—anterior chest wall tenderness with a healing surgical scar, restricted shoulder abduction and external rotation, scapular dyskinesia, and reduced inspiratory capacity with accessory muscle overuse—reflects both direct surgical trauma and the kinetic chain dysfunction that develops when patients guard the chest wall and avoid normal movement patterns. The gradual improvement over 4–6 weeks is typical as inflammation resolves and protective muscle guarding begins to normalize, though restoration of full shoulder mobility and breathing mechanics requires targeted rehabilitation to address scar tissue mobility, serratus anterior and lower trapezius function, and thoracic cage mechanics. Comorbid COPD may further limit exercise tolerance and complicate the recovery trajectory, necessitating graded aerobic progression alongside manual therapy and motor control training.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 67-year-old retired teacher reports insidious onset of pain at the base of the right thumb over the past 8 months, worsening gradually with increased hand use. The pain is described as aching with occasional sharp stabbing when grasping or pinching.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Fine motor activities—particularly opening jars, using tweezers, and writing—are significantly limited. The patient demonstrates thumb opposition weakness and avoids pinch grip; he substitutes lateral pinch for precision pinch. Pain intensifies progressively throughout the day with repeated pinching motions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient sustained a fall onto an abducted right thumb 14 months ago while hiking, causing acute pain and swelling that partially resolved over 6 weeks. He did not seek medical attention at the time. Subsequent attempts at recreational knitting and gardening triggered progressive worsening of baseline symptoms over the past year.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the radial collateral ligament stress test of the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint at 30 degrees flexion demonstrates excessive radial deviation (>30 degrees laxity compared to the left side). Pinch strength testing shows 40% reduction compared to the contralateral thumb. Mild thenar eminence atrophy is appreciated. No acute swelling or warmth is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate a small avulsion fracture fragment from the medial base of the proximal phalanx of the thumb with mild degenerative changes at the metacarpophalangeal joint. MRI shows disruption of the radial collateral ligament with chronic fibrosis and healing response; the ligament appears attenuated but intact.

puzzles.explanation: Gamekeeper's thumb is a chronic insufficiency of the radial collateral ligament (RCL) at the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint, typically resulting from an initial traumatic injury—such as forced radial deviation or an abduction fall—that is either missed acutely or inadequately treated. The condition leads to progressive laxity and functional impairment, particularly affecting precision pinch and fine motor activities like writing and knitting. In this patient, the 14-month-old untreated fall injury evolved into chronic RCL insufficiency with radiographic evidence of avulsion fracture and ligamentous fibrosis, explaining both the excessive radial deviation on stress testing and the progressive grip weakness and thenar atrophy. Early recognition and surgical reconstruction are critical to prevent the secondary osteoarthritis and permanent functional loss that develop when degenerative changes become established.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old warehouse worker reports chronic low back pain of 4 years' duration that fluctuates in severity. Pain is mechanical, reproducible, and worsens with certain movements and prolonged postures, particularly forward bending and sustained standing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitations include reduced tolerance for lifting (now limited to 15 pounds versus 50 pounds prior to symptom onset) and difficulty with sustained postures during work. The patient demonstrates guarding and splinting behavior during movement; shifting weight side-to-side improves tolerance. Pain is least with supported sitting and walking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 4 years ago following a work-related lifting incident; imaging at that time was unremarkable. He has tried multiple conservative interventions with temporary benefits. Pain intensity ranges from 2/10 at best to 7/10 at worst, with exacerbations lasting 1-2 weeks. He works in a physically demanding job and is overweight (BMI 31).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, active lumbar flexion triggers pain reproduction and is accompanied by deviation (Schober test: 4cm excursion versus normal 5cm). Prone instability test produces sharp midline lumbar pain. Lumbar multifidus muscle tone is asymmetrically diminished on the left. Abdominal bracing partially relieves pain. Strength testing and straight leg raise are normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Flexion-extension lumbar radiographs demonstrate 4mm of anterior translation at L4-L5 on flexion compared to neutral, indicating segmental hypermobility. MRI shows loss of disc height at L4-L5 with facet hypertrophy; however, no significant canal stenosis or spondylolisthesis is present. Ligamentous insufficiency with facet effusions is noted.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar instability results from loss of segmental control at the lumbar spine, typically due to degenerative disc disease, facet joint changes, or ligamentous laxity that permits excessive intersegmental motion. This condition is characterized by mechanical pain that varies with posture and loading—worsening with flexion-dominant movements and improving with external stabilization (bracing, supported sitting)—as the neuromuscular system struggles to compensate for compromised passive restraints. The 4mm anterior translation at L4-L5 on flexion imaging, combined with asymmetric multifidus atrophy and positive prone instability test, confirms the diagnosis; these findings reflect the degenerative cascade and the spine's inability to maintain neutral alignment under demand. Management focuses on restoring dynamic stability through motor control retraining and strengthening, while activity modification and weight management reduce provocative loading.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with high-impact activities presents with progressive hip or thigh pain. Pain has developed insidiously during training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports pain with running and jumping, but can walk on flat ground without exacerbation. Pain worsens with prolonged standing or stairs. High-velocity or repetitive loading activities consistently provoke symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training volume or intensity coincides with symptom onset. Risk factors including female athlete triad, osteoporosis, or energy deficiency may be present. Previous stress injuries or low bone density screening reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pain is elicited with single-leg stance and single-leg hop test. Focal tenderness elicited over the femoral neck on palpation. Pain is reproduced with hip flexion and internal rotation stress test. Positive fulcrum test for femoral neck.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may show stress fracture line or periosteal callus formation. MRI demonstrates bone stress with periosteal reaction, bone marrow edema, and crack line in the femoral neck cortex indicating active stress fracture.

puzzles.explanation: A femoral stress fracture results from cumulative microtrauma to the femoral neck—typically the femoral neck or intertrochanteric region—when repetitive loading exceeds the bone's capacity to remodel and repair. This injury is particularly common in endurance athletes and individuals with energy deficiency, low bone density, or the female athlete triad, all of which compromise bone quality and healing. The characteristic presentation of insidious pain that worsens with high-impact activities like running and jumping but spares walking reflects the load-dependency of stress injuries; the fulcrum test and single-leg stance reproduce pain by concentrating compressive and bending forces through the fracture site. MRI findings of bone marrow edema and a visible crack line in the femoral neck cortex confirm the diagnosis and guide the urgency of intervention, as femoral neck stress fractures carry risk of displacement and nonunion if not appropriately managed with load modification and bone health optimization.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old male presents with acute onset weakness and altered sensation in the right upper limb following a fall during sport 72 hours ago. He reports inability to bend his elbow and numbness in the forearm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform elbow flexion or pulling motions. Hand strength and dexterity are normal. Sensory changes are confined to the lateral forearm and thumb dorsum.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The mechanism involved forced shoulder abduction and external rotation combined with axial loading on the outstretched arm. He sustained a visible dislocation at the shoulder which was reduced in the emergency department. Radiographs excluded fractures. Prior shoulder history is unremarkable.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right elbow flexion strength is 0/5 and cannot be initiated. The biceps reflex is absent. Shoulder external/internal rotation strength normal. All other limb reflexes intact bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography performed 5 days post-injury demonstrates acute denervation changes (positive sharp waves and fibrillations) in the biceps brachii and brachialis muscles; the brachioradialis is spared. MRI of the brachial plexus reveals stretching injury of the musculocutaneous nerve at the level of the shoulder with surrounding edema and partial discontinuity.

puzzles.explanation: Musculocutaneous nerve palsy results from injury to the nerve as it branches from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus and pierces the coracobrachialis muscle. This nerve is particularly vulnerable to traction injury during traumatic shoulder dislocation with forced abduction and external rotation, as occurred in this patient — the mechanism stretches the plexus and can cause partial or complete interruption of nerve continuity. The clinical hallmark is selective weakness of elbow flexion (biceps and brachialis) with preserved shoulder and hand function, combined with sensory loss in the lateral forearm distribution. The acute denervation pattern on EMG and MRI evidence of stretching injury with edema confirm the nerve has been disrupted, though some fibers may recover over weeks to months depending on the severity of axonal damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 51-year-old woman with a 3-year history of generalised musculoskeletal pain. Burning and aching quality affecting multiple body regions. No clear injury or precipitant that she can identify.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Light physical activity—including walking and household tasks—triggers significant pain exacerbation that persists for 1-2 days. Patient reports profound fatigue, cognitive difficulties affecting work, and prolonged morning stiffness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain onset followed a severe respiratory viral infection 3 years ago. She reports a personal history of major depressive disorder (treated) and anxiety. Multiple investigations including rheumatologic workup (negative ANA, RF, and inflammatory markers), imaging, and specialist consultations have failed to identify structural pathology. Previous treatments with NSAIDs and corticosteroids provided minimal benefit.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Widespread tender points reproducible at multiple anatomic locations (cervical, trapezius, supraspinatus, costochondral junctions, lateral epicondyles, gluteal region, greater trochanters, knees). Pain evoked at reduced pressure thresholds. Strength, range of motion, reflexes, and joint examination are entirely normal. No signs of inflammation or structural abnormality on physical exam.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Laboratory investigations show all inflammatory markers and autoimmune serology within normal limits. MRI of the spine and pelvis reveals normal disc architecture with no degenerative disease, inflammatory changes, or myelopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Fibromyalgia is a central sensitization disorder characterized by amplified pain processing in the central nervous system, resulting in widespread musculoskeletal pain without structural pathology or systemic inflammation. The condition typically affects middle-aged women and often follows a triggering event—such as infection, trauma, or psychological stress—that initiates a cascade of neurobiological changes including altered serotonin and norepinephrine levels, increased cytokine activity, and reduced pain inhibition. The hallmark presentation combines generalized burning pain with disproportionate tenderness at specific anatomic sites, accompanied by fatigue, cognitive impairment, and sleep disturbance—as illustrated by this patient's post-viral onset and profound functional impact despite entirely normal investigations and examination findings. The failure of inflammatory-based treatments and the normal imaging and serology are key discriminators; diagnosis relies on clinical criteria including widespread pain distribution and symptom severity, rather than biomarkers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old with acute ankle pain and swelling following a fall. Patient reports sudden onset of inability to bear weight on the affected side. No prior ankle injuries.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Complete loss of weight-bearing tolerance. Ankle feels unstable and locked in a slightly plantarflexed position. Pain severe with any attempted ankle motion in any direction. Swelling progressing rapidly over first 2 hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fell from standing height onto heel with axial loading mechanism—foot trapped during fall. Patient recalls hearing/feeling a distinct 'crack' at moment of injury. No prior fracture history. Neurovascular sensation intact distally.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe swelling obscuring ankle contours. Palpable crepitus over anterior distal tibia and medial ankle. Medial malleolus tender to palpation. Ankle held in slight plantarflexion and inversion. No active dorsiflexion or plantarflexion achievable. Compartment firmness increasing. Fibular region non-tender.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs reveal comminuted fracture of distal tibia involving the articular surface, metaphysis, and diaphysis with multiple intra-articular fragments. Fibula fracture absent. CT imaging demonstrates complex three-dimensional fracture pattern with significant intra-articular displacement and soft tissue involvement.

puzzles.explanation: A pilon fracture is a comminuted intra-articular fracture of the distal tibia resulting from axial loading forces, classically occurring when a patient falls from height onto a plantarflexed foot with the weight-bearing axis driven through the ankle joint. The injury shatters the tibial plafond (articular surface) into multiple fragments, creating a characteristically complex three-dimensional pattern that disrupts the mortise and often involves the metaphysis and diaphysis. The presentation here—severe acute swelling, rapid progression, loss of all ankle motion, and the distal tibia crepitus with intact fibula—reflects the high-energy mechanism and articular disruption typical of this injury. Pilon fractures carry significant risk of compartment syndrome and post-traumatic arthritis due to the intra-articular involvement, making urgent assessment and often surgical intervention necessary to restore joint congruity and function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old amateur skier presents with acute onset sharp pain at the base of the left thumb following a fall 3 days ago. The thumb is swollen and tender, and any gripping aggravates the pain significantly.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot perform fine pinch grip or opposition movements; attempts to hold a pen or grasp objects trigger sharp pain at the thumb base. Functional loss is primarily in thumb-to-finger pinch and opposition.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The mechanism involved forced abduction of the thumb during the fall when the patient tried to break the fall with an outstretched hand. He applied ice and kept the thumb immobilized at home but symptoms have not improved. No prior thumb injury, and he denies any sensation of instability before the fall.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, marked swelling and ecchymosis are evident over the radial aspect of the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint. The radial collateral ligament stress test performed with the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint in 30 degrees flexion demonstrates excessive radial deviation (>20 degrees) compared to the contralateral side. Thumbs-up test shows pain with resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs of the thumb in anteroposterior and lateral views are normal with no fractures or avulsion fragments visible. MRI demonstrates disruption of the radial collateral ligament with complete separation of the ligament from its proximal insertion; associated edema and effusion within the metacarpophalangeal joint capsule are present.

puzzles.explanation: Gamekeeper's thumb is an acute injury to the radial collateral ligament (RCL) of the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint, typically caused by forced abduction during a fall on an outstretched hand—exactly the mechanism seen here. The RCL is crucial for pinch grip stability, so complete disruption results in loss of thumb-to-finger opposition and inability to generate grip force, while the radial deviation evident on stress testing confirms ligamentous insufficiency. Named historically after repetitive trauma in gamekeepers, modern cases are predominantly sports injuries; the acuity of onset, imaging confirmation of ligament separation, and significant functional loss distinguish this from chronic hypermobility or minor sprains. Early surgical repair offers the best functional outcome, as prolonged conservative management in complete tears risks chronic thumb instability and weakness in pinch and opposition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 46-year-old woman presents with anterior chest wall pain that started 2 weeks ago. The pain is sharp, positional, and worsens with certain movements and breathing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is reproducibly triggered by deep inspiration, coughing, laughing, and reaching movements. Pressure over the painful area during activity intensifies symptoms. Anti-inflammatory medication and heat provide partial relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset followed 3 weeks of a persistent dry cough from a recent viral upper respiratory infection. The patient reports working at a desk with suboptimal ergonomics and has been doing garden renovation involving repetitive overhead activities. She denies trauma, fever, or constitutional symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, well-localized tenderness is reproduced with direct palpation of the costochondral junction at the 4th rib on the left side. The Tietze test (reproduction of pain with passive vertical flexion of the thorax) produces sharp left parasternal pain. No joint swelling, warmth, or erythema is appreciated; no heart or lung findings on auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest radiographs are normal with no infiltrates, pleural effusion, or mediastinal widening. MRI of the chest wall demonstrates mild soft tissue edema at the left 4th costochondral junction with subtle enhancement on post-gadolinium imaging. No cardiac abnormality, pulmonary pathology, or mediastinal masses are identified.

puzzles.explanation: Costochondritis is inflammation of the cartilaginous junction between the rib and sternum, typically self-limited and triggered by mechanical stress, repetitive upper limb activity, or prolonged coughing. The condition classically presents with sharp, pleuritic anterior chest wall pain that worsens with deep breathing, coughing, and trunk movements — exactly the pattern seen in this patient following her viral cough and overhead gardening work. Diagnosis relies on reproducible tenderness at the specific costochondral junction on palpation and positive manoeuvres like the Tietze test, with imaging serving primarily to exclude more serious cardiopulmonary or malignant pathology. Most cases resolve within weeks with activity modification, NSAIDs, and local heat; the mild soft tissue edema on MRI reflects the inflammatory process but is expected and does not alter management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old patient presenting with acute loss of leg function and sensation following high-speed motor vehicle collision with significant impact 3 days ago. Loss of consciousness reported at scene. Currently reports no sensation in lower body and inability to move legs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Complete loss of independent mobility—unable to walk, stand, or move legs voluntarily. Loss of bowel and bladder control noted. Weakness and sensory loss do not follow a single nerve or root distribution; affects both legs equally and symmetrically.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury mechanism: high-impact trauma with direct force to trunk. No prior neurological disease, spinal surgery, or significant medical history. Onset was immediate and catastrophic—not gradual. Symptom level remained stable since injury (no improvement or deterioration over 72 hours).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sharp sensory level identified at T12 dermatome—sensation preserved above this line, completely absent below. Bilateral lower extremity strength 0/5 below level of injury. Hyperreflexia and increased tone in lower extremities; absent abdominal reflexes. Positive bilateral Babinski sign. Upper extremity strength and sensation normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervicothoracolumbar spine shows focal spinal cord signal abnormality at T12-L1 level consistent with hemorrhage and edema. Vertebral column intact without fracture or dislocation. Cord cross-sectional involvement indicates central gray matter damage extending bilaterally.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic spinal cord injury at the T12-L1 level results from direct traumatic insult to the cord itself, causing hemorrhage and edema that damage both gray and white matter tracts. The sharp sensory level at T12—with preserved sensation above and complete loss below—combined with bilateral lower extremity paralysis and loss of bowel/bladder control, reflects complete transection of all ascending and descending pathways at that spinal segment. The hyperreflexia, increased tone, and positive Babinski signs indicate spasticity from unopposed supraspinal influence, a classic pattern seen when descending inhibitory tracts are disrupted. Unlike incomplete injuries, the acute catastrophic presentation and stable plateau within 72 hours suggest severe central cord damage with minimal prospect for spontaneous recovery, making this a clinical emergency requiring immediate spinal cord protection protocols and long-term rehabilitation focused on functional adaptation and secondary complication prevention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 38-year-old male. Acute onset polyarticular joint pain and swelling in knees, ankles, and wrists over the past 10 days. Joints warm and progressively more swollen. No prior joint disease or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk without assistance due to bilateral lower-limb pain. Grip strength severely limited in both hands. Symptoms worst in early morning; some improvement with gentle movement and activity throughout the day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute gastroenteritis with diarrhea 2 weeks prior to joint symptoms. No fever currently. No recent sexually transmitted infection symptoms. No axial spine involvement or heel pain reported. Previously healthy, no autoimmune history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symmetric erythema, warmth, and swelling over bilateral knees and ankles. Right knee with palpable effusion. Wrist joints swollen; flexion-extension range limited. No skin rash, oral ulcers, or ocular conjunctivitis noted. No enthesopathy. No cardiac murmurs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Inflammatory markers elevated (CRP 8.2 mg/dL, ESR 42 mm/hr). HLA-B27 positive. Stool culture negative for Salmonella, Shigella, Yersinia; serologic panel negative for Chlamydia trachomatis genital infection. Plain radiographs show joint effusion but no erosions or bone changes.

puzzles.explanation: Reactive arthritis is an acute inflammatory polyarthritis triggered by infection elsewhere in the body—most commonly enteric pathogens like Shigella, Salmonella, or Yersinia—that develops 1–4 weeks after the initial infection resolves. The mechanism involves cross-reactive T-cell responses to bacterial antigens presenting on HLA-B27, explaining both the temporal link to his recent gastroenteritis and his positive HLA-B27 status. The symmetric, polyarticular involvement of large joints (knees, ankles, wrists) with prominent morning stiffness and inflammatory markers, in the absence of erosive changes or systemic features like conjunctivitis or mucocutaneous lesions, is characteristic of this condition. Most cases resolve spontaneously within weeks to months, though HLA-B27–positive individuals carry a higher risk of chronicity or recurrence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old man reports progressive abnormal head posture with involuntary leftward tilt developing over 18 months. Symptoms worsen throughout the day and with emotional stress.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports neck pain and muscle fatigue worsening with sustained positioning, particularly during reading or computer work. Symptoms paradoxically worsen in crowded environments but completely resolve when recumbent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset was insidious without preceding trauma or infection. A family history of essential tremor is noted in his mother. He has no psychiatric history, prior movement disorders, or medication exposure. Symptoms have not responded to NSAIDs, muscle relaxants, or physical therapy over 12 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Examination reveals involuntary head positioning can be transiently suppressed with conscious effort. Hypertonic sternocleidomastoid and trapezius bilaterally, predominant on left. Sensory examination, motor strength (5/5 throughout), and reflexes normal. No focal neurological deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG demonstrates burst-like muscle activity in sternocleidomastoid and trapezius characteristic of dystonic muscle contraction. Cervical spine MRI and brain MRI are structurally normal.

puzzles.explanation: Torticollis is a focal dystonia characterized by involuntary, sustained muscle contractions of the neck that produce abnormal head posture and movement — in this case, leftward tilt from predominantly left sternocleidomastoid and trapezius overactivity. Unlike structural causes (trauma, infection, or cervical pathology), adult-onset torticollis is a primary movement disorder with insidious progression, often showing a genetic predisposition as suggested by the family history of essential tremor. The distinctive dystonic pattern — worsening with emotional stress and sustained positioning, paradoxically improving when recumbent, and transiently suppressible with conscious effort — combined with EMG evidence of burst-like muscle activation and normal structural imaging, confirms the diagnosis. Torticollis typically responds poorly to conservative therapy but shows dramatic response to botulinum toxin injection, making early neurology referral essential for optimal management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old recreational athlete reports sudden-onset sharp pain in the lower leg during a training session. Pain is severe enough to stop activity immediately.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot walk without pain or limping. Attempts to stand on tiptoe or perform calf raises provoke sharp pain and are not tolerated.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred without direct trauma—patient felt a sudden 'pop' or tearing sensation in the midcalf while accelerating. No significant prior leg injury, but returned to running after 2 weeks off. No calf cramps or DVT symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the calf muscles. Resisted plantarflexion or knee flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption, fluid collection, or hematoma within the calf. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Calf strain results from excessive tensile loading or rapid stretch of the gastrocnemius or soleus muscles, most commonly during explosive plantarflexion activities like sprinting or jumping. The classic presentation—sudden sharp pain with an audible or palpable 'pop' during acceleration—reflects acute muscle fiber disruption, typically at the musculotendinous junction where tensile forces concentrate. The inability to perform plantarflexion against resistance or rise onto tiptoe makes this condition mechanically distinctive; the muscle simply cannot generate sufficient force through the injured fibers. Bilateral nocturnal symptoms are absent here (distinguishing this from systemic conditions), while imaging findings of hematoma and fiber discontinuity confirm the structural defect rather than a cramp or circulation issue.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young adolescent presents for routine screening. No back pain or functional complaints reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports no difficulty with activities of daily living or sports. No complaints with posture or endurance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The curve has developed during growth and typically progresses during adolescent growth spurt. Family history of scoliosis may be present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On forward bend test, asymmetric rib prominence noted. Shoulder and hip heights are uneven. Thoracic curve suspected.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate the scoliotic curve with measurement of Cobb angle. Spine maturity is assessed. MRI may be obtained if atypical features present.

puzzles.explanation: Idiopathic adolescent scoliosis is a three-dimensional spinal curvature that develops during the growth years, affecting 2–3% of adolescents with a female predominance for curve progression. Unlike painful or secondary forms of scoliosis, idiopathic cases are asymptomatic and often detected incidentally during screening or through the characteristic asymmetries visible on forward bending—the rib hump and uneven shoulders seen here. The condition progresses most rapidly during periods of rapid skeletal growth, particularly in younger, less mature skeletons, making early identification and monitoring critical to prevent significant deformity. Management is guided by skeletal maturity and Cobb angle magnitude: curves under 25° in growing children warrant observation, while larger curves may require bracing to halt progression during the adolescent growth spurt.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult presenting for routine health screening. Reports no specific complaints, though some generalized aching in the spine and hips has been noted recently.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports reduced confidence with stairs and walking on uneven surfaces due to fear of falling. Avoids high-impact activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Postmenopausal female, 15 years since menopause. Long-term corticosteroid use for COPD. Mother had hip fracture at age 72.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Height loss of 4 cm compared to documented height 20 years ago. Thoracic kyphosis present. Able to walk independently but with cautious gait pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) demonstrates bone mineral density in the osteoporotic range. Radiographs may show compression fractures.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporosis is a systemic condition of reduced bone mineral density and deteriorated bone microarchitecture, leading to increased fragility and fracture risk. Multiple factors converged in this case: postmenopausal oestrogen deficiency, long-term corticosteroid use suppressing osteoblast activity, and likely genetic predisposition given the family history of hip fracture. The height loss, thoracic kyphosis, and cautious gait reflect cumulative vertebral compression fractures and the patient's understandable fear-avoidance response, which paradoxically reduces the mechanical loading needed to maintain bone density. DEXA scanning remains the diagnostic standard, with T-scores below −2.5 defining osteoporosis and guiding decisions about pharmacological intervention and fall prevention strategies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with acute pain in the anterior thigh that began during a training session. Pain is sharp and well-localised to one area.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activities combining hip flexion with knee extension—sprinting, kicking, jumping—consistently provoke pain. Patient avoids rapid hip/knee movement but tolerates walking and static stretching.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient is a competitive sprinter with no significant prior lower-limb injury. Onset was sudden mid-sprint; he felt a sharp pop or stabbing sensation. No preceding stiffness or warning signs.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the rectus femoris. Resisted knee extension or hip flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption or hematoma within the rectus femoris. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Rectus femoris strain occurs when the musculotendinous fibers of this biarticular quadriceps muscle are acutely disrupted, typically during movements that combine hip flexion with knee extension under load—exactly the demands of sprinting and kicking. The sudden sharp sensation and immediate functional loss, as experienced here during sprint acceleration, reflect the high eccentric forces the rectus femoris must resist when transitioning from hip flexion to rapid knee extension. Because this muscle crosses both the hip and knee, injury is particularly common in athletes performing explosive, dynamic movements; the anterior thigh tenderness and positive resisted tests clinically isolate the rectus femoris from the broader quadriceps group. Ultrasound and MRI confirmation of fiber disruption and hematoma differentiate this acute muscle tear from chronic overuse or referred hip pathology, guiding early load management and graduated return to sport.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old returns to hobby cycling after 6-month break. Sudden sharp pain in back of lower leg during a hill climb. Pain is focal, unilateral, and immediate in onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot pedal or climb stairs. Walking downhill is particularly painful. Able to walk on flat ground but with obvious limp. Calf stretching and sustained plantarflexion reproduce symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No preceding trauma or impact. Long sedentary period before returning to activity. No previous calf injury. No calf pain in opposite leg. Symptoms constant since onset 48 hours ago; swelling noticed in past 24 hours.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable muscle tenderness in medial calf. Calf circumference increased 2 cm compared to unaffected side. Pain on resisted plantarflexion and resisted knee flexion. No calf firmness or cord-like hardness. Ankle dorsiflexion mildly limited by pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic disruption within medial gastrocnemius muscle fibers with small perilesional fluid collection. No complete discontinuity of muscle. Doppler shows normal vascular flow. Findings consistent with partial-thickness muscle tear.

puzzles.explanation: Calf strain results from partial disruption of muscle fibers within the gastrocnemius or soleus complex, typically occurring when forceful plantarflexion or knee extension demands exceed the muscle's load tolerance — particularly in deconditioned individuals returning to activity. The sudden onset during hill climbing, combined with focal medial gastrocnemius tenderness and positive resisted plantarflexion, reflects acute myofascial injury rather than neural or vascular pathology. The hypoechoic disruption on ultrasound with preserved muscle continuity and the perilesional fluid collection distinguish this from complete rupture, explaining why the patient retains some function on flat ground despite significant pain with eccentric loading (downhill walking, stair descent) and sustained plantarflexion. Localized swelling, elevated calf circumference, and the pain pattern on resisted knee flexion — which loads the gastrocnemius across both the ankle and knee — confirm involvement of the biarticular muscle rather than isolated soleus pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old female. Gradual onset of deep pain in lower back and buttock region over past 8 weeks. Pain is mechanical in nature, worse by end of day, sometimes radiating down posterior thigh.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with prolonged standing, stairs, and rolling over in bed. Symptoms eased by lying down and wearing supportive belt around hips. Pain worsens with asymmetrical weight-bearing and walking on uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Currently 24 weeks gestation, second pregnancy. Onset coincided with second trimester. No antecedent trauma. First pregnancy was uncomplicated. No prior back pain or musculoskeletal conditions. Pain progressive since onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation reveals tenderness over sacroiliac joints and pubic symphysis region. Positive Patrick's test and positive pelvic distraction test bilaterally. Lumbar spine ROM normal. No neurological deficits. Mild swelling noted around sacroiliac joint area.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows widening of pubic symphysis (>6 mm) and increased vascularity. MRI (if performed) demonstrates increased signal intensity around sacroiliac joints and widened symphyseal gap consistent with ligamentous laxity related to hormonal changes of pregnancy.

puzzles.explanation: Antenatal pelvic girdle pain arises from ligamentous laxity and microinstability of the sacroiliac joints and pubic symphysis, driven by pregnancy-related hormonal changes—particularly relaxin—that increase collagen extensibility in preparation for labour. The condition typically emerges in the second or third trimester and presents with deep pelvic pain worsened by asymmetrical loading and mechanical activities like stair climbing, as the pregnant pelvis struggles to stabilize under increasing load and altered biomechanics. The bilateral sacroiliac and symphyseal tenderness, positive provocation tests (Patrick's and pelvic distraction), and imaging evidence of widened symphyseal gap and ligamentous swelling are hallmark features distinguishing this from isolated mechanical low back pain. Supportive belts and activity modification that favour symmetrical loading provide meaningful relief by reducing excessive joint motion and restabilizing the kinetic chain during pregnancy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old reports insidious shoulder pain worsening with overhead reaching. Reports difficulty sleeping due to night pain when lying on affected side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by overhead movements, lifting activities, or activities requiring arm elevation. The patient demonstrates functional limitation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms have developed gradually with repetitive shoulder activities or overuse. Recent increase in activity may be contributory.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive impingement signs including Neer's test and Hawkins' test are present. Pain with resisted shoulder abduction is demonstrated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates rotator cuff tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Rotator cuff tendinopathy results from repetitive microtrauma and degenerative changes within the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, or subscapularis tendons, typically occurring from chronic overuse or age-related changes in individuals over 50. The condition is characterised by nocturnal pain and functional limitation with overhead activities — the hallmark presentation — because the rotator cuff muscles are essential stabilisers during arm elevation, and inflammation or partial tearing compromises their ability to maintain glenohumeral stability. Positive impingement signs (Neer's, Hawkins') reflect secondary subacromial inflammation and subacromial space compromise, while imaging revealing tendon thickening and hypoechoic changes confirms the structural degenerative process. Unlike acute rotator cuff tears, tendinopathy typically responds well to load management, scapular stabilisation, and graduated strengthening, making early identification and conservative management critical to prevent progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult presents after a fall with acute shoulder pain and inability to use the arm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot initiate any active movement of the affected shoulder. Passive handling also provokes severe pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall from standing height approximately 2 hours ago. Known history of osteoporosis. Symptoms began immediately post-fall.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Swelling and ecchymosis visible over lateral shoulder. Painful arc present throughout all planes of passive motion. No sensation loss or motor weakness in upper limb distal to shoulder. Radial pulse intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate proximal humerus fracture with characterisation of displacement and pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Proximal humerus fractures represent one of the most common fractures in older adults, occurring when osteoporotic bone fails to absorb the energy of even a low-energy mechanism like a fall from standing height. The immediate onset of severe pain with complete loss of active shoulder motion and inability to tolerate passive movement reflects the acute periosteal disruption and capsular distension from haemarthrosis. The characteristic lateral shoulder swelling and ecchymosis, combined with neurovascular integrity (intact radial pulse and distal motor/sensory function), help distinguish this from axillary nerve injury or vascular compromise. Radiographic confirmation of displacement patterns is essential, as this guides whether conservative immobilisation or surgical fixation will best restore shoulder function in this population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old female yoga instructor reports insidious-onset bilateral pelvic pain that is asymmetric. Pain is worse on one side and occasionally refers to the groin and medial hip.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with asymmetric loading tasks (single-leg stance, unilateral hip extension). Relieved by symmetrical positions and activities that distribute load evenly across both sides.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient completed a full marathon four weeks prior and has recently begun practicing more advanced yoga postures with hip rotation emphasis. They have a history of pelvic instability post-pregnancy (age 28) but have remained asymptomatic until now.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gaenslen's test positive bilaterally but asymmetric. Pain provoked with single-leg hip extension (ipsilateral posterior pelvic pain). FABER and FADIR asymmetric. Hip internal/external rotation ROM symmetrical. Sacroiliac joint palpation tenderness on symptomatic side only.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan of the pelvis demonstrates unilateral sacroiliac joint involvement with mild capsular inflammation and small osteophytes along the joint margins. No evidence of fusion or significant degenerative changes.

puzzles.explanation: Sacroiliac joint (SIJ) dysfunction arises from altered mechanics or inflammation of the SIJ—the largest axial joint in the body—often triggered by repetitive asymmetric loading, rotational forces, or regional instability from prior pregnancy and childbirth. The bilateral but asymmetric pain pattern and exacerbation with single-leg loading tasks reflect the joint's role in force transmission during unilateral weight-bearing; when motion control is compromised, shear forces concentrate on the symptomatic side, frequently referring to the groin and medial hip through overlapping innervation patterns. In this case, the cumulative demand from marathon running followed by advanced hip rotation yoga postures likely overwhelmed stabilising capacity that had been marginal since pregnancy-related pelvic ligament laxity. Imaging showing unilateral capsular inflammation and early osteophyte formation confirms structural involvement, while the positive but asymmetric provocation tests (particularly Gaenslen's and single-leg extension) are hallmark findings that differentiate SIJ dysfunction from hip pathology, where rotation ROM would typically be restricted.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old graphic designer presents with right wrist pain and mild aching that worsens with repetitive gripping and keyboard use. Notices intermittent swelling in the wrist region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Gripping and forceful wrist extension trigger mild discomfort. Symptoms worsen with prolonged keyboard use and improve with rest. Patient notes wrist feels stiff after sustained activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms developed insidiously without prior wrist trauma. Recalls a transient dull ache in the wrist several months before current presentation. No history of wrist injury, arthritis, or prior hand/wrist surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, a firm, mobile, well-defined mass is noted over the dorsal wrist at the scapholunate region. The mass becomes more prominent during active wrist extension and less noticeable after rest. No significant warmth or erythema.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates a well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechoic fluid collection measuring 1.6 by 1.2cm at the dorsal scapholunate region with thin echogenic walls; the mass does not show internal vascularity on Doppler. MRI confirms a simple cyst containing fluid signal intensity with direct communication with the dorsal wrist joint capsule.

puzzles.explanation: A ganglion cyst is a benign, fluid-filled outpouching that arises from the joint capsule or tendon sheath, most commonly at the dorsal wrist at the scapholunate interval. It develops insidiously through a one-way valve mechanism that allows synovial fluid to herniate into surrounding soft tissues, creating a pressure-dependent mass that swells with activity and diminishes with rest. The firm, mobile character on palpation and direct capsular communication seen on imaging are hallmark features that distinguish it from other wrist masses. Although ganglion cysts are often asymptomatic, this patient's occupation involving repetitive gripping and keyboard use likely provokes the mass during activity, compressing adjacent structures and causing the characteristic aching that improves with rest and reduced wrist loading.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent hand paresthesias and weakness several weeks after wrist surgery. Symptoms are not fully resolved.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to return to grip-dependent tasks (e.g., gripping tools, writing for prolonged periods). Fine motor control remains compromised.

clues.label: History
