clues.text: Surgery performed 6-12 weeks ago. Initial post-operative swelling resolved, but functional improvement plateaued. Patient reports recurrent night symptoms and occasional sharp pains at surgical site.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Scar tissue thickened and tender over carpal tunnel site. Intrinsic hand muscle strength 4/5 (abductor pollicis brevis, lumbricals). Sensation in median nerve distribution demonstrates recovery but remains duller than contralateral side. Phalen's test negative; Tinel's sign positive at incision site.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Repeat EMG/NCS shows residual median nerve conduction slowing across the wrist with reduced amplitude compared to pre-operative baseline, consistent with incomplete axonal recovery or early recurrent compression.

puzzles.explanation: Post-carpal tunnel release syndrome describes a spectrum of poor outcomes following decompression surgery, ranging from incomplete nerve recovery to recurrent compression from scar tissue formation or inadequate initial decompression. The persistent paresthesias and nocturnal symptoms weeks after surgery, combined with positive Tinel's sign at the incision site and residual conduction slowing on EMG/NCS, indicate that the nerve has not fully recovered from either the original compression or the surgical trauma itself. Intrinsic hand weakness and thickened scar tissue are hallmarks of this complication, reflecting both axonal damage and the inflammatory response to surgery. Management typically involves scar mobilization, graded functional retraining, and in cases of true recurrent compression or inadequate initial decompression, consideration of revision surgery or neurolysis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Parent concerned about child's foot appearance, noting inward ankle posture and flattened arch when standing. Child may report mild foot fatigue with prolonged activity but gait appears functional.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child able to walk and run normally but may avoid prolonged standing or long-distance walking. Symptoms worse at end of day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset gradual over years. Family history of flat feet or foot problems. Child was able to walk and run since normal developmental milestones.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, medial arch is absent or minimal when standing. Weight-bearing foot posture shows eversion and valgus alignment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing radiographs show talonavicular uncovering and increased talar-first metatarsal angle. Medial arch collapses on standing but may reconstitute on toe-standing (flexible vs rigid differentiation).

puzzles.explanation: Pediatric flat feet result from incomplete development or laxity of the medial arch and supporting ligaments, with a strong genetic component that explains the family history often present. The condition is typically flexible — the arch reconstitutes when weight is removed or during toe-standing — reflecting normal ligamentous laxity rather than structural pathology, and resolves spontaneously in most children by age 8-10 as ligaments mature. The characteristic weight-bearing collapse with valgus hindfoot alignment and midfoot eversion, evident on radiographs showing increased talonavicular uncovering, may produce mild fatigue with prolonged activity but rarely requires intervention unless rigidity develops or pain becomes limiting. Most cases are physiological variants that warrant reassurance and activity modification rather than corrective treatment, though persistent symptoms or progressive rigidity warrant further investigation for underlying pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 24-year-old athlete. Sudden onset knee pain during sport 3 days ago. Reports immediate swelling and difficulty bearing weight. Knee feels unstable or 'wobbly' with certain movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform rapid direction changes or deceleration movements without knee buckling. Walking on stairs and uneven ground is difficult. Swelling is worse after activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during a jump-landing drill with foot planted and rapid body rotation. No prior knee injury or surgery. Immediate swelling within 2 hours. Denies hearing a 'pop' sound.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive anterior drawer test at 90 degrees flexion. Lachman test positive at 30 degrees flexion. Mild pivot shift elicited. Moderate effusion present. Knee ROM 15–100 degrees with mild pain. No collateral ligament laxity. Sensation and pulses intact distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows complete discontinuity of the anterior cruciate ligament at mid-substance. Associated medial meniscal tear noted. No bone contusions. Posterior capsule intact.

puzzles.explanation: Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tears result from sudden deceleration or pivoting forces that exceed the ligament's tensile capacity, particularly in non-contact mechanisms like jump-landing with a planted foot and rapid rotation. The ACL is the primary restraint to anterior tibial translation and rotational stability, so its rupture presents with immediate haemarthrosis, functional instability (buckling with rapid direction changes), and positive anterior drawer and Lachman tests — as seen in this acute presentation. The mechanism here is archetypal: the planted foot creates a fixed distal point while the body rotates over it, creating a sudden shear force that disrupts the ligament at its mid-substance. Associated medial meniscal tears are common in this injury pattern, as the knee's rotational instability can catch and damage the meniscus during the initial trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old retired teacher. Gradual onset over 18 months of stiffness and slowness affecting daily activities. Reports feeling less coordinated. No recent trauma or falls.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty rising from low chairs and bed. Takes longer to walk, especially when initiating movement or turning. Handwriting has become smaller and cramped. Buttons and zips now challenging. Facial expression feels restricted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset without clear precipitant. No significant head injury history. No relevant medication changes. Symptoms unilateral at onset, now bilateral but asymmetric. Spouse notes patient moves less spontaneously and speaks more quietly.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Uniform resistance to passive movement throughout range, present in flexors and extensors equally. Movement initiation slow; rapid finger tapping reveals decrement in speed and amplitude. Posture slightly flexed. Gait narrow-based with reduced arm swing on one side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain normal; no structural lesions, infarcts, or hydrocephalus. Dopamine transporter SPECT scan shows bilateral reduction in striatal uptake, asymmetric, consistent with nigrostriatal denervation.

puzzles.explanation: Parkinson's disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by loss of dopamine-producing neurons in the substantia nigra, resulting in dysfunction of the basal ganglia circuits controlling movement and motor planning. The classic tetrad — resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and postural instability — reflects this dopaminergic deficit; the uniform resistance to passive movement and decrement in rapid alternating movements observed here are hallmark motor features. The insidious unilateral onset progressing to bilateral asymmetric involvement is typical, as is the constellation of secondary features including micrographia, reduced facial expression, hypophonia, and loss of spontaneous movement. The SPECT findings confirming bilateral striatal denervation, along with the normal structural imaging excluding secondary causes, establish the diagnosis of idiopathic Parkinson's disease, for which dopaminergic replacement remains the cornerstone of symptomatic management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports hip pain and significant difficulty with lower limb weight-bearing and mobility. Onset was acute, following a surgical intervention several weeks ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot abduct the hip against gravity and demonstrates a compensatory limp. Lying flat and rolling in bed are particularly difficult. Sitting is limited to 30–45 minutes before pain worsens.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient underwent a major surgical procedure on the hip joint for advanced osteoarthritis. Recovery timeline is 6–12 weeks into rehabilitation. No prior hip pathology or surgery on the contralateral side. Comorbidities include mild hypertension, well controlled.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip abduction strength is 2/5 (poor, gravity-eliminated). Hip flexion ROM is 60°, abduction 20°. Pain is sharp and anterior with internal rotation. Glute med inhibition noted on palpation. No fever, wound healing normal. SLR positive for pain inhibition.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate appropriate prosthesis positioning and seating. Radiographs exclude mechanical complications.

puzzles.explanation: Post-total hip replacement (THR) involves significant soft-tissue disruption—particularly of the hip abductors (gluteus medius and minimus)—during the surgical approach, necessitating a structured 6–12 week rehabilitation protocol to restore strength and mobility. The marked weakness in hip abduction (2/5), combined with limited flexion ROM and anterior hip pain on internal rotation, reflects post-operative gluteal inhibition and capsular tightness rather than mechanical implant failure, as confirmed by normal imaging. The difficulty with bed mobility, compensatory limp pattern, and pain with prolonged sitting are characteristic of the early post-operative phase when tissue healing remains incomplete and neuromuscular control has not yet recovered. Progressive abductor strengthening, ROM restoration, and activity grading are essential to restore functional weight-bearing and prevent chronic movement compensations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A competitive athlete experiences sudden onset knee pain during rapid deceleration with a cutting maneuver in-sport. Reports immediate sensation of the knee 'giving way' and inability to continue activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports inability to perform plant and cut movements. Single-leg stance is unstable. Fear-avoidance behaviour limits weight-bearing activities, particularly on stairs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute injury during sport with non-contact mechanism—rapid direction change or pivoting on planted foot. The patient heard or felt an audible pop at moment of injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive Lachman test and anterior drawer test demonstrate anterior knee instability. Pivot shift test may be positive, indicating rotational instability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates complete anterior cruciate ligament tear with characteristic signal discontinuity. MRI characterises associated injuries including meniscal tears and bone bruising patterns.

puzzles.explanation: Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tears result from high-energy rotational forces applied to a planted, extended knee—typically during rapid deceleration or cutting maneuvers in sport—causing disruption of the primary restraint to anterior tibial translation. The classic presentation of an audible pop followed by immediate instability and effusion reflects the acute inflammatory response and loss of dynamic restraint that occurs with complete ligamentous rupture. ACL injuries are particularly disabling because the ligament cannot heal reliably; its poor blood supply and intra-articular location mean that rehabilitation must focus on neuromuscular control and quadriceps strength to compensate for the lost restraint, particularly during rotational activities. The pivot shift test—demonstrating dynamic rotational instability—combined with positive Lachman testing on MRI confirmation of signal discontinuity, represents the diagnostic standard and confirms why these athletes experience instability with cutting and pivoting movements even during simple tasks like stair negotiation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports progressive numbness and tingling in the feet, gradually worsening over months. Some difficulty with fine motor control in the hands noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty walking on uneven surfaces and balance impairment, particularly when eyes are closed. Trips on stairs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Diabetes diagnosed 15 years ago, poorly controlled. Recent fatigue and weight loss. No prior neurological symptoms. Family history of autoimmune disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, sensory loss in stocking-glove distribution is demonstrated. Reflexes are diminished or absent. Weakness may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography demonstrates reduced amplitudes of compound muscle action potentials with normal conduction velocities, consistent with axonal degeneration. Nerve conduction study shows length-dependent pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Peripheral neuropathy encompasses a diverse group of disorders affecting the peripheral nerves, with diabetic neuropathy being the most common form in developed nations, resulting from hyperglycemia-induced axonal degeneration over time. The condition classically presents with length-dependent sensory symptoms — progressive numbness and tingling in a stocking-glove distribution affecting the distal lower limbs first — alongside proprioceptive loss that manifests as balance impairment and difficulty navigating uneven surfaces, particularly when visual compensation is removed. Axonal degeneration produces reduced compound muscle action potentials on EMG with relatively preserved conduction velocities, distinguishing it from demyelinating neuropathies, and the combination of prolonged hyperglycemia, diminished reflexes, and sensorimotor deficits in this patient represents the classic presentation of distal symmetric polyneuropathy. Early recognition and glycemic control are crucial, as progression can lead to significant functional decline and increased fall risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old warehouse worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the hand that worsens at night and affects sleep quality. The symptoms began insidiously 3 months ago without preceding trauma. Pain radiates from the elbow down to the ring and little fingers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty gripping tools at work and has dropped several items in the past month. Prolonged elbow flexion during computer work exacerbates symptoms, while extending the arm and shaking the hand provides temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works assembly lines requiring repetitive elbow flexion. Past medical history includes rheumatoid arthritis controlled on medication. The patient denies neck pain or shoulder involvement. Symptoms are worse in the morning after sleeping with the elbow bent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tinel's sign is positive at the medial elbow. Froment's sign demonstrates weakness of thumb adduction. Intrinsic hand muscle atrophy is visible between thumb and index finger. Two-point discrimination is diminished in the ulnar distribution compared to the contralateral side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging shows enlargement and hypoechoic appearance of the ulnar nerve at the medial elbow, consistent with nerve swelling. The nerve measures 0.7 cm in cross-sectional area at the compression site, well above normal limits of 0.4 cm.

puzzles.explanation: Cubital tunnel syndrome results from compression of the ulnar nerve as it travels behind the medial epicondyle of the humerus, the most common site of ulnar nerve entrapment. The condition typically presents with progressive paraesthesia in the ring and little fingers—the ulnar nerve distribution—often worse at night and upon waking, as sustained elbow flexion during sleep kinks the nerve and restricts blood flow. The warehouse worker's repetitive flexion demands and inflammatory milieu from rheumatoid arthritis both contributed to nerve swelling visible on ultrasound, leading to motor deficit evidenced by Froment's sign weakness and intrinsic hand muscle atrophy. Positive Tinel's sign at the medial elbow and diminished ulnar-distribution sensation confirm the compression site, distinguishing this from cervical or proximal ulnar nerve pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An adolescent athlete reports anterior knee pain that worsens with physical activity, particularly jumping and running sports.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot kneel without discomfort, and pain is reproducibly worse with activities involving forceful knee extension or explosive movements. Sitting with knee bent for prolonged periods is tolerated.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The adolescent is typically 11-18 years old during growth spurt. Symptoms correlate with periods of rapid growth. Sport involvement is common.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, swelling and tenderness are present at the tibial tuberosity. Pain is reproduced with knee extension against resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may show tibial tuberosity enlargement and fragmentation. MRI demonstrates tibial tuberosity inflammation and soft tissue involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is a traction apophysitis of the tibial tuberosity caused by repetitive stress on the patellar tendon insertion during periods of rapid skeletal growth, most common in adolescents aged 11–18 involved in jumping and running sports. The condition results from a mismatch between the rapid lengthening of the femur and tibia during growth spurts and the relatively inelastic musculotendinous unit, creating excessive tensile force at the apophyseal attachment. The characteristic presentation of anterior knee pain worsened by forceful knee extension and jumping — and notably spared with passive knee flexion during prolonged sitting — reflects the load-dependent nature of this tendon-bone interface injury. Tibial tuberosity swelling, tenderness, and radiographic fragmentation are pathognomonic findings that distinguish this from other causes of anterior knee pain in young athletes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports severe muscle pain and weakness developing during or immediately after an intense workout. Severe global malaise and marked fatigue noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to continue or repeat the activity that triggered symptoms. Severe global malaise and fatigue limit all physical activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Triggered by unaccustomed intense exercise (e.g., new training program, military bootcamp, extreme heat event). Patient has no prior history of similar episodes or known muscle disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe muscle tenderness and induration palpable in exercised muscle groups. Calf or quad swelling noted. Neurological examination intact. Urine dipstick positive for blood/myoglobin with absence of RBCs on microscopy.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serum creatine kinase >5000 IU/L (often >10,000). Urine myoglobin present. Serum creatinine elevated with oliguria, consistent with acute tubular necrosis from myoglobin precipitation.

puzzles.explanation: Exertional rhabdomyolysis is acute muscle breakdown triggered by unaccustomed intense exercise, where myofibrils rupture and release their contents—including myoglobin, potassium, and creatine kinase—into the circulation. The condition classically presents with severe muscle pain and swelling in the exercised compartments, accompanied by systemic symptoms like profound malaise and fatigue reflecting the massive metabolic insult. The pathognomonic finding is myoglobinuria (dipstick positive for blood without RBCs on microscopy) coupled with markedly elevated serum CK, which overwhelms the kidneys and can precipitate acute tubular necrosis and oliguria. Because it occurs in previously healthy individuals without prior muscle disease who push beyond their training threshold—particularly in hot environments where dehydration compounds the injury—early recognition and aggressive fluid resuscitation are critical to prevent renal failure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with migraine headache history presents with episodes of vertigo that occur with or separate from headache. Vertigo episodes are recurrent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports significant balance impairment during episodes, particularly difficulty with gait and head movements. Nausea and photophobia are prominent accompanying features.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Migraines began in early adulthood. Vertigo episodes last minutes to hours. Triggers include stress, certain foods, and hormonal changes. No prior head trauma or hearing loss. Patient has tried migraine prophylaxis with variable response.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positional nystagmus or gaze-evoked nystagmus may be present. Balance testing shows abnormalities. Neurological examination is otherwise normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain excludes other causes of vertigo. MRI is normal in vestibular migraine.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular migraine is a central vestibular disorder where migraine pathophysiology triggers recurrent episodes of vertigo, which can occur with or independently from headache. Unlike peripheral vestibular conditions, it typically presents with vertigo lasting minutes to hours accompanied by prominent autonomic features like nausea and photophobia, alongside gait imbalance and sensitivity to head movements. The condition emerges from brainstem and vestibular nucleus involvement during migraine activation, with common triggers including stress, dietary factors, and hormonal fluctuations—particularly affecting women in early adulthood. Normal brain imaging and a predominantly central examination pattern (gaze-evoked nystagmus rather than positional vertigo) help distinguish this from peripheral causes, making it fundamentally a migraine variant requiring prophylactic migraine management alongside vestibular rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with pain in the groin region or lower abdominal region. Pain develops with athletic activity and resolves with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or twisting movements. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. Sport involving rapid acceleration and deceleration is typical.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited in the groin region and lower abdomen. Pain is reproduced with resisted hip adduction and internal rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates adductor muscle strain or rectus femoris tear near the pubic symphysis. MRI characterises muscle injury and inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Athletic pubalgia is a chronic groin pain syndrome resulting from overuse injury to the musculotendinous structures around the pubic symphysis, particularly the adductor longus, rectus abdominis, and rectus femoris. It develops in athletes engaged in sports requiring rapid acceleration, deceleration, and cutting — the high-demand movements that overload these structures beyond their capacity to adapt. The bilateral muscular control demands of kicking and twisting, combined with sudden increases in training load, create the perfect conditions for microtrauma and inflammatory changes at the pubic insertion sites. Resisted hip adduction and internal rotation reliably provoke pain because these movements maximally stress the already-compromised adductor complex, making them invaluable clinical tests for confirmation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational athlete presents with medial-sided knee pain and mechanical symptoms. Pain is moderate intensity and worsened by certain movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with stairs, squatting, and kneeling due to joint line discomfort. Walking with an antalgic gait. Pain worsens with deep knee flexion and rotational activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of twisting injury while playing football 3 weeks ago with subsequent surgical intervention. Moderate pain interfering with sport and daily activities. No prior knee injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild joint effusion on examination. Quadriceps strength 4/5, VMO atrophy evident. Joint line tenderness medially reproduced. Knee flexion range 95 degrees with terminal extension lag 5 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows meniscal resection with intact ACL, PCL, and collateral ligaments. No acute chondral lesions identified. Joint space preserved. No loose body.

puzzles.explanation: Post-meniscectomy knee pain represents the immediate sequelae following surgical removal of damaged meniscal tissue, typically required when traumatic tears compromise joint mechanics beyond repair. The meniscus serves as both a shock absorber and load distributor; its loss increases mechanical stress on articular cartilage and alters knee biomechanics, particularly in deep flexion and rotational movements where meniscal stability is critical. The presentation here — medial joint line tenderness, difficulty with stairs and squatting, and quadriceps weakness with VMO atrophy — reflects the acute post-operative phase and loss of that protective meniscal function, combined with expected muscle inhibition and effusion following arthroscopic surgery. Recovery depends on progressive weight-bearing rehabilitation and strength restoration; persistent symptoms beyond 6–8 weeks warrant reassessment for residual damage, post-operative adhesions, or early osteoarthritis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old presents with unilateral lower back pain. Onset gradual over several months. No trauma or specific incident. Pain is mechanical in nature, varies with activity and position.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with standing and walking, especially over longer distances. Bending backward aggravates symptoms significantly. Patient reports relief when sitting or flexing forward. Difficulty with stairs and prolonged upright posture.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic intermittent symptoms with episodic flares over past 2 years. No radicular pain or leg weakness. Recent increase in gardening and yard work preceded current exacerbation. Previous episodes resolved with rest and activity modification. No significant prior spinal trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Extension and ipsilateral rotation of the lumbar spine reproduce local pain. Flexion and contralateral rotation do not provoke symptoms. Unilateral tenderness elicited on deep palpation over the posterior aspect of the spine at L4-L5 level. Straight leg raise negative. Normal lower extremity strength, sensation, and reflexes. No neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows facet joint hypertrophy and osteophyte formation at L4-L5 on one side. Mild degenerative changes within the facet joint including cartilage loss and capsular thickening. No central canal stenosis, no neural foraminal compromise, no disc herniation. Findings correlate with clinical examination and pain reproduction.

puzzles.explanation: Facet joint syndrome results from degenerative changes within the zygapophysial joints of the spine, typically involving cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and capsular inflammation that irritate the joint and surrounding soft tissues. It presents as mechanical, unilateral lower back pain that worsens with extension and ipsilateral rotation — movements that load the facet joints — and improves with flexion, as seen in this patient's gardening-aggravated presentation. Unlike nerve root compression, facet syndrome produces local pain without radiculopathy or neurological deficits, making the negative straight leg raise and normal lower limb findings key discriminators. The combination of extension-rotation provocation, posterior spine tenderness, and imaging confirmation of unilateral facet hypertrophy at L4-L5 makes this diagnosis straightforward and highly treatable through activity modification, joint mobilisation, and graduated strengthening.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An adolescent reports progressive upper back pain that worsens with activity and prolonged sitting. Pain is mechanical in nature.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty maintaining upright posture for prolonged periods. Appearance shows exaggerated rounding of the upper back.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began in early adolescence with gradual worsening over 1–2 years. Family history of similar spinal deformity is noted. No prior significant back injury or trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, fixed thoracic kyphosis is evident that does not correct with forward bending. Back pain is elicited with extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate thoracic vertebral body wedging and Schmorl's nodes characteristic of Scheuermann's disease. Kyphosis is measured.

puzzles.explanation: Scheuermann's disease is a structural disorder of the thoracic spine characterized by anterior vertebral body wedging and endplate irregularities (Schmorl's nodes) that develop during the adolescent growth spurt, likely due to biomechanical stress on weakened growth plates. The resulting fixed thoracic kyphosis distinguishes it from postural kyphosis—it persists on forward bending and cannot be corrected voluntarily, creating the progressive upper back rounding and mechanical pain worsening with activity and sitting that are hallmarks of the condition. The adolescent onset, gradual progression, and familial clustering seen here reflect its developmental nature and genetic predisposition. Pain on extension and difficulty maintaining upright posture occur because the deformity loads the posterior elements and compromises thoracic mobility; conservative management with activity modification and postural training is typically effective in preventing progression during the growth years.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old former construction worker. Gradual onset of shortness of breath over 3 years, initially only with stairs but now present at rest. Persistent dry cough. No recent infection or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk more than 50 metres without stopping to catch breath. Oxygen saturation drops with minimal exertion. Difficulty sleeping flat; requires multiple pillows. No relief with rest or positional changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Worked 40 years in dusty environments (stone cutting, demolition). Symptoms began years after occupational exposure ended. Non-smoker. No recent illness, infections, or medication changes. Progressive despite lifestyle modifications.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Fine crackles heard throughout lung bases on auscultation, worse on inspiration. Reduced air entry bilaterally. Finger clubbing present. Normal heart sounds. No oedema. Oxygen saturation 88% at rest, drops to 80% on minimal activity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest shows bilateral reticular opacities and ground-glass infiltrates in lower lung lobes with honeycombing pattern. Pulmonary function tests reveal restrictive pattern: FVC reduced, FEV1/FVC ratio elevated, DLCO significantly reduced.

puzzles.explanation: Pulmonary fibrosis is progressive scarring and stiffening of lung tissue, most commonly due to chronic inhalation injury—in this case, decades of occupational dust exposure from stone cutting and demolition. The condition develops insidiously as inhaled particles trigger inflammatory changes that evolve into irreversible collagen deposition, explaining the delayed onset years after exposure ended and the relentless progression despite cessation of work. The characteristic clinical triad—exertional dyspnoea with early desaturation, inspiratory crackles at the bases, and digital clubbing—combined with restrictive spirometry and the honeycombing pattern on high-resolution CT are diagnostic hallmarks. The significantly reduced diffusion capacity (DLCO) reflects the thickened alveolar-capillary membrane created by fibrosis, making gas exchange increasingly difficult with any demand.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old female presents with difficulty controlling bladder and bowel function that has progressed over 8 months. She reports urgency and occasional incontinence during exercise, coughing, or sneezing. There is also pain with sexual intercourse.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient has modified her routine to avoid jumping or running due to urine leakage. She performs Kegel exercises but reports poor muscle control. Prolonged sitting worsens pelvic discomfort, while lying down relieves symptoms. She avoids tampon use due to discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She had two vaginal deliveries 15 and 10 years ago with episiotomy noted during second delivery. She reports a history of chronic constipation. Menstrual cycles are regular without hormonal contraceptive use. She denies previous pelvic surgery beyond childbirth.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Internal palpation reveals weak muscle contraction during requested pelvic floor squeeze, graded 2/5. A cough stress test produces leakage before pelvic floor contraction occurs. Tender trigger points are noted in the pelvic floor muscles bilaterally during assessment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates descent of the bladder base beyond 5 cm on Valsalva maneuver, indicating hypermobility. Pelvic floor thickness measures 0.6 cm on endovaginal ultrasound, below the normal range of 0.8-1.2 cm, confirming atrophy and dysfunction.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic floor dysfunction represents a spectrum of conditions involving weakness, incoordination, or tension in the pelvic floor musculature, which normally supports continence and sexual function. In this case, the combination of stress urinary incontinence during exertion, weak voluntary muscle contraction (2/5), and imaging evidence of both muscular atrophy and bladder hypermobility reflects the classic sequel of vaginal childbirth—tissue trauma, denervation, and structural changes that compromise the pelvic floor's supportive role. The concurrent myofascial trigger points and dyspareunia suggest that patients may develop compensatory muscle tension and pain patterns alongside weakness. Management requires a multimodal approach including targeted pelvic floor muscle training, activity modification, and assessment for contributing factors such as chronic constipation, which perpetuates ongoing mechanical stress.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports pain, stiffness, and difficulty with movement following thermal injury. Onset was acute; now 6 weeks post-injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot reach across midline or overhead. Difficulty with fine hand movements and walking due to lower limb tightness. Functional independence in ADLs is severely compromised.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sustained deep partial thickness and full thickness burns to upper and lower limbs 6 weeks ago. Initial acute treatment completed; now progressing through scar maturation phase. No prior burns or significant medical history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible hypertrophic scarring over dorsal hands and anterior shins with blanching erythema. Elbow flexion limited to 100° and ankle dorsiflexion to 0° due to soft tissue tightness. No sensory loss; strength intact but guarded by pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickened dermis with heterogeneous echogenicity consistent with hypertrophic scar. No evidence of underlying tissue compromise or active infection.

puzzles.explanation: Burns rehabilitation addresses the complex tissue remodeling and functional recovery following thermal injury, particularly during the scar maturation phase where hypertrophic scarring and contracture formation dominate the clinical picture. Deep partial and full-thickness burns destroy the dermis and its elastic architecture, triggering excessive collagen deposition and myofibroblast contraction that can persist for months—the blanching erythema and thickened dermis seen here are hallmarks of this phase. The characteristic loss of overhead reach and ankle dorsiflexion reflects contracture development across joints, driven by scar tension along tissue planes and compounded by pain-guarded immobility. Early aggressive therapy—stretching, splinting, and progressive loading—is critical to counteract these contractures and restore functional range before scar tissue matures into fixed deformities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient presents with acute onset shoulder pain and visible swelling following a fall. Pain is reproduced with shoulder movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to abduct or elevate the arm. Arm splinting by patient. Unable to perform overhead activities or carry objects.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall directly onto the point of the shoulder during contact sport. Heard/felt a crack at time of injury. No prior shoulder pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable step deformity or prominence at the clavicle. Horizontal adduction (cross-body) test elicits focal pain. Scapular dyskinesis evident. No neurological deficit distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate clavicle fracture with characterisation of displacement and comminution. Imaging excludes neurovascular injury.

puzzles.explanation: Clavicle fractures result from direct trauma to the shoulder girdle, commonly occurring at the junction of the middle and lateral thirds where the bone is weakest. The acute mechanism of a fall directly onto the shoulder, combined with the audible crack reported here, is classic for this injury pattern. The palpable step deformity and focal tenderness over the clavicle are hallmark clinical signs, while the scapular dyskinesis and loss of arm elevation reflect disruption of the force couple that normally stabilizes the shoulder during movement. Radiographic confirmation of displacement and comminution helps guide management decisions, ranging from conservative care with sling immobilization for non-displaced fractures to surgical fixation when displacement is significant or comminution present.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old construction supervisor presents with severe lower back pain that began suddenly while lifting a heavy load 2 weeks ago. The pain radiates down the left leg into the foot with associated numbness in the foot. Sitting and bending forward intensely worsen the pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot sit for more than 15 minutes without severe radiating pain. Lying flat with the left leg elevated provides mild relief. Bending forward to pick up objects triggers sharp shooting pain down the left leg. Light walking is tolerated better than sitting or standing still.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 10-year history of intermittent lower back pain managed with NSAIDs and physical therapy. He reports smoking one pack daily and spends 8 hours per day in a vehicle for work. No prior episodes of leg pain. Family history includes degenerative disc disease in his father.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Straight leg raise test is positive on the left at 35 degrees with reproduction of radiating pain. Ankle dorsiflexion weakness is demonstrated as 4/5 on the left. Sensory testing shows decreased sensation in the L5 dermatome on the left foot. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are intact bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine reveals a large posterolateral disc herniation at L4-L5 with significant compression of the left L5 nerve root. The herniated disc material is noted to be sequestered, and there is associated left-sided foraminal stenosis at the level of involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar disc herniation occurs when the nucleus pulposus ruptures through the annulus fibrosus, most commonly at L4–L5 or L5–S1 where mechanical stresses concentrate. The herniated material can compress nerve roots exiting the spinal canal, causing radiculopathy — a clinical syndrome combining dermatomal pain, sensory loss, and motor weakness. The presentation here is characteristic: posterolateral herniation compressing the L5 root producing left leg pain, L5 dermatomal sensory loss, and ankle dorsiflexion weakness (foot drop risk), with a positive straight leg raise reflecting neural tension irritation. The acute onset during heavy lifting in a patient with chronic mechanical low back pain and risk factors like smoking and occupational loading illustrates how repetitive flexion and degeneration predispose to disc herniation, while the sequestered material and foraminal stenosis explain why symptom relief comes with leg elevation — a position that reduces neural tension — rather than forward bending, which further compresses the root.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old racquetball player presents with wrist pain on the ulnar side that began gradually during a tournament 6 weeks ago. Pain is located on the medial aspect of the wrist just above the wrist crease and worsens with gripping activities. There is associated clicking sensation in the wrist.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Racquet sports and activities requiring power grip aggravate the pain significantly. Twisting motions of the wrist, particularly combined with ulnar deviation, provoke sharp pain. The patient reports weakness when attempting to lift objects with the affected hand. Rest and ice provide temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has played racquetball competitively for 15 years with no prior wrist injuries. He reports recent increase in training intensity. No systemic inflammatory history. The patient is right-hand dominant with left wrist affected. He denies previous wrist fractures or dislocations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The ulnar fovea sign is positive with palpable laxity of the ulnocarpal ligaments. Positive ulnar fovea sign test reproduces the patient's clicking. The ulnar meniscus load test provokes pain with axial compression and ulnar deviation combined. Grip strength is reduced 25% on the affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the wrist shows a tear of the triangular fibrocartilage with associated fluid signal extending into the tear. There is mild signal change in the lunate bone consistent with early chondral injury. The ulnar styloid process is intact without fracture or edema.

puzzles.explanation: The triangular fibrocartilage complex (TFCC) is a load-bearing and stabilizing structure on the ulnar side of the wrist, comprising the triangular fibrocartilage disc, ulnar collateral ligament, and adjacent capsular structures that together cushion axial loads and prevent ulnar translation. TFCC tears typically occur from repetitive axial loading with ulnar deviation — exactly the mechanism in high-intensity racquetball — and present with ulnar-sided wrist pain, clicking, and weakness with gripping. The positive ulnar fovea sign and reproduction of symptoms with the ulnar meniscus load test are hallmark findings that indicate disruption of the central disc and ligamentous laxity. The gradual onset, association with increased training intensity, and MRI confirmation of a tear with early chondral changes underscore why early recognition and activity modification are critical to prevent progression toward ulnocarpal impaction syndrome.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old fell onto outstretched hand while skateboarding. Now reports sharp pain in the lateral elbow with significant swelling over the past 2 hours. Pain is constant, 7/10, and radiates slightly into the forearm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to fully straighten the elbow. Rotating the forearm (both directions) triggers sharp pain. Gripping is weak and painful. Lifting objects above shoulder height is impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct impact to the lateral elbow during fall. No prior elbow injuries. Swelling appeared within minutes. Patient denies any numbness, tingling, or neck symptoms. No loss of consciousness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized tenderness in the groove lateral to the olecranon. Elbow range slightly restricted in extension. Pain with pronation and supination, especially near midrange. Posterior interosseous nerve testing intact. No neurovascular compromise. Elbow effusion present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs (AP, lateral, radial head view) reveal a non-displaced fracture through the radial head articular surface. No associated fractures noted. CT confirms simple undisplaced pattern without comminution.

puzzles.explanation: A radial head fracture occurs when a direct fall onto an outstretched hand transmits axial load through the radius to the radial head articulation, creating an intra-articular fracture — most common at the elbow in fall mechanisms like this. The radial head's critical role in pronation and supination explains why rotation in both directions triggers sharp pain, and its contribution to elbow stability accounts for the effusion and loss of extension. Non-displaced fractures like this one carry an excellent prognosis with conservative management, since neurovascular structures remain protected and early mobilisation can prevent stiffness; the key is distinguishing simple patterns from comminuted fractures that may require surgical intervention. The absence of nerve or vascular symptoms despite swelling confirms the fracture hasn't compromised the posterior interosseous nerve or brachial artery, which guides your rehabilitation approach toward early protected range of motion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old tripped on stairs and caught themselves with outstretched arm. Now reports acute wrist pain, immediate onset swelling, and inability to move wrist. No numbness or tingling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot grip, pinch, or bear weight through affected arm. Wrist locked in slightly flexed position. Pain severe with any attempt at wrist extension or rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct fall 2 hours ago onto extended wrist. No previous wrist injuries. No osteoporosis history. No loss of consciousness. Pain progressively worsening; swelling now visible from hand to forearm.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist grossly swollen with ecchymosis developing. Bony tenderness over distal forearm. Wrist held in protective flexion. Mild loss of radial-sided hand contour. Fingers pink, warm, capillary refill normal. Sensation intact. No crepitus on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Posteroanterior and lateral wrist radiographs show transverse fracture through distal radius metaphysis with dorsal displacement and posterior angulation of the distal fragment. Radial height loss noted. No ulnar fracture. Intact radiocarpal joint space.

puzzles.explanation: A Colles fracture is a transverse fracture through the distal radius metaphysis with characteristic dorsal displacement and posterior angulation of the distal fragment, typically resulting from a fall onto an outstretched wrist (FOOSH injury) in older adults or those with compromised bone quality. The mechanism here—a direct fall with the wrist extended—is the classic presentation, and the dorsal angulation creates the distinctive 'dinner fork' deformity visible on lateral imaging. The acute severe pain, rapid swelling extending from hand to forearm, and protective wrist flexion reflect the trauma to the distal radius and surrounding soft tissues; the intact neurovascular status (normal capillary refill, sensation preserved) is reassuring for compartment syndrome risk. Recognition of the specific fracture pattern—distal radius with dorsal displacement rather than volar—is critical because it determines reduction technique and prognostic implications for wrist function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old male reports burning pain and numbness on the outer thigh that began 3 months ago and is progressively worsening. The symptoms are localized to the lateral thigh and do not extend below the knee. He describes a constant burning sensation with occasional electric shock sensations.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged standing and walking exacerbate the burning pain and numbness significantly. Wearing tight waistbands, belts, or sitting with legs crossed worsens the symptoms dramatically. Lying down with the hip flexed provides relief. The patient has reduced his walking distance from 30 minutes to 5 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 40-pound weight gain over the past 18 months and recently switched to wearing tighter fitting work pants due to his occupation. He denies lumbar pain or lower extremity radiculopathy. Recent history includes a fall onto his hip, though skin over the affected area is intact.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sensation is diminished to light touch and pinprick over the lateral thigh in the L2-L3 dermatome distribution. Straight leg raise test is negative. Hip flexion and extension strength are normal 5/5. Tapping over the inguinal ligament just medial to the anterior superior iliac spine reproduces the burning sensation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the pelvis and hip shows no mass or structural lesion at the inguinal ligament. The appearance is normal without focal nerve enlargement. Imaging confirms the absence of lumbar radiculopathy or hip joint pathology, supporting a diagnosis of nerve entrapment rather than central or referred pain.

puzzles.explanation: Meralgia paraesthetica is a neuropathy caused by entrapment of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve as it passes under or through the inguinal ligament, typically resulting from local compression rather than radiculopathy. The condition classically presents with burning pain and paresthesia confined to the lateral thigh — the nerve's sensory distribution — without motor involvement or extension below the knee. Risk factors include weight gain, tight waistbands, and direct trauma to the groin area, all of which increase compression at the inguinal ligament; symptoms characteristically worsen with prolonged standing and hip extension while improving with hip flexion and loose clothing. The diagnosis is clinical, supported by reproducible tenderness at the inguinal ligament and preserved strength and reflexes, with imaging serving mainly to exclude structural mimics like masses or hernias.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old presents with acute onset chest wall pain following a fall onto a hard surface. Pain is sharp, localised to one side, and significantly worse when taking a deep breath or coughing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports severe difficulty with coughing and unable to lie on the affected side. Pain worsens with trunk twisting and forward bending, but is relieved somewhat when patient splints chest with pillow during cough.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct impact to left lateral chest wall 3 days ago during fall at home. Pain onset was immediate. No previous chest injury. Patient reports pain gradually improving but still significant. No dyspnoea, cough, or haemoptysis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localised point tenderness elicited over lateral ribs 5-7 on left. Pain reproduced with compression across chest wall (anteroposterior squeeze). Normal breath sounds bilaterally. No crepitus, swelling, or subcutaneous emphysema. Normal cardiovascular examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest shows linear fracture through left 6th rib with minimal displacement. No pneumothorax, haemothorax, pulmonary contusion, or injury to mediastinal structures. Fracture margins are sharp without callus formation.

puzzles.explanation: Rib fractures result from direct blunt trauma causing a break in the bony rib, with pain severity driven by the rib's attachment to intercostal muscles and pleura. The acute sharp, pleuritic pain worsened by deep breathing, coughing, and trunk movement reflects both the fracture site irritation and splinting effect — patients naturally restrict chest wall excursion to reduce stress across the fracture. The localised point tenderness and positive anteroposterior squeeze test are hallmark findings, while the absence of pneumothorax or mediastinal injury on imaging indicates an uncomplicated fracture that responds well to conservative management with analgesia and respiratory physiology support. The gradual improvement over three days is typical, as simple rib fractures heal through callus formation over 6-12 weeks, though splinting and pain control remain critical to prevent secondary complications like pneumonia from restricted breathing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with repetitive occupational activity presents with pain in the proximal dorsal forearm at the second dorsal compartment. Pain is worse with gripping and resisted wrist extension.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by repetitive wrist extension and resisted finger extension. Patient reports visible swelling in the proximal forearm and pain with activities like rowing, rock climbing, or racquet sports.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms onset insidious over weeks. Patient is a rower, rock climber, or tennis player. No acute trauma, prior forearm injury, or inflammatory history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the proximal dorsal forearm where extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis cross over extensor carpi radialis and extensor digitorum communis. Crepitus or fine cracking sensation may be appreciated with movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates tenosynovitis and fluid at the intersection of the second and third dorsal compartments. MRI shows peritendinous edema and tendon sheath inflammation at the crossing point.

puzzles.explanation: Intersection syndrome occurs where the extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis tendons cross over the extensor carpi radialis and extensor digitorum communis in the proximal dorsal forearm, creating a region of mechanical friction and compression. This overuse injury typically develops insidiously in athletes and workers performing repetitive wrist extension and gripping—rowers, climbers, and racquet players are classic presentations—as the tendons glide repeatedly against one another during movement. The characteristic proximal dorsal swelling, crepitus, and pain worse with active extension reflect the underlying tenosynovitis and peritendinous inflammation at this anatomical crossing point. Unlike de Quervain's tenosynovitis, which involves the first dorsal compartment, intersection syndrome is specifically localized several centimetres proximal to the wrist and responds well to activity modification and relative rest once recognized.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Infant presents with weakness and flaccidity of one arm noticed immediately after delivery. Caregiver reports the arm hangs limp at the side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Infant unable to lift the affected arm against gravity. No protective movement or grasp response on affected side. Cries asymmetrically during distress.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia requiring significant traction. Weakness noted in the delivery room. Parents report arm has remained weak over first 2 weeks with no improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Arm held in adduction and internal rotation (waiter's tip posture). Weakness predominantly in shoulder abduction and external rotation. Biceps and deltoid strength severely reduced. Wrist and finger movements relatively preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography documents denervation changes in affected muscles. MRI may show nerve root avulsion if severe.

puzzles.explanation: Erb's palsy results from trauma to the upper trunk of the brachial plexus (C5–C6 nerve roots) during delivery, typically from excessive lateral traction on the head during shoulder dystocia. The injury causes denervation of the deltoid, rotator cuff, and biceps, leaving the infant unable to abduct or externally rotate the shoulder—hence the characteristic waiter's tip posture with the arm adducted and internally rotated. Most infants recover spontaneously within 3–6 months as nerve continuity is usually preserved, but persistent weakness beyond this window or evidence of nerve root avulsion on imaging indicates the need for surgical intervention. Early physiotherapy focusing on passive range of motion and later active strengthening is critical to prevent contracture and optimize functional recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old presents with sudden onset of weakness and numbness on one side of the body. Noticed while at rest this morning. No trauma or recent illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk or bear weight on affected leg. Difficulty with fine motor control and coordination on affected side. Unable to lift leg against gravity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began acutely 2 hours ago with no warning. Long-standing hypertension (poorly controlled) and 30-year smoking history. No prior episodes of weakness or numbness. Currently on no antiplatelet therapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lower extremity strength 3/5 on affected side; upper extremity strength 5/5 bilaterally. Hyperreflexia and extensor plantar response on affected side. Mild facial droop absent. Mild confusion noted; language intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT head non-contrast: no hemorrhage. MRI with diffusion imaging shows acute infarct in medial frontal lobe and anterior limb of internal capsule on one side. Perfusion deficit matches arterial distribution pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Anterior cerebral artery (ACA) stroke results from thrombotic or embolic occlusion of the ACA, disrupting blood flow to the medial frontal lobe and anterior limb of the internal capsule — territory classically sparing the motor cortex face area while affecting the lower extremity motor homunculus. This vascular distribution explains the selective lower limb weakness with preserved facial strength, and the medial frontal lobe involvement accounts for the mild confusion despite intact language function. Poorly controlled hypertension and decades of smoking create an atherosclerotic substrate for acute thrombotic events, with the sudden onset at rest and rapid progression to motor and cognitive deficits being hallmark presentations of acute ischemic stroke. The absence of hemorrhage on non-contrast CT and the acute diffusion restriction on MRI confirm acute ischemia rather than hemorrhage, guiding immediate thrombolytic or thrombectomy candidacy depending on time from symptom onset.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged patient presents with central wrist pain and progressive loss of grip strength. Pain develops insidiously in the wrist, often after prior wrist trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform sustained gripping (e.g., opening jars, holding tools). Fine motor tasks like writing become progressively difficult. Symptoms worsen with repeated wrist loading and ulnar-sided activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual, progressive onset over weeks to months. Patient reports past wrist trauma (fracture or ligament injury) years prior, often with disrupted vascularity. Symptoms do not improve with rest alone and show insidious progression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness elicited over the lunate (dorsal wrist midline). Wrist extension and radial deviation are restricted. Positive lunate press test (pain with axial loading over lunate). Grip strength markedly reduced compared to contralateral side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate lunate flattening, sclerosis, fragmentation, and eventual collapse. MRI shows lunate necrosis with signal changes and loss of normal marrow intensity. Sequential imaging documents progressive disease stages.

puzzles.explanation: Kienböck's disease is avascular necrosis of the lunate carpal bone, resulting from disrupted blood supply—often precipitated by prior wrist trauma that compromises the lunate's precarious end-artery blood flow. The condition presents insidiously with progressive central wrist pain, grip weakness, and restricted wrist motion, particularly with loading activities, as the lunate gradually fragments and collapses. The classic progression from lunate sclerosis to fragmentation to collapse seen on imaging reflects the stages of bone death and repair, distinguishing it from other carpal injuries. Early recognition is critical because progressive mechanical incongruence of the wrist and osteoarthritic changes become inevitable without intervention, making this diagnosis a teaching point on how seemingly minor prior trauma can set the stage for delayed degenerative disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old software developer presents with numbness and tingling in the hand that worsened over 6 weeks. Symptoms are triggered by prolonged desk work. Pain is present in the forearm and hand.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty with fine motor tasks and weakness in hand pinching. Resisted forearm movement in one direction reproduces forearm pain. Rest provides temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works 8-10 hours daily at a desk with forearm resting on a padded armrest in pronation. He denies neck pain or shoulder involvement. Symptoms began insidiously without trauma. No history of elbow fracture or subluxation. The patient is right-hand dominant with symptoms on the right forearm.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weakness demonstrated in thumb-down pronation grip (4/5 strength). Tinel's sign is positive in the proximal forearm between two muscle bodies. Median nerve distribution sensory testing shows mild diminishment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the forearm demonstrates hypoechoic swelling of the median nerve as it passes between the two heads of the pronator teres muscle. Ultrasound shows nerve compression without mass effect or fracture. Cross-sectional area of the median nerve at this level is increased compared to the contralateral side.

puzzles.explanation: Pronator syndrome is compression of the median nerve as it passes between the two heads of the pronator teres muscle in the proximal forearm, typically caused by repetitive pronation or sustained postural compression. Unlike carpal tunnel syndrome, which presents with nocturnal symptoms in a distal median distribution, pronator syndrome causes forearm pain with proximal median nerve compression, often triggered by resisted pronation or prolonged forearm positioning—exactly as seen in this desk worker resting his forearm in pronation on an armrest. The combination of weakness in pronation strength (thumb-down grip), a positive Tinel's sign between the pronator heads, and ultrasound confirmation of nerve swelling at the compression site makes this diagnosis distinct from other median nerve entrapments higher or lower in the kinetic chain. The insidious onset without trauma and clear postural aggravation factor are hallmarks of this overuse syndrome.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old male office worker presents with episodic unilateral headaches located in the temporal and occipital regions bilaterally asymmetrical. Symptoms correlate temporally with neck stiffness and worsen throughout the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Headache consistently provokes with prolonged sitting and neck extension movements. Relief noted when lying supine or with ice application. Certain neck postures reproducibly trigger pain within minutes. Neck stretching and movement paradoxically worsens the headache initially.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Three-year history of intermittent symptoms, initially triggered by a motor vehicle accident with whiplash mechanism. Progressive worsening associated with sedentary work environment. Reports no visual changes, hearing loss, or vestibular symptoms. Stress management minimally affects pain pattern.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical range of motion significantly restricted in extension (40 degrees; normal >50 degrees) and right rotation (55 degrees; normal >80 degrees). Extension immediately reproduces familiar occipital-temporal headache. Positive flexion-rotation test with pain reproduction. Cervical paraspinal muscles palpably tight and tender.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays demonstrate loss of cervical lordosis with subtle C4-C5 disc bulging. MRI shows C4-C5 and C5-C6 uncovertebral joint arthrosis with mild cervical spondylosis. Facet joint changes at C3-C4 level on the right correlate with restricted ipsilateral rotation and headache reproduction.

puzzles.explanation: Cervicogenic headache results from nociceptive input originating in the cervical spine—typically from facet joints, discs, or nerve roots—referred to the head via convergence with trigeminal afferents at the trigeminocervical nucleus. The unilateral temporal-occipital distribution and mechanical provocation with neck extension and rotation reflect the anatomy of upper cervical involvement, particularly facet-mediated pain; in this case, right-sided facet arthrosis at C3-C4 directly correlates with restricted ipsilateral rotation and reproducible headache on extension. The post-traumatic onset and progressive worsening in a sedentary posture are hallmark features—poor cervical lordosis and prolonged extension loading compound mechanical stress on already degenerative segments. Unlike primary headache disorders, cervicogenic headache is fundamentally a mechanical problem that responds to cervical mobilization and postural correction, distinguishing it from migraine or tension-type headache.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old administrative worker reports hand pain and tingling that began several weeks ago. Symptoms are worse at night and with gripping. No recent trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with fine motor tasks and sustained grip. Symptoms improve with rest and wrist splinting at night. Keyboard work aggravates tingling in thumb, index, and middle fingers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Open carpal tunnel release surgery performed 8 weeks ago for diagnosed median nerve compression. Initial post-operative improvement for 4 weeks, then gradual return of numbness and pain. No infection or wound complications reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Median nerve distribution paresthesias reproduced with sustained wrist flexion. Two-point discrimination reduced in radial three digits. Scar is well-healed and mobile. Grip strength 80% of contralateral side. Phalen's test positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Repeat nerve conduction studies show recurrent prolonged distal motor latency and reduced sensory conduction velocity across carpal tunnel. Ultrasound reveals thickened flexor retinaculum with minimal scar tissue. MRI excludes mass or hematoma.

puzzles.explanation: Post-carpal tunnel release syndrome occurs when symptoms recur after surgical decompression, either from inadequate initial release, recurrent nerve compression, or scar tissue reformation at the surgical site. Despite a well-healed wound and absence of infection or hematoma, the thickened flexor retinaculum and recurrent conduction slowing across the tunnel indicate the median nerve has been re-compressed — a known complication in 5-10% of cases, particularly with repetitive hand loading. The gradual onset weeks after initial improvement, combined with positive Phalen's testing and paresthesias in the median nerve distribution during sustained gripping, mirrors the original presentation and suggests inadequate scarring or reformation of the compression. Management typically involves modification of provocative activities, potential botulinum toxin injection to reduce muscular compression, or in resistant cases, revision surgery with consideration of flexor tenosynovial release or internal neurolysis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with acute anterior thigh pain that developed during activity. Pain is localised to the anterior thigh.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or activities requiring hip flexion and knee extension. The patient demonstrates functional limitation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset during or immediately after athletic activity. The patient may have inadequate warm-up or prior thigh injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the rectus femoris. Resisted knee extension or hip flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption or hematoma within the rectus femoris. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Rectus femoris strain occurs when muscle fibers are disrupted by excessive tensile load, typically during rapid hip flexion or knee extension—movements that place the biarticular rectus under maximal stretch and contractile demand. As the primary hip flexor and knee extensor, the rectus femoris is particularly vulnerable in athletes performing explosive activities like running and kicking, especially when warm-up is inadequate or the muscle carries a history of prior injury. The acute anterior thigh pain with reproducibility on resisted knee extension or hip flexion reflects direct muscle disruption; imaging findings of fiber discontinuity or hematoma confirm the diagnosis and grade severity, guiding return-to-sport timelines. Early recognition and load management are critical to prevent progression to complete rupture or chronic weakness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young to middle-aged adult presents with acute unilateral facial weakness and drooping that developed over hours to 48 hours. The patient reports inability to wrinkle forehead, close eye, or bare teeth on the affected side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional impairment includes inability to close the eye completely on the affected side (lagophthalmos), difficulty with oral hygiene, and loss of facial symmetry during smiling. Water escapes from the mouth during drinking due to lip weakness and drooling may occur.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset was acute, reaching maximum deficit within 72 hours. Recent upper respiratory tract infection, herpes zoster, or Lyme disease exposure may precede weakness. No prior facial weakness episodes and no significant neurological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, complete unilateral lower facial weakness is evident, with forehead involvement (distinguishing from central causes). Incomplete eye closure on affected side with Bell's sign present. Tear production may be reduced. Hyperacusis and altered taste on anterior two-thirds of tongue are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography demonstrates acute denervation changes and motor unit recruitment abnormalities consistent with peripheral facial nerve injury. Head CT or MRI is unremarkable, ruling out central brainstem lesions, stroke, space-occupying masses, or other structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Bell's palsy is an idiopathic acute peripheral facial nerve (CN VII) paralysis, often precipitated by viral reactivation (particularly herpes simplex virus) or preceding infection, that reaches maximum severity within 72 hours. The hallmark presentation is complete unilateral facial weakness affecting all branches of the facial nerve — forehead, eye, and mouth — which distinguishes it from central causes that spare the forehead due to bilateral cortical innervation. The constellation of findings here — lagophthalmos with Bell's sign, hyperacusis from stapedius paralysis, altered taste from chorda tympani involvement, and normal neuroimaging — confirms peripheral facial nerve injury without structural pathology. EMG showing acute denervation helps prognosticate severity and guides early intervention with corticosteroids and eye care, the cornerstones of management to prevent permanent denervation and corneal complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old driver. Onset of neck pain and headache 2 hours after a motor vehicle collision. No loss of consciousness. Pain radiates to shoulders and upper back.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot turn head beyond 30 degrees without sharp pain. Difficulty looking over shoulder while driving. Symptoms worse with sustained postures; brief relief with movement. No radiation past elbow.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rear-impact collision at low to moderate speed. No direct blow to head. Immediate post-impact onset within minutes to 2 hours. No prior neck problems. No loss of consciousness or concussion symptoms reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical ROM limited in all planes, especially rotation (30°) and extension. Palpable muscle guarding and tenderness in upper trapezius and cervical paraspinals. Normal grip strength, reflexes normal, no dermatomal sensory loss or motor deficits. No upper limb neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine radiographs (AP, lateral, odontoid) show loss of normal cervical lordosis and straightening. No fractures, subluxations, or listhesis. MRI cervical spine shows increased signal in cervical musculature and anterior longitudinal ligament on T2 sequences; no disc herniation or cord signal changes.

puzzles.explanation: Whiplash-associated disorder (WAD) results from rapid acceleration-deceleration forces that distort normal cervical kinematics, most commonly from rear-impact collisions. The mechanism causes microtrauma to cervical facet joints, ligaments, and musculature — particularly the cervical extensors and upper trapezius — leading to acute inflammation, muscle guarding, and restricted movement. The characteristic pattern here of immediate-onset neck pain with radiation to shoulders, severe rotational limitation despite normal neurological signs, and MRI evidence of soft tissue edema without structural injury typifies Grade II WAD. Recovery depends on early mobilization balanced with symptom management, as prolonged immobilization and catastrophizing predict poorer outcomes; most acute WAD resolves within 3-6 months with appropriate physiotherapy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young baseball pitcher presents with medial elbow pain. The pain develops during throwing activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by throwing, particularly with high-velocity throwing. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with throwing sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young athlete engaged in overhead throwing sports. Symptoms develop with increased throwing volume or intensity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, medial elbow tenderness is present over the medial epicondyle. Valgus stress test reproduces pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may demonstrate medial epicondyle avulsion or fragmentation. MRI characterises soft tissue involvement and ligamentous injury.

puzzles.explanation: Little League Elbow is a traction apophysitis of the medial epicondyle occurring in skeletally immature overhead throwers, caused by repetitive valgus stress that exceeds the tensile strength of the developing apophysis and its attachment to the flexor-pronator mass. The condition typically affects young baseball pitchers aged 9–14 whose growth plates are still open, and it develops predictably with high-velocity throwing or sudden increases in throwing volume — biomechanical stressors that generate significant medial-directed forces. The medial epicondyle tenderness and positive valgus stress test directly reflect this traction mechanism, and imaging may reveal avulsion or fragmentation as the apophysis fails under repetitive tensile load. Early recognition and activity modification are critical to prevent progression to avulsion fracture or chronic ligamentous injury of the ulnar collateral ligament.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old reports sudden loss of limb function with inability to perform activities of daily living. Experiences pain in the region where the limb was, sensation of limb still being present, and significant psychological distress about functional capacity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk independently, transfer from bed to chair, or bear weight. Experiences shooting pain and burning sensations in the absent limb, particularly with attempted movement. Fatigues quickly with minimal exertion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute limb loss 3 weeks ago from traumatic crush injury with vascular compromise requiring surgical intervention. No prior limb loss history. Currently on pain management and anti-inflammatory medications. Lives with family, motivated for functional recovery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Residual limb shows surgical scarring, moderate edema, and early tissue remodeling. Palpable bone prominence at surgical site. Intact sensation and skin integrity. Phantom sensations reproduced with residual limb movement. Trunk strength intact, upper extremities normal. Balance impaired in sitting without support.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs of residual limb show stable bone healing at amputation site with early callus formation. No signs of infection or nonunion. Soft tissue ultrasound documents residual edema and muscle atrophy progressing as expected for 3-week post-operative timeline. No imaging contraindication for prosthetic fitting initiation.

puzzles.explanation: Post-amputation rehabilitation addresses the complex physical and psychological recovery following limb loss, requiring coordinated management of residual limb healing, phantom limb pain, and progressive functional retraining. In the acute phase—as seen in this 3-week post-traumatic amputation—the focus is on edema management, scar mobility, and pain control while the residual limb undergoes tissue remodeling and bone healing. Phantom pain, the shooting and burning sensations experienced in the absent limb, is a nearly universal phenomenon driven by cortical reorganization and peripheral nerve sensitization, often exacerbated by attempted movement.
