Early rehabilitation emphasizes trunk stability, upper extremity strengthening, transfer training, and graduated weight-bearing preparation for prosthetic fitting, with psychological support integral to addressing the sudden functional loss and adjustment to living with amputation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged adult presents with neck pain that radiates into one arm. The pain has developed gradually or acutely following minor trauma or activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is reproducibly triggered by certain neck movements, particularly extension and ipsilateral rotation. The patient reports functional limitation with arm activities and difficulty maintaining posture.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of neck pain may be present. Symptoms began spontaneously or after minor trauma. The patient may have prior history of cervical spine problems. Symptoms are activity-dependent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, neck movement is limited, particularly extension and ipsilateral side-bending. Upper extremity weakness is demonstrated in a dermatomal distribution. Diminished reflexes may be present. Spurling's test reproduces radicular pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of cervical spine demonstrates disc herniation at the affected level with nerve root compression and narrowing of the neural foramen. The herniated material displaces neural structures.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical disc herniation occurs when the nucleus pulposus protrudes through a weakened annulus fibrosus, compressing the adjacent nerve root as it exits the neural foramen—a process often triggered by degenerative changes, minor trauma, or cumulative loading. The condition characteristically produces unilateral radicular pain radiating into the arm in a dermatomal distribution, accompanied by reproducible provocation during neck extension and ipsilateral rotation, as these movements further narrow the foramen and compress the nerve. Neurological deficits—including weakness, diminished reflexes, and sensory changes in the affected dermatome—reflect the degree of nerve root compromise and help localize the level of herniation. MRI confirmation of disc material displacing neural structures, combined with a positive Spurling's test reproducing the patient's radicular symptoms, provides definitive diagnostic imaging and clinical correlation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged adult presents with progressive weakness in the hands affecting fine motor tasks. The patient reports difficulty with activities including writing and buttoning clothes over the past several months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional decline is evident with impaired grip strength, difficulty with coordinated movements, and progressive inability to perform occupational tasks. The patient demonstrates a characteristic pattern of weakness that spreads to involve multiple limb regions over time.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began focally in one region and gradually spread to involve other body areas. The patient reports no sensory changes, preserved cognition, and no significant trauma or infection history. Progressive nature over 6-12 months noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visible muscle fasciculations are present in affected regions, with measurable atrophy. Hyperreflexia is noted in hyperexcitable muscle groups. Cranial nerve examination may reveal mild tongue atrophy or fasciculations. Sensory examination is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography (EMG) demonstrates acute denervation with fibrillations and positive sharp waves in affected muscles, consistent with anterior horn cell loss. MRI of brain and spine excludes structural lesions or spinal cord compression.

puzzles.explanation: Motor neurone disease is a progressive neurodegenerative condition affecting upper and lower motor neurons in the motor cortex, brainstem, and spinal cord, leading to selective loss of anterior horn cells. The disease characteristically presents with focal weakness that spreads over months to years — here beginning with fine motor dysfunction in the hands — accompanied by the pathognomonic combination of visible muscle fasciculations, atrophy, and hyperreflexia, reflecting denervation of motor units. Preserved sensation and cognition are key diagnostic features that distinguish MND from other causes of progressive weakness, while EMG findings of acute denervation potentials confirm anterior horn cell loss rather than structural compression. The median survival is 2-4 years from symptom onset, making early recognition and multidisciplinary management critical for optimising functional outcomes and quality of life.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 56-year-old accountant presents with progressive lower back pain and leg weakness that worsens with extended periods of standing or walking. The patient reports a forward-leaning posture is more comfortable than standing upright. Symptoms began 18 months ago and have gradually deteriorated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking forward uphill is tolerated better than walking backward or on flat surfaces. Prolonged standing without support causes severe pain and cramping in both legs. Sitting down or leaning forward significantly relieves symptoms within minutes. The patient can walk further when bent forward, using a shopping cart for support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has had a 20-year history of intermittent lower back pain managed conservatively. No history of prior spinal surgery. Recent fall from a ladder with direct blow to the lumbar region occurred 18 months ago, coinciding with symptom onset. He denies bowel or bladder changes. Family history of osteoporosis is noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Forward spinal flexion reproduces pain, but lumbar extension is limited and painful. Straight leg raise testing is negative. Neurological examination reveals normal strength and reflexes bilaterally. Palpation over the spinous process at L4-L5 elicits tenderness. Standing extension loading test increases symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain film X-rays show anterior slipping of L4 on L5 measuring approximately 15-20% of the vertebral body width, with associated degenerative changes. MRI confirms anterior spondylolisthesis at L4-L5 with canal narrowing and bilateral foraminal stenosis. No instability on flexion-extension radiographs. Disc signal is mildly decreased at the slipped level.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spondylolisthesis occurs when one vertebra slips anteriorly relative to the one below it, most commonly at L4-L5 due to degenerative changes, facet joint dysfunction, or acute trauma—as in this patient's post-fall presentation. The characteristic clinical pattern of neurogenic claudication (leg pain and weakness with standing/walking that improves with forward flexion) reflects canal and foraminal narrowing from both the slip itself and secondary degenerative stenosis. This patient's relief with a forward-leaning posture and ability to walk uphill (which naturally flexes the spine) are hallmark features that distinguish spondylolisthesis from other causes of back pain; extension loading, which closes the canal further, predictably worsens symptoms. Imaging confirmation of anterior slip with bilateral foraminal stenosis but negative instability testing guides conservative management, reserving fusion for those with progressive neurological loss or intractable symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with knee injury presents with pain on the lateral knee. The injury occurred with varus stress mechanism or direct contact to medial knee.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation is present with difficulty with walking and activities causing knee pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute injury mechanism documented. The patient may have prior ankle or knee injury history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, lateral knee tenderness is present. Varus stress test demonstrates excessive varus motion indicating ligamentous injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates lateral collateral ligament tear with characterisation of tear severity and associated injuries.

puzzles.explanation: Lateral collateral ligament (LCL) sprains result from varus stress or direct medial contact forces that stretch or tear the fibrous cord stabilizing the lateral knee compartment. The LCL's primary role is preventing excessive varus angulation, particularly in knee extension, making varus stress testing highly sensitive for detecting injury severity. Acute presentations with lateral knee tenderness and positive varus laxity are characteristic, and MRI can grade the tear and identify concurrent injuries to the posterolateral corner or peroneal nerve — crucial for rehabilitation planning. Unlike medial collateral ligament injuries, which often heal conservatively, LCL injuries frequently require careful assessment for associated posterolateral instability to guide appropriate management intensity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old woman reports progressive pain and swelling in her hand joints, particularly affecting the distal interphalangeal joints, with morning stiffness lasting 20 minutes. Pain is worse in the dominant hand and has progressed over several years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Fine motor activities such as writing, sewing, and opening jars are significantly limited by hand pain and weakness. Carrying objects with a firm grip worsens symptoms. Hand elevation and ice provide temporary relief; prolonged use causes pain flare-ups.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has had repetitive hand use occupationally (seamstress) for 40 years and experienced a distal interphalangeal joint injury at age 35. She has no systemic rheumatologic symptoms, fevers, or rashes. Family history significant for osteoarthritis in the paternal line.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable osteophytes (Heberden nodes) at the distal interphalangeal joints of fingers 2-5 bilaterally. Hand grip strength reduced to 18 kg on right (age-expected 25-30 kg). Finger ROM limited at distal joints, with crepitus appreciated. No synovitis or joint warmth.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing primarily at distal interphalangeal joints with prominent marginal osteophytes and subchondral bone sclerosis. No evidence of erosions, periostitis, or soft tissue calcifications. Metacarpophalangeal joints spared.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease characterized by progressive cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes, typically developing after years of mechanical loading or prior injury. Hand OA classically targets the distal interphalangeal joints, where Heberden nodes (osteophytes at joint margins) develop and morning stiffness is typically mild and brief—distinguishing it from inflammatory arthropathies. The dominance of symptoms in the dominant hand and selective DIP involvement here reflect cumulative microtrauma from decades of repetitive fine motor work combined with the patient's previous joint injury, both major contributors to localized cartilage degeneration. Plain radiographs showing joint space narrowing, marginal osteophytes, and sclerosis without erosions or periosteal involvement confirm the mechanical, non-inflammatory nature of the process.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with ankle trauma presents with acute severe pain and swelling. The injury occurred from a high-energy mechanism such as fall from height or axial loading.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation is present with complete inability to weightbear. The ankle is held in guarding and the patient requires immediate immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy trauma documented with direct axial load to the ankle joint. Associated injuries to ipsilateral leg, spine, or pelvis may be present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ankle demonstrates severe deformity with gross swelling preventing palpation of bony landmarks. Skin integrity must be assessed for open fracture. Neurovascular examination documents dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses; sensation and motor function in foot assessed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate pilon fracture with comminution of the tibial plafond and intra-articular extension. CT imaging characterises the fracture pattern with 3D reconstruction essential for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: A pilon fracture is a comminuted intra-articular fracture of the tibial plafond — the weight-bearing surface of the tibia — caused by high-energy axial loading such as falls from height or motor vehicle trauma. The force drives the talus into the tibial articular surface, shattering it into multiple fragments and creating a characteristically complex injury pattern. The severe pain, gross deformity, and complete inability to weightbear reflect both the degree of articular disruption and the acute inflammatory response, while the associated injuries to the ipsilateral leg, spine, or pelvis are common given the mechanism. CT imaging with 3D reconstruction is essential to define the fracture pattern and guide surgical fixation, as anatomic reduction of the articular surface is critical for preventing post-traumatic arthritis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old woman presents with acute-onset paralysis of the right leg that began suddenly during an emotionally stressful period at work 4 weeks ago. She is unable to walk independently and uses a wheelchair for mobility. She reports no sensory symptoms below the waist.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She demonstrates inability to bear weight on the right lower extremity, yet exhibits inconsistent functional use of the limb during natural activities and when unaware of observation. Her gait pattern when attempting to walk does not fit typical neurological disease patterns. She shows dramatic relief of symptoms during emotionally supportive interactions but sudden worsening during stressful encounters.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She experienced significant workplace conflict and interpersonal stress coinciding with symptom onset. She has a history of anxiety symptoms and perfectionist personality traits. She has seen multiple neurologists without definitive diagnosis of organic neurological disease. Prior psychiatric history includes adjustment disorder 3 years ago treated with psychotherapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, she demonstrates give-way weakness of right hip and knee with sudden collapse of effort when testing lower extremity strength. Hoover sign is positive indicating functional deficit rather than true paralysis. Gait when mobilizing without attention shows relatively preserved motor function compared to formal strength testing. Sensory examination is completely normal throughout. Reflexes are symmetric and normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spine MRI including cervical, thoracic, and lumbar regions is entirely normal without spinal cord lesions, compression, or structural abnormality. Brain MRI is normal with no white matter abnormalities, acute stroke, or demyelinating lesions. Routine laboratory studies including CBC and comprehensive metabolic panel are normal. Comprehensive neurophysiological testing is entirely normal without evidence of neuromuscular disease.

puzzles.explanation: Functional neurological disorder (FND) is a condition where neurological symptoms arise from altered processing of sensory and motor information rather than from structural pathology, typically triggered by psychological stressors like the workplace conflict and anxiety that preceded this patient's acute paralysis. The hallmark features—inconsistent weakness that worsens with attention and improves during distraction, positive neurological signs like give-way weakness and positive Hoover sign, and completely normal imaging and electrophysiology—distinguish FND from organic neurological disease and reflect the functional (not structural) nature of the deficit. The dramatic fluctuation of symptoms in relation to emotional support versus stress, combined with her history of perfectionism and prior adjustment disorder, is characteristic of how psychological factors modulate motor control in FND. Early recognition and supportive psychophysiological rehabilitation, rather than repeated investigations, offers the best prognosis for recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with running activity presents with progressive metatarsal pain. Pain develops in the ball of the foot.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running and impact activities. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with weightbearing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training volume or intensity coincides with symptom onset. Female athletes with amenorrhea are at increased risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, focal tenderness is present over the affected metatarsal. Pain is reproduced with single-leg hopping.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may show callus formation at chronic stage. MRI demonstrates bone stress with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema.

puzzles.explanation: Metatarsal stress fractures result from repetitive microtrauma exceeding the bone's capacity to remodel, commonly occurring in runners who suddenly increase training volume or intensity. The metatarsal heads bear substantial forefoot loading during running, making them particularly vulnerable to cumulative stress — especially in female athletes with amenorrhea, where hormonal dysregulation impairs bone mineral density. The characteristic focal tenderness over the metatarsal shaft and pain with single-leg hopping reflect the localized periosteal irritation and stress reaction within the bone; early imaging may appear normal, but MRI sensitively detects bone marrow edema and periosteal thickening before callus formation is radiographically visible. Management focuses on activity modification, load management, and addressing underlying risk factors such as training errors and relative energy deficiency.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old female reports jaw pain localized to the joint in front of the ear that has progressively worsened over 4 months. She describes clicking or popping sounds with opening the mouth and occasional jaw locking that interferes with eating. Pain is dull and achy, worsening toward the end of the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Chewing tough foods and prolonged talking exacerbate pain and clicking significantly. The patient reports difficulty eating harder foods and now avoids them. Yawning reproduces the locking sensation. Resting the jaw and applying ice provide moderate relief. She clenches her teeth during stressful situations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient reports a high-stress occupation with recent significant life changes. She works in a fast-paced environment and admits to daytime jaw clenching and nighttime teeth grinding. Dental history includes recent orthodontic treatment completed 2 years ago. No history of jaw trauma or prior TMJ disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation over the temporomandibular joint bilaterally reveals tenderness on the affected side. Mouth opening measures 38 mm with deviation toward the right side. Clicking is audible during opening and closing of the mouth. Palpation of the masseter and temporalis muscles reveals significant myofascial trigger points with referred pain pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows displacement of the articular disc anteriorly with possible reduction during opening movements, indicating disc displacement with reduction. Mild joint space narrowing is present. Signal intensity changes in the joint fluid suggest mild inflammatory changes. No evidence of osteoarthritis or significant degenerative changes on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Temporomandibular joint disorder (TMD) encompasses a spectrum of musculoskeletal and neuromuscular conditions affecting the jaw joint and associated structures, often involving disc-condyle dysfunction, myofascial pain, or both. The clicking and occasional locking with jaw opening reflect anterior disc displacement with reduction — the disc subluxes forward during closure but recaptures during opening, a mechanical pattern highly characteristic of TMD. Stress-induced muscle tension and parafunctional habits like clenching and grinding typically drive the myofascial component, explaining the significant trigger points in the masseter and temporalis alongside the progressive, end-of-day symptom pattern. This presentation — mechanical joint sounds combined with muscular dysfunction and imaging confirmation of disc displacement — distinguishes TMD from isolated myofascial pain, dental issues, or joint capsulitis alone, and responds well to conservative management including ergonomic modification, stress reduction, and manual therapy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old fell backward onto outstretched hand. Acute onset severe wrist pain, swelling, and inability to move wrist. No numbness or tingling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot flex or extend wrist. Hand hangs passively. Grip strength absent. Pain with any attempt at forearm rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism: fell from standing height onto palm with wrist in flexion. Immediate swelling. No prior wrist injury or arthritis. No neurovascular symptoms at time of injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist held in slight flexion. Prominent swelling over volar (palmar) aspect of distal forearm. Bony tenderness over distal radius. No crepitus. Radial pulse intact, capillary refill normal, sensation intact to light touch.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Wrist radiographs: distal radius fracture with volar angulation of distal fragment (reversed deformity). Fracture line transverse across distal metaphysis. No intra-articular extension.

puzzles.explanation: Smith's fracture is a transverse fracture of the distal radius with volar (palmar) angulation of the distal fragment—essentially the reverse deformity of a Colles' fracture. It typically results from a fall onto a flexed wrist, as seen here, and is less common than Colles' but carries greater risk of complications due to the mechanism's violence and the fragment's displacement toward vital structures. The volar swelling, loss of wrist extension, and inability to maintain wrist posture reflect both the fracture itself and associated disruption of the extensor mechanism. Neurovascular integrity at presentation is a good prognostic sign, but close monitoring remains essential given the proximity of the flexor tendons and median nerve to the displaced fragment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A recreational runner presents with pain and stiffness at the back of the lower leg. Pain is localised to the Achilles region and develops with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specifically triggered by running and activities involving plantarflexion. The patient experiences morning stiffness that improves with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. The patient may have prior history of Achilles pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable along the Achilles tendon. Crepitus may be appreciated with tendon movement. Resisted plantarflexion reproduces pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates Achilles tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation confirming tendinopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Achilles tendinopathy is a chronic degenerative condition of the Achilles tendon characterized by microtrauma, failed healing, and intratendinous inflammation rather than acute rupture. It typically develops in response to sudden increases in training load—particularly activities demanding rapid plantarflexion such as running—that exceed the tendon's adaptive capacity. The characteristic morning stiffness that improves with activity reflects the inflammatory nature of early-stage tendinopathy and the tendon's response to loading; imaging findings of thickening and hypoechoic changes represent the structural disorganization and tissue remodeling that occurs with cumulative microtrauma. Recurrent or bilateral involvement is common in active individuals, making load management and graduated return to activity central to recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old former tennis player presents with progressively worsening shoulder pain for 8 months, particularly when lifting the arm overhead. Pain is located over the top of the shoulder and radiates down the lateral arm. Sudden acute flare-ups occur with minor overhead movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Overhead reaching activities, particularly reaching behind the head, provoke sharp pain and weakness. Carrying groceries with the affected arm is no longer possible due to pain and weakness. Lying on the affected shoulder is uncomfortable. Rest and ice provide temporary relief, but symptoms return quickly with activity resumption.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient played competitive tennis for 30 years, retiring 10 years ago. She reports a fall on the outstretched hand 8 months ago that preceded current symptoms. Past medical history includes hypertension controlled with medication and mild osteoarthritis in other joints. She denies previous shoulder surgery or dislocations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Abduction of the arm reproduces sharp pain, particularly between 60-120 degrees. Supraspinatus strength testing shows weakness with painful arc. Positive Hawkins test and Neer's impingement sign are elicited. Infraspinatus strength is 5/5. Palpation reveals tenderness over the supraspinatus insertion on the humeral head.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain film X-ray of the shoulder demonstrates a focal rounded density over the supraspinatus tendon insertion area. CT imaging shows a calcification measuring 12 mm with a heterogeneous appearance located within the supraspinatus tendon. The calcification is located 8 mm lateral to the humeral head articular surface margin.

puzzles.explanation: Supraspinatus tendon calcification is the deposition of hydroxyapatite crystals within the rotator cuff tendon, most commonly occurring in the supraspinatus due to its relative avascularity and high mechanical loading. The condition typically affects middle-aged individuals with a history of overhead activities or prior microtrauma—here, the tennis background and FOOSH injury likely triggered inflammation and subsequent crystal nucleation within the tendon. The presentation is characteristically painful with a positive painful arc between 60–120° of abduction, reflecting the tight subacromial space where the calcified tendon impinges against the acromion during this range. Acute flare-ups occur when crystals rupture into the subacromial bursa, triggering inflammatory responses; imaging demonstrates the pathognomonic heterogeneous density within the tendon substance itself, distinguishing this from other rotator cuff pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old male presents with acute onset inability to move his affected leg after undergoing hip replacement surgery. He cannot walk and cannot lift his leg off the bed. The anterior thigh is noticeably swollen and tender.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform active hip flexion or knee extension. Sensory loss noted over the anterior thigh and medial lower leg in dermatomal pattern. Non-weight bearing and requires assistance with all mobility.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred intraoperatively during total hip arthroplasty when the femoral nerve was stretched or contused during retraction for surgical access. Post-operative imaging revealed significant hematoma in the psoas and iliacus region. No prior neurological deficits. The patient underwent urgent hematoma evacuation 24 hours after surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion strength 0/5 with no visible iliopsoas contraction. Knee extension absent. Patellar reflex absent on affected side. Sensory loss to light touch in L2-L4 distribution. Complete paralysis of quadriceps and hip adductors.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the hip and proximal femur shows large hematoma in the iliopsoas and psoas major extending along the femoral nerve course. Nerve signal is interrupted at the level of the hematoma. Post-hematoma evacuation imaging shows partial reduction in swelling but persistent nerve compression. Nerve edema is visible extending distally.

puzzles.explanation: Femoral nerve palsy results from compression or stretch injury to the femoral nerve as it emerges from the psoas major and descends through the groin. This complication is a recognized risk of hip arthroplasty, where aggressive retraction during surgical access can stretch the nerve or cause it to be compressed by postoperative hematoma in the iliopsoas region — exactly the mechanism in this case. The clinical picture of acute paralysis affecting hip flexion (iliopsoas) and knee extension (quadriceps) with sensory loss in the L2-L4 distribution is pathognomonic for femoral nerve involvement. The bilateral consequences here — complete loss of hip flexion strength and absent patellar reflex — underscore the severity of compression, and while hematoma evacuation may decompress the nerve, recovery depends on whether the lesion is predominantly neurapraxia or involves axonal damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old retired educator experiences chronic heel and arch discomfort extending across the sole of the foot. Morning pain is severe, necessitating 15-20 minutes of slow movement before tolerance improves. This chronic presentation affecting weight-bearing structures requires differentiation from multiple structural and systemic conditions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: New or increased walking beyond 2 kilometers triggers escalation of pain for the following 24-48 hours. Elevated heels provide some relief. Running is not tolerated; even brisk walking produces discomfort within 10 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 18 months ago following increased daily walking activity post-retirement. Patient has type 2 diabetes controlled by metformin with BMI 29. Previously experienced plantar fasciitis 10 years ago that resolved with conservative treatment within 6 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, pain is reproduced with palpation along the medial longitudinal arch. Windlass test produces marked pain with dorsiflexion of the hallux. Ankle plantarflexion range is normal; however, dorsiflexion is limited to 8 degrees bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals chronic thickening of the plantar fascia measuring 5.1 mm at the insertion point with heterogeneous signal intensity. Small calcaneal heel spur is visible. Marrow signal intensity at the calcaneal tuberosity is mildly altered, consistent with chronic inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Plantar fasciitis results from chronic overload and microtrauma to the plantar fascia, the thick connective tissue running along the sole of the foot from the calcaneal tuberosity to the metatarsal heads. The condition classically presents with intense heel pain worst in the morning — as the fascia stiffens overnight and stretches acutely with the first steps — and characteristically worsens with activities exceeding the individual's tolerance, particularly in middle-aged or older adults with metabolic factors like diabetes or increased BMI. The dorsiflexion limitation and positive windlass test reflect the fascial tightness and the mechanical coupling between plantarflexion, arch height, and fascia tension during gait. In chronic cases like this, MRI typically shows fascia thickening, heterogeneous signal, and adjacent bone marrow changes at the insertion, findings that correlate with duration and severity but do not necessarily predict treatment response — most cases resolve with activity modification, calf stretching, and arch support within weeks to months.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old diabetic woman reports gradual progressive weakness in her right leg over the past 3 months without preceding injury. She describes the leg feeling heavy and numb.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She reports severe difficulty with stairs and standing from a chair. Her walking tolerance has dropped significantly. She notes night-time thigh pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 15-year history of poorly controlled Type 2 diabetes with recent HbA1c of 9.2%. She reports unintentional weight loss of 12 pounds over the past 3 months. No history of leg trauma or prior neurological disorders. Recent imaging ruled out lumbar pathology. Vascular history is significant for peripheral vascular disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion strength is 2/5 with minimal antigravity power. Knee extension is similarly weak. Patellar reflex is diminished on the affected side. Sensory loss follows L2-L4 distribution.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar plexus and proximal femur shows signal abnormality within the psoas and iliacus muscles consistent with denervation edema. The femoral nerve appears enlarged proximally with T2 hyperintense signal consistent with inflammation. No focal mass or compressive lesion. Findings are consistent with diabetic femoral neuropathy.

puzzles.explanation: Femoral nerve palsy in this context reflects diabetic femoral neuropathy, an ischemic mononeuropathy of the proximal femoral nerve that typically affects older adults with poorly controlled diabetes. The condition arises from microvascular insufficiency of the nerve's blood supply rather than compression, leading to denervation of hip flexors and knee extensors — which explains her profound difficulty with stairs and rising from a chair. The gradual progressive weakness over months, combined with the characteristic L2-L4 sensory distribution, nocturnal thigh pain, and MRI evidence of denervation edema in the psoas and iliacus muscles, is classic for this entity. Diabetic femoral neuropathy carries significant functional morbidity but can stabilize or partially recover with improved glycaemic control, making aggressive diabetes management a key component of rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 14-year-old competitive athlete. Localized pain and swelling below the knee that appeared gradually over 2–3 months. Pain is activity-related and does not wake them at night. No history of direct trauma or fall.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens significantly with jumping, sprinting, and cutting movements. Symptoms ease with rest. Difficulty returning to full training intensity. Pain is reproducible and consistent with activity level.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rapid growth phase (tall for age). Recent increase in training volume and intensity over past 3 months. Previous episodes of anterior knee discomfort that resolved with rest. No systemic illness, fever, or constitutional symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Focal bony prominence and tenderness over the anterior tibial tubercle. Pain elicited with resisted knee extension and single-leg squat. Mild local swelling; skin warm and non-erythematous. Knee range of motion preserved. No effusion or ligamentous laxity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lateral knee radiographs show fragmentation and irregularity of the tibial tubercle apophysis with surrounding soft tissue swelling. No fracture line or displaced fragment. Findings consistent with apophyseal traction injury.

puzzles.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is an apophyseal traction injury affecting the tibial tubercle, most common in adolescents during rapid skeletal growth when the extensor mechanism's tensile forces exceed the apophysis's structural capacity. The condition arises from repetitive patellar tendon traction on the developing tibial tuberosity, typically triggered by growth spurts combined with increased training volume—as seen in this competitive athlete whose symptoms coincided with both rapid height gain and intensified jumping and sprinting. Pain localised below the knee, reproducible with knee extension loading and exacerbated by explosive movements, is characteristic; importantly, the absence of nocturnal pain and systemic features distinguishes it from inflammatory or systemic pathology. Radiographic fragmentation of the apophysis confirms the diagnosis, and the condition typically resolves once skeletal maturity is reached, making activity modification and relative rest the cornerstones of management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent fatigue and shortness of breath with minimal exertion. Symptoms began after a prolonged period off work due to acute illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot climb stairs without stopping for breath and becomes exhausted after light household tasks. Walking >100m triggers symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prior illness, hospitalization, or prolonged immobility is documented. The patient has been inactive for weeks to months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Heart rate and blood pressure rise disproportionately with minimal activity. Resting heart rate elevated at 95 bpm. Six-minute walk test shows marked reduction in distance with dyspnoea at low workload.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cardiopulmonary exercise testing shows VO2 max 40% below age-predicted values. Echocardiography and pulmonary function tests are normal; no structural cardiac or lung disease detected.

puzzles.explanation: Deconditioning is a systemic loss of cardiovascular and musculoskeletal fitness resulting from prolonged inactivity, immobility, or bed rest — in this case following acute illness and hospitalization. The condition reflects reversible physiological changes: decreased stroke volume, reduced arteriovenous oxygen extraction, loss of skeletal muscle oxidative capacity, and autonomic dysregulation, all of which manifest as exaggerated heart rate and blood pressure responses to minimal exertion. The marked reduction in VO2 max with normal cardiopulmonary and structural testing is pathognomonic; deconditioning is a functional limitation without underlying organ disease. Recovery is dependent on graded aerobic and resistance reconditioning, making early mobilization and structured rehabilitation critical in the post-acute illness window.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient presents with recurrent episodes of wheezing, dyspnoea, and chest tightness. Symptoms are variable and intermittent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports breathlessness and chest tightness triggered by exertion, cold air exposure, or allergen contact. Audible breathing sounds present during episodes. Symptoms resolve within minutes to hours of rest or symptom cessation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Unable to sustain aerobic activities without symptom onset. Nocturnal coughing occasionally reported. Symptoms worsen during high pollen seasons. No fever or recent infection preceding onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Auscultation reveals bilateral wheeze during or immediately after exertion. Respiratory rate elevated during symptoms. Accessory muscle use may be evident. Examination normal between episodes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spirometry demonstrates obstructive pattern with FEV1/FVC ratio reduced. Bronchial hyperresponsiveness confirmed on challenge testing. Significant reversibility documented after bronchodilator administration.

puzzles.explanation: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease characterised by reversible airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and variable symptoms triggered by exercise, allergens, cold air, or infections. The condition results from airway inflammation, smooth muscle constriction, and mucus production, which narrow the airways and increase work of breathing—hence the bilateral wheeze on auscultation and reduced FEV1/FVC ratio seen here. Asthma's hallmark is reversibility: the obstructive pattern completely resolves with bronchodilators or rest, distinguishing it from fixed obstructive diseases like COPD. The intermittent, trigger-dependent nature of symptoms and normal examination findings between episodes are classic, as is the exertional and nocturnal cough; exercise-induced bronchoconstriction occurs due to airway cooling and drying during high ventilation demand.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with pain in the groin region or lower abdominal region. Pain develops with athletic activity and resolves with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or twisting movements. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. Sport involving rapid acceleration and deceleration is typical.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resisted hip adduction reproduces pain. Palpation over adductor insertion at pubis is tender. Pain localised to medial groin with positive squeeze test (resisted hip adduction in supine).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates adductor muscle strain or rectus femoris tear near the pubic symphysis with hypoechoic changes. MRI characterises muscle injury, inflammation, and pubic bone involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Athletic pubalgia is an overuse injury involving the musculotendinous structures around the pubic symphysis, particularly the adductors and rectus abdominis, that occurs when training load exceeds tissue capacity to adapt. It typically affects athletes in sports demanding rapid acceleration, deceleration, and multidirectional movement — the sudden directional changes stress the musculotendinous attachments at the pubis beyond their tolerance. The hallmark presentation combines exercise-induced groin pain that resolves with rest, positive resisted adduction (squeeze test), and tenderness at the adductor insertion; imaging reveals muscle strain or microstructural damage near the pubic bone. Unlike acute traumatic groin injuries, athletic pubalgia develops insidiously with training load spikes and represents a continuum of musculotendinous overload rather than a single discrete tear.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 25-year-old patient presents with recurrent shoulder pain and a sense of the shoulder 'slipping' or feeling unstable. Symptoms are triggered by overhead activities and throwing movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform overhead press or throwing activities without fear of shoulder giving way. Symptoms occur in multiple arm positions, not just one.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient often has generalized ligamentous laxity. Symptoms develop with activity or may occur spontaneously.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Apprehension sign positive in multiple planes. Sulcus sign present (inferior instability). Relocation test positive. Laxity testing reveals increased humeral head translation in anteroinferior and posteroinferior directions.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates anteroinferior and posteroinferior labral tears with enlarged capsule. Humeral head subluxation evident in multiple positions on dynamic sequences.

puzzles.explanation: Multidirectional instability (MDI) occurs when the glenohumeral joint demonstrates pathological laxity in two or more planes — typically anteroinferior and posteroinferior — often underpinned by generalized ligamentous laxity and capsular redundancy rather than a single traumatic event. Unlike unidirectional instability from a discrete labral tear, MDI presents with symptoms triggered across multiple arm positions and loading vectors, reflecting the diffuse nature of the capsular compromise. The combination of positive apprehension signs in multiple planes, sulcus sign indicating inferior translation, and imaging findings of enlarged capsule with multidirectional labral pathology defines this condition. Young, hypermobile patients like this one are particularly susceptible, and management typically emphasizes neuromuscular control and scapular stabilization before considering surgical capsulorrhaphy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 56-year-old recreational jogger presents with progressive pain and swelling on the medial ankle that began insidiously 8 months ago without preceding acute injury. The foot is progressively losing its arch and turning outward. Walking on uneven surfaces has become increasingly difficult, and the patient describes a sensation of instability.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running is no longer tolerated; the patient can only walk short distances before ankle pain and swelling worsen. Walking on uneven terrain aggravates symptoms more than flat surfaces. Prolonged standing increases pain and swelling significantly. The patient reports the foot 'giving way' occasionally with walking. Rest, ice, and elevation provide only temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 20-year history of sporadic ankle sprains, with no specific traumatic event preceding current symptoms. He runs 4 times per week on concrete surfaces. No surgical history. Past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He reports progressive loss of endurance over the past year.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant foot pronation is observed during gait, with increased medial longitudinal arch collapse. A positive single-leg stance test shows loss of balance on the affected foot. Resisted inversion strength is 4/5 on the affected side. Palpation reveals swelling and tenderness along the posterior tibial tendon insertion. Navicular drop test measures 15 mm compared to 5 mm on the unaffected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the ankle shows thickening and partial-thickness tear of the posterior tibial tendon at the medial malleolar region, measuring 8 mm in width. Surrounding soft tissue edema is present. The spring ligament shows partial disruption. Hindfoot alignment demonstrates 8-degree valgus deformity with talonavicular uncoverage on coronal imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior tibial tendon dysfunction (PTTD) results from progressive degeneration and/or partial tearing of the posterior tibial tendon, which is critical for supporting the medial longitudinal arch and controlling foot pronation during stance and gait. In Grade 2 disease, the tendon shows structural damage with partial tears and surrounding inflammation, yet the foot can still invert against resistance — distinguishing it from Grade 3, where inversion strength is lost. The progressive arch collapse, increased navicular drop, and hindfoot valgus seen here reflect the tendon's diminished ability to dynamically stabilize the midfoot, particularly under load and on uneven surfaces. Chronic ankle sprains, repetitive impact activity on hard surfaces, and the cumulative demand placed on an aging tendon predispose to this condition, which is accelerated by loss of muscular endurance and postural control.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 25-year-old with lifelong history of recurrent chest infections and persistent productive cough. Reports dyspnea with exertion and chronic fatigue. No significant smoking history.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk more than one flight of stairs without stopping to catch breath. Daily cough productive of copious, thick sputum. Difficulty with sustained aerobic activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began in infancy with failure to thrive and recurrent pneumonias. Frequent admissions for respiratory exacerbations throughout childhood. History of loose, greasy stools and poor growth despite adequate nutrition in early years. No family history of genetic disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Barrel-shaped chest noted. Clubbing of fingernails and toenails present. Decreased breath sounds bilaterally with scattered crackles throughout lung fields. Oxygen saturation drops to 88% with mild exertion. Weight and BMI below expected for age.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest shows bronchiectasis with bronchial wall thickening, bronchial dilation greater than accompanying vessels, and patchy bronchial wall opacification. Pulmonary function testing demonstrates both obstructive and restrictive patterns with reduced FEV1 (45% predicted) and FVC.

puzzles.explanation: Cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive genetic disorder affecting the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) protein, which normally regulates salt and water transport across epithelial membranes. The resulting viscous secretions in the lungs lead to chronic airway obstruction, recurrent infections, and progressive bronchiectasis — the bronchial dilation with thickened walls evident on imaging here. The constellation of lifelong recurrent infections beginning in infancy, copious thick sputum production, exercise limitation, and the mixed obstructive-restrictive pulmonary function pattern with significantly reduced FEV1 are hallmark features. Extrarespiratory manifestations like pancreatic insufficiency (reflected in early failure to thrive and steatorrhea) and the chronic tissue remodeling seen as clubbing and barrel chest further underscore the multisystem impact of defective CFTR function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 8-year-old child. Parents report slowness and awkwardness with movement since early childhood. Stiffness in legs, more noticeable on one side. Difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning shirts. No recent change in symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walks independently but with a characteristic pattern: one leg tends to stay extended and turns inward; walks on tiptoes on that side. Difficulty running and climbing stairs. Balance problems when turning quickly. Trips over own feet frequently.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms present since infancy; parents noticed stiffness and asymmetrical movement by 6 months old. Born after complicated labour; required resuscitation at birth. Early developmental milestones (sitting, walking) delayed by 3-4 months. No progressive worsening over past 5 years. No family history of neuromuscular disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Increased muscle tone (Modified Ashworth Scale 2) in right hip adductors, hip flexors, and knee extensors. Brisk reflexes with clonus at ankle. Babinski sign present bilaterally. Mild weakness noted antigravity in right lower limb. Normal sensation and cognition intact. Contractures absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI shows focal periventricular white matter loss in the region supplied by penetrating arteries, with mild ventriculomegaly. No progressive changes on comparison with prior imaging from 2 years ago. Pattern consistent with remote perinatal hypoxic-ischemic injury.

puzzles.explanation: Cerebral palsy is a non-progressive disorder of movement and posture resulting from a static lesion to the developing brain, most commonly from perinatal hypoxic-ischemic injury as evidenced by the periventricular white matter loss on this child's MRI. The characteristic clinical presentation reflects upper motor neuron dysfunction: spasticity (increased tone) predominantly affecting the antigravity muscles, hyperreflexia with clonus, and a pathological Babinski sign, with asymmetrical involvement creating the classic hemiplegic gait pattern of an extended, internally rotated leg with toe-walking on the affected side. The static nature of the injury — symptom onset in infancy without progressive worsening — distinguishes CP from degenerative or evolving neurological conditions, making it fundamentally a problem of movement control rather than progressive weakness. Early identification and physiotherapy targeting spasticity management, gait training, and prevention of contractures are essential for optimizing functional outcomes in this population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 29-year-old competitive soccer player reports acute onset anterior hip pain that started 10 days ago during a match. Pain is sharp and localized to the anterior hip region. The patient felt something give while sprinting and was unable to continue playing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Hip flexion against resistance reproduces sharp pain at the injury site. Activities requiring hip flexion with extension of the knee, such as kicking or sprinting, are particularly painful. The patient walks with a stiff gait and leans backward to minimize hip flexion. Ice, rest, and anti-inflammatory medications provide moderate relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Two-week period of increased training intensity in preparation for playoffs. Adequate warm-up performed before the injury match. No prior hip injuries, surgery, or inflammatory history. Competes at high frequency (4-6 times weekly).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion strength reduced to 4/5 with pain on resistance. Palpation tender just below the inguinal ligament. Visible bruising in anterior hip region. Hip extension range of motion appears slightly limited on the affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the hip demonstrates acute partial-thickness tear of the iliopsoas muscle at the myotendinous junction approximately 2 cm distal to the inguinal ligament. Muscle edema and hemorrhage surround the tear site. No muscle retraction. Underlying hip joint and bony anatomy appear normal. Findings consistent with acute muscle strain grade 2.

puzzles.explanation: Hip flexor strain results from acute disruption of the iliopsoas muscle, most commonly at its myotendinous junction where the muscle transitions to tendon — the mechanically vulnerable zone. These injuries classically occur in sprinting and kicking athletes when forceful hip flexion is combined with knee extension, creating high tensile loads through the muscle-tendon interface. The acute onset during sprinting, sharp anterior hip pain below the inguinal ligament, and reduced hip flexion strength with visible bruising are hallmark presentations of grade 2 strains involving partial-thickness muscle tears with associated edema and hemorrhage. Management emphasizes early activity modification to protect the healing myotendinous junction, progressive loading as tolerated, and careful progression of sprinting and kicking drills to prevent reinjury — critical for return to competitive soccer.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old office worker. Dull, aching pain in lower back region onset over 3 months. No clear injury or fall. Pain fluctuates day to day. Occasionally radiates slightly but no dermatomal pattern. No bowel or bladder changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Aggravated by prolonged sitting and sustained forward bending. Eased by lying flat, walking, and changing position frequently. No pain with standing alone. Unable to sustain desk work >2 hours without discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sedentary for 18 months (desk job, minimal exercise). No trauma or lifting incident. Recent increase in work stress and long commute. First episode of significant back pain. No prior similar episodes. No systemic symptoms, fever, or weight loss.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Active lumbar flexion and extension pain-free or mildly uncomfortable, full range achieved. Bilateral paraspinal muscle tightness L4–L5 region. Straight leg raise negative bilaterally. Lower extremity strength intact 5/5. Palpation tenderness in muscle belly, not bony landmarks. No neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine MRI: no disc prolapse, no central or foraminal stenosis, no facet arthropathy, no spondylolisthesis, no fracture, no inflammatory changes, no cord signal abnormality. Vertebral bodies and endplates normal.

puzzles.explanation: Non-specific low back pain is the most common lumbar spine presentation, defined by pain in the lower back region without identifiable structural pathology or nerve involvement—a diagnosis confirmed here by completely normal imaging and negative neurological findings. It typically arises from multifactorial causes including muscular deconditioning, postural strain, and psychosocial stressors, all evident in this sedentary office worker with recent stress and the characteristic pattern of pain provoked by prolonged sitting and forward bending but relieved by movement and position changes. The bilateral paraspinal muscle tightness and muscle belly tenderness reflect the muscular origin, while the absence of radiculopathy, dermatomal distribution, or neurological deficit excludes nerve root involvement. Management focuses on restoring activity tolerance, improving core stability and posture, and addressing the psychosocial contributors—particularly the work stress and deconditioning—rather than investigating further or pursuing passive interventions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old recreational athlete presents with acute lateral trunk pain following an overhead throwing activity. The pain is sharp and well-localized to one side of the trunk, onset immediate during sport.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient experiences severe pain with trunk rotation toward the affected side and with lateral flexion away from the side. Activities requiring core stability such as throwing, twisting, and lunging reproduce the pain sharply. Walking and rest positions provide relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred during competitive recreational baseball without prior warning. The patient had not warmed up adequately before the throwing activity. No previous history of similar injuries; overall good health with regular exercise routine.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Focal tenderness over the lateral trunk musculature at the level of injury. Pain reproducible on resisted trunk rotation and side-bending toward affected side. Palpable muscle spasm in external oblique region. No midline or spinal symptoms; pain localised to muscular region without radiation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the lateral trunk musculature shows focal hypoechoic changes within the external oblique muscle consistent with acute muscle strain. No fluid collection or significant hemorrhage noted. The muscle fibers appear partially disrupted in longitudinal view.

puzzles.explanation: An oblique strain is an acute muscular injury to the external or internal oblique muscles of the trunk, typically occurring when sudden rotational or lateral flexion forces exceed the muscle's tensile capacity—especially when movement is initiated without adequate warm-up or neuromuscular preparation. The external obliques are particularly vulnerable during overhead throwing and explosive twisting motions, as they eccentrically control trunk rotation and lateral stability. Clinically, the sharp, well-localized lateral pain that reproduces with resisted rotation and side-bending toward the affected side, combined with palpable muscle spasm and focal hypoechoic changes on ultrasound, confirms the diagnosis as a partial muscle fiber disruption rather than a deeper spinal or ligamentous pathology. Recovery typically emphasizes early pain-free range of motion followed by progressive core stabilization and rotational control to restore functional throwing mechanics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old man presents with deep, aching shoulder pain that developed insidiously 5 months ago. Pain is present at rest and notably worse at night, disrupting sleep. The discomfort is localized to the shoulder joint itself rather than radiating distally.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports marked limitation in shoulder external rotation and overhead elevation, making it impossible to reach behind the back or reach high shelves. Internal rotation is relatively preserved. Throwing and overhead sporting activities are no longer possible. Pain is partially relieved with heat and gentle therapeutic exercise within available range.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Seven months prior to symptom onset, the patient suffered a myocardial infarction and was advised to minimize shoulder activity during cardiac recovery. Stiffness and pain gradually developed in the weeks following resumption of normal activity. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 8 years ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, shoulder abduction is limited to 85 degrees and external rotation is severely restricted to 15 degrees with hard end-feel. Forward flexion is limited to 120 degrees. Passive range of motion is similarly restricted in all planes. Rotator cuff strength testing shows weakness attributable to pain inhibition rather than true muscle loss. Scapular mechanics are intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates capsular thickening and synovial inflammation with reduced joint space volume. Coronal and axial sequences show circumferential capsular fibrosis particularly affecting the anterior and posterior capsule. Rotator cuff muscles appear normal without fatty infiltration. No full-thickness tears are present. Findings are consistent with chronic adhesive capsulitis.

puzzles.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis is a self-limiting inflammatory condition of the glenohumeral joint capsule characterized by progressive fibrosis and contracture, typically occurring in middle-aged and older adults, particularly those with diabetes or recent immobility. The pathology involves initial inflammatory synovitis followed by capsular thickening and circumferential fibrosis that disproportionately restricts external rotation and overhead elevation while preserving internal rotation — a pattern reflecting the anatomical distribution of contracture. This patient's presentation is classic: insidious onset following a period of immobilization (post-MI activity restriction), gradual stiffening with nocturnal pain, and the characteristic hard end-feel on passive range restriction. The presence of diabetes significantly increases risk, and MRI findings of synovial inflammation with anterior and posterior capsular fibrosis confirm the diagnosis, distinguishing it from rotator cuff pathology where imaging would show muscle changes and strength loss would be genuine rather than pain-inhibited.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old office worker presents with 8 weeks of chronic shoulder pain. Pain is described as a deep ache with occasional sharp catching sensations. Overhead activities aggravate symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot tolerate sustained arm elevation or reaching overhead. Activities requiring repetitive mouse use and awkward shoulder posturing at work significantly worsen pain. Symptoms resolve completely when arm is at rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain developed insidiously over 8 weeks without specific injury. The patient reports increased shoulder demands from recent work changes requiring frequent computer mouse use and awkward posturing. History includes poor shoulder muscle conditioning and rounded shoulder posture.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, Neer impingement test and Hawkins test are both positive, reproducing the patient's pain. Supraspinatus strength is 4/5 with painful weakness noted. Posterior shoulder tightness is evident with cross-body adduction stretch. Scapular dyskinesis is observed during active arm elevation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder MRI demonstrates thickening of the supraspinatus tendon with mild signal hyperintensity consistent with early tendinopathy. Subacromial space is narrowed to 7mm. Acromial morphology shows a Type II hooked acromion contributing to space compromise.

puzzles.explanation: Shoulder impingement syndrome results from progressive compression of the rotator cuff tendons—particularly the supraspinatus—within the subacromial space, driven by a combination of anatomical factors (hooked acromion, space narrowing) and functional dysfunction (scapular dyskinesis, posterior tightness). The characteristic presentation of pain with overhead activity and relief at rest reflects mechanical compression of an already irritated tendon, while the induction over 8 weeks through postural stress and repetitive loading demonstrates how chronic impingement typically develops insidiously rather than acutely. Scapular dyskinesis—seen here during arm elevation—is a key driver, as altered scapular mechanics further reduce subacromial clearance and perpetuate tendon irritation. Positive Neer and Hawkins tests, combined with early tendinopathy on imaging and a hooked acromion, establish the mechanical basis; conservative management targeting scapular control, posterior shoulder mobility, and postural retraining forms the physiotherapy cornerstone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged to older adult presents with unilateral neck pain that is mechanical in nature. Pain is localised to one side of the posterior neck region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by neck extension and ipsilateral rotation. The patient demonstrates asymmetric movement restriction. Symptoms are activity-dependent and relieved by rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of motor vehicle accident or whiplash injury 2–5 years prior, with intermittent recurrent episodes of unilateral posterior neck pain. No radicular symptoms or neurological complaints reported. Pain pattern is episodic; flare-ups triggered by sustained postures or repetitive rotation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Neck extension combined with ipsilateral side-bending and rotation reproduces pain at the affected level. Unilateral posterior neck tenderness localised to facet region (just lateral to midline). Cervical rotation ROM restricted ipsilaterally by 20–30%. Upper extremity strength, reflexes, and sensation intact; no dermatomal deficit or lower limb signs. Negative neural tension tests.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates facet joint hypertrophy and joint space narrowing at the affected level, with capsular inflammation. Osteophytes project posteriorly without central canal stenosis. No foraminal narrowing or nerve root compression.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical facet joint syndrome results from degenerative changes and inflammatory irritation of the zygapophysial joints, typically following whiplash injury or cumulative mechanical stress that disrupts the joint capsule and triggers hypertrophic changes. The condition is characterized by unilateral posterior neck pain that worsens with extension and ipsilateral rotation—movements that load the facet joints directly—while neurological signs remain conspicuously absent because the degenerative changes remain localized to the joint itself without nerve root compression. The episodic flare-and-remission pattern, combined with pain reproducible on combined extension-rotation-side-bending at one level and preserved motor/sensory function, distinguishes facet syndrome from radiculopathy or myelopathy. MRI findings of facet hypertrophy and capsular inflammation without foraminal or central stenosis confirm the mechanical, non-neural origin of symptoms and support targeted facet-mediated interventions such as manual therapy, postural correction, and stabilization exercises.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old presents with progressive shortness of breath and persistent fatigue over 3 months. Symptoms worse with exertion, improving with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to climb one flight of stairs without stopping to catch breath. Flat walking limited to 50 metres before severe dyspnea. Wakes at night gasping for air; needs extra pillows to sleep comfortably.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing hypertension (15 years, on medications). Prior myocardial infarction 5 years ago. Gradual functional decline over months; no recent trauma or infection.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral ankle swelling that pits with pressure. Neck veins distended when lying reclined. Bibasal lung crackles on auscultation. Resting heart rate 105 bpm; systolic BP elevated at 155 mmHg.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiography shows left ventricular ejection fraction 35% (severely reduced). Dilated left ventricle with impaired systolic contraction. Elevated filling pressures evident on Doppler assessment.

puzzles.explanation: Heart failure is a progressive syndrome where the heart fails to pump sufficient blood to meet the body's metabolic demands, most commonly due to left ventricular systolic dysfunction from prior myocardial infarction or chronic hypertension as in this case. The severely reduced ejection fraction (35%) and dilated, poorly contracting left ventricle create a cascade of backward pressure into the pulmonary and systemic circulations, explaining the orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea requiring extra pillows, and the bibasal crackles from pulmonary edema. Peripheral signs including bilateral ankle edema, jugular venous distension, and exertional dyspnea out of proportion to modest activity reflect the systemic congestion and reduced cardiac output characteristic of decompensated heart failure. The marked functional decline — unable to climb stairs or walk 50 metres without severe breathlessness — is typical of NYHA Class III-IV disease, where the failing ventricle can no longer meet even modest aerobic demands.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An older adult presents with acute hip pain and inability to move the affected leg following a fall.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient held leg rigidly in a fixed posture and resists any movement of the hip. Severe pain with any attempt at mobility.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of osteoporosis. Fall occurred from standing height. No prior hip pain or dysfunction.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the lower extremity is held in external rotation and abduction. Significant swelling is evident over the hip.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show a fracture line between the greater and lesser trochanters, extending from the lateral femoral cortex medially across the femoral neck base.

puzzles.explanation: An intertrochanteric fracture is a break through the proximal femur between the greater and lesser trochanters, typically occurring in older adults with osteoporosis following low-energy trauma such as a ground-level fall. The fracture disrupts the blood supply to the femoral head and creates significant soft tissue trauma, explaining the acute pain, swelling, and the characteristic posture of external rotation and abduction that patients adopt to reduce muscle pull on the fracture fragments. Because the fracture line extends across the femoral neck base, it can compromise the medial femoral circumflex artery, potentially leading to avascular necrosis if healing is delayed. Early mobilization is critical for preventing complications like deep vein thrombosis and pneumonia, making rapid surgical fixation — typically with intramedullary nailing — the standard of care for most patients.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old footballer experienced sudden onset knee pain during a match. Describes a 'popping' sensation and immediate swelling on the inner knee after a twisting tackle.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot weight-bear fully on the affected leg. Pain is worse with pivoting movements and side-stepping, but forward walking is tolerated.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during contact sport when another player's shoulder drove into the patient's lateral knee while the foot was planted. No prior knee surgery, but patient reports previous ankle sprains.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Grade 2 medial joint line tenderness. Anterior drawer test is negative. Lachman test is negative. Mild effusion present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates medial collateral ligament tear with characterisation of tear severity and associated injuries.

puzzles.explanation: Medial collateral ligament (MCL) sprains result from valgus stress to a planted knee, typically during contact or pivoting activities, causing disruption of the ligament's fibers that normally stabilize the medial tibiofemoral joint. The varus-stress mechanism here—lateral force driving the knee into valgus while the foot was fixed—is the classic injury pattern. Grade 2 sprains involve partial tearing with preserved functional continuity, explaining the preserved anterior stability (negative anterior drawer and Lachman tests) despite the significant swelling and medial tenderness; the rotational instability with pivoting reflects incomplete healing of the torn fibers. MCL injuries generally have an excellent prognosis with conservative management and rarely require surgical intervention, distinguishing them from combined ligamentous injuries that often involve the ACL or posterolateral corner.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old retiree presents with progressive mechanical low back pain that has developed gradually over several years. The pain is typically localized to the low lumbar region and is worsened in the morning and by end-of-day fatigue.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are exacerbated by sustained postures, particularly prolonged sitting and standing, with the patient reporting need for frequent position changes. Walking and gentle movement provide temporary relief. Bending activities are limited due to pain rather than mechanical restriction.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient reports a 40-year history of occupational heavy lifting as a construction worker. Previous episodes of acute low back pain have occurred over the past 15 years with increasing frequency. Multiple risk factors include smoking history, obesity, and sedentary lifestyle post-retirement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, lumbar flexion is limited to 40 degrees due to pain, with loss of normal lumbar lordosis. Palpation reveals paraspinal muscle spasm. Facet joint loading tests elicit localized pain. Straight leg raise is normal to 75 degrees bilaterally. Lower extremity strength remains 5/5 throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine MRI shows multiple levels of disc degeneration with decreased disc height, particularly at L4-L5 and L5-S1. Signal loss within discs is noted on T2-weighted images. Osteophytes are present at degenerated segments without significant central canal stenosis.

puzzles.explanation: Degenerative disc disease (DDD) represents progressive loss of disc integrity due to cumulative mechanical loading, dehydration, and age-related changes in the nucleus pulposus and annulus fibrosus — a near-universal process that accelerates with occupational stress, smoking, and obesity. The condition typically presents with mechanical low back pain that worsens with end-of-day fatigue and sustained postures, as seen here, because degenerated discs lose shock-absorbing capacity and destabilize segmental motion. The characteristic MRI findings of decreased disc height, T2 signal loss, and reactive osteophytes reflect this degenerative cascade, particularly at the lower lumbar segments where cumulative loading is greatest. Notably, preserved neurological function and absence of true stenosis distinguish uncomplicated DDD from more severe variants — the pain is primarily from altered mechanics and facet joint loading rather than nerve root compromise.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient presents with acute onset of neck pain and upper back discomfort following a motor vehicle accident. Onset was immediate.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports pain radiating toward the shoulder and upper limb. Symptoms worsen with sustained postures and improve with rest and support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 weeks ago. Patient reports no loss of consciousness. No prior neck problems. Symptoms have plateaued; initial severe pain has subsided partially but stiffness persists.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical flexion limited to 20°; rotation and lateral flexion also restricted symmetrically. Palpable muscle spasm in upper trapezius and paraspinal muscles. Cervical myelopathy signs absent. Upper limb strength and reflexes intact bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain cervical radiographs show loss of normal lordosis and no acute fracture or instability. MRI demonstrates mild muscle edema in paraspinal muscles without disc herniation, cord compression, or canal stenosis.

puzzles.explanation: Whiplash-associated disorder results from rapid acceleration-deceleration forces to the cervical spine, typically in motor vehicle collisions, causing musculoligamentous injury and protective muscle guarding without structural fracture or instability. The immediate onset of neck pain with radiation toward the shoulder, combined with symmetrical restriction of all cervical movements and palpable paraspinal muscle spasm, reflects the acute inflammatory response and reflex muscle contraction protecting the injured soft tissues. The plateau in symptoms at two weeks, loss of normal lordosis on plain films, and mild muscle edema on MRI are characteristic of this whiplash pattern—a clinical diagnosis of soft-tissue injury where imaging often appears disproportionately benign compared to reported symptoms. Prognosis varies significantly; while most patients recover within weeks to months with appropriate graded mobilization and activity management, persistent stiffness and symptoms can develop in a proportion of cases.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old male woken at 2 AM with sudden-onset severe pain in the right foot. Joint is swollen, warm, and red. Pain is throbbing and disproportionate to any injury he recalls. Mobility severely limited.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot bear weight on the affected foot. Even light touch or passive movement triggers sharp pain. No morning stiffness elsewhere. Symptoms peaked overnight and remain constant; patient has tried rest and elevation with minimal relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Similar episodes occurred 3 and 5 years ago, each resolving spontaneously over 7-10 days without medical intervention. Heavy seafood dinner and several beers consumed the evening before onset. No recent trauma, falls, or overuse. No systemic illness or fever.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Exquisite point tenderness at first MTP joint line. Marked soft-tissue swelling and erythema over the great toe joint.
