Dorsiflexion severely limited and painful; passive ROM triggers disproportionate pain response. Core temperature normal. No other joints inflamed. Skin over joint appears shiny.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serum uric acid elevated at 8.2 mg/dL. Ultrasound shows hypoechoic joint effusion with synovial inflammation and increased Doppler signal. X-ray excludes fracture and significant degenerative changes. Clinical pattern + lab + imaging consistent with acute monosodium urate crystal-induced arthritis.

puzzles.explanation: Gout is an acute inflammatory arthritis triggered by monosodium urate (MSU) crystal deposition in joints, classically affecting the first metatarsophalangeal joint in middle-aged males with hyperuricemia. The condition results from supersaturation of uric acid in serum and synovial fluid—often precipitated by purine-rich foods (seafood), alcohol, dehydration, or sudden dietary changes—causing needle-shaped crystals to trigger an intense innate immune response characterized by neutrophil infiltration and IL-1β release. The sudden nocturnal onset, exquisite focal tenderness, shiny erythematous skin, and marked swelling with disproportionate pain on even passive movement are pathognomonic for acute crystal arthritis, distinguishing it from mechanical joint injury or systemic polyarthritis. The recurrent episodic pattern resolving spontaneously over days, combined with elevated serum uric acid and ultrasound confirmation of synovial inflammation and effusion, confirms MSU crystal-induced disease—the most common cause of acute monoarticular arthritis in adult males.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old woman presents with progressive pain and swelling in her forefoot, particularly affecting the great toe joint region. She first noticed a bony prominence on the medial side of her foot in her early 30s, which has steadily worsened over the past decade.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by walking in fitted shoes and high heels, with symptoms improving when shoes are removed. The patient has difficulty fitting into standard shoe sizes and has compensated by wearing increasingly wide shoes. Running and prolonged standing significantly worsen symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Strong maternal family history of progressive foot deformities. Patient wore high heels occupationally for 15+ years. No preceding trauma or acute injury. Progressive worsening despite activity modification attempts.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weight-bearing assessment reveals lateral deviation of the great toe with a prominent bony bump on the medial forefoot. The first metatarsophalangeal joint shows tenderness to palpation, mild effusion, and restricted extension range. Intermetatarsal spacing appears widened on visual inspection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing anteroposterior foot radiographs demonstrate lateral deviation of the hallux with intermetatarsal angle of 12 degrees and hallux valgus angle of 28 degrees. The medial eminence is prominent. Early degenerative changes are noted at the first MTP joint.

puzzles.explanation: Hallux valgus is a progressive deformity of the first metatarsophalangeal joint characterized by lateral deviation of the great toe and medial prominence of the first metatarsal head, driven by a combination of intrinsic biomechanical factors and extrinsic loading patterns. The condition results from a vicious cycle: abnormal pronation and hypermobility at the first ray allow progressive separation of the first and second metatarsals, which increases the intermetatarsal angle and perpetuates lateral drift of the hallux — a process accelerated by hereditary ligamentous laxity and prolonged high-heel wear that shifts load laterally. The restricted MTP extension and early degenerative changes seen here reflect secondary joint damage from altered mechanics and chronic impingement against shoe wear. While conservative measures like footwear modification provide symptomatic relief, the underlying structural deformity and widened intermetatarsal angle typically progress over time, often warranting surgical realignment if functional impairment becomes significant.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An infant presents with restricted neck motion and a persistent head preference to one side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Infant unable to turn head fully to one side and prefers to look over the same shoulder. Difficulty tracking objects across midline.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The condition is congenital, often with history of difficult delivery or positioning. Early intervention is important for outcomes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, unilateral sternocleidomastoid tightness and restriction of neck rotation are demonstrated. A mass may be palpable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates focal thickening and fibrosis of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, confirming muscle-based pathology rather than vertebral or neurological cause.

puzzles.explanation: Congenital torticollis results from unilateral fibrosis and shortening of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, typically following intrauterine positioning constraints or birth trauma that damages muscle fibres; a palpable mass (fibrous pseudotumour) may be present initially. The condition presents with a persistent head tilt toward the affected side and restricted rotation away from it, as the shortened muscle prevents the infant from achieving midline head position and crossing the visual midline during tracking. Early recognition is critical because the fibrotic muscle limits normal cervical development and visual-motor integration, potentially leading to secondary postural deformities, plagiocephaly, and asymmetric vision development if untreated. Physiotherapy focusing on passive and active stretching, positioning strategies, and developmental facilitation in infancy can prevent progression and often resolve the condition, making the first months of life a crucial window for intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete presents with anterior knee pain triggered by jumping and running. Pain is activity-dependent and reproducible.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain localised to the patellar region during eccentric loading activities, particularly landing from height and deceleration movements. Aggravated by repeated plyometric demand.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset correlates with increased training volume or intensity. Recent changes in training are often contributory.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable at the patellar tendon insertion. Pain is reproduced with resisted knee extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates patellar tendon thickening and hypoechoic changes. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Patellar tendinopathy is a chronic overuse condition affecting the patellar tendon, typically occurring in young athletes exposed to repetitive high-impact loading—particularly jumping, landing, and rapid deceleration movements that generate eccentric tensile forces at the tendon-bone junction. The condition reflects cumulative microtrauma and failed adaptive remodelling, often triggered by sudden increases in training volume or intensity, and presents with focal tenderness at the patellar insertion and pain reproducible on resisted knee extension. The imaging findings of tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, and intrasubstance signal alterations reflect the underlying collagen disruption and inflammatory response. Management emphasises load management and eccentric strengthening to restore tendon resilience and tolerance to plyometric demand.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports progressive pain across multiple joints with episodes of instability and swelling. Symptoms have been lifelong with variable severity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to participate in contact sports or high-impact activities. Experiences recurrent sprains and dislocations, particularly at ankles, shoulders, and fingers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient notes mother and two siblings with similar lifelong joint problems. Early childhood marked by unusual flexibility; no discrete injury triggering onset. Prior physiotherapy and orthopaedic assessment without definitive diagnosis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Beighton score 7/9 (hyperextension of knees and elbows, thumb opposition, forward trunk flexion). Generalised ligamentous laxity with no focal instability pattern. Strength and neurological examination intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of shoulder: no structural labral tear or rotator cuff pathology despite recurrent instability episodes. Spine imaging normal. Genetic testing confirms haploinsufficiency affecting collagen architecture, consistent with benign joint hypermobility syndrome.

puzzles.explanation: Generalised joint hypermobility results from a systemic connective tissue disorder — in this case confirmed by genetic testing showing collagen haploinsufficiency — that affects ligamentous integrity and proprioceptive control across multiple joints rather than localising to a single structure. The hallmark presentation combines lifelong, generalised flexibility (captured by the Beighton score), recurrent instability and sprains particularly at high-demand joints like the shoulder and ankle, and a strong family history reflecting autosomal dominant inheritance. What distinguishes this from structural joint pathology is the absence of discrete labral tears or rotator cuff damage despite frank episodes of instability — the problem lies in the supporting tissues themselves, not acquired trauma. Management focuses on proprioceptive training, controlled strengthening to compensate for ligamentous laxity, and load management to prevent cumulative microtrauma rather than surgical stabilisation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old struck from behind in traffic 3 days ago. Now reports neck pain, upper back stiffness, and bilateral shoulder tension. Pain onset was delayed—minimal symptoms immediately after incident, but worsening progressively. Also experiencing intermittent headaches at base of skull.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate head turns or looking over shoulder. Difficulty maintaining upright posture at desk; symptoms flare with sustained sitting. Pain increases with combined neck movements (e.g., turning head while tilting it back). Avoids driving due to pain with mirror checks and lane changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rapid deceleration injury from rear-end motor vehicle collision. No direct blow to head, no loss of consciousness, no facial or skull trauma reported. No pre-existing cervical spine disease. Initially walked away from scene with mild discomfort; pain escalated over 24–48 hours. Denies neurological symptoms (numbness, tingling, weakness) in arms or hands.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical ROM severely limited in rotation and extension due to pain; lateral flexion less restricted. Marked muscular guarding palpable in upper trapezius, levator scapulae, and cervical paraspinals bilaterally. Tenderness across midline cervical spine and lateral neck musculature. Headache reproducible with passive neck extension. Upper extremity strength normal (5/5), reflexes intact, no dermatomal sensory deficits or signs of nerve root irritation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays: normal alignment, no fracture, subluxation, or instability. MRI cervical spine: T2/STIR signal hyperintensity in cervical musculature and posterior ligaments (interspinous, supraspinous ligaments); no spinal cord edema, myelopathy, or significant nerve root compression; vertebral bodies unremarkable.

puzzles.explanation: Whiplash-associated disorder (WAD) results from rapid acceleration-deceleration forces that injure the cervical spine and surrounding soft tissues without necessarily causing fracture or neurological compromise. The characteristic delayed onset of symptoms—minimal discomfort immediately after the rear-end collision but progressive worsening over 24–48 hours—reflects the inflammatory cascade affecting muscles, ligaments, and facet joints as muscular guarding intensifies. The MRI findings of T2 hyperintensity in the cervical musculature and posterior ligaments confirm this soft-tissue injury pattern, which explains the severe loss of rotation and extension alongside normal neurological findings. Management focuses on early active mobilization, postural restoration, and addressing the fear-avoidance cycle that perpetuates guarding and functional limitation in combined neck movements.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports sudden onset of severe pain and swelling in limb 2–4 days after minor surgical procedure or wound. Pain described as out of proportion to clinical appearance. Associated with fever, chills, and feeling systemically unwell.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight or use affected limb due to pain and rapid swelling. Symptoms progressing visibly over hours rather than days. Patient reports nausea, malaise, and difficulty concentrating alongside local symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent minor surgery, injection site, or puncture wound 24–72 hours prior. No significant trauma history. Patient denies recent illness but reports rapid deterioration despite antibiotics if started. Comorbidities may include diabetes or immunosuppression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Skin erythema spreading rapidly beyond apparent wound margins. Severe pain on palpation. Palpable crepitus or tense swelling in deep tissues. Skin may show blistering or mottling. Vital signs: tachycardia, fever, hypotension. Disproportionate systemic toxicity relative to wound appearance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows extensive fluid and inflammatory changes tracking along tissue planes (fascial spaces) beyond visible skin involvement. Gas may be present in deep tissues on imaging. Imaging confirms rapid spread pattern incompatible with routine post-operative inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Necrotising fasciitis is a rapidly progressive, life-threatening soft tissue infection caused by polymicrobial or monomicrobial bacteria (commonly Group A Streptococcus, Vibrio, or Clostridium species) that spreads along fascial planes rather than vertically through tissue layers. The hallmark presentation — pain grossly out of proportion to visible skin changes, systemic toxicity, and rapid progression over hours — reflects the aggressive subcutaneous spread of infection beneath an initially deceptively normal-appearing skin surface. The combination of crepitus, mottling, blistering, and MRI evidence of fluid tracking along tissue planes establishes the fascial compartment involvement that distinguishes this from cellulitis or simple wound infection. This is a surgical emergency requiring immediate fasciotomy and broad-spectrum antibiotics; delayed recognition during rehabilitation settings is a critical risk factor for mortality.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old presents with shoulder pain and swelling, worse with overhead activities. Multiple shoulder pathologies should be considered.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform overhead reaching or repetitive arm activities without pain. Pain is worst during abduction movements; rests arm in protective position.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in overhead work over past 2–3 weeks. History of chronic shoulder strain and possible rotator cuff involvement. Onset acute rather than insidious.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, focal swelling is evident over the deltoid region. Shoulder abduction reproduces pain. Neer's test is positive, and direct palpation elicits focal tenderness. Shoulder range of motion is restricted in abduction and external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows fluid collection within the subdeltoid bursal space. Bursa is distended; no rotator cuff tear visible. Findings consistent with bursal inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Subdeltoid bursitis is inflammation of the bursa lying beneath the deltoid muscle, a potential space that normally contains only trace fluid but becomes distended when irritated by repetitive overhead activity or mechanical impingement. The bursa cushions the rotator cuff tendons and deltoid from the underlying bony structures; when compressed or irritated—particularly during abduction—it swells and produces focal tenderness and pain that is worse with overhead movements. The acute onset following increased overhead work over 2–3 weeks is classic, as is the protective arm posture and restricted abduction range that develop to reduce bursal compression. Ultrasound findings of a fluid-filled bursa without rotator cuff pathology, combined with positive impingement tests like Neer's, are diagnostically distinctive and help differentiate this from other painful shoulder conditions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 72-year-old reports dizziness and lightheadedness when standing up from bed or chair, particularly in the morning. Episodes resolve within seconds to minutes of remaining upright or sitting back down. Denies chest pain, palpitations, or hearing changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports symptoms occur reliably during positional transitions (supine to sitting, sitting to standing) but resolves quickly with continued standing. No symptoms during sustained standing, walking, or seated activities. No limitation with climbing stairs or exertion once upright.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 3 months ago, coinciding with initiation of two new blood pressure medications. Recent viral illness with diarrhea 2 weeks prior. Patient reports occasional forgetfulness with medication timing and hydration intake. No cardiac history, no prior syncope or falls.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Supine blood pressure 158/88. After 1 minute standing: 125/80. Systolic drop of 33 mmHg. Heart rate supine 68 bpm; standing 70 bpm (minimal increase). No postural tremor, no focal neurological deficits. Carotid pulses normal, no bruits. Gait steady once upright.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring during supine-to-standing transition shows inadequate heart rate response to blood pressure drop (expected 10–15 bpm increase, observed only 2 bpm). Suggests impaired autonomic reflexes rather than acute bleeding or heart failure. ECG normal; no arrhythmia.

puzzles.explanation: Postural hypotension (orthostatic hypotension) is a transient drop in blood pressure upon standing due to impaired autonomic compensation, most commonly triggered by medications, dehydration, or autonomic dysfunction. The hallmark presentation—a >20 mmHg systolic drop with symptoms that resolve quickly once recumbency is restored or standing is sustained—distinguishes it from syncope or heart failure, as the body eventually recruits compensatory mechanisms. In this case, the temporal relationship to antihypertensive initiation, recent volume depletion from gastroenteritis, and blunted heart rate response (only 2 bpm increase rather than the expected 10–15 bpm) point to a medication-induced or volume-mediated cause rather than primary autonomic failure. Management focuses on identifying and modifying the precipitant—medication adjustment, hydration, sodium intake, and positional maneuvers—rather than further diagnostic workup, given the normal cardiac examination and ECG.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old administrative professional working 8 hours daily at a computer workstation presents with bilateral forearm and wrist aching. Symptoms began insidiously 8 weeks ago with gradual onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged typing and mouse use, easing with rest breaks. Functional impact includes reduced work tolerance and awkward posture adoption to minimize discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No history of acute traumatic injury. Desk height reduction 8 weeks ago coincided with symptom onset. No prior similar episodes. Patient denies evening swelling or night symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Multiple tender points throughout flexor and extensor forearm musculature bilaterally. No positive Phalen or Tinel sign. Grip strength reduced but symmetrical. Forearm pronation/supination mildly limited.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG and nerve conduction studies normal bilaterally. Ultrasound imaging shows no tendon inflammation, nerve thickening, or structural abnormality. MRI of cervical spine unremarkable—no root compression or myelopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational overuse syndrome (OOS) is a cumulative microtrauma condition arising from prolonged, repetitive upper limb activities without adequate recovery, typically affecting keyboard workers and others in sustained postures. Unlike nerve compression syndromes (which show positive provocative tests and electrophysiological changes), OOS presents with diffuse muscular tenderness, normal nerve conduction studies, and the absence of structural pathology—the pathology is primarily myofascial fatigue and micro-inflammatory changes in muscle rather than neurological compromise. The bilateral forearm and wrist aching triggered by the ergonomic change (desk height reduction) and exacerbated by sustained typing is characteristic, as is the relief with rest breaks. Management centers on activity modification, postural correction, graduated loading, and ergonomic optimization rather than surgery or pharmaceutical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old competitive distance runner reports posterolateral knee pain that began 3 weeks ago. Sharp, localized discomfort near the knee joint line on the outside.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worsens significantly downhill running and high-speed sprinting. Subsides with walking and rest. Unable to complete training sessions. Pain on eccentric hamstring loading.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Increased weekly running distance by 40% four weeks prior to symptom onset. No history of acute hamstring tear. Had lateral knee pain from IT band syndrome two years ago, now resolved.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness over biceps femoris insertion at fibular head, exacerbated with knee flexion against resistance. Palpable tendon thickening at insertion site. Strength testing normal. Pain reproduced with resisted knee flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps femoris tendon thickening and hypoechogenicity at fibular insertion. MRI confirms tendon inflammation without full-thickness tear. Bursa involvement possible.

puzzles.explanation: Biceps femoris tendinopathy is an overuse injury of the distal biceps femoris tendon at its fibular head insertion, commonly seen in runners who rapidly increase training volume or intensity. The posterolateral knee pain with eccentric loading and resisted flexion reflects the tendon's role as a powerful knee flexor and external tibial rotator — functions heavily demanded in downhill and high-speed running. Unlike acute hamstring tears, tendinopathy develops insidiously through repetitive microtrauma and inadequate recovery, with imaging showing inflammation and tendon thickening at the insertion rather than fibre disruption. The history of prior lateral knee pain suggests altered biomechanics or chronic lower limb loading asymmetry may predispose to secondary tendon pathology on the posterolateral aspect.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 64-year-old woman with a 15-year history of progressive foot deformity. Now presenting with pain in the forefoot, particularly over a bony prominence on one toe. Discomfort worsened by footwear.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to wear narrow shoes or heels. Reports altered gait pattern and avoidance of prolonged walking. Pain prevents normal shoe selection.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong hallux valgus and crowding in foot structure contributed to hammer toe development. Inflammatory arthropathy absent. Daughters have similar foot structure. No acute trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Fixed deformity at proximal interphalangeal joint (plantarflexion position non-reducible). Metatarsophalangeal joint shows flexion contracture with dorsal prominence. Prominent callus and corn formation overlying the bony prominence.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing radiographs confirm fixed plantarflexion deformity at the proximal interphalangeal joint with metatarsophalangeal dorsal subluxation. Early degenerative changes visible at the proximal interphalangeal joint articulation.

puzzles.explanation: Hammer toe is a fixed flexion deformity at the proximal interphalangeal joint, typically developing secondary to biomechanical imbalance from hallux valgus and forefoot crowding that shifts load and muscle pull across the lesser toes. The proximal interphalangeal joint assumes a plantarflexed (hammered) posture, while the metatarsophalangeal joint compensates with dorsal subluxation, creating the characteristic bony prominence that causes pain and callus formation over the dorsum. This deformity becomes progressively non-reducible as the underlying muscles shorten, joint capsules contract, and degenerative changes develop — making early intervention important before structural changes become fixed. The functional limitation here reflects how the rigid dorsal prominence and forefoot altered mechanics conflict with normal shoe fit and walking pattern, a hallmark presentation of established hammer toe requiring either conservative pressure relief or surgical correction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old ballet dancer reports anterior hip discomfort with groin tenderness onset 2 weeks ago. Pain worse with activities involving repeated hip flexion and turning movements. Some radiation into the upper anterior thigh.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate sustained hip flexion or repetitive flexion-based movements. Stair climbing and prolonged sitting provoke symptoms. Activities requiring hip extension after flexion are particularly uncomfortable.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive ballet training 4 hours daily with recent choreography changes requiring greater hip flexibility demands. No prior hip pathology. Pain began 2 weeks after increasing flexibility training intensity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion ROM limited to 110 degrees (pain-limited). Thomas test positive suggesting hip flexor tightness. Palpation of anterior hip/groin elicits focal tenderness. Pain reproduced with combined hip extension and internal rotation (FABER modification).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hip ultrasound demonstrates bursa effusion anterior to hip joint. MRI confirms iliopsoas bursitis without labral pathology. Iliopsoas tendon visualized normally on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Iliopsoas bursitis is inflammation of the bursa lying between the iliopsoas tendon and the anterior hip joint capsule, typically triggered by repetitive hip flexion or excessive demands on hip flexibility. The condition commonly affects dancers and athletes due to the intense, repetitive flexion-extension loading required in their activities, with symptoms localizing to anterior groin pain and upper thigh radiation that worsen dramatically with sustained flexion and stair climbing. The positive Thomas test and pain with combined extension and internal rotation reflect both the underlying hip flexor tightness and direct bursal irritation as the inflamed bursa is compressed between the tendon and capsule. Ultrasound confirmation of bursal effusion, combined with normal labral and tendon imaging, distinguishes this from intra-articular or tendinopathic hip pathology, making load modification and hip flexor management the cornerstones of recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 60-year-old with several years of progressive neck pain and intermittent tingling in both arms. Pain worsens with certain neck movements. Recently noticed increasing clumsiness with fine motor tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient struggles with writing and buttoning clothes. Difficulty gripping objects with consistent force. Gait has become slightly unsteady over past 6 months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decades of desk work and poor posture. Previous imaging 10 years ago showed disc bulging. No major trauma. Symptoms stable until 18 months ago when progression accelerated. No previous neck surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, neck range of motion is limited in multiple planes. Upper extremity strength testing reveals subtle deficits. Hyperreflexia and upgoing plantars may be present, suggesting cord irritation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates multiple level disc degeneration with osteophytes and spinal cord compression. The spinal canal is narrowed with cord signal changes indicating myelopathic compromise.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical spondylosis is degenerative narrowing of the cervical spinal canal due to cumulative disc herniation, osteophyte formation, and ligamentous hypertrophy—typically the result of decades of postural stress and natural aging. When the spinal cord itself becomes compressed (cervical myelopathy), patients develop a distinctive pattern of progressive upper motor neuron signs: hyperreflexia, upgoing plantars, and upper extremity weakness coupled with fine motor dysfunction affecting writing and grip, exactly as seen in this patient's accelerating loss of manual dexterity. The bilateral arm tingling and gait unsteadiness reflect cord-level irritation rather than isolated nerve root compression, distinguishing myelopathy from radiculopathy. MRI confirmation of cord signal changes is critical, as the severity of cord compromise—not just canal narrowing alone—dictates prognosis and the need for surgical referral to prevent permanent neurological damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 3-month postpartum woman reports difficulty with functional activities and core stability. Notices her abdomen looks different and bulges when performing certain movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to return to pre-pregnancy exercise routines. Bulging worsens with carrying baby and stair climbing. Notably improves with external abdominal bracing/splinting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Normal pregnancy and vaginal delivery 3 months ago. Pre-pregnancy fitness level good with strong core baseline. Postpartum exercise initiated without clearance or modification for diastasis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Midline separation palpable, approximately 2.5 fingers width at umbilicus. Tissue laxity evident. Abdominal contraction causes lateral bulging of rectus muscles. Fascia integrity intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging shows diastasis rectus abdominis with interrectal separation 2.8 cm. Linea alba appears attenuated. No hernia. Dynamic assessment shows separation persists with contraction.

puzzles.explanation: Diastasis rectus abdominis (DRA) is a separation of the rectus abdominis muscles along the midline, caused by stretching and thinning of the linea alba during pregnancy. The condition is nearly universal in the third trimester but typically resolves within weeks postpartum; persistent separation beyond 3 months, as seen here, reflects inadequate fascial remodelling and excessive intra-abdominal pressure during functional activities. The characteristic bulging with movement and rapid improvement with external bracing occurs because the attenuated linea alba cannot adequately contain the contracting rectus muscles, forcing them to bow laterally rather than generate effective core tension. While fascial integrity is preserved, the loss of force closure means that even previously fit individuals cannot return to loaded activities or heavy resistance until progressive neuromuscular re-education and real-time feedback restore motor control of the deep stabilizers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old healthcare worker reports recurrent episodes of chest tightness and breathlessness occurring several times weekly. Episodes are accompanied by anxiety and sense of panic.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During episodes, patient experiences dizziness, lightheadedness, and a feeling of losing control. Avoidance behavior is developing—patient limits public outings due to fear of episodes occurring. Symptoms occur reliably with stress or crowded environments.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known anxiety disorder for 3 years, previously managed with CBT alone. Recently started sertraline 6 weeks ago following increase in workplace demands and workload. No prior respiratory or cardiac diagnoses. Episodes are predictably triggered by identifiable stressors.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: During controlled hyperventilation challenge, patient reproduces symptoms of paresthesia and lightheadedness. Arterial blood gas shows respiratory alkalosis (pH 7.48, low pCO2). Cardiac exam normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Capnography shows end-tidal CO2 below 30 mmHg during symptoms, confirming hyperventilation-induced hypocapnia. ECG normal excluding cardiac cause. Spirometry normal pulmonary function.

puzzles.explanation: Anxiety-related hyperventilation is a respiratory pattern disorder where psychological stress triggers sustained overbreathing, leading to excessive CO2 elimination and respiratory alkalosis rather than a primary pulmonary pathology. The resulting hypocapnia causes characteristic symptoms—paresthesia, dizziness, and chest tightness—through cerebral vasoconstriction and altered neuromuscular excitability, creating a self-perpetuating cycle where fear of symptoms drives further hyperventilation. The predictable triggering by stressors and workplace demands, combined with the reproducibility of symptoms during controlled hyperventilation challenge and documented end-tidal CO2 below 30 mmHg, confirms the diagnosis when cardiac and primary respiratory causes have been excluded. Management focuses on breathing retraining, cognitive-behavioral strategies to interrupt the anxiety-hyperventilation loop, and continuation of pharmacological anxiety management rather than respiratory interventions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 41-year-old office worker with sedentary job presents with chronic lower back pain of 6 months duration. Mild to moderate pain (4/10 at baseline) with periodic exacerbations.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting, improves with movement and standing. Functional limitations include difficulty with prolonged work postures and household activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset without specific traumatic event. Repetitive strain from workstation setup. Mild obesity (BMI 28). Previous episode of low back pain 3 years ago, self-resolved.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar segmental mobility unremarkable. Hip flexor tightness bilaterally. Core strength 4/5. Straight leg raise negative. No neurological deficits or radicular signs. Palpation of lumbar paraspinals reveals mild muscular guarding without acute tenderness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine X-rays show mild degenerative disc disease at L4-L5 and L5-S1. MRI demonstrates small disc bulging without neural compromise. No significant pathology explaining symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Non-specific low back pain (NSLBP) is chronic lumbar pain without clear structural pathology or nerve involvement—a diagnosis of exclusion that accounts for the majority of back pain presentations. It typically arises from a combination of factors including postural dysfunction, muscular deconditioning, and repetitive loading, as evidenced here by sedentary work habits, reduced core strength, and hip flexor tightness without objective neurological findings. The presence of mild degenerative changes and disc bulging on imaging is often incidental and does not necessarily explain the patient's symptoms, reinforcing why imaging alone cannot drive clinical reasoning. Management focuses on restoring mobility, strengthening core musculature, and modifying activities—particularly addressing prolonged sitting postures—rather than pursuing structural interventions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old man with hypertension and heavy smoking history presents with right-sided weakness and facial asymmetry noted while at work. Symptoms progressing over the past few hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk independently. Speech difficult to understand. Trouble understanding spoken words. Cognitive changes noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hypertension poorly controlled at admission (BP 180/110). Smokes 20 cigarettes daily. No prior neurological events. Symptoms reached maximum severity within minutes of onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left-sided facial droop noted. Right pronator drift present. Increased reflexes (3+) in right arm and leg. Babinski sign elicited on right. Visual field defect on right side bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Acute CT brain shows hypodensity in left MCA territory involving motor cortex and corona radiata. Diffusion-weighted MRI confirms acute ischemic stroke. Angiography shows MCA occlusion.

puzzles.explanation: Middle cerebral artery stroke results from acute occlusion of the MCA, the largest intracranial vessel, typically by thromboembolism in patients with significant vascular risk factors like uncontrolled hypertension and smoking. The MCA supplies the motor cortex and internal capsule, so occlusion produces the characteristic contralateral weakness and facial asymmetry seen here, along with upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski response). The rapid onset within minutes and involvement of language areas explains the speech and comprehension deficits, while the visual field defect reflects damage to the optic radiations in the MCA territory. This clinical picture—sudden focal neurological deficit in a high-risk patient with acute hypodensity on imaging and confirmed arterial occlusion—is the classic presentation of acute ischemic stroke requiring immediate thrombolytic or thrombectomy intervention to salvage ischemic tissue.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman presents with a painful ulcer on the lower leg that has been present for 4 weeks. She reports long-standing swelling and gradual skin discolouration in the same area.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is significantly worse when the leg hangs down and improves with elevation. The ulcer produces moderate exudate and requires frequent dressing changes, limiting her walking tolerance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She reports a prior episode of deep vein thrombosis in the right leg 15 years ago. Her current lower leg edema has been progressive and unresponsive to compression therapy attempts over the past several years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Medial malleolar region ulcer with depth 0.5 cm, irregular borders, beefy red base. Surrounding skin with lipodermatosclerosis and hemosiderin deposition. Pitting edema bilateral legs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Duplex ultrasound shows superficial and deep venous insufficiency with valve incompetence in great saphenous vein. No acute DVT. Ankle-brachial index 0.95, confirming adequate arterial perfusion.

puzzles.explanation: Venous leg ulcers develop when chronic venous insufficiency impairs the calf muscle pump and valve function, leading to venous hypertension, tissue hypoxia, and eventual skin breakdown — typically in the medial malleolar region where pressure is highest. A history of deep vein thrombosis, as in this patient, is a major risk factor for post-thrombotic syndrome and progressive valve incompetence. The characteristic presentation of positional pain (worse with dependency, improved with elevation), beefy red ulcer base with irregular borders, lipodermatosclerosis, and hemosiderin staining reflects the chronic inflammatory state and microcirculatory damage underlying venous pathology. Duplex ultrasound confirmation of superficial and deep venous insufficiency with an adequate ankle-brachial index distinguishes venous from arterial ulcers and guides compression therapy as the cornerstone of management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old patient presents with hip pain and difficulty with lower limb mobility that began 6 weeks ago following a surgical procedure. Pain is exacerbated by certain movement directions. Patient is currently progressing through rehabilitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient demonstrates guarded movement in specific hip directions (notably combined flexion with adduction and internal rotation). Weight-bearing is limited; ambulation only achievable with walking aid. Stairs and transfers are significantly restricted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient underwent unilateral hip surgery 6 weeks ago for chronic degenerative joint disease. No prior hip pathology or revision. Comorbidities include age-related bone quality concerns. No neurological history or contralateral symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip abductor strength is 3/5 throughout available range. Hip flexion to 80°, abduction to 25°, internal rotation severely limited. Trendelenburg sign present. No groin pain on palpation. No signs of swelling or warmth.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs confirm well-positioned prosthetic components with satisfactory bone ingrowth pattern. Cup and stem angles within normal parameters. Minimal periosteal reaction. No evidence of loosening, subsidence, or periprosthetic fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Post-total hip replacement (THR) recovery involves a predictable rehabilitation phase as soft tissues adapt to the prosthetic construct and neuromuscular control is restored. The guarded movement patterns—particularly the restriction in combined flexion, adduction, and internal rotation—reflect both protective muscle inhibition and the anatomical constraints imposed by hip precautions designed to prevent prosthetic dislocation in the early postoperative period. The weakness of hip abductors (3/5) and positive Trendelenburg sign at 6 weeks are typical findings as the gluteus medius recovers from surgical disruption and denervation, a process that typically requires 8–12 weeks or longer for full strength restoration. Well-positioned radiographic findings rule out mechanical complications, confirming that the functional deficits and pain are attributable to normal postoperative sequelae rather than component malposition or loosening, and that continued progressive loading and strengthening in physiotherapy will drive functional recovery toward the expected plateau at 3–6 months post-surgery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old man presents with sudden onset of severe shoulder pain. Woke this morning with acute discomfort in the lateral upper shoulder. No recall of specific injury or trauma. Pain is sharp and persistent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to lift arm away from body. Overhead reaching impossible. Nighttime pain disrupts sleep, particularly when lying on affected side. Pain worsens with any attempt at shoulder abduction or forward reaching.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Similar episode occurred 2 years ago with identical presentation—sudden severe pain that resolved spontaneously over 4 weeks without intervention. No repetitive overhead occupation. No prior shoulder surgery. This is the second such acute episode.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation reveals marked tenderness over the lateral shoulder, deep to deltoid. Painful arc between 60–120 degrees of abduction. Strength testing normal; unable to perform movement due to pain rather than weakness. Scapular mechanics intact. Neurovascular exam unremarkable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder X-ray shows discrete calcification within the supraspinatus tendon, 8mm, well-defined. Calcified deposit located at critical zone of tendon. No rotator cuff tear on ultrasound. No joint space narrowing or degenerative changes.

puzzles.explanation: Supraspinatus tendon calcification is a condition where hydroxyapatite crystal deposits form within the tendon, most commonly in middle-aged adults without clear mechanical cause. The sudden, severe pain and functional loss of shoulder abduction reflect an acute inflammatory phase, often triggered by calcific material irritating surrounding tissues or rupturing into the subacromial bursa—hence the characteristic painful arc and sharp quality that disrupts sleep. The recurrent nature of this presentation, with spontaneous resolution between episodes, is classic for calcific tendinopathy, which cycles through distinct phases of deposition, inflammatory flare, and resorption over weeks to months. The intact strength on testing and discrete, well-defined deposit on imaging confirm this is an inflammatory process rather than structural failure, and conservative management with activity modification, NSAIDs, and graduated rehabilitation typically resolves the acute episode as the body resorbs the calcification.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old soccer player presents with lateral midfoot pain after an inversion ankle sprain 2 weeks ago. Pain localized to lateral foot, not at ankle joint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty walking and running. Pain with weight-bearing on lateral foot edge. Unable to participate in training and competition. Worsens with plantarflexion and inversion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Inversion injury 2 weeks ago. Initial ankle swelling resolved within days, but isolated midfoot pain has persisted and worsened. No previous foot or ankle injuries. Patient is otherwise healthy with no relevant comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness over cuboid bone palpated from lateral foot. Pain with pronation and plantarflexion loading. Resisted plantarflexion and inversion reproduces pain. Positive cuboid squeeze test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Foot X-rays show normal ankle and calcaneous alignment, but cuboid appears slightly subluxed relative to navicular. Talonavicular joint alignment assessed. No fractures identified.

puzzles.explanation: Cuboid syndrome occurs when the cuboid bone subluxes from its articulation with the navicular and calcaneus, disrupting the lateral column of the foot and compromising the peroneus longus tendon that runs beneath it. The condition commonly develops after inversion ankle sprains when lateral ligamentous laxity allows the cuboid to shift medially; while ankle swelling resolves, the isolated midfoot pain persists because the biomechanical dysfunction remains. The lateral foot pain that worsens with plantarflexion and inversion, combined with positive cuboid squeeze testing and palpable tenderness over the cuboid itself, distinguishes this from residual ankle sprain or syndesmotic injury. In young athletes, cuboid syndrome is often overlooked as "just ankle sprain pain," but recognition of the persistent lateral midfoot symptoms and subtle imaging findings is essential for targeted manipulation and stabilization protocols.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 72-year-old woman with longstanding cardiac history presents with acute neurological change. Sudden difficulty with speech and left-sided limb weakness noted this morning. Symptoms of rapid onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain balance or walk independently. Requires assistance with all mobility tasks and grooming. Left-sided limbs felt 'heavy' and uncoordinated.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known atrial fibrillation for 8 years, not on anticoagulation due to patient non-adherence. Hypertension managed. Previous TIA with full recovery 2 years ago. No recent head trauma or seizures.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left facial droop with asymmetric smile. Left upper extremity weakness 2/5, lower extremity 3/5. Pronator drift present. Hyperreflexia on left side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging shows acute infarction in left middle cerebral artery territory, spanning cortical and subcortical regions.

puzzles.explanation: Middle cerebral artery stroke results from sudden occlusion of the MCA — most often due to thromboembolism from a cardiac source such as atrial fibrillation — causing acute ischaemia across the lateral cerebral hemisphere. The MCA supplies the motor and premotor cortex, internal capsule, and basal ganglia, explaining the classic presentation of contralateral facial droop, upper limb weakness greater than lower limb, and hyperreflexia seen in this patient. Her cardiac history with unmanaged atrial fibrillation created a high thromboembolic risk, and the acute onset of aphasia, hemiparesis, and ataxia reflect the large cortical-subcortical infarct territory visible on diffusion-weighted imaging. Time to thrombolytic therapy remains critical; beyond the therapeutic window, rehabilitation focuses on motor recovery, balance retraining, and preventing recurrence through anticoagulation compliance.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman, 4 weeks post-acute cardiac event, referred for supervised exercise program. Reports dyspnea, chest discomfort with exertion, fatigue, and anxiety about physical activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Exercise tolerance 2 METs at baseline. Dyspnea and chest discomfort with greater exertion. Anxiety about cardiac status limiting activity participation. Fatigue prominent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Inferior STEMI 4 weeks ago treated with primary percutaneous intervention and stent placement. Currently on guideline-directed medical therapy. Hypertension and hyperlipidemia managed. Quit smoking since event.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate elevated at 78 bpm. Blood pressure normal at current status. No arrhythmias on telemetry monitoring. Cardiac murmurs absent. Peripheral edema absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Stress echocardiography demonstrates wall motion improvement in previously infarcted territory compared to acute phase. No new wall motion abnormalities. No ischemia provoked at achieved workload. LV ejection fraction unchanged at 40%.

puzzles.explanation: Cardiac rehabilitation post-myocardial infarction is a structured, supervised program designed to restore functional capacity and reduce recurrent cardiac events in patients recovering from acute coronary syndromes. Following an inferior STEMI, the left ventricle sustains ischemic injury, initially impairing contractility and exercise tolerance — hence the severely limited 2 METs and dyspnea on exertion seen early in recovery. The rehabilitation process leverages progressive exercise training, education, and psychological support to gradually improve cardiac efficiency, reduce anginal symptoms, and rebuild patient confidence; the improving wall motion on repeat stress echocardiography and absence of inducible ischemia indicate myocardial remodeling and functional recovery. This patient's anxiety about activity, while understandable post-event, is itself a modifiable risk factor that supervised rehabilitation directly addresses through graded exposure and monitoring.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old typist reports catching sensation in right index finger with frequent triggering during work. Clicking sensation during finger flexion and extension. Progressive symptoms over 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform sustained gripping and typing activities. Morning stiffness in the right index finger that resolves within 30 minutes with activity. Most limited in data entry work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occupational repetitive strain from data entry work 8 hours daily. Gradual progressive onset. No trauma. Previous history of mild wrist pain from RSI.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nodule palpable at MCP level of index finger. A1 pulley tenderness on palpation. Triggering demonstrated with active finger motion. Mild swelling over pulley.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows flexor tendon nodule and pulley thickening at A1 level index finger. Mild effusion around tendon. No partial tear. Dynamic ultrasound confirms tendon nodule catching.

puzzles.explanation: Trigger finger results from repetitive microtrauma to the flexor tendon sheath, causing a nodule to develop on the flexor tendon that catches as it passes under the thickened A1 pulley during flexion and extension. The catching sensation—sometimes described as a click or lock—occurs because the tendon nodule becomes too large to pass smoothly beneath the pulley, particularly after periods of rest when inflammation has time to accumulate. Occupational repetitive strain from sustained gripping and data entry work, as in this case, is a major risk factor; the morning stiffness and progressive nature reflect the inflammatory cycle of swelling overnight and partial resolution with activity. Ultrasound confirmation of the tendon nodule and pulley thickening, combined with the characteristic triggering on dynamic examination, makes this diagnosis straightforward and differentiates it from other causes of finger clicking or hand pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old cyclist with chronic lateral ankle pain and lower leg aching. Pain present over 4 weeks, worsening with lateral foot-loading activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged cycling and hill training; also reports giving way sensation on uneven ground or lateral stepping movements. Significantly impacting training frequency.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previous inversion ankle sprain 18 months prior that resulted in chronic lateral ankle instability. Cycling training frequency increased 2 months ago. Weak ankle stabilizers noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Peroneal tendon palpable thickening and tenderness behind lateral malleolus. Pain reproduction with resisted foot eversion in plantarflexion. Normal anterior drawer test and talar tilt; ankle ROM preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows peroneal tendon thickening with increased echo texture heterogeneity. Mild subcutaneous fluid. No tendon subluxation. MRI demonstrates tendinopathy without tear.

puzzles.explanation: Peroneal tendinopathy results from chronic overload of the fibularis longus and brevis tendons, which course behind the lateral malleolus to stabilize the ankle and control inversion—critical functions during cycling and uneven terrain navigation. This cyclist's previous inversion sprain and subsequent chronic ankle instability created a biomechanical vulnerability; the weak ankle stabilizers and sudden increase in training volume overwhelmed the tendons' adaptive capacity, leading to the tendon thickening and heterogeneous echo texture seen on ultrasound. The characteristic pain with resisted foot eversion in plantarflexion directly loads these tendons and confirms their involvement, while the normal anterior drawer and preserved range of motion distinguish tendinopathy from acute lateral ligament injury. Unlike ligamentous insufficiency, this condition responds well to load management, eccentric strengthening, and proprioceptive retraining to restore dynamic ankle stability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old mechanic reports sudden onset of weakness in the forearm and hand 3 days ago. Notices difficulty with fine motor tasks and pinching activities. No pain, numbness, or trauma reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform precision gripping tasks required at work (holding small tools, fasteners). Weakness predominantly affecting thumb and index finger — other hand strength appears normal.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset of weakness with overhead reaching activity at work 3 days ago. No trauma but possible nerve compression from muscle hypertrophy or pronator teres tightness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Thumb IP flexion weak/absent. Index finger DIP flexion weak/absent. Grip and wrist flexion strength intact. Sensation normal in median nerve distribution including first web space. Pronation strength normal bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowed motor conduction in anterior interosseous branch of median nerve. EMG demonstrates acute denervation changes in flexor pollicis longus and flexor digitorum profundus.

puzzles.explanation: Anterior interosseous syndrome (AIS) is a pure motor branch neuropathy affecting the anterior interosseous nerve (AIN), a motor branch of the median nerve that typically arises from the median trunk distal to the elbow. It classically presents with sudden-onset weakness of thumb IP flexion (flexor pollicis longus) and index finger DIP flexion (flexor digitorum profundus I), as seen in this mechanic's acute presentation following overhead reaching—a mechanism that can compress the AIN against the pronator teres or lacertus fibrosus. The hallmark feature is preserved sensation and intact pronation strength, distinguishing it from proximal median nerve compression, which would affect pronation and sensation in the thenar eminence. Electrodiagnostic findings of slowed motor conduction in the AIN branch with acute denervation changes in the intrinsic thumb and index flexors confirm the diagnosis and localize the lesion precisely.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old woman presents 3 weeks after a fall from standing height. Minor injuries sustained but patient now demonstrates marked reluctance to bear weight or ambulate without assistance. Mobility has declined significantly.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient refuses to walk unassisted and has become housebound. She reports waking at night with panic and catastrophic thoughts about another fall, despite no residual pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall occurred while reaching for an object on a shelf. Prior to fall, patient was fully independent, lived alone, and walked regularly. Now refuses to leave home; family visits have become infrequent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gait cautious and slower, avoids turning. Functional reach test limited due to fear rather than physical limitation. No demonstrable neurological deficit. Cognitive assessment normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Functional mobility assessment (Timed Up and Go) shows marked slowing primarily during turning phase. DEXA scan normal for age. Further psychological screening (GAD-7, PCL-5) shows elevated anxiety and intrusive thoughts specific to falling.

puzzles.explanation: Post-fall anxiety syndrome is a psychological condition where a fall — even a minor one — triggers persistent fear and avoidance behaviours that become functionally more disabling than the injury itself. The syndrome reflects a maladaptive cycle: initial fear after a fall leads to activity restriction, deconditioning, reduced confidence, and escalating anxiety about falling again, ultimately causing greater functional loss than the original trauma warranted. This 76-year-old's marked reluctance to ambulate despite minimal residual pain, nocturnal panic, and catastrophic cognitions about falling are hallmark features, distinguishing it from simple injury recovery or pain-related mobility loss. The preserved neurological and cognitive function, combined with anxiety-driven limitations on functional reach and turning, confirm that the physical capacity exists but psychological fear constrains it — a crucial distinction that shapes rehabilitation away from purely impairment-focused treatment toward graded exposure and cognitive reframing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old woman develops acute onset severe neck pain after turning to reach something while seated. Neck rigidity and inability to move head. Pain sudden and severe.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to lift head from pillow without assistance. Arm and hand functional (no neurological deficit). Pain radiates across shoulders. Significant functional impairment.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden neck movement during reaching; no preceding trauma or chronic neck complaints. Symptoms began immediately without gradual progression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant cervical muscle spasm and rigidity palpable. Neck rotation severely limited in both directions. Cervical flexion-extension significantly restricted. No neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical X-rays normal; no fracture, subluxation, or significant degenerative change. Findings consistent with acute mechanical neck dysfunction without nerve root involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Acute wry neck (acute torticollis) is a sudden-onset cervical dysfunction characterised by protective muscle spasm and severe restriction of neck movement, typically triggered by an awkward reaching movement or minor rotation — as in this case. The condition results from acute facet joint irritation, disc protrusion, or cervical muscle strain that triggers reflex guarding, creating the classic presentation of inability to move the head and severe bilateral neck stiffness without neurological compromise. Normal imaging rules out fracture or significant structural pathology, confirming this is a mechanical dysfunction rather than a neurological injury. Most cases resolve within days to weeks with appropriate manual therapy, postural correction, and activity modification, though the acute presentation can be profoundly disabling in the first 24–48 hours.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old soccer player reports acute onset severe pain to the lateral leg after direct contact injury during match. Immediate swelling noticed. Pain worsens with weight-bearing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant swelling developed within minutes. Unable to weight-bear or walk normally. Ankle movements painful. Could not return to play.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct blow from another player's knee to lateral leg. Pain and swelling were instantaneous. No prior history of leg or ankle injury. No chronic conditions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant swelling and ecchymosis over lateral leg, mid-fibular region. Fibular head palpable bone tenderness. Pain with ankle plantarflexion and eversion loading. Ankle dorsiflexion intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Leg X-rays show simple transverse fracture of fibula at mid-shaft level. No associated tibia fracture. No proximal fibula injury. No ankle syndesmotic involvement.

puzzles.explanation: A fibula fracture results from a direct blow to the lateral leg, disrupting the smaller weight-bearing bone of the lower leg. While the fibula bears only ~10% of axial load compared to the tibia, isolated mid-shaft fractures like this one typically heal well without syndesmotic compromise, as the intact interosseous membrane preserves ankle stability. The immediate swelling, ecchymosis over the fibular head, and preserved dorsiflexion with pain only on plantarflexion and eversion reflect the localized nature of the injury—the peroneal nerve and anterior compartment remain unaffected, but stress through the ankle mortise reproduces symptoms. In younger athletes with simple transverse patterns and no tibia involvement, conservative management with early mobilization typically achieves good functional recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old male. Onset of leg weakness and heaviness over past 2-3 days. Legs feel tired and difficult to lift. No trauma or fall. No fever currently.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty climbing stairs and rising from seated position. Walking is slow and unsteady. Now requires handrail support. No complaints of pain, numbness, or tingling in legs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mild cough and sore throat 10 days ago, now resolved. Weakness began 3 days after respiratory symptoms cleared. Denies recent vaccinations, tick exposure, or toxin exposure. Previously healthy with no neurological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral leg strength reduced: hip and knee flexion/extension 4/5, ankle movements 3-4/5. Patellar and Achilles reflexes absent bilaterally. Plantar responses normal (downgoing). Light touch and pinprick sensation intact. Cranial nerves intact. Gait wide-based and slow.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowed velocities and prolonged latencies in lower limbs bilaterally, consistent with demyelinating process. CSF analysis reveals elevated protein (80 mg/dL) with normal white cell count (<5). MRI spine normal; no cord compression or signal change.

puzzles.explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome is an acute post-infectious autoimmune demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy, typically triggered by molecular mimicry following viral or bacterial infection — in this case, the preceding respiratory illness. The hallmark features are rapidly progressive ascending motor weakness with areflexia (or hyporeflexia) and relative preservation of sensation, reflecting selective demyelination of motor nerve roots and nerves. The characteristic triad of ascending paralysis, absent reflexes, and albuminocytologic dissociation on CSF (elevated protein with normal cell count) are pathognomonic, and nerve conduction studies showing demyelinating features confirm the diagnosis. Early recognition is critical, as respiratory muscle involvement can develop unpredictably, necessitating careful monitoring and consideration of immunomodulatory therapies such as IVIG or plasmapheresis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old recreational athlete presents with acute onset anterior thigh pain following direct contact during sport. Immediate swelling noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Knee cannot flex beyond 90 degrees. Walking is severely limited. Unable to climb stairs or bear full weight comfortably.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury sustained 2 hours ago. No prior injury to this thigh. No anticoagulant use or bleeding disorder history. Swelling rapidly progressive.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant ecchymosis and swelling over quadriceps region. Knee flexion limited to 75 degrees due to pain and swelling. Quadriceps strength 4/5 due to pain inhibition. Palpable muscle firmness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows muscle hematoma within quadriceps. Muscle fiber disruption visible with heterogeneous echotexture. No fracture seen on adjacent X-ray imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Muscle contusion results from blunt force trauma causing direct injury to muscle fibers and surrounding vasculature, with bleeding into the muscle belly and surrounding fascia creating a hematoma. The quadriceps is particularly vulnerable to contusion in direct contact sports due to its superficial anterior thigh location and the high forces involved in impact. The acute onset swelling, rapid ecchymosis, and heterogeneous ultrasound appearance reflect the inflammatory response and bleeding within the muscle tissue itself — distinguishing contusion from strains, which involve tendinous structures. Knee flexion limitation and pain-inhibited weakness are expected initially due to swelling and muscle guarding rather than true strength loss; recovery depends on hematoma reabsorption and inflammation resolution, typically over weeks to months depending on severity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 41-year-old woman reports progressive shoulder weakness and difficulty with pushing activities over the past 3 weeks. No clear injury event. Pain is minimal; the main complaint is loss of strength in the upper extremity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient struggles with functional tasks requiring arm protraction: closing doors, pushing off furniture, reaching forward against resistance. Wall push-ups and bench press-type movements markedly limited. Overhead lifting with arm raised relatively preserved.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No acute trauma identified. Possible nerve injury from heavy lifting or overhead activity. Gradual progressive onset suggesting nerve inflammation or injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Scapular winging present at rest and exacerbated with arm protraction. Serratus anterior strength 3/5 on manual muscle testing. No sensory deficits in upper extremity. Shoulder flexion and abduction ROM full and pain-free. Trapezius and rhomboid strength intact. No cervical radiculopathy signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography shows denervation of serratus anterior muscle. Motor unit action potentials reduced and reinnervation changes variable. Nerve conduction normal for other upper extremity nerves.

puzzles.explanation: Long thoracic nerve (LTN) palsy results from injury to the nerve as it descends along the chest wall to innervate the serratus anterior muscle, typically from traction, overuse, or inflammatory neuropathy rather than acute trauma. The serratus anterior is responsible for scapular protraction and upward rotation—essential for forward reaching and pushing—so LTN palsy characteristically presents with scapular winging and weakness in protraction activities like wall push-ups and door-closing, while overhead movements remain relatively preserved. The progressive three-week onset and absence of sensory involvement point to a pure motor nerve lesion, and EMG confirmation of denervation in the serratus anterior with preserved function in other upper extremity nerves clinches the diagnosis. Most cases resolve spontaneously over 6–12 months with conservative management, though severe or persistent cases may require longer rehabilitation or surgical exploration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old presents with pain on the outer aspect of the elbow. Onset gradual over several weeks. No history of trauma or fall. Pain is worse with gripping and certain arm movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient struggles with gripping objects, opening jars, and carrying bags. Pain is reproducible with wrist extension against resistance. Symptoms ease with rest and ice. No numbness or tingling in hand.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recreational racquet sports player. Doubled training frequency 8 weeks ago and switched to a stiffer racquet. No prior elbow injuries or constitutional symptoms. Pain is activity-dependent and settles overnight with rest.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness over lateral epicondyle. Pain reproduced with resisted wrist extension and gripping. Full elbow range of motion preserved. No swelling, warmth, or instability. Neurological examination normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickening and heterogeneous echotexture of the extensor carpi radialis brevis tendon at its origin. No fluid collection or bursal effusion. Findings consistent with tendinopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Lateral epicondylitis, commonly called 'tennis elbow,' arises from overload of the extensor carpi radialis brevis tendon at its origin on the lateral epicondyle, typically triggered by repetitive wrist extension or gripping activities. The gradual onset following increased training intensity and equipment change reflects cumulative microtrauma rather than acute injury—the tendon develops areas of disorganized collagen and minor inflammation, evident on ultrasound as heterogeneous thickening. Pain with resisted wrist extension and gripping is pathognomonic because these movements maximally load the injured tendon, while preserved elbow range of motion and normal neurology rule out more serious pathology like nerve compression or structural damage. Most cases resolve within 6–12 weeks with activity modification, eccentric loading, and topical or injected anti-inflammatories, making early recognition and load management crucial.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 13-year-old obese boy presents with groin pain and limping gait. Pain began 2 weeks ago with insidious onset. Hip pain worse with internal rotation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot participate in sports or climb stairs. Limping gait with externally rotated position preference. Pain worsens significantly with weight-bearing over the past 2 weeks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rapid growth during puberty. Obesity (BMI 32 at age 13). No preceding trauma. Pain with physical activity but worsens with continued loading.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip internal rotation severely restricted (near 0°) and painful throughout range. Hip external rotation preserved. Positive Patrick's test reproduction of groin pain. Knee examination and neurovascular status normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hip X-rays (frog-leg view) show widened and irregular epiphyseal plate with femoral head slippage relative to neck. Posterior displacement of capital femoral epiphysis visible. MRI confirms SCFE.

puzzles.explanation: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) occurs when the femoral head displaces posteriorly and medially relative to the femoral neck through the weakened growth plate — a Salter-Harris Type I fracture in adolescents. The condition is most common in boys aged 12–16, particularly those who are obese or experiencing rapid skeletal growth, as increased mechanical stress on a physiologically weaker physis precipitates failure. The characteristic posterior slip explains the severe restriction of hip internal rotation and the externally rotated position preference seen here; weight-bearing on an already-slipped epiphysis progressively worsens displacement, making the acute-on-chronic presentation typical. Early recognition on imaging and prompt surgical stabilization are critical to prevent avascular necrosis of the femoral head and long-term hip degenerative changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old man admitted 3 weeks ago with severe sepsis requiring mechanical ventilation. Now extubated but reports extreme fatigue and inability to perform basic movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot lift legs against gravity or roll in bed. Requires maximal assist to sit upright. No respiratory distress or pain with attempted movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: ARDS from severe sepsis requiring intubation 2 weeks. Prolonged sedation with paralytic agents. Minimal mobilization during acute illness. Nutritional support inadequate.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked generalized weakness in all muscle groups. Hip strength 2/5, knee extensors 1+/5. Hand grip minimal bilaterally. Tone decreased. Reflexes intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows diffuse myopathic pattern without denervation. CK normal. Muscle biopsy demonstrates Type 2 fiber-selective atrophy with minimal inflammatory infiltrate.

puzzles.explanation: ICU-acquired weakness (ICU-AW) is a critical illness myopathy that develops in patients recovering from severe systemic illness, particularly sepsis and ARDS, due to the combined effects of prolonged inflammation, immobilization, sedation, and muscle disuse. The condition manifests as diffuse, generalized weakness affecting all muscle groups symmetrically—as seen in this patient's profound lower limb and grip weakness—with a characteristic pattern of Type 2 fiber atrophy on biopsy and myopathic changes on EMG without denervation. Risk factors including prolonged mechanical ventilation, paralytic agents, inadequate nutrition, and minimal mobilization create a perfect storm for muscle catabolism and sarcopenia. Recovery is slow but possible with early mobilization, nutritional rehabilitation, and progressive resistance training once the acute illness resolves.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 6-week-old female infant. Parents noticed asymmetry in thigh appearance during diaper changes. No reported pain or fever. Detected during routine newborn screening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Asymmetric skin folds noted. Asymmetric hip abduction when hips flexed and externally rotated. No pain noted as infant non-verbal but shows discomfort with maneuvers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Born full-term vaginally, no complications. Female sex, first-born, positive family history of hip dysplasia in paternal grandmother. Breech presentation during labor.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ortolani test produces palpable clunk with hip reduction. Barlow test demonstrates hip instability and redislocation. Asymmetric abduction limited to ~60 degrees bilaterally. Bilateral gluteal fold asymmetry present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hip ultrasound shows dysplastic acetabulum with inadequate coverage. Femoral head position lateralized relative to acetabulum. Graf classification system used for assessment.

puzzles.explanation: Developmental dysplasia of the hip (DDH) occurs when the acetabulum fails to develop adequate depth and coverage of the femoral head, resulting in instability or frank dislocation—most common in females, firstborn infants, and those with breech presentation or positive family history. The condition is often asymptomatic in early infancy, making detection during routine screening critical; the bilateral gluteal fold asymmetry and limited hip abduction seen here are subtle but reliable clinical indicators of dysplasia. The Ortolani and Barlow tests exploit femoral head instability—a positive clunk on reduction and slip on posterior pressure confirm the diagnosis at the bedside. Early identification via ultrasound (using the Graf classification) and prompt treatment with devices like the Pavlik harness can achieve excellent outcomes by allowing the acetabulum to remodel; delayed diagnosis risks chronic pain, early osteoarthritis, and gait dysfunction in childhood.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman reports involuntary urine leakage triggered by physical exertion. Symptoms began approximately 1 year ago and have progressed gradually.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids exercise, impact activities, and public settings due to fear of incontinence episodes. Particularly problematic with jumping, running, and coughing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple vaginal deliveries with instrumentation. Menopause 8 years prior. No prior pelvic floor rehabilitation or pessary use. Denies urgency incontinence or nocturia.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weak pelvic floor muscles on examination. Positive cough stress test. Normal detrusor function on preliminary assessment. Sphincter laxity evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Urodynamics documents urethral sphincter incompetence and bladder neck hypermobility on cough stress test. Detrusor function normal. Leak point pressure reduced at 45 cm H2O.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic floor dysfunction in this case reflects stress urinary incontinence — leakage occurring when abdominal pressure exceeds urethral closure pressure due to weakened sphincter support.
