The condition arises from chronic denervation and muscular atrophy following multiple vaginal deliveries with instrumentation, compounded by the loss of estrogen at menopause, which reduces urethral mucosal coaptation and tissue elasticity. The clinical picture — exertional leakage with coughing, jumping, and running in the absence of urgency — is pathognomonic for intrinsic sphincter deficiency and bladder neck hypermobility; urodynamic documentation of reduced leak point pressure and incompetence during stress maneuvers confirms the diagnosis. Pelvic floor muscle retraining, aimed at improving dynamic support during sudden pressure spikes, forms the cornerstone of conservative management in this population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old man, hospitalized 5 days post-abdominal surgery, develops acute onset dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Hypoxemia present (SpO2 89-90% on room air). Severe dyspnea with minimal exertion; cannot lie flat or mobilize.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Post-operative day 5 from abdominal surgery for small bowel obstruction. Immobility during recovery period. Hypercoagulable post-operative state. No anticoagulation prophylaxis given.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tachycardia 115, tachypnea 28. Unilateral leg swelling and calf tenderness absent. JVD not prominent. Lung auscultation: scattered crackles bilateral bases. Lower limb DVT signs absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT pulmonary angiography shows filling defect in right lower lobar pulmonary artery confirming embolus. Bilateral subsegmental emboli visible. Echocardiography shows right ventricular strain.

puzzles.explanation: Pulmonary embolism occurs when a thrombus, typically originating from deep veins in the lower limbs, lodges in the pulmonary arterial circulation and obstructs blood flow to the lungs. Post-operative patients face dramatically elevated risk due to immobility, surgical trauma, and activation of the coagulation cascade—a hypercoagulable state that persists for weeks after surgery. The acute onset dyspnea, hypoxemia, and pleuritic chest pain reflect sudden right heart strain and ventilation-perfusion mismatch, while the absence of obvious leg swelling (as in this case) underscores that DVT can be clinically silent yet still embolic. CT pulmonary angiography with RV strain on echo provides definitive diagnosis and prognostic insight into the severity of the hemodynamic compromise.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old man reports progressive weakness and slowing down over the past year. Has lost weight unintentionally.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to climb stairs without stopping. Walking distance reduced to approximately 50 metres before fatigue forces rest. Lives independently but leaving home has become difficult.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No recent illness or injury to explain onset. Lives alone with minimal social contact. Hypertension and type 2 diabetes managed with medications. No recent hospitalisation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip extension strength 3+/5 bilaterally. Gait velocity 0.6 m/s, unsteady with reduced stride length. Current weight 10% below his usual baseline. Hand grip strength bilaterally reduced (unable to quantify precise values with standard dynamometry).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serum albumin 3.2 g/dL (low). Haemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (mild anaemia). Serum B12 and folate normal. TSH normal. No acute infection or metabolic derangement on basic metabolic panel.

puzzles.explanation: Frailty syndrome is a geriatric condition characterised by loss of physiological reserve across multiple systems — manifesting as progressive weakness, weight loss, reduced physical activity tolerance, and increased vulnerability to stressors — rather than a single disease process. It typically emerges from a combination of sarcopenia, micronutrient insufficiency, chronic low-grade inflammation, and social isolation, as reflected in this patient's unintentional weight loss, reduced grip and hip extension strength, and slow gait velocity below the frailty threshold of 0.6 m/s. The absence of acute illness, normal metabolic screening, and low-normal serum albumin point toward cumulative nutritional decline and disuse rather than reversible acute pathology. Recognition of frailty phenotype is crucial because these patients have substantially elevated risk of falls, hospitalisation, and functional decline with minimal physiological perturbation — making preventive strength training, nutritional optimisation, and social engagement core rehabilitation priorities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old assembly line worker reports radial-sided wrist pain that began gradually 2 months ago. Pain worse with gripping and pinching activities. No acute injury recalled.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Swelling visible over the radial wrist. Cannot perform repetitive gripping work. Fine pinching tasks are severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Repetitive assembly line work for 20 years with poor workstation ergonomics. Symptoms progressed insidiously over 2 months without triggering incident. No prior similar episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Finkelstein test positive reproducing pain. Tenderness at radial styloid process. Thumb abduction strength reduced secondary to pain. First dorsal compartment swelling palpable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows APL and EPB tendon thickening with synovial effusion. First dorsal compartment tendinitis evident. No tendon subluxation. Mild surrounding edema.

puzzles.explanation: De Quervain's tenosynovitis is an inflammatory condition affecting the abductor pollicis longus (APL) and extensor pollicis brevis (EPB) tendons within the first dorsal compartment of the wrist, typically triggered by repetitive thumb abduction and wrist radial deviation under load. The condition results from cumulative microtrauma and subsequent synovial inflammation rather than acute injury, making it especially common in occupations requiring sustained gripping and pinching — as exemplified by this worker's two decades of assembly line exposure. The radial-sided pain, positive Finkelstein test (which compresses the inflamed compartment), and ultrasound findings of tendon thickening with effusion are pathognomonic for this diagnosis. Early recognition and activity modification, combined with appropriate rehabilitation targeting ergonomic correction, can prevent progression to chronic tenosynovitis or tendon rupture.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old manual laborer reports acute onset lower back pain with radiation into one leg. Numbness and weakness have developed over the past week, affecting ability to work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with forward bending and prolonged sitting. Relief occurs with lying down and leg elevation. Weakness specifically affects ankle dorsiflexion and great toe extension.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset followed a heavy lifting episode with acute low back strain. Chronic degenerative disc disease predisposes to nerve root compression. Progressive neurological symptoms indicate ongoing nerve compression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Straight leg raise reproduces ipsilateral leg pain at 45 degrees. L5 dermatomal sensory loss (dorsum of foot) present. Hip abduction and ankle dorsiflexion weakness (grade 4/5). Ankle reflex normal; knee reflex intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows L5 disc herniation with compression of the right L5 nerve root in the lateral recess. Signal changes in the L5 myotome on fat-suppressed sequences confirm active nerve compression.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar radiculopathy results from mechanical compression of a nerve root as it exits the spinal canal, most commonly from disc herniation or lateral recess stenosis. The L5 nerve root distribution — dorsum of foot sensation, ankle dorsiflexion and great toe extension strength — explains the specific neurological deficits seen in this case, where acute disc herniation following heavy lifting compressed the nerve root in the lateral recess. The positional pattern is characteristic: forward bending and sitting increase intradiscal pressure and nerve root tension, while recumbency and leg elevation relieve both, as does reduction of neural mechanosensitivity over time. Straight leg raise at 45 degrees reproducing ipsilateral leg pain reflects the mechanical irritation and nerve root tension, distinguishing this from referred somatic pain, and combined with dermatomal sensory loss and myotome-specific weakness, confirms active nerve root compression rather than referred symptoms alone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old man presents with progressive loss of muscle mass and generalized weakness over 2 years. Difficulty with stairs and arising from chair. Increased fall risk and loss of independence.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant difficulty rising from low chairs without armrests, climbing stairs, and carrying groceries. Walks slowly indoors. Requires rail support for safety.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sedentary lifestyle due to knee osteoarthritis. Retirement and reduced physical activity. Underlying chronic inflammatory conditions (mild COPD). Progressive age-related changes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized muscle atrophy visible in thighs and upper arms. Leg strength testing shows hip flexors 3+/5, knee extensors 3+/5. Slow gait velocity. Poor sit-stand performance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry shows low appendicular lean mass index (ALM/height² = 5.8 kg/m²), meeting sarcopenia criteria. Serum biomarkers show elevated creatinine-to-cystatin C ratio, reflecting reduced muscle mass.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related sarcopenia is the progressive loss of skeletal muscle mass and strength that occurs with advancing age, driven by reduced physical activity, anabolic resistance (decreased response to protein and exercise stimuli), chronic inflammation, and hormonal decline. The condition becomes clinically significant when it impairs functional mobility — as seen here with difficulty rising from chairs and climbing stairs — and substantially increases fall risk and loss of independence. The combination of generalized muscle atrophy, weakness in antigravity positions (hip flexors and knee extensors particularly vulnerable), and reduced appendicular lean mass on DEXA meeting diagnostic thresholds confirms the diagnosis. Sarcopenia is distinct from simple deconditioning in that it reflects structural and metabolic muscle loss; the sedentary lifestyle and chronic inflammatory state here have accelerated an otherwise age-dependent process, making early intervention through resistance exercise and protein optimization critical to reversing functional decline.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 35-year-old male with acute onset medial thigh pain during hockey game. Unable to continue playing. Pain began suddenly with no prior groin problems.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform hip adduction maneuvers. Pain significantly worse with skating and rapid direction changes. Antalgic gait limiting walking distance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previous groin strains treated conservatively 2 years ago, fully resolved. Current injury occurred mid-stride during rapid deceleration. No neurological symptoms, no night pain, no radiation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Adductor tenderness at insertion. Pain with resisted hip adduction testing. Muscle guarding present. Swelling developing at acute phase. Antalgic gait evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows adductor muscle fiber disruption with hematoma formation. Fluid collection at injury site. Partial muscle tear confirmed. No complete rupture.

puzzles.explanation: Groin strain is an acute muscular injury to the adductors—most commonly the adductor longus—typically occurring during rapid acceleration, deceleration, or dynamic hip abduction maneuvers like those demanded in hockey. The mechanism here, sudden mid-stride deceleration, creates eccentric loading that the muscle cannot control, leading to partial fiber disruption and the characteristic hematoma and swelling visible on ultrasound. Unlike referred pain or nerve compression, groin strain presents with well-localized tenderness at the muscle insertion and pain specifically on resisted adduction—the functional plane of injury—making the diagnosis clinically straightforward. Previous episodes of the same injury, as in this player, are common due to scar tissue formation and proprioceptive deficits, underscoring the importance of comprehensive rehabilitation beyond pain resolution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 26-year-old female distance runner. Gradual onset of lower leg pain over 4 weeks. Pain is localized to inner aspect of shin. No history of trauma or acute injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with running, particularly at higher intensities and longer distances. Symptoms ease with rest days and reduced volume. Sharp ache felt along a longitudinal line on the medial shin during activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain began shortly after increasing weekly training mileage from 40 to 80 km. Previous similar episode 18 months ago, also following rapid volume escalation. Both episodes responded well to load reduction and graduated return to running. No systemic disease or metabolic abnormality.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness elicited along medial tibial border spanning middle and distal thirds of tibia. Pain reproduced with resisted plantarflexion and inversion. No swelling, erythema, or skin changes. Calf strength intact. Single-leg hop test limited by pain but no instability noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of tibia normal; no fracture line or cortical disruption. Bone scintigraphy demonstrates focal linear uptake along medial tibial surface consistent with periosteal stress response. No stress fracture on subsequent CT imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Medial tibial stress syndrome (MTSS) is a chronic periosteal inflammation of the medial tibia resulting from repetitive traction and microtrauma to the tibialis posterior attachment and surrounding fascia, common in distance runners subjected to rapid training volume increases. The condition is distinguished from stress fractures by intact cortical bone on imaging despite positive bone scintigraphy showing periosteal remodeling — a hallmark finding reflecting ongoing stress response without structural failure. The medial shin pain along the tibial border, reproducibility with plantarflexion and inversion loading, and recurrence after volume escalation are classic presentations; the bilateral periosteal involvement and history of recovery with load reduction and gradual progression indicate a training error-driven overuse injury rather than a structural fracture or systemic process. Conservative management with relative rest, controlled return to running, and attention to training loads typically resolves symptoms within 6-12 weeks.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old with acute onset severe pain in the lower leg following a fall. Immediate inability to bear weight and rapid swelling. No prior leg injury history.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Visible angular deformity of the lower leg. Gross swelling and bruising noted. Unable to tolerate any weight or passive movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mechanism occurred 6 hours ago. No relevant past medical history. No medication use. No prior lower limb injuries. Impact to anterior tibia.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible deformity of lower leg with angulation at fracture site. Significant swelling and ecchymosis. Pain with palpation over fractured area. Distal neurovascular status intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Leg X-rays show transverse fracture of mid-tibial shaft with displacement. Fibula fracture also present (associated injury). No vascular compromise. Open fracture assessment performed.

puzzles.explanation: A tibial shaft fracture results from high-energy trauma to the anterior tibia, causing a break in the main weight-bearing bone of the lower leg. The mechanism of direct impact, combined with the visible angular deformity and inability to bear weight, is classic for a displaced fracture — the tibia's poor soft tissue coverage means swelling and bruising develop rapidly as the periosteum and surrounding muscles are traumatized. Associated fibular fracture is common and often stabilizes the injury pattern. The key clinical distinction is the acute presentation with gross deformity and functional loss; careful neurovascular assessment, as performed here, is essential to rule out compartment syndrome or vascular injury, which represent true emergencies requiring urgent intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 14-year-old male basketball player presents with acute-onset knee pain below the kneecap. Pain started 2 weeks ago during jump training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is activity-dependent: reliably triggered by jumping and explosive movements during basketball. Pain-free with walking. Cannot tolerate prolonged kneeling or stairs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Adolescent growth spurt with rapid skeletal maturation. Intensive basketball training with frequent jumping. No prior knee pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness localised to inferior patellar pole on direct palpation. Pain reproduced with resisted knee extension and single-leg hop. No knee effusion. Full passive ROM. Normal patellar tracking.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Knee X-rays show small ossification center irregularity at patellar ligament insertion. MRI demonstrates inflammation at patellar ligament enthesis. Growth plate changes evident.

puzzles.explanation: Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease is an overuse traction apophysitis at the inferior pole of the patella, where the patellar ligament inserts onto a secondary ossification center in skeletally immature athletes. During growth spurts, this apophysis is vulnerable to repetitive tensile loading from explosive activities like jumping, causing inflammation and microtrauma before the growth plate fully ossifies. The classic presentation—adolescent athlete, acute onset during intensive training, tenderness at the lower patellar pole, and pain reproduced by knee extension and jumping—reflects the biomechanical overload at a site of incomplete skeletal maturation. Imaging findings of ossification irregularity and entheseal inflammation confirm the diagnosis, and importantly, the condition is self-limiting as physeal closure occurs, making load management and activity modification the primary interventions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old woman presents with diffuse anterior knee pain with functional limitation. Pain with squatting, climbing stairs, and prolonged sitting. Gradual onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform single-leg stance or hop. Knee gives way with changes in direction. Pain worse with pivoting movements and prolonged standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sedentary occupation with prolonged sitting. Recent home exercise program initiation. Possible hip weakness and flexibility issues contributing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Clarks test reproduces anterior knee pain. Vastus medialis strength reduced. Patellar tracking questionable. Pain with single-leg squat. Tight hip flexors and IT band.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows patellofemoral cartilage intact without focal lesion. Q-angle and patellar tilt measured within normal limits. No structural pathology. Diagnosis supported by clinical presentation and dynamic movement assessment.

puzzles.explanation: Patellofemoral pain syndrome is a multifactorial condition arising from altered patellar tracking and loading, typically due to muscular imbalances, particularly vastus medialis obliquus weakness, combined with hip and ankle mobility restrictions. The syndrome classically presents with diffuse anterior knee pain aggravated by squatting, stair climbing, and prolonged sitting — activities that increase patellofemoral joint reaction forces — and is often preceded by periods of inactivity or sudden increases in loading. The presentation here reflects the biomechanical hallmark: proximal hip weakness and flexibility deficits leading to dynamic knee valgus and patellar malalignment during functional tasks like single-leg squatting and directional changes. Notably, imaging is often unremarkable, making clinical examination findings — weakened vastus medialis, positive Clarke's test, and movement dysfunction — essential for diagnosis and distinguishing PFPS from structural pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old woman reports dizziness, palpitations, and fatigue that worsen significantly when standing or exercising. Symptoms limit daily activities and exercise tolerance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate prolonged upright posture (meetings, shopping). Symptoms improve when supine or sitting. Previously active but now mostly sedentary.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began abruptly 3 months after viral illness. No prior cardiovascular or autonomic symptoms. Lives in warm climate. Family history of syncope.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Supine HR 68, BP 110/70. Standing 2 minutes: HR 118, BP 108/68. No presyncope relief with squatting. Normal cardiac auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Tilt table test: HR rises from 68 to 102 bpm within 3 minutes upright, sustained throughout 10-minute test. Systolic BP drops <10 mmHg. Plasma norepinephrine abnormally elevated supine (>600 pg/mL). Echocardiogram and Holter monitor normal.

puzzles.explanation: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) is a form of autonomic dysfunction characterized by excessive heart rate elevation (≥30 bpm increase within 3 minutes of standing) without significant orthostatic hypotension, resulting from impaired sympathetic and parasympathetic regulation. The condition disproportionately affects young women and frequently emerges after viral illnesses due to autoimmune or infectious triggers affecting autonomic innervation. The markedly elevated supine norepinephrine levels here reflect compensatory hyperadrenergic activity—a hallmark that distinguishes POTS from other causes of orthostatic intolerance—explaining both the palpitations and the dramatic exercise intolerance despite normal resting hemodynamics. The abrupt post-viral onset, inability to sustain upright posture, and sustained tachycardia on tilt testing with preserved blood pressure regulation are classic diagnostic features.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old athlete with recurrent episodes of lateral ankle instability and a catching or popping sensation with activity over the past 8 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate cutting and pivoting movements in sport. Pain and instability worse with rapid ankle plantarflexion and inversion, such as during change of direction drills.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury followed an inversion ankle sprain 8 months ago. At the time of the sprain, imaging suggested peroneal tendon involvement. Episodes of instability have been intermittent but recurring with specific movements.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable subluxation of peroneal tendons posterior to lateral malleolus with ankle position changes. Positive peroneal tendon subluxation test. Pain with resisted eversion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound and dynamic MRI show peroneal tendons subluxing posterior to lateral malleolus with ankle plantarflexion and inversion. Superior peroneal retinaculum laxity noted.

puzzles.explanation: Peroneal tendon subluxation occurs when the peroneal longus and brevis tendons slip out of their groove behind the lateral malleolus, typically due to laxity or insufficiency of the superior peroneal retinaculum that normally restrains them. This injury commonly follows ankle inversion sprains that disrupt the retinaculum, as seen in this athlete, leaving the tendons vulnerable to displacing with combined plantarflexion and inversion movements. The catching or popping sensation with cutting and pivoting activities is pathognomonic, reflecting the tendons repeatedly subluxing and reducing with dynamic ankle motion. Dynamic imaging demonstrating posterior migration of the tendons during plantarflexion-inversion, combined with palpable subluxation on examination, confirms the diagnosis and distinguishes this from other causes of lateral ankle instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old with long-standing smoking history. Reports gradual onset shortness of breath that has worsened over several years. Persistent cough, mostly dry. Denies acute trauma or recent illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with stairs and walking distances that used to be manageable. Symptoms worse with exertion, better with rest. Unable to keep up with daily household tasks. No relief from position changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 40+ year smoking history. Symptoms began insidiously 5-10 years ago, progressive decline. Multiple exacerbations requiring treatment over past 2-3 years. Tried inhalers with minimal sustained benefit. No acute precipitant for current decline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced breath sounds throughout lung fields. Prolonged expiratory phase noted. Use of neck and shoulder muscles during breathing. Barrel-shaped chest. Pursed-lip breathing pattern observed. SpO2 drops with minimal activity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spirometry: FEV1/FVC ratio <70%, FEV1 significantly reduced (severity varies). Chest X-ray: hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms, increased anteroposterior diameter. ABG: hypoxemia at baseline, elevated CO2 in advanced cases.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease results from progressive, largely irreversible airflow obstruction caused by chronic inflammation and parenchymal destruction — most commonly from long-term smoking. The hallmark pathophysiology involves loss of elastic recoil, small airway collapse during expiration, and air trapping, leading to the characteristic hyperinflation, barrel chest, and prolonged expiratory phase seen on examination. The gradual onset dyspnoea worsening over years, coupled with reduced FEV1/FVC ratio on spirometry and imaging findings of flattened diaphragms, reflects the cumulative damage typical of COPD's slow progression. Unlike acute conditions, COPD presents with exertional limitation and minimal response to single-dose bronchodilators, distinguishing it from reversible airway diseases and making pulmonary rehabilitation and smoking cessation the cornerstones of management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with involuntary loss of urine during specific activities. Onset gradual over several years with progressive functional limitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot exercise or engage in high-impact activities. Leakage occurs predictably with coughing, sneezing, heavy lifting, and jumping. Avoids social situations due to unpredictable episodes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple vaginal deliveries. No history of neurological or spinal cord injury. Symptoms correlate with physical exertion, not urgency. Improves when lying down.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weak pelvic floor on assessment. Stress test positive. Bladder neck hypermobile. No urgency incontinence component.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Urodynamics confirms stress incontinence. Sphincter incompetence documented. Normal detrusor function without overactivity.

puzzles.explanation: Stress urinary incontinence results from sphincteric insufficiency and hypermobility of the bladder neck, allowing urine leakage during increases in intra-abdominal pressure — typically from coughing, sneezing, jumping, or heavy lifting. It is the most common form of incontinence and predominantly affects women due to pelvic floor trauma from vaginal delivery, chronic straining, or estrogen depletion with age. Unlike urgency incontinence, there is no detrusor overactivity; the mechanism is purely mechanical — the pelvic floor muscles and urethral support have weakened, unable to maintain continence against sudden pressure spikes. Positional improvement when supine and the predictable relationship to physical exertion, rather than urgency, are hallmarks that distinguish this from other incontinence types and make conservative pelvic floor muscle training the first-line intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 19-year-old presents 2 weeks post-incident with persistent headaches, difficulty concentrating, and feeling 'off balance'. Reports memory lapses for recent events.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cognitive difficulties including memory problems and concentration issues. Headaches daily with light and sound sensitivity. Dizziness and balance difficulty. Functional limitation with school work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Witnessed loss of consciousness at scene lasting several hours. Post-traumatic amnesia extends beyond the period of unconsciousness. ICU monitoring initiated due to concern for intracranial injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild cognitive deficits noted on basic testing. Photophobia and phonophobia present. Balance testing shows mild deficit. Headache reproducible with movement. Mood irritability noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Initial CT brain showed small epidural hematoma (managed conservatively). Follow-up CT normal. MRI shows diffuse axonal injury. Neuropsych testing shows mild cognitive deficits.

puzzles.explanation: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) results from mechanical trauma causing diffuse and/or focal damage to neural tissue, with severity ranging from mild concussion to severe diffuse axonal injury. This patient's presentation is characteristic of moderate TBI: loss of consciousness lasting hours, post-traumatic amnesia extending beyond the period of unconsciousness, and imaging confirmation of both intracranial bleeding (epidural hematoma) and diffuse axonal injury. The persistent post-concussive syndrome—photophobia, phonophobia, balance deficits, cognitive impairment, and irritability two weeks post-injury—reflects ongoing neuroinflammation and disrupted axonal function despite resolution of acute bleeding. Recovery trajectories vary widely; while some symptoms resolve within weeks, cognitive deficits and vestibular dysfunction often require structured rehabilitation to restore functional capacity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man, 4 weeks post-rotator cuff repair, begins physiotherapy. Arm immobilized with sling. Pain controlled on medication.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe restriction in arm use. Cannot elevate arm overhead or use arm for functional tasks. Pain increases sharply beyond approximately 70 degrees of flexion or 30 degrees of external rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute shoulder injury from fall. Imaging confirmed full-thickness rotator cuff tear. Recent surgical repair with protected postoperative protocol. Currently in early mobilization phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive ROM: abduction 65°, external rotation 30°, flexion 70°. Significant scapulohumeral rhythm guarding and pain-inhibited movement. No neurovascular deficits. Pain 3/10 at rest, 5/10 with passive ROM attempts.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows successful tendon repair with intact suture fixation. Minimal retraction. Post-operative edema present. No re-tear on imaging. Surgical integrity maintained.

puzzles.explanation: Post-rotator cuff repair represents the early mobilization phase following surgical reattachment of a torn rotator cuff, where the primary challenge is balancing tissue healing with prevention of stiffness. At 4 weeks post-op, the repair site is in early collagen deposition but remains mechanically fragile, necessitating strict ROM limits and pain-controlled progression—typically capped around 70° of flexion and 30° of external rotation to avoid excessive tensioning of the healing tendon. The guarding and scapulohumeral rhythm disruption reflect both pain inhibition and neuromuscular deconditioning after immobilization. Successful progression depends on gradual, symptom-limited strengthening as imaging confirms repair integrity, with the goal of restoring functional ROM and rotator cuff strength over 3–6 months while minimizing re-tear risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete reports sudden-onset knee pain and instability during a pivot-and-plant movement. The knee 'gave way' during activity and has not been the same since.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform directional changes or cutting movements without fear of knee buckling. Weight-bearing in slight knee flexion with trunk rotation reproduces apprehension.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient is a contact sport athlete with a family history of knee instability. This is the first episode of acute instability; no prior dislocations or subluxations reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive apprehension test indicates fear of redislocation. Medial restraint testing reveals ligamentous insufficiency. Quadriceps strength is diminished. Swelling and joint effusion are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate a patella that is reduced in anatomical position with possible osteochondral findings. MRI shows acute ligamentous disruption of the medial patellofemoral ligament. Imaging confirms acute patellar dislocation.

puzzles.explanation: Patellar dislocation occurs when the patella displaces laterally out of the trochlear groove, most commonly from a valgus knee flexion mechanism combined with external tibial rotation—exactly the pivot-and-plant movement pattern seen here. The medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL), the primary medial restraint to lateral patellar translation, ruptures acutely, leaving the patella vulnerable to redislocation with any cutting or rotational load. Young athletes with anatomical risk factors such as increased Q-angle, patella alta, or generalized ligamentous laxity (reflected in the family history) are particularly susceptible to first-time dislocation. The positive apprehension test captures the patient's legitimate fear of recurrence—a defining feature that distinguishes this acute instability from other causes of knee pain, and successful rehabilitation requires both quadriceps strengthening and MPFL reconstruction to restore medial stability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman living independently presents to the emergency department following a fall from standing height at home, reporting severe right hip and groin pain with inability to weight-bear.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She was unable to stand or take any steps after the fall and required an ambulance. Prior to the fall she was ambulatory with a walking stick indoors, and her mobility had been declining over the preceding months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of osteoporosis with declining bone density despite supplementation. Two previous falls in the last year without fracture, but this fall occurred from standing height only. On warfarin for atrial fibrillation, creating perioperative management complexity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The right lower limb is shortened and externally rotated at rest. There is marked tenderness over the greater trochanteric and proximal thigh region. Any attempt at active hip movement produces severe pain, and a positive log-roll test is noted with axial loading.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic AP X-ray demonstrates a fracture line below the femoral neck in the region between the greater and lesser trochanters. The fracture is classified as an unstable 4-part intertrochanteric pattern, and orthopaedic referral for dynamic hip screw fixation is arranged.

puzzles.explanation: An intertrochanteric fracture occurs between the greater and lesser trochanters, a region of metaphyseal bone with high vascularity but significant mechanical stress. This fracture pattern is particularly common in elderly patients with osteoporosis who sustain low-energy trauma, as the weakened bone fails under the shear forces created by the hip musculature and body weight. The characteristic presentation of shortening and external rotation of the limb at rest reflects the pull of the hip abductors and external rotators acting unopposed across the fracture site. Unstable 4-part patterns like this one require surgical fixation—typically with a dynamic hip screw—to restore load-bearing alignment and allow early mobilisation, which is critical in this age group to prevent deconditioning and thromboembolic complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old man reports persistent morning stiffness and aching in the shoulders and hips over the past 6 months. Symptoms gradually worsened before seeking care.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to overhead reach or comb hair due to shoulder pain and stiffness. Hip stiffness limits rising from chair and walking distance. Symptoms worst in early morning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent-onset bilateral shoulder and hip pain in a patient with elevated inflammatory markers. Minimal prior joint disease history. Rapid symptom progression over 2–3 months before presentation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral shoulder abduction and external rotation limited to 120° and 45° respectively. Hip flexion and internal rotation restricted. Proximal strength 4+/5 but pain-limited. No focal joint swelling or warmth. Grip strength normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR 52 mm/hr, CRP elevated at 8.2 mg/dL. No evidence of temporal artery tenderness or jaw claudication. Temporal artery ultrasound normal, excluding concurrent giant cell arteritis.

puzzles.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica is a systemic inflammatory condition affecting older adults (typically >50 years) that causes proximal muscle pain and stiffness without true myositis or primary muscle weakness. The hallmark presentation—bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain, marked morning stiffness lasting hours, and elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP)—distinguishes it from isolated rotator cuff pathology or osteoarthritis, though functional deficits like inability to reach overhead or rise from a chair reflect pain and stiffness rather than true myopathic weakness. The relatively preserved grip strength and absence of distal symptoms further support a systemic inflammatory pattern rather than muscular or neurological disease. Prompt recognition is critical because polymyalgia rheumatica frequently co-occurs with giant cell arteritis (approximately 15–20% of cases), necessitating temporal artery assessment, though this patient's normal ultrasound and absent jaw claudication help exclude that serious vascular complication.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old recreational runner with a 3-month history of posterior ankle pain that worsens during training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is worse with downhill running and explosive plantarflexion activities. Activities requiring repetitive push-off are increasingly restricted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual progressive symptoms with running. No specific traumatic event. Previous ankle sprain history. Tried relative rest with minimal improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Posterior ankle tenderness with palpation. Pain with passive plantarflexion and inversion positioning. Flexor hallucis longus palpable and tender. Pain reproduced with plantarflexion resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates posterior ankle impingement with flexor hallucis longus involvement. Soft tissue inflammation in posterior compartment. No bony pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior ankle impingement occurs when soft tissues in the posterior ankle—particularly the flexor hallucis longus tendon, posterior joint capsule, or interarticular soft tissue—become compressed during plantarflexion, limiting the space in an already narrow compartment. The condition develops insidiously through repetitive plantarflexion loading (like push-off in running, particularly downhill where plantarflexion is exaggerated) that irritates and inflames these structures, rather than from a single traumatic event. As seen in this runner, the flexor hallucis longus bears the brunt, becoming tender and restricted as the repetitive microtrauma accumulates over weeks to months. Diagnosis hinges on the characteristic posterior tenderness, pain with combined plantarflexion-inversion positioning that replicates the impingement mechanism, and MRI confirmation of soft tissue inflammation without bony spurs—distinguishing it from os trigonum syndrome or osteophytic changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old woman presents with progressive swelling of the right arm and hand over the past 2 years. Gradual onset, no acute trauma. Reports heaviness and difficulty with fitting of rings and bracelets.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to wear normal-fitting sleeves. Describes affected arm as persistently heavy. Increased difficulty with overhead activities and fine motor tasks of the hand.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Right breast cancer with axillary dissection 3 years prior. No recurrent cancer. Gradual progressive swelling over 2-year period.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right arm swelling with pitting edema initially, now non-pitting and indurated. Circumference difference 4 cm at midarm. Skin thickened and fibrosed. Reduced shoulder ROM secondary to edema.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates impaired lymphatic drainage in right upper extremity. Ultrasound shows increased soft tissue thickness in affected arm. Diameter discrepancy documented.

puzzles.explanation: Lymphoedema is chronic swelling resulting from impaired lymphatic drainage, most commonly occurring after lymph node removal or radiotherapy for cancer treatment. In this case, the axillary dissection for breast cancer disrupted the lymphatic vessels and removed nodal tissue, reducing the limb's capacity to manage fluid filtration and leading to the progressive, painless swelling characteristic of secondary lymphoedema. The transition from pitting to non-pitting, indurated skin reflects the chronic inflammatory phase where persistent fluid accumulation triggers fibrosis and tissue remodelling, explaining the heaviness, functional loss, and thickened appearance. Early recognition and conservative management with compression, elevation, and lymphatic drainage techniques are critical to prevent further tissue changes and functional decline.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old competitive gymnast reports wrist pain with apparatus training. Pain worsens with hand weight-bearing and wrist extension.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform landings or apparatus dismounts. Grip-based training also provokes pain. Symptoms worse after high-volume training days.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 4 weeks ago during intensive off-season conditioning. No acute injury event. Increased training volume preceding onset. No prior wrist injury or treatment.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pinpoint tenderness over distal radial physeal region. Wrist ROM full in flexion/extension and radial/ulnar deviation. No significant effusion. Grip strength intact. Pain reproduced with loaded wrist extension but not isolated passive motion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Wrist X-rays show widened distal radial growth plate with possible irregularity. MRI shows inflammatory changes at growth plate. Physis intact without displacement.

puzzles.explanation: Growth plate injuries result from repetitive microtrauma or excessive loading across the physeal region during skeletal immaturity, when the growth plate is structurally weaker than surrounding ligaments and bone. In young athletes like this gymnast, high-volume weight-bearing activities—particularly those combining axial loading with wrist extension—create shear and compressive forces across the distal radial physis that exceed its capacity to remodel. The four-week insidious onset without an acute event, combined with pinpoint physeal tenderness and pain only under loaded extension, are hallmark features distinguishing growth plate irritation from ligamentous or capsular pathology. Imaging showing widening and inflammatory changes at the physis confirms the diagnosis; importantly, the physis remains intact, indicating this is a strain rather than a Salter-Harris fracture, and typically responds well to modified loading and relative rest.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old woman in nursing home. One week post-operatively from hip surgery. Staff noticed localized area of skin discoloration over sacrum. Patient reports the area feels tender but denies significant pain. No open wounds or drainage noted by patient.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Bedbound and immobilized during post-operative recovery. Unable to reposition independently. Relies entirely on staff for turning and positioning changes. Spends prolonged periods in supine and semi-recumbent positions. Mobility expected to remain limited for several more weeks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hip fracture with surgical repair 7 days ago. No prior skin breakdown or tissue damage documented. Patient compliant with turning schedule when staff available. Nutritional intake adequate. No diabetes, vascular disease, or other comorbidities affecting skin integrity. Incontinence: occasional, managed with pads.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized area of persistent redness over sacral prominence. Redness does NOT blanch (lighten) when skin is pressed with fingertip. Skin surface completely intact—no blisters, cracks, or open areas. Underlying tissue feels firm and slightly swollen. Skin temperature normal. Sensation preserved in area. No signs of infection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bedside assessment with light blanching test performed. Prolonged capillary refill time in affected area when pressure applied and released. No ultrasound or advanced imaging needed. Clinical findings consistent with early tissue response to sustained mechanical pressure without yet breaching the skin barrier.

puzzles.explanation: A Stage 1 pressure injury represents the earliest clinical manifestation of tissue damage from sustained mechanical pressure, occurring when capillary blood flow is compromised but the skin remains intact. The hallmark finding is non-blanching erythema—redness that persists when finger pressure is applied—reflecting inflammatory response and microvascular occlusion beneath the skin surface. Bedbound post-operative patients like this woman are at particular risk due to immobility, prolonged positioning over bony prominences like the sacrum, and inability to relieve pressure independently. Early recognition and intervention at this stage, before dermal breakdown occurs, is critical for preventing progression to deeper tissue involvement and making Stage 1 injuries potentially reversible through offloading, repositioning, and skin care measures.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete experienced acute knee pain and instability during a twisting pivot-and-plant maneuver in sport. The knee gave way suddenly, and they felt something shift in the knee before it felt 'normal' again moments later.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform cutting or pivoting movements in sport. Apprehension and fear of the knee giving way limit weight-bearing during lateral movements and weight shifts. Patient reports needing to modify or avoid multi-directional agility tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute patellar dislocation occurred during athletic activity with a pivot-and-plant mechanism. Underlying anatomical predisposition such as increased Q-angle or ligamentous laxity may be present. This is the initial episode of dislocation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Apprehension test reproduces fear of redislocation. Lachman and anterior drawer tests are normal, ruling out ACL injury. Patellar tracking is abnormal with lateral glide preference. Moderate joint effusion present. VMO strength noticeably diminished compared to VL.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals acute tear of the medial patellofemoral ligament with bone marrow edema. Small osteochondral fragment visible on lateral femoral condyle. No evidence of other knee ligament injuries or meniscal pathology. Patella is concentrically reduced.

puzzles.explanation: Patellar dislocation occurs when the patella subluxates or fully dislocates laterally out of the trochlear groove, typically during a pivot-and-plant mechanism that combines knee flexion with external rotation and valgus stress. The underlying biomechanical factors—such as increased Q-angle, VMO weakness, or generalized ligamentous laxity—predispose young athletes to this injury, which commonly damages the medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL), the primary dynamic stabilizer of the patella. The acute presentation with sudden giving way, apprehension on reproduction testing, abnormal lateral patellar tracking, and associated effusion and osteochondral injury are hallmark features that distinguish this from other causes of knee instability. Recovery requires addressing both the soft tissue injury and the underlying structural or neuromuscular deficits—particularly VMO activation and hip/knee control—to prevent recurrent episodes, which occur in roughly 15-25% of first-time dislocations without adequate rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old female ballet dancer presents with a clicking sensation around the hip that started 12 months ago. Initially painless, it has recently become painful with activity and is affecting her training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The snap occurs reproducibly during hip flexion-extension and lateral movement patterns typical of her dance routine, with sharp brief pain that worsens with rehearsal intensity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She has no history of hip trauma or prior hip pathology. The onset coincided with a significant increase in her dance training hours twelve months ago. She notes the sound is audible to others in the room during warm-up exercises.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive hip flexion-extension reproduces the snap palpable over the anterior-lateral hip. Ober's test is positive. Hip ROM is full with no restrictions. Pain is elicited with resisted hip flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the hip are normal with no bony abnormality. Dynamic ultrasound imaging confirms snapping of the iliotibial band over the greater trochanter during the provocative movement, with no underlying bursitis or labral pathology identified.

puzzles.explanation: Snapping hip syndrome occurs when a musculotendinous structure—most commonly the iliotibial band—subluxates over bony prominences (typically the greater trochanter) during hip movement, creating an audible or palpable snap. In dancers and athletes performing repetitive hip flexion-extension and lateral movements, the IT band undergoes cyclical loading that can trigger inflammation and snapping, particularly when training volume increases significantly. The condition is initially painless but becomes symptomatic as irritation develops; dynamic imaging showing the band's excursion over the trochanter with normal underlying structures (no labral tear or bursal pathology) is diagnostic. Management focuses on load reduction, hip abductor and external rotator strengthening, and addressing any biomechanical contributors to reduce the band's tendency to subluxate.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old woman. Gradual onset over 6 months of persistent dry sensation in eyes and mouth. Reports constant thirst despite adequate fluid intake and frequent need to drink water with meals.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty reading and working on screens for extended periods. Trouble eating dry or hard foods—requires liquids to swallow bread, crackers, nuts. Dry mouth worsens toward evening and with speaking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious progressive onset without trauma or infection trigger. Associated with fatigue and mild symmetric joint aching in hands. No prior autoimmune diagnoses. Symptoms worsening over months despite conservative care.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Schirmer test: <5 mm tear production bilaterally in 5 minutes. Oral mucosa appears pale and sticky; reduced salivary pooling noted. Slit lamp examination shows punctate epithelial keratopathy. No parotid enlargement palpable. Mild synovitis in MCP joints.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La antibodies positive. Rheumatoid factor and ANA elevated. Labial salivary gland biopsy shows focal lymphocytic infiltration with focus score >1.

puzzles.explanation: Sjögren's syndrome is a systemic autoimmune disorder characterized by lymphocytic infiltration and destruction of the lacrimal and salivary glands, resulting in the cardinal symptoms of xerophthalmia and xerostomia. The condition predominantly affects middle-aged women and manifests insidiously with progressive dry eyes and mouth that significantly impair function — difficulty reading, trouble swallowing dry foods, and worsening symptoms through the day as gland output diminishes. Diagnosis is confirmed by objective evidence of reduced tear and salivary production (Schirmer test <5 mm, reduced salivary pooling), ocular surface damage (punctate keratopathy), characteristic salivary gland histology (focal lymphocytic infiltration), and circulating anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La autoantibodies, which distinguish primary Sjögren's from other causes of dryness. The associated systemic features — fatigue, symmetric arthralgias, and elevated inflammatory markers — reflect the broader autoimmune nature of the disease and often necessitate multidisciplinary management including rheumatology input alongside symptomatic relief strategies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old painter reports lateral elbow pain that started 6 weeks ago. Pain is worse with work activities but he cannot pinpoint a specific injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to work at full capacity. Pain limits ability to perform sustained gripping and any forceful wrist movements. Symptoms worsen as the day progresses with continued activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms started without a specific injury event. Patient has continued working despite pain. No prior elbow injuries or treatment. No systemic illness or medications relevant to joint pain. Pain does not wake him at night.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lateral elbow tenderness at epicondyle. Painful resisted wrist extension. Mill's test positive with pain reproduction. Grip strength reduced bilaterally more on affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound common extensor origin shows tendinopathic changes with heterogeneity. Mild surrounding edema. No complete rupture. Lateral collateral ligament intact on assessment.

puzzles.explanation: Lateral epicondylitis, or 'tennis elbow,' results from repetitive microtrauma and degenerative changes in the common extensor tendon origin at the lateral epicondyle, typically from sustained gripping and wrist extension activities. The insidious onset without a single inciting event is classic — chronic overload accumulates micro-tears and inflammatory changes rather than acute rupture. The characteristic presentation of lateral epicondylar tenderness with pain on resisted wrist extension and positive Mill's test reflects the biomechanical overload of the extensor carpi radialis brevis during work activities like painting; symptoms worsening throughout the day are hallmark of cumulative load intolerance. Ultrasound findings of tendinopathy with heterogeneity and edema, without complete rupture, confirm the degenerative rather than acute inflammatory nature of this condition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old construction worker. Sudden onset severe pain in elbow region after direct traumatic blow to arm. Rapid swelling. Arm held protectively and immobile. Patient reports feeling 'something shifted' in the joint at moment of injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Complete loss of elbow flexion and extension. Unable to move arm at elbow joint in any direction. Severe pain prevents active motion. Arm hangs in rigid, semi-flexed posture. Unable to lift, carry, or support any weight through affected limb.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism: caught between machinery with significant compressive force. Immediate onset; no prior elbow pathology or instability. Patient transported to ED within 30 minutes. No attempted self-reduction. First major joint injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elbow held in fixed position with visible asymmetry compared to contralateral side. Palpable abnormal bony prominence. Unable to achieve passive flexion or extension without severe pain. Neurovascular exam: distal pulses present, capillary refill normal, sensation intact in radial and ulnar distributions. No focal neurological deficit detected.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs (AP and lateral views) show ulna and radius displaced posterolaterally relative to distal humerus. Olecranon process no longer articulating with humeral trochlea. Associated fracture lines visible at olecranon and coronoid process. CT confirms displacement direction and associated bone fragments. Post-reduction imaging shows anatomic reduction achieved.

puzzles.explanation: Elbow dislocation occurs when the ulna and radius lose contact with the distal humerus, most commonly displaced posterolaterally following high-energy trauma like direct blows or compressive forces. This injury is the most common major joint dislocation and typically presents with the dramatic clinical picture seen here: severe pain, rapid swelling, loss of all elbow motion, and a visibly deformed limb held protectively in semi-flexion. The posterolateral displacement often accompanies fractures of the olecranon and coronoid process, which compromise the bony stability that normally constrains the joint. Crucially, despite the significant trauma and displacement, neurovascular structures often remain intact—as reflected by preserved distal pulses and sensation—though prompt reduction is essential to prevent vascular compromise and restore joint alignment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 81-year-old woman reports progressive difficulty with stairs and rising from a chair over the past 18 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to carry groceries or perform household tasks. Requires assistance with dressing and bathing. Lives with family due to safety concerns.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong relatively low physical activity. Post-menopausal with inadequate nutrition. Recent decline in function over 1-2 years. Multiple fall history from weakness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked generalized weakness bilaterally. Slow gait with small steps. Hip and knee strength severely reduced 2-3/5. Grip strength markedly diminished. Visible wasting.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA and bioelectrical impedance analysis show appendicular lean mass index <5.4 kg/m² (female threshold for sarcopenia). Muscle quality severely diminished.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related sarcopenia is a progressive loss of skeletal muscle mass and function that accelerates after age 70, driven by a combination of reduced physical activity, hormonal changes (particularly estrogen decline), and nutritional insufficiency. It represents a critical threshold where muscle wasting crosses from normal aging into clinically significant weakness — distinguished by appendicular lean mass indices below the diagnostic cutoff and documented functional impairment in daily tasks. The marked bilateral hip and knee weakness (2–3/5), difficulty with transfers, and reduced grip strength reflect how sarcopenia compromises the antigravity muscles essential for independence. This case exemplifies the downward spiral: low lifetime activity accelerated by menopause and poor nutrition led to rapid functional decline and recurrent falls, making early resistance training and nutritional intervention essential preventive strategies in older adults.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman with progressive groin and medial thigh pain reports numbness and weakness. Pain radiates along medial thigh. Progressive symptoms over 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports functional limitation with hip flexion past 90 degrees and internal rotation. Pain worsens with prolonged walking. No difficulty with abduction or external rotation. Able to perform most daily activities but avoids stairs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No preceding trauma or injury. Patient denies recent hip surgery or groin procedures. No history of pelvic mass or malignancy. Symptoms began insidiously while doing yoga with deep hip flexion and adduction positions. No improvement with rest or NSAIDs over 2 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip adductor (gracilis, adductor longus) strength 4/5 bilaterally, weaker on right. Hip flexion strength intact. Palpable tenderness at groin with hip flexion and internal rotation. Hip abduction strength normal. No gluteal involvement. Femoral nerve examination normal. Adductor reflex diminished on right.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows nerve compression at obturator foramen with signal change in obturator nerve. Possible anatomic variation or hypertrophied obturator externus. No mass. Compression correlates with clinical distribution of sensory loss on medial thigh below knee.

puzzles.explanation: Obturator nerve entrapment occurs when the nerve is compressed as it passes through the obturator foramen in the pelvis, often due to anatomic variations, muscle hypertrophy, or repetitive positioning that increases compression at the foramen. The nerve innervates the adductor muscles (gracilis, adductors longus and brevis) and provides sensory innervation to the medial thigh, making weakness in hip adduction and medial thigh pain characteristic of this condition. The insidious onset following deep hip flexion and adduction yoga positions, combined with preserved hip abduction and flexion strength with selective adductor weakness and diminished adductor reflex, clearly localizes the pathology to the obturator nerve rather than proximal hip structures. MRI confirmation of nerve signal change at the obturator foramen definitively establishes compression at this anatomically vulnerable site where the nerve is tethered and prone to entrapment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 31-year-old with acute wrist pain and swelling after fall. Wrist held protectively. Unable to bear weight through hand or perform fine motor tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with radial/ulnar deviation and gripping. Swelling visible within 30 minutes. Bruising emerging over dorsal wrist.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall occurred during sport. Patient recalled immediate sharp pain but continued activity briefly before realising severity. No prior wrist injury. Wearing standard shoes, no medical comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist swelling and ecchymosis present throughout dorsal wrist. Wrist motion limited by pain in all planes. Neurovascular intact distally. Scaphoid and lunate palpable but no isolated step-off tenderness. Ligamentous laxity testing inconclusive due to pain and swelling.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays negative for fracture. MRI shows soft tissue injury between scaphoid and lunate with surrounding edema and fluid. No skeletal involvement. Ligamentous structures intact. Consistent with moderate wrist sprain.

puzzles.explanation: A wrist sprain represents ligamentous injury to the intrinsic or extrinsic ligaments stabilizing the radiocarpal and intercarpal joints, typically from forced deviation or compression during a fall. The immediate swelling and dorsal ecchymosis seen here reflect acute soft tissue disruption and hemorrhage into the joint capsule and surrounding tissues — hallmark features that distinguish a ligamentous injury from fracture. This patient's preserved neurovascular status, absence of bony involvement on imaging, and intact (though tender) ligamentous structures despite pain-limited testing indicate a moderate sprain where the ligaments have stretched or partially torn but retained functional continuity. Recovery depends on the degree of ligamentous disruption; moderate sprains typically require 4–6 weeks of protected mobilization before returning to gripping and weight-bearing activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 32-year-old athlete reports sudden onset groin pain during sport. Sharp quality, acute functional loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain provoked specifically with hip adduction and hip flexion/external rotation activities. Difficulty with side-stepping and diagonal movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior groin injuries. Recent increase in training volume over past 3 weeks. Flexible hips but limited adductor flexibility. Warm-up completed before injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness along medial thigh in longitudinal distribution. Pain on resisted hip adduction (30° hip flexion). Adductor bulk palpable but no obvious discontinuity. Antalgic gait with hip adduction avoidance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows muscle fiber disruption with surrounding inflammation. Hematoma forming. Adductor muscle involvement confirmed. No complete rupture.

puzzles.explanation: Adductor strain represents a partial tear of the adductor muscle complex (longus, brevis, or magnus), typically occurring at the musculotendinous junction when eccentric loading overwhelms the muscle's tensile capacity. Young athletes are particularly vulnerable during rapid changes of direction or side-stepping movements that combine hip flexion with external rotation — exactly the diagonal movements that provoked pain in this case. The sudden onset during sport, combined with recent training volume escalation and paradoxically limited adductor flexibility despite good overall hip mobility, created the perfect scenario for acute fiber disruption. Ultrasound findings of muscle fiber discontinuity with inflammation and hematoma confirm the partial tear, distinguishing this from the compensatory adductor tightness that often follows overuse or from a complete rupture with visible gap.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old man with chronic dizziness for 10 months reports constant swaying sensation. Balance difficulty with movement. Anxiety about symptoms prominent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids crowded environments and driving. Symptoms worse when focusing on visual targets or when walking in open spaces. Social withdrawal due to fear of symptom onset in public.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mild head injury with brief dizziness 10 months prior. Dizziness persisted after physical recovery. Progressive anxiety worsened symptoms. Multiple specialist evaluations unrevealing. Hyperawareness of bodily sensations and catastrophizing about movements.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Postural sway present but mild on standing. Dizziness reproducible with rapid head turns and gaze shifts. Romberg test normal; tandem stance mild instability. Hyperventilation provokes dizziness and lightheadedness. Nystagmus absent. Dix–Hallpike and head roll tests negative. Fear-avoidance behaviour observed during testing. Symptoms intensify with attention to balance task.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Caloric testing normal, head impulse test normal. Videonystagmography within normal limits. MRI head and internal auditory canals normal. No evidence of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, vestibular neuritis, or central pathology. Diagnosis supported by persistent symptoms despite normal investigations and psychological overlay.

puzzles.explanation: Persistent Postural-Perceptual Dizziness (PPPD) is a chronic vestibular disorder characterized by heightened sensitivity to motion, visual stimuli, and postural threats—often triggered by an initial vestibular event but sustained by maladaptive central processing and anxiety rather than ongoing peripheral dysfunction. Unlike conditions such as BPPV or vestibular neuritis, PPPD produces constant swaying sensations and balance apprehension without objective findings on standard vestibular testing; the normal caloric responses, head impulse test, and imaging here are hallmark features. The condition thrives on attention to bodily sensations and fear-avoidance behaviour, creating a self-perpetuating cycle where psychological overlay and hypervigilance amplify dizziness symptoms—explaining why the patient's symptoms intensify during focused balance tasks and in visually complex environments like crowded spaces. Effective management requires vestibular rehabilitation paired with cognitive-behavioural strategies to desensitize the nervous system and break the cycle of anxiety-driven symptom amplification.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 15-year-old basketball player with knee pain during intensive training. Onset gradual over past 4-6 weeks, worse with jumping and running.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate plyometric training or high-impact sports. Symptoms worsen with repetitive loading. Some relief with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive training program with frequent jumping and running. Rapid growth during adolescent spurt. Inadequate recovery between training sessions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized tenderness over medial aspect of proximal tibia. Pain reproduction on single-leg hop test. No effusion. Knee ligament stability tests normal. Full ROM.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Widened growth plate with increased T2 signal on MRI at proximal tibial physis. No metaphyseal fracture line or avulsion. Physis remains open and intact.

puzzles.explanation: Growth plate injuries occur when the weaker physeal cartilage undergoes overuse or acute trauma before skeletal maturity, particularly during adolescent growth spurts when the physis is naturally more vulnerable and longitudinal growth rate is highest. The proximal tibia is a common site due to its role in shock absorption during jumping and running, making young athletes in high-impact sports especially susceptible. The widened, edematous physis seen on MRI—without associated fracture—reflects inflammation from repetitive microtrauma and inadequate recovery rather than acute structural failure. Management centers on relative rest from high-impact activities and graduated return to sport, as premature resumption risks progression to physeal stress fracture or permanent growth disturbance.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 29-year-old rugby player injured their ankle during a tackle. Reports pain above the ankle joint, worse with pivoting and weight-bearing movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight comfortably or change direction quickly. Pain reproduced with external rotation movements of the ankle.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 3 days ago. Patient reports immediate swelling and severe pain, preventing return to sport. No prior ankle injuries. No physiotherapy yet attempted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Syndesmotic tenderness above ankle joint, between tibia and fibula. Pain with external rotation stress test positive. Squeeze test positive (pain with compression of tibia/fibula).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ankle X-rays with weight-bearing show widened tibiofibular space suggesting syndesmotic disruption. Stress radiographs demonstrate increased talar rotation. CT further characterizes injury.

puzzles.explanation: A high ankle sprain involves disruption of the syndesmotic ligaments—the interosseous ligament, anterior tibiofibular ligament, and posterior tibiofibular ligament—that stabilize the distal tibiofibular joint rather than the lateral ankle ligaments involved in conventional ankle sprains. Unlike inversion injuries, high ankle sprains typically result from external rotation or forced abduction mechanisms, as occurred during this tackle, and present with pain above the ankle joint line with characteristic positive external rotation and squeeze tests reflecting the injury mechanism. The widened tibiofibular space on weight-bearing radiographs and increased talar rotation on stress imaging confirm syndesmotic disruption, which is the defining feature distinguishing this injury from lateral ligament sprains. High ankle sprains generally carry a longer recovery timeline and greater functional demands, making early recognition critical for appropriate load management and returning athletes to sport safely.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old male boxer presents with acute hand pain and swelling after a punch during a match.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot make a closed fist. Inability to clench hand without sharp pain over the knuckle area.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 hours ago during training. Player felt immediate sharp pain and heard/felt a crack. No prior hand injuries or fracture history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Fifth metacarpal head swelling, ecchymosis, and angulation. Pain with palpation over fracture site. Prominence of knuckle reduced (depressed fracture appearance).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays show fifth metacarpal neck fracture with apex dorsal angulation (classic 'boxer's fracture'). Displacement and angulation measured on X-rays.

puzzles.explanation: A boxer's fracture is a neck fracture of the fifth metacarpal (or sometimes fourth) caused by a direct axial load through a clenched fist, classically from punching. The characteristic apex dorsal angulation occurs because the interossei and intrinsic hand muscles pull the distal fragment dorsally after the fracture, combined with the inherent biomechanics of the metacarpal neck. The loss of knuckle prominence and inability to form a closed fist without sharp pain are hallmark signs reflecting both the structural disruption and reactive inflammation. While many boxer's fractures can be managed conservatively with early mobilization, the degree of angulation and displacement must be assessed carefully, as excessive dorsal angulation (typically >40°) risks long-term functional impairment and cosmetic deformity of the hand.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old recreational tennis player presents with posterior elbow pain that worsens with forceful arm movements. Pain began 4 weeks ago and has been gradually worsening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform overhead throwing or serving motions. Pushing and pressing movements (like bench press or wall push-ups) reproduce pain. Training intensity significantly reduced.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Increased tennis training volume focusing on serving technique. Repetitive overhead serving causing triceps overload. Progressive symptoms over 4 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked tenderness over triceps insertion at olecranon. Resisted elbow extension reproduces sharp pain. Triceps strength 4/5 due to pain inhibition. Possible palpable swelling or thickening at insertion site.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows triceps tendon thickening at insertion with heterogeneous echotexture. Possible small tear or chronic inflammation. Olecrannon bursa may show fluid.

puzzles.explanation: Triceps tendinopathy is a chronic overuse injury where the triceps tendon, particularly at its insertion on the olecranon, develops degenerative changes and inflammation from repetitive high-load extension activities. In overhead athletes like tennis players, aggressive serving motions and sudden increases in training volume create excessive tensile stress through the extensor mechanism, leading to microtrauma and progressive tendon breakdown. The posterior elbow pain with resisted extension, combined with imaging evidence of tendon thickening and heterogeneous echotexture, confirms the degenerative process rather than acute rupture. Management focuses on load modification, eccentric strengthening, and addressing the underlying technical or training errors that drove the initial overload.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old woman with progressive low back pain and neurogenic claudication over 2 years. Pain radiates to buttock and upper thigh. Unable to walk beyond 100 metres before requiring rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged standing and walking downhill. Significant relief with sitting or forward bending. Difficulty with stairs and sustained postures. Unable to work as retail supervisor due to prolonged standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prior non-operative management failed (6 months physiotherapy and analgesia). Imaging showed L4-L5 disc degeneration with facet arthropathy and central stenosis with intermittent neurogenic claudication. Symptoms progressively worsened over 2 years despite conservative care. Underwent lumbar fusion 6 weeks ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Current post-operative presentation: Lumbar ROM: flexion 20°, extension 10°, lateral flexion 15° bilaterally. Straight leg raise 70° bilaterally, pain-free. Hip and knee ROM full and pain-free. Neurological examination normal. Incision well-healed, no signs of infection. Pain 2-3/10 at rest, 4-5/10 with movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-ray (6 weeks): Fusion hardware properly positioned across L4-L5. Lateral view shows appropriate restoration of disc height and sagittal alignment. No evidence of hardware loosening, malposition, or breakage. Early bony bridging between vertebral bodies visible on CT.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spinal fusion is a definitive surgical intervention for symptomatic lumbar stenosis with neurogenic claudication that has failed conservative management, involving arthrodesis across degenerated segments to eliminate motion and decompress neural structures. This patient's significant stenosis at L4-L5 with facet arthropathy and two years of progressive symptoms despite physiotherapy and analgesia made her an appropriate surgical candidate. The post-operative imaging demonstrates successful hardware positioning with early bony bridging, while the markedly reduced neurogenic claudication distance prior to surgery — limited to 100 metres — reflects the mechanical decompression achieved by restoring disc height and sagittal alignment.
