Current ROM restrictions and modest pain with movement are typical early post-operative findings; neurological recovery and functional capacity continue to improve over the following months as bony fusion matures and protective neuromuscular patterns normalize.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 32-year-old manufacturing worker. Onset of cough and chest tightness over past 8 weeks. Denies fever, recent illness, or smoking history. No prior respiratory diagnosis. Reports general fatigue and occasional wheezing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms noticeably worse during work shifts; improve significantly on days off and during vacation week. Difficulty with sustained exertion at work. Chest feels tight mid-shift. Symptoms resolve within 24–48 hours away from workplace.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Works on assembly line with exposure to chemical aerosols and dust. Started current role 6 months ago. Symptoms appeared after 2 months on job. History of seasonal allergies. No prior lung disease, no significant sick leave before this year.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Peak expiratory flow (PEF) readings show >20% variability between work days and rest days. Spirometry demonstrates reversible airflow obstruction (FEV1/FVC 0.65 improving to 0.78 post-bronchodilator). Chest clear to auscultation. No signs of cardiac disease.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Methacholine bronchial challenge test positive (PC20 <8 mg/mL). Specific IgE testing identifies sensitization to workplace chemical exposure. Chest X-ray normal; no infiltrates, hyperinflation, or structural abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational asthma is work-related airway hyperresponsiveness triggered by exposure to specific workplace agents — in this case, chemical aerosols and dust on the assembly line — leading to reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial inflammation. Unlike pre-existing asthma, occupational asthma develops after a latency period of sensitization (typically weeks to months after exposure begins), which explains this worker's symptom onset two months into the role. The clinical hallmark is the temporal relationship between workplace exposure and symptoms: chest tightness and airflow obstruction that worsen during shifts and resolve within 1–2 days away from work, paired with documented >20% PEF variability and positive methacholine challenge reflecting bronchial hyperresponsiveness. Specific IgE sensitization to the workplace agent and reversible spirometric obstruction confirm the diagnosis and distinguish it from other occupational lung diseases like hypersensitivity pneumonitis or toxic exposure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior episodes of vertigo. Patient reports gradual return to upright posture tolerance over 48 hours. No medication changes. Works in office environment with minimal physical demands.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI inner ear structures normal, no T2/FLAIR signal abnormality in IAC. No enhancement of CN VIII. Auditory evoked responses normal. Videonystagmography shows reduced caloric response ipsilateral to positive head impulse test.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular neuritis is acute inflammation of the vestibular nerve (CN VIII) resulting in sudden, severe vertigo often preceded by viral illness—the viral trigger likely caused immune-mediated inflammation of the nerve as it travels through the internal acoustic canal. The hallmark presentation is unilateral vestibular loss with constant, position-independent vertigo that peaks within 24–48 hours and gradually resolves over days to weeks, as seen here with preserved hearing distinguishing it from labyrinthitis. The head impulse test reveals the pathognomonic catch-up saccade, confirming unilateral peripheral vestibular hypofunction, while caloric testing and videonystagmography document the asymmetric vestibular paresis—normal MRI and auditory responses rule out central causes and cochlear involvement. Postural instability and nystagmus beating toward the unaffected side reflect central compensation mechanisms and unopposed activity of the contralateral vestibular system.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old dancer reports acute onset anterior thigh pain during rapid knee flexion and hip extension movement. Sharp discomfort with immediate functional loss and swelling over anterior thigh.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain reproduced with resisted knee flexion and combined hip extension. Unable to perform high kicks or explosive lunges. Weight-bearing through leg possible but significantly limited. Difficulty with stairs and sustained standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden onset during ballistic warm-up movement. No prior thigh injury. Symptoms started acutely without preceding warning signs. Inadequate dynamic stretching prior to activity. Immediate pain and loss of load tolerance.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness localised to rectus femoris muscle belly, mid-anterior thigh. Pain with resisted knee flexion and combined hip extension. Possible small myofascial defect palpable on direct compression. Rectus femoris strength 4/5 secondary to pain. Hip flexor strength intact. Swelling and bruising visible over anterior thigh.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows partial muscle fiber disruption of rectus femoris with surrounding edema and intramuscular fluid collection. No complete muscle rupture or retraction. Strain injury with acute inflammation and minimal haematoma.

puzzles.explanation: Adductor strain represents an acute musculotendinous injury to the adductor muscle group, typically occurring with explosive hip abduction or external rotation movements combined with rapid knee flexion — a common mechanism in dancers and athletes. The rectus femoris involvement seen here, with partial fiber disruption and immediate loss of load tolerance, reflects the violent nature of the injury during ballistic warm-up without adequate neuromuscular preparation. The localized tenderness, palpable myofascial defect, and preserved hip flexor strength help distinguish this from more global hip flexor pathology or iliopsoas involvement. Recovery depends on tissue healing phase and graduated loading progression, with ultrasound evidence of partial disruption typically requiring 4-8 weeks of activity modification before return to high-demand ballistic movements.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old motorcyclist presents 3 weeks after a fall with shoulder dislocation. Reports progressive weakness in upper arm and shoulder with associated pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weakness predominantly affects shoulder abduction and external rotation with inability to lift arm against gravity. Patient reports altered sensation in lateral shoulder and thumb region.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Post-injury imaging demonstrated edema and hemorrhage in brachial plexus region. No prior neurological history. Patient reports symptom progression over 3 weeks with no improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows Erb's palsy pattern: waiter's tip position of arm with internal rotation, adduction, and elbow extension. Supraspinatus/infraspinatus atrophy visible. Upper limb reflexes (biceps, brachioradialis) diminished or absent on affected side. Horner syndrome absent (excluding lower trunk).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG/NCS shows acute denervation potentials in biceps, supraspinatus, and deltoid muscles with slowed conduction velocities. Motor amplitudes reduced in axillary and suprascapular nerves. MRI demonstrates brachial plexus edema and hemorrhage at level of upper trunk with partial continuity.

puzzles.explanation: Brachial plexus injury results from traumatic stretching or tearing of nerve roots (C5-C8, T1) that form the plexus, commonly from high-energy mechanisms like motorcycle crashes with associated shoulder dislocation. Upper trunk injuries (Erb's palsy) characteristically spare the lower trunk, producing the classic waiter's tip posture — internal rotation and adduction from pectoralis dominance with loss of rotator cuff function — and preserved hand intrinsic strength, as seen here. The progressive weakness and denervation pattern on EMG over three weeks reflects ongoing axonal degeneration following the initial trauma, with partial nerve continuity offering potential for recovery through axonal regeneration over months. The preserved bilateral reflexes and absent Horner syndrome help localize the injury to the upper trunk rather than root avulsion, which is prognostically important for rehabilitation planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old office worker reports progressive clumsiness of both hands over 6 months. Difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning and writing. Also notes unsteady gait and increased stumbling. Wife reports patient walking more cautiously and using wider stance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Hands show more functional loss than expected from strength alone—dropping objects, trouble manipulating small items. Gait worsens on stairs and uneven ground. Difficulty with rapid hand movements and coordination. Arm swing reduced bilaterally. Symptoms progress throughout day with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decades of intermittent neck stiffness and occasional pain. Motor vehicle accident 5 years ago—no fracture at that time. Symptoms began insidiously 6 months ago without clear trigger. Progressive course over this period. Smoker, age 58. No recent trauma or acute event.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hyperreflexia in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Positive Hoffmann's sign bilaterally. Gait shows spasticity and imbalance. Fine finger coordination impaired—difficulty with rapid alternating movements. Mild weakness in intrinsic hand muscles. Sensory testing reveals loss of vibration sense in feet. Neck extension reproduces lower limb symptoms slightly.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine demonstrates multilevel degenerative changes with osteophytes at C5-6 and C6-7. Spinal canal narrowed to 10mm at most compressed level. T2-weighted imaging shows central cord signal hyperintensity indicating myelopathic edema. No acute fracture or instability. Cord appears compressed between osteophytes and posterior ligament.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical myelopathy results from chronic compression of the spinal cord in the cervical spine, most commonly from degenerative spondylosis with osteophyte formation narrowing the canal over time. The hallmark presentation combines upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, Hoffmann's sign, spasticity) with lower motor neuron features in the hands (intrinsic weakness, incoordination) reflecting direct cord compression at multiple levels. The progressive clumsiness, gait dysfunction, and bilateral hand incoordination seen here exemplify how myelopathy affects both fine motor control and postural stability, often worsening throughout the day as activity increases cord ischemia. MRI findings of canal stenosis with central T2 signal hyperintensity confirm cord edema from chronic compression—a critical distinction from acute trauma and a prognostic indicator that conservative management windows are narrowing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old woman with 4 days of progressive weakness starting in the legs. Reports gastroenteritis with diarrhea about 10 days ago, which seemed to resolve. Now weakness is worsening and spreading upward.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk independently. Difficulty speaking clearly and chewing. Has noticed mild shortness of breath with exertion. Weakness symmetric on both sides of body.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior neurological or autoimmune disease. Vaccination history unremarkable. Symptoms appeared suddenly 4 days after GI illness resolved, and have progressed daily without improvement. No similar presentations in family.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral lower extremity weakness symmetric. Absent reflexes throughout. Ascending pattern with hip and knee involvement. Upper extremity strength testing begins to show weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CSF shows elevated protein with minimal cells (albuminocytologic dissociation). EMG shows demyelination pattern. Nerve conduction studies show conduction blocks. Respiratory function monitored.

puzzles.explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome is an acute autoimmune-mediated demyelinating polyneuropathy that typically follows an infectious trigger — in this case, gastroenteritis — by 1–3 weeks, when molecular mimicry between pathogen antigens and myelin glycoproteins drives autoimmune attack on peripheral nerve roots. The syndrome presents with characteristic ascending paralysis beginning distally in the legs and progressing proximally, along with areflexia and autonomic involvement, as seen in this patient's worsening weakness, respiratory compromise, and bulbar features. The elevated CSF protein with relatively normal cell count (albuminocytologic dissociation) and demyelination on EMG with conduction blocks form the diagnostic triad. Most patients recover fully with aggressive supportive care and immunotherapy, though the acute phase can be life-threatening, particularly when respiratory muscles are affected — necessitating close monitoring of vital capacity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 65-year-old man of Northern European ancestry presents with progressive difficulty extending his hand. He first noticed a firm lump in his palm 2 years ago and has increasingly struggled to straighten his fingers, particularly the ring and little fingers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot easily perform activities requiring open palm positioning—difficulty with writing, typing, and personal hygiene. Grip strength subjectively reduced but pain minimal.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Family history of similar hand contractures in father and uncle. Northern European ancestry (Swedish descent). Age-related presentation typical for Dupuytren disease. No significant trauma or repetitive hand use occupation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination reveals firm palmar nodules along flexor surface. Palpable longitudinal cords extending from palm into ring and little finger. Ring finger MCP joint flexion contracture 45 degrees, PIP involvement minimal. Table top test fails with inability to place palm flat on surface. Skin dimpling and pit formation noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates hypoechoic fascial cords extending from palm into ring and little finger rays with MCP joint involvement. No evidence of nerve or vessel entrapment. Severity graded moderate.

puzzles.explanation: Dupuytren's contracture is a progressive fibroproliferative disorder of the palmar fascia that gradually shortens and thickens, pulling fingers into flexion and limiting extension. The condition has a strong genetic component, particularly in Northern European populations, and typically emerges in the sixth decade or later; the bilateral family history and Swedish ancestry seen here are classic risk factors. Clinically, it presents insidiously with firm nodules and longitudinal cords in the palm that advance along the flexor surface, with the ring and little fingers affected most frequently and the MCP joint typically more involved than the PIP joint. Diagnosis is clinical—the table-top test failure and palpable cords are pathognomonic—and while ultrasound confirms fascial involvement, neither pain nor grip loss is typical; the functional impact comes purely from loss of extension.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain for 3 weeks. Pain began during overhead serve motion. Reports occasional clicking sensation in shoulder during reaching.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp anterior shoulder pain with overhead activities, serving, and throwing motions. Pain exacerbated by resisted elbow flexion and supination. Discomfort improves with rest and ice. Patient experiences pain at night when lying on affected shoulder.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive tennis participation (4 times weekly) over the past 12 months. Gradual symptom onset rather than traumatic incident. No previous shoulder surgery. Age 52 with no prior upper limb pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Screening examination revealed weakness and poor control of rotator cuff musculature. Speed's test positive with anterior shoulder pain. Palpable tenderness over biceps groove. Subacromial space appears narrowed clinically.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening (5mm) with hypoechoic signal and increased vascularity on Doppler imaging consistent with tendinopathy. Subacromial space narrowed suggesting associated subacromial impingement. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration of proximal biceps tendon without full thickness tear.

puzzles.explanation: Biceps tendinopathy results from cumulative microtrauma to the long head of the biceps tendon, typically from repetitive overhead loading—as occurs with frequent serving and throwing—combined with relative subacromial impingement that increases mechanical stress on the tendon. The condition is characterized by intrasubstance degeneration, inflammation, and neovascularization rather than structural failure, explaining the gradual onset and reproducibility with overhead and resisted flexion/supination movements. The narrowed subacromial space and rotator cuff weakness evident on examination are key features: they create a biomechanical trap where the biceps tendon is progressively compressed during shoulder elevation, perpetuating irritation. In middle-aged recreational athletes, this combination of chronic overuse, age-related tendon changes, and secondary impingement makes biceps tendinopathy a common cause of anterior shoulder pain that responds well to activity modification, strengthening, and load management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged office worker presents with hand paresthesias and numbness. Symptoms are worse at night and disturb sleep. Fine motor tasks and gripping activities feel more difficult than usual.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptom pattern is distinctly nocturnal—worst at night and upon waking. Shaking or flicking the hand provides transient relief. Daytime symptoms correlate with repetitive hand-intensive work but are far less bothersome than night symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began insidiously 3–4 months ago during a period of increased keyboard and administrative work. Patient denies preceding trauma or acute injury. Similar symptoms reported in contralateral hand but milder. No prior episodes of paresthesias or neurological symptoms elsewhere.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, Tinel's sign is positive over the median nerve at the wrist. Phalen's test reproduces symptoms after 60 seconds. Two-point discrimination is reduced in median-innervated fingers. Grip strength is diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyographic testing demonstrates median nerve conduction slowing across the wrist with fibrillation potentials. Nerve conduction studies confirm focal demyelination at the carpal tunnel. The diagnosis is established by neurophysiological testing.

puzzles.explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome results from compression of the median nerve as it traverses the carpal tunnel at the wrist, most commonly due to tenosynovial thickening, inflammation, or space-occupying changes in response to repetitive loading. The condition classically presents with nocturnal paresthesias in the median nerve distribution (thumb, index, and middle fingers) that disturb sleep—a pattern so characteristic that nocturnal symptoms alone carry high diagnostic weight. The insidious onset following increased keyboard work, combined with the transient relief from shaking the hand and the bilateral but asymmetrical presentation, reflects cumulative microtrauma and sustained wrist flexion during sleep. Positive Tinel's and Phalen's tests, reduced two-point discrimination, and electrophysiological evidence of focal demyelination at the wrist confirm the diagnosis and localize the site of compression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old with 2-week history of progressive shortness of breath. Chest feels heavy. Exercise tolerance declining. No fever or cough.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dyspnea worsens with exertion and lying on left side. Able to walk short distances on flat ground but limited by breathlessness. Positional component—more comfortable sitting upright.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Completed chemotherapy 4 weeks prior for known malignancy. Gradual symptom onset over 2 weeks despite medical management. No recent infection, trauma, or heart failure exacerbation reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Dullness to percussion at bilateral lung bases, more pronounced on left. Reduced breath sounds and tactile fremitus at bases. Oxygen saturation 88% on room air at rest. Egophony present over left base.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray: blunting of left costophrenic angle with meniscus sign. CT confirms moderate left-sided collection (4 cm) and smaller right-sided collection. Pleural fluid aspiration shows exudative characteristics with malignant cells on cytology.

puzzles.explanation: Pleural effusion is an abnormal accumulation of fluid between the visceral and parietal pleura, which restricts lung expansion and reduces gas exchange. In this post-chemotherapy patient, the malignant cells identified on cytology indicate a malignant effusion—a common complication of advanced malignancy where tumor cells invade the pleura and increase vascular permeability. The exudative nature of the fluid, bilateral involvement (more pronounced on the left), and progressive dyspnea over two weeks despite medical management are classic for this presentation. The positional worsening on lying left, combined with dullness and egophony at the bases, reflects the volume and location of fluid accumulation limiting ventilation; oxygen desaturation at rest signals significant physiological impairment warranting urgent drainage consideration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old woman awakens with sudden onset facial droop on right side and cannot close right eye properly. No prior neurological history. Onset occurred overnight without prodrome.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Right lower facial weakness greater than upper face. Complete eye closure impossible on right. Water drips from affected side during drinking. Difficulty with oral intake and speech distortion. Pain in right ear preceding facial weakness by 24 hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Right-sided facial weakness affecting ability to smile, close eye, and maintain oral competence. Water dribbles from mouth during drinking. Difficulty articulating consonants. Note: ear pain preceded weakness onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Complete inability to wrinkle right forehead and close right eye. Asymmetric smile with right lip droop. Light touch sensation normal across all trigeminal divisions. Hearing normal bilaterally. Gag reflex intact. No visible vesicles in external auditory canal, post-auricular area, or palate.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of brain and internal acoustic meatus shows enhancement of right facial nerve within temporal bone but excludes mass, stroke, or other structural lesion. No evidence of parotid gland pathology. Electromyography shows denervation in right facial muscles consistent with acute nerve injury.

puzzles.explanation: Bell's palsy is an acute, idiopathic paralysis of the facial nerve (CN VII) that typically strikes without warning, often preceded by ear pain as the swollen nerve becomes compressed within the tight confines of the temporal bone. The classic presentation—sudden unilateral facial weakness with forehead involvement, eye closure deficit, and lower facial drooping—reflects the anatomical course of the facial nerve as it innervates muscles of facial expression throughout its path from the brainstem through the temporal bone to the periphery. The absence of sensory changes, vesicles (excluding Ramsay Hunt syndrome), and structural lesions on imaging, combined with denervation on EMG, confirms a peripheral nerve palsy rather than central stroke or mass effect. With roughly 70–80% of patients achieving full recovery over weeks to months with appropriate care including eye protection and corticosteroids, the prognosis remains favourable despite the dramatic initial presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old recreational athlete presents following acute knee injury during sports. The patient reports sudden-onset knee pain and immediate functional loss. The knee gave way and could not bear weight.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to return to pivoting/cutting sports. Attempts to change direction cause sudden instability and knee collapse. Straight-line walking is tolerated but rotational control is absent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute injury occurred during a deceleration-plant-and-cut maneuver in sports. Mechanism involved combined flexion, external tibial rotation, and valgus stress. This acute ACL injury requires surgical consultation and management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Lachman test indicates anterior tibial translation. Positive pivot shift test demonstrates rotational instability. Mild effusion is present. Pain limits examination, but clinical signs are consistent with ACL rupture.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates disruption of the anterior cruciate ligament with discontinuity of fibers. Bone bruising is visualized at the expected locations. Associated meniscal or collateral ligament injuries may be present. Imaging confirms complete ACL tear.

puzzles.explanation: The anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) is the primary restraint to anterior tibial translation and rotational control of the knee, and its rupture typically occurs during high-demand activities involving sudden deceleration combined with pivoting or cutting — exactly the mechanism seen in this acute sports injury. Complete ACL tears present with immediate functional loss, a sensation of the knee 'giving way,' and marked rotational instability that becomes evident on the pivot shift test, where the tibia subluxates and then reduces with knee flexion. The inability to return to pivoting and cutting sports, despite tolerance for straight-line walking, is pathognomonic of ACL insufficiency, as rotational control is abolished while simple anteroposterior stability during gait may be partially compensated by muscular guarding. MRI confirmation of fiber discontinuity, combined with the acute mechanism and positive Lachman and pivot shift tests, makes ACL reconstruction the standard operative approach for younger, active patients to restore dynamic stability and prevent further intra-articular damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman presents with progressive height loss over the past year and chronic mid-back pain. Recent fall onto outstretched wrist resulted in fracture.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform overhead activities or carry loads due to thoracic pain and kyphosis. Unable to tolerate prolonged standing. Reports increasing fear of falling and declining balance confidence.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Menopause 15 years ago without hormone replacement therapy. History of poor calcium intake and limited outdoor activity. Mother had vertebral fractures in her 70s. Recent corticosteroid use for respiratory condition (6 months, moderate dose).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Thoracic kyphosis 45 degrees measured via digital inclinometry. Spinal palpation tenderness at T7-8 with palpable step-off. Grip strength 18 kg bilaterally. Functional reach test 6 inches. No focal neurological deficit. Pain with spinal extension worse than flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan shows T-score of -2.8 at lumbar spine and -2.5 at femoral neck (osteoporotic range). Vertebral fracture assessment shows compression fracture at T7 (15% height loss). Biochemical markers show elevated P1NP and CTX-1 indicating high bone turnover.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporosis is a systemic condition characterized by decreased bone mineral density and deterioration of bone microarchitecture, leading to increased fragility and fracture risk. It results from an imbalance between bone resorption and formation — here evidenced by elevated P1NP and CTX-1 indicating high bone turnover — and develops through multiple converging risk factors including estrogen deficiency (15 years post-menopause without HRT), inadequate calcium intake, sedentary lifestyle, glucocorticoid use, and genetic predisposition. The clinical presentation of progressive height loss, thoracic kyphosis, vertebral compression fracture at T7, and low-energy wrist fracture are characteristic hallmarks of established osteoporosis, while the elevated fall risk and reduced functional reach reflect both spinal deformity and the systemic nature of bone fragility. DEXA scores in the osteoporotic range (T-score ≤ −2.5) confirm the diagnosis and necessitate intervention to reduce fracture risk through exercise, load management, and pharmacological therapy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 35-year-old man presents with acute onset severe pain in the buttock and posterior thigh following a fall with penetrating trauma. Reports inability to move foot normally and describes dragging sensation in lower leg when walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to lift foot off ground or point toes downward. Difficulty walking with characteristic high-stepping gait to clear the affected limb. Pain and weakness localized to buttock, posterior thigh, and lower leg on one side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 days ago from deep laceration to buttock sustained during fall on broken glass. Immediate onset of neurological symptoms at time of injury. No prior nerve problems or chronic conditions. Wound managed but patient reports progressive weakness despite pain control.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Motor deficit: foot drop present, unable to dorsiflex ankle or extend great toe. Sensation reduced in lateral lower leg and dorsum of foot in consistent dermatomal pattern. Tinel sign positive over buttock wound site. Ankle reflex diminished on affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of posterior thigh shows focal nerve swelling and disruption at gluteal region corresponding to wound location. Nerve conduction studies demonstrate prolonged distal motor latencies and reduced compound muscle action potentials in tibial and peroneal divisions only, with normal proximal responses.

puzzles.explanation: Sciatic nerve injury results from direct trauma to the nerve as it exits the pelvis through the gluteal region, in this case from penetrating laceration. The sciatic nerve's dual motor innervation — the tibial division (plantarflexion and toe flexion) and peroneal division (dorsiflexion and foot eversion) — means injury typically presents with foot drop and inability to dorsiflex the ankle, as seen in this patient's high-stepping gait. The localized swelling at the wound site with positive Tinel sign indicates acute nerve disruption, while the reduced sensory response in the lateral lower leg reflects involvement of the sural and superficial peroneal sensory branches. Nerve conduction studies showing slowed distal latencies with normal proximal responses confirm a focal lesion at the level of injury rather than a proximal root or plexus problem, guiding both prognosis and rehabilitation planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old man presents 6 weeks after hand surgery for a finger contracture. Reports excellent initial progress with restored movement, but over the past week has noticed increasing tightness returning and range of motion declining.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to fully flatten hand and extend fingers completely. PIP joint of ring finger particularly stiff despite good recovery in early weeks. Recent increased hand use with therapy and work contributing to symptom progression.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Original contracture measured 50 degrees pre-operatively. Surgical plan included complete fascial excision. Increased activity with physical therapy and return to work commenced 4-5 weeks post-op, coinciding with symptom return.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Range of motion measurement shows MCP extension improved to 10 degrees flexion contracture (from pre-op 45 degrees) but PIP extension limitation developing. Scar tissue palpable along surgical site with firmness increasing. Minimal swelling and erythema. No signs of infection or wound complications.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound at 6 weeks post-op shows intact fasciectomy with resected tissue planes visualized. No fluid collection or hematoma. However, early recurrent disease process evident with hypoechoic nodules reforming at operative margins and PIP joint region. Scar tissue shows intermediate echogenicity consistent with early proliferative phase.

puzzles.explanation: Dupuytren's disease is a chronic fibroproliferative disorder affecting the palmar fascia, characterized by progressive contracture of the hand — particularly at the MCP and PIP joints — with a strong male predominance and typically increasing prevalence after age 50. While fasciectomy successfully removes diseased tissue and restores motion acutely, recurrence is common, especially with aggressive early mobilization and increased hand use in the post-operative period. The return of tightness and developing PIP joint stiffness seen here, coupled with ultrasound evidence of hypoechoic nodules reforming at the surgical margins, reflects the disease's inherent aggressive biology rather than surgical failure — Dupuytren's tends to recur in 20–50% of cases as the underlying fibroblastic process reactivates. Managing post-operative progression requires careful balance between early mobilization to prevent stiffness and activity modification to minimize the stimulus for recurrent nodule formation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old office worker presents with acute onset severe shoulder pain yesterday during routine desk work. Sharp anterior shoulder pain unrelated to specific trauma. Pain awakens patient at night with inability to rest arm comfortably.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe limitation with shoulder abduction and internal rotation; reaching across body markedly painful. Pain prevents active motion through guarding; passive range of motion not yet assessed. Discomfort worse with lying on affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No preceding trauma or overuse history. Office work involves minimal overhead demands. Pain intensity markedly disproportionate to mechanism (routine desk task). Similar acute presentations often self-resolve over weeks despite severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe tenderness over supraspinatus tendon insertion. Passive range of motion better preserved than active motion. Supraspinatus strength testing limited by pain rather than true weakness. Dugas test negative (shoulder can still adduct). Hawkins test markedly positive reproducing sharp pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder X-ray (AP and scapular Y views) demonstrates dense calcium deposit within supraspinatus tendon measuring 8x6mm. Calcium appears radiodense with well-defined borders. Associated soft tissue swelling visible around deposit. MRI shows T2 hypointense deposit with surrounding fluid signal indicating active inflammation and resorption phase.

puzzles.explanation: Calcific tendinitis is an idiopathic inflammatory condition characterized by calcium hydroxyapatite deposition within tendons—most commonly affecting the rotator cuff, particularly the supraspinatus. Unlike chronic calcification, acute calcific tendinitis results from resorption and inflammation of existing deposits rather than new formation, producing disproportionately severe pain, nocturnal symptoms, and dramatic loss of function that appears incongruent with the minimal precipitating trauma. The combination of well-demarcated radiodense calcium on imaging with surrounding inflammatory fluid signal on MRI, along with the marked functional limitation despite preserved passive range and true strength, is pathognomonic for this condition. The natural history is typically self-limited over weeks as the inflammatory phase resolves, making conservative management with NSAIDs, activity modification, and judicious physiotherapy the standard approach, though subacromial injection or rarely ultrasound-guided aspiration may accelerate symptom resolution in severe cases.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old man with 12-year diabetes history presents with progressive symmetrical distal sensory symptoms — numbness and burning pain in feet over the past 2 years, affecting sleep quality.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports loss of protective sensation in feet — recently slipped in shower because unable to sense hot water temperature. Symptoms worse at night, interfering with sleep. No upper limb involvement noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Poor glycemic control with HbA1c 8.5% throughout disease course. No prior neuropathy screening or intervention. Sedentary lifestyle and obesity (BMI 32). Recent addition of statin medication 6 months ago. Multiple vascular risk factors including hypertension and smoking history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Diminished vibratory sensation at great toe (tuning fork 128Hz lost after 3 seconds versus normal 10+ seconds). Monofilament testing shows loss of protective sensation in both feet using 10g monofilament. Ankle reflexes absent bilaterally. Mild weakness in ankle dorsiflexion 4/5. Proprioception decreased in lower extremities.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction study shows reduced amplitudes in sural sensory response bilaterally with prolonged distal latencies. Motor conduction of peroneal nerve shows reduced velocities (38 m/s). EMG demonstrates chronic denervation changes in foot intrinsic muscles. MRI excludes other spinal causes. Fasting glucose 185 mg/dL.

puzzles.explanation: Peripheral neuropathy in diabetes results from chronic hyperglycemia causing glycosylation of nerve proteins, myelin damage, and microvascular ischemia — a process accelerated by poor glycemic control and additional metabolic stressors like dyslipidemia and hypertension. Distal symmetrical sensory neuropathy is the most common form, characteristically affecting the feet first and progressing proximally, with patients losing protective sensation and experiencing nocturnal burning pain that disrupts sleep quality. The loss of vibratory sense, absent reflexes, and denervation changes on EMG reflect axonal degeneration affecting both large myelinated fibres (responsible for proprioception and vibration) and small unmyelinated C-fibres (pain and temperature). Early screening and aggressive glycemic management are critical, as this patient's prolonged poor control and sedentary lifestyle have allowed significant irreversible nerve damage — evidenced by reduced conduction velocities and chronic denervation — substantially increasing his risk of foot ulceration and amputation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 5-month postpartum woman reports persistent midline abdominal wall separation and weakness. Functional limitation with daily activities. Concerned about long-term cosmetic outcome.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 5-month postpartum woman reports persistent abdominal weakness and difficulty with functional activities. Notices visible bulging along midline when exerting herself.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cannot return to pre-pregnancy exercise intensity. Reports pain and bulging with coughing, sneezing, and straining. Difficulty maintaining upright posture for prolonged periods.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible abdominal bulging with contraction. Palpable separation 3.5 fingers wide at umbilicus. Reduced abdominal wall tension. Rectus contraction shows lateral bulging. No signs of hernia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates diastasis rectus measuring 4.2 cm interrectal distance with thin linea alba. No fascial dehiscence. Cross-sectional imaging confirms bilateral muscle separation.

puzzles.explanation: Diastasis rectus abdominis is a functional separation of the rectus abdominis muscles along the linea alba, most commonly occurring during pregnancy as hormonal relaxation (relaxin) and mechanical stretching accommodate the gravid uterus. The condition persists postpartum when the linea alba remains thin and lax, preventing adequate force closure of the abdominal wall — as evidenced by the visible bulging with contraction and the 4.2 cm interrectal distance on imaging. The characteristic midline bulging with exertion, weakness with functional activities, and difficulty returning to exercise intensity reflect impaired load distribution and neuromuscular control rather than a true hernia. Recovery depends on restoration of linea alba integrity and coordinated recruitment of the deep core musculature, which typically requires targeted rehabilitation over 6–12 months postpartum.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational runner presents with buttock pain for 8 weeks that worsens during runs over 5km. Pain localized to lower buttock region near ischial tuberosity. Recent increase in training volume and intensity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worse during running, especially hills and sprinting. Sitting on hard surfaces increases discomfort (describing difficulty with office chair use). Pain radiates to posterior thigh but does not extend below knee. Morning stiffness in buttock lasting 10-15 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 8 weeks correlating with increased running mileage (40 to 55 miles per week). No acute traumatic event. Prior history of lower back tightness and hip flexor tightness. Weak gluteal activation noted. Running on cambered surfaces recently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness over ischial tuberosity region with pain on hamstring contraction against resistance. Positive modified Puranen-Orava test (buttock pain with hip flexion and knee extension). Pain with straight leg raise 70 degrees. Slump test negative (radicular component excluded). Hip internal rotation limited compared to external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows increased T2 signal in proximal hamstring tendons at ischial attachment with tendon thickening (8mm versus normal 5mm). No full-thickness tear visualized. Associated inflammation around ischial tuberosity. Ultrasound confirms hypoechoic tendon with loss of normal fibrillar pattern and increased vascularity on Doppler imaging at attachment point.

puzzles.explanation: Proximal hamstring tendinopathy results from cumulative microtrauma to the common tendon insertion at the ischial tuberosity, typically triggered by excessive running volume, hill training, or biomechanical factors like weak gluteal activation and hip internal rotation restriction. The condition classically presents with localized buttock pain at the ischial tuberosity that worsens with running, hill sprinting, and prolonged sitting—a constellation that reflects the tendon's vulnerability to both compressive loading (sitting) and eccentric lengthening (running downhill or accelerating). Imaging characteristically shows tendon thickening, increased T2 signal, and hyperemia on Doppler, confirming reactive tendinopathy rather than acute rupture. The positive Puranen-Orava test (pain with combined hip flexion and knee extension) and pain-free slump test help distinguish this from neural or discogenic pathology, making it a mechanical diagnosis responsive to load management and motor control rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Dyspnea and fatigue

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worse with exertion and lying flat; improves with sitting upright

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hypertension × 15 years, poorly controlled; recent MI 6 months ago

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: JVD, crackles at lung bases, S3 gallop, peripheral edema, hepatomegaly

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ejection fraction 28%, dilated ventricles, pulmonary edema on CXR

puzzles.explanation: Heart failure results from the heart's inability to pump sufficient blood to meet the body's metabolic demands, most commonly from left ventricular dysfunction following myocardial infarction or chronic hypertension. The classic presentation of dyspnea worse when supine reflects pulmonary congestion from elevated left atrial pressures, while sitting upright improves symptoms by reducing venous return and redistributing fluid away from the lungs. The examination findings here—elevated JVD, basilar crackles, an S3 gallop, and hepatomegaly—comprise the hallmark signs of biventricular failure with systemic and pulmonary congestion. The markedly reduced ejection fraction of 28% with dilated ventricles confirms systolic dysfunction, distinguishing this from restrictive or diastolic patterns and explaining why exercise tolerance plummets as cardiac output cannot increase to meet demands.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 75-year-old osteoporotic woman falls from standing height onto outstretched arm. Immediate severe shoulder pain and inability to move arm. Right shoulder swelling and deformity noted at scene.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot attempt any active shoulder movement. Rapid swelling extends into the upper arm over minutes. Pain severity out of proportion to typical soft tissue injury alone. Severe guarding prevents passive range of motion testing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known osteoporosis with prior fragility fractures. Low-energy mechanism (ground-level fall). No prior shoulder surgery. Immediate symptom onset with no prior warning or prodrome.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder deformity with loss of normal contour. Crepitus palpable at fracture site. Axillary nerve testing shows sensory loss to lateral shoulder and difficulty with deltoid contraction (0/5). Radial pulse intact and capillary refill normal. Sensation intact in radial and ulnar distributions but diminished axillary.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows 4-part proximal humerus fracture with displacement. Greater tuberosity fragment displaced 1.2cm. Humeral head retropulsion present. CT scan confirms comminution with axillary nerve involvement. Angiography normal excluding vascular injury but confirms soft tissue trauma with hematoma.

puzzles.explanation: Proximal humerus fractures occur when trauma disrupts the metaphyseal region of the humerus, with elderly osteoporotic patients at particular risk from low-energy falls onto an outstretched arm. This 4-part fracture pattern—involving the surgical neck, greater tuberosity, and lesser tuberosity—results in significant displacement and fragmentation, making it one of the most severe and unstable presentations. The axillary nerve vulnerability in proximal fractures is a critical complication here, evidenced by the sensory loss over the lateral shoulder and deltoid paralysis despite intact vascular status; this nerve runs posteriorly through the axillary space immediately adjacent to the fracture site. The rapid swelling, visible deformity, and severe guarding reflect the combined insult of bony disruption, soft tissue trauma, and hematoma formation—a teaching reminder that comminuted proximal fractures demand urgent imaging and careful neurovascular assessment to guide surgical versus conservative management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old woman presents with diffuse pain for 5 years affecting multiple body regions. Pain described as burning and aching with atypical features including disproportionate severity and non-mechanical quality.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports light touch causes significant pain (allodynia). Sleep severely disrupted with non-restorative pattern. Symptoms worsen with stress and weather changes. Concurrent memory and concentration difficulties reported.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual insidious onset 5 years ago following minor neck strain. No significant trauma or incident. Multiple negative investigations over years. History of anxiety and depression. Recent major life stressors and significant emotional distress. Pain catastrophizing evident in interview.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows widespread tender points (11+ of 18 tender points positive). Allodynia confirmed to light touch across multiple body areas. No neurological deficits, no synovitis, no muscle atrophy. Objective findings notably disproportionate to severity of pain complaints.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Laboratory studies all normal including inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, and thyroid function. MRI cervical and lumbar spine normal without disc herniation or stenosis. Sleep study shows non-restorative sleep pattern (reduced deep sleep stages). Quantitative sensory testing shows lowered pain thresholds throughout body (wind-up amplification confirmed).

puzzles.explanation: Central sensitisation syndrome (CSS) is a state of amplified nociceptive processing in the central nervous system, where the brain and spinal cord become hyper-responsive to pain signals, leading to widespread pain that far exceeds what peripheral tissue damage would predict. It typically emerges insidiously following minor injury or emotional stress in predisposed individuals, particularly those with concurrent anxiety and depression, and is characterised by allodynia (pain to normally non-painful stimuli), non-restorative sleep, cognitive dysfunction, and a striking mismatch between subjective pain severity and objective physical findings. The normal laboratory and imaging results, combined with reduced pain thresholds on quantitative sensory testing and evidence of wind-up amplification, distinguish CSS from primary inflammatory or structural pathology. Recognition of this maladaptive pain processing state is crucial, as it reframes treatment toward neuromodulation, cognitive-behavioural approaches, and graded activity rather than investigations or tissue-focused interventions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old man presenting 8 weeks after major surgery. Reports pain at surgical site, unusual cramping and pressure sensations in the affected area, and significant difficulty with movement and positioning. Expresses frustration about loss of independence and uncertainty about recovery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to move independently from bed to chair or perform transfers without substantial assistance. Severe loss of balance and stability when upright or seated without upper extremity support. Reluctant to participate in prescribed exercises or mobility training. Reports fear of falling and moving the surgical area.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing Type 2 diabetes with poor glycemic control and chronic nerve damage from diabetes. Underwent emergency surgery 8 weeks ago for severe infection that did not respond to conservative treatment. Lives alone with limited family involvement in rehabilitation. Sedentary lifestyle prior to surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Surgical site shows firm scar without drainage, erythema, or dehiscence. Significant swelling in surgical region requiring compression garment. Hip muscles on affected side measure 3+/5 strength. Trunk stability fair; requires hand support for sitting balance. Transfer assessment shows moderate dependence. Mood assessment indicates depression (PHQ-9 = 18). Sensation intact in residual region.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiograph of surgical site shows solid bone fusion at operative level without prominent bony overgrowth. Ultrasound confirms normal scar tissue maturation and appropriate tissue planes. Vascular study shows adequate blood perfusion to surgical site (ankle-brachial index 0.7). Rehabilitation imaging assessment clears patient for prosthetic fitting with microprocessor-assisted device recommended for gait safety.

puzzles.explanation: Post-amputation rehabilitation is the intensive, multifaceted recovery process following limb loss, requiring not only tissue healing and scar maturation but also neuromuscular re-education, prosthetic fitting, and psychological adjustment to profound functional change. The 8-week timeframe here represents a critical window where surgical healing is complete enough for prosthetic training, yet the patient faces compounding challenges: weak hip musculature (3+/5) needed for prosthetic ambulation, poor trunk stability limiting transfer capacity, and significant depression (PHQ-9 = 18) that commonly co-occurs with sudden disability and identity loss. Pre-existing diabetes with neuropathy and a sedentary baseline further complicate the motor learning and cardiovascular demands of prosthetic use. Success depends on addressing not just the biomechanics of the residual limb—its edema control, scar mobility, and strength progression—but equally on managing pain perception, fall anxiety, and depression that often become the true barriers to functional independence in this population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old collegiate soccer player reports acute anterior knee pain during game when planting foot during pivot maneuver. Severe pain with sensation of something tearing in anterior knee. Immediate swelling and loss of function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe anterior knee pain with any attempt at active knee extension. Unable to perform straight leg raise. Rapid swelling developed within 30 minutes of injury. Guarding of entire knee joint.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young athletic male with high-demand sport (soccer). Acute traumatic mechanism during cutting maneuver. No prior knee surgery or injury. Recent growth spurt (18 years old) with some residual cartilage sensitivity. Full participation in sport up to injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Complete loss of active knee extension strength (0/5 against gravity). Gross anterior knee swelling with visible hematoma localised to tibial tubercle region. Severe point tenderness over tuberosity. Knee effusion present. Quadriceps muscle palpable but unable to generate contraction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP, lateral, and Merchant views) shows avulsion of tibial tuberosity fragment with 1.5cm displacement. CT confirms fracture extends into articular surface of tibial plateau. MRI shows complete disruption of patellar tendon at bone insertion with severe edema. Associated bone marrow edema in proximal tibia. Soft tissue trauma with hematoma extensive.

puzzles.explanation: A tibial tuberosity avulsion fracture occurs when the patellar tendon's insertion point on the proximal tibia is forcefully avulsed, typically during powerful knee extension against resistance—in this case, the deceleration required for a cutting maneuver in a young athlete. The growth plate region at the tuberosity remains a structural weak point in skeletally immature athletes, making this injury most common in males aged 15–25 during high-demand sports. The complete loss of active knee extension and inability to perform a straight leg raise reflect the mechanical failure of the extensor mechanism itself, while the rapid hemarthrosis and localized hematoma over the tibial tuberosity are hallmark findings. Surgical reattachment is typically required to restore both bone anatomy and patellar tendon continuity, making prompt recognition and imaging essential for optimal functional outcome.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old man presents with deep buttock pain with walking for 3 months. Pain described as sharp and localized deep in buttock. Difficulty with prolonged ambulation (limited walking distance to 500 meters).

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Deep buttock pain worse with walking, particularly during hip adduction phase of gait. Pain relieved by sitting and lying down. Difficulty transitioning from sitting to standing. Pain not radiating down leg (no sciatica pattern). Groin pain sometimes accompanying buttock discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual insidious onset over 3 months. No significant trauma reported. Occupational sitting 8 hours daily. Hip tightness with limitation in internal rotation. History of groin strain 2 years prior to current symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pain with hip adduction across midline against resistance. Ischiofemoral stretch test reproduces deep buttock discomfort. Limited hip internal rotation (25 degrees versus 45 degrees opposite side). Tenderness with deep palpation in infrapiriformis region. Hamstring muscle tenderness present. Gait antalgic with reduced weight bearing phase.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI pelvis shows narrowing of ischiofemoral space measuring 18mm (normal >24mm) with adjacent quadratus femoris muscle edema. Hamstring origin tendinopathy visualized at ischial tuberosity. Gadolinium-enhanced imaging confirms inflammation in deep hip musculature. No femoroacetabular impingement or labral pathology identified.

puzzles.explanation: Ischiofemoral impingement occurs when the space between the ischium and the lesser trochanter narrows, compressing the quadratus femoris muscle and adjacent soft tissues—a less common but clinically significant cause of deep buttock pain that differs from the more familiar femoroacetabular impingement or sciatic compression. The condition typically develops insidiously in middle-aged adults, often with occupational sitting and hip tightness that restricts internal rotation, allowing the femur to migrate posteriorly and further encroach the already compromised ischiofemoral space. The deep, localized buttock pain with hip adduction during gait, relief in sitting, and absence of true sciatica—alongside MRI findings of narrowed ischiofemoral space with quadratus femoris edema—make this diagnosis distinctive and distinguish it from piriformis syndrome or hamstring origin issues that may coexist. Restoration of hip internal rotation mobility and functional neuromuscular control are central to conservative management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 7-year-old boy. Parents report he seems clumsy and accident-prone compared to classmates. Struggles with tasks other children his age do easily. No significant medical history, no recent trauma or illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with handwriting and drawing — letters poorly formed, inconsistent spacing, takes longer than peers. Also struggles with self-care: buttoning shirts, tying shoelaces. PE class is a challenge: cannot skip or hop on one leg reliably, running appears awkward.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Difficulties noticed since early preschool and have persisted despite normal development otherwise. No significant delays in speech or cognitive ability. Full-term uncomplicated birth. No history of seizures, head injury, or neurological illness. Skills plateau rather than improve with practice.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tandem stance: loses balance in <10 seconds. Single-leg hop: poor control and coordination. Gait: slightly asymmetric arm swing, reduced dynamic stability. Nine-hole peg test: 2.5 minutes (normal ~1 minute for age). Pencil grip awkward and fatigues quickly. Standard neurological exam: normal strength, reflexes, sensation throughout. No focal weakness or spasticity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Movement Assessment Battery for Children: scores <5th percentile in both gross and fine motor domains. Brain MRI: normal, no structural lesions or white matter abnormality. Cognitive and language testing: within normal limits. Hearing normal. Pattern indicates generalized motor control deficit without neurological or structural explanation.

puzzles.explanation: Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD) is a neurodevelopmental condition characterized by significant difficulty acquiring and executing coordinated motor skills, without identifiable neurological lesion or cognitive impairment. The disorder reflects a fundamental deficit in motor planning and execution — the ability to organize, sequence, and refine movement — such that everyday tasks like handwriting, buttoning, and running remain persistently clumsy and effortful despite normal intelligence and typical neurological findings on examination. Children with DCD typically show marked difficulty with both fine motor control (evident in the awkward pencil grip and poor hand dexterity on the peg test) and gross motor coordination (poor balance, asymmetric gait, inability to skip or hop), with skills plateauing rather than improving with practice as would be expected in typical development. Early identification and motor-specific physiotherapy intervention focusing on task-specific training and adaptive strategies are key, as these children often benefit from explicit instruction in movement patterns that peers acquire intuitively.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman on long-term corticosteroid therapy for rheumatoid arthritis falls from standing height onto her left side. Acute severe left hip pain with inability to bear weight. Left leg held in external rotation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain with any hip movement. Cannot transfer bed-to-chair or ambulate. Left leg adopts external rotation and flexion posture. Marked swelling in proximal thigh with visible deformity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prolonged corticosteroid use (15 years) for autoimmune disease causing osteoporosis. Low-energy mechanism (standing fall) concerning for pathological fracture. History of previous right hip fracture 8 years ago. Female postmenopausal age 62. History of insufficient vitamin D.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip shortened 2cm compared to contralateral side. Leg externally rotated and flexed. Severe tenderness over greater trochanteric region and proximal femur. Hip abduction strength impossible to test due to pain. Groin pain with passive internal rotation. Neurovascular examination intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows transverse subtrochanteric fracture with lateral comminution. Fracture line extends from medial aspect at junction of femoral neck to lateral cortex. Medial cortex disruption with 30-degree valgus angulation. CT confirms comminuted fracture pattern with significant soft tissue trauma. DEXA T-score -3.5 confirming osteoporosis.

puzzles.explanation: A subtrochanteric fracture occurs in the proximal femur just below the greater trochanter, a region subjected to intense compressive and tensile forces during hip motion and weight-bearing. This patient's fracture resulted from a low-energy fall combined with severe osteoporosis induced by 15 years of corticosteroid therapy—a classic pathological fracture scenario. The characteristic external rotation and flexion posture, limb shortening, and inability to bear weight reflect the biomechanical displacement caused by the deforming forces of the hip musculature (particularly the iliopsoas and external rotators) acting on an unstable fracture. Subtrochanteric fractures carry high nonunion risk due to poor vascularity at this level and the difficulty in maintaining reduction against these powerful deforming forces, making early surgical stabilization essential for recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 60-year-old patient with a 10-year history of intermittent neck pain now reports progressive tingling and weakness in both arms over the past 6 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot work at a desk for more than 30 minutes without symptom escalation. Activities requiring overhead reaching and downward gaze (reading, cooking) trigger acute worsening. Symptoms do not improve with rest alone.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports morning stiffness lasting 45 minutes. No prior trauma or neck injury. No imaging performed previously. Symptoms gradually worsened despite two courses of physiotherapy (6 months apart). Intermittent episodes of grip weakness and difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning, writing).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Neck flexion limited to 30°. Bilateral hand intrinsic weakness (grip strength 3+/5 bilaterally). Brisk reflexes in upper extremities (3+). Positive Lhermitte sign (electric shock down spine with neck flexion). Reduced finger dexterity noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine shows osteophyte-mediated stenosis at C4–C6 with canal diameter reduced to 8 mm (normal >13 mm). T2 hyperintense signal within spinal cord at C5 level. Cord compression ratio >60% with flattening evident and sustained clonus reproducible on examination.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical spondylosis is a degenerative condition of the cervical spine characterized by osteophyte formation, disc degeneration, and progressive stenosis that can compress the spinal cord and nerve roots. The progressive bilateral arm weakness, morning stiffness, and functional decline over months reflect myelopathic progression — the spinal cord compression evident on MRI with T2 hyperintensity indicating cord edema and ischaemic change. The positive Lhermitte sign, brisk reflexes, and clonus are hallmark upper motor neuron findings that distinguish myelopathy from radiculopathy and signal the need for urgent intervention to prevent irreversible cord damage. Unlike isolated neck pain or radiculopathy that may respond to conservative management, the combination of progressive grip weakness, fine motor dysfunction, and imaging showing >60% cord compression ratio indicates this patient has crossed the threshold where surgical decompression becomes the standard of care.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior episodes of vertigo. Patient denies hearing changes or tinnitus. No recent head trauma, otologic procedures, or autoimmune history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI inner ear structures and cerebellopontine angle normal, excluding vestibular schwannoma or brainstem lesion. No abnormal cranial nerve enhancement. Differential diagnosis now narrows to viral-inflammatory vestibular nerve pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular neuritis is a sudden-onset peripheral vestibular disorder caused by viral inflammation of the vestibular nerve, typically following an upper respiratory infection. The condition presents with constant, severe vertigo and postural instability — often worst within the first 24–48 hours — alongside prominent nausea and vomiting, and notably spares hearing because only the vestibular division of CN VIII is affected. The positive head impulse test with catch-up saccades and reduced caloric response demonstrate unilateral peripheral vestibular hypofunction, while normal imaging excludes central causes like brainstem stroke or tumour. Recovery is generally favourable over weeks to months as central vestibular compensation occurs, though some patients experience persistent motion sensitivity or recurrent episodes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old man presents 1 day after catching ring finger in a door. Reports sharp pain at the tip of the finger and swelling over the dorsal aspect of the distal phalanx. Finger rests in slight flexion at the tip. No numbness, tingling, or pain elsewhere in hand.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to straighten the fingertip (DIP joint) despite attempting active extension. Can flex the finger normally. Gripping and fine motor tasks are awkward due to the drooping posture of the affected fingertip.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute mechanism: finger caught in door during closing, causing sudden forced flexion at the tip. No prior hand injuries. No systemic arthritis, inflammatory conditions, or neurological history. Injury occurred 24 hours ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: DIP joint held in approximately 20–30 degrees flexion at rest. Active extension of DIP joint absent or severely limited. Passive extension possible but patient reports pain. Grip strength intact. Sensation and capillary refill normal. No lacerations to skin. Proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint and metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joint motion normal and painless.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of finger show small bony avulsion fragment from the dorsal base of the distal phalanx. No fracture of the phalanges themselves. No joint subluxation. MRI or ultrasound confirms disruption of the extensor tendon insertion at the dorsal DIP joint with retraction.

puzzles.explanation: Mallet finger is a loss of active extension at the distal interphalangeal joint caused by disruption of the extensor digitorum tendon at its insertion onto the dorsal base of the distal phalanx. The injury typically results from a sudden forced flexion mechanism — such as a finger caught in a door — that avulses the tendon insertion, sometimes tearing away a small fragment of bone with it. The resulting clinical picture is pathognomonic: the fingertip rests in slight flexion and cannot be actively extended, despite intact sensation, motor power in the flexors, and normal motion at more proximal joints. Treatment depends on whether the injury is purely tendinous or involves a bony avulsion with significant intra-articular involvement, but early immobilization of the DIP joint in extension is the foundation of management in both cases.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old man presents with acute-onset painful swelling in knees and ankles 2 weeks after an acute diarrheal illness. Reports fever (101°F), fatigue, and malaise. Morning stiffness lasts approximately 2 hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk comfortably due to knee and ankle pain. Also experiencing dysuria and penile discharge. Complains of eye redness and discomfort bilaterally.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute diarrhea 2 weeks prior to joint symptoms (self-limited 3-day illness with bloody diarrhea). Recent unprotected sexual contact 4 weeks ago with new partner. Family history negative for autoimmune disease. No prior joint problems. Previously healthy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination: warmth and effusion in knees and ankles; asymmetric polyarthritis noted. Urethral discharge visualized. Bilateral conjunctivitis with mild mucoid discharge. Distinctive psoriasiform papules (keratoderma blennorrhagicum) present on palms and soles. Shallow oral ulcers noted on palate.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR elevated at 68 mm/hr, CRP 12 mg/dL (elevated). Rheumatoid factor negative. Anti-CCP negative. HLA-B27 positive. Stool culture positive for Shigella flexneri. Serology positive for chlamydial antibodies (microimmunofluorescence). Synovial fluid shows inflammatory non-septic pattern (WBC 8,000, PMN-predominant).

puzzles.explanation: Reactive arthritis is an acute, sterile inflammatory polyarthritis that occurs 1–4 weeks following infection with enteric pathogens (such as Shigella, Salmonella, or Yersinia) or sexually transmitted organisms (Chlamydia, Ureaplasma). The classic triad of arthritis, urethritis, and conjunctivitis—often accompanied by characteristic skin lesions like keratoderma blennorrhagicum and oral ulcers—reflects a post-infectious immune dysregulation rather than active infection within the joint. HLA-B27 positivity is strongly associated with this condition and predicts more severe or recurrent disease. The asymmetric, predominantly lower-limb involvement with elevated inflammatory markers but negative RF/anti-CCP (distinguishing it from rheumatoid arthritis) and non-septic synovial fluid confirms the diagnosis and suggests the immune system is cross-reacting against joint tissue following the triggering infection.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old man with Type 2 diabetes reports sudden-onset triggering sensation in fingers bilaterally, with difficulty extending the digits in the morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional impact worsens with repetitive gripping and activities requiring fine motor control. Significant functional limitation by evening, though symptoms partially resolve overnight.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Diabetes diagnosed 8 years ago, poorly controlled (HbA1c 8.5%). No prior hand pathology. Symptoms began 1 month ago with rapid bilateral involvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nodular thickening palpable at A1 pulley level bilaterally. Triggering phenomenon reproducible with grasping. Pain with triggering event. Reduced grip strength secondary to pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound bilateral index and middle fingers shows flexor tendon nodular thickening and pulley hypertrophy. Inflammatory changes around tendon sheath. No tendon tearing.

puzzles.explanation: Trigger finger (stenosing tenosynovitis) occurs when nodular thickening of the flexor tendon catches against the A1 pulley during extension, creating a mechanical block that forces sudden, jerky extension with a characteristic "triggering" sensation. The condition is particularly common in diabetes due to glycation of tendon and pulley collagen, reducing tissue elasticity and promoting inflammatory changes — explaining the rapid bilateral onset and pulley hypertrophy seen on ultrasound in this poorly controlled diabetic patient. Morning stiffness and symptom exacerbation with repetitive gripping are hallmark features, as the tendon swelling worsens throughout the day with loading and partially resolves after rest. Conservative management focuses on activity modification, NSAIDs, and corticosteroid injection into the tendon sheath to reduce inflammation; improved glycemic control is essential to prevent progression and bilateral involvement in diabetic patients.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old rugby player sustained an acute injury to the ring finger during a tackle. Sharp pain and sudden inability to flex the ring finger. Swelling developed rapidly in the forearm within minutes of injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Ring finger held in fixed extension. Unable to make a fist with the ring finger. PIP joint can bend slightly but with significant pain and weakness. Pain worse with any attempt to grip or squeeze.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic mechanism with finger caught in jersey with sudden traction injury. Injury occurred 2 hours ago. Sport involves high-contact mechanism. Young athletic male with full mobility demands. No prior finger trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ring finger held in extension at DIP joint with inability to actively flex (0/5 DIP flexion). PIP flexion intact at 4/5 strength. Mild swelling noted in volar forearm proximal to wrist. Tender palm with fullness along flexor digitorum profundus tendon region. No sensory loss or vascular compromise. No wound or skin disruption.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows no bone avulsion at distal phalanx base (distinguishes from bony mallet). MRI demonstrates complete rupture of flexor digitorum profundus tendon at level of wrist crease. Tendon retracted proximally with gap of 3cm. Associated edema in forearm soft tissues. Ultrasound confirms tendon discontinuity with fluid collection (hematoma) tracking proximally in forearm.

puzzles.explanation: Jersey finger is a complete rupture of the flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) tendon, typically occurring when a player's finger is forcibly extended while gripping—such as being caught in an opponent's jersey during a tackle. The injury causes immediate loss of active distal interphalangeal (DIP) flexion while proximal interphalangeal motion remains relatively intact, as the superficialis tendon is still functional. The retracted tendon and proximal hematoma tracking into the forearm are hallmark findings that demand urgent surgical repair within 7-10 days to restore hand grip function. This injury is common in contact sports and requires operative intervention; delayed presentation significantly worsens outcomes due to muscle atrophy and tendon degeneration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old reports acute onset of neck pain with numbness and weakness in one arm following a position change during lifting. Symptoms began 2 weeks ago and are affecting work capacity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain radiates distally in a linear pattern down the arm. Neck movements in certain directions provoke symptoms; brief rest and gentle traction provide temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has chronic neck stiffness from prior years of desk work. Current acute flare coincided with moving furniture. No prior imaging of the neck. Similar but milder episodes resolved within weeks previously.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, neck range of motion is limited in flexion and rotation. Dermatome-specific sensory changes are present. Motor weakness corresponds to the affected nerve root. Reflexes may be diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates intervertebral disc herniation or spondylotic changes compressing the nerve root at the appropriate spinal level. The compression level corresponds to clinical findings. Imaging confirms mechanical nerve root compression.
