puzzles.explanation: Cervical radiculopathy results from mechanical compression of a nerve root as it exits the cervical spine, most commonly from intervertebral disc herniation or osteophytic narrowing of the neural foramen. The condition presents with dermatomal pain radiating distally down the arm, accompanied by sensory changes and motor weakness specific to the compressed root — a pattern that precisely localizes the lesion. The acute onset following a lifting provocation, combined with the chronic degenerative backdrop of prolonged desk work, is typical; cervical spondylosis predisposes to radiculopathy when superimposed loading or positional strain triggers disc displacement. The temporary relief with neck traction reflects reduced foraminal pressure, though definitive management depends on imaging confirmation of the mechanical lesion and the severity of neurological deficit.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 54-year-old woman. Progressive hand weakness and clumsiness over several months. Gait becoming unsteady. Bilateral arm tingling and numbness in fingertips.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with fine motor tasks: buttoning clothes, writing, holding utensils. Walking requires more concentration; balance worsening. Symptoms not relieved by rest or medication alone.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 6 months. No single injury or trauma. MRI shows spinal cord compression from degenerative changes. Conservative treatment (physiotherapy, medications) attempted but symptoms continue to worsen. Surgical intervention recommended to prevent further neurological decline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Upper extremity strength 3+/5 bilaterally (hand grip weak). Hyperreflexia in arms and legs. Positive Hoffmann's sign. Gait broad-based and cautious. Two-point discrimination reduced in hands. Neck movement full but patient reports increased symptoms with extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine shows significant cord compression at C5-C6 level with T2 signal changes in spinal cord indicating myelopathy. Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) performed with allograft and plate fixation. Post-operative X-rays confirm proper alignment and hardware positioning. Follow-up MRI shows decompression achieved and cord signal normalizing.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical myelopathy from degenerative disc disease causes progressive spinal cord compression, leading to the characteristic constellation of bilateral upper extremity weakness, hyperreflexia, and gait disturbance seen in this patient. The T2 signal changes and cord compression at C5-C6 reflect demyelination and axonal injury from chronic mechanical pressure, explaining why conservative measures fail to halt neurological decline. Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) removes the offending disc material and restores disc space height, directly decompressing the cord and preventing irreversible myelopathic changes. The normalizing cord signal and functional recovery post-operatively underscore why surgical intervention is critical when imaging and clinical signs indicate active myelopathy — timing is essential to prevent permanent neurological loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with lateral hip pain for 4 weeks exacerbated by walking and lying on affected side. Pain localized to outer hip region. Affects sleep and daily walking tolerance limited to 1 block.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp lateral hip pain worse with walking, especially on stairs and uneven surfaces. Pain severe when lying on affected side at night. Difficulty crossing legs and internal rotation of hip increases discomfort. Pain improves with rest and ice.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset 6 weeks ago coinciding with new exercise program focused on walking/hill work. No traumatic event. Sedentary desk job. Prior episodes of lower back pain (resolved). Mild bilateral hip abductor weakness noted, worse on affected side. No previous hip injections or formal physio.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness directly over greater trochanter. Positive Trendelenburg test showing gluteal dysfunction. Limited hip abduction strength 3+/5 on affected side. Noble compression test reproduces lateral hip pain. Single-leg stance balance reduced. Lumbar spine examination normal (no radiation).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray hip shows no osteoarthritis or structural abnormality. MRI reveals gluteus medius tendinopathy with intrasubstance signal changes. Modest edema in greater trochanteric bursa. No labral pathology. Ultrasound demonstrates tendon thickening and hypoechoic degeneration with increased Doppler vascularity. Bursal thickening confirmed.

puzzles.explanation: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome (GTPS) arises from gluteus medius and minimus tendinopathy and bursitis at the greater trochanter, typically triggered by sudden increases in loading (like the new walking/hill program here) in individuals with underlying abductor weakness or motor control deficits. The condition is exacerbated by sustained hip adduction and internal rotation — positions that compress the inflamed bursa and stress the degenerated tendons — which explains why lying on the affected side and stair climbing provoke such sharp pain. The positive Trendelenburg sign and reduced single-leg stance reflect the gluteal dysfunction that both contributes to and perpetuates the overload at the greater trochanter. Imaging confirmation of tendinopathy with bursal thickening distinguishes true GTPS from other lateral hip presentations and guides a rehabilitation approach centred on progressive abductor strengthening and load management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old male reports gradually worsening difficulty understanding speech over 5 years. Family members note he frequently requests repetition and has increased television volume. Denies ear pain, drainage, fullness, or dizziness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Most difficulty in noisy environments—restaurants, group gatherings. One-on-one conversations in quiet settings manageable. Reports constant high-pitched sound in ears. Withdrawing from social activities due to communication difficulty.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Bilateral and symmetrical symptom onset. Four decades of occupational noise exposure (machinery) without hearing protection. No head trauma, ear infections, ototoxic medications, or prior ear surgery. Father had similar age-related changes. Significant smoking history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Otoscopy: clear tympanic membranes bilaterally, no cerumen or debris. Weber test: no lateralization. Rinne test: air conduction greater than bone conduction both ears (normal pattern). Whisper test reduced bilaterally, worse at higher frequencies.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Audiometry shows bilateral symmetrical sensorineural pattern: bone and air conduction thresholds equally elevated (50–60 dB) at frequencies >2 kHz. No air-bone gap. Tympanometry type A; acoustic reflexes intact. Speech discrimination 68% at conversational intensity.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related sensorineural hearing loss (presbycusis) results from progressive degeneration of cochlear hair cells and neural structures, typically affecting high frequencies first and progressing symmetrically over decades. This pattern is accelerated by chronic noise exposure and smoking, both of which damage the inner ear's sensory epithelium, and often runs in families due to genetic susceptibility to age-related change. The bilateral, symmetrical high-frequency loss with intact bone conduction (no air-bone gap) and normal middle ear function—as evidenced by normal Weber, Rinne, and tympanometry—definitively localizes the problem to the cochlea rather than conduction pathways. The particular difficulty in noisy environments reflects recruitment and poor speech discrimination at higher intensities, a hallmark of sensorineural loss where the ear struggles to process competing signals.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 32-year-old woman. Acute onset blurred vision in one eye with pain behind the eye. Also reports new weakness in both legs and unusual fatigue over past 3 weeks. Symptoms came on suddenly without clear trigger.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot climb stairs without holding rail. Frequent tripping and falls. Difficulty walking distances >100m before legs feel heavy and weak. Vision problems make balance worse. Reports pins-and-needles in feet.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Similar episode 18 months ago—sudden arm numbness lasting 6 weeks, then mostly resolved. Last year brief period of double vision that spontaneously improved. No major illness or trauma. No relevant past medical history. No current medications.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visual acuity reduced right eye only. Lower limb strength 4/5 bilaterally with increased muscle tone. Brisk reflexes and ankle clonus present. Positive Babinski sign both feet. Gait shows weakness and imbalance. Mild dysmetria on finger-to-nose test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI: multiple white matter lesions around ventricles and in deep brain, some with enhancement. Spinal cord MRI: focal lesion at cervical level. Contrast-enhanced sequences show active inflammation in 2-3 lesions. Pattern consistent with demyelinating disease affecting brain and spinal cord.

puzzles.explanation: Multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune demyelinating disease in which the immune system attacks the myelin sheaths of axons in the central nervous system, disrupting nerve conduction and causing neurological dysfunction. The hallmark clinical feature is a relapsing-remitting pattern of distinct neurological episodes separated in time and anatomical location — here evident in the previous episodes of optic neuritis and transient diplopia, now presenting with acute optic neuritis in the contralateral eye alongside spinal cord and brainstem involvement causing bilateral leg weakness and cerebellar signs. The MRI findings of multiple periventricular and juxtacortical white matter lesions with active enhancement, combined with spinal cord demyelination, fulfill the radiological criteria for dissemination in space and time. The acute presentation, female gender, young adult age, and documented history of recurrent demyelinating events are all classic for MS, making early immunomodulatory therapy critical to slow disease progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old office worker presents with progressive hand clumsiness and unsteady gait over 6 months. Reports difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning. Wife noted increased stumbling and careful walking pattern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dexterity impaired more than strength in hands. Gait described as wide-based and cautious with reduced arm swing. Patient reports difficulty with stairs and uneven surfaces. Symptom progression noted with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long history of cervical spine stiffness. Previous minor car accident 5 years ago. Gradual symptom progression over months suggesting chronic cord compression. No acute trauma. Smoking and age as risk factors.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lhermitte sign positive on forward neck flexion. Bilateral hyperreflexia with Hoffman's reflex present and pathologic. Lower extremity hyperreflexia with spasticity. Gait shows bilateral stiffness pattern. Weakness mild to moderate in hand intrinsics with loss of fine coordination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine shows multilevel cervical spondylosis with cord compression at C5-6 and C6-7 levels. T2-weighted imaging demonstrates myelopathic changes with central cord signal hyperintensity. Sagittal diameter reduced to 10mm at most compressed level. No acute fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical myelopathy results from chronic spinal cord compression, most commonly due to spondylotic changes—disc herniation, osteophytes, and ligamentous hypertrophy—that progressively narrow the cervical canal. The condition typically presents insidiously in middle-aged and older adults with multilevel involvement, causing a characteristic pattern of hand dysfunction disproportionate to weakness, combined with gait ataxia and hyperreflexia reflecting upper motor neuron involvement. The progressive hand clumsiness with preserved grip strength, positive Lhermitte sign on neck flexion, and bilateral hyperreflexia with myelopathic signal changes on MRI are hallmark features that distinguish myelopathy from isolated radiculopathy. This patient's chronic spondylosis with cord compression below 10mm sagittal diameter and T2 signal hyperintensity represents established cord injury; early recognition and intervention are critical to prevent irreversible neurological damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old female recreational runner with anterior knee pain limiting running distance and intensity. Pain localizes to retropatellar region and periarticular knee. Worsens progressively with running volume increase.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform single-leg squat or step down without anterior knee pain. Pain worse with prolonged sitting (movie sign). Running pace and hill running both aggravate; pain resolves with reduced volume.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Increased running frequency without adequate recovery. Previous episode of knee pain 2 years prior resolved with training modification. Recurrent with current training increase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: VMO weakness confirmed on manual test. Knee valgus noted during single-leg squat. Pain with patellar compression during weightbearing activities. Q-angle elevated. No focal tendon tenderness; patellofemoral joint line tender to palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays normal; no OA. MRI normal; no cartilage damage, no tendinopathy, no ligamentous pathology. Diagnosis confirmed clinically — structural imaging excludes alternative causes.

puzzles.explanation: Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS) is a clinical diagnosis characterized by anterior or retropatellar knee pain arising from altered patellofemoral biomechanics rather than structural pathology — imaging is typically normal, ruling out cartilage damage, tendinopathy, or ligamentous injury. The pain mechanism involves increased patellofemoral joint reaction forces driven by proximal weakness (particularly vastus medialis obliquus deficiency) and dynamic knee valgus during loading tasks like running, squatting, and descending stairs. The prolonged sitting discomfort and progressive worsening with training volume increases are hallmark features reflecting cumulative microtrauma from repetitive loading in the context of inadequate neuromuscular control. Management focuses on restoring VMO strength and hip abductor function to normalize patellar tracking and reduce compression forces, alongside load management — which explains why previous episodes resolved with training modification and why current recurrence correlates with sudden volume escalation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged woman presents with intermittent hip pain following return to recreational dancing. The patient reports functional limitations with lower extremity mobility. Lifelong history of hip tightness and restricted mobility predates current symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports pain worsens with hip flexion combined with internal rotation and adduction. Struggles with lunging, pivoting movements, and crossing legs. Pain is predictable and reproducible with these combined movement planes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has had long-standing hip stiffness since adolescence but was previously asymptomatic. Returned to dancing 3 months ago after 2-year break. No previous imaging. No hip trauma or surgery history. Symptoms gradually worsened over first 4 weeks of renewed activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion ROM 110°, internal rotation 15° (restricted bilaterally, more so on symptomatic side). Pain reproduced at end-range when combining flexion with adduction and internal rotation. Hip flexor tightness noted in prone lying. Pain anterolateral hip region, not groin. No catch or giving way. Neurology intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates cam-type morphology at femoral head-neck junction with associated labral tear. No hip dysplasia. Mild synovitis present.

puzzles.explanation: Femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) occurs when abnormal bone morphology at the femoral head-neck junction (cam lesion) or acetabular rim (pincer lesion) mechanically restricts hip motion, leading to labral and cartilage damage with repeated end-range loading. The cam-type morphology seen here creates a focal prominence that encroaches on the joint space during combined flexion, internal rotation, and adduction — the very movements that provoked this patient's pain. Individuals with longstanding hip stiffness and restricted internal rotation are predisposed to symptomatic FAI when returning to dynamic activities; the labral tear reflects repetitive microtrauma from the impingement mechanism over those initial weeks of renewed dancing. Unlike capsular tightness alone, FAI presents with reproducible pain at specific combined movement endpoints and is defined by the structural morphology on imaging rather than mobility restrictions alone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old recreational volleyball player reports anterior knee pain for 6 weeks that worsens with jumping and explosive movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform repetitive jumping or spiking without pain. Eccentric loading during deceleration significantly aggravates symptoms. Pain settles quickly with rest but reliably returns when returning to sport participation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 6-week history correlating with increased training frequency (4x weekly volleyball). No acute injury event. Prior similar anterior knee discomfort 2 years ago (self-resolved). Recent decrease in hamstring and quadriceps flexibility noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness directly over proximal patellar tendon at superior patellar pole. Quadriceps contraction against resistance elicits pain. Single-leg squat shows valgus knee position (femoral internal rotation) suggesting biomechanical dysfunction. Knee flexion 0-120 degrees pain-free but 120-140 degrees aggravating. Palpable swelling at insertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic thickening of proximal patellar tendon (8mm versus normal 5-6mm) with loss of fibrillar echotexture. Increased Doppler signal indicating inflammation and neovascularity. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration without full-thickness tear. T2 signal abnormality within proximal third of tendon.

puzzles.explanation: Quadriceps tendinopathy is a chronic overuse condition affecting the proximal patellar tendon, typically arising from repetitive loading or sudden increases in training volume—as seen in this volleyball player's jump-based sport. The condition reflects microtrauma and failed tendon adaptation, with histological changes including collagen disorganization, increased ground substance, and neovascularization evident on ultrasound and MRI. The characteristic pattern here—pain with eccentric loading during deceleration, aggravation in the 120–140° knee flexion range (where tendon stress peaks), and exacerbation with explosive movements—reflects the tendon's vulnerability during the loading phase of jumping. Reduced hamstring and quadriceps flexibility coupled with valgus knee positioning during single-leg squat indicates biomechanical dysfunction that increases proximal patellar tendon strain, a modifiable risk factor central to both initial injury and recurrence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 26-year-old overhead athlete reports progressive shoulder weakness over 4 weeks. Difficulty with sustained throwing and pressing movements. No single traumatic event. Shoulder fatigue and loss of control during sport-specific activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain force during repetitive overhead reaching. Push-ups and bench press cause rapid fatigue and loss of stability. Symptoms worse with forward arm elevation and sustained weight-bearing through upper limbs. Able to perform daily activities but sport performance significantly impacted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training volume without adequate recovery. No acute injury, fall, or nerve-stretching mechanism. Previously asymptomatic this season. Trains 5–6 days weekly with minimal deload. No prior upper limb nerve conditions or cervical spine injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visibly prominent medial scapular border during push-up test—scapular winging evident. Weakness with forward reaching (3+/5 shoulder abduction). Scapular wall slide test positive with loss of scapular control. Thoracic kyphosis noted. Rotator cuff strength intact. No sensory deficits or hand symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Needle EMG shows motor unit action potentials with increased recruitment threshold in serratus anterior; minimal spontaneous activity. Long thoracic nerve conduction studies reveal reduced compound muscle action potential amplitude (50% contralateral) with normal conduction velocity. MRI shoulder normal; no structural tears. Imaging confirms nerve-level involvement without structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Serratus anterior injury results from long thoracic nerve neuropathy, which can develop insidiously from repetitive overhead loading, postural strain, or sudden increases in training volume — as seen in this overhead athlete without a single traumatic event. The muscle is essential for scapular upward rotation and protraction during forward elevation, so its dysfunction manifests as scapular winging (medial border prominence) and loss of stability during weight-bearing activities like push-ups and throwing. The reduced CMAP amplitude on nerve conduction studies with preserved conduction velocity, combined with EMG evidence of increased motor unit recruitment threshold and positive scapular wall slide testing, confirms a long thoracic nerve conduction block rather than structural damage. Conservative management emphasizing scapular stabilization, postural correction of the kyphosis, and graded return to overhead loading typically allows functional recovery, making early recognition critical to prevent chronic functional impairment in this athletic population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old male presenting with acute onset paralysis of both lower extremities and loss of sensation below the mid-torso following high-speed motor vehicle collision. Loss of consciousness at time of injury. Currently unable to move legs or feel touch/pain in lower body.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Complete inability to ambulate or move lower extremities voluntarily. Loss of bladder and bowel control with absent sensation in these regions. Requires urinary catheterization. Upper extremity function and strength fully preserved.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic injury from high-velocity blunt force to the torso during motor vehicle collision. Mechanism involved significant deceleration and impact. Onset was instantaneous at time of trauma with immediate loss of consciousness. No gradual progression; deficits present from moment of injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral lower extremities flaccid with 0/5 strength throughout hip, knee, and ankle. Deep tendon reflexes absent in lower extremities (areflexic). Sharp demarcation of sensory loss at approximately T8 level—preserved sensation above, absent below. Perianal sensation absent; anal wink reflex absent. Upper extremity motor and sensory function intact bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI spine shows cord signal abnormality with hemorrhage/edema spanning multiple levels in the mid-thoracic region. CT chest confirms blunt traumatic injury to thoracic vertebral bodies with evidence of fracture-dislocation. Imaging correlates with clinical sensory level and indicates direct traumatic injury to spinal cord parenchyma.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic spinal cord injury results from direct trauma to the cord parenchyma, typically from fracture-dislocation or penetrating injury that disrupts ascending and descending neural pathways. This patient's acute onset complete paraplegia with a sharp sensory level at T8—preserved sensation above, absent below—combined with absent lower extremity reflexes and loss of bladder/bowel control reflects a complete transection or severe contusion with hemorrhagic necrosis at the mid-thoracic level. The preservation of upper extremity function confirms that spinal cord injury is isolated below the cervical enlargement, while the flaccid paralysis in the acute phase reflects spinal shock with temporary loss of all reflex activity below the lesion. High-velocity blunt trauma with vertebral fracture-dislocation, as seen on imaging here, is the classic mechanism causing this devastating neurological pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old girl presents with knee pain and morning stiffness in both knees lasting 2 hours daily. Mother notes decreased activity compared to peers. Swelling in knees apparent after child wakes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to participate in normal play activities; limping gait that improves with activity and warms up. Difficulty with stairs and prolonged standing. Swelling noticeable to parents in both knees after rest periods.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset of symptoms 2 months ago insidious without trauma. Negative for recent infections or illness triggers. Family history negative for autoimmune disease. Otherwise healthy child with normal development. No rash or systemic symptoms reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows bilateral knee effusions with warmth and swelling. Knee flexion contractures bilateral measuring 15 degrees. Decreased knee active range of motion. Mild pain with passive range of motion. No joint asymmetry. Gait shows antalgic pattern with shortened step. Absence of rash and hepatosplenomegaly.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR elevated at 32 mm/hr, CRP 8 mg/dL. Antinuclear antibody positive (homogeneous pattern). Rheumatoid factor negative. Anti-CCP antibody negative. Knee X-rays show no structural damage early on. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with joint effusions. Complete blood count shows mild anemia (Hb 10.2).

puzzles.explanation: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) is a chronic autoimmune inflammation of the joints occurring in children under 16, with polyarticular onset affecting multiple joints symmetrically. The presentation here—bilateral knee involvement with morning stiffness exceeding one hour, improvement with activity, and contracture development—is classic for polyarticular JIA, which accounts for about 25–30% of cases and typically carries a more guarded prognosis than monoarticular forms. The positive ANA with negative rheumatoid factor is characteristic of JIA (unlike adult RA), and the elevated inflammatory markers combined with synovial hypertrophy on ultrasound confirm active synovitis; early structural damage is often absent initially, but flexion contractures develop quickly without aggressive anti-inflammatory management. This presentation demands prompt initiation of DMARDs and potentially biologics to prevent irreversible joint damage and functional decline during critical growth years.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old man woke this morning with inability to lift his wrist and weakness extending his fingers. Some tingling reported in the hand. No recent trauma or heavy lifting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot lift wrist against gravity. Completely unable to extend thumb at interphalangeal joint. Fingers cannot be extended at metacarpophalangeal joints to make a fist properly. No sensory deficit noted on self-examination.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began immediately upon waking. Patient recalls arm was draped over chair edge during sleep with pressure on the lateral upper arm. No prior episodes of nerve injury or arm weakness. No numbness or tingling before sleep.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist held in flexion with inability to dorsiflex (0/5 wrist extension). Thumb interphalangeal joint extension weak (1/5). Finger metacarpophalangeal extension weak (2/5) for all digits. Sensory loss in first dorsal web space only (posterior interosseous nerve sparing). Triceps and elbow extension strength intact. Sensation in remaining hand normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG/NCS shows reduced motor amplitude in posterior interosseous nerve branch with slowed conduction velocity. Sensory conduction normal in superficial radial sensory branch. Denervation pattern in extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis muscles. MRI shows nerve compression at level of spiral groove with surrounding edema. No fracture or other structural lesion.

puzzles.explanation: Radial nerve palsy results from compression or injury to the radial nerve as it traverses the spiral groove of the humerus, a vulnerable site where the nerve is tethered against bone. The nerve's motor branches innervate the wrist and finger extensors (via the posterior interosseous nerve) and supply sensation to the dorsal first web space, making wrist drop and finger extension weakness with minimal sensory involvement the pathognomonic presentation. Sleep-related compression palsy, as occurred here with the arm draped over the chair edge, is a classic mechanism — the nerve's superficial course at the spiral groove renders it susceptible to sustained external pressure. Electrodiagnostic findings demonstrating reduced motor amplitude in the posterior interosseous branch with denervation of the extensor carpi radialis muscles, combined with preserved sensory conduction and MRI evidence of localized compression with edema, confirm the diagnosis and localize the lesion precisely.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old distance runner reports lateral knee pain that develops during running. Symptoms onset gradually over recent weeks and resolve with rest days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp lateral knee pain elicited during running, particularly with downhill running and repetitive knee flexion-extension cycles. Pain increases as running distances increase. Resolves quickly with rest. Pain not present with walking or static stretching.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 3 weeks coinciding with increase in weekly mileage (30 to 50 miles) and switch to hillier running routes with cambered surfaces. Patient notes recent history of hip weakness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Ober test indicating ITB tightness and hip abduction contracture. Noble compression test reproduces lateral knee pain over ITB insertion. Iliotibial band palpable tenderness at knee level. Hip abduction strength weak (3/5). Glute med weakness with positive Trendelenburg sign. Gait shows excessive knee valgus on single leg stance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows thickened ITB with edema and signal changes at distal insertion point at knee. Inflammation at ITB-knee lateral compartment interface. No meniscal or ligamentous injury. Associated hip abductor muscle edema (gluteus medius and minimus). Ultrasound confirms ITB thickening with hypoechoic signal change and Doppler hyperemia.

puzzles.explanation: Iliotibial band syndrome results from repetitive friction of the thickened ITB across the lateral femoral epicondyle during knee flexion-extension, typically in runners exposed to sudden increases in mileage or cambered surfaces. The condition is fundamentally a proximal stability problem: weakness in hip abductors — particularly gluteus medius and minimus — forces the knee into valgus during terminal stance, increasing ITB tension and friction at its insertion. The sharp, activity-dependent lateral knee pain that emerges with running (especially downhill) but spares walking reflects the specific biomechanical loading pattern; the pain resolves with rest because friction ceases. Positive Ober test and Noble compression test, combined with weak hip abduction strength and MRI evidence of ITB thickening with hip abductor edema, confirm that proximal hip weakness drives the distal knee pathology — a classic example of why isolated knee treatment fails without addressing upstream motor control deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old male with 8 weeks of gradual-onset groin pain that worsens with activity and improves with rest. Pain began coinciding with increased physical demands at work. Describes anterior hip discomfort affecting functional mobility.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain predominantly with hip adduction (bringing legs together) and combined hip flexion-internal rotation. Worse with activities requiring forceful hip squeezing. Pain resolves with activity modification but patient cannot sustain prolonged standing or walking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 8 weeks with increased training frequency (3 sessions per week). Recent change in sport technique 2 months ago affecting weight transfer mechanics. Long history of activity participation without prior hip issues. Age-related tissue changes contributing. Limited hip adductor flexibility.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Focal tenderness at pubic bone near medial thigh. Hip adduction strength grossly reduced; hip flexion and internal rotation limited compared to contralateral. Abdominal strength normal. Antalgic gait with reduced hip extension on stance phase. Single-leg stance balance maintained. No neurovascular signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows adductor longus tendinopathy at proximal insertion with T2 signal hyperintensity. Pubic bone edema present at adductor origin. Associated labral fraying at anteroinferior labrum. Iliopsoas myotendinopathy present as secondary finding. Ultrasound confirms thickened adductor tendon with mixed echotexture and Doppler neovascularization.

puzzles.explanation: Golfer's hip—or adductor-related groin pain—arises from chronic overload of the adductor longus tendon at its proximal insertion on the pubic bone, typically triggered by increased training volume or altered mechanics that increase hip adduction and flexion-internal rotation demands. The condition commonly presents in middle-aged athletes with sudden increases in activity, and the focal tenderness at the pubic origin combined with reduced adduction strength and tendinopathic changes on imaging are hallmark features. Secondary pathology, including labral fraying and iliopsoas involvement, often coexists due to compensatory loading patterns and the hip's integrated kinetic chain. Activity modification, eccentric loading, and address of underlying flexibility deficits and technique errors remain the foundation of management, with most cases resolving within 6-12 weeks with appropriate load management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old retired man with 3 years of lower back pain. Pain intensity 4–5/10 at baseline, variable throughout the day. Reports frustration and worry that something serious has been missed despite multiple evaluations.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered by both prolonged sitting (>20 min) and prolonged standing (>15 min), but paradoxically improves with walking >30 minutes. Inconsistent relationship between activity duration and pain severity. Avoids bending due to fear of injury.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset 3 years ago without clear injury or event. Underwent multiple imaging studies and specialist consultations. Tried manual therapy, epidural injections, NSAIDs, and muscle relaxants with minimal sustained benefit. Reports significant sleep disruption and low mood. States pain behavior exceeds what functional testing would predict.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral hip flexor tightness; lumbar extension ROM reduced 20% but lateral flexion and rotation preserved. Weak transversus abdominis motor control (3+/5). Straight leg raise normal bilaterally. No dermatomal sensory loss, motor weakness, or reflex changes. Generalized paraspinal muscle guarding on palpation. Pain behavior inconsistent across repeated tests.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar MRI shows mild-to-moderate disc bulging at L4–L5 and L5–S1 without neural compression, bilateral facet arthrosis, and mild endplate changes. These findings are common in asymptomatic age-matched individuals and do not correlate with pain severity or functional limitation reported by patient.

puzzles.explanation: Non-specific low back pain (NSLBP) is mechanical low back pain without identifiable neurological, structural, or systemic pathology — a diagnosis of exclusion that accounts for 85–90% of all low back pain cases. The hallmark features here are the mismatch between mild structural findings (disc bulging and facet arthrosis are near-universal aging changes) and the patient's reported disability, combined with inconsistent pain provocation during examination and disproportionate pain behavior relative to objective findings. The multifactorial presentation — persistent symptoms despite multiple interventions, fear-avoidance driving functional loss, sleep disruption, and low mood — highlights how NSLBP is shaped by neurobiological sensitization, psychological factors, and maladaptive beliefs rather than peripheral pathology alone. Management shifts from imaging-chasing and passive interventions toward graded exercise, cognitive restructuring, and restoration of movement confidence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old trail runner presents with insidious onset lateral ankle and lower leg pain. Pain localized behind the lateral malleolus and along lateral foot border.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with trail running and inversion activities. Subsides with rest. Unable to continue training volume. Tightness along lateral ankle and foot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in running mileage by 30% over 3 weeks. Previous history of inversion ankle sprain 6 months ago with incomplete rehabilitation. Post-sprain residual lateral ankle instability noted initially but now resolved.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness over peroneal groove behind lateral malleolus. Pain with resisted foot eversion. Swelling along lateral ankle. Pain reproduced with single-leg stance on uneven surface.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates peroneal tendon thickening and hypoechogenicity over 1.5 cm length behind lateral malleolus. No subluxation noted. Mild fluid surrounding tendon. Increased Doppler signal indicating neovascularization within tendon sheath.

puzzles.explanation: Peroneal tendinopathy results from repetitive microtrauma to the peroneus longus and brevis tendons as they run through the peroneal groove behind the lateral malleolus, most commonly triggered by sudden increases in loading—particularly inversion and eversion demands in running. The tendons become inflamed and degenerative, with ultrasonic evidence of thickening, hypoechogenicity, and neovascularization reflecting the chronic irritation and failed healing response. Trail runners are especially vulnerable, particularly those with recent ankle sprains and subsequent instability that forces compensatory peroneal activation; in this case, the 30% mileage increase combined with residual proprioceptive deficits from the previous sprain created a perfect storm for tendon overload. Pain with resisted eversion and provocation on uneven surfaces are hallmark findings because the peroneal muscles must work harder to stabilize a laterally unstable ankle, making this a common post-sprain sequela when early rehabilitation is incomplete.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 22-year-old presents with sudden onset of foot and ankle pain. Reports swelling that developed over several hours. Difficulty weight-bearing on affected side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with inversion movements and walking on uneven surfaces. Able to ambulate with limp but reports instability sensation. Swelling localized to lateral aspect of ankle.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 days ago during pivoting movement in basketball. No prior ankle injuries. Immediate sharp pain at moment of injury with rapid swelling. No history of ankle instability or chronic pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness elicited along lateral ligamentous structures, particularly anterior aspect. Anterior drawer test demonstrates increased translation. Inversion stress test reproduces lateral ankle pain. Single-leg stance significantly compromised. Able to perform single-leg hop with difficulty.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing ankle radiographs rule out fracture or syndesmotic injury. MRI shows partial discontinuity in anterior talofibular ligament with surrounding edema and joint capsule involvement. No peroneal tendon pathology identified.

puzzles.explanation: Lateral ankle sprains result from inversion injuries that stretch or tear the lateral ligamentous complex, most commonly the anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL), which is the weakest of the three lateral stabilizers. The mechanism here—a pivoting movement in basketball—is classic for this injury, as the ankle is forced into inversion while bearing weight, disrupting the ligament's continuity. The rapid swelling, positive anterior drawer test, and reproduction of pain on inversion stress testing reflect the acute ligamentous damage and loss of restraint to anterolateral translation. Recovery typically focuses on early weight-bearing as tolerated, proprioceptive retraining, and peroneal strengthening to restore dynamic stability and prevent chronic ankle instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old woman post-hysterectomy reports urine loss with coughing, exercise, and straining. Small volume leakage with physical activities. Significant social limitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with high-impact activities (running, jumping, aerobics). Leakage during exercise predictable and reproducible. No nighttime symptoms or constant dribbling.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple vaginal deliveries prior. Hysterectomy 5 years prior. Menopause 3 years ago. Progressive incontinence worsening with activity. Previous bladder symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weak pelvic floor on assessment. Positive cough stress test. Bladder neck hypermobile on straining. No involuntary contractions. Positive stress incontinence sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Urodynamics shows stress incontinence with sphincter incompetence. Normal detrusor function. Bladder neck descent on cough. Urethral pressure profile weak.

puzzles.explanation: Stress urinary incontinence results from inadequate urethral closure pressure during activities that increase intra-abdominal pressure—a structural and functional problem rather than a neurogenic one. The condition arises from pelvic floor weakness and bladder neck hypermobility, often following childbirth trauma, hormonal changes, or pelvic surgery; in this case, multiple vaginal deliveries combined with hysterectomy and menopause created a perfect storm of connective tissue laxity and sphincter incompetence. The hallmark presentation—small-volume leakage triggered predictably by coughing, exercise, or straining, with normal detrusor function and no involuntary contractions—distinguishes it from urgency incontinence. Weakness of the urethral pressure profile and descent of the bladder neck during straining confirm the mechanical insufficiency underlying the problem, making pelvic floor rehabilitation the cornerstone of conservative management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man presents with burning pain and numbness on lateral thigh surface for 6 months. Symptoms unilateral on right side. Burning dysesthesia constant but variably intensified. No lower extremity weakness reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Burning pain and paresthesias (pins-and-needles) affecting lateral thigh from hip to knee region. Pain worse with standing and walking, improved by sitting and lying down. Sensation described as burning and hypersensitive to touch. No pain in lower leg or foot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual insidious onset over 6 months without clear inciting event. Occupational sitting with tight waistband pressure (construction supervisor). Obesity present with BMI 31. Recent weight gain of 15 pounds over past year. No recent trauma or nerve compression episode.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sensory loss to light touch in lateral femoral cutaneous nerve distribution (lateral thigh). Dysesthesia and allodynia present in same distribution. No motor weakness noted. Lower extremity strength intact (5/5 throughout). Straight leg raise normal and painless. Palpable tenderness at inguinal ligament where nerve may be compressed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG/NCS shows reduced sensory amplitude in lateral femoral cutaneous sensory response. Motor conduction studies normal (femoral, peroneal, tibial). MRI shows no structural mass or pathology compressing nerve at groin level. Ultrasound demonstrates nerve thickening at inguinal ligament with hypoechoic changes. CT confirms mild nerve compression at inguinal canal.

puzzles.explanation: Meralgia paresthetica is a mononeuropathy resulting from compression of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve as it passes under or through the inguinal ligament at the groin—a purely sensory nerve that provides sensation to the lateral thigh. The condition typically develops insidiously in middle-aged individuals with obesity or recent weight gain, compounded by mechanical pressure from tight waistbands or prolonged sitting, and presents with the characteristic burning dysesthesia and sensory changes confined to the lateral thigh in the absence of motor involvement. The unilateral, burning quality of symptoms that worsen with standing and improve with sitting, combined with sensory loss and allodynia strictly in the lateral femoral cutaneous distribution, are hallmark features that distinguish it from radiculopathy or femoral neuropathy. Electrodiagnostic confirmation via reduced sensory amplitude with normal motor studies, together with ultrasound evidence of nerve thickening at the inguinal ligament, establishes the diagnosis and rules out alternative structural pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old woman presents with progressive weakness and functional decline over 2 years. Difficulty rising from chair without armrest support. Walking slower and shorter distances than previously. Recent fall from trip on uneven pavement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Reduced grip strength (12 kg, versus 22 kg in right hand). Symmetrical weakness affecting both upper and lower extremities. Difficulty with isolated fine motor tasks and sustained gripping. No focal weakness or sensory loss noted. Weakness not attributable to pain or joint limitation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 76 with sedentary lifestyle and reduced physical activity. Inadequate protein intake (40g daily versus recommended 1.0-1.2 g/kg body weight). Loss of appetite with reduced food intake. Multiple comorbidities including osteoarthritis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease limiting activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible muscle wasting in bilateral upper extremities and thighs. Hip flexor and knee extensor strength 4/5 bilaterally; shoulder and elbow strength 4+/5. Tight hip flexors, otherwise no contractures. Normal tone. No tremor, fasciculations, or sensory loss. Mild difficulty with single-leg stance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan: fat-free mass index 5.2 kg/m² (below sarcopenia cutoff <5.75). MRI thigh: marked reduction in muscle cross-sectional area with diffuse intramuscular fat infiltration; no focal muscle edema or signal change to suggest inflammatory myopathy. Prealbumin 17 mg/dL (low, <20). Albumin 3.1 g/dL (low). CRP 2.1 mg/L (normal). TSH, creatine kinase normal. Vitamin D 18 ng/mL (deficient).

puzzles.explanation: Sarcopenia is age-related loss of skeletal muscle mass and function, resulting from the combined effects of reduced physical activity, inadequate protein intake, and hormonal changes inherent to aging. The condition is characterized by progressive, symmetrical weakness and visible muscle atrophy — particularly in antigravity muscles like hip flexors and knee extensors — accompanied by infiltration of muscle with fat and connective tissue, as evident on imaging here. This patient's presentation is classic: sedentary lifestyle, protein malnutrition, and multiple comorbidities (COPD, osteoarthritis) that further limit activity and compound muscle loss, while the preserved reflexes and normal inflammatory markers rule out myopathy or neuropathy. The resulting weakness in lower extremities and impaired balance directly explain her functional decline, slower gait, and increased fall risk — making early recognition and intervention with resistance training and nutritional rehabilitation critical to prevent further deterioration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 35-year-old woman. Eight months of widespread neck and upper back pain, muscle tension, and poor sleep. Reports fatigue and difficulty concentrating. No clear mechanism for current symptoms. Exercises less than before.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain and stiffness worst in upper cervical and thoracic spine. Neck movement guarded and restricted. Symptoms intensify unpredictably during certain activities—loud noises, busy environments, or confined spaces trigger muscle tightness. Avoids driving and prolonged sitting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe motor vehicle collision 8 months ago with brief loss of consciousness and hospitalization. Initial whiplash with soft tissue injuries that resolved within weeks, but musculoskeletal pain and tension have persisted and worsened. Patient reports intrusive thoughts about the accident and actively avoids driving and similar situations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical and thoracic ROM significantly limited in all planes with protective muscle guarding. Paraspinal and upper trapezius muscles show elevated resting tone, multiple trigger points, and forward head posture. Special tests for nerve compression and structural injury unremarkable. No focal neurological deficit. Patient demonstrates heightened startle response and visible tension with unexpected stimuli during assessment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine unremarkable—no fracture, disc herniation, cord pathology, or structural explanation for persistent pain. Psychological assessment reveals high scores on pain catastrophizing and fear-avoidance scales. Symptoms do not map to single anatomical structure; pattern suggests centralized pain processing amplified by ongoing psychological distress and hypervigilance.

puzzles.explanation: Post-traumatic stress disorder with physical rehabilitation needs represents a distinct presentation where psychological trauma becomes embodied in persistent musculoskeletal dysfunction, particularly in the neck and upper back—the region often associated with threat detection and protective responses. Following the motor vehicle collision, while acute soft tissue injury resolved, the nervous system remained sensitized, manifesting as chronic muscle guarding, hypervigilance to sensory triggers (loud noises, confined spaces), and centralized pain processing that extends beyond the original injury distribution. The protective muscle tension in the cervical and thoracic spine reflects ongoing defensive posturing rather than structural pathology, explaining why imaging is unremarkable yet symptoms persist and intensify unpredictably. Rehabilitation here requires addressing both the musculoskeletal manifestations—through graded movement retraining and trigger point management—and the underlying hyperarousal and fear-avoidance patterns that perpetuate the protective cycle, often necessitating integrated care with mental health professionals.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete experiences acute knee pain and instability following a twisting injury during sport. The knee felt like it 'gave out' during activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot perform lateral movements or cutting maneuvers without fear of instability. Weight-bearing on a bent knee triggers apprehension. Cannot return to pivoting sports.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred during a sudden deceleration with foot planted. Patient reports this is the first time this has happened. Physiotherapist notes family history of knee instability and patient's naturally loose joints.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Apprehension sign present with lateral patellar pressure. Medial patellofemoral ligament tenderness on palpation. Significant knee effusion restricts active extension in final 10 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate a patella that is reduced in anatomical position with possible osteochondral findings. MRI shows acute ligamentous disruption of the medial patellofemoral ligament. Imaging confirms acute patellar dislocation.

puzzles.explanation: Patellar dislocation occurs when the patella displaces laterally out of the trochlear groove, most commonly in young athletes during rapid deceleration with the foot planted and the knee flexed — a mechanism that overloads the dynamic stabilizers. The medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL) is the primary restraint to lateral patellar translation, and acute disruption of this ligament is the hallmark finding in first-time dislocations, often accompanied by osteochondral injury as the patella tracks abnormally across the femoral condyle. Generalized ligamentous laxity and anatomical predisposition (such as increased Q-angle or patella alta) significantly elevate re-dislocation risk, making this injury a critical teaching point for patellar tracking restoration through VMO strengthening and neuromuscular retraining. The apprehension sign with lateral pressure, MPFL tenderness, and effusion-limited extension are classic acute presentation findings that guide both diagnosis and early rehabilitation emphasis on quadriceps activation and proprioceptive control.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old retired teacher reports dizzy spells upon standing from a seated or supine position, occurring several times daily. Experiencing presyncope without loss of consciousness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms resolve within seconds to minutes of remaining still. Patient restricts activities due to fall risk concern. Slower positional changes help reduce episodes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of hypertension managed with antihypertensive medications including ACE inhibitor and calcium channel blocker. Recent dose increase 2 weeks prior. No cardiac arrhythmias noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Systolic blood pressure drops 25 mmHg when rising from supine to standing (sitting 142/88, standing 117/82). Heart rate appropriately increases from 62 to 80 bpm. No orthostatic tachycardia or paradoxical bradycardia. Gait steady, no balance deficits. No focal neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring during orthostatic challenge shows no arrhythmias or conduction abnormalities. Echocardiogram normal with preserved left ventricular function and no valvular disease. Doppler studies show appropriate peripheral vascular response to postural change.

puzzles.explanation: Postural hypotension (orthostatic hypotension) is a transient drop in blood pressure upon assumption of upright posture, resulting from inadequate peripheral vasoconstriction or insufficient sympathetic compensation during the postural transition. In this case, the 25 mmHg systolic drop with appropriate heart rate compensation indicates a primary vasomotor regulation problem rather than a cardiac or arrhythmic cause — particularly notable given the recent intensification of antihypertensive therapy, which can impair the baroreceptor reflex response needed to maintain cerebral perfusion. The rapid resolution of symptoms with recumbency and the normal cardiac structure and rhythm differentiate this from autonomic failure or structural heart disease. Management typically involves medication review, salt and fluid intake optimization, and education on gradual positional changes to allow adequate sympathetic engagement before standing fully upright.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 61-year-old man 6 weeks post-major cardiac surgery presents with progressive dyspnea and chest discomfort triggered by minimal activity. Exercise tolerance severely limited. Reports fatigue affecting daily function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dyspnea limiting walking to 100 metres on flat ground. Chest wall pain restricts overhead reaching and shoulder mobility. Marked tachycardia response: HR 110 at rest, 140 with minimal walking. Disproportionate heart rate response compared to activity level.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six weeks post-CABG x3 with saphenous vein grafts. Ejection fraction improved from 35% to 50% post-operatively. Pre-operative sedentary lifestyle. Comorbidities: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes. No recent complications reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sternotomy incision well-healed without dehiscence. Shoulder abduction limited to 100 degrees due to guarding and incisional pain. Paradoxical breathing pattern with chest wall splinting evident. Orthostatic vital signs normal. Gait slow and cautious. No signs of acute cardiac compromise at rest (HR 85, BP 138/82).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative echocardiography: ejection fraction 50% with anterior wall motion normalising. ECG normal sinus rhythm, no ischemic changes. Cardiac biomarkers (troponin, BNP) normalized. Stress testing at 6 weeks: no ischemic changes, target heart rate achieved. Conclusion: exercise limitation attributable to deconditioning and musculoskeletal guarding rather than cardiac ischaemia or graft compromise.

puzzles.explanation: Post-cardiac surgery rehabilitation addresses the complex recovery phase following major cardiac procedures like CABG, where patients must gradually restore cardiopulmonary fitness and musculoskeletal function while managing surgical trauma and cardiac remodeling. In the early post-operative period (4-12 weeks), exercise intolerance typically reflects deconditioning and protective guarding rather than cardiac dysfunction — as evidenced here by normalized ejection fraction and stress testing, yet marked dyspnea and disproportionate tachycardia with minimal exertion. Sternotomy creates a significant musculoskeletal insult: chest wall splinting, restricted shoulder mobility, and paradoxical breathing patterns perpetuate a cycle of reduced activity and further deconditioning. Effective rehabilitation progressively challenges cardiovascular capacity while addressing incisional pain, breathing mechanics, and shoulder mobility through graded aerobic training, breathing re-education, and early range-of-motion work — allowing safe progression toward functional recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 24-year-old hockey player presents with acute onset anterior chest wall pain after collision during play. Reports sudden swelling and visible deformity over medial shoulder region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to adduct arm across body without sharp anterior chest pain. Cross-body reach test highly restricted. Overhead reaching also limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during high-impact collision with direct anterior force. No prior shoulder or clavicular pathology. No neurovascular symptoms reported at time of injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked tenderness over sternoclavicular joint. Medial clavicle palpably prominent and mobile superiorly. Pain reproduction with cross-body adduction. Upper extremity strength 5/5 throughout. Pulses and sensation intact distally. Deep palpation suggests soft tissue involvement extending posteriorly toward sternum.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP and 40-degree cephalic tilt views) shows anterior displacement of medial clavicle with joint space widening. CT chest demonstrates clavicle positioned anterior to sternum. No vascular compromise on imaging. Soft tissue swelling around joint evident on advanced imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Sternoclavicular joint injury, typically anterior dislocation, results from high-impact direct anterior force to the medial clavicle, forcing it anteriorly out of its articulation with the sternum. The joint's saddle morphology and relatively lax capsule make anterior displacement more common than posterior, though the latter carries greater risk of neurovascular or tracheal compromise. The characteristic presentation here—acute swelling with visible deformity, severe pain on cross-body adduction (which loads the SC joint), and palpable superior prominence of the medial clavicle—reflects disruption of the costoclavicular ligament and joint capsule. Imaging with cephalic tilt views and CT confirmation of anterior clavicular displacement is essential to rule out the more dangerous posterior dislocation and ensure intact neurovascular structures before conservative management with sling immobilization and progressive rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old man reports progressive anterior shoulder pain over 6 months. He struggles with tasks like putting on shirts and reaching behind his back. Pain is worse at night and disrupts sleep. History of overhead work activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform the lift-off test (hand locked against back). Weakness with internal rotation strength testing. Sharp anterior pain on resisted internal rotation. Scapular winging observed during arm elevation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic rotator cuff pathology with degenerative changes. Progressive symptoms over 6 months despite physical therapy. Age-related tissue degeneration contributing. History of overhead work stress. Poor proprioceptive control and scapular dyskinesis noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lift-off test impossible (0/5 power, hand cannot lift from back). Bear-hug test shows 3/5 weakness. Positive anterior slide test. Tenderness over subscapularis insertion. Full passive internal rotation preserved; weakness only with resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows subscapularis tendon tear with full-thickness component at musculotendinous junction. Associated rotator cuff pathology with supraspinatus and infraspinatus involvement. Subscapularis muscle atrophy evident. Bone marrow edema at lesser tuberosity insertion site. Ultrasound confirms tear with loss of normal tendon architecture.

puzzles.explanation: Subscapularis tear represents disruption of the largest and strongest rotator cuff muscle, which originates from the anterior scapula and inserts on the lesser tuberosity to provide internal rotation and anterior shoulder stability. The lift-off test failure is pathognomonic — patients cannot lift their hand away from the small of the back because the subscapularis cannot fire, reflecting the functional demand this muscle serves in daily activities like dressing and reaching behind the body. Chronic overhead work and age-related degeneration predispose to progressive rotator cuff failure, and when the subscapularis tears, associated supraspinatus involvement and scapular dyskinesis often follow, creating a cascade of compensatory dysfunction. The preserved passive range of motion but severe weakness with resistance, combined with MRI confirmation of full-thickness tendon disruption at the musculotendinous junction, distinguishes true structural failure from other causes of shoulder dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 82-year-old woman reports two falls in the past month. First fall occurred when standing from sitting; second while walking to bathroom at night. Denies loss of consciousness. Expresses reduced confidence with mobility.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Requires contact guard during walking. Complains of lightheadedness when changing position from sitting to standing. Demonstrates hesitant, slow movement pattern. Avoiding previously tolerated activities due to fear of recurrence.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 82 with multiple fall risk factors: osteoporosis, hypertension on multiple medications causing orthostatic hypotension, mild cognitive impairment, vision reduced from cataracts. Lives alone in cluttered home environment. History of vitamin D insufficiency.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Timed up and go test 28 seconds (abnormal >12 seconds). Single leg stance 3 seconds each leg (abnormal <10 seconds). Berg Balance Scale score 38/56 (high fall risk <45). Orthostatic vital signs positive (systolic BP drop 20 mmHg on standing). Gait assessment shows reduced stride length, shuffle pattern, and reduced arm swing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan confirms osteoporosis (T-score -2.9). Brain MRI normal. Vestibular assessment normal. Medication review confirms orthostatic-inducing antihypertensive regimen contributing to BP drops on standing.

puzzles.explanation: Falls in older adults represent a multifactorial geriatric syndrome where age-related physiological decline intersects with modifiable risk factors—orthostatic hypotension from polypharmacy, reduced balance confidence, slower reaction times, and environmental hazards converge to destabilize mobility. This 82-year-old's recurrent falls during positional changes and nighttime walking reflect the classic vulnerability window: orthostatic blood pressure drops combined with impaired postural reflexes and diminished sensory feedback create a cascade where even routine transitions become hazardous. The significantly prolonged Timed Up and Go test, reduced single-leg stance duration, and abnormal Berg Balance Scale reflect the neuromotor integration failure characteristic of fall-prone older adults. Beyond falling mechanics, the reduced confidence and activity avoidance highlight the secondary psychological impact—deconditioning and fall anxiety perpetuate the cycle, making comprehensive assessment of medications, home environment, strength, and balance retraining essential to breaking this pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old male presents with pain and swelling at the tip of his ring finger. Onset was sudden 2 days ago. He reports inability to straighten the end of the finger, though he can move the middle joint normally. No numbness or tingling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot actively extend the distal finger joint against gravity. Functional impact includes difficulty with fine gripping tasks and buttoning. Pain is localised to the dorsal aspect of the fingertip and worsens with attempted extension.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury mechanism: finger caught forcefully in a door during closure 2 days ago. No laceration, no visible bruising on dorsal surface, minimal bleeding. Patient has no prior hand injuries, no rheumatological disease, no medications affecting healing. Occupational demand is low-to-moderate.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: DIP joint held in approximately 30° flexion and cannot be actively straightened. Passive extension is possible but painful. No skin breach. Sensation intact in distal fingertip. Grip strength normal bilaterally. Proximal interphalangeal joint moves freely. No palpable step-off or bony deformity at DIP level.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show intact distal phalanx with no fracture. Soft tissue examination reveals avulsion-type injury at the insertion of the extensor digitorum tendon on the dorsal base of the distal phalanx. MRI confirms complete discontinuity of the terminal extensor mechanism with 3mm retraction of the tendon.

puzzles.explanation: Mallet finger is an avulsion injury of the extensor digitorum tendon from its insertion at the base of the distal phalanx, typically caused by forced flexion of an extended finger — as occurred here when the ring finger was caught in a door. The injury disrupts the terminal extensor mechanism, leaving the DIP joint unopposed by extensor force and resulting in the characteristic resting flexion deformity and inability to actively extend the distal joint. Despite intact sensation and normal proximal joint mobility, imaging reveals the hallmark finding: complete discontinuity of the extensor tendon with retraction from its insertion. Early recognition and immobilization of the DIP joint in extension for 6-8 weeks allows healing of the avulsed tendon and restoration of active extension, making conservative management the gold standard for this common injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old woman reports palpitations, lightheadedness, and tremor that consistently occur when standing up from sitting or lying down.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate standing for >10-15 minutes without symptom worsening. Exercise causes disproportionate fatigue and palpitations. Lying down provides rapid symptom relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms present for 3 years with gradual onset post-viral illness. No cardiac history. Symptoms worsen with heat, dehydration, and menstrual cycle.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting supine vitals normal (HR 62 bpm, BP 118/76). On standing, heart rate rises to 115 bpm within 5 minutes with systolic BP drop <10 mmHg. Patient reports palpitations and lightheadedness with standing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Tilt table testing (70° for 10 minutes): heart rate increases from 64 to 118 bpm while systolic BP remains stable at 116 mmHg. Plasma norepinephrine elevated when upright (500 pg/mL). Echocardiography and ECG normal.

puzzles.explanation: Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS) is a form of dysautonomia characterized by excessive heart rate acceleration (≥30 bpm increase within 3 minutes of standing) without significant orthostatic hypotension, reflecting underlying autonomic dysfunction and impaired blood pooling compensation. The condition disproportionately affects young women and frequently emerges post-viral infection through autoimmune-mediated damage to autonomic nerve fibers, explaining the 3-year history following viral illness in this case. The hallmark presentation—rapid symptom relief on recumbency, intolerance of upright posture and sustained standing, and exacerbation with heat and dehydration—reflects the pathophysiology of impaired peripheral vasoconstriction and increased sympathetic activation with positional changes. Elevated supine catecholamine levels during upright posture, combined with the diagnostic tilt table findings and normal cardiac structure, confirm dysautonomic etiology rather than primary cardiac disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 22-year-old overhead athlete presents with recurrent shoulder pain and episodes of the arm feeling 'loose' or 'slipping out.' Symptoms occur during throwing or overhead movements. Patient reports episodes of sharp pain followed by relief after brief rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain and instability sensation triggered specifically by throwing, serving, or overhead pressing activities. Patient avoids cocking phase of throw. Difficulty with rapid acceleration movements. Symptoms ease with arm at side and gentle internal rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 6 months during competitive season. No traumatic fall, dislocation, or hyperextension injury. Family history of shoulder laxity. Prior shoulder subluxation episodes during sport; each time arm position reset the episode. No prior imaging or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Increased glenohumeral external rotation at 90° abduction (>70°) compared to contralateral side. Humeral head palpably translates anteriorly on supine testing. Scapular dyskinesis noted during arm elevation. Rotator cuff strength intact. No pain with isometric internal rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows intact rotator cuff tendons and labrum with no tears. Arthroscopic findings demonstrate generalized capsular laxity and anterior humeral head translation of >50% glenoid width on dynamic stress imaging. No bony Bankart lesion or Hill-Sachs defect.

puzzles.explanation: Glenohumeral instability results from excessive laxity of the glenohumeral capsule and ligamentous structures, allowing abnormal translation of the humeral head on the glenoid—particularly anteriorly during loaded overhead positions. Unlike traumatic dislocation, this multidirectional or anterior-inferior pattern often develops insidiously in overhead athletes due to repetitive microtrauma, capsular stretch, and altered neuromuscular control, particularly during the late cocking phase of throwing when external rotation demands peak stress on already lax restraints. The intact labrum and absence of bony lesions (Bankart or Hill-Sachs) distinguish this atraumatic form from recurrent traumatic instability; the key pathology is capsuloligamentous redundancy rather than structural damage. Scapular dyskinesis compounds the problem by reducing the dynamic centering effect of the rotator cuff, allowing greater humeral head migration during acceleration—a critical finding that guides rehabilitation focus toward neuromuscular re-education rather than surgical stabilization alone in most cases.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old man with a 20-year history of skin disease presents with 6 months of progressive joint pain affecting fingers, wrists, and knees. Morning stiffness lasting 1.5 hours. Insidious onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to grip effectively (reduced functional grasp). Difficulty with fine finger movements and wrist flexion/extension. Morning stiffness prevents normal morning routine for first 1-2 hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Skin condition flared 2 months before joint symptoms began. Strong family history of both skin disease and inflammatory arthritis. No previous joint problems. No recent infections or trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Asymmetric polyarthritis with dactylitis (sausage appearance of fingers). Nail dystrophy with pitting and onycholysis. Psoriatic plaques on elbows, knees, and scalp. Tenderness at Achilles insertions bilaterally. Wrist and finger joint swelling with warmth. Reduced grip strength (28 kg). Joint flexion contractures developing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR elevated at 42 mm/hr, CRP 8.2 mg/dL. Rheumatoid factor negative, anti-CCP negative (seronegative pattern). HLA-B27 positive (positive in 70% of PsA). Hand and wrist X-rays show periosteal new bone formation (fluffy periostitis) and pencil-in-cup deformity early stages. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with power Doppler activity indicating active inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Psoriatic arthritis (PsA) is a seronegative inflammatory arthropathy that occurs in 5–12% of patients with psoriasis, driven by shared immune dysregulation and often triggered by the skin disease itself. The pathophysiology involves Th17-mediated inflammation affecting not just synovium but also entheses and bone, producing the characteristic features seen here: asymmetric polyarthritis with dactylitis (transverse synovitis and flexor tenosynovitis creating the 'sausage digit'), nail involvement reflecting shared keratinization pathology, and entheseal inflammation at sites like the Achilles insertion. The temporal relationship between skin flare and joint onset, combined with the distinctive imaging pattern of periosteal new bone formation and early pencil-in-cup erosions, distinguishes PsA from rheumatoid arthritis—a key clinical distinction reinforced by negative RF and anti-CCP antibodies despite significant inflammatory markers. Morning stiffness and progressive functional loss reflect the erosive potential of untreated PsA, making early aggressive management essential to prevent the joint damage already beginning to show radiographically.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old sprinter. Sudden onset posterior thigh pain during high-intensity sprint work. Pain began mid-race without preceding trauma. Unable to continue activity immediately.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered by combined hip flexion with knee extension (e.g., kicking, reaching forward). Cannot run, jump, or perform explosive movements. Pain eases with hip and knee flexion (sitting, lying down).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 18 hours ago during acceleration phase of 400-meter race. No prior injury to this leg. Minimal warm-up before race. Swelling and bruising began appearing within 6-12 hours. No numbness or tingling reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized palpable tenderness at mid-belly of posterior thigh. Visible swelling and ecchymosis at injury site. Straight leg raise limited to 40 degrees by pain. Hip extension strength 3/5 against resistance. Proprioception intact. Antalgic gait with reduced stride length on affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows disruption of muscle fibers at mid-musculotendinous junction with anechoic fluid collection consistent with hemorrhage. MRI confirms partial-thickness muscle tear with associated edema and intramuscular hematoma. Grade 2 strain pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Hamstring strain is an acute musculotendinous injury resulting from excessive tensile loading during high-velocity hip flexion with knee extension — the exact mechanism that occurs during sprint acceleration. The rapid force demand on muscles not adequately prepared (minimal warm-up) causes partial tearing of muscle fibers, typically at the myotendinous junction where mechanical stress concentrates. The sudden onset mid-race, combined with immediate functional loss and rapid development of swelling and bruising within hours, reflects the acute inflammatory response to a Grade 2 (partial-thickness) tear with intramuscular bleeding. Hip extension weakness and severely limited straight leg raise are hallmark findings, as the hamstrings are the primary hip extensors and knee flexors — exactly the opposite of the injuring movement pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old woman presents with catching and locking sensation in the right ring finger at the MCP joint. Clicking noise with finger flexion and extension. Worsens in morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with fine motor tasks including writing and typing. Painful with full finger extension. Functional limitation in hand dexterity and grip activities.

clues.label: History
