clues.text: Gradual onset over 3 months. No acute trauma. Repetitive gripping activities in workplace (packing work). Symptoms progressive with worsening locking episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nodular thickening palpable over A1 pulley at MCP joint. Triggering reproduced with active finger flexion and extension. Triggering relieved with passive extension by opposite hand. Swelling at pulley level.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates nodular thickening of flexor tendon at A1 pulley with increased fluid signal around tendon. Swelling is localised to pulley, with no evidence of osteoarthritic changes or joint pathology. Findings consistent with stenosing tenosynovitis.

puzzles.explanation: Trigger finger (stenosing tenosynovitis) occurs when nodular thickening of the flexor tendon develops at the A1 pulley level, creating a mechanical mismatch between the tendon and the restrictive pulley opening. The condition typically affects patients with repetitive gripping or in those with inflammatory predispositions, causing the tendon to catch during flexion and lock during extension—the characteristic triggering sensation. Morning stiffness and progressive locking episodes, as in this case, reflect overnight fluid accumulation around the inflamed tendon sheath; symptoms often improve with activity as synovial fluid distributes. Ultrasound confirmation of localized tenosynovitis without joint pathology is typical, distinguishing trigger finger from osteoarthritis or other hand conditions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old sprinter presents with acute onset posterior thigh pain. Pain began mid-competition during a high-speed running effort. Unable to continue activity immediately following onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp pain localizes to posterior mid-thigh during running and kicking motions. Visible swelling and bruising developed within 24 hours. Antalgic gait with markedly reduced stride length.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic mechanism during sprinting with forceful hip flexion and knee extension (acceleration phase of running). Young athletic male with high-demand sport. No prior hamstring injury. Pre-existing hip flexor tightness noted. Inadequate warm-up prior to race.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tender mass at mid-thigh with localized warmth. Straight leg raise limited to 40° due to pain. Hip extension strength 3/5 against resistance. Pain on resisted knee flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI confirms Grade II partial thickness hamstring strain at musculotendinous junction of biceps femoris. Hematoma present at injury site (4cm x 3cm). Surrounding muscle edema. Intact fascial integrity (not retracted). Ultrasound shows hypoechoic fluid collection at strain site with intact muscle architecture proximally and distally.

puzzles.explanation: Hamstring strain results from excessive tensile loading of the muscle–tendon unit, most commonly occurring during the acceleration phase of sprinting when the hamstring must eccentrically control hip flexion and knee extension simultaneously. Grade II strains involve partial muscle fiber disruption, typically at the musculotendinous junction where mechanical stress concentrates — in this case affecting the biceps femoris. The acute onset during high-speed running, rapid swelling and bruising from the associated hematoma, marked weakness on resisted knee flexion, and significantly limited straight leg raise all reflect the substantial muscle disruption while intact fascial architecture preserves structural continuity. Pre-existing hip flexor tightness and inadequate warm-up in a young athlete undertaking maximal effort created the perfect mechanical storm for this injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old man with 5-year history of progressive lower back pain and bilateral leg pain. Symptoms worsening over past 6 months with increasing heaviness and fatigue in legs with walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Back pain and leg symptoms worse with standing and lumbar extension; significantly relieved by sitting and forward bending. Walking tolerance severely limited; symptoms do not improve when standing still, only with rest in flexion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual progressive course. Prior imaging noted degenerative disc changes in lower lumbar spine. Occupational history: 20 years heavy lifting and repetitive twisting. Limited core strength and sedentary lifestyle outside work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar extension reproduces back pain and bilateral leg heaviness. Forward flexion limited (hands to knees). Right ankle dorsiflexion weakness 4/5 (L5 myotome). Right ankle reflex diminished. Bilateral straight leg raise limited to 45° by pain in posterior leg.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows L4-5 spondylolisthesis with L4 vertebra slipped anteriorly 3mm relative to L5. Degenerative disc changes at L4-5 and L5-S1. MRI confirms spondylolisthesis with significant central spinal stenosis at L4-5 level (50% canal narrowing). Ligamentum flavum hypertrophy and disc bulging contributing. Foraminal stenosis on left side.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spondylolisthesis is anterior slippage of one vertebra on another, commonly occurring at L4-L5 due to degenerative changes, facet joint arthrosis, and loss of disc height—particularly in patients with chronic loading histories like heavy occupational work. The condition becomes symptomatic when the slip narrows the spinal canal and neural foramina, compressing nerve roots and the cauda equina; the characteristic claudicant pattern here—leg heaviness and fatigue with standing and extension that resolves with sitting and forward flexion—reflects dynamic stenosis worsening with extension and improving with spinal flexion. The bilateral leg pain with focal L5 radicular signs (ankle dorsiflexion weakness, diminished reflex) and central canal narrowing on imaging explain both the lower limb neurological deficit and the walking limitation despite initially normal standing. Progressive degenerative spondylolisthesis like this often requires activity modification and core strengthening initially, though significant canal stenosis may ultimately warrant surgical decompression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old man presents 12 weeks after a significant hip injury with progressive loss of motion, pain, and swelling in the hip region. Activities of daily living increasingly difficult. Hip feels increasingly stiff and restricted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot dress or perform hip-intensive ADLs due to motion loss. Hip flexion contracture present; internal and external rotation markedly restricted. Pain limits all hip movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy posterior hip dislocation with complex femoral head fracture 12 weeks prior. Significant soft tissue trauma and prolonged immobilization. Male, age 42.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion contracture present (unable to achieve full extension). External and internal rotation severely limited (<15 degrees each direction). Abduction limited to 10 degrees. Palpable stiffness with firm end-feel on passive motion. Tenderness over hip region. Functional limitation with gait and transfers.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows extensive bony bridging across hip joint with mature ossification. CT demonstrates mature bone formation in soft tissues surrounding hip capsule. Associated joint space narrowing noted.

puzzles.explanation: Heterotopic ossification (HO) is abnormal bone formation within soft tissues, occurring when osteogenic precursor cells differentiate into bone-forming cells following trauma, surgery, or prolonged immobilization. High-energy injuries like posterior hip dislocation with complex fracture carry substantial risk for HO due to extensive soft tissue trauma and the inflammatory cascade it triggers; maturation typically occurs over 12–24 weeks post-injury. The extensive bony bridging across the hip capsule seen on imaging here explains the severe restriction in all planes of motion, the firm end-feel on passive testing, and the progressive functional decline in ADLs—the heterotopic bone acts as a mechanical block to movement. Early recognition and preventive strategies (NSAIDs, indomethacin, or prophylactic radiation in high-risk cases) remain critical, as established HO is difficult to manage and surgical excision requires careful timing to avoid recurrence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 38-year-old warehouse worker. Chronic lower back pain for 3 years. Pain described as dull ache, worse as work week progresses, improves with rest days. No leg pain or neurological symptoms reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain aggravated by repetitive lifting, bending, and sustained standing. Cannot sit longer than 30 minutes without discomfort. Symptoms ease with movement breaks and light activity. Takes occasional weeks off work during flare-ups.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 3 years, no specific traumatic injury. Symptoms cyclical—worse mid-to-late week, improve over weekends. No prior episodes of sciatica or nerve-related pain. General fitness level moderate; occasional gym attendance. No comorbidities affecting spine.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Forward flexion limited 15 cm from toes due to pain; hamstring tightness present. Straight leg raise 60° bilaterally, limited by muscular tension not neural signs. Lumbar rotation more restricted than flexion. Bilateral lumbar paraspinal muscle guarding and tenderness. Neurologically intact: normal strength, reflexes, and sensation throughout lower limbs. Single-leg stance stable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine X-rays: normal disc heights, no acute fracture or severe degeneration. MRI: mild degenerative changes at L4-L5 and L5-S1 consistent with age; no disc herniation, stenosis, or nerve root compression. No inflammatory markers on blood work. Findings non-specific; imaging does not explain symptom severity or work-related pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Work-related lower back pain stems from cumulative microtrauma and postural strain in occupational settings rather than discrete structural pathology, making it one of the most common musculoskeletal conditions in physically demanding jobs. The warehouse environment — with repetitive lifting, bending, and sustained standing — creates cyclic loading that fatigues the stabilizing musculature and compounds postural dysfunction, particularly when rest and recovery are insufficient during the work week. This patient's pattern of progressive symptoms that worsen mid-to-late week and resolve with rest days is the hallmark presentation, often preceding any significant degenerative changes on imaging. The absence of neurological signs, normal imaging findings, and dramatic improvement with activity modification and time off confirm a mechanical, non-neuropathic origin — the key to differentiating work-related mechanical pain from serious pathology and guiding rehabilitation toward load management, motor control, and workplace ergonomic optimization.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old male with progressive lower back and buttock pain over 8 months. Pain is deep, aching in character. Notable feature: stiffness is worst in the morning, lasting 1–2 hours, then improves as the day progresses. Pain also worsens with prolonged rest or inactivity at night, sometimes waking him from sleep.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Forward bending and spinal flexion are significantly limited and painful. Symptoms improve markedly with activity and light exercise. Prolonged sitting or lying down worsens discomfort. Associated fatigue and general malaise reported. No clear mechanical trigger (no lifting injury, trauma, or occupational strain identified).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset without preceding trauma, infection, or specific inciting event. No prior spinal imaging or rheumatologic evaluation. Patient reports no significant past medical history. Family history status unknown but worth clarifying. Timeline and pattern suggest inflammatory rather than degenerative or mechanical aetiology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Schober's test shows markedly reduced lumbar flexion (< 3 cm excursion). Occiput-to-wall distance increased, indicating thoracic kyphosis and loss of cervical lordosis. Sacroiliac joint tenderness bilaterally on palpation. Spinal mobility globally restricted in all planes. Neurological examination normal; no lower limb weakness, sensory loss, or reflex changes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI sacroiliac joints: bilateral sacroiliitis with erosions and inflammatory edema in bone marrow. Lumbar spine MRI: early syndesmophyte formation bridging adjacent vertebral bodies. HLA-B27 positive. Inflammatory markers elevated: ESR 48 mm/hr, CRP 9.2 mg/dL. Full blood count and metabolic panel unremarkable.

puzzles.explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic inflammatory spondyloarthropathy driven by HLA-B27 genetic predisposition, predominantly affecting young males and causing progressive fusion of the spine through ossification of spinal ligaments and intervertebral discs. The hallmark inflammatory pattern — bilateral sacroiliitis with early syndesmophyte formation — distinguishes it from mechanical back pain, as does the characteristic inflammatory profile of worst morning stiffness that improves with activity and elevated systemic markers (ESR, CRP). The progressive loss of spinal mobility across all planes, combined with development of thoracic kyphosis and cervical spine straightening, reflects ongoing ankylosis that ultimately restricts function. This insidious presentation in a young man with deep, aching buttock and lower back pain that worsens with rest is the classical phenotype, making early recognition and anti-inflammatory management crucial to slow radiographic progression and preserve function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 54-year-old man, post-operative day 3 from major abdominal surgery. Worsening shortness of breath and oxygen saturation drop to 90% on room air. Fever 38.5°C. Significant pain at surgical site limiting ability to breathe deeply.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform incentive spirometry or effective coughing due to pain. Prefers shallow, rapid breathing pattern. Reluctant to mobilise or change position due to discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Anesthesia 3 hours. Standard post-operative analgesia regimen (opioid-based). No prior lung disease or smoking history. Mobilisation deferred due to pain severity. Patient reports feeling 'unable to take a deep breath' and avoiding coughing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: RR 24/min. Breath sounds diminished at both lower lobes, particularly left. Dullness to percussion over left lower lobe field. Coarse crackles in lower zones on inspiration. SpO₂ 90% RA, improves to 95% with supplemental oxygen. Fever 38.5°C. Chest wall splinting evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows opacification and collapse of left lower lobe fields consistent with atelectasis. Right lower lobe shows similar but less severe changes. Elevated hemidiaphragm on left. CT chest confirms left lower lobe consolidation and volume loss. No pneumonia (no peripheral consolidation pattern initially).

puzzles.explanation: Atelectasis is collapse of previously aerated lung tissue due to inadequate ventilation, most commonly occurring post-operatively when pain, opioid analgesia, and immobility prevent deep breathing and effective coughing. The pathophysiology here is classic: surgical pain causes chest wall splinting and shallow breathing, secretions pool in dependent airways (particularly the left lower lobe in supine or semi-recumbent positions), and without the mechanical force of deep inspiration and cough to re-expand alveoli, these areas collapse within hours. The diminished breath sounds, dullness to percussion, crackles, and hypoxemia that improves with oxygen reflect this loss of functional lung volume, while the fever and elevated respiratory rate represent the body's compensatory response. Early mobilisation, incentive spirometry, and adequate analgesia to permit deep breathing remain the cornerstones of prevention and treatment in the post-operative setting.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman with 8 years of chronic lower back pain. Gradual onset without inciting event. Pain mechanical in nature, worse with activity and sitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Morning stiffness lasting 30 minutes that improves with movement. Difficulty with forward bending. Declining standing tolerance by end of day. Pain relief with specific position changes and recumbency.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 62 with degenerative changes expected with aging. Occupational prolonged standing and repetitive bending for 35 years. History of heavy lifting in younger years. Sedentary lifestyle contributing to deconditioning. Smoking history 30 pack-years with known effects on disc health.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar forward flexion limited to fingertips 20cm from toes. Straight leg raise normal (70 degrees) without radiculopathy. Lumbar paraspinal muscles tight with guarding. One-legged stance normal without pain. Neurologically completely intact. Gait normal without antalgic pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine X-rays show multilevel degenerative disc disease with loss of disc height at L4-5 and L5-S1. Osteophyte formation present (bone spurs). Facet joint osteoarthritis. MRI shows disc desiccation with decreased T2 signal in multiple levels. Modest disc bulging at L4-5 without stenosis. No neural compromise.

puzzles.explanation: Degenerative disc disease (DDD) is a progressive loss of disc integrity due to age-related dehydration, structural breakdown, and failure of the nucleus pulposus to maintain turgor—a natural consequence of aging that accelerates with smoking, heavy loading, and sedentary deconditioning. The presentation here is textbook: mechanical pain worse with activity and sitting, morning stiffness that improves with movement, and declining tolerances reflecting the inflammatory and biomechanical dysfunction characteristic of DDD. The imaging findings of disc desiccation, loss of height, osteophyte formation, and facet joint changes at multiple levels confirm the degenerative cascade, while the intact neurological examination and normal straight leg raise indicate that, despite modest bulging, there is no neural compromise—the pain arises from discogenic irritation and mechanical instability rather than nerve root involvement. At this stage, management focuses on activity modification, core stabilization to offload compromised discs, and maintenance of movement tolerance rather than imaging-directed intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old elite endurance athlete presents with persistent fatigue and declining athletic performance despite increased training volume over 3 months. Resting heart rate elevated 15 beats above baseline. Multiple minor illnesses over past 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to sustain previous training paces. Completes sessions but lacks usual power and speed. Feels 'heavy' during running. Takes 48-72 hours to feel recovered between hard sessions versus previous 24-hour recovery. Irritable and unmotivated about training despite previous enthusiasm.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive endurance training program with progressively increased volume without adequate recovery. Mileage increased 50% over 3 months. Inadequate sleep averaging 6 hours nightly. Poor nutritional recovery with insufficient caloric intake. Psychological stress from competition and team dynamics.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate elevated at 75 bpm (personal baseline 60 bpm). Heart rate recovery after exercise test shows delayed normalization. VO2 max testing shows 10% reduction compared to previous testing. Psychological assessment shows mood disturbance and depression symptoms. Musculoskeletal examination normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Blood work shows normal thyroid function excluding thyroiditis. Normal complete blood count excluding anemia. Serum cortisol elevated with abnormal circadian rhythm. Ferritin at lower limit of normal suggesting iron depletion. CPK mildly elevated at 380 IU/L suggesting muscle stress. No viral serology positive.

puzzles.explanation: Overtraining syndrome is a state of maladaptation to excessive training load combined with inadequate recovery, characterized by persistent fatigue, declining performance, and systemic physiological disruption. It results from the accumulated stress of high training volume, poor sleep, caloric deficit, and psychological pressure overwhelming the athlete's capacity to recover — the 50% mileage increase without proportional recovery time created a cumulative stimulus that exceeded adaptive capacity. The hallmark presentation here — elevated resting heart rate, delayed heart rate recovery, reduced VO2 max despite continued training, mood disturbance, and elevated cortisol with blunted circadian rhythm — reflects central nervous system fatigue and neuroendocrine dysregulation rather than local tissue injury or organic disease. Unlike acute overuse injuries, overtraining syndrome requires a substantial reduction in training load and extended recovery, often necessitating a return to baseline performance before resuming progressive training.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 79-year-old woman reports progressive tiredness and slowed pace over the past year. No single acute event. General sense of weakness and difficulty maintaining usual routines.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot rise from a chair without using armrests. Walks slowly indoors, avoids stairs. Requires assistance bathing and dressing. Unintentionally stopped attending weekly social group 3 months ago due to fatigue.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Unintentional weight loss of 8 kg over 8 months despite adequate food access. Poor appetite. Lives alone with family visiting twice weekly. Type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease present but historically stable. Medications frequently missed. No recent hospitalization or acute illness. Increased daytime sleep; early morning awakening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Grip strength 12 kg bilaterally (well below age/sex norms). Knee extension 3/5 bilaterally. Timed Up and Go 35 seconds. Mini-Cog 2/3 (mild cognitive impairment). Resting heart rate 92, BP 138/82. General appearance: thin, slow movements, reduced spontaneous activity. Gait narrow-based but unsteady.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan shows osteopenia. Routine labs: albumin 3.2 g/dL (low), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia), TSH normal. Echocardiogram unchanged from prior. No acute findings on recent imaging. Frailty phenotype score positive for 4 of 5 criteria: weakness, slowness, weight loss, exhaustion, reduced activity.

puzzles.explanation: Frailty syndrome is a geriatric syndrome characterized by diminished physiological reserves and increased vulnerability to stressors, resulting from accumulated deficits across multiple systems—sarcopenia, malnutrition, reduced activity, and systemic inflammation. Rather than a disease itself, it reflects a state of biological dysregulation where small perturbations can trigger cascading functional decline, as evidenced here by progressive unintentional weight loss, profound weakness (grip 12 kg, knee extension 3/5), slowness (TUG 35 seconds), and withdrawal from meaningful activities. The constellation of low albumin, anemia, osteopenia, and cognitive slowing reflects the multisystem nature of the syndrome. Recognition of frailty phenotype status—positive for weakness, slowness, weight loss, exhaustion, and inactivity—is crucial because these patients require integrated rehabilitation addressing nutrition, progressive resistance training, cognitive engagement, and medical optimization to prevent further decompensation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman reports progressive difficulty walking over the past 18 months. Gait has become noticeably abnormal with short shuffling steps. Multiple falls and requires constant supervision.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient demonstrates short, shuffling steps (approximately 15cm stride). Arm swing is absent and posture is stiff. Balance is severely impaired even with contact guard. Walking becomes noticeably worse when she tries to perform another task simultaneously (e.g., talking while ambulating).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has severe cognitive impairment limiting her ability to follow movement instructions or adapt to corrections. She has been sedentary in a care facility for several years and has lost 8 kg over the past year. Known hypertension and osteoporosis present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gait speed measured at 0.3 m/sec. Hip and knee flexion markedly reduced during swing phase. Patient cannot lift feet properly, dragging them forward. Stance transitions reveal significant postural instability and risk of collapse. Forward head posture and stooped trunk evident. Patient cannot correct gait errors even when instructed verbally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI shows severe cortical atrophy with enlargement of lateral ventricles and multiple white matter changes. The pattern of atrophy involves frontal and parietal regions including motor and executive control areas. No acute focal lesions, stroke, or intracranial hemorrhage.

puzzles.explanation: Dementia-related mobility decline reflects progressive neurodegeneration affecting frontal and parietal motor control regions, leading to a characteristic syndrome of gait disturbance, postural instability, and movement inflexibility. The shuffling gait with reduced stride length, absent arm swing, and marked difficulty with dual-tasking (talking while walking) exemplify the loss of automatic motor planning and executive control typical of this condition. Cognitive impairment prevents compensatory strategies and conscious error correction, distinguishing this from primary Parkinsonian or cerebellar disorders. The severe cortical atrophy and white matter changes on imaging, combined with weight loss and prolonged sedentariness, compound fall risk and functional decline, making early recognition and environmental modification critical for safety.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old presents with insidious-onset knee pain and stiffness over several years. Pain worsens with activity and is present first thing in the morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports pain peaks mid-to-late afternoon after activity. Swelling appears after prolonged standing. Walking on flat surfaces is less symptomatic than walking downhill or descending stairs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No significant acute trauma. Patient reports previous knee ligament injury 15 years ago. Progressive worsening over past 3 years despite activity modification. One prior intra-articular steroid injection 6 months ago with temporary relief.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Joint line tenderness on palpation. Crepitus on active ROM throughout range. Flexion limited to 100°, extension lag of 10°. Mild palpable effusion. McMurray's test negative; Lachman's test negative (ruling out ligamentous instability). Quadriceps strength 4/5 bilaterally, hip abductors 4+/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing radiographs demonstrate medial compartment joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation at joint margins, and subchondral sclerosis. Sunrise view shows lateral patellar tracking. MRI confirms full-thickness cartilage loss in medial compartment with bone marrow edema and no meniscal tears.

puzzles.explanation: Knee osteoarthritis is a progressive degenerative joint disease characterized by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes, typically developing over years through a combination of aging, prior injury, and cumulative joint loading. The insidious presentation with morning stiffness, activity-related pain peaks, and reproducible symptoms with weight-bearing activities like stair descent reflects the mechanical nature of the condition — cartilage loss increases friction and alters load distribution across the joint. The medial compartment involvement, crepitus throughout range of motion, quadriceps weakness, and imaging evidence of full-thickness cartilage loss with bone marrow edema are hallmark findings; prior ligamentous injury can accelerate focal cartilage degeneration through altered biomechanics. While intra-articular steroid injections provide temporary symptom relief, they address inflammation rather than cartilage loss itself, making long-term management focused on load management, strength restoration, and consideration of joint-preserving or arthroplasty interventions based on functional decline.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old construction worker reports lateral lower back pain that developed gradually over 6 weeks. Pain worsens with twisting and bending movements typical of his job.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform sustained rotational work or reach across his body. Simple bed rest relieves symptoms within 24 hours. Functional impact prevents normal construction work duties.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset correlates with a particularly demanding construction project involving repetitive lifting and twisting in awkward postures. Prior lumbar strain 2 years prior resolved with conservative care. No acute trauma or fall. Patient admits to minimal exercise outside of work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness along thoracolumbar fascia region bilaterally (flanks at L4-5 level). Pain with trunk rotation against resistance. Reduced lumbar rotation in both directions (limited by pain). Palpable muscle guarding in quadratus lumborum region. Single leg stance provokes lateral back pain. Neurologically intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine X-rays normal excluding fracture or structural abnormality. MRI shows fascial thickening and edema in thoracolumbar fascia region. Quadratus lumborum muscle shows edema and signal changes consistent with myofascial injury. No nerve compression or disc pathology. Ultrasound confirms fascial thickening with echo changes.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracolumbar fascia pain arises from inflammation and microtrauma within the dense connective tissue layer that envelops the lumbar spine and assists force transmission between the upper and lower body. This structure is particularly vulnerable in occupations demanding repetitive twisting and lifting in poor postures, as the fascia becomes overloaded and develops localised edema and thickening — precisely what imaging revealed here. The condition characteristically provokes pain with rotational movements and resisted trunk rotation while sparing neural structures, and often responds rapidly to load modification and rest, reflecting its mechanical rather than neurogenic nature. The bilateral flank tenderness and quadratus lumborum involvement underscore how the fascia's broad attachments distribute load across both sides of the lumbar region, making functional restoration through progressive motor control and postural retraining essential for sustained recovery in demanding work environments.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old woman with 6-week history of gradual-onset joint pain and swelling affecting both hands. Symptoms worse in the morning, improving somewhat with activity. Reports fatigue and general malaise.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty gripping objects, dressing, and fine motor tasks. Pain and swelling predominantly in hands bilaterally. Functional impact worse after prolonged rest or overnight inactivity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No preceding injury or trauma. Family history significant for autoimmune thyroid disease and lupus. Recent mild weight loss. No improvement with rest or non-steroidal anti-inflammatories. Patient previously well with no medical comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symmetric swelling and warmth of metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Grip strength bilaterally reduced (14 kg versus baseline 30 kg). Rheumatoid nodules palpable on elbows. Positive squeeze test of metacarpophalangeal joints reproducing pain. Reduced hand function with difficulty making fist.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Rheumatoid factor positive (titer 1:1280). Anti-CCP positive (256 IU/mL). ESR elevated at 52 mm/hr, CRP 18 mg/dL. Hand X-rays show early periarticular osteopenia with marginal erosions. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with power Doppler activity in affected joints. Complete blood count shows mild anemia (Hb 10.8).

puzzles.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis is a systemic autoimmune condition in which anti-CCP antibodies and rheumatoid factor drive chronic synovial inflammation, leading to progressive joint destruction if untreated. The hallmark symmetric polyarticular presentation affecting small joints of the hands—particularly the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints—combined with morning stiffness that improves with activity distinguishes RA from mechanical joint disorders. The presence of rheumatoid nodules, significant morning stiffness worse after overnight rest, reduced grip strength, positive squeeze test, and systemic features like fatigue and anemia reflect the aggressive inflammatory nature of this condition. Early radiographic findings of periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions, together with elevated acute phase reactants and positive serologies, confirm active RA requiring prompt disease-modifying therapy to prevent irreversible joint damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 38-year-old woman with 8 months of pain during sexual activity. Postmenopausal for 1 year. Significant relationship impact and mood disturbance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain occurs only with penetration attempt, preventing intercourse. No pain with non-penetrative contact or daily activities. Pain severity has worsened progressively over 8 months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset 8 months ago without clear inciting event. Post-menopausal status 1 year prior contributing to tissue changes. No prior pelvic trauma or surgery. Stress and relationship factors contributing. History of anxiety and depression. Recent hormonal changes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pelvic floor examination shows hypertonic pelvic floor muscles with trigger points palpable. Tenderness on introital palpation. Pelvic floor muscle spasm evident during examination. Levator ani muscles tight bilaterally. Vulvar erythema present in introital region. Speculum examination normal excluding other causes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Transvaginal ultrasound shows increased echogenicity and thickening of pelvic floor muscles. No endometriosis, adenomyosis, or structural lesions. Findings consistent with myofascial hypertrophy and spasm.

puzzles.explanation: Dyspareunia is pain during sexual intercourse, most commonly resulting from a combination of physiological and psychological factors that trigger protective pelvic floor muscle guarding. In this postmenopausal patient, declining estrogen has caused vulvovaginal atrophy and reduced tissue elasticity, while concurrent anxiety and relationship stress have driven chronic pelvic floor hypertonicity and myofascial trigger point development. The hypertonic, spasmed pelvic floor muscles create a protective barrier that makes penetration painful and physically impossible — a cycle where pain begets more guarding, perpetuating the dysfunction. Treatment typically requires a multimodal approach addressing both the hormonal (topical/systemic estrogen) and neuromuscular components (pelvic floor relaxation, manual therapy, psychological support) to break the pain-muscle tension cycle.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old woman presenting with persistent pain in multiple body regions (back, neck, shoulders, hips, knees). Onset 4 years ago following a mechanical low back strain. Pain described as constant with variable intensity throughout the day. Associated with poor sleep quality and daytime fatigue.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation: difficulty prolonged sitting or standing, unable to return to prior work duties, reduced tolerance for household tasks. Symptoms do not clearly follow expected dermatomal or myotomal patterns. Minimal relief with rest or positional changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain began after low back strain but did not improve as expected over months. Has sought care from multiple providers (orthopedics, neurology, rheumatology). Reports severe sleep disruption with unrefreshing sleep pattern. Denies recent illness, fever, weight loss, or new joint swelling. No significant past medical history of autoimmune or neurological disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Widespread hyperalgesia to light touch across trunk and limbs. Tender points reproducible at multiple non-articular sites. Range of motion preserved in spine with inconsistent restriction to effort. Neurological examination normal (strength 5/5 throughout, sensation intact, reflexes normal). No evidence of joint swelling, inflammation, or systemic findings.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI spine, pelvis unremarkable—no disc herniation, canal stenosis, or structural abnormality to explain widespread pain distribution. Laboratory studies normal: inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), metabolic panel, thyroid function, rheumatologic screen (ANA, RF) all negative. Quantitative sensory testing shows reduced pain thresholds and wind-up phenomenon consistent with altered pain processing.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic pain syndrome represents a transition from acute nociceptive pain to a centralized pain state characterized by amplified pain processing in the central nervous system, often following an initial peripheral injury that has since resolved structurally. The hallmark features—widespread hyperalgesia, preserved neurological examination, normal imaging and labs, and sleep disruption—reflect maladaptive neuroplasticity rather than ongoing tissue damage, which distinguishes it from mechanical or inflammatory diagnoses. The progression from a focal low back strain to multiregional pain with reduced sensory thresholds and wind-up phenomenon demonstrates the self-perpetuating cycle of central sensitization. Effective management requires shifting the therapeutic focus from tissue-based interventions to addressing pain neurophysiology through graded rehabilitation, sleep optimization, and cognitive-behavioural approaches alongside judicious use of pharmacotherapy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old female athlete presents with insidious-onset pain in the forefoot. Pain worsens with weight-bearing activities over the past 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate impact activities and sports. Swelling visible over the forefoot. Antalgic gait with avoidance of push-off phase during walking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Athlete in repetitive jumping/running sport. No acute injury. Symptoms gradually worsening despite activity modification and rest. No prior treatment sought yet.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness directly over second metatarsal head (dorsal aspect). Swelling and prominence of metatarsal head region. Pain with passive dorsiflexion of second metatarsophalangeal joint. Limited metatarsophalangeal joint motion secondary to pain. Gait antalgic with reduced stance phase on affected foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows flattening and fragmentation of second metatarsal head consistent with Freiberg's infraction (osteonecrosis). Dorsal hypertrophic changes present. MRI shows osteochondral lesion with bone marrow edema. T1 signal hypointensity within metatarsal head indicating necrosis. Associated cartilage irregularity.

puzzles.explanation: Freiberg's disease is an osteonecrosis of the metatarsal head, most commonly affecting the second metatarsal in adolescent athletes during periods of rapid growth. The condition arises from repetitive compressive loading and shear forces across the metatarsophalangeal joint, leading to disruption of blood supply and subsequent bone death and fragmentation. The insidious onset in a jumping athlete with progressive forefoot pain, along with the characteristic flattening and fragmentation visible on imaging, is typical of this condition—teenagers involved in high-impact sports bear the greatest risk. Management focuses on activity modification, offloading the metatarsal head through footwear or orthotic intervention, and in some cases surgical debridement if significant osteochondral damage or joint stiffness develops.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 22-year-old male. Sudden onset severe shoulder pain during contact sport. Arm feels unstable and 'out of place.' Unable to use arm. Significant anxiety about the injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Complete loss of shoulder function immediately after injury. Cannot lift arm or move it away from body. Pain severe with any attempted movement. Arm held motionless against torso.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during tackle—arm forcibly driven backward and outward. No prior shoulder problems, no instability history, no connective tissue disorders. First traumatic shoulder injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder visibly squared-off with fullness anteriorly. Humeral head palpable anteriorly below clavicle. Arm locked in internal rotation and adduction. Unable to externally rotate or abduct. Axillary nerve sensation intact. Rotator cuff strength untestable due to guarding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP, scapular Y, axillary views) shows humeral head displaced anteriorly relative to glenoid fossa. Post-reduction imaging reveals associated glenoid rim fracture and possible labral avulsion. MRI confirms rotator cuff intact but demonstrates labral pathology consistent with acute traumatic event.

puzzles.explanation: Shoulder dislocation occurs when the humeral head is displaced from the glenoid fossa, most commonly anteriorly (>95% of cases), typically from high-energy trauma forcing the arm into abduction and external rotation. The characteristic squared-off appearance and anterior fullness reflect the humeral head's anterior displacement, while the arm's locked position in internal rotation and adduction is a protective mechanism against the severe pain and apprehension that accompany acute instability. The mechanism in this case—forceful backward and outward driving of the arm during contact—is classic for anterior dislocation in young, active individuals without prior shoulder pathology. Associated injuries like glenoid rim fractures and labral avulsions are common with first-time traumatic dislocations and significantly increase recurrence risk; the intact rotator cuff and preserved axillary nerve sensation are favorable prognostic findings, though the degree of labral damage will largely determine the need for surgical stabilization versus conservative management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old overhead athlete reports intermittent shoulder pain with a sensation of the shoulder 'giving way' during throwing. Symptoms are vague but reproducible with certain movements in sport.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate cocking phase of throw without apprehension. Unable to perform weighted activities (e.g., push-ups, bench press) without feeling shoulder is unstable. Symptoms worse in positions of shoulder abduction and external rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Overhead athlete with progressive symptoms over past 2 seasons correlating with increased throwing volume. No discrete traumatic injury. Denies prior shoulder surgery or known hypermobility syndrome. Reports sense of fatigue in shoulder during extended practice.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive sulcus sign (inferior shoulder subluxation with traction). Positive apprehension and relocation tests. Laxity demonstrated with inferior translation in dependent position (drawer sign). Scapular dyskinesis evident during active motion. Reduced internal rotation relative to external rotation (reverse position). Strength 4+/5 indicating dynamic stabilizer weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows enlarged rotator cuff interval with increased capsular volume. Posterior labral fraying (SLAP pathology) associated lesion. Excessive glenohumeral translation documented on imaging. No discrete labral detachment (rules out Bankart). Ultrasound demonstrates increased capsular volume and laxity on dynamic maneuvers.

puzzles.explanation: Multidirectional instability (MDI) represents excessive glenohumeral translation in multiple planes—superior, inferior, and posterior—stemming from capsular laxity and dynamic stabilizer insufficiency rather than a discrete labral tear. Unlike unidirectional instability from acute trauma, MDI develops insidiously in overhead athletes through repetitive microtrauma, scapular dyskinesis, and rotator cuff fatigue that overwhelms the dynamic restraints; the progressive symptoms over multiple seasons with increased throwing volume and absent traumatic history are classic. The clinical presentation here—positive sulcus and drawer signs indicating inferior laxity, apprehension in the cocking phase, and reduced internal rotation from posterior capsular tightness—reflects the pathological suprasensitivity to positional stress. MRI findings of enlarged rotator cuff interval, increased capsular volume, and posterior labral fraying (SLAP lesions) confirm the capsular origin and associated degenerative changes common in chronic MDI.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Neonate presents with upper limb weakness and loss of function noted shortly after birth. Parents report decreased movement and apparent pain or discomfort with arm handling during the first few days of life.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Infant demonstrates severe limitation in shoulder movement. The arm is held in a characteristic resting posture with reduced abduction and external rotation. Upper limb protective positioning is evident; infant resists passive shoulder movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Delivery was complicated by shoulder dystocia requiring manual traction to deliver the infant. Neonatal exam performed within 24 hours of birth noted the motor deficit. No prior upper limb trauma or congenital anomalies.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination: biceps and deltoid strength grossly weak (0-1/5). Moro reflex is absent on affected side; lower limb Moro intact. Passive shoulder abduction and external rotation severely limited. No claw hand or wrist drop observed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brachial plexus reveals avulsion of the C5-C6 nerve roots from the spinal cord. The upper trunk demonstrates signal abnormality and discontinuity. Nerve root avulsion is confirmed with imaging findings consistent with preganglionic injury.

puzzles.explanation: Erb's palsy results from avulsion or severe stretching of the upper trunk of the brachial plexus (C5-C6 nerve roots), most commonly occurring during difficult deliveries involving shoulder dystocia and excessive traction on the neonatal head and neck. The characteristic presentation reflects the motor innervation of these roots: profound weakness in the deltoid and biceps with loss of shoulder abduction and external rotation, leaving the arm in the classic 'waiter's tip' posture of adduction and internal rotation. The absent ipsilateral Moro reflex with preserved lower limb reflexes helps localize the injury to the upper plexus, distinguishing it from other brachial plexus injuries or central causes. While most cases recover spontaneously within weeks to months as edema resolves, preganglionic avulsion injuries like this one carry a poorer prognosis and may require surgical intervention if function does not return by 3-4 months of age.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 30-year-old athlete reports recurrent episodes of ankle giving way, particularly during cutting or pivoting movements. The instability started after a previous ankle injury several months ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk confidently on uneven terrain without fear of ankle giving way. Struggles with quick directional changes and single-leg activities. Normal weight-bearing walking is mostly stable, but sudden movements trigger instability.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic ligamentous laxity follows previous ankle sprains and incomplete rehabilitation. Proprioceptive deficits persist despite symptom resolution. Multiple recurrent inversion episodes have established chronic instability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive anterior drawer test indicates significant tibiotalar translation. Inversion stress testing demonstrates hypermobility. Proprioceptive testing reveals impaired balance. Ankle proprioception is diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates chronic lateral ligament attenuation with scar tissue. Anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL) shows partial thickening and signal changes consistent with healed but weakened ligament. No osteochondral lesion or syndesmotic involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic ankle instability develops when previous ankle sprains result in permanent ligamentous laxity and proprioceptive deficits, leaving the ankle mechanically unstable and neuromotorily compromised. The lateral ligament complex—particularly the anterior talofibular ligament—fails to fully restore its structural integrity, leading to the characteristic 'giving way' episodes during dynamic activities requiring rapid directional control. This patient's positive anterior drawer test and hypermobility on inversion stress reflect the underlying ligamentous attenuation visible on imaging, while the proprioceptive deficits explain why even normal walking remains vulnerable to sudden perturbations. Rehabilitation must address both mechanical stability through strengthening and neuromuscular control through proprioceptive retraining, as scar tissue alone cannot fully restore the functional restraint of healthy ligament.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young child presents with developmental delay in motor milestones and abnormal movement patterns. Muscle tone abnormalities and stiffness restrict normal motor function. The condition is nonprogressive but becomes more apparent with age and development.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child walks on toes with knees bent and legs crossing in front of each other. Hip adduction is pronounced bilaterally. Unable to achieve normal heel-strike during ambulation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Birth complicated by prolonged labour and low Apgar scores. Motor difficulties were apparent within first weeks of life. Parents note the pattern of movement dysfunction has not worsened or improved significantly over years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked spasticity in bilateral lower extremities with hyperreflexia (3+/5) and sustained ankle clonus. Hip flexors and adductors tight bilaterally. Scissor gait evident. Upper extremities demonstrate normal tone and strength. No weakness in antigravity muscles; tone abnormality is primary finding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain demonstrates periventricular leukomalacia with signal changes in white matter around the ventricles. Ventricular dilation may be present secondary to white matter loss. The imaging findings correlate with the pattern of spastic diplegia observed clinically.

puzzles.explanation: Cerebral palsy is a permanent, nonprogressive disorder of movement and posture resulting from brain injury sustained during the perinatal period, most commonly from hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy as reflected in the complicated birth history here. The spastic diplegic form — characterized by bilateral lower limb spasticity with relatively preserved upper limb function — typically results from periventricular leukomalacia, a pattern of white matter injury around the lateral ventricles that disrupts the corticospinal pathways controlling the legs. The characteristic scissor gait with toe-walking, hip adduction, and knee flexion emerges as growth outpaces the child's ability to control increasingly lengthening limbs against tight hip flexors and adductors. While the underlying brain lesion does not worsen, the functional consequences become more apparent during development, making early recognition and management of spasticity critical to preserving motor function and preventing contractures.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old reports persistent low mood, fatigue, and difficulty concentrating over past 8 months. Describes feeling unmotivated and withdrawn from usual activities. No recent major life stressors or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot sustain focus at work or engage socially. Avoids previously enjoyed hobbies. Noticed mood temporarily lifts during physical activity—symptoms return within hours of stopping. No improvement with rest alone.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset 8 months ago. Previously active and engaged. No psychiatric history. Attempted one antidepressant (sertraline) for 6 weeks with minimal effect. Started attending gym 4× weekly 10 weeks ago; reported sustained mood improvement correlating with exercise frequency.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate decreased from 82 to 68 bpm over 10 weeks. VO₂ max improved 15% on repeat cardiopulmonary testing. PHQ-9 score reduced from 18 to 7. No neurological deficits. Cardiovascular examination normal. Sleep log shows consistent 7–8 hours nightly.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: TSH, B12, and metabolic panel normal. No anaemia or thyroid dysfunction. Brain imaging not indicated. Psychiatric evaluation excluded bipolar disorder and major depressive disorder requiring pharmacotherapy. Mood trajectory correlates precisely with exercise adherence and fitness gains; no other interventions changed.

puzzles.explanation: Exercise-induced mood improvement reflects the neurobiological effects of regular physical activity on monoamine neurotransmission, hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis regulation, and brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) expression—mechanisms that can be as potent as pharmacological intervention for mild-to-moderate mood symptoms. In this case, the patient's sustained improvement correlating precisely with exercise frequency and fitness gains, combined with normal thyroid and metabolic screening, demonstrates that symptoms were responsive to non-pharmacological intervention rather than indicating treatment-resistant depression. The transient nature of mood elevation during activity followed by symptom return highlights the state-dependent benefit of acute exercise, whereas the cumulative 11-point PHQ-9 reduction reflects adaptations from consistent training—improved sleep architecture, cardiovascular efficiency (resting heart rate drop), and enhanced aerobic capacity. This presentation is characteristic of individuals whose mood dysregulation responds robustly to structured exercise, making exercise prescription a first-line consideration in primary care physiotheapy assessment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged woman with diabetes presenting with shoulder pain. Onset gradual over recent weeks. Pain present at rest and with activity, disrupting sleep and daily routines.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to reach overhead or behind back. All shoulder movements restricted. Patient reports difficulty with dressing, grooming, and sleeping on affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Risk factors include middle age, female gender, and poorly controlled diabetes mellitus. The condition often follows minor shoulder trauma or immobilization. Onset is typically gradual with progressive loss of motion over weeks to months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive external rotation severely limited (near 0 degrees). Scapulohumeral rhythm abnormal with early scapular elevation. Discomfort at end-range in capsular pattern. No weakness detected.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the shoulder are typically normal without bony abnormality. MR arthrography demonstrates thickening and enhancement of the joint capsule. The rotator cuff interval is tightened with loss of the normal axillary pouch distensibility.

puzzles.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis is a self-limiting inflammatory condition characterized by fibrosis and contracture of the glenohumeral joint capsule, resulting in global loss of passive and active motion in a capsular pattern. The condition develops idiopathically or following minor trauma or immobilization, with strong associations to middle age, female gender, and diabetes—likely due to altered collagen cross-linking and impaired inflammatory resolution in hyperglycemic states. The hallmark presentation combines insidious onset of severe restriction (particularly external rotation near zero), pain that disrupts sleep and function, and normal radiographs alongside MR arthrography findings of capsular thickening and loss of axillary pouch distensibility. Diagnosis rests on clinical recognition of the capsular pattern of restriction rather than imaging, as the condition is self-resolving over 12–36 months, with physiotherapy targeting gentle mobilization and restoration of motion during the later phases.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of urinary urgency and frequency that significantly impacts her quality of life. She experiences a persistent urge to urinate 12-14 times daily and 3-4 times nightly, even when the bladder contains minimal urine.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient has modified daily activities to stay near bathroom facilities. Social activities and travel are significantly limited. Sleep is disrupted by nocturia with 3-4 nighttime voids.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began insidiously without identified trigger or prior urinary tract infection. No history of pelvic surgery, trauma, or radiation. Denies use of diuretics or caffeine excess. No diabetes or neurological conditions reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vital signs normal; abdominal examination reveals no masses or tenderness. Pelvic floor muscle assessment shows normal strength but evidence of increased tone and difficulty with relaxation. Neurological examination of lower extremities and perineal sensation are intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bladder ultrasound performed post-void demonstrates normal post-void residual volume of 25 mL. Renal ultrasound is unremarkable with no hydronephrosis. Urinalysis is completely normal with negative culture. Cystoscopy reveals no structural abnormalities, malignancy, or interstitial cystitis features.

puzzles.explanation: Overactive bladder (OAB) is a syndrome of urgency with or without incontinence, characterized by involuntary detrusor contractions during the storage phase that override normal voluntary control—typically idiopathic in origin. The diagnosis is fundamentally clinical, defined by symptoms rather than urodynamic findings: urgency and frequency with a normal post-void residual, normal imaging, and negative urinalysis rule out structural, infectious, and neurological causes. The pelvic floor hypertonicity observed here is common in OAB, as patients often develop compensatory muscle tension in response to frequent urgency, creating a vicious cycle of tension and symptom amplification. This 55-year-old's insidious onset without prior trauma or systemic disease, combined with normal neurological examination and intact bladder capacity, points to primary OAB rather than neurogenic or post-void-related dysfunction, making behavioral modification and antimuscarinic therapy the first-line approach.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young athlete reports anterior knee pain that worsens with jumping and cutting activities. Pain is activity-dependent, onset gradual over weeks to months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain during landing from jumps and deceleration activities. Single-leg stance and stairs provoke symptoms. Pain often resolves during activity but returns severely after sport, with stiffness the next morning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset follows increased training volume or intensity, particularly jumping-based sports. Weakness in hip or core muscles may contribute to abnormal loading patterns. Previous patellar or knee pathology may predispose to tendinopathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is localized to the patellar tendon insertion at the tibial tubercle. Single leg squats demonstrate poor motor control and knee valgus. Pain occurs with resisted knee extension testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging reveals thickened patellar tendon with mixed echogenicity and increased vascularity on Doppler. MRI demonstrates signal changes within the tendon substance. Imaging findings correlate with symptom duration and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Patellar tendinopathy is a chronic overuse injury of the patellar tendon, typically affecting young athletes in jumping sports due to repetitive eccentric loading and microtrauma that exceeds the tendon's capacity to remodel. The condition is characterized by activity-dependent anterior knee pain that paradoxically improves during exercise but returns with delayed-onset stiffness, reflecting the tendon's initial mobilization followed by inflammatory response. Key to diagnosis are localized tenderness at the tibial tubercle insertion and the imaging findings of tendon thickening with increased vascularity, which correlate with chronicity and represent failed healing with pathological neovascularization. Hip and core weakness commonly contribute by increasing knee valgus during landing, creating abnormal patellar tracking and eccentric loading — addressing these deficits forms the foundation of effective management alongside load modification and tendon-loading rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old male presents with acute-onset weakness and numbness in the shoulder region following a severe fall with direct shoulder impact.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot initiate any movements requiring shoulder abduction. Passive range of motion is preserved. Weakness is the primary functional limitation rather than pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct trauma from fall onto shoulder from standing height. No prior neurological symptoms, shoulder pathology, or recurrent dislocations. First neurological symptoms noted immediately post-injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, deltoid muscle is atrophic and significantly weakened with shoulder abduction strength 2/5. Sensation is diminished over the lateral shoulder in the regimental badge distribution. Axillary nerve traction test is positive. Rotator cuff strength is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder X-rays rule out fracture or dislocation. MRI shows no rotator cuff tear but reveals signal changes in the axillary nerve course with surrounding soft tissue edema. Electromyography demonstrates denervation potentials in deltoid and axillary nerve conduction slowing.

puzzles.explanation: Axillary nerve palsy results from compression or traction injury to the axillary nerve as it passes through the quadrangular space, bounded by the teres major and minor, subscapularis, and long head of triceps. This nerve is particularly vulnerable to injury following direct shoulder trauma or traction during falls, as occurred here, where the force disrupts the nerve without necessarily causing fracture or dislocation. The clinical hallmark is selective weakness of the deltoid with preserved rotator cuff function and a characteristic sensory loss in the regimental badge distribution over the lateral shoulder, reflecting the axillary nerve's purely motor and sensory supply to these structures. MRI evidence of nerve signal changes and denervation potentials on EMG confirm the neuroaxial injury, distinguishing this from suprascapular or other brachial plexus lesions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old with severe shoulder pain and marked loss of arm mobility. Symptoms acute in onset 6 weeks ago. Pain constant, worse with any attempted movement. No significant relief with rest or NSAIDs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot lift arm away from body or reach overhead. Dependent on others for dressing, grooming, and personal care. Unable to sleep on affected side. Pain reproduced with both active and passive motion attempts.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute symptom onset 6 weeks ago coincided with a major orthopedic intervention for chronic joint disease. Pre-intervention ROM was already severely restricted. Recovery trajectory slower than expected. Patient reports feeling apprehensive with passive stretching attempts.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive forward flexion 60° with pain and guarding. Passive external rotation severely limited to 20°. End-range passive motion elicits apprehension. Shoulder musculature visibly atrophic. No active rotator cuff strength testing performed due to pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs demonstrate secure metallic component fixation with no evidence of loosening, migration, or subsidence. No heterotopic ossification. Soft tissue swelling consistent with expected post-operative inflammation. Articular surfaces show appropriate positioning.

puzzles.explanation: Post-total shoulder replacement (TSR) arthroplasty represents a surgical intervention for end-stage glenohumeral arthropathy, yet the early post-operative period carries significant risk for stiffness and dysfunction—particularly when pre-operative mobility was already compromised. The acute, severe loss of motion with pain on both active and passive attempts, combined with marked guarding and apprehension, reflects the inflammatory cascade and protective muscle splinting that commonly follows TSR, especially in patients with poor baseline range. The imaging findings of secure component fixation and expected soft tissue inflammation confirm a mechanically successful procedure, yet this does not guarantee functional recovery; aggressive pain management and carefully graded passive mobilization during this critical 6-week window are essential to prevent adhesion formation and arthrofibrosis. The visible atrophy and psychological apprehension underscore why early physiotherapy intervention is crucial in TSR rehabilitation, as neuromuscular re-education and progressive loading must resume promptly once acute inflammation is managed.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A woman in her 60s reports sensation of heaviness and pressure in the pelvis that worsens as the day progresses. She also notices increased urinary frequency and occasional urgency.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports she must avoid prolonged standing and cannot perform heavy lifting without significant symptom exacerbation. Symptoms improve within minutes of lying flat.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Parity and vaginal deliveries are significant risk factors for pelvic floor dysfunction. Chronic straining from constipation or chronic cough may contribute. Advancing age and menopause worsen pelvic floor laxity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On pelvic examination, anterior vaginal wall bulging is evident with Valsalva maneuver. The bladder prolapse may be visualized or palpated during straining. Grade of prolapse is classified from 1-4 based on extent of descent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dynamic pelvic MRI shows anterior compartment prolapse with bladder descent of >2 cm below pubic symphysis during straining, consistent with grade 2-3 cystocele. No posterior compartment involvement.

puzzles.explanation: A cystocele is herniation of the bladder into the anterior vaginal wall, resulting from weakness or separation of the supporting fascia and pelvic floor musculature. This typically develops after repeated childbirth trauma, chronic increases in intra-abdominal pressure (from straining or cough), or age-related loss of pelvic floor elasticity and innervation — mechanisms clearly at work in a woman of advancing age with significant parity. The characteristic progressive heaviness and urinary symptoms that worsen with standing and improve with recumbency reflect the gravitational worsening typical of pelvic floor dysfunction, and the anterior wall bulging evident on Valsalva during examination is the diagnostic hallmark. Dynamic imaging demonstrating bladder descent beyond the pubic symphysis during strain confirms the diagnosis and grades severity, guiding decisions about whether conservative pelvic floor rehabilitation or surgical intervention is indicated.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 64-year-old reports acute onset hip pain with severe functional limitation. Pain is constant and worsens with movement. Patient describes inability to perform basic self-care and mobility tasks. Onset was sudden, approximately 1 week ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain significantly worse with hip flexion beyond 60 degrees and adduction across midline. Patient unable to weight bear independently and requires walker. Dressing, toileting, and bed transfers severely restricted. Symptoms not relieved by rest or positioning adjustments.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began acutely 5-7 days ago without trauma. Patient reports preceding chronic hip pain and stiffness that had progressively worsened over months. No recent falls, direct injury, or acute inflammatory symptoms. Prior conservative management attempted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion limited to 45 degrees, abduction to 15 degrees, internal rotation severely restricted. Marked muscle guarding around hip. Trendelenburg sign positive. Straight leg raise weak and painful. Surgical scar present on anterior hip with appropriate healing, minimal drainage or erythema. No calf swelling, warmth, or signs of DVT.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show metallic femoral component and acetabular cup in anatomically appropriate position. No lucencies, loosening, or dislocation evident. CT may show component alignment within normal parameters. Clinical presentation and imaging timeline consistent with expected early post-operative phase and protective muscle guarding response.

puzzles.explanation: Post-total hip replacement (THR) represents the early phase following surgical arthroplasty for end-stage osteoarthritis, typically within the first 2-6 weeks when tissue healing and neuromuscular re-education are paramount. The acute onset of pain and severe functional limitation in this patient reflects expected post-operative inflammation, muscle guarding, and protective inhibition rather than implant failure — plain radiographs confirm proper component positioning without signs of loosening or dislocation. The dramatic restrictions in hip flexion beyond 60 degrees and adduction, combined with positive Trendelenburg sign and weak straight leg raise, are characteristic of the immediate post-operative period when hip musculature is inhibited by pain and surgical trauma to the hip abductors and flexors. Successful rehabilitation demands progressive passive and active-assisted range of motion, protected weight-bearing progression, and resolution of muscle guarding over weeks to months, with most patients achieving functional independence by 8-12 weeks if complications such as DVT, infection, or instability are absent.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-week postpartum woman reports visible abdominal bulging at the midline and generalized abdominal weakness. Concerned about functional recovery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform sit-ups or roll bed-side without support. Difficulty carrying infant. Symptoms worsen with straining or Valsalva maneuver but improve with abdominal bracing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Uncomplicated vaginal delivery 6 weeks prior. Weight gained 16 kg during pregnancy. Return to exercise initiated without postpartum rehabilitation guidance. No diastasis history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable gap of 3 fingers width between rectus muscles at midline above umbilicus, reducing to 2 fingers below umbilicus. Rectus muscles separate further with abdominal contraction. Intact fascia, no hernia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound confirms diastasis rectus abdominis with interrectal distance 3.5 cm. Linea alba thickness normal. No ventral hernia present. Dynamic ultrasound shows muscle separation increase with contraction.

puzzles.explanation: Diastasis rectus abdominis is a separation of the rectus abdominis muscles along the linea alba, occurring when pregnancy-related hormonal laxity (particularly relaxin) and increased intra-abdominal pressure overwhelm the tensile capacity of the fascial midline. It is not a hernia but rather a structural separation that impairs force transmission across the anterior core, particularly during loaded functional tasks like sit-ups, rolling, or carrying. The condition typically peaks around 6 weeks postpartum and often resolves with targeted motor control training focusing on abdominal bracing and progressive load management, though persistence beyond 12 months or worsening with active contraction — as seen with this patient's dynamic separation on ultrasound — suggests need for structured rehabilitation. Early return to exercise without neuromuscular re-education, as occurred here, frequently delays recovery by perpetuating intra-abdominal pressure strategies rather than restoring linea alba integrity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A weightlifter reports sudden sharp pain in the anterior chest during a heavy bench press. Immediate weakness and inability to continue lifting. Notices visible change in chest appearance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot initiate or perform any pushing movements. Unable to perform horizontal adduction against gravity. Pain increases with any attempt to adduct the arm across the body.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rupture typically occurs during heavy weightlifting with eccentric loading during bench press. The distal tendon attachment at the humerus is the most common rupture site. Steroids or chronic tendon pathology may predispose to rupture.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Strength testing confirms profound weakness in horizontal adduction and internal rotation. Resisted adduction produces no contraction. Palpation along the anterior chest reveals a palpable gap or indentation where muscle continuity is lost. Asymmetry with the contralateral side is striking.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the chest reveals discontinuity of the pectoralis major tendon near its humeral insertion. Muscle retraction and edema are evident at the rupture site. Imaging confirms the site and extent of rupture for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: Pectoralis major rupture occurs when the distal tendon avulses from its humeral insertion, typically during heavy eccentric loading such as bench pressing—the muscle simply cannot tolerate the combined forces of deceleration and maximal contraction. The dramatic presentation reflects the sudden loss of the primary horizontal adductor and internal rotator of the shoulder; patients lose all ability to initiate pushing or adduction movements and often report a palpable gap or visible indentation across the anterior chest as the muscle retracts proximally. Steroids, prior tendon pathology, or repetitive heavy loading can weaken the tendon-bone interface, making rupture more likely even during routine lifts. MRI confirmation of tendon discontinuity and muscle retraction guides surgical repair, which is typically recommended in active individuals to restore strength and function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports knee pain with intermittent mechanical symptoms. Pain is localized around the knee joint. Swelling develops gradually, particularly worse after prolonged weight-bearing activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to perform rotational movements of the knee under load. Squatting and deep flexion with rotation are significantly limited. Occasional episodes of knee 'giving way' or sudden inability to extend fully.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tear may follow a specific injury with twisting mechanism or develop insidiously in degenerative knees. Previous meniscal pathology or knee injuries increase risk. Degenerative tears are common in middle-aged and older patients.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Joint line palpation reproduces localized tenderness. Knee effusion present. Range of motion limited in flexion, particularly with rotation. Special tests for meniscal irritation are positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates meniscal tear in the medial or lateral meniscus with classification of tear pattern. T2 signal intensity within the meniscal substance indicates tear. Imaging localizes the tear and guides treatment decisions.

puzzles.explanation: A meniscal tear involves disruption of the fibrocartilage menisci that act as shock absorbers and stabilizers within the knee joint. These tears can occur acutely from a twisting injury—particularly during loaded rotation—or gradually in degenerative knees where the tissue loses its structural integrity. The characteristic presentation here, with mechanical symptoms like giving way, inability to achieve full extension, and sharp pain on rotational movements under load, reflects the meniscus's critical role in knee mechanics; when torn, it cannot distribute compressive forces normally or provide proprioceptive stability. Joint line tenderness and a positive McMurray's or Thessaly test, combined with MRI confirmation of meniscal signal change, are the clinical hallmarks that distinguish this from other causes of knee pain like ligamentous injuries or patellofemoral dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old man reports progressive difficulty perceiving sound over past 5 years. Describes sounds as muffled and unclear. Wife reports he frequently asks for repetition and turns up television volume significantly. No ear pain, drainage, or recent trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Struggling to follow conversations in noisy environments (restaurants, group settings). Difficulty with phone calls. Avoids social situations due to communication frustration. Finds higher-pitched sounds particularly hard to distinguish. Performance worsens with background noise.

clues.label: History
