clues.text: Gradual onset without specific injury or infection. 45-year occupational exposure to loud machinery (machinist) without consistent hearing protection. No ototoxic medications, ear surgery, or recent infection. Father had similar age-related hearing difficulties. 40 pack-year smoking history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weber test lateralizes to right (better hearing side). Rinne test: air conduction audible longer than bone conduction bilaterally, but gap present. Otoscopy shows cerumen impaction; removed bilaterally. Post-removal, sound perception unchanged. Otoscopic findings otherwise normal. Speech discrimination reduced at 68%.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Audiometry: bilateral sensorineural loss predominantly above 2000 Hz; air-bone gaps 15-25 dB across all frequencies. Bone conduction thresholds elevated at 4000-8000 Hz (classic pattern). Tympanometry normal (Type A). Acoustic reflexes intact bilaterally. Results consistent with noise exposure and age-related contribution.

puzzles.explanation: Hearing loss results from damage to the inner ear sensory structures (cochlea and hair cells), most commonly from cumulative noise exposure, aging, or both acting synergistically. This patient's bilateral sensorineural pattern with high-frequency emphasis (4–8 kHz) is the classic fingerprint of noise-induced hearing loss, compounded by age and further shaped by smoking and genetic predisposition; the 45-year occupational exposure without consistent protection accounts for the progressive presentation. Difficulty in noisy environments and reduced speech discrimination reflect the specific vulnerability of high-frequency perception, which is essential for consonant recognition and conversational clarity. Weber lateralization to the better ear and intact bone conduction thresholds confirm sensorineural rather than conductive pathology, distinguishing this from the cerumen impaction that was incidentally removed but not causative.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete reports insidious onset of pain in the lower abdomen and groin region over several weeks. Pain is activity-related and worsens with sport participation. No acute injury or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is provoked by hip adduction and flexion under load. Crossing legs, rapid directional changes, and forceful kicking reproduce symptoms. Symptoms settle with activity modification but recur with return to sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Condition occurs in athletes with repetitive hip adduction and flexion activities. Asymmetric hip strength and flexibility contribute to excessive shear at the symphysis. Previous hip or groin injuries increase risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness is elicited over the pubic symphysis, worse with direct palpation. Pain is reproduced with resisted hip adduction in supine, and with combined flexion-adduction against resistance. Pain is NOT reproduced with hip abduction or external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate symphyseal joint changes including widening, sclerosis, and bone resorption. Bone scintigraphy shows increased uptake at the pubic symphysis. MRI reveals edema and inflammatory changes at the symphyseal joint.

puzzles.explanation: Osteitis pubis is an overuse injury affecting the pubic symphysis, typically seen in athletes performing repetitive hip adduction and flexion under load—such as kicking sports, rapid directional changes, and crossing movements. The condition arises from chronic shear stress at the symphyseal joint, often exacerbated by asymmetric hip strength and flexibility that concentrates loading asymmetrically across the pubis. The insidious onset and activity-related pattern are characteristic, with pain reproducing specifically with resisted adduction and flexion-adduction rather than abduction or external rotation, reflecting the biomechanical nature of the injury. Radiographic evidence of symphyseal widening, sclerosis, and bone resorption—along with increased bone uptake on scintigraphy—confirms the inflammatory and degenerative process at the joint margin, distinguishing it from acute symphyseal injury or other groin pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old woman presents with progressive weakness and functional decline over 2 years. Difficulty rising from chair without armrest support. Walking slower and shorter distances than previously. Recent fall from trip on uneven pavement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Reduced grip strength (12 kg versus 22 kg in contralateral hand). Difficulty with stairs and walking long distances. Loss of independence with heavy household tasks. Slow gait velocity (0.7 m/sec versus normal 1.4 m/sec). Difficulty getting out of bed and toilet independently.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 76 with sedentary lifestyle and reduced physical activity. Inadequate protein intake (40g daily versus recommended 1.0-1.2 g/kg body weight). Loss of appetite with reduced food intake. Multiple comorbidities including osteoarthritis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease limiting activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible muscle wasting in upper extremities and thighs. Reduced grip strength 12 kg (abnormal cutoff <16 kg for women). Slow gait speed 0.7 m/sec (abnormal <1.0 m/sec). Reduced lower extremity strength particularly hip flexors and knee extensors (4/5). Difficulty rising from chair in <12 seconds (requires 18 seconds).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan shows normal bone mineral density ruling out primary osteoporosis. Body composition analysis demonstrates fat mass normal but lean muscle mass significantly reduced at 35% total body weight (normal >40% in women age 75). No focal vertebral fractures or lytic lesions present.

puzzles.explanation: Sarcopenia is age-related skeletal muscle loss and weakness, resulting from a combination of reduced physical activity, inadequate protein intake, and the natural decline in anabolic signaling that occurs with aging. The condition is characterized by progressive loss of muscle mass and strength disproportionate to changes in body weight, reflected here in reduced lean muscle mass despite normal fat distribution and bone density. This patient's presentation—visible muscle wasting, grip strength below the female cutoff of 16 kg, gait speed of 0.7 m/sec, and difficulty with basic functional tasks like rising from a chair—exemplifies the functional impairment that defines sarcopenia and increases fall risk. Management focuses on progressive resistance training and optimized protein intake (1.0–1.2 g/kg body weight daily), which can meaningfully improve muscle mass, strength, and functional independence in older adults.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old former construction worker. Progressive breathlessness on exertion over 18 months. Persistent dry cough. Reports reduced energy and feels generally deconditioned.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk more than 100 metres without needing to stop and catch breath. Climbing stairs leaves patient significantly winded. Dyspnea out of proportion to activity level. Fatigue limits all sustained activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual insidious onset without acute illness trigger. 40-year history of occupational dust exposure (construction/masonry). Never smoker. No recent infections. Progressive decline over past 18 months despite rest and conservative management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Fine crackles heard at lung bases bilaterally on auscultation, predominantly end-inspiratory. Clubbing of fingernails present. Reduced chest expansion symmetrically. Mild cyanosis noted. Oxygen saturation drops with mild exertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest: bilateral reticular patterns with traction bronchiectasis in basilar distribution, consistent with usual interstitial pneumonia pattern. Pulmonary function tests: restrictive pattern with FVC 55% predicted, DLCO significantly reduced at 35% predicted.

puzzles.explanation: Pulmonary fibrosis is a progressive lung condition characterized by excessive collagen deposition and scarring of the alveolar tissue, leading to loss of elasticity and impaired gas exchange. In occupational fibrosis like this, prolonged inhalation of inorganic dust (silica, asbestos, or other construction materials) triggers chronic inflammation that gradually transforms healthy lung parenchyma into rigid fibrous tissue. The bilateral basilar crackles, clubbing, and severe dyspnea out of proportion to exertion are hallmark features reflecting the restrictive physiology—reduced lung compliance means patients require excessive effort to inflate stiffened lungs, while the dramatically reduced DLCO and oxygen desaturation on activity indicate significant alveolar-capillary impairment. The insidious 18-month progression despite conservative management reflects the relentless nature of established fibrosis, where the damage is largely irreversible and functional decline accelerates as the fibrotic process spreads.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old female cyclist with chronic anterior knee pain reports increasing difficulty with cycling distances. Pain worsens with climbing hills and longer rides. Progressive functional decline.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with squatting and descending stairs. Pain on deep knee bend. Unable to perform single-leg activities. Activity limitations more pronounced during eccentric loading tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cycling hobby with recent increase in distance and intensity. No acute injury. Gradual progressive onset. Multiple attempted conservative interventions with limited benefit.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive J-sign patellar tracking. VMO strength weak. Patellar irritation with movement. Pain with loaded knee extension. Tight hip muscles evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows normal articular cartilage and no structural pathology. No meniscal involvement. Mild synovial effusion present. Femoral anteversion noted on imaging. No evidence of ligamentous injury or osteochondral lesion.

puzzles.explanation: Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS) is a common overuse condition arising from altered patellar tracking and load distribution at the tibiofemoral joint, typically without structural damage—a key distinction from other knee pathologies. The condition results from the dynamic interplay of hip weakness (particularly VMO insufficiency and tight hip musculature), femoral anteversion, and training errors; eccentric loading during stairs and hills exacerbates pain by increasing patellofemoral contact forces. This cyclist's gradual onset without acute injury, combined with normal imaging and the characteristic J-sign tracking abnormality during movement, exemplifies PFPS, where symptoms reflect neuromuscular dysfunction rather than structural pathology. Success typically requires addressing the kinetic chain deficits—hip and core strengthening—rather than simply modifying cycling load alone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old reports acute sharp chest wall pain that worsens with deep breathing. Onset was sudden, 2 days ago. Pain is focal and constant but exacerbated by movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to lie on affected side. Reaching across body and bending forward are particularly limited. Difficulty with overhead activities and sustained postures.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Strain typically follows direct trauma, coughing, or sudden twisting movement. Heavy lifting or forceful physical exertion may precipitate the injury. Previous chest wall strain increases recurrence risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, tenderness is elicited along the intercostal muscles. Reproducing pain with intercostal space compression confirms muscular origin. Restricted breathing pattern is evident with protective guarding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows localized edema and small hematoma within the 5th–6th intercostal muscles. Rib imaging is normal; no fracture or pneumothorax.

puzzles.explanation: Intercostal muscle strain results from acute micro-tearing of the muscles between the ribs, typically triggered by sudden forceful movements like coughing, twisting, or heavy lifting. The injury is characterised by focal, sharp chest wall pain that worsens with respiration and movement — the muscles must contract with every breath, making even deep inspiration painful — combined with reproducible tenderness on palpation and intercostal compression. The protective guarding and restricted breathing pattern seen in these injuries reflect both pain inhibition and the respiratory demands placed on injured musculature with each breath cycle. Ultrasound confirmation of localised oedema and haematoma, with normal rib imaging, excludes fracture and more serious cardiopulmonary pathology, making prognosis favourable with activity modification and gradual restoration of intercostal function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Adult presents with intermittent neck pain and episodes of stiffness. Symptoms worse with certain head movements. History of connective tissue laxity with generalized joint hypermobility. No recent trauma reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot turn head fully to either side without discomfort. Difficulty looking down when flexing neck. Avoids rapid or large cervical movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known connective tissue laxity throughout the body with hypermobile joints in multiple limbs. Two episodes of transient arm weakness and paresthesias over past 6 months. Never had rheumatoid arthritis screening. No recent trauma or falls.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sharp-Purser test reproduces transient tingling in fingers. Bilateral upper limb hyperreflexia (3+) and positive Babinski bilaterally. Lower limb strength intact 5/5. Sensation intact. Generalized hypermobility evident on Beighton score.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the cervical spine demonstrates increased atlantodental interval measuring greater than 5mm. Cervical myelopathy is evident with T2 signal changes in the spinal cord at C1-C2 level. The odontoid process is displaced posteriorly with significant cord compression. Imaging confirms preganglionic compression of the cervical myelon.

puzzles.explanation: Atlantoaxial instability results from excessive laxity at the C1-C2 articulation, allowing abnormal motion and anterior displacement of the atlas relative to the axis—a particularly common finding in connective tissue disorders where ligamentous laxity affects the alar and transverse ligaments. This instability can lead to progressive spinal cord compression, especially with flexion or rotation movements, explaining both the movement-related neck pain and the transient neurological episodes (arm weakness, paresthesias) seen here. The posterior displacement of the odontoid process with myelopathic cord changes on imaging represents frank spinal cord compression, evidenced clinically by hyperreflexia and bilateral Babinski signs indicating upper motor neuron involvement. Sharp-Purser testing reproducing symptoms confirms the functional instability at C1-C2, making careful movement restriction and surgical stabilization essential to prevent progressive neurological deficit.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports a palpable lump on the wrist or hand that may be painless. The cyst may enlarge and shrink over time. Some patients report aching pain or functional limitation depending on cyst location and size.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty with sustained gripping or repetitive wrist extension. Pain worsens when bearing weight through the hand (e.g., leaning on palm).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient recalls gradual onset without clear injury, though minor wrist trauma occurred 2-3 months prior. No constitutional symptoms. No prior treatment attempted. Similar lesions have not appeared elsewhere.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation reveals a smooth, compressible mass that transilluminates brightly, confirming fluid-filled nature. Mass is fixed to underlying joint capsule. Wrist extension ROM is restricted compared to unaffected side. Scaphoid tenderness is absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates a unilocular, anechoic lesion arising directly from the scapholunate joint capsule, measuring 1.2 cm with clear cystic boundaries and no internal septations.

puzzles.explanation: A ganglion cyst is a benign, fluid-filled outpouching that arises from joint capsules or tendon sheaths, most commonly at the dorsal wrist near the scapholunate joint. The exact etiology remains unclear, though repetitive microtrauma and joint capsule weakening are implicated—consistent with this patient's minor wrist trauma preceding gradual onset. Clinically, ganglion cysts present as smooth, transilluminant masses that may fluctuate in size and cause functional limitation through mechanical compression, particularly with gripping or weight-bearing activities that stress the underlying joint. Ultrasound confirmation of a unilocular anechoic lesion directly attached to the joint capsule is diagnostic and rules out other soft-tissue pathologies; while many resolve spontaneously, symptomatic cysts may require aspiration or surgical excision if they persistently limit function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old female rugby player sustains direct blow to left thigh during match. Acute severe pain and rapid swelling over lateral thigh. Unable to continue playing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to weight-bear on left leg. Rapid onset of extensive bruising visible within minutes. Unable to perform kicking or change of direction movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tackle occurred 2 hours ago. No previous thigh injuries or bleeding disorders. Patient takes no anticoagulants. Swelling has continued to progress since impact.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant swelling and bruising visible over lateral thigh. Hip abduction painful. Knee ROM limited by thigh pain. Strength testing limited by guarding. Visible muscle deformation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows muscle hematoma with fiber disruption. Vastus lateralis and biceps femoris involvement noted. Hematoma measures 6x8 cm. No fracture or compartment syndrome signs.

puzzles.explanation: Muscle contusion is a blunt force injury causing myofibril disruption and bleeding within muscle tissue, ranging from minor fiber damage to extensive hematoma formation depending on impact force and mechanism. The lateral thigh is particularly vulnerable in contact sports due to minimal soft tissue coverage over the vastus lateralis and biceps femoris, explaining the rapid swelling and visible deformation within minutes of the tackle. The progressive hematoma and pain-limited weakness reflect ongoing hemorrhage and inflammatory edema rather than structural disruption severe enough for fracture or true compartment syndrome, though monitoring for evolving compartment pressure remains essential in the acute phase. Recovery depends on hematoma resorption and myofibril regeneration, typically progressing over weeks as pain-free ROM is restored before return to sport.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old with acute onset severe hip pain and significant walking difficulty. Pain is constant, worsened by movement, partially relieved by rest. Patient reports feeling unstable and requiring walking aids. No trauma or fall reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot bend hip fully or cross legs without sharp pain and protective muscle tightening. Low chairs and car transfers are impossible. Side-lying preferred over back-lying. Pain and stiffness worse with adduction movements across the body midline.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset 2 weeks ago. Patient reports prior chronic hip pain, morning stiffness, and progressive difficulty with walking over previous months. Recent period of reduced activity. No fall, trauma, or infection signs. Started supervised physiotherapy within 48 hours of symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion and adduction severely restricted and guarded. Hip abduction and external rotation relatively preserved. Anterior hip and proximal thigh tender to palpation. Positive Trendelenburg sign. Normal temperature. No warmth, redness, or effusion. No hip instability on gentle passive motion within restricted ranges.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show femoral and acetabular metallic components in appropriate neutral alignment. Acetabular component anteversion 15–20 degrees. Femoral component well-fixed without subsidence. Symmetric soft tissue contours. No radiolucencies, osteolytic changes, or periprosthetic lucency. No evidence of malposition or loosening.

puzzles.explanation: Post-total hip replacement complications present with acute onset pain and functional loss in the weeks following surgery, often stemming from soft tissue inflammation, capsular tightness, or early hip precaution violations rather than implant failure. The severe restriction in flexion and adduction with preserved abduction and external rotation is characteristic of capsular contracture or anterior soft tissue irritation, commonly triggered by crossing the midline — a motion that violates the 90-degree flexion precaution critical in the early post-operative phase. Normal radiographic alignment and absence of loosening or radiolucencies rule out mechanical implant problems, pointing instead to soft tissue healing dysfunction and motor control deficits, as reflected in the positive Trendelenburg sign. Early physiotherapy addressing gentle progressive range of motion, weight-bearing tolerance, and hip precaution education is essential to prevent chronic stiffness and restore functional independence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old woman with shoulder pain and stiffness. Onset 8 weeks ago. Pain worse with movement, especially overhead activities. Some days better than others. Taking regular analgesia.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot lift arm above shoulder height independently. Difficulty reaching behind back or across body. Cannot carry shopping bags or perform sustained gripping tasks. Pain limiting most upper limb activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Shoulder gradually became painful and weak over several months before recent intervention. No clear traumatic event recalled. No prior shoulder problems or surgery. Not diabetic. Motivated and compliant with rehabilitation program.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive forward flexion 110°, abduction 95°. Active forward flexion 45°, abduction 20°. Weakness on isometric supraspinatus testing with guarding. Scapular dyskinesis visible. Palpable scar tender. Neurovascular intact. Pain limits strength assessment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows repaired supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons in anatomical position with no gap, retear, or fluid collection. Subacromial bursa unremarkable. Repair integrity maintained. No acute inflammation or haematoma.

puzzles.explanation: Post-rotator cuff repair represents the immediate post-operative phase following surgical reattachment of a torn rotator cuff tendon, typically lasting 8–12 weeks as healing progresses through early collagen deposition and neovascularization. The marked gap between passive and active range of motion—passive forward flexion of 110° against active range of only 45°—is pathognomonic of this phase: the repair is mechanically intact (confirmed on ultrasound), but neuromotor control and muscular strength remain substantially compromised due to pain inhibition, deconditioning, and the biological lag in tendon-bone healing. Scapular dyskinesis and guarding on supraspinatus testing reflect protective muscle splinting and altered neuromuscular patterning common in early repair stages. Progressive restoration of active motion and strength depends on graded loading within tissue tolerance, making adherence to structured rehabilitation protocol essential to prevent stiffness while protecting repair integrity during this vulnerable window.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old athlete presents with severe leg pain following a crush injury to the lower leg during contact sport. Pain severity seems excessive relative to the visible soft tissue damage.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain dramatically worsens with passive stretching of the ankle and foot. Active ankle movements are increasingly difficult. Pain continues to escalate despite standard pain relief measures.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 4-6 hours ago from direct blunt crush during sport. Visible swelling began immediately and has worsened steadily. No fracture was identified on initial assessment. Patient reports the pain trajectory is unusually rapid for a simple contusion.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Anterior leg is visibly swollen and firm/tense. Passive ankle plantarflexion elicits severe pain. Foot sensation is diminished in a stocking-like pattern. Pulses are palpable bilaterally, but the skin appears pale. No obvious fracture deformity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Compartment pressure measurement via needle manometer shows anterior compartment pressure of 65 mmHg at rest, exceeding the critical threshold of 60 mmHg. CT imaging of the leg demonstrates swollen muscles with no fracture. MRI shows edema within the anterior compartment muscles.

puzzles.explanation: Acute compartment syndrome develops when increased pressure within a closed fascial compartment compromises microvascular perfusion and tissue oxygenation, typically following crush injury, major trauma, or severe soft tissue swelling. The hallmark clinical triad — pain out of proportion to visible injury, pain on passive stretch of muscles within the compartment, and progressive neurological deficits — reflects ischemic muscle and nerve tissue; in this case, the anterior compartment pressure of 65 mmHg exceeding the critical threshold of 60 mmHg confirms the diagnosis. The stocking-like sensory loss and pale, tense anterior leg despite palpable pulses distinguish compartment syndrome from simple contusion or vascular occlusion: pulses persist because arterial pressure initially exceeds intracompartmental pressure, yet microvascular flow and muscle viability are already compromised. This is a surgical emergency requiring urgent fasciotomy to restore tissue perfusion and prevent permanent contracture and neurological damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports knee pain that comes and goes unpredictably. Sometimes the knee feels like it's catching or getting stuck, requiring a shift in position to feel normal again. Swelling develops, particularly after activities like walking or standing for extended periods.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform rapid directional changes or pivoting movements without fear of the knee giving way. Deep squatting and kneeling are intolerable. Stair descent is cautious and controlled.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tear may follow a specific injury with twisting mechanism or develop insidiously in degenerative knees. Previous meniscal pathology or knee injuries increase risk. Degenerative tears are common in middle-aged and older patients.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive McMurray test with reproduction of clicking at joint line. Thessaly test positive at 20 degrees knee flexion. Significant joint effusion with positive ballottement sign. Medial or lateral joint line tenderness present depending on meniscal location.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates a vertical or complex tear within the medial meniscus extending to the articular surface, with elevated T2 signal indicating fluid within the tear. Tear configuration and location guide meniscectomy vs. repair planning.

puzzles.explanation: A meniscal tear represents disruption of the fibrocartilage menisci that act as shock absorbers and load distributors within the knee joint. Tears occur either acutely from twisting mechanisms—particularly with combined flexion and rotation—or insidiously through degenerative changes in middle-aged and older populations. The characteristic mechanical symptoms of catching, clicking, and giving way reflect the unstable meniscal fragment interfering with normal tibiofemoral mechanics, while the unpredictable swelling and effusion following weight-bearing activities occur as synovial inflammation develops around the injured tissue. McMurray's test and Thessaly testing, combined with joint line tenderness and MRI confirmation of the tear configuration, allow clinicians to distinguish meniscal pathology from other intra-articular knee injuries and determine whether the tear is amenable to repair or requires meniscectomy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old right-handed graphic designer. Gradual onset of dorsal wrist pain over 8 weeks. Pain is sharp, localized to one side of the wrist, and triggered by specific hand movements. No swelling, no numbness or tingling in fingers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with sustained gripping and repetitive wrist extension (reaching, lifting objects, prolonged mouse use). Symptoms improve with rest. No pain at night or with passive wrist movements. Pain-free range of motion exists, but resisted movements in one direction reproduce symptoms significantly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began after increasing workload and poor desk ergonomics 2 months ago. No history of wrist trauma, rheumatoid arthritis, or systemic inflammatory disease. Previous similar episode 3 years ago that resolved with activity modification. No morning stiffness. Symptoms do not affect the opposite wrist.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness along the dorsal wrist over the bony prominence of the radius. Pain reproduced with resisted wrist extension against resistance. No joint swelling, warmth, or effusion. Grip strength reduced on affected side due to pain avoidance. Sensation and nerve palpation normal. Pain-free passive wrist flexion and extension present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickened extensor carpi radialis brevis tendon (3.2 mm, normal <2 mm) with hypoechoic areas and increased Doppler flow signal. No tendon tear or free fluid. MRI confirms T2 signal hyperintensity within the tendon substance without discontinuity. Findings consistent with reactive tendinopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Wrist tendinopathy represents a continuum of tendon pathology characterized by thickening, disorganization of collagen fibers, and neovascularization in response to cumulative microtrauma — rather than frank inflammation as the primary driver. In this case, the extensor carpi radialis brevis tendon has developed reactive changes from repetitive wrist extension loading combined with poor ergonomics, explaining why resisted extension reproduces pain while passive range remains pain-free and imaging shows thickening with increased vascularity but no tear. The recurrent nature and predisposition to exacerbation with activity overload reflect the underlying biomechanical deficit and reduced tendon resilience. Management focuses on load management, progressive strengthening, and ergonomic correction rather than anti-inflammatory strategies alone, as the pathology is fundamentally degenerative-adaptive rather than inflammatory.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports progressive weakness and pain in the forearm during gripping tasks. Symptoms began insidiously over weeks. Age 45, plays recreational tennis twice weekly.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activities involving sustained grip or repetitive pronation/supination significantly worsen symptoms. Patient reports pain when opening jars or using a screwdriver, worse by end of day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms followed a period of intensive racquet sports training 3 months ago. No prior trauma or surgery. Patient reports similar symptoms did not improve with rest alone over 6 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness localized to a point 3–4 cm distal to the lateral epicondyle along the dorsal forearm. Resisted supination with elbow extended reproduces pain more reliably than resisted pronation. Finger extensor strength 4/5 on manual testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution ultrasound shows focal enlargement of the radial nerve at the proximal third of the forearm, consistent with compression. Nerve conduction studies demonstrate slowing of motor conduction across the proximal forearm with preserved distal segments.

puzzles.explanation: Radial tunnel syndrome occurs when the radial nerve is compressed as it passes through the proximal forearm, typically at one of several potential entrapment points including the arcade of Frohse or the extensor carpi radialis brevis origin. Unlike radial nerve palsy at the spiral groove, radial tunnel syndrome spares the radial sensory branch, presenting with pure motor weakness and pain rather than sensory loss—the focal nerve enlargement and conduction slowing seen on imaging here are characteristic. The condition classically develops from repetitive pronation/supination activities or sustained gripping, particularly in overhead athletes, and resisted supination with the elbow extended is the most reliable provocative test because it maximizes tension on the compressed nerve segment. Conservative management with activity modification, pronation/supination avoiding exercises, and bracing typically precedes intervention, though those who fail conservative treatment over 6-12 weeks may benefit from surgical decompression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports sharp shoulder pain with onset following a specific shoulder injury. Pain is worse with overhead reaching and when crossing the arm across the body.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform pressing movements (e.g., push-ups, bench press) and struggles with activities requiring arm adduction across midline. Overhead reaching is severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred from direct shoulder impact or fall onto outstretched arm approximately 1 week ago. Patient reports visible deformity or prominence at the top of the shoulder. No significant prior shoulder history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited directly over the AC joint. Crossover adduction test reproduces pain at the AC joint. The O'Brien test may also elicit AC joint pain with positive result.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate clavicular displacement and AC joint space abnormalities based on sprain grade. Weighted views show degree of clavicular elevation if AC ligament is disrupted. MRI reveals ligament injury and associated findings.

puzzles.explanation: Acromioclavicular joint sprains result from ligamentous injury to the joint capsule and coracoclavicular ligaments following direct trauma or fall onto the shoulder, with severity graded by the degree of clavicular displacement relative to the distal clavicle. The AC joint's superficial location makes it particularly vulnerable to traumatic injury, and the mechanism of direct impact or FOOSH (fall on outstretched shoulder) seen in this case is classically responsible. Pain with cross-body adduction and overhead loading is characteristic because these movements compress the joint and stress the damaged ligaments, while visible superior displacement at the shoulder tip reflects disruption of the coracoclavicular ligaments that normally anchor the clavicle. Imaging findings of clavicular displacement on weighted radiographs, combined with positive crossover adduction and O'Brien tests, reliably localize the pathology and guide management based on sprain grade.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old woman with a non-healing wound on the lower leg for several months. History of leg swelling. Denies recent trauma to the area.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Swelling worsens with prolonged standing. Patient reports heaviness and aching in the leg by end of day. Compression clothing provides temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Swelling began gradually over 10 years. Prior leg ulcer healed 2 years ago. Current wound opened in the same medial lower leg location 3 months ago despite initial healing. No significant trauma or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Medial leg ulceration with surrounding hemosiderin staining and lipodermatosclerosis. Edema pitting on pressure. Calf circumference increased bilaterally. Venous stasis signs present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Venous duplex ultrasound demonstrates valvular incompetence in superficial and deep veins. Reflux pattern documented. Ankle-brachial index normal excluding arterial disease.

puzzles.explanation: Venous leg ulcers result from chronic venous insufficiency, where damaged or incompetent valves allow retrograde blood flow and venous hypertension in the lower limbs—typically from deep vein thrombosis, varicose veins, or primary valve failure. These ulcers characteristically develop on the medial lower leg and gaiter region, where venous pressure is highest, and are preceded by years of progressive swelling, heaviness, and skin changes like hemosiderin staining and lipodermatosclerosis from chronic inflammation and tissue remodeling. The recurrent ulceration at the same site reflects persistent underlying venous dysfunction; without addressing the valvular incompetence documented on duplex imaging, the wound remains in a high-pressure environment prone to breakdown. Compression therapy remains the cornerstone of management, counteracting the elevated interstitial pressure that impairs healing and drives the cycle of ulceration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Infant presents with persistent head tilt to one side. Parents report the child prefers to keep head in this position and seems resistant to turning the other way. Age of presentation varies from birth to early infancy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child demonstrates difficulty tracking objects or following caregivers' faces when turning head toward the restricted side. Passive attempts to correct the head position meet resistance, and the child returns to the preferred tilt.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Birth history unremarkable or notable for difficult/instrumented delivery or breech presentation. Condition present from birth or noticed within first 2–3 months of life. No recent trauma. Family history not contributory.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, a tight mass or rope-like sternocleidomastoid muscle is palpable on the affected side. Head tilt toward the affected side with chin rotation away is evident. Facial asymmetry may be present with flattening on the affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the sternocleidomastoid muscle demonstrates focal enlargement and echogenic appearance consistent with fibrosis. MRI may show muscle pathology and structural changes. Imaging rules out spinal or skeletal abnormalities.

puzzles.explanation: Congenital torticollis results from fibrosis and shortening of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, typically following birth trauma, difficult delivery, or intrauterine positioning that causes muscle damage and subsequent scar tissue formation. The condition presents in early infancy with characteristic ipsilateral head tilt and contralateral chin rotation, alongside a palpable 'rope-like' or fibrotic mass within the affected muscle belly — distinguishing it from other causes of torticollis such as neurological or vertebral abnormalities. The infant's resistance to passive correction and preferential head positioning reflect the mechanical contracture of the shortened muscle, which can progressively worsen facial asymmetry if left untreated. Early recognition and intensive stretching protocols are critical to restore cervical mobility and prevent secondary deformities of the face, skull, and spine.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports acute onset severe elbow pain and rapid swelling following a direct blow or fall. The arm is held protectively in a flexed position and any attempt to straighten it causes intense pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform any functional activities requiring the arm — cannot push off a chair, cannot use the arm for self-care, cannot support weight through the affected arm.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fracture typically follows direct blow to the olecranon or fall on flexed elbow. High energy trauma results in displaced fractures. Low energy injuries may result in undisplaced fractures.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, significant swelling and ecchymosis are present over the olecranon. A palpable step-off may be felt at the fracture site. Crepitus may be elicited with movement of fracture fragments.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate olecranon fracture with classification of displacement and comminution. Fracture line is visible with relationship to the joint surface assessed. Imaging guides treatment decisions regarding operative intervention.

puzzles.explanation: Olecranon fractures result from direct trauma to the posterior elbow or falling on a flexed arm, disrupting the bony prominence that forms the insertion of the triceps and is critical for elbow extension. This injury presents acutely with severe pain, rapid swelling, and a characteristic inability to actively extend the elbow — the loss of triceps function is the clinical hallmark that distinguishes it from soft tissue injuries. Displacement and comminution determine treatment; undisplaced fractures may be managed conservatively with immobilization, while displaced or comminuted patterns typically require surgical fixation to restore the articular surface and restore functional extension. The step-off deformity and visible fracture line on imaging confirm the diagnosis and severity, guiding whether operative intervention is needed.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No trauma, otalgia, or recent air travel. Patient reports mild imbalance persisting even at rest, but no positional component and no fluctuating symptoms suggestive of Menière disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain and internal auditory canal normal with no T2 signal abnormality or enhancement of vestibulocochlear nerve. Excludes demyelinating disease and structural lesions. Findings consistent with peripheral vestibular origin.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular neuritis is inflammation of the vestibular portion of the vestibulocochlear nerve, typically viral in origin and often preceded by upper respiratory infection as seen here. It presents with sudden, severe vertigo and postural instability but distinctively spares hearing and shows no positional component — differentiating it from BPPV and Menière disease. The positive head impulse test with catch-up saccades and unilateral caloric paresis reflect acute unilateral peripheral vestibular dysfunction, while the normal neuroimaging excludes central causes and demyelination. Recovery typically occurs over weeks to months as central compensation gradually restores vestibular balance.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old presents with significant back pain and stiffness. Reports acute worsening 2–3 weeks ago. Movement is severely limited; cannot bend forward or rotate trunk. No trauma reported. Pain is constant, worse with any spinal movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate sitting longer than 10–15 minutes. Bed mobility requires assistance; rolling over causes sharp pain. Walking limited to short distances with support. Bending, twisting, and lifting are impossible. Dressing and personal hygiene tasks require adaptive strategies or help.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Major spinal procedure performed 2–4 weeks ago for longstanding degenerative disease and nerve compression. Pre-operative symptoms included radiating leg pain and claudication with walking. Post-operative restrictions include strict bracing and no spinal flexion/extension. This is first spinal intervention. Incision healing without signs of infection.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Surgical scar clean, dry, well-healed without dehiscence. Spinal range of motion severely restricted (<10° flexion/extension, minimal rotation). Muscle strength in lower extremities reduced (4/5 in key myotomes). Sensory changes persist in dermatomal pattern. Reflexes intact or slightly diminished. No signs of active infection or wound complications.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays demonstrate correct hardware positioning, intact instrumentation, no screw loosening or migration. Early bridging callus visible at fusion levels. MRI shows adequate decompression of neural structures, no epidural hematoma, no residual canal stenosis. Vertebral body alignment maintained.

puzzles.explanation: Post-spinal fusion syndrome represents the immediate post-operative recovery phase following instrumented fusion for degenerative disc disease and spinal stenosis, during which the patient must maintain strict immobilization and activity restriction while bone fusion consolidates and neural tissues recover from decompression. The acute worsening 2–3 weeks post-operatively, coupled with severe movement-related pain and profound functional limitation in sitting, bending, and mobility, reflects normal post-operative inflammation, surgical trauma to musculature, and adherence to critical bracing protocols—not hardware failure or infection, as imaging confirms proper alignment and early callus formation. Persistent lower extremity weakness and sensory changes are common in this phase as demyelinated nerve roots gradually remyelinate following decompression; these typically resolve over weeks to months with structured rehabilitation. The rehabilitation focus at this stage emphasizes gradual mobilization within post-operative restrictions, core activation without flexion/extension, and progressive functional retraining to restore tolerance for sitting, standing, and walking.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult reports gradual onset of involuntary shaking at rest and difficulty with fine motor tasks. Movement feels slower and stiffer overall. Recent falls despite no acute injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot button clothing or use cutlery with usual dexterity. Walking requires concentration and has become slow and shuffling. Difficulty rising from chairs and turning in bed.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began gradually 18 months ago. No acute injury or recent medication change. One relative diagnosed with similar movement disorder in their 70s. Symptoms have progressively worsened without remission.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, resting tremor is evident at 4-6 Hz frequency. Rigidity is present with cogwheel quality on passive motion testing. Gait demonstrates shuffling pattern with reduced arm swing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain may show midbrain atrophy. PET imaging demonstrates reduced dopamine uptake in striatum confirming diagnosis. DaTscan shows reduced dopaminergic activity in the putamen.

puzzles.explanation: Parkinson's disease results from progressive degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra, leading to dopamine depletion in the striatum and disruption of motor control circuits. The classic triad of resting tremor, rigidity with cogwheel quality, and bradykinesia emerges gradually over months to years, reflecting the slow neuronal loss — by symptom onset, approximately 50–60% of dopaminergic neurons are already lost. The shuffling gait with reduced arm swing and postural instability seen here reflect the loss of automatic motor regulation, while difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning reflects the basal ganglia's crucial role in movement initiation and scaling. DaTscan and PET imaging demonstrating reduced striatal dopamine uptake provide objective confirmation of the underlying neurochemical deficit, distinguishing Parkinson's from other movement disorders and guiding dopaminergic therapy.

aliases: Benign Nocturnal Limb Pains of Childhood

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A school-age child reports intermittent pain in the lower legs, typically in the anterior tibia or calf region. Pain is bilateral and comes and goes unpredictably over weeks to months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain occurs predominantly at rest in the evenings and nights. Activity during the day does not typically provoke symptoms. Child is symptom-free by morning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms correlate with known growth spurts in children typically between ages 3-10. No history of injury or trauma. Family history of growing pains is common. Symptoms are self-limited and have not required intervention.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, no objective findings are present. Lower extremity range of motion is normal and pain-free. Gait is normal during examination with no limp observed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays are normal with no evidence of fracture, physeal injury, or bony lesion. Diagnosis is clinical and based on characteristic presentation; imaging is typically unnecessary unless red flags present.

puzzles.explanation: Growing pains are a benign, self-limited condition of childhood arising from rapid skeletal growth outpacing soft tissue adaptation, particularly during growth spurts between ages 3–10. The characteristic bilateral lower leg pain occurring at rest and predominantly at night reflects the mechanical stress placed on muscles and fasciae during periods of accelerated bone lengthening—symptoms typically resolve by morning and do not correlate with daytime activity. The absence of objective examination findings, normal imaging, and family clustering make this a clinical diagnosis of exclusion; the key to confident diagnosis is recognizing the distinctive nocturnal pattern combined with growth history rather than searching for pathology that isn't there. Reassurance and simple analgesia form the mainstay of management, as symptoms invariably resolve with skeletal maturity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports sudden brief episodes of severe facial pain. Pain is electric shock-like in quality affecting one side of the face. Pain episodes are unpredictable but may be triggered by touch or chewing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports functional avoidance of eating, speaking, and facial grooming due to pain episodes. Difficulty maintaining oral hygiene and sustaining conversations. Quality of life significantly impacted by unpredictability of triggers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Trigeminal neuralgia typically presents in older adults with increased prevalence after age 50. Multiple sclerosis increases risk particularly in younger patients. Vascular compression of the trigeminal nerve root is proposed mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Facial sensation intact in most cases with no dermatomal loss. Pain reproducibly triggered by light touch to perioral region, cheek, or chin. Trigeminal motor function (masseter strength, jaw deviation) normal. No other cranial nerve deficits elicited.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain and brainstem assesses for vascular loop compressing the trigeminal nerve. T1 weighted imaging demonstrates anatomical relationships. High-resolution CISS sequences show nerve-vessel interface.

puzzles.explanation: Trigeminal neuralgia is a neuropathic pain disorder resulting from aberrant firing of the trigeminal nerve, most commonly due to vascular compression of the nerve root at the cerebellopontine angle. The condition is classically characterized by sudden, shock-like pain in one trigeminal distribution (V1, V2, or V3) triggered by innocuous stimuli such as touch, chewing, or speaking — features that make this condition distinctly different from other facial pain syndromes. The marked functional avoidance of eating and speaking, combined with preserved sensory and motor examination findings, is highly characteristic: pain is severe and activity-limiting despite objective neurological findings remaining normal. Diagnosis relies on clinical presentation and high-resolution MRI to identify vascular loops compressing the trigeminal nerve root, which informs both medical management and consideration of surgical decompression in refractory cases.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete reports progressive medial-sided elbow pain that worsens with repetitive overhead activity over several weeks. Pain is activity-dependent and improves with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform throwing activities without exacerbating symptoms. Pain onset correlates with throwing frequency and intensity. Complete cessation of throwing resolves symptoms within days to weeks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Little League elbow occurs in young pitchers typically before skeletal maturity. Excessive throwing volume and poor mechanics increase risk. Rapid growth during adolescence predisposes to apophyseal injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited over the medial epicondyle. Valgus stress testing reproduces medial-sided elbow pain. Forearm pronation and wrist flexion against resistance may reproduce symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show medial epicondyle apophysitis with fragmentation or widening of the apophysis. MRI demonstrates edema and inflammatory changes at the medial epicondyle. Advanced imaging confirms extent of injury.

puzzles.explanation: Little League elbow is an apophyseal injury of the medial epicondyle that occurs in skeletally immature overhead athletes, typically young pitchers whose growth plates are still vulnerable to traction forces. The condition develops from repetitive valgus loading during throwing, which concentrates tensile stress on the medial epicondyle's apophysis rather than the mature ligamentous structures; excessive throwing volume and poor biomechanics accelerate this risk. The medial epicondyle tenderness with positive valgus stress testing reflects the underlying inflammation and potential fragmentation at the apophyseal attachment of the flexor-pronator mass. Unlike adult medial epicondylitis, this injury is entirely activity-dependent and resolves reliably with load cessation, making early recognition and modified activity essential to prevent chronic instability before skeletal maturity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An elderly patient reports acute severe pain in the hip region following a fall. Pain is constant and significantly worsened by any leg movement. Patient is unable to ambulate or weight bear on the affected side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Inability to internally rotate the hip. Pain is severely reproduced by any attempt to move the leg, particularly flexion and external rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Osteoporosis documented on prior DEXA scan. Patient lives alone and has had multiple falls over the past 18 months. No significant trauma history otherwise.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the hip is often held in external rotation and flexion. Tenderness over the hip and groin is severe. Crepitus may be palpated with leg movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs confirm femoral neck fracture with posterior displacement. CT shows comminution of the intertrochanteric region. No associated pelvic injury.

puzzles.explanation: Hip fracture, most commonly a femoral neck or intertrochanteric break, occurs when the proximal femur sustains a break—typically from a fall in an elderly patient with compromised bone quality such as osteoporosis. The severe pain and complete inability to weight-bear are characteristic hallmarks, reflecting both the fracture itself and the disruption of the hip capsule and surrounding soft tissues. The characteristic posture of external rotation and flexion, seen here alongside palpable crepitus and severe groin tenderness, reflects the muscle pull across the fracture site and the associated soft tissue trauma. Posterior displacement and comminution, as confirmed on imaging, indicate a more complex fracture pattern that typically requires surgical intervention and represents a significant mobility threat and mortality risk in the elderly population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Chronic pelvic pain

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with menstruation and deep penetration during intercourse

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive dysmenorrhea over years; infertility workup initiated; prior pelvic surgery negative

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nodularity palpable along uterosacral ligaments; fixed retroverted uterus; tenderness with cervical motion

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows T2 hyperintense lesions in ovary with restricted diffusion and chocolate cysts bilaterally

puzzles.explanation: Endometriosis is a chronic condition in which endometrial tissue grows outside the uterus, most commonly on the ovaries, peritoneum, and uterosacral ligaments, triggering local inflammation and fibrosis. The hallmark presentation of progressive dysmenorrhea and deep dyspareunia reflects the cyclical bleeding and inflammation within these ectopic foci, particularly when lesions involve the posterior compartment as evidenced by nodularity along the uterosacral ligaments and a fixed retroverted uterus. MRI findings of T2 hyperintense foci with restricted diffusion—characteristic of blood products within chocolate cysts—combined with the clinical examination findings of cervical motion tenderness provide strong diagnostic support. The association with infertility results from the inflammatory milieu, adhesions, and ovulatory dysfunction that often accompany more advanced disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old recreational athlete reports anterior knee pain for 6 weeks that worsens with explosive movements and is aggravated by training volume increases. Pain is localized around the knee but character and specific location not yet defined.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform jumping or deceleration-based movements without sharp anterior knee pain. Pain-free with walking and static activities. Symptoms reproducible and consistent with sport participation but resolve completely with 48-hour rest. No night pain or swelling that persists overnight.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset correlating with increased training volume (volleyball 4x per week). Repetitive jumping and landing mechanics in sport. No acute traumatic event. History of patellar tendon soreness 2 years ago. Adequate flexibility initially but recently decreased.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness directly over proximal patellar tendon at superior patellar pole. Quadriceps contraction against resistance elicits pain. Single-leg squat shows valgus knee position (femoral internal rotation) suggesting biomechanical dysfunction. Knee flexion 0-120 degrees pain-free but 120-140 degrees aggravating. Palpable swelling at insertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic thickening of proximal quadriceps tendon (8mm versus normal 5-6mm) with loss of fibrillar echotexture. Increased Doppler signal indicating inflammation and neovascularity. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration without full-thickness tear. T2 signal abnormality within proximal third of tendon.

puzzles.explanation: Quadriceps tendinopathy is a chronic overuse injury affecting the proximal quadriceps tendon at its insertion on the superior pole of the patella, resulting from repetitive eccentric loading during jumping and deceleration activities. The condition develops through cumulative microtrauma and failed tendon healing, characterized by intratendinous degeneration, disorganized collagen fibers, and inflammatory neovascularization — exactly what the ultrasound and MRI demonstrated here. The presentation is classically load-dependent: pain with explosive movements and high training volumes that resolves with rest, while static activities remain pain-free, reflecting the mechanical irritation of a structurally compromised tendon. The biomechanical dysfunction noted on examination — valgus collapse and decreased dorsiflexion mobility — typically perpetuates the problem by increasing quadriceps demand during deceleration, making this a prime target for clinical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old reports overwhelming fatigue disproportionate to activity level, persistent for several months. Also notes generalized weakness, poor sleep quality, and difficulty concentrating. No recent illness or fever.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to return to pre-illness exercise routine. Becomes short of breath and fatigued after 10-15 minutes of light walking. Difficulty with household tasks, climbing stairs. Symptoms do not improve with rest alone.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decline began 6-9 months ago following intensive chemotherapy and/or radiation therapy completed 4-6 months prior. Previously very active. Reports chemotherapy-related peripheral neuropathy in hands and feet. No cardiac history before treatment.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate 105 bpm. Blood pressure slightly elevated. Reduced exercise tolerance on low-intensity treadmill test—dyspnea and fatigue at 50% predicted exertion. Diminished grip strength bilaterally. Mild edema in lower legs. Lung bases show reduced breath sounds.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (40-50%) consistent with chemotherapy-induced cardiotoxicity. Chest X-ray reveals ground-glass opacities in lower lobes suggesting pulmonary toxicity from prior radiation. Pulmonary function testing shows reduced FEV1 and diffusion capacity.

puzzles.explanation: Cancer rehabilitation addresses the complex, multisystem sequelae of oncologic treatment that extend far beyond remission—in this case, chemotherapy-induced cardiotoxicity and radiation-induced pulmonary fibrosis combining to create severe functional limitation. The disproportionate fatigue, dyspnea at minimal exertion, and reduced ejection fraction reflect direct myocardial and pulmonary toxicity from anthracyclines and thoracic radiation, compounded by chemotherapy-related peripheral neuropathy that further limits physical conditioning. Cancer survivors often present with this constellation of treatment-related complications requiring integrated cardiopulmonary rehabilitation, graded exercise prescription, and psychosocial support—a distinct rehabilitation paradigm from single-system recovery. Recognizing these toxicities early allows physiotherapists to safely rebuild exercise tolerance while monitoring cardiac and respiratory thresholds in a population where conventional exercise guidelines must be substantially modified.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports lateral midfoot pain with acute onset during a pushing-off movement. Pain is sharp and localized to the lateral foot, worsened by weight bearing and running.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running and rapid directional changes provoke pain. Single-leg stance and explosive push-off activities are not tolerated. Swelling develops in the lateral midfoot with prolonged activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-week history of progressive lateral midfoot pain in a distance runner. Recent increase in training volume. Alternatively, acute onset after inversion injury during sport. Area notably slow to improve with conservative rest and ice.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pinpoint tenderness at the lateral aspect of the midfoot, just proximal to the fifth metatarsal base. Pain reproduced by single-leg stance and hopping. Lateral foot swelling present. Inability to bear weight on lateral border of foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate transverse fracture at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal. CT imaging assesses fracture displacement and healing. Advanced imaging guides timing of weight bearing progression.

puzzles.explanation: A Jones fracture is a transverse break at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal, typically occurring in athletes during explosive push-off or rapid deceleration movements. Unlike the more common avulsion fracture at the metatarsal base, this fracture sits in a watershed zone with relatively poor blood supply, making it notoriously slow to heal and prone to nonunion even with appropriate rest. The acute lateral midfoot pain during running, combined with the history of progressive symptoms or sudden onset during sport, reflects the high shear forces generated through the lateral column during push-off activities. Diagnosis relies on radiographs showing the characteristic transverse fracture line, and management often requires prolonged immobilization or surgical fixation given the healing challenges and the typical patient's desire to return to high-level running.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports anterior chest and shoulder pain. Onset gradual over 2-3 weeks. Aggravated by upper extremity activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to perform overhead pressing at gym. Throwing activities impossible. Horizontal adduction movements consistently reproduce pain. Symptoms worse with repetitive scapular protraction-based activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient is competitive swimmer training 5 days per week with recent increase in volume. Acute on chronic presentation — underlying tight pectoralis minor noted on previous physio assessment 6 months ago. No direct trauma. Pain started during training block.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness and spasm in pectoralis minor in anterior axilla. Resisted horizontal adduction and scapular protraction reproduce sharp pain. Scapular positioning asymmetrical — anteriorly tilted on affected side. No neurological signs. Shoulder ROM otherwise preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates hyperechoic edema within pectoralis minor muscle belly with disruption of normal fibre echotexture. No full-thickness tear. MRI T2 signal changes localised to medial pectoralis minor, consistent with acute muscle strain. Anterior shoulder structures otherwise unremarkable.

puzzles.explanation: Pectoralis minor strain results from overload of this small but mechanically critical muscle, which originates on ribs 2–4 and inserts on the coracoid process to control scapular positioning and protraction. In overhead and throwing athletes like competitive swimmers, repetitive high-volume training combined with pre-existing muscle tightness creates a vulnerable substrate for acute-on-chronic injury, particularly when training volume spikes. The characteristic anterior chest and shoulder pain with reproduction on horizontal adduction and scapular protraction, alongside the anteriorly tilted scapular positioning on examination, reflects the muscle's role in dynamic scapular stability; strain here destabilizes the shoulder girdle and alters glenohumeral mechanics. Imaging findings of localized edema and disrupted fiber architecture confirm the diagnosis and help rule out referred pain from neural or articular sources, which would present with neurological signs.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 72-year-old presents with 5 days of cough and shortness of breath. Reports feeling unwell, with fatigue and difficulty sleeping. No recent travel or sick contacts reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk more than a few metres without stopping to rest. Difficulty lying flat at night. Minimal exertion triggers breathing difficulty. Previously independent with activities of daily living.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing history of chronic lung disease. Symptoms onset acute over 5 days. No recent falls or trauma. Current smoker. Lives at home with family. Rapid functional decline from baseline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Respiratory rate 28 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 88% on room air, temperature 38.5°C, heart rate elevated. Auscultation reveals crackles in right lower lung field. Increased tactile fremitus over same area. No focal limb weakness or neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows segmental consolidation in right lower lobe with air bronchograms. Blood cultures positive for bacterial pathogen. Sputum culture confirms gram-positive cocci consistent with community-acquired infection.

puzzles.explanation: Pneumonia is an acute infection of the lung parenchyma caused by bacterial, viral, or fungal pathogens that triggers inflammation and consolidation of affected alveoli. In this case, the acute onset of cough, fever, and hypoxia over five days in a patient with underlying chronic lung disease reflects community-acquired bacterial pneumonia — a common complication in smokers with pre-existing airway disease who have reduced mucociliary clearance and compromised host immunity. The clinical findings are characteristic: crackles and increased tactile fremitus in the right lower lobe indicate consolidated tissue, while the segmental consolidation with air bronchograms on imaging confirms alveolar filling with inflammatory exudate. The marked functional decline and severe dyspnoea on minimal exertion underscore the significant physiological impact of infection in a patient with limited respiratory reserve.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Worker reports episodic finger blanching and color changes triggered by cold or vibration exposure. Fingers turn white, then red and painful as they rewarm. Numbness and tingling accompany episodes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen with prolonged grip or repetitive hand tasks. Patient avoids cold environments due to severe finger reactions. Occupational exposure is unavoidable given job demands.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Vibration white finger develops from chronic exposure to vibrating power tools. Jackhammers, chainsaws, and pneumatic tools are common culprits. Duration and intensity of tool exposure correlate with symptom severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Affected fingers show poor color return after cold provocation test. Grip strength reduced in affected hand. Two-point discrimination diminished in fingertips. Pulse oximetry shows reduced oxygen saturation in affected digits compared to unaffected fingers.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nailfold capillaroscopy demonstrates abnormal capillary patterns characteristic of vascular dysfunction. Thermography shows abnormal temperature distribution in affected fingers. Doppler ultrasound assesses digital artery flow and perfusion.

puzzles.explanation: Vibration white finger (VWF) is a secondary Raynaud's phenomenon caused by chronic exposure to vibrating power tools, leading to progressive vascular and neurological damage in the digits. The condition results from repeated mechanical trauma to blood vessels and nerves, causing vasospastic episodes triggered by cold or vibration — presenting as the classic triphasic color changes of white (vasospasm), red (reactive hyperemia), and painful rewarming. The occupational history and correlation between tool exposure duration and symptom severity are hallmark features, distinguishing VWF from primary Raynaud's disease. Objective findings such as diminished two-point discrimination, reduced digital artery flow on Doppler, and abnormal capillary patterns on nailfold capillaroscopy confirm the underlying vascular and somatosensory dysfunction, making this a diagnosis with clear occupational attribution and potential for disability if exposure continues.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old woman with 4 weeks of progressive bilateral leg weakness. Reports fatigue and difficulty concentrating at work. Symptoms began 2 months after a viral illness-like episode.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot climb stairs due to leg weakness. Difficulty with balance and coordination. Numbness in feet and lower legs. Increased bladder urgency. Pain noted when moving eyes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple prior episodes of unexplained neurological symptoms that resolved and recurred. Family history of autoimmune conditions. Current presentation began 2 weeks after upper respiratory infection. Symptoms have been waxing and waning since onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Eye examination shows reduced visual acuity 20/40 right eye with afferent pupillary defect. Visual field testing shows central scotoma on right. Lower extremity strength 4/5 with increased tone and hyperreflexia. Positive Babinski signs bilaterally. Gait shows spasticity and ataxia. Finger-to-nose dysmetria present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain shows multiple T2 hyperintensities in periventricular distribution with some showing gadolinium enhancement. Spinal cord MRI reveals focal T2 hyperintense lesion at cervical level. CSF oligoclonal bands present. Visual evoked potentials prolonged bilaterally.

puzzles.explanation: Multiple sclerosis is a demyelinating autoimmune disease where the body's immune system attacks the myelin sheaths of central nervous system axons, causing inflammation and neurodegeneration. The hallmark feature is dissemination in both space and time — multiple lesions in different CNS locations (here, brain, spinal cord, and optic nerves) occurring at separate times, which explains the relapsing-remitting pattern of unexplained neurological episodes followed by partial recovery. The bilateral optic neuritis with central scotoma and afferent pupillary defect, combined with spinal cord involvement causing lower limb weakness and hyperreflexia, are classic MS presentations; the periventricular and gadolinium-enhancing lesions on MRI, prolonged visual evoked potentials, and oligoclonal bands in CSF together establish the diagnosis. Post-viral onset and female predominance (3:1 ratio) are well-documented risk factors in MS pathogenesis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports sudden-onset unilateral leg swelling and calf pain. Onset occurred over hours to 1–2 days. Pain is constant and aching in character.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot walk normally due to pain and swelling. Lying flat with leg elevated temporarily reduces discomfort. No relief with rest alone; swelling persists.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Risk factors include recent surgery, immobilization, or prolonged travel. Malignancy, hypercoagulable states, and previous DVT increase risk. Oral contraceptive use and pregnancy are important risk factors in women.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Calf circumference on affected side is 3+ cm greater than opposite side. Calf is warm and mildly erythematous. Passive dorsiflexion of ankle reproduces calf pain. No significant asymmetry in ankle or foot pulses bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Venous duplex ultrasound demonstrates absence of venous compressibility and thrombus visualization. Compression ultrasound shows no flow in the involved vein. CT or MR venography confirms diagnosis and extent of thrombosis.

puzzles.explanation: Deep vein thrombosis occurs when a thrombus forms within a deep vein, typically in the lower limb, and results from Virchow's triad: venous stasis (immobilization, surgery), endothelial injury, and hypercoagulability (malignancy, oral contraceptives, pregnancy). The sudden onset of unilateral calf swelling, warmth, and erythema with positive Homan's sign reflects acute venous obstruction and associated inflammation—hallmarks that distinguish DVT from chronic venous insufficiency or simple edema. The 3+ cm circumference difference between limbs captures the significant fluid accumulation proximal to the occluded vein, while preserved distal pulses rule out arterial insufficiency. Compression ultrasound remains the diagnostic standard, showing loss of normal venous compressibility and direct visualization of thrombus, making this a medical-surgical emergency requiring immediate anticoagulation to prevent pulmonary embolism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports localized chest wall pain that occurs during or after running sessions. Pain is described as superficial burning or irritation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specifically aggravated by prolonged running distance and improves with rest. Symptoms worsen in warm weather and with worn or poorly fitting athletic tops.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Runner's nipple occurs from repetitive friction between clothing and chest wall. Male runners are more commonly affected than females. Increased running volume and intensity increase symptom risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, localized erythema and skin breakdown at the nipple are evident. Range of motion and shoulder function are normal. No systemic signs of infection. Reproduction of symptoms occurs when clothing is moved across the affected area.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging is not routinely performed. If obtained, plain radiographs and ultrasound are normal with no underlying bony or soft tissue pathology. Diagnosis is confirmed clinically by history of friction exposure, visible signs of superficial skin damage at the nipple, and resolution with protective measures.

puzzles.explanation: Runner's nipple is a superficial dermatological condition caused by repetitive friction between the nipple and athletic clothing during prolonged running, most common in male runners due to lack of breast tissue coverage. The condition presents as localized erythema, burning, and skin breakdown at the nipple—a purely mechanical injury with no underlying structural pathology. The characteristic worsening with increased mileage, warm weather (which increases moisture and friction), and ill-fitting garments, combined with rapid resolution through friction avoidance or protective measures like lubricants or adhesive patches, makes the diagnosis straightforward and confirms its purely frictional etiology.
