Management is entirely preventive: proper fitted apparel, vaseline or anti-friction products, and consideration of nipple shields in susceptible runners.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old ballet dancer presents with gradually worsening posterior ankle pain over recent weeks. Pain is activity-related and limits training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform pointe work or sustained plantarflexion. Pain worsens with intensity of rehearsal and is preventing return to full training load.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training volume over the past 6 weeks. No prior ankle injury or imaging. Previous episodes of ankle discomfort resolved with rest.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness in posterior ankle compartment. Pain with plantarflexion and inversion combined movement. Resisted plantarflexion reproduces pain. Full ankle ROM available.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ankle MRI shows impingement of posterior structures (flexor hallucis longus and soft tissues) with plantarflexion. Possible posterior talus talar chondral lesion. Achilles intact.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior ankle impingement occurs when plantarflexion compresses soft tissues and bony structures in the posterior ankle compartment, classically affecting dancers and athletes who chronically load plantarflexion positions. The flexor hallucis longus, posterior joint capsule, and talus can be irritated or traumatised by this repeated compression, particularly with the combined plantarflexion and inversion loading seen during pointe work. The condition develops insidiously with training volume increases rather than from acute injury, and distinguishes itself from other causes of posterior ankle pain by its clear mechanical reproduction on combined plantarflexion-inversion and its imaging evidence of posterior soft-tissue crowding on MRI. Management centres on load reduction, plantarflexion avoidance, and careful progressive return to plantarflexion-dominant activities once inflammation settles.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports pain radiating from the low back down one leg. Numbness and tingling accompany the pain in a dermatomal distribution. Functional limitation occurs with bending and walking activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports symptoms are worse in the morning and after prolonged sitting. Standing and walking initially worsen pain but symptoms may ease with continued walking. Certain postures like slumped sitting dramatically increase leg pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset followed a heavy lifting episode 3 months ago. Progressive worsening over weeks. No constitutional symptoms or night pain. Prior episodes of low back pain without radiation resolved spontaneously.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Straight leg raise test is positive with pain reproduction below the knee at 45 degrees. Calf and foot dorsiflexion weakness noted on manual muscle testing. Ankle reflex diminished compared to opposite side. Palpation over the affected nerve root pathway reproduces radiating symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates posterolateral disc herniation at L5-S1 with compression and displacement of the traversing nerve root. T2-weighted images show high signal intensity in the affected dermatomal distribution on DTI, consistent with nerve root edema.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar radiculopathy results from mechanical compression of a spinal nerve root, most commonly by disc herniation, causing pain that radiates distally in a dermatomal distribution. The characteristic presentation here—unilateral leg pain with numbness and tingling following the L5-S1 dermatomal pattern, positive straight leg raise, and associated motor weakness in ankle dorsiflexion—reflects acute nerve root irritation and inflammation. The posterolateral disc herniation directly compressing the traversing nerve root, combined with neuritic edema visible on imaging, explains both the sharp radicular pain and the functional limitations with postures that increase nerve root tension. The mechanical nature of the condition is reinforced by positional sensitivity: symptoms worsen acutely with slumped sitting (which increases nerve root tension) but may improve with progressive walking as spinal motion promotes fluid reabsorption and neurodynamic adaptation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old recreational athlete reports sharp, localized pain at the front of the ankle. Symptoms have been gradual over 6 months. No acute trauma. Pain is reproducible with specific movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with plantarflexion and combined inversion movements. Difficult to walk on uneven terrain and descend stairs comfortably. Prolonged standing on toes or climbing stairs triggers discomfort. Dorsiflexion and eversion movements ease pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple ankle sprains over past 5 years. Most significant inversion injury 18 months ago with inadequate rehabilitation. Symptoms began after that injury but progressively worsened. Conservative treatment (rest, physiotherapy) has provided minimal relief.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness localized to anterolateral ankle gutter. Combined plantarflexion-inversion movement reproduces exact pain. Passive dorsiflexion relieves discomfort. Mild anterior ankle swelling noted. Ligamentous stability intact on stress testing. No significant loss of range of motion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates soft tissue impingement in anterior ankle joint space. Osteochondral lesion or hypertrophic bone formation visible at anterolateral talocrural joint. No syndesmotic involvement. Findings correlate with symptomatic pinching on dorsiflexion reversal test.

puzzles.explanation: Ankle impingement syndrome occurs when soft tissue structures—synovitis, scar tissue, or bone spurs—become trapped in the anterior ankle joint space, typically following inversion injury and inadequate rehabilitation. The condition is characterized by sharp, reproducible pain during plantarflexion and inversion (the mechanism that created the original impingement), with relief in dorsiflexion as the impinged structures escape the joint space. The progressive worsening over 18 months after the initial sprain reflects ongoing mechanical irritation and potential osteochondral changes or hypertrophic bone formation at the anterolateral talocrural joint. Intact ligamentous stability and the localized anterolateral tenderness, combined with positive impingement testing, distinguish this from syndesmotic involvement or residual instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports progressive weakness in hand grip and pinch strength. Fine motor control is affected. Pain or tingling may occur in the medial (ulnar) side of the hand.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with sustained gripping, pinching, and repetitive hand use. Weakness becomes more apparent with fine motor tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of repetitive hand use, vibration exposure, or occupational trauma. Recent onset. No prior wrist or hand surgery. No systemic neurological disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, hypothenar muscle weakness and atrophy are evident. Froment sign is positive indicating thumb interphalangeal flexion with key pinch. Sensation is intact in the medial palm as the dorsal sensory branch is spared.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the wrist demonstrates mass lesion or cyst compressing the ulnar nerve in Guyon's canal. Ultrasound visualizes nerve compression and causative lesion. Nerve conduction studies confirm ulnar nerve motor involvement distal to the wrist.

puzzles.explanation: Guyon's canal syndrome results from compression of the ulnar nerve as it travels through Guyon's canal at the hypothenar eminence, most commonly from a ganglion cyst, lipoma, or occupational trauma. Unlike carpal tunnel syndrome, this condition spares sensation in the medial palm since the dorsal sensory branch of the ulnar nerve branches proximal to the canal—the hallmark finding here. Motor involvement is selective: hypothenar muscles weaken and atrophy, while a positive Froment sign (compensatory thumb IP flexion during key pinch) reveals weakness of the adductor pollicis, which relies on ulnar innervation. Repetitive gripping and vibration exposure increase risk, and nerve conduction studies localizing motor slowing distal to the wrist confirm the diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports progressive foot deformity with pain on the inner side of the ankle. Symptoms worsen with prolonged weight-bearing activities. Onset was gradual over months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty with high-impact activities and single-leg stance. Cannot run or jump. Notices progressive difficulty pushing off with the inner forefoot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 6-12 months in a patient with a history of pes planus or prior ankle injury. No acute trauma. Progressive nature despite conservative treatment. Associated with activities involving prolonged standing or walking on uneven surfaces.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visible flatfoot deformity is evident with loss of medial arch height. Medial ankle tenderness and swelling are present. The single leg heel rise test demonstrates weakness or inability to rise on the medial forefoot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates thickened and abnormal tibialis posterior tendon. Tendon signal changes indicate inflammation and partial tears. The degree of arch collapse and talonavicular subluxation is assessed on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior tibial tendon dysfunction (PTTD) results from degeneration and partial tearing of the tendon that provides dynamic arch support and inversion strength — the primary dynamic stabilizer of the medial longitudinal arch. In Grade 2, the tendon shows structural damage with inflammation and partial tears, causing progressive flatfoot deformity and medial arch collapse as the tendon fails to maintain its supportive function during weight-bearing. The characteristic inability to perform a single-leg heel rise and pain with prolonged standing reflect both the tendon's weakness and the mechanical stress placed on degenerative tissue. PTTD typically develops insidiously over months in individuals with prior ankle injury, systemic conditions predisposing to tendon breakdown, or repetitive high-demand activities — making early recognition and activity modification critical, as Grade 2 represents a window before irreversible structural changes occur.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old patient reports localized lower back pain that worsens with certain movements and prolonged positions. Pain occasionally radiates into the buttock or upper thigh. Symptoms have been present intermittently for several months, with recent worsening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports significant difficulty with prolonged standing and walking; symptoms improve when lying down. Bending forward provides temporary relief, but the patient cannot maintain this position for extended periods.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has a 10-year history of intermittent back pain with gradual progression. Works in manual labor involving frequent bending and lifting. Recent imaging noted degenerative changes in the lumbar spine. No significant trauma or acute injury recalled.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination: full lumbar flexion is pain-free with good ROM; extension is limited and reproduces pain. Ipsilateral side-bending and rotation toward the affected side increase symptoms. Neurological examination is intact; no lower limb weakness or sensory changes. Straight leg raise is negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates facet joint hypertrophy and osteoarthritic changes. Signal changes indicate inflammation within the joint. Degree of facet-mediated stenosis is assessed if neurological symptoms are present.

puzzles.explanation: Facet joint syndrome results from degenerative changes and hypertrophy of the zygapophyseal joints, which bear increased load when disc height diminishes with age. Unlike discogenic pain, facet-mediated symptoms are mechanical — consistently provoked by extension and ipsilateral rotation that compresses the joints, while flexion relieves pain by unloading them. The pattern here is classic: localized lower back pain with buttock referral (within the facet distribution), worse with standing and extension, improved lying down, combined with intact neurological findings that distinguish it from nerve root compression. Repetitive loading from manual labor accelerates the osteoarthritic process, explaining both the chronic intermittent history and recent progression despite imaging changes that may have been present for years.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports sudden onset of widespread joint pain and muscle aches. Also complaining of a new rash on lower legs. General malaise and fatigue noted. Symptoms began 2–3 weeks ago and have been progressively worsening despite rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk long distances due to leg pain and swelling. Difficulty with stairs and standing for prolonged periods. Upper limb overhead activities limited by generalized weakness. Functional mobility significantly reduced compared to baseline.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No known autoimmune disease or significant past medical history. Symptoms began 3–4 weeks after a respiratory infection (cough, sore throat). No recent trauma, travel, or medication changes. Symptoms have not improved with rest or over-the-counter analgesia.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable, non-blanching rash on shins and buttocks. Bilateral ankle and knee swelling with warmth and tenderness on palpation. Mild abdominal tenderness noted. Elevated resting heart rate (96 bpm) and low-grade fever (37.8°C). No focal neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Blood work shows elevated inflammatory markers (ESR >40, CRP >50 mg/L). Antigen testing positive for ANCA or complement abnormalities. Skin or kidney biopsy (if performed) shows small-vessel inflammation consistent with vasculitis. Imaging rules out alternative diagnoses.

puzzles.explanation: Vasculitis is inflammation of blood vessel walls, typically triggered by autoimmune mechanisms, infectious agents, or immune complex deposition—in this case, likely post-infectious small-vessel vasculitis following the recent respiratory infection. The condition classically presents with the triad of palpable purpura (the non-blanching rash on shins and buttocks), arthralgias, and systemic symptoms including malaise and fever, reflecting widespread vascular inflammation. The bilateral joint and ankle involvement, progressive nature despite conservative management, and markedly elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP) are hallmark features that distinguish vasculitis from simple post-viral arthralgia. Confirmation via serology (ANCA positivity or complement abnormalities) and histopathology showing small-vessel inflammation establishes the diagnosis and guides immunosuppressive therapy to prevent organ involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports deep groin pain that started insidiously several months ago. Pain is worse with certain movements and activities but improves with rest. Occasionally feels a catching sensation in the hip.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform cutting and pivoting movements required for sports. Pain is reproducible when hip is positioned in deep flexion combined with internal rotation. Single-leg weight-bearing activities cause discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young athletic patient (25–40 years) with insidious groin pain from repetitive rotation-based sports (soccer, tennis, dance). No acute trauma recalled. Symptoms progressed despite activity modification. Previous labral pathology in ipsilateral or contralateral hip reported by some.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: FABER test, anterior impingement test (90/90 position), and passive internal rotation in hip flexion reliably reproduce anterior groin pain. Hip abduction and external rotation ROM notably reduced. Log roll test and FADIR test exacerbate symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI arthrography demonstrates labral tear with visualization of contrast leaking through the tear. MRI shows the tear morphology and associated findings. Imaging guides treatment decisions regarding surgical intervention.

puzzles.explanation: A hip labral tear occurs when the fibrocartilaginous labrum — which deepens the hip socket and stabilizes the joint — is disrupted, typically through repetitive rotational forces or cumulative microtrauma in athletes. The labrum is particularly vulnerable anterosuperiorly, where deep flexion combined with internal rotation compresses and shears the tissue; this is why the catching sensation and pain with pivoting movements are so characteristic, as patients lose the dynamic sealing effect that prevents femoral head translation. Young athletic populations engaged in cutting sports experience labral tears more commonly through this insidious mechanism than through acute trauma, and the progressive nature despite rest reflects the labrum's limited healing capacity. MRI arthrography, which allows contrast to permeate through the tear site, remains essential for confirming the diagnosis and determining whether conservative physiotherapy or surgical repair is indicated.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old squash player presents with radial wrist pain affecting athletic performance. Pain on thumb side of wrist with gripping racquet. Symptoms over 3 weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with gripping and racquet handling in sport. Pain worsens with forceful thumb pinch and wrist deviation. Functional limitation in sport and work activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in squash training frequency with 4 matches per week. Repetitive gripping and dorsiflexion movements during sport.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Radial-sided wrist pain reproduced with Finkelstein test. Thumb abduction strength testing painful. Swelling visible over radial styloid region. Pain with thumb opposition against resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates first dorsal compartment tenosynovitis with fluid around APL and EPB tendons. Tendons thickened with hypoechoic appearance. No subluxation evident.

puzzles.explanation: De Quervain's tenosynovitis is an inflammatory condition affecting the first dorsal compartment of the wrist, where the abductor pollicis longus (APL) and extensor pollicis brevis (EPB) tendons are compressed as they pass over the radial styloid. It typically develops from repetitive thumb abduction and wrist extension movements — precisely the gripping and racquet handling demands that triggered symptoms in this athlete's increased training load. The condition is clinically distinctive for its radial-sided pain, positive Finkelstein test (thumb flexion within a clenched fist reproduces sharp pain), and the characteristic swelling over the radial styloid that ultrasound confirms as tenosynovitis with tendon thickening. Functionally, patients lose precision pinch and grip strength, making activities like racquet sports particularly provocative — the very mechanism that drives pain with forceful thumb opposition and repetitive loading.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports neck pain with radiating pain down the arm. Numbness and tingling in the hand. Difficulty gripping and fine motor tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports symptoms are worse with neck extension and turning the head away from the affected side. Lying flat with neck support provides relief. Symptoms are intermittent, triggered by prolonged desk work and poor posture.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient is a 52-year-old with a 4-week history of gradual symptom onset. Symptoms began after increased computer work and prolonged overhead activities. Prior episodes of neck stiffness, now worsening. No significant trauma or acute onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, upper limb strength testing reveals dermatomal weakness corresponding to nerve root involved. Sensory testing demonstrates dermatomal sensory loss. Spurling test reproduces the radiating arm pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine shows left-sided disc herniation at C6-C7 with compression of the C7 nerve root in the neural foramen. No evidence of myelopathy. Imaging correlates with dermatomal weakness and sensory loss on examination.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical radiculopathy results from nerve root compression within the cervical spine, commonly due to disc herniation, osteophytes, or foraminal stenosis that irritates or compresses the exiting nerve root. The dermatomal pattern of weakness, sensory loss, and radiating arm pain reflects irritation of a specific cervical nerve root — in this case C7 — causing predictable motor and sensory deficits in its distribution. Neck extension and contralateral rotation mechanically narrow the neural foramen, classically worsening symptoms, while flexion and ipsilateral rotation enlarge the foramen and provide relief, explaining the postural relationship. The correlation between imaging findings, clinical examination (Spurling test reproduction, dermatomal weakness), and the subacute presentation following postural stress makes cervical radiculopathy a mechanically driven condition amenable to conservative management focusing on posture correction and activity modification.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old woman presents with symmetrical muscle pain and stiffness in shoulders and hips. Pain and morning stiffness worse upon waking, gradually improving with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation with shoulder and hip mobility. Difficulty getting out of bed, dressing, and lifting arms. Morning stiffness lasting 1+ hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden onset 2 weeks ago with symmetrical pattern. No prior rheumatologic history. Associated low-grade fever and malaise. Rapid functional decline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Painful shoulder abduction and hip flexion bilaterally. Generalized tenderness over shoulder girdle and hip musculature. No focal joint swelling or warmth. Normal neurological examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR markedly elevated at 92 mm/hr with CRP 8.5 mg/dL. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies negative. No evidence of structural damage on shoulder/hip ultrasound.

puzzles.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) is an inflammatory condition affecting proximal muscles and their surrounding tissues, predominantly in adults over 50, characterized by a dramatic onset of bilateral shoulder and hip pain with profound morning stiffness. The pathophysiology remains incompletely understood but involves systemic inflammation, often with associated giant cell arteritis risk; the markedly elevated inflammatory markers (ESR >90) are hallmark findings, while negative RF/anti-CCP exclude rheumatoid arthritis despite clinical overlap. The symmetrical proximal distribution, dramatic functional limitation with morning stiffness lasting over an hour, and rapid progression over weeks—combined with absence of structural joint damage—are the classic presentation that distinguish PMR from other myalgias. Early recognition is critical because rapid corticosteroid response is both diagnostically confirmatory and therapeutically urgent, given the significant risk of concurrent temporal arteritis and vision-threatening complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports bilateral anterior shin pain that occurs predictably during running activity. Pain develops after consistent running distance and resolves completely with rest. The condition recurs with return to running at similar intensity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is reproducible and occurs during running or intense lower leg activity. The pain is bilateral and symmetric in the anterior tibial compartments. Complete pain relief occurs within 15-30 minutes of ceasing activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient unable to sustain running beyond 20-30 minutes despite normal walking tolerance. Pain is reproducible and symmetric bilaterally. Able to perform daily activities without symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pre-exercise examination may be normal with no resting compartment tenderness. Post-exercise examination reveals tight and tender anterior compartments. Compartment pressure measurement at rest is normal but elevated post-exercise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Compartment pressure measurement obtained immediately post-exercise (within 1-5 minutes) demonstrates elevated pressures in anterior compartment. Post-exercise pressure exceeds 30 mmHg with slow return to normal. Serial measurements during standardized exercise protocol establish diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic exertional compartment syndrome (CECS) occurs when muscles within a fascial compartment swell during exercise, raising intracompartmental pressure beyond the threshold that perfusion can tolerate, causing exercise-induced pain that resolves completely with rest. Unlike acute compartment syndrome, CECS develops insidiously in active individuals and is entirely asymptomatic at rest—the anterior tibial compartments in this runner appear normal on examination until after activity, when pressure monitoring reveals the pathologic elevation. The predictable, symmetric bilateral presentation during running at consistent intensity, with complete resolution within minutes of cessation, is classic; the normal resting pressures combined with elevated post-exercise pressures (>30 mmHg) that normalize slowly are the diagnostic hallmark. Successful management typically involves activity modification, training adjustments, or surgical fasciotomy in refractory cases, making accurate diagnosis critical to prevent unnecessary restrictions in active populations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 72-year-old man hospitalized post-stroke with right-sided weakness. Caregiver reports noticing a localized area of skin discoloration over the lower back that appeared several days ago. Patient reports the area feels slightly tender but denies pain, blistering, or drainage.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to reposition himself due to hemiplegia. Remains in bed in same positions for extended periods—primarily supine and left side-lying. No active weight-shifting or independent transfers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute ischemic stroke 3 weeks ago causing right hemiplegia. Admitted to acute care ward. Skin change first noted by nursing staff approximately 5 days ago over a bony prominence—specifically sacral area. No recent trauma, falls, or skin breakdown preceding the discoloration.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized area of redness over sacrum, warm to touch with slight firmness (induration) on palpation. Surrounding skin normal color and temperature. Area blanches minimally or not at all when pressed with fingertip. No visible blistering, maceration, or open wounds. Skin integrity intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bedside skin assessment and photography document non-blanching erythema confined to epidermis with intact skin barrier. Clinical staging per NPUAP criteria assigns Stage 1 classification. No ultrasound or imaging evidence of subcutaneous tissue involvement, edema, or necrosis.

puzzles.explanation: A Stage 1 pressure injury represents the earliest sign of pressure-related skin damage: localized non-blanching erythema over a bony prominence where reactive hyperemia (redness) fails to resolve within 30 minutes of pressure relief. It occurs when sustained mechanical load occludes capillary blood flow to the skin, triggering inflammatory changes that manifest as visible redness and induration, but with the epidermis and dermis remaining structurally intact. The sacral area is particularly vulnerable in immobilized patients like this stroke survivor, where inability to reposition combined with prolonged pressure over the sacrum creates an ideal environment for ischemic injury. Early recognition at Stage 1 is critical—offloading and repositioning at this point can reverse the damage, whereas progression to deeper stages involves tissue necrosis and becomes increasingly difficult to manage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young female with insidious onset of joint pain and significant fatigue over several weeks. Symptoms wax and wane unpredictably.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Photosensitive skin rash develops or worsens with sun exposure. Morning joint stiffness exceeds one hour.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Condition characterized by unpredictable flares and remissions. Recent initiation of hormonal contraception preceded symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the characteristic malar or butterfly rash is visible across the cheeks and nose. Symmetric polyarthritis is evident with swelling of multiple joints. Oral ulcers may be present on palate or buccal mucosa.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serology testing reveals positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) with high titers. Anti-double stranded DNA antibody and anti-Smith antibody are positive indicating specific SLE diagnosis. Complement levels (C3, C4) are low reflecting disease activity.

puzzles.explanation: Systemic lupus erythematosus is an autoimmune condition characterized by loss of immune tolerance to nuclear antigens, resulting in immune complex deposition across multiple organ systems—particularly skin, joints, and kidneys. It predominantly affects young women of childbearing age and classically presents with the insidious waxing and waning pattern seen here: the pathognomonic malar rash, symmetric polyarthritis, and constitutional symptoms like fatigue reflect widespread inflammation driven by circulating autoantibodies (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith) and immune complex formation. The photosensitivity and flare triggered by hormonal contraception are hallmark features—UVB exposure activates keratinocytes to express autoantigens, while estrogen acts as an immunological adjuvant amplifying disease activity. Low complement levels (C3, C4) alongside positive high-titre ANA confirm active disease and immune complex consumption, making serological testing essential for diagnosis and monitoring disease flares.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A worker reports progressive pain in the upper extremity that worsens throughout the workday. Symptoms interfere with job performance and daily tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are reproducible with specific repetitive hand and wrist motions required by the job. Sustained gripping or typing postures particularly provoke pain. Overnight rest provides temporary relief but symptoms recur within hours of returning to work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began insidiously 6–8 weeks ago after increased workload. Patient has no prior history of upper limb injury. Works in assembly line role involving repetitive forceful pinching and gripping for 8 hours daily without job rotation. No recent change in technique or equipment.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Point tenderness elicited in the forearm and anteromedial elbow region. Grip strength reduced by 30% compared to non-dominant side. Pain reproduced with resisted wrist flexion and finger flexion. Neurological examination intact with normal sensation and reflexes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates diffuse hypoechoic thickening of the flexor carpi radialis tendon at the elbow with peritendinous edema. No nerve compression or structural tear. Findings consistent with insertional tendinopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Work-related upper limb disorder encompasses cumulative trauma conditions arising from repetitive, forceful, or sustained occupational activities without adequate recovery — in this case, the insidious onset of flexor tendinopathy after increased assembly line work. The pathophysiology involves microtrauma to tendon fibres and their insertions, triggering inflammation and maladaptive remodelling; the diffuse hypoechoic thickening with peritendinous oedema on ultrasound reflects this tendinopathic process rather than acute rupture or nerve involvement. What distinguishes this condition clinically is its reproducibility with specific job tasks (pinching and gripping), rapid recurrence within hours of returning to work despite overnight relief, and the absence of neurological signs — reflecting a primary mechanical overload problem rather than nerve compression or systemic pathology. Prevention hinges on job rotation, ergonomic modification, and pacing strategies to allow adequate tissue recovery during the workday.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports acute severe knee pain and rapid swelling after a high-impact injury. Unable to bear weight on the affected leg.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot stand or walk. Knee is held in slight flexion and resists any attempt at straightening. Swelling is tense and effusion is clinically apparent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred during a motor vehicle accident at highway speeds. Patient recalls immediate giving way and inability to weight bear. No prior knee injury or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe effusion present with ballottement sign. Anterior drawer and Lachman tests are positive suggesting ACL involvement. Varus/valgus stress testing elicits pain but laxity is difficult to assess due to guarding. Crepitus palpable over anterolateral tibial plateau.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate tibial plateau fracture with depression and comminution severity. CT imaging shows three-dimensional fracture anatomy guiding surgical planning. Associated ligamentous and meniscal injuries are identified.

puzzles.explanation: Tibial plateau fractures result from axial loading combined with varus or valgus stress, commonly occurring in high-energy mechanisms like motor vehicle accidents where the femoral condyles are driven into the proximal tibia. These intra-articular fractures range from simple depressions to complex comminuted patterns, with severity determining prognosis and treatment approach — the depression and comminution seen here typically require surgical reconstruction to restore articular congruity and prevent post-traumatic osteoarthritis. The acute effusion, positive anterior drawer test, and palpable crepitus over the anterolateral plateau reflect both the fracture itself and the frequent association with ACL and meniscal injuries that occur when the tibia is violently displaced beneath the femur. CT imaging is essential for surgical planning, as it reveals the three-dimensional fracture anatomy in ways plain radiographs cannot, guiding whether the fracture can be managed conservatively or requires internal fixation to restore weight-bearing surfaces.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young adult reports progressive tall stature, joint pain with instability, and chest discomfort with exertion. Family members have similar features and cardiac concerns.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform overhead activities or heavy lifting without chest pain. Avoids contact sports and running due to medical guidance. Progressive limitation in endurance over past 2 years.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mother and maternal grandmother both have tall stature and joint flexibility issues. Maternal grandfather died suddenly in his 40s from cardiac event. Patient's symptoms began in early adolescence, progressing through teenage years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive thumb sign and wrist sign. Arm span-to-height ratio >1.05. Pectus carinatum with narrowed anterior-posterior chest diameter. Generalized hypermobility on Beighton score (≥6/9). Cardiac auscultation reveals early diastolic murmur.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiography shows aortic root diameter of 5.2 cm (z-score >3) with evidence of aortic regurgitation. Pectus deformity confirmed on chest X-ray. Lens subluxation visualised on slit-lamp examination. Genetic testing pending.

puzzles.explanation: Marfan syndrome is a systemic connective tissue disorder caused by mutations in the FBN1 gene, which encodes fibrillin-1—a critical structural protein in the extracellular matrix. The condition classically presents with skeletal features (tall stature, arachnodactyly, pectus deformities, and generalized hypermobility), ocular manifestations (lens subluxation), and critically, cardiovascular complications including progressive aortic root dilatation that can lead to sudden aortic dissection—explaining why this patient requires strict activity restriction and why the family history includes sudden cardiac death. The combination of positive thumb and wrist signs, arm span-to-height ratio >1.05, hypermobility, and significant aortic dilatation with regurgitation on echocardiography makes Marfan syndrome a medical emergency requiring cardiothoracic surveillance and beta-blocker or ARB therapy to slow aortic progression. Genetic confirmation and cascade screening of first-degree relatives is essential given the autosomal dominant inheritance pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Chronic productive cough with fever and recurrent chest infections. Progressive shortness of breath on exertion over months to years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient requires frequent postural drainage and manual therapy to mobilise secretions. Lying flat worsens cough; benefits from semi-upright positioning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of severe respiratory infection (e.g., measles, whooping cough, tuberculosis) or recurrent childhood pneumonias. Alternatively, immunodeficiency disorder or congenital ciliary dysfunction suspected. Symptoms worsened over years despite antibiotic courses.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Coarse bibasal crackles that clear partially with coughing. Clubbing of fingers and toes. Hyperinflation visible on chest wall inspection. Accessory muscle use during conversation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates irreversible bronchial dilation with bronchial wall thickening. Traction bronchiectasis and bronchial distortion are visible. Extent and distribution pattern (cylindrical, varicose, or cystic) are documented on CT.

puzzles.explanation: Bronchiectasis is a chronic, irreversible condition characterised by abnormal dilatation and destruction of the bronchial walls, typically resulting from severe infection (measles, tuberculosis, whooping cough) or underlying immunodeficiency that overwhelms mucociliary clearance. The progressive accumulation of secretions in these dilated airways drives the characteristic features: recurrent bacterial infections, chronic productive cough that improves with postural drainage, and coarse crackles that partially clear with expectoration. Finger clubbing reflects the chronicity and chronic hypoxia of progressive lung damage. The high-resolution CT findings of bronchial-to-arterial ratio exceeding 1:1 and persistent bronchial dilatation to the lung periphery confirm the diagnosis and help classify severity, guiding the intensity of airway clearance therapy needed to manage these patients long-term.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports chronic knee pain with morning stiffness that improves with activity. Pain worsens with prolonged standing and walking. Functional limitation occurs with stairs and uneven ground.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with single-leg stance and step-downs. Pain described as mechanical, worse mid-day after repetitive loading. Morning pain resolves within 20-30 minutes of movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 55+. History of ACL injury 10 years prior, managed conservatively. BMI 28. Works in construction with 20+ years of repetitive squatting. Symptoms began insidiously 2 years ago, progressive. Prior physiotherapy provided temporary relief only.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, knee effusion may be present with positive ballottement test. Crepitus is palpated with knee motion. Range of motion is limited with pain at end-range flexion and extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing radiographs (AP, lateral, patellofemoral views) show focal joint space narrowing medial compartment, marginal osteophytes at femoral condyles, and subchondral sclerosis. Severity Kellgren-Lawrence grade 2-3. No loose bodies or cruciate laxity.

puzzles.explanation: Knee osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease characterized by progressive loss of articular cartilage, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes, typically affecting weight-bearing compartments. The classic presentation of morning stiffness that resolves with activity, mechanical pain that worsens with repetitive loading, and difficulty with dynamic control tasks like step-downs reflects the hallmark pattern of post-traumatic OA — here accelerated by the prior ACL injury disrupting normal knee biomechanics and combined with decades of occupational squatting loading. Radiographic findings of medial joint space narrowing, marginal osteophytes, and sclerosis at Kellgren-Lawrence grade 2–3 confirm established cartilage loss, while the presence of effusion and crepitus on examination indicate active synovitis and cartilage surface irregularity. Management focuses on load management, quadriceps strengthening for dynamic stability, and activity modification to slow progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 57-year-old female. Progressive swelling of right arm and hand over 8 months. Reports heaviness, tightness, and reduced mobility. Denies acute injury or recent infection.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot wear watch or rings on affected arm. Difficulty with repetitive gripping and sustained overhead activities. Symptoms worsen with heat exposure, prolonged arm dependency, and air travel. Swelling improves with elevation and rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Right-sided upper limb involvement. Symptoms began months after completion of treatment for localized disease. No prior significant trauma or surgery to that extremity. Progressive worsening despite conservative measures attempted at home.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Circumference asymmetry: 4 cm difference at wrist, 6 cm at forearm compared to contralateral side. Stemmer sign positive. Skin firm, non-pitting in dorsum of hand. Normal range of motion. No sensory deficits. No skin erythema or warmth.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates delayed lymph transit time in right upper extremity with dermal backflow pattern. Reduced uptake in right axillary nodes. Asymmetric lymphatic drainage compared to left side, consistent with obstructive lymphatic pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Breast cancer lymphedema develops when cancer treatment — particularly axillary lymph node dissection and/or radiation — damages or removes lymph nodes, impairing the drainage capacity of the upper limb. The resulting chronic accumulation of protein-rich fluid manifests as non-pitting edema, heaviness, and progressive circumferential asymmetry, classically worse with heat, dependency, and activity as seen in this case. The positive Stemmer sign (inability to pinch skin fold on the dorsum of the hand) and the lymphoscintigraphic findings of dermal backflow and delayed transit time are hallmark features distinguishing true lymphatic obstruction from other causes of arm swelling. Unlike acute post-operative swelling, lymphedema emerges insidiously months after treatment completion and requires long-term management to prevent fibrosis and functional decline.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 78-year-old woman. Sudden loss of balance and fell to the ground twice in the past 6 weeks. No loss of consciousness, no seizure activity. She describes feeling 'faint' or 'lightheaded' just before each episode. Now reluctant to walk independently.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dizziness and unsteadiness worse when standing up quickly from sitting or lying down. Gait visibly slower and unsteady; needs to hold onto walls. Reports feeling 'woozy' when reaching overhead or turning head. Avoids walking without support due to fear.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hypertension for 12 years on amlodipine and lisinopril. Recent addition of metoprolol 4 weeks ago for palpitations. Lives alone; home has scatter rugs and low lighting. Admits to poor nutrition and rarely goes outside. Last eye exam 3 years ago. No prior falls.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sitting BP 145/88, standing BP 118/70 (drop 27 mmHg systolic). Resting heart rate 58. Gait narrow-based with short stride length and reduced arm swing. Tandem stance unable to hold >3 seconds. Proprioception intact. Cranial nerves grossly normal. Pupils reactive. No focal weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ECG shows sinus bradycardia. Head CT normal—no acute stroke, bleed, or atrophy. Carotid ultrasound normal flow bilaterally. Medication review: metoprolol dose excessive for age and renal function (eGFR 42). Timed Up and Go 26 seconds. Orthostatic hypotension confirmed on repeat vital sign assessment.

puzzles.explanation: Falls in the elderly result from a complex interplay of intrinsic factors—declining balance, strength, cognition, and sensory function—combined with extrinsic environmental hazards and often iatrogenic medication effects. This case exemplifies how polypharmacy in older adults can precipitate falls: the recent addition of metoprolol, dosed inappropriately for renal impairment, combined with existing antihypertensive therapy, produced significant orthostatic hypotension (27 mmHg drop) and bradycardia (HR 58), directly causing the presyncope and balance loss before each fall. Her impaired postural control—demonstrated by poor tandem stance and slow gait—reflects age-related declines in proprioception and vestibular compensation, while environmental factors (scatter rugs, low lighting, social isolation) and deconditioning from reduced activity compounded the risk. Recognition of medication-induced orthostatic hypotension, combined with balance retraining and environmental modification, represents the cornerstone of fall prevention in this population.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old distance runner presents with medial knee pain located just below the joint line, accompanied by mild swelling in the same region. Pain is reproducible with weight-bearing activities and has developed over the past six weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running, particularly on cambered surfaces or hills, triggers sharp anteromedial knee pain within 10-15 minutes. Walking on level ground is minimally symptomatic. Pain relief occurs with ice, elevation, and anti-inflammatory medications.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient increased training volume by 40% over the past two months in preparation for a marathon. They recently switched running shoes and report tightness in the hamstring muscles bilaterally. No prior knee pathology or previous running-related injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized tenderness over the pes anserine insertion with palpable swelling. Noble test is negative. Single-leg squat triggers medial knee discomfort. Hamstring tightness confirmed with straight-leg raise limited to 60 degrees bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Knee MRI reveals fluid collection within the pes anserine bursa (12mm in transverse diameter) with surrounding soft tissue edema. No meniscal tears, ligamentous injuries, or articular cartilage damage identified. Tibia and femur intact.

puzzles.explanation: Pes anserine bursitis results from inflammation of the bursa located at the insertion of the conjoined tendons (sartorius, gracilis, semitendinosus) on the anteromedial tibia, typically triggered by repetitive knee flexion and internal rotation or chronic hamstring tightness. The condition is particularly common in distance runners, especially with rapid training load increases or biomechanical changes such as footwear modifications. The localized swelling below the joint line with tenderness at the insertion point, combined with reproduction of symptoms during single-leg squats and running on hills, is classic for this diagnosis—the cambered and inclined surfaces emphasize valgus stress and internal tibial rotation. Hamstring flexibility plays a key role; bilateral tightness increases tension through the pes anserine attachment, making this finding both a contributing factor and a central target for rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports hand paresthesias and grip weakness that began insidiously over months prior to presentation. Symptoms are worst at night and with repetitive gripping activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to perform fine pinch tasks and reports dropping objects. Grip strength significantly reduced compared to contralateral side. Activities requiring sustained wrist extension or forearm supination exacerbate symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent palmar-side hand surgery 3 weeks ago with pre-operative median nerve compression symptoms refractory to conservative management for 8+ months. No confounding history of diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, or pregnancy. Post-operative hand swelling and stiffness reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Incision well-healed without erythema or drainage. Two-point discrimination reduced in median nerve distribution compared to ulnar nerve. Phalen's test negative; Tinel's sign mildly positive at thenar eminence. Abductor pollicis brevis strength 4/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowed median motor latency (7.2 ms, normal <4.2 ms) and prolonged distal motor latency with reduced amplitude. Sensory conduction severely reduced. Findings confirm focal demyelination across carpal tunnel with axonal loss.

puzzles.explanation: Post-carpal tunnel release syndrome can occur weeks to months after surgical decompression, resulting from residual nerve compression, inadequate release, scar tissue formation, or iatrogenic injury during the procedure. Despite successful surgical intervention for the original median nerve compression, persistent or recurrent focal demyelination—evidenced here by slowed motor latencies and reduced sensory conduction—indicates incomplete decompression or post-operative scarring restricting nerve gliding. The reduced two-point discrimination and weakness in abductor pollicis brevis, combined with the well-healed incision and absence of systemic factors, points to a local post-operative complication rather than recurrent idiopathic carpal tunnel syndrome. Further imaging or exploration may be warranted if symptoms fail to resolve with conservative management over the coming weeks, as some post-operative dysfunction improves with gradual desensitization and mobilization.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent pain in a location where sensation should not exist. Quality described as cramping, burning, or sharp stabbing. Onset occurred several months ago. Pain is distressing and difficult to manage with standard analgesics.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain fluctuates with emotional stress, weather changes, and fatigue. Symptoms worsen during prolonged inactivity and peak at night. Certain movement patterns or activities involving the affected region provide temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lower limb amputation performed 8 months ago following traumatic injury. Patient experienced significant pain in the limb prior to surgery. No recent infection, skin breakdown, or acute inflammation at surgical site. Pain has not resolved despite adequate residual limb healing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Residual limb appears well-healed with intact skin and no signs of infection or neuroma. Pain cannot be elicited by palpation or passive movement of the stump. Sensory testing of residual limb is normal. Patient demonstrates normal strength and ROM in ipsilateral hip. Pain is only reported in the absent distal segment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: fMRI during pain episodes shows abnormal activation and reorganization in the primary sensory cortex corresponding to the absent limb. PET imaging demonstrates increased metabolic activity in pain processing regions (anterior cingulate, insular cortex). No structural lesions identified in spinal cord or brain.

puzzles.explanation: Phantom limb pain is a neuropathic pain syndrome arising from central nervous system reorganization and maladaptive neuroplasticity following amputation, where the brain continues to generate pain signals from the missing limb despite absence of peripheral input. The condition reflects a fundamental mismatch between the motor cortex's learned motor programs and the absence of sensory feedback, compounded by cortical map reorganization in the somatosensory cortex—visible on imaging as aberrant activation patterns in pain processing regions. Unlike residual limb pain, phantom pain cannot be reproduced by physical examination of the stump and persists despite optimal healing; emotional stress and fatigue intensify symptoms by increasing central sensitization, while certain movement strategies or mirror therapy may provide relief by recalibrating the disrupted body schema. This presentation, emerging months post-amputation with burning or cramping quality and resistance to standard analgesics, is characteristic of phantom limb pain and typically requires multimodal intervention including neuromodulation, graded motor imagery, and psychological support.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports progressive finger deformity affecting hand function. Gripping and fine motor control have deteriorated over recent weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot actively straighten the affected finger at the middle joint, even with assistance. Attempts to extend the PIP joint fail despite intact sensation and strength in the intrinsic muscles.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Dorsal midline hand or finger trauma from laceration, crush injury, or blunt blow to the back of the hand. Initial injury disrupted the extensor mechanism over the middle joint. Deformity has worsened progressively over weeks if untreated.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the PIP joint is held in flexion and resists passive extension. The DIP joint lies in hyperextension. Palpation reveals a palpable gap or defect over the dorsal PIP joint. The Fowler test (passive flexion at MCP with extended DIP) shows loss of DIP extension when PIP is relaxed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows disruption or attenuation of the central slip of the extensor tendon at the level of the PIP joint. Associated soft tissue swelling and possible avulsion fracture at the dorsal base of the middle phalanx confirm the diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Boutonniere deformity results from disruption of the central slip of the extensor tendon at the PIP joint, usually following dorsal hand trauma such as laceration or crush injury. This loss of active PIP extension allows the lateral bands of the extensor mechanism to migrate volarward, creating the characteristic posture: PIP flexion with compensatory DIP hyperextension — resembling a button pulled through a buttonhole. The deformity worsens progressively if the central slip is not repaired or immobilized early, as the volar displacement of the lateral bands becomes increasingly difficult to reverse. The inability to actively extend the PIP joint despite normal strength in the intrinsic muscles, combined with the fixed flexed posture and palpable dorsal defect, makes diagnosis straightforward and highlights why early recognition and immobilization are critical to prevent permanent functional loss and joint contracture.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Overhead athlete reports shoulder pain during training sessions. Onset was gradual over recent weeks with increasing training intensity. Pain is diffuse around the shoulder with overhead movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to complete full training sets due to pain. Symptoms worsen proportionally with session duration and frequency. Rest days provide temporary relief but symptoms recur with return to activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent addition to competitive squad led to doubled training volume 3 weeks ago. No prior shoulder injuries. Previous physiotherapy for similar complaints in other overhead athletes suggests predisposition. No neurological symptoms or night pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, shoulder weakness is evident particularly with external rotation. Scapular dyskinesis is noted with altered scapulohumeral rhythm. Positive impingement signs are elicited on examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates rotator cuff tendinopathy with thickening and signal changes. MRI shows supraspinatus tendon involvement and inflammatory changes. Imaging correlates with clinical examination findings.

puzzles.explanation: Swimmer's shoulder is a chronic overuse syndrome resulting from repetitive overhead activities that create cumulative microtrauma to the rotator cuff, particularly the supraspinatus, and associated soft tissues. The sudden doubling of training volume overwhelmed the tendon's adaptive capacity, leading to tendinopathy characterized by inflammatory changes and structural thickening visible on imaging. Scapular dyskinesis — impaired scapulohumeral rhythm — is the mechanical hallmark, reducing the space available for the supraspinatus and creating secondary impingement during overhead movements. The pattern of pain that worsens with duration and frequency but resolves with rest, combined with external rotation weakness and positive impingement signs, reflects the classic overload mechanism: weakened rotator cuff muscular control allows excessive scapular anterior tilt, compressing the subacromial structures during the swimming stroke.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports sudden-onset severe joint pain in the foot, worse with weight bearing. Pain began overnight and is limiting mobility significantly.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot tolerate footwear or pressure on the affected joint. Symptoms are worst in early morning and evening; daytime rest provides temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Middle-aged male with history of similar acute episodes. Recent heavy alcohol use and consumption of red meat. Takes a diuretic for hypertension. Episodes resolve spontaneously over 1-2 weeks. No prior diagnosis established.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked erythema and swelling over the first MTP joint with warmth to palpation. Exquisite point tenderness at the joint line. Pain reproduced with passive dorsiflexion and plantarflexion. No signs of instability. Strength and sensation intact distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serum uric acid level is elevated confirming hyperuricemia. Joint aspiration demonstrates monosodium urate crystals confirming diagnosis. Radiographs may show tophaceous changes in chronic gout.

puzzles.explanation: Gout is an acute inflammatory arthropathy triggered by monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints, occurring when serum uric acid exceeds saturation point—a condition common in middle-aged men with risk factors like alcohol consumption, purine-rich diet (red meat), and diuretic use that impairs urate excretion. The first metatarsophalangeal joint is the classic site due to its lower temperature and mechanical stress, causing the sudden overnight onset of severe pain with marked local inflammation that resolves spontaneously within 1-2 weeks as the acute inflammatory cascade self-limits. The exquisite point tenderness, erythema, and warmth reflect the intense innate immune response to needle-shaped crystals phagocytosed by synovial fluid macrophages, a process distinct from mechanical joint injury. Joint aspiration revealing intracellular monosodium urate crystals remains the gold standard for diagnosis and allows differentiation from other acute arthritides.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports mild pain on the medial side of the foot during weight-bearing activities. Pain is vague in onset and worsens progressively through the day with standing or walking. Rest provides relief.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty maintaining prolonged walking or standing. Single-leg activities and dynamic weight shifts increase symptoms. Running is not tolerated well.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset over weeks to months. No clear traumatic event. Patient reports gradual worsening with increased activity demand or change in footwear. No prior foot injuries or significant comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, mild tenderness is present behind the medial ankle. Single leg heel rise is possible but may produce mild discomfort. Arch height is maintained with intact support mechanism.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates tibialis posterior tendon inflammation with mild thickening. Signal changes within the tendon indicate inflammation. Arch alignment remains normal without structural changes at this stage.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior tibial tendon dysfunction (PTTD) results from inflammation and degenerative changes in the tibialis posterior tendon, which is the primary dynamic stabilizer of the medial longitudinal arch. Grade 1 PTTD is characterized by early tendon inflammation and mild thickening on imaging without yet compromising arch support — as demonstrated here by preserved single-leg heel rise ability and maintained arch height despite medial pain. The insidious, activity-related onset with progressive worsening through the day reflects the cumulative microtrauma typical of overload injuries, particularly common with changes in training volume or footwear. Early recognition and load management at this stage are critical, as the tendon retains its structural integrity; continued overload can progress to later stages with arch collapse and functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old reports persistent moderate low back pain six months after a work-related strain. Pain has not resolved despite reassurance and normal healing timelines for the initial injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient actively avoids bending, lifting, and prolonged sitting. Family reports progressive restriction of activities, including avoidance of movements previously tolerated. Patient expresses catastrophic thoughts about movement causing harm.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Significant anxiety history predates the initial injury by several years. The injury occurred in a high-stress work environment with ongoing job dissatisfaction. Previous pain episodes resolved fully with activity resumption.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination reveals pain responses and guarding that exceed findings on imaging. Strength testing is inconsistent and variable. Patient demonstrates difficulty tolerating repeated movement trials. Mood and anxiety screening indicates elevated distress.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates resolution of inflammatory changes from the initial injury with normal spinal cord and nerve root anatomy. Imaging findings do not explain the extent of functional limitation and pain reported by the patient.

puzzles.explanation: Kinesiophobia is an excessive, irrational fear of movement or re-injury that develops when patients catastrophize about pain and overestimate threat, despite objective evidence of healing. It represents a shift from nociception to fear-avoidance behaviour, where the anticipated harm from moving becomes more disabling than the original injury itself. The discrepancy between normal imaging findings and disproportionate functional limitation—combined with inconsistent physical findings, avoidance of previously tolerated activities, and elevated anxiety—is the hallmark presentation. Kinesiophobia is particularly common in patients with pre-existing anxiety, workplace stressors, or inadequate reassurance during recovery, and it perpetuates disability through deconditioning and maladaptive coping rather than ongoing tissue damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 10-year-old competitive soccer player presents with heel pain that worsens during and after training sessions. Pain is localized to the posterior heel with occasional radiation to the plantar surface. Onset was gradual over 4-5 weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by running, jumping, and high-impact training. Patient reports difficulty with prolonged standing and weight-bearing activities. Symptoms resolve quickly with rest but return immediately with activity resumption.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient experiences morning stiffness and pain in the heel upon weight-bearing. Pain resolves quickly with rest but returns with any running or jumping activity. Symptoms are worse after high-volume training days.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Midline calcaneal tenderness is elicited with direct palpation and medial-lateral compression of the calcaneus reproduces pain. Ankle dorsiflexion is limited, and gastrocnemius tightness is evident on assessment. The heel appears mildly swollen relative to the contralateral side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show fragmentation and sclerosis of the calcaneal apophysis consistent with apophysitis. No frank fracture or epiphyseal displacement is present. Imaging findings, combined with age, activity level, and clinical presentation, confirm the diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Sever's disease is an apophysitis of the calcaneal growth plate, occurring in skeletally immature athletes when repetitive traction and shear forces on the developing apophysis exceed the tissue's load tolerance. The condition is self-limited, resolving once the apophysis fuses, but typically causes significant pain during the growth spurt years—especially in running and jumping sports like soccer. The bilateral heel compression test and morning stiffness with rapid resolution on rest are hallmark features, reflecting inflammation at the apophyseal junction rather than structural injury. Gastrocnemius-soleus tightness amplifies apophyseal stress, making eccentric calf strengthening and flexibility work central to management alongside activity modification.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman presents with progressive height loss over several years and chronic mid-back pain. She sustained a wrist fracture from a minor fall 2 weeks ago. Reports increasing stooped posture.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight through wrist for 3 weeks post-injury. Thoracic pain prevents lying flat and limits walking distance to <10 minutes. Difficulty rising from low chairs and bed.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Menopause at age 57 without hormone replacement. Long-standing low calcium and vitamin D intake. Sedentary lifestyle with minimal weight-bearing exercise. Family history of osteoporosis in mother. Limited sunlight exposure and dietary restrictions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Height loss 2 inches confirmed. Thoracic kyphosis ~50 degrees on measurement. Grip strength 3/5 bilateral. Percussion tenderness over T7 spinous process. Timed Up and Go 18 seconds (prolonged). No lower limb neurological deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan shows T-score of -2.8 at lumbar spine and -2.5 at femoral neck (osteoporotic range). Vertebral fracture assessment shows compression fracture at T7 (15% height loss). Biochemical markers show elevated P1NP and CTX-1 indicating high bone turnover.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporosis is a systemic condition characterized by low bone mineral density and deterioration of bone microarchitecture, leading to increased fragility and fracture risk—often without symptoms until a break occurs. In this case, the combination of postmenopausal status, inadequate calcium and vitamin D intake, and sedentary lifestyle created the perfect conditions for accelerated bone loss, evidenced by the T-score of -2.8 and high bone turnover markers. The compression fracture at T7 with progressive thoracic kyphosis and height loss represents the hallmark skeletal manifestation: vertebral bodies become insufficiently mineralized and collapse under normal loading, leading to the characteristic stooped posture and chronic pain that limits mobility and further reduces mechanical loading on bone. Early recognition through DEXA screening in at-risk populations—particularly postmenopausal women—remains critical, as lifestyle modification and pharmacological intervention can slow bone loss and reduce fracture risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old with a history of mild osteoporosis presents with sudden-onset sharp chest wall pain following a minor fall from standing height. Pain is strictly localized to one side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is markedly aggravated by deep inspiration, coughing, and any trunk flexion or rotation movements. The patient splints the injured side when coughing. Lying on the affected side is intolerable.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has been on corticosteroids for 2 years due to polymyalgia rheumatica, increasing fracture risk. No previous rib injuries are reported. Smoking history is significant with 30 pack-years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Precise point tenderness elicited over the posterolateral chest wall at one rib level. Direct palpation reproduces sharp pain. Intercostal muscle guarding is marked.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates a linear cortical break through one rib with minimal displacement. No associated flail segment or pulmonary compromise identified.

puzzles.explanation: Rib fractures result from direct trauma or significant mechanical stress to the thoracic cage, with risk substantially elevated by osteoporosis, corticosteroid use, and smoking—all of which compromise bone mineral density and collagen integrity. The sharp, pleuritic chest wall pain that worsens with deep inspiration and trunk movements is pathognomonic, driven by mechanical instability of the fracture site and irritation of intercostal nerves and musculature during rib excursion. The posterolateral localization and severe splinting behavior reflect the high mobility and muscular demand of this anatomical region during respiration. Management focuses on analgesia, breathing exercises to prevent hypoventilation-related complications, and early mobilization, as modern evidence shows rigid external stabilization can worsen outcomes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old patient reports gradual-onset knee pain over 2 years. Pain is worse in the morning but eases with light activity. Pain returns with prolonged walking or standing. Patient reports difficulty with stairs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports knee swelling by end of day after activity. Pain is mechanical: worse with weight-bearing but improves with rest overnight. Morning stiffness typically lasts 15–20 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient sustained a meniscal tear in right knee 5 years ago; mild surgery was avoided. BMI is 31. Works in construction with repetitive squatting. Progressive onset; no acute injury precipitated current worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, knee effusion may be present with positive ballottement test. Crepitus is palpated with knee motion. Range of motion is limited with pain at end-range flexion and extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing AP radiographs show medial compartment joint space narrowing with marginal osteophytes and subchondral sclerosis. Lateral and skyline views show mild patellofemoral changes.

puzzles.explanation: Knee osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease characterized by progressive loss of articular cartilage, bone remodeling, and osteophyte formation, typically developing over years from cumulative mechanical stress and prior joint injury. The classic presentation here—morning stiffness that eases with activity, mechanical pain that worsens with weight-bearing and prolonged use, and end-of-day swelling—reflects the inflammatory response to cartilage breakdown and synovial irritation. This patient's trajectory is typical: the prior meniscal tear compromised shock absorption and load distribution, while obesity and repetitive occupational loading accelerated degenerative changes in the medial compartment. Imaging findings of joint space narrowing, osteophytes, and subchondral sclerosis, combined with crepitus and effusion on examination, confirm cartilage loss and reactive bone changes that distinguish osteoarthritis from other knee pathologies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old desk worker presents with chronic upper back and neck tension with associated cervical headaches. Symptoms have developed gradually over 3 years of sedentary work, particularly affecting the cervical and upper thoracic spine.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen significantly with prolonged computer work and improve with movement breaks and stretching. Forward shoulder posture increases pain within 15-20 minutes. Thoracic extension and scapular retraction relieve symptoms temporarily.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works 9-10 hours daily at a desk with a poorly positioned monitor and keyboard. Previous physiotherapy provided only temporary relief. No significant trauma history, though postural habits have been poor since adolescence.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Postural assessment reveals forward head posture and rounded shoulders. Palpation confirms significant upper trapezius and pectoralis tightness. Scapular dyskinesis noted on movement screening. Lower trapezius and serratus anterior strength testing shows grade 3-4/5 bilaterally. Cervical extension ROM limited to 35 degrees. No neural signs on upper limb neurodynamic testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical and thoracic spine X-rays negative for disc herniation, spondylosis, or subluxation. Postural analysis using plumb-line assessment shows head projection 3 cm anterior to acromion. Scapulohumeral rhythm is asymmetrical with restricted upward rotation bilaterally. Findings confirm postural dysfunction without underlying structural disease.

puzzles.explanation: Upper crossed syndrome is a postural dysfunction pattern characterized by tightness in the upper trapezius and pectoralis muscles paired with weakness in the lower trapezius and serratus anterior, creating a muscular imbalance across the cervical and shoulder girdle. This pattern develops insidiously in sedentary workers through prolonged forward head and rounded shoulder postures, which gradually reinforce dysfunctional motor patterns and alter scapulohumeral rhythm. The cervical headaches and limited extension ROM reflect secondary cervical spine loading as the posterior chain becomes overworked to stabilize a persistently anteriorly-positioned head — a compensation that becomes increasingly entrenched over years of poor postural habits. Treatment success requires not just temporary relief through stretching, but systematic restoration of lower trapezius and serratus anterior activation combined with sustained postural correction, distinguishing this from acute mechanical neck pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old office worker presents with bilateral posterior neck stiffness that emerged 4 weeks ago. Symptoms are worse at end of day and improve with rest on weekends.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Neck tension worsens progressively through the workday and is worst by late afternoon. Taking frequent breaks and performing neck stretches provides 30-45 minutes of relief. Symptoms are minimal on weekends and during vacations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient transitioned to a home office setup with suboptimal ergonomics (laptop screen positioned too low). No history of neck trauma, whiplash, or previous neck symptoms. Stress levels have increased due to workload.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral upper trapezius muscle tightness with palpable trigger points. Cervical ROM is full but restricted by muscle guarding. Forward head posture noted. No neurological signs, nerve tension, or radicular pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical X-rays normal—no degenerative changes, fracture, or alignment abnormality. Imaging ruled out structural disease; diagnosis confirmed by symptom resolution with ergonomic correction over 2 weeks.

puzzles.explanation: Neck tension from prolonged desk work results from sustained low-level muscle activation and poor postural control, typically developing when ergonomic demands exceed the cervical spine's capacity to maintain neutral alignment. The bilateral upper trapezius involvement and forward head posture seen here are classic adaptations to a low-mounted monitor—the patient must maintain cervical extension and horizontal abduction to maintain visual focus, creating continuous low-grade muscular fatigue. The characteristic pattern of symptom escalation through the workday and rapid improvement with breaks or postural change distinguishes this from structural pathology, and the swift resolution following ergonomic correction confirms the mechanical origin. Unlike true myofascial pain syndrome or cervical radiculopathy, pure postural tension responds predictably to workstation modification and strategic movement breaks rather than requiring advanced imaging or intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old former smoker reports progressive shortness of breath over several years, now limiting daily activities. Chronic productive cough most noticeable in mornings. Denies acute trauma or recent illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to climb stairs or walk uphill without stopping to catch breath. Light housework leaves patient fatigued. Symptoms worse in cold air and with exertion; slightly better when sitting upright or resting indoors.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 40-year pack-year smoking history; quit 5 years ago. Symptoms gradual over past decade. No prior diagnosis of asthma. No recent infections. Lives at sea level. Denies orthopnea or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Barrel chest appearance noted. Pursed-lip breathing observed. Diminished breath sounds bilaterally at bases. Prolonged expiratory phase. No crackles. Oxygen saturation 92% at rest. Normal heart sounds.
