clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spirometry shows FEV1/FVC ratio <70% with reduced FEV1 (45% predicted). Chest X-ray demonstrates hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms and increased AP diameter. CT shows emphysematous changes. No acute infiltrates.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease results from progressive, largely irreversible airflow obstruction caused by emphysema and chronic bronchitis, most commonly from smoking-induced destruction of alveolar walls and chronic airway inflammation. The combination of significant pack-year history, insidious dyspnoea worsening over years, and morning productive cough reflects the typical disease trajectory — the emphysematous changes visible on imaging destroy elastic recoil, trapping air and causing the barrel chest and hyperinflation evident on examination. The prolonged expiratory phase and pursed-lip breathing are adaptive strategies to maintain positive airway pressure and prevent dynamic airway collapse during exhalation. Spirometry showing FEV₁/FVC <70% confirms the obstructive pattern, while the reduced FEV₁ (45% predicted) indicates moderate-to-severe disease — consistent with the functional limitations in stair climbing and exertion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old presents 6 weeks post-operatively with persistent knee pain and stiffness that has not improved as expected. Pain is present at rest and with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient is unable to fully extend the knee during walking and struggles with stair descent. Knee swelling increases with activity lasting longer than 30 minutes. Quadriceps control is noticeably weak.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient had severe pre-operative knee flexion contracture of 15 degrees. A continuous passive motion machine was used post-operatively for 10 days. Compliance with home exercises has been suboptimal.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Knee extension is -10 degrees (extension lag present during straight leg raise). Knee flexion reaches 85 degrees actively. Quadriceps strength is 4/5. Significant anterior knee swelling is noted with a positive joint effusion sign. No signs of infection or deep vein thrombosis are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs show correct component alignment and positioning. No evidence of hardware failure, loosening, or fracture. Mechanical integrity of the reconstruction is sound.

puzzles.explanation: Post-total knee replacement (TKR) complications in the early recovery phase commonly involve stiffness and functional deficits when rehabilitation targets are not met, particularly in patients with pre-existing contractures. This patient's persistent extension lag, limited active flexion of 85 degrees, and quadriceps weakness reflect inadequate neuromuscular re-education and insufficient aggressive stretching during the critical 6-week window when scar tissue formation is still reversible. The pre-operative 15-degree flexion contracture compounded by suboptimal compliance with home exercises created a high-risk scenario for extension deficit; combined with significant effusion from activity-induced inflammation, the knee has stiffened rather than progressed. Since imaging confirms proper component alignment and no mechanical failure, the prognosis is favourable with intensive physiotherapy focusing on quadriceps activation, progressive extension stretching, and pain-controlled range-of-motion exercises — the mechanical reconstruction is sound, but the soft tissue recovery has lagged.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 54-year-old female, 8 weeks post-operative. Reports progressive restriction in left shoulder mobility and persistent tightness across left chest wall and upper back. No acute pain, fever, or systemic symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot reach overhead or behind back with left arm. Difficulty with grooming, dressing, and lifting. Symptoms worse with sustained postures or repetitive reaching. Swelling noted in left upper arm and hand by end of day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent surgical procedure involving left chest wall with axillary dissection (lymph node removal levels 1–2). Incision healed without complications. No chemotherapy or radiation therapy initiated. Right-hand dominant.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left shoulder passive ROM significantly limited in abduction and external rotation. Palpable firmness and restricted tissue mobility in left axilla and chest wall. No palpable mass or fluid collection. Mild pitting edema left forearm and hand. Scapular positioning asymmetrical; left scapula slightly elevated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of left axilla: no seroma, abscess, or lymph collection. Soft tissue density consistent with post-surgical scar and fibrous adhesions in axillary region. No mechanical obstruction or hematoma. Clinical diagnosis confirmed by functional deficit pattern typical of axillary web syndrome and post-surgical adhesions.

puzzles.explanation: Post-mastectomy rehabilitation addresses the predictable sequelae of breast cancer surgery, particularly the loss of shoulder mobility and functional capacity that follows axillary dissection. The removal of lymph nodes and surrounding tissue creates scar formation and fibrous adhesions in the axilla — a phenomenon sometimes termed 'axillary web syndrome' — which mechanically restricts shoulder abduction and external rotation. Eight weeks post-operatively, the progressive restriction in overhead reaching and the palpable firmness across the chest wall and axilla reflect the maturing scar tissue creating adhesions between muscle layers and the surgical bed. The concurrent lymphedema (swelling in the forearm and hand) is a common companion to axillary dissection, resulting from disrupted lymphatic drainage; early intervention with gentle ROM, soft tissue mobilization, and compression management is critical to prevent contracture and restore functional independence in grooming, dressing, and upper limb activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old overhead athlete presents with progressive anterolateral shoulder pain during explosive overhead activities. Onset was 8 weeks ago and has worsened despite activity modification. Pain is reproducible with sport-specific movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specific to overhead activities performed explosively and is worst during the late cocking and acceleration phases of throwing. Rest provides partial relief but symptoms recur immediately upon return to sport. Repetitive activities reproduce pain reliably.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Training volume increased significantly 2 months prior to onset. No traumatic event. History of postural dysfunction (lower crossed syndrome). Previous shoulder episodes responded to rest but this presentation is more persistent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Scapular medial border winging noted during active arm elevation. Serratus anterior and lower trapezius weakness on strength testing. Tightness in posterior shoulder and pectoralis. Supraspinatus sign positive (pain 80–120 degrees abduction).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI: mild supraspinatus tendinopathy without full-thickness tear. Rotator cuff otherwise intact. No labral pathology or glenohumeral joint abnormality. Subacromial space narrowing consistent with impingement secondary to scapular positioning.

puzzles.explanation: Scapular dyskinesis is abnormal scapular positioning and movement pattern during arm elevation, typically caused by weakness or inhibition of the scapular stabilizers—particularly the serratus anterior and lower trapezius—combined with tightness in antagonist muscles like the pectoralis and posterior shoulder structures. In overhead athletes, this leads to progressive subacromial impingement as the scapula fails to upwardly rotate and posteriorly tilt adequately during arm elevation, compressing the rotator cuff tendons during the late cocking and acceleration phases of throwing. The medial border winging and supraspinatus tendinopathy seen here are hallmark consequences: altered scapular mechanics force the humeral head anterosuperiorly, creating secondary impingement rather than primary rotator cuff pathology. Treatment focuses on restoring scapular stability through serratus anterior and lower trapezius activation, addressing postural dysfunction, and gradually restoring overhead load tolerance—distinguishing it from primary rotator cuff injury or labral pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old presents 12 weeks post-partum with pain during intercourse and occasional loss of control of intestinal gas. She reports these symptoms began after delivery but have not fully resolved.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Stress urinary incontinence occurs with coughing, sneezing, and running. The patient has avoided exercise and high-impact activities for 12 weeks due to fear of leakage and pelvic discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The labor lasted 16 hours with instrumental assistance in the second stage. Perineal tears occurred but were repaired immediately post-delivery. The patient is breastfeeding exclusively, which affects hormonal status. No episiotomy was performed.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pelvic floor muscle strength is reduced (2/5 on manual assessment). Voluntary contraction fatigue occurs rapidly within 2–3 repetitions. Palpation reveals tender scar tissue and fascial restrictions in the perineal body. Anal sphincter tone is intact and symmetric.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Transperineal ultrasound shows intact external anal sphincter and no defects in the perineal body. Pelvic floor muscle thickness is reduced compared to age-matched controls, consistent with muscular atrophy rather than structural trauma.

puzzles.explanation: Postnatal pelvic floor dysfunction results from mechanical trauma, neurogenic injury, and deconditioning of the pelvic floor musculature during labor and delivery. Instrumental delivery and prolonged labor increase the risk of muscle fiber injury and denervation, leading to weakness and rapid fatigue — particularly evident on manual testing and reflected in the reduced muscle thickness on ultrasound. The constellation of stress urinary incontinence with cough, dyspareunia, and fecal urgency reflects incompetence of both the urethral and anal closure mechanisms, compounded by scar tissue restriction and muscular atrophy from 12 weeks of activity avoidance. Intact external anal sphincter anatomy on imaging confirms that dysfunction here is primarily myofascial and neuromotor rather than structural, making progressive pelvic floor rehabilitation the cornerstone of recovery in this window when neural plasticity and tissue remodeling remain active.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old recreational athlete presents with insidious-onset anterolateral shoulder pain over 3 months, worsened by activity and overhead movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is worse with overhead reaching and cross-body adduction. Sleeping on the affected side aggravates symptoms. Rest provides gradual relief over days to weeks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Dominant right shoulder affected. Symptom onset coincided with increased training load and frequency of repetitive overhead activities. No acute trauma or fall. Previous shoulder problems denied.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Hawkins and Neer impingement tests. Supraspinatus weakness present but no complete loss of strength. Scapular kinematics normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickening and heterogeneity of the supraspinatus tendon with intact fiber continuity confirming tendinopathy. No full-thickness tear is present. Subacromial subdeltoid bursa shows mild fluid consistent with secondary bursitis.

puzzles.explanation: Rotator cuff tendinopathy represents a spectrum of degenerative change within the rotator cuff tendons—most commonly the supraspinatus—driven by repetitive microtrauma, inadequate recovery, and age-related tissue changes rather than a single traumatic insult. The insidious onset over months following increased training load, combined with pain in overhead and cross-body adduction patterns, reflects cumulative overuse in the dominant shoulder without acute structural failure. Positive impingement signs and subacromial bursa involvement occur because thickened, heterogeneous tendon tissue irritates the subacromial space; critically, tendon continuity remains intact on imaging, distinguishing tendinopathy from a tear. The gradual relief with rest and preservation of strength despite positive weakness testing are hallmarks of inflammatory tendon reaction rather than structural insufficiency, making conservative loading management and activity modification the foundation of recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old with chronic ankle pain and stiffness progressing over 5 years, particularly with recent acceleration. Long history of recurrent ankle injuries in the same ankle.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens significantly with ambulation on uneven ground and with pivoting movements. Walking tolerance has decreased from 45 minutes to 20 minutes due to progressive ankle pain. Morning stiffness lasting 30 minutes is reported upon waking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple ankle sprains over 30 years have progressively damaged the joint surfaces and ligamentous support. No significant trauma in the past year, but cumulative joint loading has been high from walking/hiking activities. Previous physiotherapy for ankle stability was completed 10 years ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ankle dorsiflexion is limited to 5 degrees (normal 15 degrees) and plantarflexion to 35 degrees (normal 50 degrees). Significant anteromedial ankle joint line tenderness is elicited. The gait demonstrates a stiff ankle pattern with circumduction. Weight-bearing calf raise produces pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing radiographs show marked joint space narrowing at the tibiotalar joint with marginal osteophytes and subchondral sclerosis. No significant subluxation or instability is evident. Imaging confirms advanced ankle osteoarthritis with degenerative changes consistent with post-traumatic disease.

puzzles.explanation: Ankle osteoarthritis is progressive degenerative joint disease of the tibiotalar joint, often developing years or decades after recurrent inversion injuries that progressively compromise articular cartilage and joint stability. Unlike other causes of ankle pain, post-traumatic osteoarthritis typically affects a single ankle and produces the characteristic pattern of morning stiffness, reduced dorsiflexion with plantarflexion relatively preserved, and anteromedial joint line tenderness—all reflecting the tibiofibular contact patterns altered by long-standing ligamentous laxity. The marked loss of dorsiflexion here, combined with the stiff gait and circumduction compensation, illustrates how even modest arthroscopic changes severely restrict ankle mobility and functional weight-bearing tolerance. Weight-bearing radiographs remain essential to confirm the diagnosis and grade severity, distinguishing osteoarthritis from other causes of chronic ankle pain in this demographic.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old athlete reports deep hip or groin pain that began 3 months ago. Pain is sharp or catching in character. No clear traumatic event. Pain is intermittent but reproducible.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with running, cutting, and pivoting movements. Patient also reports difficulty with deep squatting and bringing knee toward chest while sitting. Symptoms worse with high-impact activities; walking is tolerated.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset during increased training volume. No previous hip injury or surgery. Patient reports occasional catching or clicking sensation in the hip during certain movements. Pain does not radiate distally. No constitutional symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: FABER test (flexion-abduction-external rotation) reproduces groin pain. Anterior hip impingement test positive. Hip internal rotation in flexion is restricted (less than 10 degrees) and painful. Hip abductor strength normal. No neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the hip shows a tear in the anterosuperior labrum. T2 hyperintense signal within labral tissue. Tear extends from 12 o'clock to 2 o'clock position. No loose bodies or significant chondral damage.

puzzles.explanation: A hip labral tear involves disruption of the fibrocartilaginous labrum that deepens the acetabular socket and stabilizes the femoral head. The anterosuperior labrum is particularly vulnerable in athletes performing high-impact rotational activities — running, cutting, and pivoting — where repetitive impingement or shear forces gradually compromise labral integrity without requiring obvious trauma. The sharp, catching quality of pain combined with positive anterior impingement testing and restricted internal rotation in flexion reflects the mechanical irritation and loss of load-sharing capacity at the anterior hip joint. MRI confirmation with T2 hyperintensity in the labral tissue from the 12 to 2 o'clock position is classic for this injury pattern in young athletes, distinguishing it from other sources of deep hip pain like femoroacetabular impingement or muscle strains.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old recreational athlete reports sudden-onset severe pain in the posterior lower leg during a tennis match. A sharp, tearing sensation was felt, followed by immediate difficulty with weight-bearing. No prior calf symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports severe swelling has developed over the first 24-48 hours post-injury. Bruising is becoming visible extending from mid-calf toward the ankle. Morning stiffness and pain with any weight-bearing are significant.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports an episode of acute calf pain 10 years prior that self-resolved without intervention. Current injury occurred during explosive movement in tennis. No chronic calf problems or previous Achilles issues. No recent corticosteroid use or systemic illness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant calf swelling is noted with bruising extending toward the ankle over days post-injury. Plantarflexion strength is severely weakened (1-2/5) with pain limiting effort. The Thompson test is negative (indicating intact Achilles tendon). A midcalf muscle defect may be palpable on careful examination. Considerable ecchymosis is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic disruption in the gastrocnemius-soleus muscle complex with edema and hemorrhage consistent with a partial muscle tear (grade 2-3 strain). The tear is localized to the medial gastrocnemius. No Achilles tendon discontinuity is identified.

puzzles.explanation: Tennis leg is an acute partial tear of the medial gastrocnemius, typically occurring during explosive plantarflexion or dorsiflexion movements in recreational athletes. The injury classically presents with sudden severe pain, an audible or palpable tear sensation, and rapid development of swelling and ecchymosis tracking distally—exactly as seen in this mid-calf disruption. The negative Thompson test is diagnostically reassuring, confirming the Achilles tendon remains intact and distinguishing this from more serious musculotendinous injuries. Despite the dramatic presentation and prior similar episode, most tennis leg injuries recover well with conservative management, though early aggressive rehabilitation is needed to restore plantarflexion strength and prevent re-injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old desk worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the medial hand (ring and little fingers) over 4 months. Symptoms are worse at night and have begun affecting grip strength.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged typing exacerbates symptoms significantly. Sleeping with the elbow flexed triggers symptoms that wake the patient during the night. Activities requiring sustained elbow flexion (holding phone to ear) reproduce paresthesias within 5-10 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient works with poor ergonomic setup: armrest too low, keyboard too high, elbow flexed for prolonged periods. No recent trauma to the elbow.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's sign is elicited by percussing the ulnar nerve at the elbow (just posterior to the medial epicondyle). The Froment test is positive with inability to adduct the thumb without flexing the interphalangeal joint. Intrinsic hand muscle atrophy is subtle but present in the interossei. Two-point discrimination is diminished in the little finger.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of conduction velocity across the elbow segment consistent with cubital tunnel syndrome. EMG demonstrates denervation activity in intrinsic hand muscles and mild motor involvement. MRI shows compression of the ulnar nerve at the level of the cubital tunnel.

puzzles.explanation: Cubital tunnel syndrome results from compression of the ulnar nerve as it passes through the cubital tunnel at the elbow, most commonly due to repetitive flexion loading, prolonged pressure, or anatomical narrowing at the medial epicondyle. The condition classically presents with paresthesia in the ring and little fingers — the ulnar nerve's territory — that worsens with sustained elbow flexion and nocturnal symptoms from sleeping posture. The progressive intrinsic hand muscle atrophy and positive Froment sign reflect motor denervation, indicating moderate severity requiring intervention; cubital tunnel is particularly sensitive to ergonomic factors and positional loading, making desk work and flexed-elbow sleeping positions potent perpetuators. Nerve conduction abnormalities localizing to the elbow segment, combined with Tinel's sign at the posterior medial epicondyle, confirm the diagnosis and distinguish this from more distal ulnar compression at Guyon's canal.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 26-year-old rugby player. Ring finger caught forcefully during tackle. Sharp pain and immediate inability to bend finger at one joint. Swelling developed quickly in the forearm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot actively straighten the ring finger at rest; finger held in bent position. Pain worsens when attempting to bend the finger. Foregrip and gripping activities severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 hours ago during competitive contact sport. No prior hand or finger injuries. Patient is fully active athlete with high functional demands.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ring finger held in extension at DIP joint with inability to actively flex (0/5 DIP flexion). PIP flexion intact at 4/5 strength. Mild swelling noted in volar forearm proximal to wrist. Tender palm with fullness along flexor digitorum profundus tendon region. No sensory loss or vascular compromise. No wound or skin disruption.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows no bone avulsion at distal phalanx base (distinguishes from bony mallet). MRI demonstrates complete rupture of flexor digitorum profundus tendon at level of wrist crease. Tendon retracted proximally with gap of 3cm. Associated edema in forearm soft tissues. Ultrasound confirms tendon discontinuity with fluid collection (hematoma) tracking proximally in forearm.

puzzles.explanation: Jersey finger is an acute rupture of the flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) tendon, typically from a sudden forceful extension force applied to a flexed finger — classically seen when a player's finger is caught during a tackle. The FDP is the sole active flexor of the distal interphalangeal joint, so rupture results in complete loss of DIP flexion with the finger held in extension, as seen in this rugby player. The tendon retracts proximally into the forearm due to the pull of muscle belly, creating a palpable gap and associated edema; early imaging and surgical repair within 7-10 days is critical to restore function, as delayed repair compromises tendon quality and outcome. This injury demands urgent hand surgical referral and is one of the few acute tendon ruptures in hand physiotherapy where prompt intervention dramatically influences long-term functional recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 67-year-old woman. Gradual onset of leakage with coughing, sneezing, and laughing over past 18 months. Also reports sudden urge to void with occasional inability to reach toilet in time. Frequency increased to 10-12 times daily. Wakes 3-4 times nightly to void.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Leakage occurs with high-impact activities: coughing, jumping, carrying groceries. Manages by limiting fluid intake and planning toilet access. Avoids exercise and prolonged social outings due to unpredictable symptoms. Sleep quality poor from repeated nighttime voids.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began after third vaginal delivery 8 years ago; worsened significantly post-menopause (3 years ago). No prior pelvic surgery. Denies chronic cough, straining, or heavy lifting occupation. No neurological history, diabetes, or spinal pathology. Previous pelvic floor physiotherapy attempted 2 years ago with minimal benefit.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable anterior vaginal wall laxity and reduced tone on digital assessment. Weak voluntary contraction: unable to sustain squeeze >2 seconds, poor endurance. Pelvic heaviness sensation on Valsalva maneuver. Mild bulging of anterior compartment noted. Hip and lumbar spine mobility normal. No pain on palpation or movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Transperineal ultrasound shows minimal pelvic floor muscle contraction (bladder neck descent 8mm on Valsalva; normal <6mm). Anterior vaginal wall descent consistent with mild cystocele. Pelvic floor thickness reduced compared to normative data. Post-void residual <50mL. No structural nerve impingement on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic floor dysfunction encompasses weakness and neuromuscular impairment of the muscles supporting the bladder, uterus, and bowel — in this case manifesting as combined stress and urgency incontinence. The condition typically results from childbirth trauma (particularly vaginal delivery), hormonal decline post-menopause, or chronic straining, with cumulative damage reducing muscle tone, contractility, and endurance. The clinical picture here is classic: stress leakage with cough and impact activities alongside urge symptoms and nocturia, reflecting both structural weakness (evident as reduced pelvic floor thickness on ultrasound and anterior vaginal wall laxity) and compromised neuromuscular control. Successful management typically requires directed pelvic floor muscle training, though the prior failed physiotherapy attempt in this case may reflect inadequate initial assessment, pelvic floor hypertonicity masking weakness, or the need for alternative modalities such as biofeedback or neuromodulation given the significant functional impact and reduced muscle contractility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with 6 months of burning pain in the ball of the foot. Pain radiates into the toes and is notably aggravated by tight footwear and prolonged standing. Symptoms are intermittent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking on hard surfaces and ascending stairs exacerbate symptoms. Patient reports a sharp shooting sensation into specific toes during gait, particularly in the push-off phase. Activities requiring forefoot gripping (e.g., standing on tiptoes) are avoided.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has a documented history of the same condition on the contralateral foot 8 years ago, resolved with conservative measures. Symptoms are recurrent in the same occupation. No history of foot trauma or inflammatory arthropathy. No sensory disturbance elsewhere in the foot.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Direct compression between the metatarsal heads reproduces sharp pain into the 3rd and 4th toes (Mulder's test positive). A palpable click or 'pop' may be felt with this compression. Plantar palpation elicits exquisite tenderness in the interspace. Sensation to light touch is normal in the affected toes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic nodular mass between the 3rd and 4th metatarsal heads consistent with a neuroma (typically 3-5mm). MRI confirms a focal mass in the plantar intermetatarsal space with T2 signal intensity consistent with nerve tissue edema. Imaging rules out other forefoot pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Morton's neuroma is a benign proliferation of nerve tissue at the plantar interdigital nerve, typically between the 3rd and 4th metatarsal heads where the nerve is compressed between the metatarsal heads and deep transverse metatarsal ligament. The condition develops insidiously from chronic repetitive compression during forefoot loading — particularly during push-off and with tight footwear — causing perineural fibrosis and demyelination rather than true neoplastic growth. The sharp, shooting pain radiating into specific toes with a positive Mulder's test and the characteristic palpable 'pop' reflect the mechanical compression of the intermetatarsal nerve; notably, true neurological deficit (sensory loss) is absent because the nerve is compressed rather than transected. Recurrence on the contralateral foot years later is common given that biomechanical factors and footwear habits that predispose one foot typically affect the other, making bilateral presentation over time a recognized pattern in this condition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old admitted 4 days ago following hip fracture repair. Family reports sudden onset of confusion and disorientation that seems to come and go. Patient alternates between agitation and lethargy. Baseline cognition was borderline—family notes he was becoming more forgetful before admission.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During the day, patient is drowsy and difficult to engage in therapy. At night, he becomes agitated and tries to get out of bed unsupervised. Staff report he doesn't seem to know where he is or what time of day it is. Mood and alertness shift unpredictably hour to hour.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pre-admission cognition: early dementia with slow decline. Post-operatively: opioids introduced for pain, two sleep aids prescribed. Medical team has investigated and ruled out infection, electrolyte abnormality, and dehydration. Patient has no prior delirium episodes. This is his first time in hospital.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bedside cognition testing: cannot name the date or hospital location. Digit span: struggles to repeat 4 digits forward. Attention test (months backwards): stops after 3 months. No focal weakness, sensation intact, cranial nerves normal. Heart rate 78, BP 138/82, temperature 37.1°C.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT head performed to exclude acute intracranial pathology. No acute stroke, no subdural hematoma, no bleed. Only finding: chronic small vessel ischemic changes consistent with age. Structural causes ruled out. Clinical diagnosis of delirium confirmed.

puzzles.explanation: Delirium is an acute confusional state characterized by inattention, disorganized thinking, and altered consciousness that develops over hours to days—distinct from the gradual cognitive decline of dementia. In this post-operative context, delirium arose from multiple precipitating factors layered onto a vulnerable substrate: pre-existing mild cognitive impairment, opioid introduction, sedating medications, and the physiological stress of hip fracture and surgery, all creating a drug-induced and metabolic delirium despite normal investigations. The fluctuating presentation—daytime somnolence alternating with nocturnal agitation and disorientation to time and place—is classically delirious, as is the impaired attention demonstrated by poor digit span and inability to sequence months backward. Recognition of delirium as a medical emergency requiring medication review, pain optimization, sleep hygiene, mobility, and cognitive reorientation is critical to rehabilitation success; it is not dementia progression, and early intervention can prevent functional decline and prolonged hospital stay.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old man presents with progressive stiffness and loss of motion in the hip, developing over the past 3 months. Pain and swelling in the hip region limiting daily activities like dressing and walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressive loss of hip internal and external rotation. Hip flexion contracture developing (unable to fully extend hip). Abduction and adduction limited. Pain with attempted motion. Functional decline with difficulty dressing and toileting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hip posterior dislocation with femoral head fracture sustained 12 weeks ago from high-energy trauma. Significant soft tissue injury. Prolonged immobilization in acute phase followed by delayed mobilization.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion contracture (unable to achieve neutral extension). Severe restriction of internal rotation and external rotation (<15° each direction). Firm, non-painful end-feel on passive range. Palpable stiffness in periarticular tissues. No neurological deficit. Antalgic gait.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray hip demonstrates extensive bone formation in soft tissues surrounding the hip joint with bridging across the articulation, graded as severe. CT imaging shows mature ossified bridges in capsular and periarticular tissues. MRI confirms ossification signal consistent with mature bone.

puzzles.explanation: Heterotopic ossification is the pathological formation of bone in soft tissues where it normally does not exist, commonly occurring after high-energy trauma, hip dislocation, or fracture with significant soft tissue injury. The condition typically develops over weeks to months as inflammatory cytokines and bone morphogenetic proteins trigger mesenchymal cell differentiation into osteoblasts within damaged muscle, fascia, and joint capsules. The progressive loss of hip motion with a firm end-feel and the extensive periarticular bridging seen on imaging reflect mature heterotopic bone formation that mechanically restricts joint mobility — this is why motion loss accelerates in the weeks following trauma despite early immobilization. Prevention through NSAIDs or radiation in high-risk cases remains more effective than treatment once ossification is established, making early recognition after traumatic hip injury critical for prognosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes for 12 years presents with progressive pain in the feet and lower legs over the past 8 months. Pain is described as burning, electrical, or 'pins and needles' with a distal-to-proximal pattern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Light touch or clothing contact triggers sharp pain out of proportion to stimulus severity. Symptoms are notably worse at night and wake the patient from sleep. Prolonged standing or walking significantly exacerbates discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Glycemic control has been inconsistent (HbA1c 7.8–8.5% over 2 years). Patient is also hypertensive. No preceding injury or trauma to the feet. Symptoms began insidiously without clear trigger.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sharp-dull discrimination is diminished in the feet bilaterally with a stocking distribution. Vibration sense is reduced at the ankles. Monofilament testing (10g) shows loss of sensation in the plantar surface. Temperature sensation is mildly altered. Reflexes are diminished at the ankles. Motor strength is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show reduced motor and sensory conduction velocities in bilateral lower extremities consistent with demyelination. EMG demonstrates mild denervation in distal foot muscles. DN4 neuropathic pain questionnaire score is elevated, supporting neuropathic origin.

puzzles.explanation: Neuropathic pain arises from direct injury or dysfunction of the somatosensory nervous system, rather than from ongoing tissue damage—a distinction that fundamentally shapes both presentation and management. In this case, longstanding poorly controlled diabetes has caused distal sensorimotor polyneuropathy, progressively damaging small and large nerve fibres through metabolic and ischaemic mechanisms; the resulting demyelination and axonal loss explain the diminished vibration sense, stocking-distribution sensory loss, and slowed nerve conduction velocities. The hallmark burning, electric quality of neuropathic pain—worse at night and triggered disproportionately by light touch (allodynia)—reflects abnormal spontaneous firing in damaged nociceptors and sensitised dorsal horn neurons. This pattern of distal, bilateral, progressive symptoms with inconsistent glycaemic control and hypertension is characteristic of diabetic peripheral neuropathy, one of the most common causes of neuropathic pain in clinical practice.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 78-year-old reports increasing difficulty with memory and complex task management over 18 months. Spouse notices forgetfulness of recent conversations and confusion with familiar activities. Onset is gradual and progressive.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot manage new learning or complex sequential tasks without written support. Medication management with multiple prescriptions is now unreliable; requires supervision for unfamiliar activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of transient ischemic attack 5 years prior. Currently managed for hypertension and high cholesterol but medication adherence is inconsistent. Depression screening unremarkable. No acute neurological events in recent months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Formal cognitive screening using Montreal Cognitive Assessment shows score of 22/30 (below age-adjusted norms). Memory is impaired for recent events but long-term memory is preserved. Attention and executive function are mildly affected. Speech is normal and no focal neurological deficits are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals small vessel ischemic disease affecting white matter tracts with mild age-appropriate brain atrophy. No acute infarction, hemorrhage, or mass. Pattern consistent with vascular cognitive impairment.

puzzles.explanation: Cognitive impairment in older adults results from progressive deterioration in memory, attention, and executive function due to neurodegeneration or vascular pathology. This patient's gradual 18-month decline in recent memory and complex task management, combined with MRI findings of white matter ischemic disease, is characteristic of vascular cognitive impairment—a state where cerebrovascular changes compromise the integrity of neural networks without a single acute stroke event. The preserved long-term memory and absence of focal deficits help distinguish this from dementia with prominent language or motor involvement. Vascular risk factors like hypertension and prior TIA are key drivers; early recognition and aggressive management of these modifiable factors may slow further decline and are central to physiotherapy and multidisciplinary rehabilitation planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman fell onto an outstretched hand from standing height and reports immediate wrist pain with visible swelling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot grip or lift objects. Wrist movement in all directions is severely limited by pain. She is unable to perform self-care tasks involving hand use.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known osteoporosis with progressive balance impairment contributing to falls. No significant prior wrist injuries. Fall occurred at standing height without additional trauma context.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The wrist demonstrates a 'dinner fork' deformity with dorsal displacement of the distal fragment. Marked swelling of the dorsal wrist is evident. Tenderness is greatest over the distal radius. Radial and ulnar pulses are intact. Hand color and temperature appear normal, and finger sensation is preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain wrist radiographs (posteroanterior and lateral views) show a dorsally displaced metaphyseal fracture of the distal radius with comminution of the dorsal margin. Ulnar styloid fracture is present in 50% of cases. CT may be used to assess fracture complexity for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: A Colles fracture is a dorsally displaced fracture of the distal radius, typically occurring within 2–3 cm of the wrist joint, most commonly from a fall onto an outstretched hand in older adults with osteoporosis. The characteristic 'dinner fork' deformity—dorsal prominence of the distal fragment—results from the direction and mechanism of injury and is nearly pathognomonic on clinical inspection. The marked functional limitation in grip and wrist movement reflects both the fracture itself and the associated soft tissue trauma and swelling, though preserved neurovascular findings (intact pulses and sensation) indicate the fracture is stable from a vascular standpoint. Management depends on fracture complexity: simple fractures often heal well with reduction and immobilization, but comminuted or intra-articular patterns—as suggested by dorsal margin comminution here—may require surgical intervention to restore wrist alignment and facilitate early mobilization.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 7-year-old presents with a fourth fracture in 18 months after falling from a low height during play. Parents report the child has always bruised easily and been unusually fragile compared to siblings.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child is unable to weight-bear on the fractured limb and refuses to participate in running or contact play. Motor milestones have been delayed; walking began at 20 months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mother and maternal grandmother have history of recurrent fractures and hearing loss. Child's growth has trailed below 10th percentile since infancy. Audiometry at school screening shows conductive hearing loss. Dental clinician noted enamel hypoplasia with unusually early decay despite good hygiene.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized ligamentous laxity evident; passive range of motion at all major joints exceeds normal limits. Muscle bulk and tone are diminished throughout. Respiratory effort appears increased at rest. Skin is translucent with visible veining. Palate is high-arched.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays of the fractured limb show the acute fracture with osteopenic bone (reduced bone density) and thin cortices. Generalized skeletal survey reveals healed fractures in various stages of healing and evidence of previous long bone deformities. Bone density assessment (DEXA scan) shows significantly reduced bone density. Genetic testing confirms collagen I abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta is a genetic disorder of collagen I synthesis resulting in defective bone matrix formation, leading to increased fragility and susceptibility to fractures from minimal trauma. The recurrent fractures from low-energy falls, delayed motor milestones, and markedly reduced bone density on imaging are hallmark skeletal manifestations; the systemic connective tissue involvement explains the generalized ligamentous laxity, translucent skin, and high-arched palate seen here. Extra-skeletal features including conductive hearing loss (from ossicular involvement), enamel hypoplasia, and the vertical transmission pattern through the maternal line are equally diagnostic and reflect the ubiquitous role of collagen I across tissues. Genetic confirmation of the collagen I defect establishes the diagnosis and guides genetic counseling and management of this lifelong condition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old presents with 2 months of progressive deep shoulder pain and weakness. Pain is localised posteriorly but non-radiating. Weakness is noted with certain arm movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform overhead activities without compensation and reports sharp pain with throwing. Reaching behind the back is markedly limited. Interestingly, passive range of motion appears relatively preserved.

clues.label: History
clues.text: A fall on an outstretched arm with external rotation occurred 3 months ago, initially attributed to a minor strain. Symptoms have progressively worsened rather than improving. No significant prior shoulder history. The patient is right-hand dominant.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked weakness of external rotation (1-2/5) with reproduction of pain. Supraspinatus strength is preserved. Posterior shoulder atrophy is evident. Infraspinatus tenderness elicited on palpation beneath posterior deltoid.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows a full-thickness tear of the infraspinatus with mild retraction of the muscle belly. The supraspinatus is normal. Fatty infiltration is absent, indicating acute injury. Ultrasound confirms infraspinatus tear with gap at the musculotendinous junction. The tear size and patient age determine management approach.

puzzles.explanation: An infraspinatus tear results from disruption of the posterior rotator cuff, typically following traumatic external rotation forces like a fall on an outstretched arm — as occurred here three months prior. The infraspinatus is the primary external rotator of the shoulder, so full-thickness tears present with marked external rotation weakness (often 1–2/5) alongside posterior shoulder pain and atrophy, while passive motion remains relatively preserved since the glenohumeral joint itself is unaffected. The progressive worsening over weeks to months is characteristic of acute rotator cuff injury, distinguishing it from chronic degenerative tears; the absence of fatty infiltration on imaging confirms this acute timeline. Preserved supraspinatus strength helps differentiate this from superior rotator cuff lesions, making external rotation weakness the clinical hallmark that points specifically to infraspinatus pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old teacher with 3 days of fever and new-onset cough. Reports feeling acutely unwell with fatigue and general malaise.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cough is worsening and now productive. Shortness of breath with minimal exertion such as walking up stairs. Unable to return to work; lying flat makes cough worse.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previously healthy. Symptoms started abruptly 3 days ago without known exposure or recent travel. No prior respiratory conditions. High fever present since onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Temp 38.8°C, RR 22, HR 102, O₂ sat 94% on room air. Localized crackles heard on auscultation in left lower lobe. Dullness to percussion over same area. Egophony present. No wheezing or focal consolidation signs elsewhere.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows lobar consolidation in left lower lobe with air bronchograms. WBC elevated at 14,500/mL. Procalcitonin elevated. Sputum gram stain shows neutrophils and gram-positive cocci.

puzzles.explanation: Pneumonia is an acute infection of the lung parenchyma causing inflammation, consolidation, and impaired gas exchange — most commonly bacterial in origin with sudden onset as seen here. The clinical picture of high fever, productive cough, dyspnoea, and acute systemic illness reflects the inflammatory response to bacterial colonisation, while the localised left lower lobe findings on examination and imaging are typical of lobar pneumonia with characteristic signs of consolidation: dullness to percussion, bronchial breathing, and egophony. The elevated WBC, procalcitonin, and gram-positive cocci on sputum culture point toward typical community-acquired pneumonia pathogens. Early recognition and antibiotic therapy are critical, as untreated pneumonia can rapidly progress to respiratory compromise, sepsis, or pleural involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old desk worker presents with swelling over the posterior aspect of the elbow that developed insidiously over 2 weeks. Pain is mild and localised to direct pressure on the swollen area; elbow movement remains largely pain-free.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Swelling is significantly worse after resting or leaning on the elbow for extended periods. Activities requiring active elbow movement are tolerated well; however, passive end-range elbow extension and direct contact with the swollen area provoke discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient recalls no specific trauma but notes increased time leaning on elbows while working at a desk over the past 3 months. No fever, chills, or systemic symptoms are reported. No intravenous drug use. Rheumatoid arthritis screening has been negative.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination: prominent soft, fluctuant swelling localised to the olecranon bursa. Overlying skin is normal in appearance without erythema or warmth. Palpation of the bursal fluid elicits tenderness, but the olecranon bone itself is non-tender. Elbow range of motion is full and pain-free. No systemic signs: no fever, no local erythema, lymph nodes normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a fluid collection in the olecranon bursa without internal echoes (simple fluid) or thick septations, consistent with bursitis. Bursal wall is thickened. No osteophytes or bone erosion is evident. Imaging rules out septic bursitis (which would show echogenic debris or thick loculations).

puzzles.explanation: Olecranon bursitis is inflammation of the fluid-filled sac overlying the olecranon process, typically caused by repetitive pressure or minor trauma rather than acute injury. The classic presentation—insidious swelling localised to the posterior elbow with pain only on direct pressure or palpation of the bursa itself, while active movement remains pain-free—reflects the bursa's superficial anatomy and non-critical role in joint mechanics. Chronic leaning on the elbow, as in this desk worker, is a common culprit for mechanical irritation and fluid accumulation. The absence of fever, erythema, systemic signs, and echogenic debris on ultrasound confirms this is aseptic (non-infectious) bursitis; simple fluid aspiration or conservative offloading (pressure relief, relative rest) typically resolves the condition within weeks.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports acute lateral midfoot pain during or immediately after a push-off movement or twisting incident. Weight bearing is significantly limited. Pain onset is sudden.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Lateral foot pain is reproducible with single-leg hop and push-off. Swelling develops within hours. Pain does not resolve with rest and anti-inflammatory management over several days.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 weeks ago during running. Initial swelling and bruising were pronounced. Patient attempted to continue activity. Pain has not improved despite relative rest. No prior foot injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness is sharply localized to the lateral midfoot base (fifth metatarsal region); palpation reproduces exact pain location. Weight bearing on the affected foot is severely limited. Ankle inversion and eversion are pain-free. Resisted plantarflexion provokes sharp lateral foot pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate transverse fracture at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal. CT imaging assesses fracture displacement and healing. Advanced imaging guides timing of weight bearing progression.

puzzles.explanation: A Jones fracture is a transverse break at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal, a distinct injury from the more common avulsion fracture at the metatarsal base. It typically occurs in athletes during push-off or twisting movements due to the combination of plantarflexion loading and inversion stress at a zone of naturally poor blood supply. The fracture's location explains why resisted plantarflexion reproduces pain while ankle inversion remains painless — the force vector stresses the fracture site directly rather than the lateral ligaments. These injuries are notoriously slow to heal and prone to non-union due to their metaphyseal location and the high mechanical demands placed on the fifth metatarsal during running, making imaging assessment and judicious weight bearing progression essential to a successful outcome.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old reports pain in the face near the ears, onset over 2 months. Describes occasional clicking or popping sensation when moving the jaw. No trauma or recent dental work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty eating hard or chewy foods. Prolonged talking worsens symptoms. Patient notices jaw feels stiff in the morning. Cannot open mouth as wide as usual without discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset without clear precipitant. No recent oral surgery, orthodontics, or whiplash. Reports high stress and jaw clenching habit, especially at night. No systemic rheumatologic history. Symptoms bilateral but asymmetric.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Maximum mouth opening 35mm (normal 40–50mm). Jaw midline deviates 10mm leftward on opening. Bilateral tenderness on palpation over joint capsules. Palpable clicking reproducible during opening/closing cycle. Masseter and temporalis muscles tender with myofascial trigger points. No trismus, facial edema, or neurological deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of temporomandibular joints shows bilateral disc displacement with reduction on the affected side. Mild joint effusion and condylar cartilage thinning noted. Muscles appear normal without denervation. No fracture, tumor, or severe degenerative changes on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Temporomandibular joint disorder encompasses a spectrum of mechanical and muscular dysfunctions affecting the jaw joint and surrounding musculature, commonly triggered by parafunctional habits like clenching and grinding, postural stress, or psychological tension. The condition often presents with the characteristic triad of joint noise (clicking or popping from disc displacement), restricted mouth opening, and myofascial pain in the jaw muscles—as seen in this patient's gradual onset with nocturnal clenching and bilateral muscular tenderness. The disc displacement with reduction noted on MRI reflects the most common anatomical finding: anterior displacement of the meniscus that briefly relocates during opening, producing the audible click. Management typically emphasizes activity modification, muscle relaxation, and postural/stress reduction rather than invasive intervention, as most cases resolve with conservative care.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old presents with sharp anterior chest wall pain on the left side for 3 weeks, worse with deep breathing and coughing. The pain is localized and reproducible. Cardiac causes are being considered in the differential.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Movements that increase chest wall tension or rib cage excursion aggravate symptoms, particularly heavy lifting and repetitive pushing. Sleeping on the left side is uncomfortable. Symptoms improve with arm support and reduced upper body activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient had an upper respiratory infection with persistent cough 2 months ago; however, the cough has resolved, but pain persists. No trauma to the chest wall or weight lifting occurred. Previous episode of similar pain occurred 3 years ago and resolved in 6 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness is elicited on direct palpation of specific points on the anterior rib cage. Chest wall ROM is unrestricted. Cardiac auscultation is normal. Respiratory examination reveals no abnormalities.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows no acute findings. ECG is normal, excluding cardiac causes. Clinical examination alone is sufficient to establish the diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Costochondritis is inflammation of the cartilaginous junctions between the ribs and sternum, typically self-limiting but often protracted. It commonly follows upper respiratory infections with persistent coughing, as the repetitive rib cage excursion causes mechanical irritation of these articulations; the history of preceding cough followed by pain localized to specific costochondral junctions is characteristic. The condition is exacerbated by any movement increasing chest wall tension—deep breathing, coughing, heavy lifting, or sleeping on the affected side—and is reproduced reliably on direct palpation of the tender junction, making clinical examination diagnostic without imaging. Recurrent episodes are common, and recovery typically spans 6-12 weeks, though some patients experience prolonged symptoms that warrant monitoring for other musculoskeletal or systemic contributors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old presents with a lifetime history of joint instability and diffuse joint pain that worsens with activity. Frequent episodes of partial dislocations, particularly in shoulders and fingers, have occurred without obvious trauma. Friends have remarked on unusual flexibility since childhood.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot sustain repetitive fine motor activities (typing, playing instruments) without pain and apprehension. General exercise tolerance is preserved, but dynamic multi-joint movements (jumping, pivoting, contact sports) consistently provoke instability episodes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong pattern of joint instability with no single injury explaining the distribution. Mother has documented joint hypermobility. Patient reports easy bruising, delayed wound healing, and childhood ability to perform extreme flexibility feats (thumb-to-wrist bending, full splits).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Beighton score 6/9 (thumbs touch forearm, fingers hyperextend >90°, palms flat on floor with knees straight, elbow hyperextension, knee hyperextension). Shoulder girdle strength reduced symmetrically. Proprioceptive testing shows subtle deficits; postural stability during single-leg stance is reduced.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Beighton score 6/9 (thumbs touch forearm, fingers hyperextend >90°, palms flat on floor with knees straight, elbow hyperextension, knee hyperextension). Shoulder girdle strength reduced symmetrically. Proprioceptive testing shows subtle deficits; postural stability during single-leg stance is reduced.

puzzles.explanation: Generalised joint hypermobility reflects a systemic connective tissue disorder affecting collagen synthesis or organization, resulting in ligamentous laxity and reduced joint stability across multiple joints. The condition manifests early in life — often recognized through childhood flexibility feats and a familial pattern — and presents clinically with recurrent partial dislocations, diffuse joint pain exacerbated by dynamic activities, and proprioceptive deficits that undermine neuromuscular control. The Beighton score of 6/9, combined with functional limitations in repetitive fine motor tasks and multi-joint movements like jumping and pivoting, captures the core challenge: while passive range of motion is excessive, the musculoskeletal system lacks the stability to protect joints during loaded or coordinated activity. Management hinges on neuromuscular re-education, proprioceptive training, and graduated strengthening of shoulder girdle and core stabilizers to compensate for inherent ligamentous insufficiency.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain for 3 weeks. Pain began during overhead serve motion. Reports occasional clicking sensation in shoulder during reaching.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate overhead throwing or serving. Pain worsens with sustained reaching above shoulder height. Symptoms improve partially with rest and ice, but night pain disturbs sleep, particularly when lying on the affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Repetitive overhead sports participation (tennis 4x/week) for past year. Gradual onset suggesting tendinopathy rather than acute tear. No prior shoulder surgery. Underlying rotator cuff dysfunction noted on screening examination.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Speed's test (supinated elbow flexion against resistance) reproduces anterior shoulder pain. Yergason test (supinated forearm with elbow flexion) also positive. Palpable tenderness over bicipital groove. Apprehension test negative. Rotator cuff strength 4+/5 suggesting dynamic instability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening (5mm) with hypoechoic echotexture and increased Doppler signal indicating active inflammation. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration of the proximal biceps tendon with intact fibres and no full-thickness tear.

puzzles.explanation: Biceps tendinopathy results from repetitive microtrauma and inflammation of the long head of the biceps tendon, typically arising in overhead athletes with concurrent rotator cuff dysfunction that creates abnormal glenohumeral kinematics. The proximal biceps is particularly vulnerable during overhead motions like tennis serving, as it acts as a dynamic stabilizer when cuff muscles fatigue or underperform—explaining why gradual onset and underlying rotator cuff weakness characterize this patient's presentation. The thickened, hypoechoic tendon with Doppler signal reflects active inflammation in the bicipital groove, while positive Speed's and Yergason's tests confirm biceps involvement and differentiate it from other anterior shoulder pathologies. Night pain and symptoms exacerbated by sustained reaching above shoulder height are hallmark features of biceps tendinopathy, distinguishing it from subacromial impingement alone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old presents 4 weeks post-operatively from a high-energy elbow injury requiring surgery. Progressive pain and severe loss of motion have developed despite appropriate post-operative management. Swelling persists.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Elbow extension severely limited to approximately 60 degrees of flexion; passive attempts provoke significant pain and guarding. Basic functional activities like reaching overhead and grooming are now impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sustained the original injury 4 weeks ago from a fall from height. Required open surgical reduction and internal fixation due to fracture-dislocation. Significant soft tissue trauma at time of injury. Post-operative wound healing and rehabilitation have progressed normally otherwise, but motion loss and pain are worsening despite therapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe soft tissue swelling persists over the antecubital fossa and dorsal elbow. Elbow flexion is approximately 90 degrees. Attempts at passive extension reproduce significant pain and apprehension. Skin temperature is slightly elevated. Radial and ulnar pulses remain intact. Fingers are warm with normal color.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs reveal cloud-like opacities within soft tissues around the elbow with early demarcation—consistent with heterotopic bone formation. CT demonstrates extent of ossification in relation to the surgical fixation. Bone scan shows marked uptake in the affected region.

puzzles.explanation: Myositis ossificans is pathological heterotopic bone formation within soft tissues, most commonly triggered by high-energy trauma, surgical intervention, or neurological injury—particularly around the elbow and hip. The condition progresses through predictable phases: an acute inflammatory phase (weeks 1–2) with swelling and pain, followed by a fibroblastic phase where primitive mesenchymal cells differentiate into osteoblasts and lay down immature bone matrix, visible radiographically as the characteristic cloud-like opacities seen here. The severe loss of motion and persistent pain developing in the weeks following ORIF for fracture-dislocation reflect this active ossification process; early aggressive passive stretching can paradoxically worsen heterotopic bone formation, making pain-guided gentle mobility and anti-inflammatory measures (indomethacin or prophylactic radiation in high-risk cases) the cornerstone of management during this critical window. Recognition of this complication is essential, as untreated myositis ossificans can result in permanent joint ankylosis and profound functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports sharp shoulder pain localized to the top of the shoulder following direct impact during contact sport or from a fall. Pain worsens with arm movements that cross the midline or reach overhead.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform bench press or push-ups. Symptoms flare when throwing or reaching across the body, such as fastening a seat belt. Overhead lifting is markedly painful.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during contact sport or fall onto shoulder 1 week ago. Immediate sharp pain and swelling noted. Patient has been unable to return to sport. No previous shoulder injuries or AC joint problems.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked tenderness directly over the AC joint with joint line palpation. Crossover arm adduction test reproduces sharp pain at the joint. Shoulder abduction strength is intact but adduction under load causes discomfort. No signs of glenohumeral instability or rotator cuff pathology.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate clavicular displacement relative to the acromion based on sprain grade. Weighted views show degree of clavicular elevation if AC ligament is disrupted. MRI reveals ligamentous injury (AC and coracoclavicular ligaments) with associated capsular inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Acromioclavicular joint sprains result from disruption of the ligamentous complex (AC and coracoclavicular ligaments) following direct trauma or fall onto the shoulder, with severity graded by the degree of clavicular displacement and ligament involvement. The sharp, localized pain directly over the AC joint that worsens with cross-body adduction and overhead movements is characteristic, as these positions create compressive and shear forces through the injured joint. The mechanism here—direct impact during contact sport—combined with immediate swelling and MRI confirmation of ligamentous injury with capsular inflammation, is typical of acute sprains. Recovery depends on grade; while grade I-II sprains often respond well to conservative management focusing on relative rest and graduated strengthening, higher grades may require surgical stabilization, particularly in overhead athletes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 64-year-old presents 7 days post-operatively from abdominal surgery with acute pain at the surgical site and visible leakage of fluid from the incision. Patient reports sudden onset during the recovery period.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform normal post-operative mobilisation. Straining, coughing, or bending triggers sharp pain and increased fluid drainage from the site. Ambulation is severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Initial post-operative course was unremarkable until day 6, when sudden pain and drainage developed. Patient has significant post-operative cough and BMI of 32. No prior wound complications or immunosuppression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visual inspection of the incision reveals partial separation of the wound edges with visible subcutaneous tissue in the area of separation. Serosanguineous drainage is present. The surrounding skin is erythematous. No systemic signs of infection are present (normal temperature, vital signs stable). Some wound edges remain intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound confirms separation of fascial and subcutaneous layers with a fluid collection measuring 3 cm. No evidence of loculated abscess. Intact deep fascia noted on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Wound dehiscence is a partial or complete separation of surgical wound layers occurring within the early post-operative period, typically days 5–10, when primary closure strength is still developing. It results from inadequate tensile strength of healing tissue combined with mechanical stress — here, the combination of post-operative cough and elevated BMI created excessive intra-abdominal pressure at a vulnerable phase. The acute presentation with serosanguineous drainage and visible subcutaneous tissue separation, while the deep fascia remains intact, is classic for partial dehiscence; full-thickness separation involving fascia carries risk of evisceration and requires urgent surgical review. Management depends on severity: superficial dehiscence may heal with conservative management and dressing, but this patient requires close monitoring and likely surgical assessment given the extent of separation and fluid collection.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old man wakes with sudden severe unilateral neck pain and inability to move his head normally. Onset during sleep with no recall of trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot turn head to the affected side. Rotation in opposite direction is possible but painful. All neck movements exacerbate symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior neck problems. No trauma recalled. Symptoms started immediately upon waking; patient had slept on an unfamiliar pillow.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant muscle spasm in ipsilateral sternocleidomastoid and upper trapezius visible. Neck rotation severely restricted in direction of pain, approximately 20 degrees. Palpable muscle tightness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays show normal alignment and no acute bony pathology. No evidence of subluxation or fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Acute wry neck, or acute torticollis, is a sudden-onset cervical dysfunction characterized by severe unilateral muscle spasm—typically in the sternocleidomastoid or upper trapezius—that constrains neck rotation and causes significant pain. The condition most commonly arises from sustained awkward positioning during sleep or minor mechanical strain, where prolonged muscle tension or microtrauma triggers protective muscle guarding that becomes self-perpetuating. Unlike structural pathology, imaging remains normal because the problem is muscular rather than skeletal; the marked rotational restriction and ipsilateral muscle spasm seen here reflect the reflex guarding response rather than bony involvement. Recovery typically occurs within days to weeks with early mobilization, heat, and gentle stretching, though the acute phase can be profoundly limiting for neck motion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 48-year-old with acute onset shoulder pain and stiffness over the past 2 weeks. Pain is constant, worse with arm movement, and disrupts sleep. No recent trauma reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to lift arm overhead or reach behind back. Sharp pain with any elevation beyond 90 degrees. Symptoms ease slightly with arm at rest in sling position.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient had chronic shoulder pain and stiffness for months prior, worse with overhead activities. Recent intervention performed. Pre-operative imaging showed degenerative changes and subacromial inflammation. Good compliance with post-operative rehabilitation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Supraspinatus and infraspinatus weakness with guarding. Positive apprehension with external rotation. Scapular dyskinesis during arm elevation. Tenderness over anterior shoulder and AC joint. Range of motion limited in abduction and external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative ultrasound confirms intact rotator cuff with no new tears. Subacromial space is enlarged. Osteophytes have been removed. Persistent mild subdeltoid edema present, consistent with post-operative inflammation and healing phase.

puzzles.explanation: Shoulder arthroscopy is a minimally invasive surgical procedure used to diagnose and treat intra-articular and subacromial pathology, including rotator cuff tears, impingement, and osteoarthritis. In this case, the patient underwent arthroscopic subacromial decompression to relieve chronic impingement—the removal of osteophytes and inflamed tissue explains the enlarged subacromial space on post-operative imaging. The acute post-operative phase typically presents with pain, stiffness, and guarding (as seen with the supraspinatus and infraspinatus weakness), which gradually resolve with appropriate rehabilitation over weeks to months. The intact rotator cuff and mild subdeltoid edema are reassuring findings consistent with normal healing, and symptom management through progressive physiotherapy remains the priority during this recovery window.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old with 3-month history of progressive mid-back pain. Describes a tight, banding sensation across the chest and upper abdomen. No prior significant trauma or fall.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worse with prolonged sitting and forward bending. Difficulty maintaining upright posture. Pain does not radiate into limbs but trunk discomfort limits daily activities. No relief with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sedentary desk job. Symptoms began insidiously without incident. Minimal previous back problems. Progressive worsening over 3 months despite conservative measures including rest and activity modification. No constitutional symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Thoracic spine tenderness at mid-thoracic levels. Mild lower extremity weakness and hyperreflexia bilaterally. Reduced abdominal sensation in a band-like distribution. Gait remains intact. No focal neurological deficit in upper extremities.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows central disc herniation at T7–T8 with cord compression. T2-weighted sequences reveal hyperintense signal within the spinal cord consistent with cord edema. Degree of canal stenosis correlates with clinical presentation.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic disc herniation results from nucleus pulposus extrusion through the annulus fibrosus in the thoracic spine, most common at the mid-thoracic levels where the canal is already narrower and the cord more vulnerable to compression. This condition typically presents insidiously in middle-aged individuals with poor postural habits—the banding chest and trunk pain reflects direct compression of the spinal cord and ventral nerve roots without the radicular limb pain seen in cervical or lumbar herniations. The combination of progressive mid-back pain worsened by flexion, bilateral lower extremity hyperreflexia with band-like sensory loss, and cord edema on MRI are hallmark features that distinguish thoracic disc herniation from mechanical back pain; the cord signal change indicates myelopathic involvement and underscores why surgical decompression is often considered rather than conservative management alone. Sedentary postures with sustained thoracic kyphosis accelerate discogenic degeneration, making ergonomic factors and postural correction central to both prevention and rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old with sudden onset of severe vertigo and nausea, started 3 days after an upper respiratory infection resolved. Vertigo is worse with any head movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot walk without severe imbalance and falls risk. Cannot tolerate reading, screen use, or rapid eye movements. Has vomited multiple times since onset.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden hearing loss noted in left ear since vertigo onset. No prior episodes of dizziness or hearing problems. No head trauma, no medication changes. Lives alone, unable to care for self currently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Spontaneous nystagmus present, fast component beating toward left ear. Head impulse test abnormal on leftward rotation—unable to maintain target fixation. Romberg test positive. Nausea reproduced with rapid head turns. Weber test lateralizes to left (conductive pattern). Rinne shows air-bone gap on left.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI with contrast shows T2 hyperintensity in the labyrinth on the affected side consistent with labyrinthitis. The vestibular nerve may show enhancement. Audiometry documents sensorineural hearing loss in the affected ear. Imaging and audiometry confirm viral labyrinthitis with associated hearing loss.

puzzles.explanation: Labyrinthitis is acute inflammation of the inner ear labyrinth, typically following viral upper respiratory infection, causing sudden-onset vertigo, nausea, and audiovestibular dysfunction. The condition damages both the semicircular canals (producing severe vertigo worse with head movement and spontaneous nystagmus) and the cochlea simultaneously, explaining the sensorineural hearing loss that develops alongside the balance disturbance. The leftward-beating nystagmus and abnormal head impulse test reflect acute vestibular hypofunction on the affected side, while the MRI hyperintensity and audiometric confirmation of sensorineural loss distinguish true labyrinthitis from peripheral vestibular causes like benign paroxysmal positional vertigo. Recovery typically occurs over weeks to months as inflammation resolves, though vestibular rehabilitation is often needed to retrain central compensation and restore balance confidence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with chronic dizziness and vertigo sensation lasting 8 months. Symptoms fluctuate but persistently present. Not true vertigo but persistent dizziness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids busy visual environments and crowded spaces. Difficulty walking straight lines. Symptoms worse with head movements and self-motion. Better when standing still or holding onto stable support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Dizziness onset post-vestibular neuritis 8 months prior. Acute vestibular symptoms resolved but dizziness persisted and worsened. Anxiety developed from symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Head impulse test normal (preserved VOR gain). Tandem stance and Romberg stable. Positive visually-evoked vestibulo-ocular reflex sensitivity (symptoms provoked by visual motion). Gaze stability normal. No spontaneous nystagmus.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ENG: previously showed vestibular asymmetry now resolved. MRI brain: normal. Caloric testing: normal vestibular function bilaterally. Diagnosis of PPPD confirmed by chronic symptoms persisting 8+ months post-vestibular neuritis with normalization of objective vestibular testing and absence of CNS pathology.
