puzzles.explanation: Persistent Postural-Perceptual Dizziness (PPPD) is a chronic vestibular disorder characterized by ongoing dizziness and postural instability that develops following an acute vestibular event—in this case, vestibular neuritis—but persists long after objective vestibular function has normalized. The condition represents a maladaptive interaction between the vestibular system, visual processing, and postural control, where heightened sensitivity to visual motion and self-motion cues perpetuates symptoms even though the underlying peripheral vestibular pathology has resolved. Patients typically develop avoidance behaviors around visual complexity and movement, which paradoxically reinforces the central sensitization; the normal head impulse test and preserved caloric responses here confirm resolution of the initial peripheral deficit, yet symptoms persist due to central gain mechanisms and anxiety amplification. Recognition of PPPD is crucial because it requires vestibular rehabilitation focused on desensitization to motion and visual triggers, rather than further investigation or peripheral interventions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old secretary reports progressive numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, and middle fingers bilaterally, worse on the right. Symptoms are particularly troublesome at night, frequently awakening her from sleep.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged typing aggravates symptoms within 30 minutes of work. Gripping objects produces numbness and tingling. Driving worsens symptoms. Night symptoms interfere with sleep quality, requiring frequent position changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Works 8 hours daily at a computer with poor ergonomic positioning. No prior wrist trauma or fracture history. Recent thyroid screening was normal.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's sign is elicited by percussing over the median nerve at the wrist crease. Phalen's test (wrist flexion held 30 seconds) reproduces numbness and tingling within 10 seconds bilaterally. Two-point discrimination is diminished on the thumb and index finger. Hand strength is normal in the intrinsic muscles tested.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show prolonged latency of median sensory and motor nerves across the wrist compared to the forearm, confirming conduction slowing at the carpal tunnel level. Electromyography shows mild denervation in the abductor pollicis brevis. MRI shows flattening of the median nerve at the wrist. Studies confirm carpal tunnel syndrome bilaterally.

puzzles.explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome results from compression of the median nerve as it passes through the carpal tunnel at the wrist, most commonly due to tenosynovial thickening, repetitive flexion loading, or systemic factors. The condition classically presents with nocturnal paraesthesia in the thumb, index, and middle fingers—the median nerve distribution—that wakes patients from sleep, as seen with this secretary whose prolonged typing and poor ergonomic positioning increase intraneural pressure. The bilateral involvement reflects the occupational nature of the precipitant and cumulative loading pattern. Diagnosis is confirmed by positive Phalen's and Tinel's tests, sensory changes in the median nerve distribution, and electrophysiologic evidence of slowed conduction across the wrist—findings that help distinguish carpal tunnel from more proximal median nerve lesions or cervical radiculopathy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 22-year-old collegiate soccer player with acute anterior knee pain during a cutting maneuver. Reports sensation of something tearing. Immediate inability to continue activity with rapid swelling onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform straight leg raise or active knee extension. Sharp pain with any attempt to straighten the knee or bear weight through extended leg. Functional guarding of the entire knee.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked anterior knee swelling with visible hematoma over tibial tuberosity. Complete loss of active knee extension strength (0/5). Quadriceps musculature palpable but unable to contract. Large knee effusion. Patellar tendon tense and exquisitely tender.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young athletic male with high-demand sport (soccer). Acute traumatic mechanism during cutting maneuver. No prior knee surgery or injury. Recent growth spurt (18 years old) with some residual cartilage sensitivity. Full participation in sport up to injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP, lateral, and Merchant views) shows avulsion of tibial tuberosity fragment with 1.5cm displacement. CT confirms fracture extends into articular surface of tibial plateau. MRI shows complete disruption of patellar tendon at bone insertion with severe edema. Associated bone marrow edema in proximal tibia. Soft tissue trauma with hematoma extensive.

puzzles.explanation: A tibial tuberosity avulsion fracture occurs when forceful quadriceps contraction or sudden deceleration avulses the bony insertion of the patellar tendon from the proximal tibia, typically in skeletally immature or recently mature athletes during high-demand activities. This injury is distinct from isolated patellar tendon rupture because the fracture fragment remains attached to the tendon, creating a bony block to knee extension and rendering the extensor mechanism completely non-functional—hence the 0/5 active extension strength and inability to perform a straight leg raise. The acute traumatic cutting maneuver in a young soccer player with recent skeletal maturation is the classic mechanism, as the tibial tuberosity remains relatively weak until full physeal closure in the early twenties. The marked anterior swelling, palpable tense patellar tendon, and imaging confirmation of displacement with articular surface involvement signal a significant injury requiring surgical reduction to restore the extensor mechanism and prevent long-term quadriceps weakness and functional limitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old presents 8 weeks after an ankle inversion injury. Initial injury seemed minor, but pain and swelling have progressively worsened and remain out of proportion to the original trauma. Pain is described as burning and constant.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot tolerate any weight-bearing and avoids contact with the affected foot. Even light touch provokes severe burning pain that is disproportionate to normal sensory stimulation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Initial ankle sprain from walking. Pain has escalated progressively over 8 weeks despite appropriate rehabilitation and ligamentous healing. Patient reports secondary depression and anxiety.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Examination reveals bright red, warm, edematous ankle and foot with shiny skin appearance. Sudomotor changes are evident with increased sweating and skin moisture. Vasomotor changes include color variation from red to purple depending on dependency. Nails appear brittle. Pain assessment with gentle touch demonstrates allodynia. Strength testing is limited by pain, not by motor deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Three-phase bone scan shows increased uptake in the delayed phase with blood pooling pattern. Radiographs demonstrate spotty, patchy osteoporosis. MRI reveals edema in bone and soft tissues. Combined clinical and imaging findings meet diagnostic criteria for the suspected condition.

puzzles.explanation: Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) is a chronic pain condition characterized by disproportionate pain, autonomic dysfunction, and motor impairment following an initially minor injury—in this case, a seemingly simple ankle sprain. The hallmark of CRPS is pain vastly exceeding what the original tissue damage would predict, combined with allodynia (pain to light touch), vasomotor instability (color and temperature changes), sudomotor changes (excessive sweating), and trophic changes (edema, shiny skin, brittle nails) that reflect dysregulation of the sympathetic nervous system. The progressive worsening over 8 weeks despite appropriate healing, along with the three-phase bone scan showing blood pooling and patchy osteoporosis, are classic findings that support early diagnosis and justify aggressive interdisciplinary treatment. Early recognition is critical because prolonged immobilization and pain avoidance—though understandable—paradoxically worsen outcomes; graded exposure, mirror therapy, and pain neuroscience education offer the best prognosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old presents with sudden onset severe headache, dizziness, and nausea. Unable to walk without assistance. Symptoms began without warning while at home this morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot stand or walk without severe vertigo and loss of balance. Any attempt at fine motor tasks (picking up objects, writing) causes incoordination. Nausea worsens with head movement and positional changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and hyperlipidemia. Heavy smoker. No prior stroke or TIA. Onset was sudden with no prodrome. Patient has not fallen or struck head recently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cognition and speech comprehension intact. Horizontal nystagmus present on lateral gaze. Dysmetria demonstrated on bilateral finger-to-nose testing. Gait is wide-based and severely ataxic; unable to perform tandem stance or single-leg stance. Strength and sensation normal throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain with DWI shows acute restricted diffusion lesion in the posterior fossa, predominantly affecting the cerebellum. Cerebrospinal fluid is clear. Posterior circulation vasculature patent on MR angiography.

puzzles.explanation: Cerebellar stroke results from acute ischaemia to the cerebellum, most commonly via posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) occlusion in the context of vertebrobasilar disease—a particular risk in patients with hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and smoking history. The cerebellum's critical role in motor coordination and balance means that even modest infarction produces dramatic functional deficits: the ataxic gait, dysmetria on finger-to-nose testing, and inability to maintain stance all reflect disruption of cerebellar output to the brainstem and spinal cord. The sudden onset, preserved cognition and sensation, and characteristic nystagmus help distinguish this from other causes of acute dizziness and vertigo. Cerebellar stroke is a neurosurgical emergency—swelling can compress the brainstem and obstruct the fourth ventricle, making early imaging and close monitoring essential.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old tennis player felt a sudden sharp sensation in the back of the lower leg during an explosive pushing movement in play. Immediate severe pain and inability to continue. No prior significant leg injuries.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot rise up on toes or push off the ground. Severe pain and instability with any attempt to plantarflex the ankle under load. Walks with foot held in slight plantarflexion. Stair climbing severely restricted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during explosive push-off movement. No warm-up prior to match. Otherwise sedentary between competitions; age 52. No prior significant calf problems.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant calf swelling is immediately visible, with bruising developing within hours and advancing distally toward the ankle. Plantarflexion strength is 0-1/5 (severe weakness). The Thompson test is negative (calf squeeze does not produce plantarflexion, suggesting gastrocnemius-soleus tear rather than Achilles rupture). Calf tenderness is marked in the midsubstance region.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic disruption in the gastrocnemius or soleus muscle with surrounding edema and hemorrhage (grade 2-3 muscle strain). The musculotendinous junction is intact. Achilles tendon is continuous. Imaging grade guides management (conservative for grade 1-2, possibly surgical for grade 3).

puzzles.explanation: Calf strain occurs from excessive or sudden loading of the gastrocnemius-soleus complex, typically during explosive plantarflexion activities like tennis push-offs—a mechanism that commonly affects older, sedentary athletes whose tissues lack conditioning and elasticity. The severity here (grade 2-3) is reflected in immediate profound weakness, inability to generate plantarflexion force, and the characteristic "Thompson-negative" presentation: the muscle is torn but the Achilles tendon remains intact. The rapid progression of swelling and bruising distally toward the ankle reflects muscle fiber disruption with hemorrhage accumulating along tissue planes. Management hinges on the imaging grade, with grade 2-3 strains requiring extended offloading and progressive rehabilitation to restore both strength and plantarflexion endurance.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 5-year-old boy is referred by his parents because he is falling frequently and struggling to keep up with age-matched peers during play. Over the past year, he has noticed increasing difficulty with running and climbing stairs. His parents report he was reaching normal developmental milestones until recently.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child demonstrates a characteristic sign when rising from the floor: he uses his hands to 'climb up' his own legs. He walks with an exaggerated waddling gait and toe-walks when running. Stairs require bannister support; jumping is impossible. Generalized proximal weakness is evident.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The child achieved normal early motor milestones. His maternal uncles had similar childhood-onset progressive weakness. No siblings or father are affected. Onset was insidious—no trauma or specific event triggered the decline. Progressive functional loss over one year.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Strength testing: hip and shoulder girdle 2-3/5; ankle plantarflexion preserved at 5/5 (disproportionate to hip weakness). Calf muscles appear enlarged despite weakness. Waddling gait with marked lumbar lordosis. Scapular winging. No sensory loss. Reflexes preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serum creatine kinase markedly elevated (>10,000 IU/L). Muscle biopsy immunostaining shows complete absence of dystrophin protein. Genetic testing confirms mutation in the DMD gene. EMG demonstrates myopathic pattern with brief, small polyphasic motor units.

puzzles.explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) is an X-linked recessive disorder caused by mutations in the dystrophin gene, leading to progressive degeneration of muscle fibers due to loss of the dystrophin protein that normally stabilizes the sarcolemmal membrane. It classically presents in boys aged 3–5 years with proximal weakness, the characteristic Gowers' sign (climbing up the legs to rise from the floor), and a waddling gait—reflecting early involvement of hip and pelvic stabilizers. The pseudohypertrophy of calves, alongside profound proximal weakness and markedly elevated CK levels, reflects rapid myonecrosis and replacement with fat and fibrotic tissue. X-linked inheritance explains the maternal family history of affected uncles and the complete absence of dystrophin on immunostaining, establishing the diagnosis and predicting relentless progression without current disease-modifying treatments.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old presents 6 weeks after a motor vehicle accident with ongoing headache, difficulty concentrating, and dizziness. A concussion was diagnosed acutely after loss of consciousness. Persistent post-concussion symptoms are limiting return to work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Headache, difficulty concentrating, and dizziness are limiting daily function. Symptoms worsen with cognitive exertion and visual/vestibular demands. Patient is unable to sustain work tasks or driving.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Moderate-speed motor vehicle collision with loss of consciousness for 15-20 seconds. No focal neurological injury identified acutely. Patient is slowly returning to activities but symptoms persist weeks beyond expected recovery. Previous concussion history is negative.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cognitive testing shows mild slowing on reaction time tasks and impaired working memory. Oculomotor testing (smooth pursuit) shows minor asymmetry. Balance testing (tandem stance) is mildly abnormal. Vestibulo-ocular reflex testing may show minor impairment. Headache provocation testing reproduces pain with cognitive exertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI is structurally normal with no focal lesions, hemorrhage, or contusion. Diffusion tensor imaging may show subtle white matter microstructural changes. Imaging rules out structural complications and supports clinical diagnosis via SCAT5 (Sport Concussion Assessment Tool).

puzzles.explanation: Post-concussion syndrome (PCS) is a persistent cluster of physical, cognitive, and vestibular symptoms that extend beyond the typical 7–14 day recovery window following a concussion, reflecting ongoing neurometabolic dysfunction and subtle axonal injury rather than structural brain damage. The hallmark presentation—protracted headache, concentration difficulties, dizziness, and symptom exacerbation with cognitive or visual demands—arises from disrupted neurotransmitter balance and impaired cerebral autoregulation following the initial traumatic injury. In this case, the 6-week timeline well beyond acute resolution, combined with objective deficits in reaction time and balance alongside normal structural imaging, is classic for PCS. Rehabilitation focuses on graded vestibular and cognitive challenge, as premature return to cognitively demanding tasks typically perpetuates symptoms through a cycle of exertional intolerance.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old reports onset of dizziness and spinning sensation 3 weeks ago. Symptoms began shortly after a viral upper respiratory infection. Patient describes feeling unsteady and experiencing blurred vision when moving the head quickly.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Head movements—especially turning or tilting—trigger or worsen dizziness and visual blur. Patient naturally compensates by keeping head still and tilting toward one side. Walking and balance are relatively preserved when head remains stationary.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Dizziness began acutely 3 weeks ago following viral URI. Initial severe spinning lasted approximately 5 days and has gradually improved, but head movement intolerance persists. No hearing loss, tinnitus, or ear fullness. No trauma or prior episodes of vertigo.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR) appears reduced on one side when performing head impulse test—eyes lag behind head movement on affected side. Gaze remains relatively stable when head is still. No nystagmus at rest. Balance and coordination testing are largely normal. Hearing is grossly normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain and internal auditory canal normal—rules out structural lesion or stroke. Videonystagmography shows reduced VOR gain on the affected side. Caloric testing confirms reduced vestibular response on the affected side compared to normal side (unilateral weakness).

puzzles.explanation: Unilateral vestibular hypofunction results from partial or complete loss of function in one vestibular system—the balance organs in the inner ear and their central connections—most commonly following viral labyrinthitis, head trauma, or idiopathic causes. The acute onset after a viral URI followed by gradual improvement over weeks is classic for post-viral vestibular dysfunction, where viral infection inflames the vestibular nerve or labyrinth. The pathognomonic finding is an impaired vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR) on the affected side—during head turns, the eyes lag behind because the damaged vestibular system cannot generate the compensatory eye movement needed to maintain stable gaze, causing the characteristic head movement intolerance and visual blur. Unilateral lesions typically spare hearing and preserve balance when the head is still, since the opposite vestibular system and other proprioceptive/visual feedback can maintain postural control; caloric testing and videonystagmography confirm asymmetric vestibular weakness and are essential for lateralizing the lesion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old recreational cyclist presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the ring and little fingers after recent increases in cycling volume (now 40+ miles weekly). Weakness in finger spreading movements is developing. Onset was gradual over the past 3–4 weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Gripping handlebars reliably triggers numbness in the affected fingers within 30 minutes of cycling. Fine motor tasks (typing, writing) are increasingly difficult. The patient reports finger clumsiness when attempting delicate hand tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-distance cycling is the main recreational activity with recent increase in cycling distance (from 20 to 40+ miles per week). Handlebar type and positioning have not changed. No previous nerve problems reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Finger abduction and adduction strength are reduced (4/5) indicating intrinsic hand muscle weakness. Two-point discrimination is decreased in the ring and little fingers. Sensation to light touch is mildly reduced. The ulnar pulse is palpable at the wrist. No atrophy is evident yet (recent onset).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of ulnar nerve conduction across the wrist and palm, consistent with compression at the wrist level (Guyon canal region). EMG shows mild denervation activity in interossei muscles. Ultrasound may show compression of the ulnar nerve at the wrist from external pressure. Imaging confirms ulnar neuropathy from compression.

puzzles.explanation: Cyclist's palsy is a compression neuropathy of the ulnar nerve at the wrist, typically in the Guyon canal region, caused by prolonged pressure from gripping handlebars during cycling. The ulnar nerve innervates the intrinsic hand muscles (interossei and hypothenar muscles) and the medial one-and-a-half fingers, explaining why this patient's ring and little finger numbness occurs alongside weakness in finger spreading—a hallmark sign of intrinsic muscle dysfunction. The gradual onset over weeks with reliable reproduction on handlebar contact, combined with preserved ulnar pulse and absence of atrophy, is classic for external compression rather than systemic ulnar nerve pathology. Nerve conduction abnormalities localized to the wrist level and mild denervation in the interossei confirm the diagnosis; relief typically follows handlebar padding modifications and load management rather than surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old male reports frequent urination (10-12 times daily, 4-5 times nightly) for 6 months with sudden, difficult-to-postpone urges to void. Denies pain, dysuria, or fever.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids travel, public venues, and prolonged seated activities due to unpredictable urge timing. Nocturia severely disrupts sleep quality and daytime alertness. Symptoms occur independent of posture, activity level, or specific movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms escalated 6 months ago following initiation of new antihypertensive medication. History of benign prostate enlargement managed medically. No urinary tract infections, trauma, or neurological disease. Independent living; primary concern is social restriction and fatigue.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pelvic floor muscles demonstrate normal voluntary contraction strength and resting tone. Lower limb sensation and reflexes intact. Post-void residual 60 mL by ultrasound (mild). Abdominal exam unremarkable. Mild prostate enlargement on digital rectal exam, no nodules or tenderness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Urodynamic study shows involuntary detrusor muscle contractions during bladder filling phase unrelated to voluntary initiation. Normal bladder compliance and post-void residual <100 mL. Renal ultrasound normal; no hydronephrosis or structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Overactive bladder (OAB) is a syndrome of urinary urgency, typically accompanied by frequency and nocturia, resulting from involuntary detrusor contractions during the filling phase—as demonstrated on urodynamics here. The condition reflects a loss of normal inhibitory control over the detrusor muscle, most commonly idiopathic but frequently exacerbated by medications (particularly certain antihypertensives), mild outlet obstruction, or age-related changes in neural signalling. The hallmark presentation of sudden, difficult-to-postpone urges occurring independent of posture or activity, combined with preserved pelvic floor strength and normal post-void residual, distinguishes OAB from neurogenic dysfunction or outlet obstruction. While the benign prostate enlargement and recent medication change are relevant factors, the urodynamic finding of involuntary detrusor activity during filling is the pathophysiological cornerstone that defines this diagnosis and guides conservative management through bladder retraining and antimuscarinic therapy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old presents with several months of burning pain in the sole of the foot and medial ankle region. Symptoms worsen with activity and are particularly bothersome at night. Mild ankle swelling is noted. Pain is described as sharp and burning rather than dull ache.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Standing and walking aggravate the burning sensation in the foot. Tight shoes worsen symptoms. Sleeping position changes are frequent due to nighttime foot pain. Running is not attempted due to symptom aggravation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a history of ankle inversion sprains and currently has mild ankle instability. Mild varicose veins are present. No significant trauma precipitated current symptoms, which developed insidiously over months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's sign at the posterior tibial nerve (behind medial malleolus) reproduces tingling into the sole of the foot. Pain is localized to the medial ankle and sole of foot (not lateral ankle as in peroneal neuropathy). Toe sensation is reduced, particularly medial toes (plantar surface).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of posterior tibial nerve conduction velocity specifically across the ankle region. EMG demonstrates denervation pattern in abductor hallucis. MRI reveals thickening of the flexor retinaculum compressing the posterior tibial nerve within the tarsal tunnel space.

puzzles.explanation: Tarsal tunnel syndrome results from compression of the posterior tibial nerve as it passes beneath the flexor retinaculum behind the medial malleolus, most commonly from tenosynovial inflammation, ganglion cysts, or structural narrowing of the tarsal tunnel space. The condition presents with burning pain and paraesthesia in the sole of the foot and medial heel—the plantar nerve distribution—often worse with prolonged standing, walking, and at night when recumbency allows swelling to compress the nerve further. The bilateral nocturnal symptoms and reduced sensation in the medial plantar toes, combined with positive Tinel's sign reproducing symptoms at the medial ankle, are classic presentations. Electrodiagnostic confirmation via slowed posterior tibial nerve conduction velocity across the ankle, together with denervation in foot intrinsics like the abductor hallucis, distinguishes this from generalized peripheral neuropathy or lumbar radiculopathy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 14-year-old girl presents after her school health check revealed uneven shoulder and hip height. Her mother has noticed this asymmetry has become more obvious over the past 2 years. The patient is otherwise healthy and has no pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports occasional mid-back discomfort when sitting at her desk for long periods. Otherwise, she participates fully in sports and daily activities without restriction. Main concern is the visible asymmetry.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Menarche occurred 18 months ago, indicating rapid skeletal growth. The patient is tall for age (85th percentile). No significant family history of scoliosis is reported. General health is good.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Postural assessment reveals right shoulder higher than left and right hip higher than left. Adam's forward bend test shows rib hump on the right side, indicating structural scoliosis. Truncal asymmetry is evident. Neurological examination is normal with no deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Posteroanterior standing spine radiographs show right thoracic curve measuring 28 degrees (Cobb angle measurement). No significant structural abnormalities or vertebral anomalies are noted. Skeletal maturity assessment using Risser sign is 2/5, indicating significant remaining growth. MRI is not indicated given the benign clinical presentation and normal neurological exam.

puzzles.explanation: Scoliosis is a three-dimensional spinal deformity characterized by lateral curvature and vertebral rotation, most commonly idiopathic and appearing during the adolescent growth spurt. Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) typically emerges in girls aged 10–16 and progresses proportionally to skeletal growth velocity, making the post-menarchal period with ongoing skeletal maturity (Risser 2/5) a critical window for curve progression. The structural nature of this 28° right thoracic curve, confirmed by the rib hump on forward bending and radiographic Cobb measurement, distinguishes it from postural asymmetry and warrants monitoring given the remaining growth potential. Early detection through school screening, combined with serial imaging during periods of rapid growth, remains essential to identify curves at risk of significant progression and determine the need for bracing or surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 36-year-old reports bilateral head pressure that has worsened over 6 months, occurring on most days. Pain quality is described as tightening or pressing. Intensity is mild to moderate.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Headaches worsen with stress and prolonged computer use. Fatigue and poor sleep quality exacerbate symptoms. Physical activity and rest provide only partial relief. Medication is required 3-4 times weekly for pain control.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient works in high-stress environment with poor work-life balance and inconsistent sleep schedule. Caffeine consumption is 4-5 cups daily. Prior headache evaluation has excluded migraine with aura.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness of bilateral upper trapezius and cervical paraspinal muscles is palpable. Neck range of motion is mildly limited in all directions with muscle tightness. Neurological examination is normal. No vision changes, sensory deficits, or focal neurological signs are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT and MRI imaging are unremarkable when performed. Imaging adds no diagnostic value given absence of red flags (no focal neurological signs, normal vision, no acute change in headache character or severity).

puzzles.explanation: Tension-type headache is a primary headache disorder characterized by bilateral, pressing or tightening pain of mild to moderate intensity, arising from sustained contraction of cervical and scalp musculature rather than vascular or inflammatory mechanisms. Unlike migraine, it lacks aura, photophobia, and nausea, and responds modestly to rest alone—the key distinguishing feature in this case is the bilateral distribution, daily frequency, and clear relationship to muscle tension and psychosocial stressors like work stress and poor sleep. The palpable tenderness in the upper trapezius and cervical paraspinals reflects the underlying musculoskeletal component, though tension-type headache should be understood as a centrally-mediated pain state amplified by peripheral muscle dysfunction and psychological factors. Management focuses on addressing modifiable triggers—stress reduction, postural awareness, sleep hygiene, and caffeine modulation—alongside manual therapy and cervical stabilization exercises to break the cycle of muscular guarding.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 8-month-old child. Parents report one leg appears visibly shorter than the other and sits lower when infant is lying on their back. No known injury or trauma. Child otherwise developing normally.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty achieving symmetric sitting position. When placed on back with hips and knees bent, one knee drops outward noticeably lower than the other. Limitation in bringing knees together.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Born full-term, uncomplicated delivery. Not identified as high-risk in newborn screening. Family history significant: maternal aunt required hip surgery as a young child. No imaging obtained in first months of life.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked asymmetry in hip abduction range—one side opens freely, other side resists and restricts. Palpable clunking sensation with gentle hip manipulation during passive movement. Asymmetric skin creases on inner thigh and buttock area. Apparent limb length discrepancy confirmed with supine measurement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hip ultrasound shows femoral head on affected side poorly positioned within acetabulum with inadequate bony coverage. Acetabular roof angle measurements indicate significant structural insufficiency. Finding consistent with femoral head malalignment requiring surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: Developmental dysplasia of the hip (DDH) occurs when the femoral head fails to develop properly within the acetabulum, resulting in inadequate bony coverage and potential instability—a condition with strong genetic predisposition, as evidenced by the family history here. The classic presentation of unilateral involvement manifests as apparent limb length discrepancy, restricted abduction, and the pathognomonic clunk (Ortolani sign) on manipulation, reflecting the femoral head's tendency to slip in and out of a shallow socket. Early detection through newborn screening ultrasound is critical, as missed cases like this one progress to significant structural deformity and functional limitation; the asymmetric skin creases and knee positioning during sitting reflect the underlying hip dysplasia and muscle guarding. Imaging confirms the diagnosis by demonstrating poor femoral head centering and insufficient acetabular coverage, guiding early surgical intervention to prevent long-term disability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old develops progressive buttock pain with radiating discomfort down the posterior leg mimicking sciatica. Pain is worse with prolonged sitting and positional changes. No low back pain or radicular symptoms typical of disc herniation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting (especially 4-6 hours without breaks) and internal hip rotation movements. Symptoms improve significantly with standing, walking, or lying down. Sitting tolerance is severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long sedentary work history with desk-based occupation. No acute trauma or low back injury. Previous similar episodes occurred and resolved with activity modification. Chronic constipation or straining present. No imaging or formal diagnosis sought previously.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tender point over piriformis muscle just medial to the ischial tuberosity. Positive Freiberg's test (internal rotation of flexed hip reproduces buttock/leg pain). Positive Pace test (resisted external rotation reproduces pain). Straight leg raise is pain-free, differentiating from true radiculopathy.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine imaging (CT or MRI) is normal with no disc herniation or nerve root compression, ruling out true radiculopathy. Diagnostic ultrasound or MRI may show piriformis muscle enlargement, trigger points, or asymmetry. Imaging supports piriformis syndrome diagnosis by excluding lumbar pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Piriformis syndrome results from compression or irritation of the sciatic nerve as it passes through or beneath the piriformis muscle in the deep gluteal space, often triggered by prolonged sitting, muscle hypertonicity, or chronic pelvic floor dysfunction. The condition mimics radiculopathy with buttock pain radiating down the posterior leg, but crucially lacks the low back involvement and true dermatomal distribution of nerve root compression—distinguishing features reinforced here by normal imaging and a pain-free straight leg raise. Sedentary occupations and positional loading patterns that maintain hip flexion and internal rotation create the perfect environment for piriformis irritability and sciatic nerve compromise. Freiberg's and Pace tests, combined with point tenderness over the muscle belly and imaging exclusion of lumbar pathology, form the clinical signature of this diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports bilateral anterior shin pain that occurs predictably during running activity. Pain develops after consistent running distance and resolves completely with rest. The condition recurs with return to running at similar intensity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot sustain running beyond a specific distance threshold before anterior compartment tightness forces activity cessation. Pain does not occur with walking or cycling at moderate intensity. Symptoms do not occur with sport-specific drills performed at low speed, only with sustained endurance running.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset correlates with increased running volume or intensity. The athlete may have history of shin splints or tibial stress. Repetitive high-impact activities such as running on hard surfaces exacerbate symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pre-exercise examination may be normal with no resting compartment tenderness. Post-exercise examination reveals tight and tender anterior compartments. Compartment pressure measurement at rest is normal but elevated post-exercise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Compartment pressure measurement obtained immediately post-exercise (within 1-5 minutes) demonstrates elevated pressures in anterior compartment. Post-exercise pressure exceeds 30 mmHg with slow return to normal. Serial measurements during standardized exercise protocol establish diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic exertional compartment syndrome (CECS) occurs when the fascia surrounding a muscle compartment becomes inelastic, causing interstitial pressure to rise excessively during exercise and compromise microvascular perfusion. Unlike acute compartment syndrome, CECS develops insidiously in athletes with high training loads and presents as predictable, reproducible pain that emerges at a consistent threshold and completely resolves with rest — a pattern that distinguishes it from stress fractures or chronic periostitis. The bilateral anterior shin presentation in this runner is typical, reflecting the symmetric loading demands of running. Diagnosis relies on post-exercise compartment pressure measurement, as resting pressures are normal; the sustained elevation and slow normalization following activity confirm the pathophysiology of exercise-induced volume expansion within an unyielding fascial compartment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old office worker reports acute onset severe shoulder pain that began yesterday during desk work. Sharp anterior pain with nocturnal symptoms disrupting sleep. No preceding trauma or specific movement recalled.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform overhead tasks. Pain markedly worsens with abduction and cross-body reaching. Avoids lying on affected side. Active range of motion severely limited by guarding; passive movements tolerated better.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior shoulder injury or overhead/repetitive activities. Pain intensity appears excessive relative to desk-based occupation, suggesting acute inflammatory process rather than cumulative strain. Previously asymptomatic.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe tenderness over supraspinatus tendon insertion. Passive range of motion better preserved than active motion. Supraspinatus strength testing limited by pain rather than true weakness. Dugas test negative (shoulder can still adduct). Hawkins test markedly positive reproducing sharp pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder X-ray (AP and scapular Y views) demonstrates dense calcium deposit within supraspinatus tendon measuring 8x6mm. Calcium appears radiodense with well-defined borders. Associated soft tissue swelling visible around deposit. MRI shows T2 hypointense deposit with surrounding fluid signal indicating active inflammation and resorption phase.

puzzles.explanation: Calcific tendinitis is a self-limiting inflammatory condition caused by deposition of calcium hydroxyapatite crystals within tendons, most commonly affecting the supraspinatus of the shoulder in middle-aged individuals. The acute phase occurs during resorption, when macrophages mobilize the calcium deposit and trigger a vigorous inflammatory response—explaining the disproportionate pain relative to a minor desk-based injury and the nocturnal symptoms that disrupt sleep. The characteristic imaging findings of a dense, well-demarcated deposit with surrounding fluid signal indicate active inflammation, while the preservation of passive range of motion with severely guarded active motion reflects pain-mediated inhibition rather than structural damage. Importantly, calcific tendinitis typically resolves spontaneously over weeks to months as the calcium is reabsorbed, making this a condition where reassurance, anti-inflammatory management, and activity modification are often more effective than aggressive intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with progressive sensation of heaviness and dragging discomfort in the lower pelvis that worsens as the day goes on. She reports feeling uncomfortable during sexual activity. Symptoms began gradually several years ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Heaviness sensation worsens with prolonged standing, lifting, or straining. Walking longer distances triggers worsening symptoms. Symptoms improve significantly when lying down. Exercise and physical activity are limited by perineal discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient is gravida 4 para 4 (four pregnancies, four vaginal deliveries). Menopause occurred 8 years ago without hormone replacement therapy. Chronic straining from constipation has been a lifelong issue. Previous pelvic surgery has not been performed.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visual inspection at rest shows the vaginal tissues bulging slightly. Valsalva maneuver exacerbates the bulge significantly with visible prolapse of the vaginal vault. Palpation confirms laxity of the pelvic floor support structures. Pelvic floor contraction strength is reduced (2/5).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound or MRI with Valsalva demonstrates significant descent of the vaginal vault and bladder neck below the pelvic floor plane, consistent with Stage III-IV prolapse. Imaging also reveals rectocele component, indicating concurrent posterior compartment involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic organ prolapse results from loss of structural support to the pelvic organs, typically due to cumulative trauma from multiple vaginal deliveries, chronic straining, and pelvic floor muscle denervation and atrophy. The progressive heaviness and dragging sensation that worsens with positional loading—prolonged standing, lifting, walking—reflects herniation of pelvic structures (here involving the vaginal vault and rectocele) beyond their normal anatomical support plane. Postmenopausal status accelerates progression through loss of estrogen-mediated collagen integrity, and the significantly reduced pelvic floor contractility (2/5) demonstrates the underlying neuromuscular dysfunction that permits descent. Stage III-IV prolapse with concurrent rectocele, as shown on imaging with Valsalva strain, explains both the mechanical symptoms and sexual dysfunction, and indicates the need for both conservative pelvic floor rehabilitation and consideration of surgical intervention depending on symptom severity and patient goals.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 74-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with acute-onset mid-back pain following a minor fall from standing height. Pain is localized to the thoracic spine and began immediately after the incident.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient walks with a flexed posture and is unable to extend the trunk without significant pain. Self-care tasks such as dressing and bathing require assistance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Osteoporosis was diagnosed 5 years ago (T-score -3.2) but treatment adherence has been poor. The patient is a long-time smoker with low body weight (BMI 19). No previous fracture history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness is elicited over the mid-thoracic spine. Trunk extension is severely limited by pain, whereas forward flexion is relatively preserved. Neurological examination is intact with no focal deficits or myelopathic signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain thoracic radiographs demonstrate anterior vertebral body wedging with loss of height at the thoracic level. MRI shows acute bone marrow edema (STIR signal increase) within the affected vertebra, confirming an acute fracture with no canal compromise.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures occur when weakened vertebral bodies fail under axial loading, typically from minimal trauma in patients with severe bone mineral density loss. The thoracic spine is particularly vulnerable due to its natural kyphosis and load-bearing role, and postmenopausal women face accelerated bone loss from estrogen deficiency—compounded here by smoking and low body weight, which further compromise bone quality. The acute onset following a simple fall, combined with the characteristic wedge-shaped anterior vertebral collapse and bone marrow edema on MRI, is pathognomonic for a fresh fracture. The flexed posture and pain-limited extension reflect both the acute fracture pain and the biomechanical deformity; preserved forward flexion and intact neurology indicate no cord compression, making this a stable fracture suitable for conservative management and aggressive bone-protective therapy to prevent recurrence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old reports progressive weakness in the feet and lower legs over the past 5 years, with increasing difficulty walking and running. Patient notes high-arched feet and curled toes. Symptoms began in late adolescence.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient experiences recurrent inversion ankle sprains and foot instability. Ankle dorsiflexion weakness is evident during gait. Fine motor tasks involving foot intrinsic muscles are impaired.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Similar symptoms present in father (diagnosed with inherited neuropathy). Patient's symptoms began in late adolescence with gradual, insidious progression. No trauma, toxin exposure, diabetes, or metabolic disease. Symmetrical distal-to-proximal progression pattern noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lower leg and foot muscle atrophy is prominent with 'stork leg' appearance (wasting of calf muscles). Strength testing shows weakness of ankle dorsiflexors (2/5) and plantarflexors (3/5). Intrinsic foot muscle atrophy and weakness result in hammer toe deformities. Sensation to vibration and position sense is mildly reduced distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slow conduction velocity (demyelinating pattern) in all nerves tested, consistent with hereditary motor-sensory neuropathy. EMG shows denervation patterns in distal muscles. Genetic testing confirms PMP22 gene duplication (CMT1A). DNA sequencing identifies the specific mutation confirming diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease is a hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy caused by mutations affecting myelin formation or axonal integrity, with CMT1A (demyelinating type) resulting from PMP22 gene duplication being the most common form. It characteristically presents with distal-to-proximal weakness and atrophy beginning in late adolescence or early adulthood, classically affecting ankle dorsiflexors first and leading to the distinctive 'stork leg' appearance, pes cavus, and hammer toe deformities seen here. The combination of slow nerve conduction velocities, a positive family history, and symmetrical lower limb involvement with preserved sensation makes CMT1A clinically recognizable, though definitive diagnosis requires genetic confirmation. Progression is typically slow and gradual, and while there is no cure, physiotherapy focusing on ankle stability, foot intrinsic strengthening, and gait retraining can help manage functional decline and prevent secondary complications like recurrent inversion sprains.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old presents 10 weeks post-operatively from wrist fracture fixation with severe hand pain and swelling that seem excessive for the surgical trauma. Pain is burning in quality and triggered by minimal stimuli.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Protective posturing of the hand. Any light touch triggers severe pain; active movement attempts provoke intense pain and patient spontaneously avoids use. Patient holds hand immobilized and has withdrawn from rehabilitation participation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: A Colles fracture was treated surgically with open reduction and internal fixation. Immediate post-operative period was unremarkable for the first 2-3 weeks. Subsequently, pain has progressively worsened rather than improved. The patient is anxious about hand recovery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hand demonstrates marked edema, warmth, and erythema with cyanotic discoloration. Profuse sweating noted. Skin is glossy, thin, and fragile with visible trophic changes. Allodynia confirmed—even light pressure elicits severe pain disproportionate to stimulus.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Three-phase bone scan demonstrates increased blood pooling in delayed phase. Radiographs show patchy osteoporosis in hand and wrist bones. MRI reveals diffuse soft tissue and bone marrow edema throughout the hand.

puzzles.explanation: Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) is a maladaptive pain condition characterized by dysregulation of the peripheral and central nervous systems following tissue injury, typically a fracture or surgery. Rather than resolving with normal healing, affected patients develop a constellation of signs that reflect autonomic dysfunction—the marked edema, erythema, cyanosis, hyperhidrosis, and trophic skin changes seen here—alongside a pain response grossly disproportionate to the inciting injury. The progressive worsening beyond 2–3 weeks post-operatively, combined with severe allodynia and the patient's protective avoidance of movement, creates a self-perpetuating cycle: immobilization and pain-fear avoidance lead to reduced blood flow, osteoporosis, and further central sensitization. Early recognition and aggressive rehabilitation—despite the pain—remain critical, as delayed intervention risks irreversible functional loss and chronicity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old overhead athlete reports episodic shoulder apprehension during throwing. States fear of shoulder 'slipping out' but no confirmed dislocation history. Symptom onset insidious, worsening over past 6 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Throwing is limited by apprehension when the shoulder reaches 90 degrees abduction and external rotation position. The patient modifies throwing mechanics to reduce apprehension. Reaching behind the head with arm abducted reproduces apprehension. Sleeping on the affected shoulder is avoided.

clues.label: History
clues.text: A previous anterior shoulder dislocation occurred 2 years ago while pitching, which was reduced in the emergency department. Physical therapy was performed but return to competitive pitching resulted in symptom recurrence. The patient continues to play despite apprehension.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Apprehension test positive in supine, 90° abduction/external rotation. Relocation test positive. External rotation ROM 110° (hypermobile compared to contralateral 80°). Anterior drawer test and load-and-shift test mildly positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows possible Bankart lesion (anteroinferior glenoid labrum detachment) or reverse Hill-Sachs lesion. 3D CT reconstruction may show glenoid bone loss if engaging Hill-Sachs lesion is present. Imaging identifies labral pathology and glenoid anatomy, guiding management (conservative versus surgical).

puzzles.explanation: Glenohumeral instability results from disruption of the dynamic and static stabilizers of the shoulder joint, most commonly the glenoid labrum and capsular structures, allowing excessive humeral head translation. In this overhead athlete with prior dislocation, the initial traumatic event created a Bankart lesion (anteroinferior labral detachment) that impairs the labrum's role in deepening the glenoid socket and restraining anterior translation. The characteristic apprehension in the 90° abduction and external rotation position reflects the compromised restraint at the end-range position critical to throwing mechanics, and the positive relocation test confirms the anterior instability pattern. The combination of hypermobility, positive load-and-shift findings, and labral pathology on imaging distinguishes true instability from pure hypermobility, and the presence of glenoid bone loss or engaging Hill-Sachs lesions may determine whether conservative rehabilitation or surgical stabilization offers the best prognosis for return to competitive sport.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old with a history of prematurity and early developmental delay presents with progressive difficulty with coordinated movement. Parents report the child is clumsy and unsteady, particularly when trying to pick up objects or walk quickly.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking is unsteady with a wide-based gait. Running is impossible. Fine motor tasks (writing, eating utensil use, building with blocks) are significantly impaired. The child cannot jump or skip with typical age-appropriate coordination.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Born at 34 weeks with documented intraventricular hemorrhage (grade 2). Developmental milestones were delayed. CP diagnosis established at 18 months. No history of spasticity, seizures, or progressive regression. Premature birth with IVH is the known etiology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pronounced intention tremor is evident when the child reaches for objects with the arms, worsening as the hand approaches the target (cerebellar dysmetria). Wide-based gait with poor balance and trunk control is observed. Muscle tone is low-normal (hypotonia). Reflexes are normal. No spasticity is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI shows cerebellar hypoplasia with reduced cerebellar volume and thin cerebellar folia. The cerebral cortex appears structurally normal. White matter appears appropriately myelinated. The imaging pattern is consistent with cerebellar hypodevelopment from perinatal injury.

puzzles.explanation: Ataxic cerebral palsy results from perinatal damage to the cerebellum or its connections, disrupting the coordination circuits essential for smooth, purposeful movement. Unlike the more common spastic forms of CP, ataxic CP presents with hypotonia, intention tremor, and dysmetria—the hallmark triad reflecting cerebellar dysfunction rather than upper motor neuron pathology. The wide-based gait, poor balance, and progressive difficulty with fine motor control seen in this child reflect the cerebellum's critical role in coordinating muscle timing and amplitude across multiple joints. Premature birth with intraventricular hemorrhage is a well-recognized risk factor for cerebellar injury in neonates, and the MRI findings of cerebellar hypoplasia confirm this as the structural basis of the movement disorder.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old presents with persistent leg pain and weakness 6 weeks after lumbar spinal surgery. Symptom improvement has plateaued despite expectations for continued recovery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking tolerance remains limited to 15 minutes despite expected improvement to 30+ minutes by this point in recovery. Standing from sitting is difficult. Running is not attempted. The patient remains on significant opioid pain medication.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe lumbar stenosis at L3-L4 with left leg radiculopathy was treated surgically with left L4 laminectomy and L4-L5 decompression. Pre-operative imaging clearly showed severe stenosis and neural foraminal compression. The patient had significant pre-operative leg pain (7/10).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left ankle dorsiflexion strength remains 3+/5, unchanged from pre-operative. Hip flexion strength is intact at 5/5. Palpation reveals no surgical site tenderness or instability. Lumbar segmental mobility at L4-L5 is present but guarded. No lower limb reflexes are diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative MRI shows adequate decompression with relief of stenosis and neural foraminal opening at L4-L5. However, adjacent-level changes and subtle degenerative disc disease at L3-L4 are now more apparent. No recurrent stenosis at the surgical level. Findings suggest adjacent-segment dysfunction contributing to persistent symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Post-laminectomy syndrome describes persistent or recurrent symptoms following spinal decompression surgery, occurring in 10–15% of cases despite technically adequate neural decompression. The condition arises from multiple mechanisms: incomplete resolution of pre-existing nerve damage (as reflected in the persistent ankle dorsiflexion weakness matching the pre-operative deficit), adjacent-segment degeneration and instability from altered biomechanics at the surgical level, and occasionally scar tissue formation or residual inflammation. In this case, adequate decompression of the stenosis and neural foraminal opening is confirmed on imaging, yet the plateau in functional recovery and persistent radicular-pattern weakness at 6 weeks—coupled with emerging adjacent-level changes—points to irreversible nerve injury from chronic compression rather than ongoing mechanical obstruction. Management typically shifts from surgical expectations to rehabilitation focused on neural mobilisation, functional loading tolerance, and addressing the secondary degenerative changes driving the sustained neuropathic component.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old male with insidious onset lower back and buttock pain over 8 months. Morning stiffness lasting 1–2 hours daily. Pain improves with movement and activity, worsens with rest and inactivity. Nocturnal pain disrupting sleep. Associated fatigue.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Marked difficulty with forward spinal bending and flexion. Pain worsens after prolonged sitting >30 minutes. Stiffness improves substantially within 1–2 hours of getting up and moving. Exercise and walking provide symptom relief. Progressive loss of lumbar range of motion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain began gradually 8 months ago without preceding trauma or injury. No recent infections, diarrhea, or abdominal symptoms. Denies eye redness, skin lesions, or joint swelling elsewhere. No significant medical history or current medications. First episode of spinal symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral buttock tenderness on palpation. Significantly reduced lumbar forward flexion (Schober's test <4 cm). Straight leg raise painless bilaterally. Sacroiliac joint compression tenderness elicited. Thoracic kyphosis slightly increased. Hip flexion contractures present bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI sacroiliac joints: bilateral erosions with inflammatory edema and bone marrow signal changes. Lumbar spine MRI shows syndesmophyte formation bridging adjacent vertebral bodies (early bamboo spine pattern). HLA-B27 positive. Inflammatory markers elevated: ESR 48 mm/hr, CRP 9.2 mg/dL.

puzzles.explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic inflammatory spondyloarthropathy driven by HLA-B27 genetic predisposition, typically affecting young males with insidious onset of axial inflammation at the sacroiliac joints and spine. The disease has a pathognomonic presentation: inflammatory back pain that paradoxically improves with movement and worsens with rest and inactivity—the opposite pattern of mechanical pain—combined with prolonged morning stiffness reflecting the inflammatory nature of the process. Progressive ankylosis occurs through syndesmophyte formation, which bridges adjacent vertebrae and eventually limits spinal mobility, as evidenced here by the bilateral sacroiliac erosions, early bamboo spine pattern, and significantly restricted lumbar flexion. The elevated inflammatory markers, positive HLA-B27, and nocturnal pain disrupting sleep are hallmark features that distinguish this from mechanical low back pain and make early diagnosis critical to prevent progressive spinal fusion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old overhead athlete presents with 3 months of progressive deep posterior shoulder pain and weakness. No prior trauma. Pain worsens with overhead activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Overhead serving in volleyball triggered symptom onset during high-intensity training. Cannot perform overhead activities due to combined pain and weakness. Also carries heavy backpacks recreationally.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Athlete trains 5 days per week with high-volume overhead work and rapid shoulder movements. Recently increased training intensity. No previous shoulder pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Deep posterior shoulder ache is elicited with palpation over the suprascapular notch. External rotation at 0 degrees abduction is significantly weak (2/5) as is abduction strength (3/5). Supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscle atrophy is evident on visual inspection and measurement. The patient demonstrates wasting of the supraspinous and infraspinous fossae.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG demonstrates fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves in supraspinatus and infraspinatus with normal motor conduction in proximal upper limb. Nerve conduction velocity shows slowing across suprascapular notch. MRI reveals ganglion cyst or ligamentous hypertrophy at suprascapular notch causing compression.

puzzles.explanation: Suprascapular neuropathy results from compression of the suprascapular nerve as it passes through the suprascapular notch, most commonly caused by ganglia, ligamentous thickening, or anatomic stenosis — in this athlete's case, imaging confirmed mechanical compression at the notch itself. The nerve supplies both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles, so compression produces the characteristic pattern of deep posterior shoulder pain with selective weakness and atrophy in these two muscles, particularly affecting external rotation and initiation of abduction. This condition is distinctly seen in overhead athletes due to repetitive traction on the nerve during rapid shoulder movements and loading; the absence of preceding trauma and progressive nature over months help distinguish it from acute brachial plexus injury. EMG findings of denervation in the infraspinatus and supraspinatus with preserved proximal motor conduction confirm the diagnosis as a focal nerve lesion rather than a proximal plexopathy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old presents with persistent ankle swelling and severe stiffness several weeks following ankle trauma. Range of motion remains significantly restricted. Pain limits functional recovery and return to normal activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Still requires crutches for ambulation at 8 weeks. Unable to progress weight-bearing as anticipated. Walking causes significant ankle pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Significant soft tissue trauma accompanied the initial fracture injury. Followed standard post-operative protocol: 6 weeks immobilization, then progressive weight-bearing over past 2 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant ankle swelling persists with pitting edema extending into the foot. Ankle dorsiflexion is limited to 5 degrees (normal 15 degrees). Plantarflexion is limited to 25 degrees (normal 50 degrees). Medial and lateral malleoli are tender. Hardware prominence is palpable. No signs of infection (normal temperature, incision healed).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs confirm anatomical reduction of ankle mortise without articular step-off, hardware in correct position, and maintained syndesmotic reduction. No evidence of hardware loosening, migration, or fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Post-ankle open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) represents a critical recovery phase following operative management of displaced ankle fractures, where restoration of anatomical alignment must be followed by careful tissue healing and progressive mobilisation. The persistent swelling, restricted dorsiflexion and plantarflexion, and ongoing weight-bearing limitations at 8 weeks reflect the significant soft tissue injury accompanying the initial fracture and the inflammatory cascade triggered by surgical trauma — normal radiographic findings confirm the hardware is well-positioned, but soft tissue healing and capsular/ligamentous recovery lag behind bony healing. The pitting edema and pronounced stiffness are characteristic of post-operative ankle immobility; prolonged immobilisation preserves fracture stability but increases risk of periarticular fibrosis and arthrokinematic dysfunction. Early progressive mobilisation within safe load parameters, oedema management, and graded functional exercises are essential to restore dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, and weight-bearing tolerance before these restrictions become chronic.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old with osteoporosis presents with severe elbow pain and visible deformity after a fall on an outstretched arm. The elbow is swollen and bruised.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform any elbow motion and is unable to perform self-care activities such as dressing or eating. The arm is immobilized and severely guarded against any movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall occurred while descending stairs, landing directly on the posterior elbow. Patient reports taking oral corticosteroids for 5 years (COPD). No history of prior elbow injury or arthropathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elbow held in slight flexion; marked posterior prominence of olecranon visible. Full thickness ecchymosis over posterior elbow. Radial and ulnar pulses normal and symmetric. Finger sensation and motor function intact. Pain severely limits any passive range testing beyond 20 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: AP and lateral radiographs show a comminuted fracture at the distal humerus with displacement of both medial and lateral condyles. Fracture extends into the articular surface. No associated avulsion fractures. No neurovascular compromise evident.

puzzles.explanation: A distal humerus fracture is a break through the bone just above the elbow joint, commonly occurring in older adults with osteoporosis following a fall on an outstretched arm or direct posterior impact, as in this case. The comminuted pattern seen here—with involvement of both condyles and the articular surface—is characteristic of high-energy trauma in this population and carries significant risk for complications including neurovascular compromise, malunion, and post-traumatic arthritis. The posterior prominence of the olecranon reflects the displacement and fracture fragmentation, while the severe guarding and immediate loss of function underscore why these injuries demand urgent immobilization and orthopedic assessment. Long-term corticosteroid use compounds fracture risk by accelerating bone loss, making this presentation particularly common in patients with chronic lung disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old presents with progressive right groin pain worsened by weight-bearing over 3 years. Morning stiffness in the hip lasting 30 minutes, improving somewhat with initial activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking tolerance has decreased from 45 minutes to 15 minutes due to hip pain. Stairs (particularly descending) provoke sharp groin pain. Putting on shoes and socks requires modification. Standing from a seated position causes pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No significant hip trauma or previous hip surgery. The patient has worked a labor-intensive job requiring prolonged standing for 40 years. Obesity is present with BMI 32. Morning pain is worst, improving with activity but worsening with prolonged exertion.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion limited to 100 degrees, internal rotation severely restricted (15 degrees). Positive Thomas test (15-degree flexion contracture). Hip abductor weakness (3+/5). Positive Trendelenburg sign. Pain reproduced at end-range flexion and internal rotation. Anterior groin palpation tender but non-specific.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hip radiographs show joint space narrowing, osteophytes at the femoral head-neck junction and acetabular rim, and subchondral sclerosis of the femoral head and acetabular roof. Mild acetabular dysplasia may be noted. MRI confirms cartilage loss and bone marrow edema consistent with advanced osteoarthritis.

puzzles.explanation: Hip osteoarthritis results from progressive cartilage degradation within the hip joint, typically driven by cumulative mechanical stress, obesity, and age-related changes in joint homeostasis. The characteristic presentation of morning stiffness improving with activity, combined with progressive weight-bearing pain and a flexion contracture with severely restricted internal rotation, reflects the structural changes visible on imaging — cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis. The positive Trendelenburg sign and hip abductor weakness arise because pain and joint degeneration impair the gluteus medius's ability to stabilize the pelvis during single-leg stance. In this case, decades of labor-intensive work and obesity created repetitive loading stress that accelerated degenerative changes, making hip OA the primary driver of functional decline rather than capsular restriction or acute injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old American football player reports acute onset severe pain and immediate swelling in the anterior thigh following blunt trauma during play. Unable to continue activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Knee flexion severely restricted; unable to walk without significant pain and limp. Weight-bearing tolerance markedly diminished. Complete functional loss for sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Single direct blow to anterior thigh from player contact. First thigh injury; no prior episodes. Excellent premorbid conditioning and no medical comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gross anterior thigh swelling visibly deforming the contour. Ecchymosis developing. Knee flexion severely restricted (pain and mechanical block). Quadriceps strength testing impossible due to pain inhibition. Neurovascular examination intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates intramuscular hematoma and muscle fiber disruption within quadriceps. Radiographs exclude fracture. Serial imaging in 3-4 weeks required to detect heterotopic bone formation, a known complication of high-impact muscular trauma.

puzzles.explanation: Quadriceps contusion is a direct blunt trauma injury to the thigh that disrupts muscle fibers and surrounding vasculature, causing acute intramuscular hemorrhage and edema. The severity depends on the force of impact and depth of penetration; high-energy contact in athletes like football players commonly produces substantial hematomas with visible ecchymosis and dramatic loss of knee flexion due to both pain inhibition and mechanical obstruction from swelling. The key clinical concern beyond the acute phase is heterotopic ossification—pathologic bone formation within muscle tissue—which occurs in roughly 5-20% of significant contusions and can permanently restrict range of motion if not monitored. Serial imaging over weeks is essential to detect early calcification, allowing intervention through aggressive mobilization or, rarely, prophylactic indomethacin or radiation to prevent functional limitation in this otherwise straightforward muscular injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 35-year-old with sudden onset of severe heel pain and swelling after a fall from height. Unable to bear weight. Pain began immediately upon impact.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk or stand. Pain worsens with any attempt to push off the ground or move the ankle downward. Swelling rapid and marked over the heel and sole of foot. Skin appears tight and discolored.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism: fall from 10 feet landing on heels bilaterally. Injury 4 hours ago. Brief loss of consciousness at time of impact. No prior foot injuries or significant medical history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe bilateral heel swelling with ecchymosis. Palpation over heel bone elicits extreme tenderness. Varus deformity of the heel developing. Pain with passive ankle and foot movement. Calf compartment firm; early signs of compartment syndrome (foot numbness, pain with passive stretch, cyanosis).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Heel X-rays reveal fracture of the calcaneus with intra-articular extension and comminution. CT imaging demonstrates fracture pattern, number of bone fragments, subtalar joint involvement, and degree of displacement—findings that guide decision between conservative and surgical management.

puzzles.explanation: Calcaneal fractures result from high-energy axial loading through the heel, typically from falls from height or motor vehicle trauma, and represent approximately 2% of all fractures but up to 60% of tarsal injuries. The mechanism here—bilateral heel strike from a 10-foot fall—is classic, causing the talus to drive downward through the calcaneus, shattering it into multiple fragments with frequent subtalar joint involvement. These injuries present with immediate severe pain, inability to bear weight, and rapid swelling due to extensive soft tissue trauma and bleeding; the varus heel deformity and developing compartment syndrome reflect both the fracture displacement and the significant soft tissue injury that must be monitored closely. Intra-articular comminution, as demonstrated on CT, is the critical prognostic factor—it determines whether conservative treatment or operative reduction offers the best functional outcome, making imaging assessment essential for treatment planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old presents with persistent leg pain 10 weeks after lumbar spine surgery. Expected recovery has not occurred. Pain radiates distally and remains severe.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot tolerate walking or prolonged sitting/standing. Pain is predominantly unilateral and follows a dermatomal pattern to the foot. No improvement in function or pain severity post-operatively.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lumbar spine surgery performed 10 weeks ago for disc herniation with neural compression. Surgical indication was initially straightforward, yet post-operative symptoms have not resolved as anticipated.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left ankle dorsiflexion remains weak at 3/5 with minimal recovery. Straight leg raise is markedly limited. Neurological examination suggests ongoing compression of the L5 nerve root with dermatomal motor and reflex deficits rather than post-operative inflammation alone.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative MRI shows the laminectomy defect with adequate decompression at the original level. However, recurrent disc material is evident at the same level or a different lumbar segment. Re-herniation occurs in 5-15% of cases. Imaging findings determine if revision surgery is indicated or conservative pain management should continue.

puzzles.explanation: Post-laminectomy syndrome describes persistent or recurrent radicular symptoms following lumbar decompressive surgery, most commonly due to re-herniation at the same level or adjacent segment pathology, occurring in 5–15% of post-operative cases. The mechanism here involves recurrent neural compression of the L5 root, evidenced by the unchanged dermatomal motor deficit (weak ankle dorsiflexion at 3/5) and limited straight leg raise 10 weeks post-operatively — timepoints where functional improvement should be evident. Unlike post-operative inflammation which typically resolves within 4–6 weeks, true re-compression produces persistent or worsening radicular pain with ongoing neurological deficits. Management hinges on imaging confirmation of structural recurrence and the patient's tolerance for conservative care versus revision decompression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Office worker reports progressive pain in the wrist and forearm that worsens toward the end of the workday. Notices stiffness in the morning that improves with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to grip a pen or keyboard for more than 30 minutes without sharp pain in the thumb and index finger. Fine motor tasks deteriorate as the day progresses.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Employed as a keyboard operator for 8 hours daily over the past 18 months. No prior arm injuries. Workstation lacks proper ergonomic support. Symptoms began 4 months ago and have progressed despite occasional days off.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Phalen's test and Tinel's sign over the median nerve at the wrist. Grip strength reduced on the affected side. Intrinsic hand muscle atrophy noted. Two-point discrimination mildly diminished in radial digits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows median nerve compression at the carpal tunnel with mild signal changes. No tendon tear. Nerve conduction studies confirm slowed latency across the wrist consistent with carpal tunnel syndrome.

puzzles.explanation: Work-related upper limb disorder (WRULD) encompasses cumulative trauma conditions arising from prolonged, repetitive occupational activities — in this case, eight hours daily of keyboard work without adequate ergonomic support. The median nerve compression at the carpal tunnel reflects tenosynovial irritation and swelling from sustained wrist flexion and repetitive gripping, manifesting as progressive wrist and forearm pain that worsens through the workday before improving with rest and activity the following morning. The constellation of positive Phalen's and Tinel's signs, reduced grip strength, intrinsic muscle atrophy, and slowed nerve conduction across the wrist confirms carpal tunnel syndrome as the underlying mechanism — a hallmark of WRULD. Prevention hinges on ergonomic modification, work pacing, and early intervention; once established, these conditions respond best to activity modification combined with structured rehabilitation to restore neuromuscular control.

Benign Nocturnal Limb Pains of Childhood
